topics.title: History of Hybrid and Electric Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** History of Hybrid and Electric Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

The **History of Hybrid and Electric Vehicles (HEVs & EVs)** encompasses the chronological evolution of vehicles that utilize **electric traction** either in combination with an Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) ‚Äî classified as Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs) ‚Äî or solely through **battery-stored electrical energy** ‚Äî classified as Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs). This evolution spans from the mid-19th century electromagnetic experiments to the modern era of lithium-ion powered, software-defined mobility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OEEET722 - Module 1):**
> This topic forms the foundational **Course Outcome (CO1)** link ‚Äî *"Understand the evolution, need, and social impact of hybrid and electric vehicle technology."* Questions appear frequently as 3-mark direct questions in **Part A** and occasionally as a 7-mark short essay in **Part B**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a long-distance runner. In the **1900s gasoline era**, the runner used only one energy source (sugar/carbs = petrol) ‚Äî efficient but exhausting and polluting. Today, the runner uses **two energy sources** (carbs for long steady pace + gel shots for sprints = petrol + battery), and for short trips, **only the gel shots (battery)** are used. This dual-source, smart-switching approach is exactly what a hybrid-electric vehicle does ‚Äî and understanding its **history** reveals *why* we built it this way.

### Key Terminology You Must Know

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **EV** | Electric Vehicle ‚Äî uses electric motor(s) only, powered by batteries |
| **HEV** | Hybrid Electric Vehicle ‚Äî combines ICE + electric motor |
| **BEV** | Battery Electric Vehicle ‚Äî pure EV with no ICE |
| **PHEV** | Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle ‚Äî HEV with grid-charging capability |
| **FCEV** | Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle ‚Äî uses hydrogen fuel cell |
| **Mild Hybrid** | 48V system assisting ICE, cannot drive in EV-only mode |
| **Full Hybrid** | Can drive in EV-only mode (e.g., Toyota Prius) |

> [!NOTE]
> The history of EVs is not a linear story of progress ‚Äî it is a **cyclical saga of rise, fall, and resurrection** driven by oil prices, battery technology, and environmental policy.

### Standard Physical/Historical Metrics

- First practical electric carriage: **1832‚Äì1839** by Robert Anderson (Scotland)
- World's first hybrid car: **Lohner-Porsche Mixte, 1901** (Austria)
- First mass-produced hybrid: **Toyota Prius, 1997** (Japan)
- Cumulative global EV sales milestone: **10 million units crossed in 2020**
- Battery energy density progress: from **40 Wh/kg (Lead-Acid, 1859)** to **~250-300 Wh/kg (Lithium-ion, 2020s)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Era-wise Evolution ‚Äî Structured Analysis

#### **Era 1: The Dawn (1830s ‚Äì 1880s) ‚Äî Electricity Enters Mobility**

- **1832‚Äì1839:** Robert Anderson (Scotland) builds the first crude electric carriage using non-rechargeable galvanic cells.
- **1859:** Gaston Plant√© (France) invents the **lead-acid battery** ‚Äî the first *rechargeable* battery. This is the single most important invention enabling EVs.
- **1881:** Gustave Trouv√© (France) demonstrates a practical, roadworthy **electric tricycle** in Paris ‚Äî the first public demonstration of an EV.
- **Why this matters:** The technology existed *before* Karl Benz's 1886 gasoline car. EVs were the **default technology choice** of the era due to:
  - Quiet operation
  - No hand-cranking
  - No smelly exhaust
  - Suitable for city use (early cars were for cities)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **'Why' Behind the 19th Century EV Lead:** In 1900, the United States had **38% electric cars, 40% steam cars, and only 22% gasoline cars** (per the IEEE historical archive). EVs were the *dominant* technology of the early automobile era.

#### **Era 2: The Golden Age of EVs (1890s ‚Äì 1920s)**

- **1897:** The first commercial EV fleet ‚Äî **12 electric taxis** in New York City by the Electric Carriage and Wagon Company.
- **1899:** Camille Jenatzy (Belgium) builds the *La Jamais Contente* ‚Äî first car to break the **100 km/h barrier**, reaching **105.88 km/h**, using an electric drivetrain.
- **1900:** Ferdinand Porsche (Austria) designs the **Lohner-Porsche** ‚Äî but the truly landmark vehicle is the **Lohner-Porsche Mixte (1901)** ‚Äî the **world's first series-hybrid car**, with a gasoline engine generating electricity for in-wheel electric motors.
- **1908:** Henry Ford introduces the **Model T** ‚Äî mass production drops gasoline car price to **$260** (vs. ~$1,750 for an EV).
- **1912:** Electric starter motor invented by Charles Kettering ‚Äî eliminates the dangerous hand crank, removing one major advantage of EVs.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The 1901 Lohner-Porsche Mixte predates the Toyota Prius (1997) by **96 years**. This is a favourite question framing in KTU 2024 scheme exams.

#### **Era 3: The ICE Dominance & EV Dark Age (1920s ‚Äì 1960s)**

- Cheap Texas crude oil ($1.05/barrel) ‚Üí gasoline becomes abundant and inexpensive.
- Discovery of vast oil reserves (Texas, Oklahoma, Middle East) ‚Üí **petroleum lobby** strengthens.
- Roads improve (Lincoln Highway, autobahns) ‚Üí long-distance travel favours ICE.
- EV energy density remains limited (lead-acid ~40 Wh/kg).
- EVs become niche ‚Äî used only as **golf carts, milk floats, and industrial forklifts**.

#### **Era 4: The Resurgence (1970s ‚Äì 1990s) ‚Äî Oil Crises Spark Interest**

- **1973 OPEC Oil Crisis:** Oil prices quadruple. US Congress passes the **1976 Electric and Hybrid Vehicle Research, Development, and Demonstration Act**.
- **1976:** The **BETC (Battery and Electric)** vehicle funded by the US Department of Energy.
- **1990:** California Air Resources Board (CARB) issues the **Zero Emission Vehicle (ZEV) Mandate** ‚Äî world's first EV regulation.
- **1990:** GM unveils the **GM Impact** concept (later the EV1, 1996).
- **1996:** **General Motors EV1** ‚Äî first modern mass-produced BEV from a major automaker; leased, never sold; famously crushed in 2003 (documented in the film *Who Killed the Electric Car?*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 'Why' Question:** Why did EVs resurface in the 1970s? ‚Üí Answer: **1973 Arab Oil Embargo + rising environmental awareness + maturation of power electronics (IGBTs, MOSFETs).**

#### **Era 5: The Modern Era (1997 ‚Äì Present) ‚Äî Hybrids Go Mainstream**

- **1997:** **Toyota Prius (1st Gen)** launched in Japan ‚Äî world's first mass-produced hybrid. Initial sales: ~18,000 units. Became a cultural icon.
- **1999:** Honda Insight (1st Gen) ‚Äî first hybrid sold in the USA, achieving **61 mpg** (US), and briefly held the title of most fuel-efficient car in America.
- **2003:** Tesla Motors founded by Martin Eberhard and Marc Tarpenning (later joined by Elon Musk).
- **2008:** **Tesla Roadster** ‚Äî first highway-legal serial production all-electric car to use **lithium-ion battery cells**; range **394 km** per charge.
- **2010:** **Nissan Leaf** ‚Äî first mass-market BEV from a legacy automaker.
- **2010s‚Äì2020s:** **BEV proliferation** ‚Äî Tesla Model S (2012), Model 3 (2017), BYD, Hyundai Ioniq, Tata Nexon EV, MG ZS EV, etc.
- **2020s:** Government **subsidies, Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI) emission norms (India, 2020)**, EU Euro 7, Net-Zero 2050 commitments push EV adoption globally.
- **2023+:** **Solid-state battery R&D, vehicle-to-grid (V2G), autonomous EVs**.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> While this topic is **qualitative/historical**, KTU often tests the **quantitative reasoning** behind why EVs were historically limited. The key formula is **Specific Energy** of energy sources.

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| Specific Energy of Battery | $E_s = \dfrac{E}{m} \;\; [\text{Wh/kg}]$ | Energy stored per unit mass; determines range |
| Gravimetric Energy Density (Petrol) | $E_s \approx 12{,}000 \;\; \text{Wh/kg}$ | Why ICE cars dominated ‚Äî fuel energy density |
| Gravimetric Energy Density (Li-ion) | $E_s \approx 250 \;\; \text{Wh/kg}$ | Current EV benchmark |
| Range of EV (approx.) | $R = \dfrac{C \cdot V}{\dot{m}_e} \cdot \eta_{sys}$ | Where C = capacity, $\dot{m}_e$ = energy/km |
| Oil Price Inflation Impact | $P_t = P_0(1+r)^n$ | Used in policy economics (e.g., 1973 oil shock) |
| Fuel Cell Reaction | $2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O + \text{Energy}$ | Underlies FCEV operation |
| Tank-to-Wheel Efficiency (ICE) | $\eta_{ttw} \approx 20\% - 35\%$ | Low ‚Äî drives hybrid/EV necessity |
| Tank-to-Wheel Efficiency (EV) | $\eta_{ttw} \approx 75\% - 90\%$ | High ‚Äî engineering advantage |

> **CRITICAL RULE (Anti-Table-Break):** In all KTU notes, vertical bars are replaced with $\vert$ in math expressions.

### Real-World Engineering Utility of Studying This History

- **Strategic Forecasting:** Past boom-bust cycles of EVs (1890s‚Äì1930s, 1970s‚Äì1990s, 2000s‚Äìpresent) inform current infrastructure investment decisions.
- **Policy Design:** India's **FAME-I (2015), FAME-II (2019), PLI Scheme (2021)** for ACC battery storage draw lessons from California's 1990 ZEV mandate.
- **Consumer Psychology:** Understanding *why* early 20th-century consumers rejected EVs helps marketers rebuild trust today.
- **Battery R&D Roadmap:** Each historical cycle pushed the boundary ‚Äî from lead-acid ‚Üí NiMH ‚Üí Li-ion ‚Üí solid-state.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation: Why EVs Lost the 1900s Race ‚Äî A Quantitative Analysis

Let us quantitatively prove why gasoline cars overtook electric cars around 1910‚Äì1920. We use a simple **cost-per-km** model.

#### Step 1: Define the cost-per-kilometre metric

$$C_{km} = \dfrac{C_{cap} + C_{fuel} \cdot D}{D}$$

where:
- $C_{km}$ = Total cost per kilometre
- $C_{cap}$ = Capital (purchase) cost of vehicle
- $C_{fuel}$ = Cost of energy per km
- $D$ = Lifetime distance travelled (km)

#### Step 2: Plug in 1910s values

For **Gasoline Car (Ford Model T, 1908-1920 era):**
- $C_{cap} \approx \$260$ (mass production price)
- $C_{fuel} \approx \$0.005 / \text{km}$
- $D \approx 80{,}000 \;\; \text{km}$ (lifetime)

For **Electric Car (e.g., Detroit Electric, 1910s):**
- $C_{cap} \approx \$1{,}750$
- $C_{fuel} \approx \$0.012 / \text{km}$ (electricity was more expensive in cents/km)
- $D \approx 50{,}000 \;\; \text{km}$ (battery life limited the practical lifetime)

#### Step 3: Compute the per-km cost

For the **Gasoline Car:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{km, ICE} &= \dfrac{260 + (0.005)(80{,}000)}{80{,}000} \\
&= \dfrac{260 + 400}{80{,}000} \\
&= \dfrac{660}{80{,}000} \\
&= \$0.00825 \;\; \text{per km}
\end{aligned}$$

For the **Electric Car:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{km, EV} &= \dfrac{1{,}750 + (0.012)(50{,}000)}{50{,}000} \\
&= \dfrac{1{,}750 + 600}{50{,}000} \\
&= \dfrac{2{,}350}{50{,}000} \\
&= \$0.047 \;\; \text{per km}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 4: Comparative analysis

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost Ratio} &= \dfrac{C_{km, EV}}{C_{km, ICE}} \\
&= \dfrac{0.047}{0.00825} \\
&\approx 5.7
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** In the 1910s, **operating an EV cost ~5.7√ó more per km** than a gasoline car. Combined with the ~6.7√ó higher capital cost, EVs lost on both upfront and operational metrics. This is the **quantitative reason** for the EV dark age of 1920‚Äì1970.

### Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Era-by-Era Comparison

| Era | Dominant Technology | Key Innovation | Driving Force | Limiting Factor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1832‚Äì1880** | Early EV experiments | Rechargeable lead-acid (Plant√©, 1859) | Scientific curiosity | Battery weight |
| **1890‚Äì1920** | EVs + Steam + ICE | Lohner-Porsche Mixte (1901) | City cleanliness, no cranking | High cost, low range |
| **1920‚Äì1965** | ICE dominance | Electric starter (1912) | Cheap oil, mass production | EV range, charging infra |
| **1970‚Äì1990** | R&D revival | BETC, EV1 concept | 1973 oil crisis, CARB mandate | Battery energy density |
| **1997‚Äì2010** | Hybrid mainstreaming | Toyota Prius (1997) | Environmental awareness | Public acceptance |
| **2008‚Äì2020** | Modern BEVs | Tesla Roadster, Li-ion | Climate policy, performance | Charging infrastructure |
| **2020‚ÄìPresent** | BEV/HEV proliferation | Solid-state R&D, V2G | Net-zero commitments, BS-VI | Raw material supply chains |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** Examiners prefer **tabulated answers** in this section. Always present a matrix when asked "Compare the development phases of EVs" ‚Äî full marks require both technical and socio-economic factors.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023]**
**List the major limitations of early electric vehicles that led to their decline in the 1920s.**

**Model Answer (3 marks ‚Äî distribute as 1.5 + 1 + 0.5):**

1. **Poor Energy Density (1.5 marks):** Lead-acid batteries of the era offered only ~30-40 Wh/kg versus gasoline's ~12,000 Wh/kg (energy equivalent). This severely restricted range to 50-80 km per charge.
2. **High Capital Cost (1 mark):** EVs cost ~$1,750 while Ford's mass-produced Model T cost $260 ‚Äî a 6.7√ó premium made them unviable for the common consumer.
3. **Lack of Charging Infrastructure (0.5 mark):** Rural electrification had not reached most of the US/Europe; EVs were confined to cities with private stables (used as garages).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often answer only "battery problems." KTU expects **economic + infrastructural + technological** dimensions. Writing only one reason gets a maximum of **1 mark**.

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024]**
**What was the significance of the 1973 OPEC oil crisis in the resurgence of electric vehicles?**

**Model Answer (3 marks ‚Äî distribute as 1 + 1 + 1):**

1. **Triggered Policy Response (1 mark):** The US Congress passed the Electric and Hybrid Vehicle Research, Development, and Demonstration Act (1976), directly funding EV R&D ‚Äî the first major government push since the 1900s.
2. **Reframed the Energy Narrative (1 mark):** It exposed the **vulnerability of oil-dependent transport** and pushed nations to seek diversification of energy sources, including electricity.
3. **Catalyzed CARB Mandate (1 mark):** The crisis-era environmental awareness directly led to the **1990 California ZEV Mandate** ‚Äî the world's first regulatory force for EV commercialization, paving the way for the GM EV1 and Toyota Prius.

---

### Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ‚Äî [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**Trace the historical evolution of Hybrid Electric Vehicles from the early 1900s to the modern era, with special emphasis on the technological milestones and the socio-economic factors that influenced their development.**

**Sub-part (a) ‚Äî 7 Marks**
**Discuss the early hybrid/electric era (1832‚Äì1920) and identify three key technological milestones.**

**Model Answer (7 marks ‚Äî distribute as 2 + 2 + 2 + 1):**

1. **The Anderson Carriage and Plant√© Battery (2 marks):** In 1832, Robert Anderson built the first crude electric carriage. In 1859, Gaston Plant√© invented the **rechargeable lead-acid battery**, providing the foundational energy storage that made EVs practical. The 1881 Trouv√© tricycle proved EVs were roadworthy, attracting public attention in Paris.

2. **The Lohner-Porsche Mixte Hybrid (2 marks):** In 1901, Ferdinand Porsche designed the **Lohner-Porsche Mixte** ‚Äî a series hybrid where a gasoline engine drove a generator that powered in-wheel electric motors. This was the **world's first functional hybrid car**, predating the Toyota Prius by 96 years. It demonstrated that combustion + electric cooperation was viable.

3. **Performance Records and Commercial Adoption (2 marks):** In 1899, *La Jamais Contente* became the first car to exceed 100 km/h, using electric propulsion. By 1900, ~38% of US cars were electric, used heavily as taxis (e.g., NYC fleet of 12 cabs) and delivery vehicles. This proves that EVs were not "marginal" in the early 20th century.

4. **Limitation that Ended the Era (1 mark):** The 1908 Ford Model T ($260) and 1912 electric starter (Kettering) **erased the ICE disadvantages**, while EVs remained expensive. Combined with cheap Texas oil, this led to ICE dominance by 1920.

**Sub-part (b) ‚Äî 7 Marks**
**Explain the modern resurgence of hybrid/electric vehicles (1970s onwards), highlighting key milestones in policy, technology, and commercialization.**

**Model Answer (7 marks ‚Äî distribute as 2 + 2 + 2 + 1):**

1. **Policy-Driven R&D (2 marks):** The 1973 OPEC oil crisis led to the **US 1976 EV Act** and the 1990 **CARB ZEV Mandate** in California. These forced automakers to develop EVs (e.g., GM EV1 in 1996) and hybrids, providing regulatory pressure that technology alone could not create.

2. **Technological Breakthroughs (2 marks):** The transition from lead-acid (~40 Wh/kg) to **NiMH (1990s)** to **Lithium-ion (post-2008)** batteries increased energy density by **6√ó**, making modern BEVs viable. The 1997 Toyota Prius used NiMH, while the 2008 Tesla Roadster demonstrated Li-ion's potential for 394 km range.

3. **Commercialization and Ecosystem (2 marks):** The **1997 Toyota Prius** became the first mass-produced hybrid, with cumulative global sales of over **5 million units** by 2023 (all generations). The **2008 Tesla Roadster** shattered the "EV = golf cart" perception with sports-car performance. India's BS-VI norms (2020) and FAME-II subsidies (2019) closed the global loop.

4. **Future Outlook (1 mark):** The current decade focuses on **solid-state batteries, V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid), and hydrogen FCEVs**, supported by Net-Zero 2050 commitments of over 140 countries.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Part B Pitfalls:**
> - **Do not** write a one-sided answer. Part (a) **must** cover pre-1920 history; Part (b) **must** cover 1970+. Mixing them loses coherence marks.
> - **Do not** omit the **Lohner-Porsche Mixte (1901)** ‚Äî it is a KTU 2024 scheme favourite and worth 2 marks alone.
> - **Do not** present only technology. Always link to **policy + socio-economics** ‚Äî full marks require all three dimensions.
> - **Diagrams in Part B:** A simple timeline earns **+1 to +2 marks** beyond content quality. Always include one.

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2023]**

**Critically analyze the role of oil crises, environmental policy, and battery technology breakthroughs in shaping the modern hybrid and electric vehicle industry.**

**Sub-part (a) ‚Äî 7 Marks**
**Discuss how the 1973 and 1979 oil crises served as catalysts for the global EV/HEV movement.**

**Model Answer (7 marks ‚Äî distribute as 2 + 2 + 1.5 + 1.5):**

1. **Economic Shock and Awareness (2 marks):** Crude oil prices rose from $3 to $12/barrel (1973) and to $40/barrel (1979). This exposed the **energy security vulnerability** of oil-dependent transport and made EVs economically attractive due to cheaper electricity.

2. **Policy & Funding (2 marks):** The US 1976 EV Act, UK Energy Act, and similar Japanese/European policies funded **BETC, NASA's hybrid research**, and academic-industry collaborations that developed the foundations of modern power electronics and motor control.

3. **First-Generation Hybrids (1.5 marks):** Companies like **VW (1973 Elektrotransporter), Briggs & Stratton, and General Electric** built experimental hybrids. Lessons learned from this era informed the 1997 Prius architecture.

4. **Public Consciousness (1.5 marks):** The 1979 crisis popularized terms like *"energy independence"* and *"renewable mobility"* ‚Äî values that later drove the **2008 Tesla founding** and India's 2013 National Electric Mobility Mission Plan (NEMMP).

**Sub-part (b) ‚Äî 7 Marks**
**Evaluate the impact of battery technology evolution (lead-acid ‚Üí NiMH ‚Üí Li-ion ‚Üí solid-state) on the commercialization of EVs and HEVs.**

**Model Answer (7 marks ‚Äî distribute as 1.5 + 1.5 + 2 + 2):**

1. **Lead-Acid Era (Pre-1990):** Specific energy ~30-40 Wh/kg, range 50-80 km, heavy and slow-charging. Limited EVs to niche city use.

2. **NiMH Era (1990‚Äì2010):** Specific energy ~60-80 Wh/kg. Enabled the **1997 Toyota Prius** (1st Gen) and GM EV1. The NiMH Prius set the hybrid benchmark for the next decade.

3. **Lithium-Ion Revolution (2008‚ÄìPresent):** Specific energy ~150-250 Wh/kg. **Tesla Roadster (2008)** achieved 394 km range; **Nissan Leaf (2010)** achieved 117 miles EPA. Cost dropped from ~$1,200/kWh (2010) to ~$130/kWh (2023), making BEVs cost-competitive with ICE.

4. **Solid-State Future (2025+):** Promised specific energy ~400-500 Wh/kg, faster charging, and higher safety. Toyota, QuantumScape, and Samsung SDI lead R&D. Will likely define the **next KTU-history-cycle** of EVs.

> [!WARNING]
> **Alternative Choice Strategy for KTU Students:**
> - **Question A** is more **chronological/narrative** ‚Äî choose this if you have a strong memory of dates and milestones.
> - **Question B** is more **analytical/critical** ‚Äî choose this if you prefer reasoning over recall. Both carry equal 14 marks.

---

- [x] **India:** NEMMP 2013, FAME-I 2015, FAME-II 2019, BS-VI 2020
- [x] **Energy Density:** Lead-acid (~40 Wh/kg) ‚Üí NiMH (~80 Wh/kg) ‚Üí Li-ion (~250 Wh/kg) ‚Üí Solid-state (~400 Wh/kg)
- [x] **Cycle Pattern:** Rise (1832-1920) ‚Üí Decline (1920-1965) ‚Üí Revival (1973-1997) ‚Üí Mainstream (1997-Present) ‚Üí Future (2025+)

> **Final Tip:** Always mention **technological, economic, and policy** drivers together in any KTU answer on this topic. Examiners reward **tri-dimensional reasoning** with full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Social and Environmental Importance of Hybrid and Electric Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Social and Environmental Importance of Hybrid and Electric Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Hybrid and Electric Vehicles (HEVs/EVs)** are automotive propulsion systems designed to reduce dependence on petroleum-based internal combustion engines (ICE) by integrating electrified powertrains, energy storage systems, and regenerative energy recovery mechanisms. Their **Social and Environmental Importance** stems from their capacity to mitigate four critical crises linked to conventional transportation:

1. **Greenhouse Gas (GHG) Emissions** ‚Äî particularly Carbon Dioxide (CO‚ÇÇ), Methane (CH‚ÇÑ), and Nitrous Oxide (N‚ÇÇO).
2. **Criteria Air Pollutants** ‚Äî Carbon Monoxide (CO), unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC), Nitrogen Oxides (NO‚Çì), and Particulate Matter (PM2.5/PM10).
3. **Energy Security & Fossil Fuel Depletion** ‚Äî strategic dependence on imported crude oil.
4. **Urban Noise Pollution and Public Health Burden.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Focus:** This topic forms the **strategic foundation** for the entire HEV/EV course. Examiners frequently test the *quantitative comparison* between conventional and electrified vehicles using Well-to-Wheel (WTW) and Tank-to-Wheel (TTW) metrics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî "The Centenarian Tea Stall"

Imagine two tea stalls serving the same neighbourhood:

- **The Conventional Coal-Chulha Stall (ICE Vehicle):** Burns dirty coal, billows black smoke into the shared street, wastes 75% of its heat as hot gases up the chimney, and the owner must haul fresh coal sacks every single day. The neighbourhood *breathes* the pollution, but the stall keeps running because the fuel is cheap to buy.
- **The Electric Induction Stall (HEV/EV):** Plugs into the city grid, heats only the water it needs, vents no smoke into the street, and the "fuel" (electricity) is delivered invisibly through wires. Yes, the induction stove costs more upfront ‚Äî but the street stays clean, the neighbourhood stays healthier, and the long-term running cost is dramatically lower.

**Translation:** Conventional vehicles "burn" fuel *where you live*, polluting the immediate atmosphere. HEVs/EVs centralise the pollution at a distant power plant (or, in renewable scenarios, eliminate it entirely), making it governable, filterable, and progressively cleaner as the grid decarbonises.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| CO‚ÇÇ Global Warming Potential (GWP‚ÇÅ‚ÇÄ‚ÇÄ) | **GWP = 1** (reference) | Baseline for GHG accounting |
| CH‚ÇÑ GWP‚ÇÅ‚ÇÄ‚ÇÄ | **GWP = 28‚Äì34** | Natural gas vehicles |
| N‚ÇÇO GWP‚ÇÅ‚ÇÄ‚ÇÄ | **GWP = 265‚Äì298** | Catalytic converter byproduct |
| Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Gasoline) | **AFR = 14.7 : 1** | Combustion calculation baseline |
| Average CO‚ÇÇ per litre of gasoline | **~2.31 kg CO‚ÇÇ/L** | EU Regulation baseline |
| WHO PM2.5 Annual Guideline | **‚â§ 5 ¬µg/m¬≥** | Air quality compliance |
| Global transport CO‚ÇÇ share | **~24%** (IEA 2023) | Sectoral emission data |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 14.7:1 matters:** This is the chemically perfect mixture of air and gasoline that ensures *complete combustion*. Real engines run richer (lower AFR) under load, producing CO and unburnt HC ‚Äî a direct source of the pollution HEVs/EVs are designed to eliminate at the tailpipe.

---

## 1.4 The Four-Pillar Importance Framework

```
SOCIAL + ENVIRONMENTAL IMPORTANCE OF HEV/EV
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ 1. CLIMATE CHANGE MITIGATION
‚îÇ      ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ Reduce CO‚ÇÇ, CH‚ÇÑ, N‚ÇÇO ‚Üí Paris Agreement 1.5¬∞C target
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ 2. URBAN AIR QUALITY
‚îÇ      ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ Eliminate tailpipe NO‚Çì, PM, CO, HC ‚Üí Public health
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ 3. ENERGY SECURITY
‚îÇ      ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ Decouple transport from crude oil imports
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ 4. NOISE & QUALITY OF LIFE
       ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ Reduce 70‚Äì80 dB ICE noise ‚Üí 50‚Äì60 dB EV operation
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Well-to-Wheel (WTW) vs Tank-to-Wheel (TTW) Analysis

The **WTW analysis** is the gold-standard method for comparing the *true* environmental footprint of any powertrain. It is divided into two sequential stages:

### Stage A ‚Äî Well-to-Tank (WTT) / "Upstream"
This covers everything **before** the fuel enters the vehicle:
- Crude oil extraction
- Refining / electricity generation
- Transportation & distribution

### Stage B ‚Äî Tank-to-Wheel (TTW) / "Downstream"
This covers the energy conversion **inside** the vehicle itself:
- Combustion in ICE, or
- Electrochemical discharge in battery, or
- Hybrid combination of both

The product of both stage efficiencies gives the **overall WTW efficiency**.

$$\eta_{WTW} \;=\; \eta_{WTT} \;\times\; \eta_{TTW}$$

## 2.2 Efficiency Chain of Conventional vs Electric Powertrains

| Stage | Gasoline ICE | Battery EV (Grid Mix Avg.) | HEV (Series-Parallel) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Extraction / Generation | 85% | **35‚Äì55%** (thermal plants) | 85% |
| Refining / Distribution | 90% | 95% (grid T&D losses) | 90% |
| Storage / Charging | 98% | 90% (charger + battery) | 95% |
| **WTT Sub-total** | **~75%** | **~30‚Äì47%** | **~73%** |
| Engine / Motor Efficiency | 25‚Äì35% | **85‚Äì92%** (electric motor) | 30‚Äì40% (combined) |
| Transmission | 90% | 95% | 90% |
| **TTW Sub-total** | **~25%** | **~85%** | **~30%** |
| **Overall Œ∑_WTW** | **~18‚Äì20%** | **~25‚Äì40%** | **~22‚Äì28%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Even with India's current coal-heavy grid (~73% fossil mix), a Battery EV delivers a *higher* WTW efficiency than a gasoline ICE ‚Äî because the electric motor's 90% efficiency overwhelms the grid's inefficiency. As India's grid adds more renewables (target: 500 GW non-fossil by 2030), the EV advantage compounds further.

## 2.3 Combustion Stoichiometry ‚Äî The Source of Tailpipe CO‚ÇÇ

The complete combustion of gasoline (approximated as iso-octane, C‚ÇàH‚ÇÅ‚Çà) is:

$$\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} \;+\; 12.5\,\text{O}_2 \;+\; 12.5 \times 3.76\,\text{N}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; 8\,\text{CO}_2 \;+\; 9\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \;+\; 47\,\text{N}_2$$

Because air contains **79% N‚ÇÇ and 21% O‚ÇÇ**, the molar ratio of N‚ÇÇ to O‚ÇÇ is 3.76:1. This reaction is the *root chemical source* of transport CO‚ÇÇ emissions.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet ‚Äî High-Yield Equations

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{WTW} = \eta_{WTT} \cdot \eta_{TTW}$ | Well-to-Wheel efficiency | dimensionless |
| 2 | $m_{CO_2} = m_{fuel} \times \frac{n_C \times 44}{M_{fuel}}$ | Mass of CO‚ÇÇ per kg fuel (n_C = carbon atoms) | kg CO‚ÇÇ/kg fuel |
| 3 | $\%\,\text{Reduction} = \frac{E_{ICE} - E_{EV}}{E_{ICE}} \times 100$ | % emission reduction vs ICE baseline | % |
| 4 | $\text{LCA}\,CO_2 = E_{manuf} + E_{use} + E_{EOL}$ | Lifecycle CO‚ÇÇ total (incl. battery) | kg CO‚ÇÇ-eq |
| 5 | $\text{BSFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{P_{brake}}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | g/kWh |
| 6 | $\text{Payback} = \frac{\Delta C_{EV}}{S_{fuel} + S_{maint}}$ | EV incremental cost recovery period | years |
| 7 | $\text{PM}_{emitted} = k \times \dot{m}_{fuel} \times \phi$ | Particulate matter emission (œÜ = load factor) | g/km |
| 8 | $E_{kin} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Regenerative braking energy recovered | Joules |
| 9 | $\text{WHTC}_{CO_2} = \sum_i (m_i \times d_i)$ | World Harmonised Transient Cycle weighted CO‚ÇÇ | g/km |
| 10 | $NO_x = f(T_{comb}, AFR, t_{res})$ | Thermal NO‚Çì (Zeldovich mechanism) | g/km |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Marks are often lost when students confuse **GWP weighting** (CH‚ÇÑ = 28) with absolute mass. Always convert: 1 kg CH‚ÇÑ = 28 kg CO‚ÇÇ-equivalent. Use `\\vert` or `\\mid` in tables to avoid the markdown pipe issue.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Policy Making:** India's **FAME-II** scheme (Faster Adoption and Manufacturing of Electric Vehicles) uses *TTW CO‚ÇÇ* as the eligibility threshold for subsidies.
- **OEM Marketing:** Tesla, Tata Motors, and BYD publish *WTW CO‚ÇÇ g/km* to substantiate "zero-emission" claims ‚Äî which are only accurate at the *tailpipe*, not across the full lifecycle.
- **Urban Planning:** WHO 2022 data attributes **~7 million premature deaths/year** to air pollution (PM2.5 + NO‚ÇÇ), of which transport contributes ~20‚Äì30% in cities.
- **Battery LCA Hotspot:** Manufacturing a 60 kWh Li-ion battery pack emits ~**4‚Äì6 tonnes CO‚ÇÇ-eq**. This is *recovered* over ~15,000‚Äì25,000 km of EV operation vs an equivalent ICE vehicle.

---

## 2.6 Comparative Emissions Table (Real-World India / Bharat Stage VI)

| Pollutant | Petrol ICE (g/km) | Diesel ICE (g/km) | HEV (g/km) | BEV (g/km) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CO‚ÇÇ | 120‚Äì180 | 110‚Äì160 | 70‚Äì100 | **0 (tailpipe)** |
| CO | 0.5‚Äì1.0 | 0.3‚Äì0.6 | 0.1‚Äì0.3 | 0 |
| NO‚Çì | 0.05‚Äì0.10 | 0.30‚Äì0.80 | 0.02‚Äì0.05 | 0 |
| PM2.5 | 0.005 | 0.025 | 0.003 | 0 |
| HC | 0.05‚Äì0.10 | 0.02‚Äì0.05 | 0.01‚Äì0.03 | 0 |
| Noise (dB) @ 50 km/h | 68‚Äì74 | 70‚Äì76 | 60‚Äì66 | **52‚Äì58** |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚Äî Tailpipe CO‚ÇÇ Mass from Gasoline Combustion

**Given:** 1 kg of iso-octane (C‚ÇàH‚ÇÅ‚Çà, M = 114 g/mol) undergoes stoichiometric combustion.

### Step 1 ‚Äî Balance the combustion equation
From the reaction: C‚ÇàH‚ÇÅ‚Çà + 12.5 O‚ÇÇ ‚Üí 8 CO‚ÇÇ + 9 H‚ÇÇO

### Step 2 ‚Äî Count carbon atoms
- 1 mole of C‚ÇàH‚ÇÅ‚Çà contains **8 carbon atoms**.
- These 8 carbons each form 1 molecule of CO‚ÇÇ.
- Therefore, **1 mole of fuel produces 8 moles of CO‚ÇÇ**.

### Step 3 ‚Äî Convert to mass
- Mass of 8 moles CO‚ÇÇ: $8 \times 44 \text{ g/mol} = 352 \text{ g CO}_2$
- Mass of 1 mole fuel: $114 \text{ g}$

### Step 4 ‚Äî Calculate mass ratio
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass ratio} &= \frac{m_{CO_2}}{m_{fuel}} \\
&= \frac{8 \times 44}{1 \times 114} \\
&= \frac{352}{114} \\
&= 3.087 \; \text{kg CO}_2 \text{ per kg fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 ‚Äî Generalised formula (any hydrocarbon C‚ÇìH·µß)

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{CO_2} \;&=\; m_{fuel} \times \frac{n_C \times 44}{M_{fuel}} \\
\text{where:} \;& n_C = \text{number of carbon atoms per molecule} \\
& M_{fuel} = \text{molar mass of fuel in g/mol} \\
& 44 = \text{molar mass of CO}_2 \text{ in g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 ‚Äî Apply to diesel (C‚ÇÅ‚ÇÇH‚ÇÇ‚ÇÉ, M = 170 g/mol)
$$
m_{CO_2} \;=\; 1 \;\text{kg} \times \frac{12 \times 44}{170} \;=\; \frac{528}{170} \;=\; 3.106 \;\text{kg CO}_2/\text{kg diesel}
$$

**Observation:** Diesel is *slightly* more carbon-dense per kg than gasoline. However, diesel engines are 25‚Äì30% more thermally efficient, so per kilometre the emissions are comparable.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚Äî Percentage Emission Reduction (ICE ‚Üí BEV)

**Given:** A mid-size sedan consumes **6 L/100 km** of gasoline. India grid emission factor = **0.72 kg CO‚ÇÇ/kWh**. EV efficiency = **15 kWh/100 km**.

### Step 1 ‚Äî Tailpipe CO‚ÇÇ from ICE (BS-VI)
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{ICE} &= \text{fuel consumption} \times \text{CO}_2 \text{ per litre} \\
&= 6 \; \text{L/100 km} \times 2.31 \; \text{kg CO}_2/\text{L} \\
&= 13.86 \; \text{kg CO}_2/\text{100 km} \\
&= 138.6 \; \text{g CO}_2/\text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Well-to-Wheel CO‚ÇÇ from BEV
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{BEV} &= \text{electricity} \times \text{grid factor} \\
&= 15 \; \text{kWh/100 km} \times 0.72 \; \text{kg CO}_2/\text{kWh} \\
&= 10.8 \; \text{kg CO}_2/\text{100 km} \\
&= 108 \; \text{g CO}_2/\text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Percentage reduction
$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,\text{Reduction} &= \frac{E_{ICE} - E_{BEV}}{E_{ICE}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{138.6 - 108}{138.6} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{30.6}{138.6} \times 100 \\
&= 22.07\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Even with India's current coal-dominant grid, switching from a 6 L/100 km ICE to a 15 kWh/100 km BEV reduces WTW CO‚ÇÇ emissions by ~22%. With a renewable-heavy grid (factor drops to ~0.05 kg CO‚ÇÇ/kWh), the reduction exceeds **90%**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚Äî EV Payback Period (Economic-Environmental Crossover)

**Given:**
- EV incremental cost over ICE: **‚Çπ4,00,000**
- Annual fuel saving: **‚Çπ60,000** (diesel ‚Üí electricity)
- Annual maintenance saving: **‚Çπ15,000** (fewer moving parts, no oil change)
- Battery replacement avoided: 0 (within 8-year warranty)

### Step 1 ‚Äî Total annual saving
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{total} &= S_{fuel} + S_{maint} \\
&= 60{,}000 + 15{,}000 \\
&= 75{,}000 \text{ ‚Çπ/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Payback period
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{payback} &= \frac{\Delta C_{EV}}{S_{total}} \\
&= \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{75{,}000} \\
&= 5.33 \; \text{years}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Environmental breakeven (battery LCA offset)

Assuming battery manufacturing emits 5 tonnes CO‚ÇÇ-eq, and the EV saves 0.306 kg CO‚ÇÇ/km over ICE:
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{offset} &= \frac{5{,}000 \; \text{kg CO}_2\text{-eq}}{0.306 \; \text{kg CO}_2/\text{km}} \\
&\approx 16{,}340 \; \text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

For an average Indian car running **12,000 km/year**, the **environmental breakeven** is reached in **~1.4 years**, while the **economic breakeven** is **~5.3 years**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Combined Social-Environmental Impact Index

| Dimension | ICE (Score / 10) | HEV (Score / 10) | BEV (Score / 10) | KTU 2024 Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tailpipe CO‚ÇÇ | 2 | 6 | **10** | 25% |
| Urban Air Quality (NO‚Çì, PM) | 2 | 6 | **10** | 20% |
| Noise Pollution | 3 | 6 | **9** | 10% |
| Energy Security (Import ‚Üì) | 2 | 5 | **9** | 20% |
| Lifecycle GHG (LCA) | 4 | 6 | **7** | 15% |
| Affordability & Equity | **8** | 5 | 3 | 10% |
| **Weighted Total (/10)** | **3.10** | **5.65** | **8.50** | 100% |

**Interpretation:** The BEV scores 2.7√ó higher on a holistic social-environmental index than the ICE, validating the global policy push toward electrification.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Well-to-Wheel (WTW) Comparative Flow Diagram

```mermaid

    A[CRUDE OIL SOURCE] --> B[REFINERY]
    B --> C[GASOLINE / DIESEL TANK]
    C --> D[ICE ENGINE]
    D --> E[WHEELS]
    D -. CO2 NOX PM NOISE .-> ENV1[URBAN ATMOSPHERE]

    F[POWER PLANT COAL SOLAR WIND] --> G[ELECTRICAL GRID]
    G --> H[CHARGING STATION]
    H --> I[EV BATTERY PACK]
    I --> J[ELECTRIC MOTOR]
    J --> K[WHEELS]

    J -. NEAR SILENT .-> ENV2[URBAN ATMOSPHERE CLEAN]

    style J fill:#43a047,color:#ffffff

```

**Reading Guide:** Top branch (brown/red) = conventional ICE pathway producing tailpipe pollutants. Bottom branch (blue/green) = electric pathway with **centralised, governable** emissions and zero local pollution.

---

## 4.2 Lifecycle Assessment (LCA) of an EV ‚Äî Block Architecture

```mermaid

    subgraph PHASE_A[PHASE A RAW MATERIAL EXTRACTION]
        A1[Litium Cobalt Nickel Mining] --> A2[Refining and Chemical Processing]
    end

    subgraph PHASE_B[PHASE B MANUFACTURING]
        B1[Battery Cell Production 4 to 6 tonnes CO2]
        B2[Electric Motor and Power Electronics]

    end

    subgraph PHASE_C[PHASE C USE PHASE 15 to 20 YEARS]
        C1[Grid Charging with Declining CO2 Factor]
        C2[Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery]
        C3[Maintenance and Tyre Replacement]
    end

    subgraph PHASE_D[PHASE D END OF LIFE]
        D1[Second Life Battery Storage]
        D2[Material Recycling 90 percent target]
        D3[Safe Disposal of Electronics]
    end

    A2 --> B1

    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1

    C3 --> D2
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3

    style PHASE_A fill:#fff3e0,color:#000000
    style PHASE_B fill:#ffebee,color:#000000
    style PHASE_C fill:#e8f5e9,color:#000000
    style PHASE_D fill:#e3f2fd,color:#000000

```

---

## 4.3 The Four-Pillar Decision Tree ‚Äî Why HEV or EV?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Transportation Sustainability Goal] --> Q1{Is Daily Driving Less Than 80 km}
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2{Is Home Charging Available}

    Q2 -- YES --> BEV[CHOOSE BATTERY EV BEV]
    Q2 -- NO --> HEV
    BEV --> R1[ZERO TAILPIPE EMISSIONS]
    HEV --> R2[40 TO 60 PERCENT EMISSION REDUCTION]
```

---

## 4.4 Emissions Reduction Hierarchy (Schematic Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CONVENTIONAL ICE 100 percent BASELINE] --> B[MILD HYBRID 48V 15 to 20 percent REDUCTION]
    B --> C[FULL HYBRID HEV 30 to 40 percent REDUCTION]

    D --> E[BEV RENEWABLE GRID 90 to 100 percent REDUCTION]

    style A fill:#b71c1c,color:#ffffff

```

**Reading Guide:** Each progressive electrification stage delivers a *compounding* emission reduction. The 48V mild-hybrid is the simplest entry point, while the BEV on renewable grid approaches true zero-emission mobility.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**Q: Define Well-to-Wheel (WTW) efficiency. Why is it a more accurate metric than Tank-to-Wheel (TTW) for environmental comparison of vehicles?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Well-to-Wheel (WTW) efficiency is the product of Well-to-Tank (WTT) efficiency and Tank-to-Wheel (TTW) efficiency, representing the *total* energy delivered to the wheels per unit of primary energy extracted from the source. (1 mark)

$$
\eta_{WTW} = \eta_{WTT} \times \eta_{TTW}
$$

TTW only measures the vehicle's internal efficiency, ignoring the energy cost of fuel extraction, refining, and distribution. (1 mark) WTW captures the *complete* upstream + downstream footprint, making it the only scientifically valid basis for comparing an ICE (whose fuel has been refined and transported) with a BEV (whose electricity has been generated and transmitted). (1 mark)

---

### Question A2
**Q: List four tailpipe pollutants emitted by a BS-VI gasoline ICE vehicle that are eliminated in a Battery EV. State one health impact for each.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **CO (Carbon Monoxide)** ‚Äî binds to haemoglobin, reduces blood oxygen-carrying capacity ‚Üí causes hypoxia, headaches, and in extreme cases, death. (0.75)
2. **NO‚Çì (Nitrogen Oxides)** ‚Äî irritates respiratory tract, forms ground-level ozone and smog ‚Üí causes asthma and bronchitis. (0.75)
3. **PM2.5 (Particulate Matter < 2.5 ¬µm)** ‚Äî penetrates deep into alveoli ‚Üí causes cardiovascular disease, lung cancer, and premature death. (0.75)
4. **Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC)** ‚Äî some are carcinogenic (e.g., benzene) and contribute to photochemical smog. (0.75)

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) ‚Äî Mandatory Choice Option

**Q: With reference to the social and environmental importance of Hybrid and Electric Vehicles, answer the following:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) ‚Äî 7 Marks
**Explain the Well-to-Wheel (WTW) and Tank-to-Wheel (TTW) concepts. Calculate the WTW efficiency of a Battery Electric Vehicle whose grid electricity has a generation-plus-transmission efficiency of 38% and an electric drivetrain efficiency of 88%. Compare it with a gasoline ICE vehicle having refining+distribution efficiency of 90% and engine+drivetrain efficiency of 28%.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Definitions (2 marks)**
- **WTT (Well-to-Tank):** All energy losses *before* fuel reaches the vehicle ‚Äî extraction, refining, transport, distribution.
- **TTW (Tank-to-Wheel):** All energy losses *inside* the vehicle ‚Äî engine/motor, transmission, auxiliary loads.
- **WTW:** The product $\eta_{WTT} \times \eta_{TTW}$, representing cradle-to-grave energy delivery to the wheels.

**Step 2 ‚Äî BEV WTW Calculation (2.5 marks)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{WTT,BEV} &= 0.38 \\
\eta_{TTW,BEV} &= 0.88 \\
\eta_{WTW,BEV} &= 0.38 \times 0.88 = 0.3344 = 33.44\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî ICE WTW Calculation (1.5 marks)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{WTT,ICE} &= 0.90 \\
\eta_{TTW,ICE} &= 0.28 \\
\eta_{WTW,ICE} &= 0.90 \times 0.28 = 0.252 = 25.2\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Comparison & Interpretation (1 mark)**
The BEV has a **33.44% WTW efficiency vs 25.2% for the ICE**, an improvement of **8.24 percentage points** or ~32.7% relative gain. This demonstrates that even with India's coal-heavy grid, the BEV's superior electric drivetrain more than compensates for upstream losses. As the grid decarbonises, the BEV's advantage grows asymptotically toward 88%.

---

#### Part (b) ‚Äî 7 Marks
**A household runs a Tata Nexon EV (battery 30.2 kWh, real-world consumption 14 kWh/100 km) and previously ran a Hyundai Creta petrol (mileage 12 km/L, BS-VI CO‚ÇÇ emission = 145 g/km). The Indian grid emission factor is 0.72 kg CO‚ÇÇ/kWh. Calculate: (i) the CO‚ÇÇ emitted per km by the EV (WTW), and (ii) the annual emission savings if the car runs 15,000 km/year. Comment on the social significance.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚Äî EV WTW CO‚ÇÇ per km (2 marks)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EV CO}_2 \text{ per 100 km} &= 14 \;\text{kWh} \times 0.72 \;\text{kg CO}_2/\text{kWh} \\
&= 10.08 \;\text{kg CO}_2/\text{100 km} \\
&= 100.8 \;\text{g CO}_2/\text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî ICE CO‚ÇÇ per km (1 mark)**
$$
E_{ICE} = 145 \;\text{g CO}_2/\text{km} \quad \text{(given)}
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Saving per km (1 mark)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E &= E_{ICE} - E_{BEV} \\
&= 145 - 100.8 \\
&= 44.2 \;\text{g CO}_2/\text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Annual savings (1.5 marks)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Annual saving} &= 44.2 \;\text{g/km} \times 15{,}000 \;\text{km} \\
&= 663{,}000 \;\text{g} \\
&= 663 \;\text{kg CO}_2 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Social significance comment (1.5 marks)**
Saving **663 kg CO‚ÇÇ/year per vehicle** is equivalent to the carbon sequestered by **~11 mature trees annually**. If India's target of **30% EV penetration by 2030** is achieved (~10 crore vehicles), the cumulative saving is ~**66 million tonnes CO‚ÇÇ/year** ‚Äî comparable to shutting down **15 coal-fired power plants of 500 MW each**. This translates directly to reduced urban PM2.5 exposure, lower respiratory disease burden (saving ~‚Çπ50,000 crore in healthcare costs annually), and progress toward India's **Panchamrit** climate commitments announced at COP-26 (reducing emission intensity by 45% by 2030, net-zero by 2070).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî Alternative Choice Option

**Q: Discuss the role of Hybrid and Electric Vehicles in mitigating urban air pollution and enhancing energy security. Support your answer with quantitative reasoning and reference to Indian policy frameworks.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) ‚Äî 7 Marks
**Quantify the impact of replacing 1 lakh petrol cars (each emitting 1.5 g/km NO‚Çì and 0.005 g/km PM2.5) with EVs in a city where average daily driving is 40 km. State the corresponding health impact assuming 1 ¬µg/m¬≥ PM2.5 increase correlates with 6% rise in respiratory mortality (WHO).**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Annual NO‚Çì avoided (1.5 marks)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Annual km per car} &= 40 \;\text{km/day} \times 365 = 14{,}600 \;\text{km/year} \\
\text{Annual NO}_x \text{ per car} &= 1.5 \;\text{g/km} \times 14{,}600 \;\text{km} \\
&= 21{,}900 \;\text{g} = 21.9 \;\text{kg/year/car} \\
\text{Fleet total} &= 21.9 \;\text{kg} \times 1{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}190 \;\text{tonnes NO}_x/\text{year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Annual PM2.5 avoided (1.5 marks)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Annual PM2.5 per car} &= 0.005 \;\text{g/km} \times 14{,}600 \;\text{km} \\
&= 73 \;\text{g/year/car} \\
\text{Fleet total} &= 73 \;\text{g} \times 1{,}00{,}000 = 7{,}300 \;\text{kg} = 7.3 \;\text{tonnes PM2.5/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Citywide ambient concentration reduction (1.5 marks)**
Assuming the 1 lakh cars are distributed over an urban airshed of **100 km √ó 100 km √ó 1 km mixing height = 10¬π‚Å∞ m¬≥**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C_{PM2.5} &= \frac{7.3 \times 10^6 \;\text{g}}{10^{10} \;\text{m}^3 \times 10^6 \;\text{g/m}^3 \text{ conversion}} \\
&\approx 0.73 \;\mu\text{g/m}^3 \text{ reduction}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Mortality impact (1.5 marks)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,\text{mortality benefit} &= 0.73 \times 6\% = 4.38\% \\
\text{If baseline mortality in exposed cohort} &= 10{,}000/\text{year} \\
\text{Lives saved} &\approx 438 \;\text{per year per 10 lakh exposed population}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Interpretation (1 mark)**
The 7.3 tonnes/year PM2.5 reduction across a city prevents an estimated **400+ premature deaths annually** and reduces chronic asthma incidence by 8‚Äì12%, validating the public-health case for EV adoption.

---

#### Part (b) ‚Äî 7 Marks
**Explain India's energy-security rationale for EVs. If India imports 85% of its crude oil (total crude imports ~4.5 million barrels/day in 2023) and 1 kWh of electricity displaces ~0.08 L of petrol, calculate the daily petrol-displacement potential if 10 million EVs each consume 0.4 kWh/day in their share of charging.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Energy security rationale (2 marks)**
India's **~85% crude oil import dependency** exposes the economy to geopolitical price shocks (e.g., the 2022 Russia-Ukraine war pushed Brent crude to $120+/barrel). Transport accounts for ~**70% of India's petroleum demand**. EVs decouple mobility from crude imports, redirecting expenditure to the domestic electricity sector, which is **~70% indigenous** and increasingly renewable. This improves the **Current Account Deficit (CAD)**, stabilises the **rupee**, and aligns with the **Atmanirbhar Bharat** mission.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Total daily electricity consumption of 10 million EVs (1.5 marks)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= 10^7 \times 0.4 \;\text{kWh} \\
&= 4 \times 10^6 \;\text{kWh/day} \\
&= 4 \;\text{GWh/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Petrol displaced (1.5 marks)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{petrol} &= 4 \times 10^6 \;\text{kWh} \times 0.08 \;\text{L/kWh} \\
&= 3.2 \times 10^5 \;\text{L/day} \\
&= 3.2 \;\text{lakh litres/day} \\
&\approx 20{,}160 \;\text{barrels/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Annual forex savings (1 mark)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Annual petrol displaced} &= 3.2 \times 10^5 \times 365 = 1.168 \times 10^8 \;\text{L} \\
\text{At ‚Çπ100/L} &= ‚Çπ1{,}168 \;\text{crore/year in forex savings}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Policy framework (1 mark)**
- **FAME-II** (Faster Adoption and Manufacturing of Electric Vehicles) ‚Äî ‚Çπ10,000+ crore outlay for demand incentives and charging infrastructure.
- **PM E-Drive Scheme** ‚Äî successor to FAME-II with expanded scope.
- **PLI Scheme** for ACC battery storage (‚Çπ18,100 crore) ‚Äî boosts domestic cell manufacturing.
- **EV30@30 Campaign** (NITI Aayog) ‚Äî targets 30% private EV sales by 2030.
- **Panchamrit Commitments (COP-26)** ‚Äî 500 GW non-fossil capacity, net-zero by 2070.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ‚Äî Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing TTW with WTW** ‚Äî Many students claim EVs are "zero-emission" without clarifying this is only at the *tailpipe*. KTU examiners deduct **1‚Äì2 marks** for this imprecision.
> 2. **Ignoring battery LCA** ‚Äî A complete answer must mention the ~5 tonnes CO‚ÇÇ-eq manufacturing burden and the ~15,000‚Äì25,000 km environmental payback.
> 3. **Wrong unit conversion** ‚Äî Mixing g/km with kg/km or forgetting to convert L/year to barrels/day costs easy marks.
> 4. **Forgetting GWP weighting** ‚Äî When asked about non-CO‚ÇÇ GHGs, students must apply the 100-year GWP factors (CH‚ÇÑ = 28, N‚ÇÇO = 265).
> 5. **Skipping policy linkage** ‚Äî Indian-context answers (FAME-II, PLI, NITI Aayog EV30@30) score significantly higher than generic global statements.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### üìå Rapid Revision Checklist

- **WTW = WTT √ó TTW** ‚Äî the foundational equation; memorise it.
- **Gasoline CO‚ÇÇ factor:** 3.087 kg CO‚ÇÇ/kg fuel (from C‚ÇàH‚ÇÅ‚Çà stoichiometry).
- **Diesel CO‚ÇÇ factor:** 3.106 kg CO‚ÇÇ/kg fuel (slightly higher per kg, but engine is more efficient).
- **BS-VI CO‚ÇÇ (typical Indian petrol car):** 120‚Äì180 g/km.
- **BEV efficiency range:** 85‚Äì92% (electric motor) vs 25‚Äì35% (ICE).
- **Four pillars of HEV/EV importance:** Climate, Air Quality, Energy Security, Noise.
- **GWP‚ÇÅ‚ÇÄ‚ÇÄ values:** CO‚ÇÇ = 1, CH‚ÇÑ = 28, N‚ÇÇO = 265 (memorise for any GHG problem).
- **India's grid emission factor:** ~0.72 kg CO‚ÇÇ/kWh (2023 average).
- **Battery LCA burden:** ~4‚Äì6 tonnes CO‚ÇÇ-eq per 60 kWh pack; recovered in 15,000‚Äì25,000 km.
- **FAME-II & PM E-Drive** are India's flagship demand-incentive schemes.
- **Panchamrit targets** (COP-26): 500 GW non-fossil, 45% emission-intensity cut by 2030, net-zero by 2070.
- **WHO data:** ~7 million premature deaths/year attributed to air pollution globally.
- **EV payback components:** fuel saving + maintenance saving + battery longevity + residual value.
- **Noise reduction:** ~15 dB drop (from ~70 dB ICE to ~55 dB EV at 50 km/h).
- **Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX tables** instead of `|` to avoid markdown breakage.
- **Distinguish tailpipe emissions from lifecycle emissions** ‚Äî a recurring KTU 2024 exam differentiator.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle Dynamics and Load Forces: Mathematical Models to Describe Vehicle Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Vehicle Dynamics and Load Forces: Mathematical Models to Describe Vehicle Performance # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Vehicle Dynamics** is the engineering discipline that studies the motion of road vehicles and the forces acting upon them, expressed through mathematical relationships between vehicle parameters (mass, geometry, drivetrain), external loads (gravity, aerodynamics, rolling friction), and the driver's input (throttle, brake, steering). In the context of **Hybrid and Electric Vehicles (HEVs/EVs)**, vehicle dynamics provides the foundational mathematical model that determines the **tractive force**, **power demand**, **energy consumption**, and **performance envelope** (top speed, acceleration, gradeability) of the vehicle. The KTU 2024 syllabus frames this as the *quantitative bridge* between propulsion system sizing and real-world driving capability. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** > Students must be able to derive and apply the **tractive force equation** at the wheel-road interface, identify the **four primary resistive forces**, and compute the **instantaneous power demand** at the wheels for given speed, acceleration, and road grade conditions. This is a **CO1 / CO2 mapped** topic under the *Engineering Knowledge* and *Problem Analysis* Bloom levels (Apply, Analyze). ## 1.2 The Four Primary Vehicle Load Forces A vehicle moving on a road must overcome the following resistive forces simultaneously: | # | Force | Symbol | Origin | Direction | |---|-------|--------|--------|-----------| | 1 | **Rolling Resistance** | $F_{rr}$ | Tire deformation on road | Opposes motion | | 2 | **Aerodynamic Drag** | $F_{ad}$ | Air displacement by vehicle body | Opposes motion | | 3 | **Climbing (Grade) Force** | $F_{hc}$ | Gravitational component along slope | Opposes motion (uphill) | | 4 | **Linear Acceleration Force** | $F_{la}$ | Newton's 2nd Law ($F=ma$) | Opposes motion (when accelerating) | > [!NOTE] > **Definition ‚Äì Tractive Force ($F_t$):** > The net forward force that the propulsion system (engine/motor) must deliver to the driven wheels to sustain motion against all four resistive forces. It is the *minimum* force required at the tire contact patch. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: "The SUV on a Hilly Highway" > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Analogy ‚Äî The Keralite Hill-Climb Drive:** > Imagine you are driving a Maruti Ertiga from **Thiruvananthapuram to Ponmudi**. As you cruise on the flat NH-66 at 80 km/h, your motor is mainly fighting **air drag** (wind pushing against the front) and a little **rolling friction** (tires flexing). As the ghat road begins, gravity now pulls the car backward along the slope ‚Äî this is the **grade force** suddenly becoming dominant. When you press the accelerator to overtake a truck, you must additionally overcome an **inertia force** proportional to the car's mass. The motor's **tractive effort** at the wheel is the *sum* of all these four forces. Designing a hybrid or electric powertrain means sizing the motor/battery so this tractive effort can be delivered at every point of this drive cycle ‚Äî that is precisely what the **mathematical model** quantifies. ## 1.4 The Coordinate System Convention For all derivations in this topic, the KTU convention uses a **road-aligned coordinate system** with the *x-axis* along the vehicle's longitudinal direction (positive forward) and the *y-axis* perpendicular. The road grade angle $\alpha$ is measured from the horizontal. A positive grade means an **uphill climb**. ## 1.5 Standard Vehicle Parameters Used in Modeling The mathematical model uses the following lumped parameters: - $M$ ‚Äî Vehicle total mass (kg) [kerb mass + payload] - $g$ ‚Äî Gravitational acceleration = **9.81 m/s¬≤** - $\rho_a$ ‚Äî Air density = **1.202 kg/m¬≥** (at 20¬∞C, sea level) - $C_d$ ‚Äî Coefficient of aerodynamic drag (dimensionless) ‚Äî typically **0.25‚Äì0.40** for modern cars - $A_f$ ‚Äî Frontal area (m¬≤) - $f_r$ ‚Äî Coefficient of rolling resistance (dimensionless) ‚Äî typically **0.008‚Äì0.015** for radial tires - $\eta_{dr}$ ‚Äî Drivetrain efficiency (dimensionless) ‚Äî typically **0.85‚Äì0.95** - $r_w$ ‚Äî Effective wheel radius (m) - $v$ ‚Äî Vehicle speed (m/s) - $\alpha$ ‚Äî Road grade angle (rad or degrees) - $\delta$ ‚Äî Mass factor (rotational inertia coefficient) ‚âà **1.05‚Äì1.10** for vehicles # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Resistive Forces ‚Äî Physics & Engineering Logic ### 2.1.1 Rolling Resistance Force ($F_{rr}$) **Physical Origin:** When a tire rolls on a flat surface, the contact patch deforms. The deformation is *asymmetric* ‚Äî more energy is absorbed in the front half of the contact patch (compression) than is released in the rear half (rebound). This hysteresis loss manifests as a net resistive force. **Why it matters:** At low speeds (below ~60 km/h), $F_{rr}$ is often the dominant resistive force. In EVs, low-rolling-resistance tires are critical to extend range. $$F_{rr} = f_r \cdot M \cdot g \cdot \cos\alpha$$ **Engineering Insight:** The coefficient $f_r$ depends on tire pressure, material, speed, and temperature. Higher tire pressure ‚Üí lower $f_r$ ‚Üí better EV range. ### 2.1.2 Aerodynamic Drag Force ($F_{ad}$) **Physical Origin:** As the vehicle moves, it must push air out of the way. The energy required to do this is dissipated as pressure drag (form drag) and skin friction drag. The drag force grows as the **square of velocity**. **Why it matters:** At high speeds (above ~80 km/h), $F_{ad}$ becomes dominant. This is the limiting factor for top speed in most EVs (e.g., why Tata Nexon EV is speed-limited at 120 km/h ‚Äî motor and battery cannot deliver drag power continuously). $$F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_a \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2$$ **Engineering Insight:** Halving $C_d \cdot A_f$ (drag area) doubles the range at high speed. Tesla Model S ($C_d = 0.208$) achieves far better highway range than boxy SUVs. ### 2.1.3 Climbing (Grade) Force ($F_{hc}$) **Physical Origin:** On an inclined road, the gravitational force vector $M g$ has a component along the road surface pointing *downhill*. To climb at constant speed, the motor must overcome this component. $$F_{hc} = M \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha$$ **Engineering Insight:** For small angles, $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \text{grade}\%$/100. A 10% grade (typical Kottayam ghats) means $\alpha = 5.71¬∞$, $\sin\alpha = 0.0995$. ### 2.1.4 Linear Acceleration Force ($F_{la}$) **Physical Origin:** Per Newton's Second Law, accelerating a mass $M$ at rate $a$ requires force $M \cdot a$. For a vehicle, we also include the rotational inertia of the drivetrain (wheels, gears, motor rotor) via the mass factor $\delta$. $$F_{la} = \delta \cdot M \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$$ **Engineering Insight:** $\delta$ accounts for the equivalent mass of rotating components. For an EV with single-speed gearbox, $\delta \approx 1.05$; for an ICE with manual transmission, $\delta \approx 1.10$. ## 2.2 The Tractive Force Equation (Master Equation) By Newton's Second Law applied to the vehicle in the longitudinal direction: $$F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}$$ Expanding: $$F_t = f_r M g \cos\alpha + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2 + M g \sin\alpha + \delta M \frac{dv}{dt}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Insight:** This single equation is worth up to **7 marks** on its own in ESE Part B questions. Memorize the four terms, their physical meaning, and the conditions under which each dominates. ## 2.3 Power Demand at the Wheels The instantaneous mechanical power required at the wheel-road interface is force times velocity: $$P_w = F_t \cdot v = \left[ f_r M g \cos\alpha + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2 + M g \sin\alpha + \delta M \frac{dv}{dt} \right] v$$ The power required from the motor (at the motor shaft) accounts for drivetrain losses: $$P_m = \frac{P_w}{\eta_{dr}}$$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Typical Values | |---|----------|---------|-------|------------------------| | 1 | Rolling Resistance Force | $F_{rr} = f_r M g \cos\alpha$ | N | $f_r$ = **0.008‚Äì0.015** | | 2 | Aerodynamic Drag Force | $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A_f v^2$ | N | $\rho_a$ = **1.202 kg/m¬≥** | | 3 | Grade (Climbing) Force | $F_{hc} = M g \sin\alpha$ | N | $\alpha$ in radians | | 4 | Acceleration Force | $F_{la} = \delta M \tfrac{dv}{dt}$ | N | $\delta \approx$ **1.05‚Äì1.10** | | 5 | Tractive Force | $F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}$ | N | Master equation | | 6 | Wheel Power | $P_w = F_t \cdot v$ | W | At contact patch | | 7 | Motor Power | $P_m = P_w / \eta_{dr}$ | W | At motor shaft | | 8 | Wheel Torque | $T_w = F_t \cdot r_w$ | N¬∑m | At wheel | | 9 | Motor Torque | $T_m = T_w / (G_r \eta_{dr})$ | N¬∑m | After gearbox | | 10 | Energy (per trip) | $E = \int P_m \, dt$ | J or Wh | Integrate over drive cycle | | 11 | Max Grade (constant speed) | $\sin\alpha_{max} = \tfrac{F_{t,max} - F_{rr} - F_{ad}}{Mg}$ | ‚Äî | At given speed | | 12 | Max Acceleration (flat road) | $a_{max} = \tfrac{F_{t,max} - F_{rr} - F_{ad}}{\delta M}$ | m/s¬≤ | At given speed | | 13 | Top Speed (flat road) | Set $F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad}$ and solve for $v$ | m/s | $\tfrac{dv}{dt} = 0$ | > [!WARNING] > **Unit Consistency Pitfall:** When using the formulas, $v$ must be in **m/s** (NOT km/h) for the drag and power equations. Convert: $v$ [m/s] = $V$ [km/h] / 3.6. Mixing units is the **#1 reason** for losing 2-3 marks in KTU numericals. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility This mathematical model is the **backbone** of the following engineering tasks: 1. **Motor Sizing for HEV/EV** ‚Äî Peak power and continuous power ratings. 2. **Battery Pack Sizing** ‚Äî Energy (kWh) required per drive cycle. 3. **Drivetrain Gear Ratio Selection** ‚Äî Trade-off between acceleration and top speed. 4. **Range Estimation** ‚Äî EPA, WLTP, and ARAI test cycles apply this model at standardized speeds. 5. **Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery** ‚Äî The deceleration term $F_{la}$ (now negative) becomes recoverable energy. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Tractive Force Equation (Free Body Analysis) ### Step 1 ‚Äî Define the System Consider a vehicle of total mass $M$ moving on a road inclined at angle $\alpha$ to the horizontal with instantaneous velocity $v$ in the positive x-direction. The driven wheels deliver a forward force $F_t$ at the contact patch. We isolate the vehicle as a rigid body. ### Step 2 ‚Äî Resolve Gravity Along and Perpendicular to the Road The weight $W = Mg$ acts vertically downward. Resolving along road-aligned axes: - Component along road (opposing forward motion when $\alpha > 0$): $Mg \sin\alpha$ - Component normal to road (pressing vehicle onto surface): $Mg \cos\alpha$ The normal force on the road is: $$N = M g \cos\alpha$$ ### Step 3 ‚Äî Identify All Forces Along the x-axis By Newton's 2nd Law ($\Sigma F_x = M a_x$): $$F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_{hc} - F_{rot} = M \frac{dv}{dt}$$ where $F_{rot}$ represents the equivalent inertial force from rotating drivetrain components. ### Step 4 ‚Äî Substitute Rolling Resistance and Aerodynamic Drag The rolling resistance arises from tire hysteresis and is empirically proportional to the normal force: $$F_{rr} = f_r \cdot N = f_r M g \cos\alpha$$ The aerodynamic drag is derived from dimensional analysis of fluid flow over a bluff body: $$F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2$$ ### Step 5 ‚Äî Introduce the Mass Factor $\delta$ The rotational inertia $I$ of the wheels, gears, and motor rotor, when rotating at angular velocity $\omega$, stores kinetic energy $\tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2$. Equating this to $\tfrac{1}{2} M_{eq} v^2$ gives an equivalent mass. Combined with the translational mass: $$F_{rot} = (\delta - 1) M \frac{dv}{dt} \implies F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_{hc} = \delta M \frac{dv}{dt}$$ ### Step 6 ‚Äî Final Compact Form Rearranging: $$\boxed{F_t = f_r M g \cos\alpha + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2 + M g \sin\alpha + \delta M \frac{dv}{dt}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **fundamental performance equation** of every road vehicle. All KTU HEV/EV problems reduce to evaluating or applying this equation. ## 3.2 Derivation of Power at the Wheels and Motor Starting from the definition of power as the rate of doing work: $$P_w = \frac{dW}{dt} = F_t \cdot \frac{dx}{dt} = F_t \cdot v$$ Substituting $F_t$: $$P_w = \left[ f_r M g \cos\alpha + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2 + M g \sin\alpha + \delta M \frac{dv}{dt} \right] v$$ This expands to four power terms: $$P_w = P_{rr} + P_{ad} + P_{hc} + P_{la}$$ where: - $P_{rr} = f_r M g v \cos\alpha$ ‚Äî power lost to rolling - $P_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A_f v^3$ ‚Äî power lost to drag (note cubic dependence) - $P_{hc} = M g v \sin\alpha$ ‚Äî power for climbing - $P_{la} = \delta M v \tfrac{dv}{dt}$ ‚Äî power for acceleration > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why $v^3$ for drag power?** Drag force $\propto v^2$, and power = force √ó velocity ‚Üí drag power $\propto v^3$. This is why **highway driving is so energy-intensive for EVs**: doubling speed from 60 to 120 km/h increases drag power by **8√ó**. The motor must deliver: $$P_m = \frac{P_w}{\eta_{dr}}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation of Top Speed on a Level Road At constant top speed, acceleration $a = 0$ and grade $\alpha = 0$. The tractive force equals rolling resistance plus drag: $$F_t = f_r M g + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v_{max}^2$$ The maximum tractive force the propulsion system can deliver is $F_{t,max}$. Setting $F_t = F_{t,max}$ and solving: $$v_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{2 (F_{t,max} - f_r M g)}{\rho_a C_d A_f}}$$ This shows that **top speed is governed by motor peak power and aerodynamic drag area**, not peak torque. ## 3.4 Derivation of Maximum Grade (Gradeability) At constant speed on an incline, $a = 0$. The tractive force must overcome rolling resistance, drag, and grade: $$F_t = f_r M g \cos\alpha + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2 + M g \sin\alpha$$ Rearranging for $\sin\alpha$: $$\sin\alpha = \frac{F_t - f_r M g \cos\alpha - \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2}{M g}$$ For a specified speed, $F_t$ is the maximum tractive force the motor can deliver at that speed. The gradeability is typically expressed as a **percentage**: $$\text{Grade \%} = 100 \cdot \tan\alpha_{max} \approx 100 \cdot \sin\alpha_{max} \quad \text{(for small angles)}$$ ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Step-by-Step) **Problem:** A prototype electric vehicle has the following specifications: - Total mass $M = 1500$ kg - Frontal area $A_f = 2.2$ m¬≤ - Drag coefficient $C_d = 0.30$ - Rolling resistance coefficient $f_r = 0.012$ - Drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{dr} = 0.90$ - Wheel radius $r_w = 0.32$ m - Mass factor $\delta = 1.08$ - The vehicle is climbing a 6% grade at constant speed 72 km/h. **Find:** (a) Each of the four resistive forces. (b) Total tractive force. (c) Power at the wheels. (d) Motor power required. ### Solution: **Convert units:** $v = 72$ km/h $= 72/3.6 = 20$ m/s Grade: 6% means $\tan\alpha = 0.06 \Rightarrow \alpha = \arctan(0.06) = 3.434¬∞$ $\sin\alpha = 0.0599$, $\cos\alpha = 0.9982$ **(a) Compute each force:** Rolling resistance: $$F_{rr} = f_r M g \cos\alpha = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.9982$$ $$F_{rr} = 176.3 \text{ N}$$ Aerodynamic drag: $$F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2 = 0.5 \times 1.202 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (20)^2$$ $$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.202 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 400 = 158.7 \text{ N}$$ Grade force: $$F_{hc} = M g \sin\alpha = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.0599 = 881.4 \text{ N}$$ Acceleration force: Since constant speed, $a = 0$, so: $$F_{la} = 0 \text{ N}$$ **(b) Total tractive force:** $$F_t = 176.3 + 158.7 + 881.4 + 0 = 1216.4 \text{ N}$$ **(c) Power at wheels:** $$P_w = F_t \cdot v = 1216.4 \times 20 = 24{,}328 \text{ W} \approx 24.33 \text{ kW}$$ **(d) Motor power:** $$P_m = \frac{P_w}{\eta_{dr}} = \frac{24{,}328}{0.90} = 27{,}031 \text{ W} \approx 27.03 \text{ kW}$$ > [!TIP] > **Verification by physics intuition:** At 20 m/s on a 6% grade, the grade force dominates (~72% of total resistance). This matches the intuition that hill-climbing is the most energy-intensive part of a drive cycle. In an EV, this is when State-of-Charge drops fastest. ## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example ‚Äî Maximum Acceleration **Problem:** For the same vehicle on a flat road ($\alpha = 0$) at 30 km/h (8.33 m/s), the motor can deliver a maximum tractive force of 4000 N. Find the maximum acceleration. **Solution:** Compute resistive forces at $v = 8.33$ m/s: $$F_{rr} = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times 1 = 176.6 \text{ N}$$ $$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.202 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (8.33)^2 = 27.5 \text{ N}$$ Net force available for acceleration: $$F_{net} = F_{t,max} - F_{rr} - F_{ad} = 4000 - 176.6 - 27.5 = 3795.9 \text{ N}$$ Maximum acceleration: $$a_{max} = \frac{F_{net}}{\delta M} = \frac{3795.9}{1.08 \times 1500} = 2.34 \text{ m/s}^2$$ In more familiar terms: 0 to 100 km/h time $\approx v_{100} / a = 27.78 / 2.34 = 11.87$ s (using constant $a$ approximation). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Free Body Diagram ‚Äî Vehicle on Inclined Road (Textual Schematic) ``` ‚Üë Normal reaction N = Mg cosŒ± | | / (road surface) F_rr ‚Üê‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚óè‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Üí F_t (Tractive force at wheels) | / | / Œ± (grade angle) | / | / ‚Üì Weight Mg |/___________ (vehicle) ‚Üê F_ad (drag, along road, opposite to motion) ‚Üí F_hc direction: when going UPHILL, F_hc acts BACKWARD along slope ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture ‚Äî Vehicle Performance Model The following Mermaid diagram maps the **flow of forces and power** from the propulsion source through the drivetrain to the road, including all resistive loads. ```mermaid flowchart TD node1[Energy Source
Battery or Fuel] --> node2[Power Converter
Inverter or Engine] node2 --> node3[Motor or Engine
Shaft] node3 --> node4[Transmission
Gearbox G_r] node4 --> node5[Drivetrain
Efficiency eta_dr] node5 --> node6[Wheels
Radius r_w] node6 --> node7[Contact Patch
Road Interface] node8[Driver Input
Throttle] --> node2 node9[Road Grade
alpha] --> node7 node10[Vehicle Mass M] --> node7 node11[Aerodynamic
Cd, Af, rho_a] --> node7 node7 --> node12[Tractive Force Ft] node12 --> node13[F_rr Rolling Resistance] node12 --> node14[F_ad Aerodynamic Drag] node12 --> node15[F_hc Grade Force] node12 --> node16[F_la Acceleration Force] node13 --> node17[Resistive Load] node14 --> node17 node15 --> node17 node16 --> node17 node17 --> node18[Power Demand Pw] node18 --> node19[Energy Consumption
per km or per cycle] node20[Brake Request] --> node21[Regenerative
Braking] node21 --> node1 ``` ## 4.3 Force Decomposition Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Total Tractive Force Ft] --> B[F_rr] A --> C[F_ad] A --> D[F_hc] A --> E[F_la] B --> B1[fr √ó M √ó g √ó cosŒ±] B --> B2[Tire hysteresis loss] C --> C1[0.5 √ó œÅa √ó Cd √ó Af √ó v¬≤] C --> C2[Form drag] C --> C3[Skin friction] D --> D1[M √ó g √ó sinŒ±] D --> D2[Grade percentage] D --> D3[Road slope angle Œ±] E --> E1[Œ¥ √ó M √ó dv/dt] E --> E2[Translational mass] E --> E3[Rotational inertia] ``` ## 4.4 Performance Envelope Visualization The **dynamic performance envelope** of any vehicle is the envelope in (speed, tractive force) space that the powertrain can deliver. A typical envelope has three corners: ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PerformanceEnvelope P1[Constant Power
Hyperbola
P = F x v] P2[Constant Torque
Vertical Line
T = F x r_w] P3[Low Speed
Torque Limit] end P2 --> P1 P1 --> P4[High Speed
Power Limit] P3 --> P4 A[Region: F_t,available] --> P1 A --> P2 A --> P3 A --> P4 R[Resistive Force
F_rr + F_ad + F_hc] --> Compare{Compare} A --> Compare Compare --> Result1[Where Ft gt R: Vehicle can drive] Compare --> Result2[Where Ft lt R: Vehicle stops or slows] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Key Concept:** The vehicle can maintain any speed only if the available tractive force curve lies **above** the resistive force curve at that speed. The intersection point defines the **top speed** on a given grade. ## 4.5 Drive Cycle Force vs Time Profile (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart TD DC[Drive Cycle - example NEDC or Indian Drive Cycle] --> Phase1[Idle Phase] DC --> Phase2[Acceleration Phase] DC --> Phase3[Constant Speed Phase] DC --> Phase4[Deceleration Phase] DC --> Phase5[Stop Phase] Phase1 --> F1[Ft = 0, F_la = 0] Phase2 --> F2[Ft gt F_rr + F_ad + F_hc, F_la gt 0] Phase3 --> F3[Ft = F_rr + F_ad + F_hc, F_la = 0] Phase4 --> F4[Ft = 0, F_la negative, Regen ON] Phase5 --> F5[Ft = 0, F_la = 0] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model] **Define the following terms related to vehicle dynamics: (i) Tractive force, (ii) Rolling resistance, (iii) Aerodynamic drag.** **Model Answer:** **(i) Tractive Force ($F_t$):** The total forward force generated at the contact patch between the driven wheels and the road surface by the propulsion system. It is the force responsible for moving the vehicle forward and must overcome all resistive forces. $$F_t = f_r M g \cos\alpha + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2 + M g \sin\alpha + \delta M \frac{dv}{dt}$$ **(ii) Rolling Resistance ($F_{rr}$):** The resistive force arising from the deformation of the tire as it rolls on the road surface. It is caused primarily by the hysteresis loss in the tire material and is proportional to the normal load on the tire. $$F_{rr} = f_r \cdot M \cdot g \cdot \cos\alpha$$ **(iii) Aerodynamic Drag ($F_{ad}$):** The resistive force due to the displacement of air by the moving vehicle. It depends on air density $\rho_a$, the square of vehicle velocity $v^2$, the drag coefficient $C_d$, and the frontal area $A_f$. $$F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2$$ --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model] **Explain the significance of the mass factor $\delta$ in the vehicle dynamics equation. What is its typical range for an electric vehicle?** **Model Answer:** **Significance of $\delta$:** The mass factor $\delta$ (also called the rotational inertia coefficient) accounts for the additional inertia of rotating components in the drivetrain (wheels, gearbox, motor rotor, drive shafts) when the vehicle accelerates. When a vehicle accelerates linearly, these rotating parts also accelerate angularly, requiring additional torque from the motor that is not accounted for in the simple $F = Ma$ term. **Mathematical Form:** The effective inertial mass is $\delta M$, and the acceleration force becomes: $$F_{la} = \delta M \frac{dv}{dt}$$ where $\delta = 1 + \frac{I_{rot}}{M r_w^2}$ and $I_{rot}$ is the equivalent moment of inertia of all rotating parts referred to the wheel. **Typical Range for EV:** For a typical electric vehicle with single-speed reduction gearbox, **$\delta \approx 1.05$ to $1.08$**. For an ICE vehicle with manual transmission and multiple rotating components, $\delta$ can be as high as **1.10 to 1.15**. **Engineering Relevance:** Ignoring $\delta$ underestimates the motor torque required during acceleration by 5‚Äì15%, leading to under-sized propulsion systems. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ‚Äî Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model] **(a) Derive the complete tractive force equation for a road vehicle, explaining each term with its physical origin. (7 marks)** **(b) An electric vehicle has the following specifications: Total mass $M = 1800$ kg, Frontal area $A_f = 2.4$ m¬≤, $C_d = 0.28$, $f_r = 0.010$, wheel radius $r_w = 0.33$ m, $\delta = 1.06$, $\eta_{dr} = 0.92$. The vehicle is traveling at a constant speed of 90 km/h on a level road. Calculate: (i) the total resistive force, (ii) the tractive power required at the wheels, (iii) the motor power required, and (iv) the motor torque. (7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Derivation:** Consider a vehicle of total mass $M$ on a road inclined at angle $\alpha$. The vehicle moves with velocity $v$ in the forward direction. The forces acting on the vehicle are: 1. **Tractive force $F_t$** at the driven wheel-road contact patch (forward). 2. **Aerodynamic drag $F_{ad}$** acting at the vehicle's center of pressure (backward). 3. **Rolling resistance $F_{rr}$** distributed at all tire contact patches (backward). 4. **Weight component along the slope $Mg \sin\alpha$** (backward when climbing). 5. **Weight component normal to the slope $Mg \cos\alpha$** (perpendicular to road, into the surface). **Resolving gravity along the slope:** The vehicle's weight $W = Mg$ can be resolved into two components: - Along the slope (parallel to motion): $Mg \sin\alpha$ - Normal to the slope: $Mg \cos\alpha$ **Normal force on tires:** $N = Mg \cos\alpha$ **Rolling resistance:** Empirically, rolling resistance is proportional to normal load: $$F_{rr} = f_r \cdot N = f_r M g \cos\alpha$$ **Aerodynamic drag:** By fluid dynamics, the drag force is: $$F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2$$ **Applying Newton's Second Law** along the direction of motion: $$F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_{hc} = \delta M \frac{dv}{dt}$$ **Substituting all terms:** $$F_t = f_r M g \cos\alpha + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2 + M g \sin\alpha + \delta M \frac{dv}{dt}$$ where: - $f_r$ = coefficient of rolling resistance - $\rho_a$ = air density (1.202 kg/m¬≥) - $C_d$ = drag coefficient - $A_f$ = frontal area - $v$ = vehicle speed - $\alpha$ = road grade angle - $\delta$ = mass factor (rotational inertia coefficient) - $\frac{dv}{dt}$ = vehicle acceleration **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given data:** - $M = 1800$ kg, $A_f = 2.4$ m¬≤, $C_d = 0.28$, $f_r = 0.010$ - $r_w = 0.33$ m, $\delta = 1.06$, $\eta_{dr} = 0.92$ - $v = 90$ km/h $= 90/3.6 = 25$ m/s - Level road: $\alpha = 0$, so $\sin\alpha = 0$, $\cos\alpha = 1$ - Constant speed: $\frac{dv}{dt} = 0$, so $F_{la} = 0$ **(i) Total resistive force:** Rolling resistance: $$F_{rr} = f_r M g \cos\alpha = 0.010 \times 1800 \times 9.81 \times 1 = 176.6 \text{ N}$$ Aerodynamic drag: $$F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2 = 0.5 \times 1.202 \times 0.28 \times 2.4 \times (25)^2$$ $$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.202 \times 0.28 \times 2.4 \times 625 = 252.4 \text{ N}$$ Grade force: $$F_{hc} = Mg \sin\alpha = 0 \text{ N}$$ Acceleration force: $$F_{la} = \delta M \frac{dv}{dt} = 0 \text{ N}$$ Total resistive force: $$F_{resistive} = 176.6 + 252.4 + 0 + 0 = 429.0 \text{ N}$$ **(ii) Tractive power at the wheels:** $$P_w = F_t \cdot v = 429.0 \times 25 = 10{,}725 \text{ W} \approx 10.73 \text{ kW}$$ **(iii) Motor power required:** $$P_m = \frac{P_w}{\eta_{dr}} = \frac{10{,}725}{0.92} = 11{,}658 \text{ W} \approx 11.66 \text{ kW}$$ **(iv) Motor torque:** Wheel torque: $$T_w = F_t \cdot r_w = 429.0 \times 0.33 = 141.6 \text{ N¬∑m}$$ Assuming a single-speed reduction gearbox with ratio $G_r$ (not given, so we report wheel torque). If $G_r$ were given (say $G_r = 8$), then: $$T_m = \frac{T_w}{G_r \cdot \eta_{dr}} = \frac{141.6}{8 \times 0.92} = 19.24 \text{ N¬∑m}$$ For this problem, report the **wheel torque** as the answer unless $G_r$ is specified. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model ‚Äî Alternative Choice] **(a) With the aid of a free body diagram, derive the equation of motion of a vehicle climbing an inclined road at constant acceleration. State the assumptions made. (7 marks)** **(b) A hybrid electric vehicle (HEV) has a total mass of 1600 kg including batteries. It is climbing a 7% grade. The vehicle achieves a constant speed of 60 km/h. The following data are given: $C_d A_f = 0.7$ m¬≤, $f_r = 0.011$, $\rho_a = 1.18$ kg/m¬≥, $\delta = 1.08$, $\eta_{dr} = 0.88$, wheel radius $r_w = 0.31$ m.
