Determine: (i) the tractive effort required, (ii) the power at the wheels, (iii) the total energy required to maintain this climb for 5 minutes. (7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Derivation with FBD and Assumptions:** **Assumptions:** 1. The road is a straight incline of constant grade angle $\alpha$. 2. The vehicle is treated as a rigid body (no internal dynamics). 3. No wind: aerodynamic drag is purely from the vehicle's own motion. 4. Drivetrain is in steady state; gear ratio and efficiency are constants. 5. Tire slip is neglected; wheel rolls without slipping. 6. Cornering forces and lateral dynamics are neglected (purely longitudinal motion). **Free Body Diagram (description):** - Vehicle on inclined plane with angle $\alpha$ to the horizontal. - $F_t$ at the rear (driven) wheel contact patch pointing up the slope. - $F_{rr}$ at each tire contact patch pointing down the slope. - $F_{ad}$ at the vehicle's center of pressure pointing down the slope. - $W = Mg$ at the center of gravity pointing vertically downward. - $N$ (normal reaction) perpendicular to the road surface. **Resolving the weight:** - Component along slope (down): $Mg \sin\alpha$ - Component perpendicular to slope (into surface): $Mg \cos\alpha$ **Forces perpendicular to motion:** Balanced (no acceleration normal to surface): $$N = Mg \cos\alpha$$ **Forces along motion (positive up the slope):** Applying Newton's 2nd Law: $$F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - Mg\sin\alpha = \delta M a$$ where $a = \frac{dv}{dt}$ is the linear acceleration of the vehicle center of mass. **Substituting the expressions:** - $F_{rr} = f_r N = f_r M g \cos\alpha$ - $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2$ $$F_t = f_r M g \cos\alpha + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2 + M g \sin\alpha + \delta M a$$ **For constant speed ($a = 0$):** $$F_t = f_r M g \cos\alpha + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2 + M g \sin\alpha$$ **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given data:** - $M = 1600$ kg, grade = 7% ‚Üí $\tan\alpha = 0.07$ - $\alpha = \arctan(0.07) = 4.004¬∞$, $\sin\alpha = 0.0698$, $\cos\alpha = 0.9976$ - $v = 60$ km/h = $16.67$ m/s - $C_d A_f = 0.7$ m¬≤, $f_r = 0.011$, $\rho_a = 1.18$ kg/m¬≥ - $\delta = 1.08$, $\eta_{dr} = 0.88$, $r_w = 0.31$ m - $a = 0$ (constant speed) **(i) Tractive effort required:** Rolling resistance: $$F_{rr} = f_r M g \cos\alpha = 0.011 \times 1600 \times 9.81 \times 0.9976 = 172.2 \text{ N}$$ Aerodynamic drag: $$F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2 = 0.5 \times 1.18 \times 0.7 \times (16.67)^2$$ $$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.18 \times 0.7 \times 277.8 = 114.7 \text{ N}$$ Grade force: $$F_{hc} = M g \sin\alpha = 1600 \times 9.81 \times 0.0698 = 1095.4 \text{ N}$$ Acceleration force: $$F_{la} = 0 \text{ N}$$ Tractive effort: $$F_t = 172.2 + 114.7 + 1095.4 + 0 = 1382.3 \text{ N}$$ **(ii) Power at the wheels:** $$P_w = F_t \cdot v = 1382.3 \times 16.67 = 23{,}043 \text{ W} \approx 23.04 \text{ kW}$$ **(iii) Energy required for 5 minutes:** Time = 5 min = 300 s Assuming constant speed, the power at the wheels is constant. Energy at wheels: $$E_w = P_w \times t = 23{,}043 \times 300 = 6{,}912{,}900 \text{ J} \approx 6.91 \text{ MJ}$$ Energy from the motor (input energy): $$E_m = \frac{E_w}{\eta_{dr}} = \frac{6{,}912{,}900}{0.88} = 7{,}855{,}568 \text{ J} \approx 7.86 \text{ MJ}$$ Converting to kWh: $$E_m = \frac{7{,}855{,}568}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 2.18 \text{ kWh}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ‚Äî Common Mistakes on This Topic:** > 1. **Unit Conversion Error (‚Äì2 marks):** Failing to convert km/h to m/s when computing drag or power. Always convert $v$ to m/s *first*. > 2. **Forgetting $\cos\alpha$ in $F_{rr}$ (‚Äì1 to ‚Äì2 marks):** On a grade, the normal force is $Mg\cos\alpha$, not $Mg$. Students often write $F_{rr} = f_r Mg$, losing accuracy. > 3. **Including $F_{la}$ when $a=0$ (‚Äì1 mark):** For constant speed problems, $F_{la} = 0$ must be explicitly stated. > 4. **Drivetrain Efficiency Direction (‚Äì2 marks):** When finding motor power from wheel power, **divide** by $\eta_{dr}$. When finding wheel power from motor power, **multiply** by $\eta_{dr}$. Many students reverse this. > 5. **Mixing $C_d$ and $C_d A_f$:** Some textbooks give $C_d$ alone, others give the drag area $C_d A_f$ as a single quantity. Read the problem statement carefully. > 6. **No units in final answer (‚Äì0.5 to ‚Äì1 mark):** Always state the units (N, kW, kWh) explicitly. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist ‚Äî Vehicle Dynamics & Load Forces** ### Core Equations (MUST memorize) - [ ] $F_t = f_r M g \cos\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2 + M g \sin\alpha + \delta M \tfrac{dv}{dt}$ - [ ] $P_w = F_t \cdot v$ - [ ] $P_m = P_w / \eta_{dr}$ - [ ] $T_w = F_t \cdot r_w$ ### Standard Constants & Typical Values - [ ] $g = 9.81$ m/s¬≤ - [ ] $\rho_a = 1.202$ kg/m¬≥ (at 20¬∞C, sea level) - [ ] $C_d$ range: 0.20 (Tesla) ‚Äî 0.40 (truck) - [ ] $f_r$ range: 0.008 (low-rr EV tire) ‚Äî 0.015 (typical car) - [ ] $\delta$ range: 1.05 (EV) ‚Äî 1.15 (ICE with accessories) - [ ] $\eta_{dr}$ range: 0.85 ‚Äî 0.95 ### Key Physical Insights - [ ] Drag force $\propto v^2$; Drag power $\propto v^3$ ‚Äî explains highway energy penalty - [ ] At low speeds ($< 40$ km/h), rolling resistance dominates - [ ] At high speeds ($> 80$ km/h), aerodynamic drag dominates - [ ] Grade force is independent of speed (only depends on slope and mass) - [ ] Acceleration force depends on $\delta M$, not just $M$ - [ ] Top speed is set by the balance of tractive force and drag - [ ] Gradeability is set by available tractive force at low speed (max torque) ### Problem-Solving Strategy 1. Convert all units to SI (kg, m, m/s, s). 2. Identify whether speed is constant ($a = 0$), road is level ($\alpha = 0$), or both. 3. Compute each of the four forces separately. 4. Sum for total tractive force. 5. Multiply by $v$ for wheel power. 6. Divide by $\eta_{dr}$ for motor power. 7. Multiply by time for energy. 8. State units explicitly in every step. ### Common Exam Traps - The mass factor $\delta$ applies only to the *acceleration* force, not the total mass in rolling resistance, drag, or grade force. - For "instantaneous" problems at a given speed, $F_{la} = \delta M a$ where $a$ is the *acceleration at that instant*, not a target average. - Energy per unit distance (kWh/100 km) is often a more practical metric than energy per trip. - Braking (negative $F_{la}$) recaptures energy via regenerative braking in HEV/EV. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Memory Aid ‚Äî The "FADMA" Mnemonic for Vehicle Forces:** > **F**riction (rolling) ‚Üí **A**erodynamic (drag) ‚Üí **D**escent (grade) ‚Üí **M**omentum (acceleration) ‚Üí **A**pplied (tractive) > "$F_t$ equals FADMA" ‚Äî recall the order: rolling ‚Üí drag ‚Üí grade ‚Üí acceleration.

topics.title: Vehicle Load Forces: Aerodynamic Drag
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Load Forces: Aerodynamic Drag

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Aerodynamic Drag** is the resistive force generated by a vehicle's motion through a fluid medium (typically air) due to the relative velocity between the vehicle body and the surrounding air mass. It is one of the four primary **Vehicle Load Forces** (alongside Rolling Resistance, Gradient/Climbing Resistance, and Inertial Force) that the traction system of a Hybrid or Electric Vehicle must overcome during operation.

Mathematically, the Aerodynamic Drag Force ($F_d$) is expressed as:

$$F_d = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot V^2$$

where each symbol carries precise engineering meaning as defined in Section 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Aerodynamic Drag is a high-weightage topic in the **"Vehicle Load Forces"** cluster. It is directly tested in numerical problems related to power-train sizing, battery range estimation for Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs), and energy management strategies in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Stuck in Molasses" Effect

Imagine pushing a flat wooden board through still water versus pushing a streamlined torpedo-shaped object. The flat board feels like you are "puckering through molasses" ‚Äî water smashes into its broad face, swirls turbulently behind it, and resists every inch of your push. The torpedo slips through almost effortlessly.

A moving vehicle faces exactly the same problem with air:

- **Air is invisible, but it has mass.** At highway speeds, a car is essentially pushing through a wall of air roughly **1.225 kg/m¬≥** in density (at sea level, **15 ¬∞C**).
- **The faster you go, the exponentially harder the push becomes.** Because drag scales with $V^2$, doubling your speed **quadruples** the drag force, and the power required to overcome it **octuples** (since $P \propto V^3$).
- **Shape is destiny.** A truck with a flat front face has a drag coefficient $C_d \approx 0.8$‚Äì$1.0$, while a Tesla Model S achieves $C_d \approx 0.208$. Same frontal area, dramatically different energy bill.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why EV Engineers Obsess Over Drag:** In an Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) vehicle, aerodynamic drag accounts for roughly **50‚Äì60%** of total driving resistance at highway speeds (80‚Äì120 km/h). For an Electric Vehicle, since there is no engine idling loss and regenerative braking recovers kinetic energy, **aerodynamic drag becomes the single largest energy sink** on extended highway drives. This is why every modern EV silhouette is sculpted, wind-tunnel-tested, and continuously optimized.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Air Density (sea level, 15 ¬∞C) | $\rho$ | **1.225** | kg/m¬≥ |
| Drag Coefficient (sedan EV) | $C_d$ | **0.20 ‚Äì 0.30** | dimensionless |
| Frontal Area (mid-size car) | $A_f$ | **2.0 ‚Äì 2.5** | m¬≤ |
| Velocity | $V$ | varies | m/s or km/h |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Breakdown of the Drag Equation

The aerodynamic drag equation is **not arbitrary** ‚Äî it emerges rigorously from dimensional analysis and fluid dynamics. Let us dissect each variable and its physical origin.

#### 2.1.1 Air Density ($\rho$)

- **Definition:** Mass per unit volume of the air the vehicle moves through.
- **Standard Value:** $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at sea level, **15 ¬∞C (ISA conditions)**.
- **Real-world variation:** Drops to ~$1.06 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at 1500 m altitude (hill stations like Munnar, Ooty).
- **Engineering impact:** A vehicle driven at altitude encounters roughly **13‚Äì15% less drag**, which is why EV range increases slightly in mountainous terrain despite the gradient force.

#### 2.1.2 Drag Coefficient ($C_d$)

- **Definition:** A dimensionless shape-efficiency factor measuring how "slippery" the vehicle body is through the air.
- **Components:**
  - **Pressure Drag (~90%):** Caused by airflow separation and the resulting low-pressure wake behind the vehicle.
  - **Skin Friction Drag (~10%):** Caused by viscous shearing of air along the body surface.
- **Typical Values:**

| Vehicle Type | $C_d$ Range |
|---|---|
| Old-style boxy truck | 0.8 ‚Äì 1.0 |
| Conventional sedan | 0.30 ‚Äì 0.40 |
| Modern hybrid (Toyota Prius) | 0.24 ‚Äì 0.26 |
| Tesla Model S | 0.208 |
| Lucid Air (production record) | 0.197 |
| Teardrop ideal shape | 0.04 ‚Äì 0.10 |

#### 2.1.3 Frontal Area ($A_f$)

- **Definition:** The projected cross-sectional area of the vehicle as seen from directly in front.
- **Engineering trade-off:** A taller vehicle (SUV) has larger $A_f$ but better ground clearance; a lower vehicle (sports car) has smaller $A_f$ but poorer visibility.

#### 2.1.4 Velocity ($V$)

- **Critical insight:** $V$ is **squared** in the force equation and **cubed** in the power equation. This non-linear scaling is the single most important aerodynamic fact for KTU numerical problems.

### 2.2 The Power Required to Overcome Drag

Since power is force times velocity, the power needed to push the vehicle through air alone is:

$$P_d = F_d \cdot V = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot V^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The $V^3$ Rule:** Tripling your speed increases the **power** needed to fight air by a factor of **27**. This is why an EV cruising at 60 km/h may achieve 8 km/kWh, but the same EV at 120 km/h may drop to under 5 km/kWh ‚Äî and it is *entirely* because of the cubic relationship between speed and aerodynamic power loss.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Memorization for KTU University Exam:** Memorize the variables, units, and the $V^3$ power relation. Most numericals in Module 1 are built on these.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variable Definitions | Boundary / Validity Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Drag Force | $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2$ | $\rho$ = air density (kg/m¬≥); $C_d$ = drag coeff. (‚Äì); $A_f$ = frontal area (m¬≤); $V$ = velocity (m/s) | Valid for steady-state, incompressible, sub-sonic flow ($V < \sim 0.3 \cdot \text{Mach}$) |
| 2 | Drag Power | $P_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^3$ | $P_d$ = aerodynamic power loss (W) | Same as above; $V$ must be in m/s for SI units |
| 3 | Unit Conversion | $V_{\text{m/s}} = \tfrac{V_{\text{km/h}}}{3.6}$ | Velocity conversion | Always convert km/h ‚Üí m/s before substituting |
| 4 | Drag Energy over Distance | $E_d = F_d \cdot d = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2 \cdot d$ | $d$ = distance (m); $E_d$ = energy (J) | Useful for range calculations in BEVs |
| 5 | Total Resistive Force | $F_t = F_{rr} + F_d + F_{grade} + F_{acc}$ | Sum of all four resistive forces (N) | Traction force must equal or exceed $F_t$ |
| 6 | Altitude Correction | $\rho(h) = \rho_0 \cdot e^{-h/H}$, $H \approx 8400$ m | $\rho_0 = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at $h=0$ | Used when vehicle operates at altitude |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EV Range Estimation:** Software such as Tesla's trip planner computes remaining range by integrating $P_d$ over the predicted velocity profile.
- **Hybrid Energy Management:** In a parallel HEV, the supervisory controller decides when to run the ICE based partly on whether aerodynamic losses justify keeping the engine off (low-speed urban) versus on (high-speed highway).
- **Brake Regeneration Tuning:** At highway speeds, the regenerative braking force is limited to avoid instability ‚Äî and the recovered energy is partially offset by ongoing $F_d$, so net recovery is speed-dependent.
- **Cooling System Design:** Lower $C_d$ bodies often restrict airflow to the radiator, forcing active grille shutters ‚Äî a classic EV/HEV design trade-off.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Aerodynamic Drag Force Equation (from First Principles)

The drag equation is derived using a **control-volume momentum-balance** approach on a vehicle moving through still air. This is the most common derivation required in KTU 14-mark questions.

#### Step 1: Set Up the Control Volume

Consider a vehicle of frontal area $A_f$ moving at velocity $V$ through stationary air of density $\rho$. In the vehicle's reference frame, the air approaches with velocity $V$.

The **mass flow rate** of air intercepted by the vehicle's frontal area per unit time is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A_f \cdot V$$

**Explanation:** In each second, a cylindrical column of air of cross-section $A_f$ and length $V$ (meters) strikes the vehicle. The mass of that air column equals density √ó volume = $\rho A_f V$.

#### Step 2: Apply Momentum Conservation

The drag force equals the rate of change of momentum of the air being deflected. In the ideal limit where air is brought to rest against the front face and then re-accelerated around the body, the momentum change per unit time is proportional to $\dot{m} \cdot V$.

The momentum flux argument gives a "reference" force scale:

$$F_{\text{ref}} \propto \rho \cdot A_f \cdot V^2$$

#### Step 3: Introduce the Shape Factor (Drag Coefficient)

In real flow, the air is not fully stopped ‚Äî it flows around the body, forms a wake, and creates pressure differentials. We therefore introduce a **proportionality constant** $C_d$ (the drag coefficient) that captures all shape-dependent losses:

$$F_d = C_d \cdot \rho \cdot A_f \cdot V^2$$

#### Step 4: Normalize via Buckingham Pi Theorem (Dimensional Check)

Dimensional analysis confirms a factor of $\tfrac{1}{2}$ must appear for consistency with kinetic energy per unit volume ($\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2$ is the dynamic pressure). The complete drag force equation becomes:

$$F_d = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot V^2$$

#### Step 5: Derive the Power Relation

Power delivered by the traction system to overcome drag is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_d &= F_d \cdot V \\
P_d &= \left( \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot V^2 \right) \cdot V \\
P_d &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot V^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering takeaway:** The cubic dependence on $V$ is why EV highway range is so critically sensitive to small speed increases.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:**
> An electric sedan has the following specifications:
> - Frontal area $A_f = 2.2 \text{ m}^2$
> - Drag coefficient $C_d = 0.25$
> - Air density $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (sea level)
> - Cruise speed $V = 90 \text{ km/h}$
>
> **Calculate:**
> 1. The aerodynamic drag force at cruise speed (in Newtons).
> 2. The power required to overcome drag (in kW).
> 3. The energy consumed by drag over a 100 km highway drive (in kWh).
> 4. How much drag force increases if the speed is doubled to 180 km/h.

#### Step 1: Convert Velocity to SI Units

$$V = 90 \text{ km/h} = \frac{90}{3.6} = 25 \text{ m/s}$$

#### Step 2: Calculate the Drag Force

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_d &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot V^2 \\
F_d &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 2.2 \cdot (25)^2 \\
F_d &= 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 2.2 \cdot 625 \\
F_d &= 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.55 \cdot 625 \\
F_d &= 0.5 \cdot 0.67375 \cdot 625 \\
F_d &= 210.55 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $F_d \approx 210.6 \text{ N}$ at 90 km/h.

#### Step 3: Calculate the Power to Overcome Drag

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_d &= F_d \cdot V = 210.55 \cdot 25 \\
P_d &= 5263.75 \text{ W} \\
P_d &\approx 5.26 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $P_d \approx 5.26 \text{ kW}$.

#### Step 4: Energy Over 100 km

Distance: $d = 100 \text{ km} = 100{,}000 \text{ m}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_d &= F_d \cdot d = 210.55 \cdot 100{,}000 \\
E_d &= 21{,}055{,}000 \text{ J} \\
E_d &= 21.055 \text{ MJ} \\
E_d &= \frac{21.055}{3.6} = 5.85 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $E_d \approx 5.85 \text{ kWh}$ consumed purely to fight air over 100 km.

#### Step 5: Drag at Doubled Speed

At $V_2 = 180 \text{ km/h} = 50 \text{ m/s}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{d,2} &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 2.2 \cdot (50)^2 \\
F_{d,2} &= 210.55 \cdot 4 = 842.2 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Doubling speed quadruples drag force ($F_{d,2} \approx 842.2 \text{ N}$). **Power increases by a factor of 8** ($P_{d,2} \approx 42.1 \text{ kW}$).

---

### 3.3 Comparison Matrix: Drag vs Other Vehicle Load Forces

| Load Force | Equation | Speed Dependency | Dominant Regime |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rolling Resistance $F_{rr}$ | $\mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$ | Independent of $V$ | Low-speed urban driving |
| **Aerodynamic Drag $F_d$** | $\tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2$ | $\propto V^2$ | **Highway driving (>60 km/h)** |
| Gradient Force $F_{grade}$ | $m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$ | Independent of $V$ | Hill climbing |
| Inertial Force $F_{acc}$ | $m \cdot a$ | Independent of $V$ | Acceleration/braking |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Aerodynamic Drag Generation ‚Äî Physical Mechanism

The following diagram illustrates **how** aerodynamic drag physically arises as a vehicle moves through air, breaking the process into the contributing sub-phenomena.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle moves at velocity V through still air] --> B[Frontal area A_f intercepts air column]
    B --> C[Air mass flow rate rho times A_f times V]
    C --> D{Pressure Drag Component}
    C --> E{Skin Friction Drag Component}
    D --> D1[Airflow separates at body rear edges]
    D1 --> D2[Low-pressure turbulent wake forms]
    D2 --> D3[Net pressure differential across vehicle]
    D3 --> F[Resultant Drag Force Fd]
    E --> E1[Air shears viscously along body surface]
    E1 --> E2[Boundary layer friction losses]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Fd = 0.5 times rho times Cd times Af times V squared]
    G --> H[Traction power Pd = Fd times V = 0.5 rho Cd Af V cubed]
    H --> I[BEV range impact: highway range shrinks dramatically]
```

### 4.2 Vehicle Load Force Block Architecture (Full Module 1 View)

This diagram situates Aerodynamic Drag within the broader Vehicle Load Force framework as covered in Module 1 of OEEET722.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TotalResistiveForce[Total Resistive Force on Vehicle F total]
        F1[Rolling Resistance F rr]
        F2[Aerodynamic Drag F d]
        F3[Gradient Force F grade]
        F4[Inertial Force F acc]
    end
    F1 --> SUM[Vector Sum of all forces]
    F2 --> SUM
    F3 --> SUM
    F4 --> SUM
    SUM --> TRACT[Required Traction Force F tract]
    TRACT --> MOTOR[Electric Motor or ICE]
    MOTOR --> ENERGY[Battery Fuel Energy Demand]
    ENERGY --> RANGE[Vehicle Driving Range km]
```

### 4.3 Force vs Speed Profile (Behavioural Visualization)

This chart-style diagram (rendered as a Mermaid block due to platform constraints) shows how each load force scales with vehicle velocity ‚Äî visually justifying why aerodynamic drag dominates at highway speeds.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[Low Speed 0 to 30 km/h] --> R1[Rolling Resistance DOMINANT]
    M[Medium Speed 30 to 80 km h] --> R2[Rolling plus Aerodynamic Comparable]
    H1[High Speed above 80 km h] --> R3[Aerodynamic Drag DOMINANT]
    H2[Very High Speed above 120 km h] --> R4[Aerodynamic Drag OVERWHELMING V cubed penalty]
```

### 4.4 EV Design Optimization Loop (Engineering Application)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Target EV Range km] --> S2[Energy Budget per km kWh per km]
    S2 --> S3[Allocate to F rr F d F acc F grade]
    S3 --> S4[Optimize Cd via wind tunnel CFD]
    S3 --> S5[Optimize Af via vehicle packaging]
    S4 --> S6[Final Cd and Af values]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Simulate drive cycles WLTP NEDC]
    S7 --> S8{Meets range target?}
    S8 -->|Yes| DONE[Production release]
    S8 -->|No| S2
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, OEEET722, Module 1]`

**Q: Define aerodynamic drag coefficient and state its typical range for modern electric vehicles.**

**Model Answer:**

The **aerodynamic drag coefficient ($C_d$)** is a dimensionless number that quantifies how streamlined a vehicle body is ‚Äî specifically, the ratio of the actual drag force experienced by the body to the reference drag force computed from the dynamic pressure and frontal area. Mathematically:

$$C_d = \frac{F_d}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho A_f V^2}$$

For modern electric vehicles, $C_d$ typically lies in the range **0.20 ‚Äì 0.30**, with record-holding production EVs (e.g., Lucid Air) achieving $C_d \approx 0.197$. This is significantly lower than conventional ICE sedans ($C_d \approx 0.30$‚Äì$0.40$) because EVs lack a large open grille for engine cooling, allowing a smoother front fascia.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award full marks only if you (a) state it is **dimensionless**, and (b) give the modern EV range **0.20 ‚Äì 0.30**.

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, OEEET722, Module 1]`

**Q: Why is the power required to overcome aerodynamic drag proportional to the cube of vehicle velocity?**

**Model Answer:**

Power is defined as force times velocity: $P = F \cdot V$. Since the drag force itself is proportional to $V^2$ (from $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V^2$), the power becomes:

$$P_d = F_d \cdot V \propto V^2 \cdot V = V^3$$

Hence $P_d \propto V^3$. This cubic relationship means that **tripling the speed increases the aerodynamic power loss by a factor of 27**, which is the primary reason highway driving drastically reduces EV driving range compared to city driving.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks ‚Äî Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** Each Part B question on Vehicle Load Forces carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Sample Pattern, OEEET722, Module 1]` ‚Äî 14 Marks

**Q: (a) Derive the aerodynamic drag force equation for a road vehicle from fundamental principles, clearly stating all variables, units, and assumptions. (7 Marks)**

**(b) An electric bus operates at a constant speed of 72 km/h on a flat highway. The bus has a frontal area of 7.5 m¬≤ and a drag coefficient of 0.55. Assuming standard air density of 1.225 kg/m¬≥, calculate: (i) the aerodynamic drag force in N, (ii) the aerodynamic drag power in kW, and (iii) the energy consumed in overcoming drag over a 50 km trip in kWh. (7 Marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Derivation:**

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the full derivation. The required answer steps are:

1. Define control volume and state $\dot{m} = \rho A_f V$.
2. Apply momentum balance to get $F \propto \rho A_f V^2$.
3. Introduce drag coefficient $C_d$ and dynamic-pressure factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$:

$$F_d = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2$$

4. State variables: $\rho$ (kg/m¬≥), $C_d$ (‚Äì), $A_f$ (m¬≤), $V$ (m/s).
5. State assumptions: steady-state, incompressible, sub-sonic flow, constant $C_d$ independent of $V$.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Convert velocity:**

$$V = \frac{72}{3.6} = 20 \text{ m/s}$$

**(i) Drag Force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_d &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.55 \cdot 7.5 \cdot (20)^2 \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.55 \cdot 7.5 \cdot 400 \\
&= 1010.625 \text{ N} \\
F_d &\approx 1010.6 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Drag Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_d &= F_d \cdot V = 1010.625 \cdot 20 \\
&= 20{,}212.5 \text{ W} \\
P_d &\approx 20.21 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Energy over 50 km:**

Distance: $d = 50 \text{ km} = 50{,}000 \text{ m}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_d &= F_d \cdot d = 1010.625 \cdot 50{,}000 \\
&= 50{,}531{,}250 \text{ J} \\
&= \frac{50.531}{3.6} \\
E_d &\approx 14.04 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** (i) $1010.6 \text{ N}$, (ii) $20.21 \text{ kW}$, (iii) $14.04 \text{ kWh}$.

---

#### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Sample Pattern, OEEET722, Module 1]` ‚Äî 14 Marks

**Q: (a) Explain the four primary vehicle load forces that resist motion of a road vehicle. Discuss the role of aerodynamic drag in determining the highway driving range of a Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV). (7 Marks)**

**(b) A BEV sedan with $C_d = 0.24$, $A_f = 2.3 \text{ m}^2$, and a 75 kWh usable battery is driven at a constant 100 km/h on a flat road in still air ($\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$). Estimate the theoretical maximum range in km if all battery energy were used solely to overcome aerodynamic drag. State two practical reasons why actual range will be lower. (7 Marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Qualitative Answer (7 marks):**

The four primary vehicle load forces are:

| # | Force | Formula | Physical Cause |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rolling Resistance $F_{rr}$ | $\mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta$ | Tire deformation + road friction |
| 2 | **Aerodynamic Drag $F_d$** | $\tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2$ | Air resistance due to vehicle motion |
| 3 | Gradient Force $F_{grade}$ | $m g \sin\theta$ | Gravity component along slope |
| 4 | Inertial Force $F_{acc}$ | $m \cdot a$ | Newton's second law during accel/brake |

**Role in BEV Range:** At highway speeds ($>$80 km/h), aerodynamic drag becomes the **dominant** resistive force because it scales with $V^2$ (force) and $V^3$ (power). Unlike ICE vehicles, BEVs have **no engine idling losses** and recover energy via regenerative braking ‚Äî so on extended highway cruises, $F_d$ represents the single largest sustained energy drain. Reducing $C_d$ by 0.01 (e.g., 0.24 ‚Üí 0.23) yields a **~4‚Äì5% range improvement** at highway speeds, which is why EV manufacturers invest heavily in wind-tunnel testing and computational fluid dynamics (CFD).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Convert velocity:** $V = \frac{100}{3.6} = 27.78 \text{ m/s}$

**Calculate Drag Force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_d &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.24 \cdot 2.3 \cdot (27.78)^2 \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.24 \cdot 2.3 \cdot 771.6 \\
&= 0.3381 \cdot 771.6 \\
&= 260.86 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Energy available:** $E_{\text{batt}} = 75 \text{ kWh} = 75 \times 3.6 \times 10^6 = 2.7 \times 10^8 \text{ J}$

**Theoretical range (drag-only):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\max} &= \frac{E_{\text{batt}}}{F_d} = \frac{2.7 \times 10^8}{260.86} \\
&= 1{,}035{,}072 \text{ m} \\
d_{\max} &\approx 1035 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Two practical reasons why actual range is lower:**

1. **Other resistive forces (rolling resistance, drivetrain losses, HVAC, auxiliary loads) consume energy** in addition to aerodynamic drag. In reality, $F_d$ is only ~50‚Äì60% of total resistance at 100 km/h.
2. **Battery usable capacity is less than nameplate** due to depth-of-discharge (DoD) limits, voltage cutoffs, and thermal management overhead. Real-world efficiency of the powertrain is also < 100% (motor ~90‚Äì95%, inverter ~95%, transmission ~97%).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s** ‚Äî this is the single most common error in Module 1 numericals and will cost you **2 full marks** even if the rest of the solution is correct.
> 2. **Using $V$ in km/h directly in the formula** produces results that are off by a factor of ~13 ‚Äî a fatal error.
> 3. **Not stating units** at every intermediate step leads to partial credit loss. Examiners expect you to write "N", "W", "J" explicitly.
> 4. **Mixing up drag *force* (‚àù V¬≤) and drag *power* (‚àù V¬≥)** in theoretical questions. Always clarify which one you are discussing.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Equation:** $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2$ ‚Äî memorize the symbols and units.
- **Power Relation:** $P_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^3$ ‚Äî drag power scales with the **cube** of velocity.
- **Unit Conversion:** Always convert km/h ‚Üí m/s by dividing by 3.6 **before** plugging into the formula.
- **$C_d$ Range:** Modern EVs achieve $C_d \approx 0.20$‚Äì$0.30$; conventional sedans $0.30$‚Äì$0.40$; trucks $0.8$‚Äì$1.0$.
- **Standard Air Density:** $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at sea level, 15 ¬∞C (ISA).
- **Drag Components:** Pressure drag (~90%) + Skin friction drag (~10%) = Total drag.
- **Dominance:** Aerodynamic drag dominates above ~80 km/h; rolling resistance dominates below ~30 km/h.
- **EV Relevance:** Because BEVs have no engine idling loss, $F_d$ is the largest sustained energy sink on highway drives ‚Äî making low $C_d$ a critical design objective.
- **Altitude Effect:** Higher altitude ‚Üí lower $\rho$ ‚Üí lower drag (good for range, but reduces engine cooling).
- **Valuation Safety:** Show every substitution step explicitly; never skip intermediate arithmetic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rolling Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Rolling Resistance # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rolling Resistance (Fr)** is the force resisting the motion of a body (typically a pneumatic tire) when it rolls on a surface. It is generated primarily due to the **non-symmetric distribution of the normal reaction force** at the tire‚Äìroad contact patch, which is caused by the viscoelastic hysteresis of the tire material and the energy dissipated during the continuous deformation and recovery cycle of the tire as it rolls. In the context of Hybrid and Electric Vehicles (HEVs/EVs), rolling resistance is one of the three primary forces opposing vehicle motion at steady-state cruising (the other two being **aerodynamic drag** and **climbing resistance**). Since EVs have no engine idling losses, reducing rolling resistance is critical to extending the **driving range** per charge ‚Äî a key performance parameter of any KTU 2024 Scheme HEV design problem. The rolling resistance is modeled as: $$F_r = \mu_r \cdot W = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta)$$ where $\mu_r$ (or $C_{rr}$) is the **dimensionless coefficient of rolling resistance** and $W = mg\cos(\theta)$ is the normal load on the tire. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-world analogy:** Imagine pushing a **deflated rubber ball** across a rough floor versus pushing a **hard steel ball** of the same weight. The rubber ball continually flattens at the contact patch, "drags," and slowly recovers its shape ‚Äî you feel a constant resistance. The steel ball barely deforms, so it rolls smoothly. The "dragging" feeling you sense on the soft ball is exactly **rolling resistance** ‚Äî energy is being wasted as heat inside the rubber every fraction of a second. For a pneumatic tire, the contact patch on the **leading edge** of the rolling tire compresses more (storing energy), while the **trailing edge** recovers (but cannot give back all the energy due to internal molecular friction of the rubber ‚Äî called **hysteresis**). This creates an asymmetric pressure distribution, leading to a net retarding moment. ## 1.3 Standard Reference Values (KTU Frequently Tested) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Typical Coefficient of Rolling Resistance ($C_{rr}$) ‚Äî for KTU numerical problems:** | Vehicle / Surface | $C_{rr}$ (Dimensionless) | |---|---| | Car tire on concrete/asphalt | **0.010 ‚Äì 0.015** | | Truck tire on asphalt | **0.006 ‚Äì 0.012** | | Bicycle tire on asphalt | **0.004 ‚Äì 0.008** | | Railroad steel wheel on steel rail | **0.0001 ‚Äì 0.0020** | | Low Rolling Resistance (LRR) EV tire | **0.005 ‚Äì 0.008** | The standard value used in most KTU textbook problems: **$C_{rr} = 0.015$** for a passenger car on asphalt. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Physics of Rolling Resistance ‚Äî Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1 ‚Äî The Contact Patch is NOT a Point:** Unlike the idealized "knife-edge" contact of a rigid wheel in classical mechanics, a pneumatic tire flattens over a finite contact area (typically 150‚Äì250 cm¬≤ for a passenger car tire) when loaded. **Step 2 ‚Äî Viscoelastic Deformation:** The rubber compound is **viscoelastic** ‚Äî it behaves partly like a spring (elastic) and partly like a viscous fluid (energy-dissipating). When the tire enters the contact zone, it must do **work to deform**, but when it leaves, it recovers **less energy** than was put in. The difference is dissipated as heat in the tire. **Step 3 ‚Äî Asymmetric Pressure Distribution:** Because of this hysteresis, the **vertical reaction force is shifted forward** in the direction of wheel rotation. The resultant normal force $N$ effectively acts at a small distance $a$ ahead of the geometric center of the contact patch. **Step 4 ‚Äî The Retarding Moment:** This forward shift creates a moment $N \cdot a$ about the wheel center, which the propulsion torque must overcome. Equating the propulsion force at the axle to the moment arm of the wheel radius $r$ gives us the rolling resistance force. **Step 5 ‚Äî The Coefficient Emerges:** The ratio $\frac{a}{r}$ is found to be approximately **constant** for a given tire‚Äìsurface‚Äìspeed combination, and is defined as the **coefficient of rolling resistance**: $$C_{rr} = \frac{a}{r}$$ ## 2.2 Key Factors Affecting Rolling Resistance - **Tire Inflation Pressure** ‚Äî Higher pressure reduces contact patch area and deformation ‚Üí **lower $C_{rr}$**. - **Tire Material & Tread Design** ‚Äî Softer compounds have more hysteresis ‚Üí **higher $C_{rr}$**. - **Vehicle Speed** ‚Äî At low/moderate speeds, $C_{rr}$ is roughly constant. Above ~100 km/h, it rises approximately linearly with speed due to standing wave and aerodynamic effects. - **Surface Roughness / Hardness** ‚Äî Softer road surfaces deform more ‚Üí **higher $C_{rr}$**. - **Temperature** ‚Äî Hysteresis losses vary with tire temperature (an optimum exists). - **Wheel Diameter** ‚Äî Larger diameter reduces the deformation per unit arc length ‚Üí **lower $C_{rr}$**. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **All formulas you will need for KTU Module 1 problems on Rolling Resistance:** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $F_r = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g$ | Rolling resistance force on level road | **N** | | 2 | $F_r = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta)$ | Rolling resistance on a slope of angle $\theta$ | **N** | | 3 | $F_r = N \cdot \frac{a}{r}$ | Force from the offset-of-normal-force model | **N** | | 4 | $C_{rr} = \dfrac{a}{r}$ | Definition of coefficient of rolling resistance | dimensionless | | 5 | $P_r = F_r \cdot v = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot v$ | Power lost to rolling resistance at speed $v$ | **W (J/s)** | | 6 | $E_r = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot d$ | Energy lost over distance $d$ | **J** | | 7 | $a = C_{rr} \cdot r$ | Forward shift of resultant normal force | **m** | > **Where:** > - $m$ = vehicle mass (kg) > - $g$ = 9.81 m/s¬≤ (gravitational acceleration) > - $v$ = vehicle speed (m/s) > - $r$ = effective tire radius (m) > - $a$ = offset of the normal reaction (m) > - $\theta$ = road gradient angle > - $N$ = normal load on the wheel (N) ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In modern **EV and HEV design**, rolling resistance directly determines the **battery energy consumed per kilometer**. A reduction in $C_{rr}$ from 0.015 to 0.008 (achievable with LRR tires) can extend the driving range of a typical EV by approximately **5‚Äì8%**, which is significant for the KTU Module 1 design-analysis questions on range extension strategies. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation #1: Rolling Resistance from the Offset-of-Normal-Force Model This is the **most important derivation** for KTU Module 1 and is a frequent 7-mark question in board exams. ### Setup Consider a driven wheel of radius $r$ rolling at constant velocity on a horizontal surface. The contact patch length is $\ell$, and the resultant vertical reaction $N$ acts at a distance $a$ ahead of the geometric center of the contact patch (i.e., in the direction of rolling). **Free-Body Diagram Notation:** - $F_r$ = tractive (propulsion) force applied at the axle in the rolling direction - $N$ = normal reaction from the ground - $W = mg$ = weight of the wheel-load - $F_x$ = horizontal friction force at the ground (neglected for pure rolling) - $a$ = horizontal offset of $N$ from the wheel center (forward) ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 ‚Äî Force equilibrium in the vertical direction:** The vertical forces must balance, so the normal reaction equals the weight on the wheel: $$\sum F_y = 0 \implies N - W = 0$$ $$N = W = mg$$ **Step 2 ‚Äî Moment equilibrium about the wheel center:** The wheel rolls at constant angular velocity, so the net moment about the wheel center is zero. The propulsion force $F_r$ acts at the axle (no moment arm from $F_r$ itself for translational motion) and the normal force $N$ is offset by distance $a$ forward, creating a retarding moment $N \cdot a$ about the center. $$\sum M_{\text{center}} = 0$$ $$F_r \cdot r - N \cdot a = 0$$ > *Explanation: $F_r$ applied at the axle produces a forward rolling torque $F_r \cdot r$ about the contact patch. The offset normal $N$ acting at distance $a$ ahead of center produces an opposing moment $N \cdot a$. For steady rolling, these must balance.* **Step 3 ‚Äî Solve for $F_r$:** $$F_r \cdot r = N \cdot a$$ $$F_r = \frac{N \cdot a}{r}$$ **Step 4 ‚Äî Substitute $N = W = mg$:** $$F_r = \frac{a}{r} \cdot W = \frac{a}{r} \cdot mg$$ **Step 5 ‚Äî Define the coefficient of rolling resistance:** Define the dimensionless ratio: $$C_{rr} \equiv \frac{a}{r}$$ **Step 6 ‚Äî Final compact form:** $$\boxed{F_r = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g}$$ > **For a road inclined at angle $\theta$**, the normal component of weight is $W \cos(\theta) = mg \cos(\theta)$, so: $$F_r = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta)$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation #2: Power and Energy Lost to Rolling Resistance ### Setup A vehicle of mass $m$ travels at constant speed $v$ (m/s) on a level road. The rolling resistance force is $F_r = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 ‚Äî Definition of power:** Power is the rate at which the traction system does work against $F_r$: $$P_r = F_r \cdot v$$ **Step 2 ‚Äî Substitute the rolling resistance force:** $$P_r = (C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g) \cdot v$$ **Step 3 ‚Äî Result (in Watts, if SI units are used):** $$\boxed{P_r = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot v \quad \text{[W]}}$$ **Step 4 ‚Äî Energy lost over a distance $d$:** Since $P_r = \dfrac{dE_r}{dt}$ and $v = \dfrac{dx}{dt}$: $$\frac{dE_r}{dx} = \frac{P_r}{v} = F_r = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g$$ Integrating over distance $d$: $$\boxed{E_r = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot d \quad \text{[J]}}$$ > **Engineering Insight:** Notice that rolling resistance losses are **independent of speed** (the *force* is constant) ‚Äî but the *power* dissipated grows linearly with speed. This is why **aerodynamic drag** (which grows as $v^3$ in power) dominates at high speeds, but **rolling resistance** dominates in city driving cycles (low to medium speeds) ‚Äî a critical point in EV/HEV range calculations. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Board Style) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem:** A 1500 kg passenger EV travels at a constant 20 m/s on a level road. The coefficient of rolling resistance is 0.012, and the coefficient of aerodynamic drag is 0.30 with a frontal area of 2.2 m¬≤. Air density is 1.2 kg/m¬≥. Find: > (a) The rolling resistance force. > (b) The power required to overcome rolling resistance. > (c) The total tractive power if the rolling + aerodynamic + climbing losses are all active on a 3% upgrade. **Solution:** **(a) Rolling resistance force:** $$F_r = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81$$ $$F_r = 176.58 \text{ N}$$ **(b) Power to overcome rolling resistance:** $$P_r = F_r \cdot v = 176.58 \times 20$$ $$P_r = 3531.6 \text{ W} \approx 3.53 \text{ kW}$$ **(c) Total tractive power on 3% upgrade:** Climbing force: $F_c = mg \sin(\theta)$, where $\sin(\theta) \approx 0.03$ for a 3% grade. $$F_c = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.03 = 441.45 \text{ N}$$ Aerodynamic drag force: $F_a = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$ $$F_a = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (20)^2 = 158.4 \text{ N}$$ Total tractive force: $$F_t = F_r + F_a + F_c = 176.58 + 158.4 + 441.45 = 776.43 \text{ N}$$ Total tractive power: $$P_t = F_t \cdot v = 776.43 \times 20 = 15528.6 \text{ W} \approx 15.53 \text{ kW}$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Free-Body Diagram of a Rolling Wheel (Mermaid Block-Level) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["WEIGHT W = m g
Acts downward at wheel center"] nodeB["NORMAL FORCE N
Acts upward, offset by distance a
from geometric center of contact"] nodeC["ROLLING RESISTANCE FORCE F r
Acts at axle, opposes motion"] nodeD["WHEEL CENTER
Radius = r"] nodeE["CONTACT PATCH
Length = ell
Center of pressure shifted forward"] nodeF["PROPULSION TORQUE T
Applied at axle by motor"] nodeA --> nodeD nodeB --> nodeE nodeD --> nodeC nodeE --> nodeD nodeF --> nodeD subgraph MOMENT_BALANCE nodeM["MOMENT EQUILIBRIUM
F r times r = N times a
C rr = a divided by r"] end nodeM --- nodeD nodeM --- nodeE ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram ‚Äî Factors Influencing Rolling Resistance ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["ROLLING RESISTANCE FORCE F r"] --> nodeB["Total Vehicle Resistance"] nodeC["Tire Inflation Pressure"] --> nodeA nodeD["Tire Material and Tread"] --> nodeA nodeE["Vehicle Speed v"] --> nodeA nodeF["Road Surface Hardness"] --> nodeA nodeG["Wheel Diameter 2 r"] --> nodeA nodeH["Ambient Temperature"] --> nodeA nodeI["Vehicle Mass m"] --> nodeA nodeJ["Road Gradient theta"] --> nodeA nodeB --> nodeK["Tractive Power Required
P = F r times v"] nodeK --> nodeL["EV Battery Energy Consumption
per km of travel"] ``` ## 4.3 Schematic ‚Äî Asymmetric Pressure Distribution Inside a Rolling Tire (Block-Level) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["TIRE ENTERS CONTACT ZONE
Compression phase
Energy STORED in rubber"] --> nodeB["HIGH PRESSURE ZONE
at the leading edge
of contact patch"] nodeB --> nodeC["RESULTANT NORMAL FORCE N
shifted forward by distance a"] nodeC --> nodeD["RETARDING MOMENT
M = N times a
about wheel center"] nodeD --> nodeE["FORCE F r REQUIRED AT AXLE
to maintain constant speed"] nodeF["TIRE LEAVES CONTACT ZONE
Recovery phase
Energy RETURNED less than stored"] --> nodeB nodeG["HYSTERESIS LOSS
Energy dissipated as heat
inside the rubber compound"] --> nodeF ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The pressure-distribution block diagram illustrates *why* the resultant normal force is offset by a small distance $a$ in the direction of rolling. The energy lost to **hysteresis** is the root physical cause ‚Äî not surface friction, as is often mistakenly assumed by students. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023, Model Paper Q1]** > Define the term **coefficient of rolling resistance**. List any **four factors** that affect it. **Model Answer:** The coefficient of rolling resistance ($C_{rr}$) is defined as the ratio of the rolling resistance force to the normal load on the wheel: $$C_{rr} = \frac{F_r}{N} = \frac{a}{r}$$ where $a$ is the forward offset of the normal reaction in the contact patch and $r$ is the effective wheel radius. It is **dimensionless** and typically ranges from 0.001 (railroad) to 0.015 (car tire on asphalt). **Four factors affecting $C_{rr}$:** 1. Tire inflation pressure (lower pressure ‚Üí higher $C_{rr}$) 2. Tire material / compound (more viscoelastic ‚Üí higher $C_{rr}$) 3. Road surface hardness (softer road ‚Üí higher $C_{rr}$) 4. Vehicle speed (increases at high speeds due to standing-wave effects) --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Jun 2024, Model Paper Q2]** > Differentiate between **rolling resistance** and **aerodynamic drag** in terms of their dependence on vehicle speed. **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Rolling Resistance $F_r$ | Aerodynamic Drag $F_a$ | |---|---|---| | Force dependence on speed | **Independent** of speed | $\propto v^2$ | | Power dependence on speed | $P_r \propto v$ | $P_a \propto v^3$ | | Dominant regime | City / low-speed driving | Highway / high-speed driving | | Primary cause | Tire viscoelastic hysteresis | Air turbulence around vehicle body | At low speeds, **rolling resistance** dominates the total tractive demand. At high speeds (typically above 60‚Äì80 km/h for an EV), **aerodynamic drag** becomes the dominant term. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ‚Äî Module Internal Choice ### **Question A** (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023, Model Paper Q8]** > **(a)** Derive an expression for the **rolling resistance force** experienced by a pneumatic tire rolling on a level road, starting from the offset-of-normal-force model. Clearly define the **coefficient of rolling resistance** and state its typical value for a passenger car. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** An electric vehicle of mass **1800 kg** (including battery and passengers) is cruising at a constant speed of **90 km/h** on a level road. The coefficient of rolling resistance is **0.013**, the coefficient of aerodynamic drag is **0.28**, the frontal area is **2.4 m¬≤**, and air density is **1.225 kg/m¬≥**. Calculate: > - (i) The rolling resistance force > - (ii) The aerodynamic drag force > - (iii) The total tractive power required at the wheels **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) ‚Äî Derivation:** Refer to **Section 3.1** above for the complete step-by-step derivation. Summary of result: $$\boxed{F_r = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g, \quad \text{where } C_{rr} = \frac{a}{r}}$$ Typical value for a passenger car on asphalt: **$C_{rr} \approx 0.010$ to $0.015$** (use $C_{rr} = 0.015$ as standard). **Part (b) ‚Äî Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $m = 1800$ kg, $v = 90$ km/h $= 25$ m/s, $C_{rr} = 0.013$, $C_d = 0.28$, $A = 2.4$ m¬≤, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m¬≥, $g = 9.81$ m/s¬≤. **(i) Rolling resistance force:** $$F_r = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g = 0.013 \times 1800 \times 9.81$$ $$F_r = 229.55 \text{ N} \approx 230 \text{ N}$$ **(ii) Aerodynamic drag force:** $$F_a = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$$ $$F_a = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.4 \times (25)^2$$ $$F_a = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.4 \times 625$$ $$F_a = 257.25 \text{ N} \approx 257.3 \text{ N}$$ **(iii) Total tractive power at the wheels:** Total tractive force (level road, no grade): $$F_t = F_r + F_a = 229.55 + 257.25 = 486.8 \text{ N}$$ Total tractive power: $$P_t = F_t \cdot v = 486.8 \times 25$$ $$\boxed{P_t = 12170 \text{ W} \approx 12.17 \text{ kW}}$$ --- ### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Jun 2024, Model Paper Q9]** > **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **physical origin of rolling resistance** in a pneumatic tire. Discuss the role of **hysteresis** in its generation. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** A 1200 kg hybrid vehicle climbs a uniform slope of **5%** at a constant speed of **54 km/h**. The coefficient of rolling resistance is **0.010**, and $g = 9.81$ m/s¬≤. Calculate: > - (i) The rolling resistance force > - (ii) The climbing (grade) resistance force > - (iii) The power required to overcome **only the rolling and climbing resistance** combined **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) ‚Äî Physical Origin:** Refer to **Sections 1.2 and 4.3** above. Key points to mention in the answer: - A pneumatic tire is **viscoelastic** (combination of viscous and elastic behavior). - As the tire enters the contact zone, the rubber **deforms and stores elastic energy** ($E_{\text{stored}}$). - As the tire leaves the contact zone, the rubber **recovers and releases less energy** ($E_{\text{recovered}}$). - The difference $\Delta E = E_{\text{stored}} - E_{\text{recovered}}$ is the **hysteresis loss**, dissipated as **heat** within the rubber compound. - This asymmetric energy storage‚Äìrelease cycle causes the resultant normal force to be **shifted forward by a small distance $a$** in the direction of rolling, creating a net retarding moment. - The retarding moment must be continuously overcome by the propulsion force ‚Üí this is the **rolling resistance**. **Part (b) ‚Äî Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $m = 1200$ kg, slope $= 5\% \implies \tan(\theta) = 0.05$, $v = 54$ km/h $= 15$ m/s, $C_{rr} = 0.010$, $g = 9.81$ m/s¬≤. **Step 1 ‚Äî Slope angle:** For small angles, $\theta = \arctan(0.05) = 2.86¬∞$. Approximate (acceptable for KTU 2024): $\sin(\theta) \approx \tan(\theta) = 0.05$, $\cos(\theta) \approx 0.9987 \approx 1$. **(i) Rolling resistance force (on a slope):** $$F_r = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta)$$ $$F_r = 0.010 \times 1200 \times 9.81 \times \cos(2.86¬∞)$$ $$F_r = 0.010 \times 1200 \times 9.81 \times 0.9987$$ $$F_r = 117.56 \text{ N} \approx 117.6 \text{ N}$$ **(ii) Climbing (grade) resistance force:** $$F_c = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta) = 1200 \times 9.81 \times \sin(2.86¬∞)$$ $$F_c = 1200 \times 9.81 \times 0.0499$$ $$F_c = 587.3 \text{ N} \approx 587.4 \text{ N}$$ > *Equivalently, using $\sin(\theta) \approx \tan(\theta) = 0.05$:* $F_c = 1200 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 = 588.6$ N (very close ‚Äî both are accepted). **(iii) Combined power (rolling + climbing only):** $$F_{\text{combined}} = F_r + F_c = 117.56 + 587.3 = 704.86 \text{ N}$$ $$P_{\text{combined}} = F_{\text{combined}} \cdot v = 704.86 \times 15$$ $$\boxed{P_{\text{combined}} = 10572.9 \text{ W} \approx 10.57 \text{ kW}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Unit conversion trap:** A common 1‚Äì2 mark loss happens when students forget to convert km/h to m/s when computing power ($P = F \cdot v$). Always check: $v$ in m/s for power in Watts. > 2. **Slope formula trap:** On a slope, students often incorrectly use $F_r = C_{rr} \cdot mg$ instead of $C_{rr} \cdot mg \cos(\theta)$. The normal load on a slope is **reduced** by the factor $\cos(\theta)$ ‚Äî remember to include it. > 3. **Defining $C_{rr}$:** Examiners **deduct marks** if the student writes $C_{rr} = F_r / F_a$ or any other wrong ratio. The correct definition is $C_{rr} = a / r = F_r / N$. > 4. **Speed dependence:** Some students wrongly state that rolling resistance "increases with $v^2$" like aerodynamic drag. Rolling resistance force is **independent of speed** (at low‚Äìmoderate $v$); only its *power* is linear in $v$. > 5. **Hysteresis vs Friction:** Rolling resistance is **NOT** due to surface friction between tire and road (the contact patch is essentially stationary ‚Äî pure rolling). It is due to **internal material hysteresis**. Examiners check this distinction. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚Äî Rolling Resistance (KTU Module 1):** > > - ‚úÖ **Definition:** Rolling resistance is the retarding force due to **viscoelastic hysteresis** in the tire. > - ‚úÖ **Master Formula:** $F_r = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g$ (level) ; $F_r = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta)$ (slope). > - ‚úÖ **Coefficient Definition:** $C_{rr} = a / r$ (dimensionless, typically 0.005‚Äì0.015 for cars). > - ‚úÖ **Power:** $P_r = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot v$ (linear in $v$). > - ‚úÖ **Energy:** $E_r = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot d$ (linear in distance $d$). > - ‚úÖ **Key Insight:** Rolling resistance force is **speed-independent**; aerodynamic drag is $\propto v^2$ (power $\propto v^3$). > - ‚úÖ **Low Rolling Resistance (LRR) tires** can extend EV range by 5‚Äì8% ‚Äî high-yield KTU 2024 application point. > - ‚úÖ **Higher inflation pressure** ‚Üí smaller contact patch ‚Üí lower $C_{rr}$. > - ‚úÖ **Larger wheel diameter** ‚Üí smaller deformation per revolution ‚Üí lower $C_{rr}$. > - ‚úÖ **Hysteresis** is the root cause, NOT surface friction. > - ‚úÖ **Standard value used in KTU problems:** $C_{rr} = 0.015$ unless otherwise stated.

topics.title: Grading Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Grading Resistance # ‚öôÔ∏è MODULE 1 ‚Äî VEHICLE DYNAMICS FUNDAMENTALS ## Topic: Grading Resistance > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Anchor (KTU 2024 ‚Äî OEEET722):** *Vehicle dynamics fundamentals ‚Äî Tractive effort, total tractive resistance, grading resistance, rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, inertia, and vehicle performance characteristics for conventional, hybrid, and electric powertrains.* --- ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Board Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Grading Resistance (F_g):** The component of a vehicle's gravitational weight that acts **parallel to the road surface** in the direction opposing the vehicle's motion when the vehicle traverses a slope (incline or decline). It represents the additional force the powertrain must overcome (on an upgrade) or the force the brakes must absorb (on a downgrade) due to road gradient. Mathematically, for a vehicle of gross mass $M$ (kg) moving on a road of slope angle $\alpha$: $$F_g = M \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha)$$ where $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ is the **acceleration due to gravity** and $\alpha$ is the **angle of inclination** of the road with respect to the horizontal. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî "The Hill-Climb Intuition" Imagine you are pushing a loaded shopping trolley up the ramp of a parking garage. The ramp is tilted by some angle. You can feel a force pulling the trolley *backward* (down the slope) ‚Äî that is **gravity's parallel component** trying to roll the trolley back down. To keep it moving up, you must push *harder* than you would on flat ground. The extra push you need is exactly the **Grading Resistance**. Similarly, in an EV or HEV, when the driver presses the accelerator on a hill, the motor must supply additional torque to defeat this gravity component. This extra force requirement: - **Reduces range** in Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs). - **Increases fuel consumption** in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs). - **Demands higher peak motor torque** during design. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants Used Throughout This Note:** > - Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ > - Small-angle approximation valid for $\alpha \le 10¬∞$ (typical road grade $\le 10\%$) > - Standard road-grade convention: *positive grade = upgrade* --- ### 1.3 Role of Grading Resistance in the Total Tractive Equation (Big Picture) In the canonical KTU vehicle-dynamics model, the **Tractive Effort (F_t)** required at the wheels must balance the **Total Resistance (F_R)**: $$F_t = F_R = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_g + F_i + F_{j}$$ Where: | Symbol | Resistance Term | |:------:|:----------------| | $F_{rr}$ | Rolling Resistance | | $F_{ad}$ | Aerodynamic Drag | | $F_g$ | **Grading Resistance** (this topic) | | $F_i$ | Inertia (Mass √ó Acceleration) | | $F_j$ | Rotational inertia of drivetrain components | **Grading Resistance is the only term that depends on road geometry** ‚Äî all others depend on speed, vehicle mass, or aerodynamic shape. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Force Decomposition on an Inclined Plane When a vehicle of mass $M$ rests on a road inclined at angle $\alpha$, its weight $W = M \cdot g$ acts **vertically downward** through the center of gravity. This weight vector can be resolved into two orthogonal components relative to the road surface: **Step 1 ‚Äî Identify the weight vector.** The weight vector $W = M g$ points straight down toward the Earth's center. **Step 2 ‚Äî Resolve into slope-parallel and slope-perpendicular components.** - Component **parallel to slope** (pointing down the incline): $W \cdot \sin(\alpha) = M g \sin(\alpha)$ - Component **perpendicular to slope** (pressing into the road): $W \cdot \cos(\alpha) = M g \cos(\alpha)$ **Step 3 ‚Äî Define grading resistance.** The slope-parallel component that opposes forward motion (during an upgrade) is defined as the **Grading Resistance**: $$F_g = M \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha)$$ **Step 4 ‚Äî Recognize the perpendicular component's role.** The perpendicular component $M g \cos(\alpha)$ reduces the **normal reaction force** $N$ on the tires, which in turn reduces rolling resistance (since $F_{rr} = \mu_r \cdot N$). On very steep grades, this becomes a significant secondary effect. --- ### 2.2 Percentage Grade vs. Angle of Inclination Civil engineers and road-design codes specify gradients as **percentage grade (% grade)** rather than degrees. The conversion is: $$\text{Grade (\%)} = \frac{\text{Vertical Rise}}{\text{Horizontal Run}} \times 100 = 100 \cdot \tan(\alpha)$$ Inverting for the angle: $$\alpha = \arctan\left(\frac{\text{Grade (\%)}}{100}\right)$$ For small angles ($\alpha \le 10¬∞$, i.e., grade $\le 17.6\%$), the following approximations hold: $$\sin(\alpha) \approx \tan(\alpha) \approx \alpha_{\text{rad}} = \frac{\pi \alpha_{\text{deg}}}{180}$$ This yields the **most-used KTU engineering formula**: $$\boxed{F_g \approx M \cdot g \cdot \tan(\alpha) \approx M \cdot g \cdot \frac{\text{Grade (\%)}}{100} = 0.01 \cdot M \cdot g \cdot (\text{Grade \%})}$$ --- ### 2.3 Sign Convention (KTU Convention) | Scenario | Sign of $F_g$ | Physical Meaning | |:---------|:-------------:|:-----------------| | **Upgrade** (positive grade) | $F_g > 0$ | Opposes motion; motor must overcome | | **Downgrade** (negative grade) | $F_g < 0$ | Assists motion; brakes must dissipate energy | | **Level road** (0% grade) | $F_g = 0$ | No contribution | > [!IMPORTANT] > In the KTU model, **all resistances are summed as positive magnitudes in the direction opposing motion**, while $F_g$ may be either added (upgrade) or subtracted (downgrade) depending on slope sign. --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master these expressions ‚Äî they appear in nearly every Part-B vehicle-dynamics problem on KTU exams.** | # | Formula | Description | Typical Units | |:-:|:--------|:------------|:--------------| | 1 | $F_g = M g \sin(\alpha)$ | **Exact** grading resistance | N | | 2 | $F_g \approx M g \tan(\alpha)$ | Small-angle approximation (KTU preferred) | N | | 3 | $F_g = 0.01 \cdot M g \cdot (\%G)$ | Direct from % grade | N | | 4 | $\alpha = \arctan(\%G/100)$ | Angle from % grade | radians (convert to ¬∞) | | 5 | $P_g = F_g \cdot v$ | Grade-climbing power requirement | W | | 6 | $E_g = M g \sin(\alpha) \cdot d$ | Grade-climbing energy over distance $d$ | J | | 7 | $\cos(\alpha) = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2(\alpha)}$ | Normal force reduction factor | dimensionless | | 8 | $N = M g \cos(\alpha)$ | Normal reaction on slope | N | | 9 | $C_g = \sin(\alpha) \approx \%G/100$ | **Grade coefficient** (dimensionless) | ‚Äî | | 10 | $F_{g,\text{net}} = M g (\sin\alpha + C_r \cos\alpha)$ | Combined grade + rolling resistance | N | --- ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **EV Range Estimation on Hilly Terrain:** Tesla, BYD, and Tata Motors use $F_g$ models in their onboard range predictors. A 5% sustained grade can reduce EV range by **15‚Äì25%** compared to flat-road driving. 2. **Hybrid Control Strategy:** Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive uses grade information (from GPS + barometric sensor) to pre-charge the HV battery before anticipated climbs, ensuring adequate power margin for $F_g$. 3. **Highway Design (IRC Standards in India):** Maximum grade on NH/SH is capped at **4‚Äì6%** in plains and **6‚Äì7%** in hilly terrain ‚Äî a direct consequence of $F_g$ dictating truck/climb performance. 4. **Regenerative Braking Limits on Downgrade:** $F_g$ (with negative sign) is the "free energy" source; the regen system must limit negative torque to maintain brake-feel and stability. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation I ‚Äî Exact Grading Resistance from Newton's Second Law **Starting Point ‚Äî Free-Body Diagram on an Incline:** Consider a vehicle of mass $M$ on a road inclined at angle $\alpha$ above the horizontal. The vehicle moves up the slope at constant velocity $v$ (steady-state, no acceleration). **Step 1:** Identify all forces acting on the vehicle along the direction of motion (parallel to slope): - Tractive effort $F_t$ (forward, up the slope) - Grading resistance $F_g$ (backward, down the slope) - Rolling resistance $F_{rr}$ (backward) - Aerodynamic drag $F_{ad}$ (backward) **Step 2:** Apply Newton's Second Law in the slope-parallel direction at steady state (no acceleration, so $\sum F = 0$): $$F_t - F_g - F_{rr} - F_{ad} = 0$$ **Step 3:** Isolate the grading-resistance term for analytical clarity. The weight $W = M g$ acts vertically downward. Its component parallel to the slope (pointing down the incline) is found by projecting $W$ onto the slope-parallel unit vector. The slope-parallel unit vector (pointing up the slope) is: $$\hat{u}_{\parallel} = \cos(\alpha)\,\hat{i} + \sin(\alpha)\,\hat{j}$$ The weight vector is: $$\vec{W} = -M g \,\hat{j}$$ **Step 4:** Compute the projection (dot product): $$\vec{W} \cdot \hat{u}_{\parallel} = -M g \cdot \sin(\alpha)$$ **Step 5:** The grading resistance is the magnitude of this component (opposing the up-slope direction): $$\boxed{F_g = M \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha)}$$ **Step 6:** Verify dimensions: $[M] = \text{kg}$, $[g] = \text{m/s}^2$, $[\sin\alpha] = \text{dimensionless}$ $$[F_g] = \text{kg} \cdot \frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2} = \text{N} \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 7:** Boundary check: - If $\alpha = 0¬∞$: $F_g = 0$ (level road) ‚úì - If $\alpha = 90¬∞$: $F_g = M g$ (vertical wall ‚Äî entire weight pulls down) ‚úì - If $\alpha = 30¬∞$: $F_g = 0.5 M g$ (half the weight acts along the slope) ‚úì --- ### 3.2 Derivation II ‚Äî Grading Resistance in Terms of Percentage Grade **Step 1:** From civil engineering definition: $$\text{Grade (\%)} = 100 \cdot \tan(\alpha)$$ **Step 2:** Solve for $\tan(\alpha)$: $$\tan(\alpha) = \frac{\text{Grade (\%)}}{100} = \frac{\%G}{100}$$ **Step 3:** Apply the small-angle approximation $\sin(\alpha) \approx \tan(\alpha)$ (valid for $\alpha \le 10¬∞$ i.e., grade $\le 17.6\%$): $$F_g = M g \sin(\alpha) \approx M g \tan(\alpha)$$ **Step 4:** Substitute: $$\boxed{F_g \approx M \cdot g \cdot \frac{\%G}{100} = \frac{M \cdot g \cdot \%G}{100}}$$ **Step 5:** Engineering shortcut form (for a 1% grade): $$F_g = 0.01 \cdot M g \quad \text{per 1\% grade}$$ **Step 6:** Numerical example ‚Äî A 1500 kg EV on a 5% grade: $$F_g = 0.01 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times 5 = 735.75 \text{ N}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation III ‚Äî Grading Power & Energy Requirements **Step 1:** Mechanical power required to overcome grade resistance at constant speed $v$: $$P_g = F_g \cdot v = M g \sin(\alpha) \cdot v$$ **Step 2:** Energy required to climb a distance $d$ up the slope: $$E_g = F_g \cdot d = M g \sin(\alpha) \cdot d$$ **Step 3:** Alternatively, using the **vertical climb height** $h = d \cdot \sin(\alpha)$: $$E_g = M g h$$ This is the classic **gravitational potential energy** equation ‚Äî independent of the path taken. **Step 4:** Practical unit conversions: - Energy in Wh: $E_g [\text{Wh}] = \dfrac{M g h}{3600}$ - Energy in kWh: $E_g [\text{kWh}] = \dfrac{M g h}{3.6 \times 10^6}$ **Step 5:** Numerical sanity check ‚Äî A 1500 kg EV climbing 100 m vertically: $$E_g = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 100 = 1.4715 \times 10^6 \text{ J} = 0.4088 \text{ kWh}$$ A typical 60 kWh EV battery could deliver this climb about **147 times** before depletion ‚Äî illustrating how gravity is a "small" energy reservoir compared to rolling/aero losses on long drives. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Effect of Grade on Resistances Let $M = 1500$ kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s¬≤, $C_r = 0.012$ (typical car tire). | Grade % | $\alpha$ (¬∞) | $F_g$ (N) | $F_{rr}$ (N) | $F_g / W$ (%) | |:-------:|:------------:|:---------:|:------------:|:-------------:| | 0% | 0.00¬∞ | 0.00 | 176.6 | 0.00% | | 2% | 1.15¬∞ | 294.2 | 176.3 | 2.00% | | 5% | 2.86¬∞ | 735.8 | 175.6 | 5.00% | | 10% | 5.71¬∞ | 1471.5 | 173.0 | 10.00% | | 20% | 11.31¬∞ | 2943.0 | 165.5 | 20.00% | | 30% | 16.70¬∞ | 4414.5 | 153.6 | 30.00% | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** Grading resistance grows **linearly with % grade**, while rolling resistance decreases only slightly (because $\cos\alpha < 1$). On steep grades, $F_g$ is the **dominant resistance** by an order of magnitude. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚Äî Free-Body Analysis on a Slope ```mermaid graph TD V1["VEHICLE OF MASS M
WEIGHT W = M.g
ACTING VERTICALLY DOWN"]:::vehicle V2["ROAD SURFACE
INCLINED AT ANGLE alpha
PERCENTAGE GRADE = G%"]:::road V3["FORCE DECOMPOSITION
F_g = M.g.sin alpha
DOWN-SLOPE COMPONENT"]:::fgrade V4["NORMAL COMPONENT
N = M.g.cos alpha
PRESSES INTO ROAD"]:::normal V5["F_rr = C_r . N
ROLLING RESISTANCE
OPPOSES MOTION"]:::froll V6["F_ad = 0.5 . rho . Cd . A . v^2
AERODYNAMIC DRAG"]:::faero V7["F_t REQUIRED FROM MOTOR
F_t = F_g + F_rr + F_ad"]:::tractive V8["BATTERY / FUEL
ENERGY SOURCE"]:::battery V1 -->|Weight Vector| V3 V1 -->|Weight Vector| V4 V3 -->|Opposes motion| V7 V4 -->|Determines N| V5 V5 -->|Opposes motion| V7 V6 -->|Opposes motion| V7 V8 -->|Supplies power| V7 classDef vehicle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef road fill:#D2B48C,stroke:#654321,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef fgrade fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef normal fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#006666,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef froll fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#997A00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef faero fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#006666,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef tractive fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef battery fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚Äî Grade Resistance in the Powertrain Chain ```mermaid graph LR A["DRIVER
ACCELERATOR INPUT"]:::driver B["VEHICLE CONTROL UNIT
VCU / ECU"]:::control C["POWER REQUEST
TORQUE DEMAND"]:::request D["ENERGY STORAGE
BATTERY / FUEL CELL"]:::storage E["MOTOR / ENGINE
DELIVERS TRACTIVE EFFORT"]:::motor F["TRANSMISSION &
DIFFERENTIAL"]:::trans G["WHEELS
APPLY F_t TO ROAD"]:::wheel H["GRADING RESISTANCE
F_g = M.g.sin alpha"]:::grade I["ROLLING RESISTANCE
F_rr = C_r.M.g.cos alpha"]:::roll J["AERODYNAMIC DRAG
F_ad = 0.5.rho.Cd.A.v^2"]:::aero K["NET MOTION
v = CONSTANT OR dv/dt"]:::motion A --> B B --> C C --> E D --> E E --> F F --> G G -->|Must overcome| H G -->|Must overcome| I G -->|Must overcome| J H --> K I --> K J --> K classDef driver fill:#E0BBE4,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef control fill:#957DAD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef request fill:#D291BC,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef storage fill:#FEC8D8,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef motor fill:#FFDFD3,stroke:#CC5500,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef trans fill:#FEC8D8,stroke:#800020,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef wheel fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef grade fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF classDef roll fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#997A00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef aero fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#006666,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef motion fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚Äî Sign Convention Flowchart ```mermaid graph TD S1{"ROAD GRADIENT
DETECTED"}:::detect S2["alpha > 0
UPGRADE"]:::up S3["alpha = 0
LEVEL ROAD"]:::level S4["alpha < 0
DOWNGRADE"]:::down S5["F_g POSITIVE
MOTOR MUST OVERCOME"]:::pos S6["F_g ZERO
NO GRADE TERM"]:::zero S7["F_g NEGATIVE
BRAKING OR REGEN
RECOVERS ENERGY"]:::neg S1 -->|GPS MAP DATA| S2 S1 -->|IMU SENSOR| S3 S1 -->|NEGATIVE SLOPE| S4 S2 --> S5 S3 --> S6 S4 --> S7 classDef detect fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef up fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef level fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#006666,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef down fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef pos fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef zero fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#006666,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef neg fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The red-highlighted node "GRADING RESISTANCE $F_g$" is the focal point of this topic. In the actual physical free-body diagram, the **weight vector** $W = Mg$ points straight down, while the **grade force** $F_g$ is its projection along the slope (down-slope direction). The angle $\alpha$ is measured between the road surface and the horizontal. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### üìù PART A ‚Äî Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. Define grading resistance. Derive its expression for a vehicle moving on an inclined road.** *`[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023, OEEET722]`* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Grading resistance is the component of the vehicle's weight that acts parallel to the road surface in the direction opposing motion, when the vehicle travels on a sloped road. **Derivation (2 marks):** For a vehicle of mass $M$ on a road inclined at angle $\alpha$, the weight $W = Mg$ acts vertically downward. Resolving it parallel to the slope: $$F_g = M g \sin(\alpha)$$ For small angles, $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha_{\text{rad}}$: $$F_g \approx M g \tan(\alpha) = 0.01 \cdot M g \cdot (\%G)$$ --- #### **Q2. How does the percentage grade of a road relate to the angle of inclination? State the small-angle approximation used for grading resistance calculations.** *`[KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024, OEEET722]`* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The percentage grade is defined as: $$\%G = 100 \cdot \tan(\alpha) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \arctan(\%G / 100)$$ **Small-angle approximation (KTU standard):** For $\alpha \le 10¬∞$ (i.e., grade $\le 17.6\%$): $$\sin(\alpha) \approx \tan(\alpha) \approx \alpha \text{ (in radians)}$$ This yields the simplified form: $F_g \approx 0.01 \cdot M g \cdot (\%G)$. --- ### üìù PART B ‚Äî Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) #### **Question A: Derivation + Numerical** *`[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Model Paper, OEEET722]`* **(a) Derive the expression for the grading resistance experienced by a vehicle of mass $M$ moving up a road of slope $\alpha$. State the assumptions made. (7 marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 ‚Äî Free Body Diagram (1 mark):** Draw a vehicle of mass $M$ on a road inclined at angle $\alpha$ to the horizontal. Show the weight vector $W = Mg$ acting vertically downward through the center of gravity. **Step 2 ‚Äî Force Resolution (2 marks):** Resolve the weight into two components: - Along the slope (downward direction): $W \sin(\alpha) = M g \sin(\alpha)$ - Perpendicular to the slope: $W \cos(\alpha) = M g \cos(\alpha)$ **Step 3 ‚Äî Apply Newton's Second Law (2 marks):** For the vehicle moving up the slope at constant velocity, sum of forces parallel to the slope equals zero: $$F_t - F_g - F_{rr} - F_{ad} = 0$$ The grading resistance term: $$F_g = M g \sin(\alpha)$$ **Step 4 ‚Äî Assumptions (1 mark):** 1. Road is a straight, rigid plane inclined at constant angle $\alpha$. 2. Vehicle moves at constant velocity (no acceleration). 3. Air resistance and rolling resistance are neglected (treated separately). 4. Center of gravity lies on the road centerline. **Step 5 ‚Äî Engineering Form (1 mark):** Using the small-angle approximation and percentage grade: $$F_g = 0.01 \cdot M g \cdot (\%G) \quad \text{where } \%G = 100 \tan(\alpha)$$ --- **(b) A passenger car of mass 1800 kg climbs a 6% grade at a constant speed of 60 km/h. Calculate: (i) the grading resistance, (ii) the grade-climbing power, and (iii) the energy required per km. (7 marks)** **Given:** - $M = 1800$ kg - Grade = 6% - $v = 60$ km/h $= 16.667$ m/s - $g = 9.81$ m/s¬≤ **Step (i) ‚Äî Grading Resistance (2 marks):** $$F_g = 0.01 \cdot M \cdot g \cdot (\%G) = 0.01 \times 1800 \times 9.81 \times 6$$ $$F_g = 1059.48 \text{ N} \approx 1059.5 \text{ N}$$ **Step (ii) ‚Äî Grade-Climbing Power (2 marks):** $$P_g = F_g \cdot v = 1059.48 \times 16.667$$ $$P_g = 17{,}658 \text{ W} \approx 17.66 \text{ kW}$$ **Step (iii) ‚Äî Energy per km (3 marks):** Distance $d = 1$ km $= 1000$ m $$E_g = F_g \cdot d = 1059.48 \times 1000 = 1.059 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$$ Convert to kWh: $$E_g = \frac{1.059 \times 10^6}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 0.2942 \text{ kWh per km}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The car needs about **0.29 kWh per km** *just to fight gravity*. A typical EV consumes 0.15‚Äì0.20 kWh/km on flat roads ‚Äî so this climb **doubles** the energy demand per km. --- #### **Question B: Alternative ‚Äî Conceptual + Numerical** *`[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Supplementary, OEEET722]`* **(a) Explain the significance of grading resistance in the design of electric and hybrid vehicles. Discuss how the sign of grading resistance affects regenerative braking. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 ‚Äî Definition Recap (1 mark):** Grading resistance is $F_g = M g \sin(\alpha)$ ‚Äî the gravity component along the slope. **Step 2 ‚Äî Significance for EV/HEV Design (3 marks):** - **Battery sizing:** On mountainous routes, designers must size the battery to sustain continuous grade-climbing without thermal overload. - **Motor torque:** The traction motor must deliver peak torque sufficient to overcome worst-case $F_g$ + $F_{rr} + F_{ad}$ on the steepest expected grade. - **Thermal management:** Sustained uphill draws high current ‚Üí battery heating ‚Üí cooling system design. - **Drive-cycle planning:** WLTP and Indian Drive Cycle (IDC) include grade profiles to simulate real-world loads. **Step 3 ‚Äî Effect of Sign on Regenerative Braking (3 marks):** - **Uphill ($F_g > 0$):** Grading resistance opposes motion ‚Äî motor draws energy from the battery. - **Downhill ($F_g < 0$):** Gravity *assists* motion ‚Äî the motor can act as a generator, recovering kinetic + potential energy. - **Regen limit:** On very steep downhills, $F_g$ can exceed the regen system's capacity ‚Üí friction brakes must engage to maintain safe speed and prevent motor-generator overspeed. --- **(b) An electric bus of mass 12,000 kg operates on a route that includes a 2 km stretch of 4% upgrade followed by a 1 km stretch of 8% downgrade. If the bus travels at a constant 40 km/h, compute: (i) the total grading-resistance force on the upgrade, (ii) the total energy consumed on the upgrade, and (iii) the energy recovered (assuming 60% regen efficiency) on the downgrade. (7 marks)** **Given:** - $M = 12{,}000$ kg - Upgrade: $d_1 = 2$ km, grade $G_1 = +4\%$ - Downgrade: $d_2 = 1$ km, grade $G_2 = -8\%$ - $v = 40$ km/h - $g = 9.81$ m/s¬≤ - Regen efficiency $\eta = 0.60$ **Step (i) ‚Äî Grading Resistance on Upgrade (2 marks):** $$F_{g1} = 0.01 \times 12{,}000 \times 9.81 \times 4 = 4708.8 \text{ N} \approx 4.71 \text{ kN}$$ **Step (ii) ‚Äî Energy Consumed on Upgrade (2 marks):** $$E_{\text{consumed}} = F_{g1} \cdot d_1 = 4708.8 \times 2000 = 9.4176 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$$ $$E_{\text{consumed}} = 2.616 \text{ kWh}$$ **Step (iii) ‚Äî Energy Recovered on Downgrade (3 marks):** Grading resistance on downgrade (now *assisting* motion): $$F_{g2} = 0.01 \times 12{,}000 \times 9.81 \times 8 = 9417.6 \text{ N}$$ Available gravitational energy on downgrade: $$E_{\text{down}} = F_{g2} \cdot d_2 = 9417.6 \times 1000 = 9.4176 \times 10^6 \text{ J} = 2.616 \text{ kWh}$$ Energy recovered (60% regen efficiency): $$E_{\text{recovered}} = \eta \times E_{\text{down}} = 0.60 \times 2.616 = 1.570 \text{ kWh}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Net Result:** The bus consumes 2.616 kWh uphill and recovers 1.570 kWh downhill ‚Üí **net 1.046 kWh** per cycle. This illustrates why hilly routes still favor hybrids over pure EVs (in the absence of large batteries). --- > [!WARNING] > **üö® KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Common Pitfalls on Grading-Resistance Problems:** > 1. **Unit mismatch:** $v$ must be in **m/s** for power calculations in Watts. Students often plug in km/h directly, getting answers off by a factor of 3.6. > 2. **Angle vs. grade confusion:** Always use $\sin(\alpha)$ for the *exact* formula. For percentage grade, the form is $F_g = 0.01 \cdot M g \cdot \%G$, **not** $F_g = M g \cdot \%G$ (missing the /100). > 3. **Forgetting the small-angle approximation:** If a problem gives $\alpha = 30¬∞$, do **not** use the percentage-grade shortcut. Use $F_g = M g \sin(30¬∞) = 0.5 M g$ exactly. > 4. **Sign of grade:** On downgrades, the "energy recovered" calculation must include the regen efficiency factor.
