Many students write $E_{\text{recovered}} = F_g \cdot d$ without multiplying by $\eta$. > 5. **Mixing $M$ (mass) with $W$ (weight):** $F_g$ uses **mass** in kg, **not** weight in N. Substituting weight $W = Mg$ directly leads to $F_g = Mg^2 \sin\alpha$ ‚Äî a classic 3-mark loss. --- ### üßÝ TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Points to Memorize Before KTU Exam:** - ‚úÖ **Core formula:** $F_g = M g \sin(\alpha)$ ‚Äî exact; or $F_g = 0.01 \cdot M g \cdot (\%G)$ ‚Äî small-angle engineering form. - ‚úÖ **Percentage grade relation:** $\%G = 100 \tan(\alpha)$; $\alpha = \arctan(\%G/100)$. - ‚úÖ **Small-angle validity:** Approximation holds for $\alpha \le 10¬∞$ (grade $\le 17.6\%$). - ‚úÖ **Sign convention:** $F_g > 0$ on upgrade, $F_g < 0$ on downgrade. - ‚úÖ **Normal force on slope:** $N = M g \cos(\alpha)$ ‚Äî used in rolling resistance. - ‚úÖ **Grade-climbing power:** $P_g = F_g \cdot v$ (with $v$ in m/s). - ‚úÖ **Grade-climbing energy:** $E_g = M g h$ where $h$ is the vertical height gained. - ‚úÖ **Position in total resistance equation:** $F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_g + F_i$. - ‚úÖ **EV/HEV relevance:** $F_g$ governs battery sizing, motor torque rating, regen-braking design, and drive-cycle energy budgeting. - ‚úÖ **Boundary checks:** $F_g = 0$ at $\alpha = 0¬∞$; $F_g = Mg$ at $\alpha = 90¬∞$. - ‚úÖ **Energy unit conversions:** $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$. **One-Line Definition to Write in Exam:** > *"Grading resistance is the down-slope component of a vehicle's weight, given by $F_g = M g \sin(\alpha)$, which the powertrain must overcome during upgrades and which regenerative braking can partially recover on downgrades."*

topics.title: Vehicle Acceleration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Vehicle Acceleration # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) **Vehicle Acceleration** in the context of performance analysis of conventional, hybrid, and electric vehicles is defined as the rate of change of vehicle velocity with respect to time, governed by the **Fundamental Tractive Force Equation** of vehicle dynamics. It is derived from Newton's Second Law applied to a vehicle moving along a road with grade: > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition:** *The tractive force (F_t) required at the wheel-road interface must overcome the sum of four resistive forces: Rolling Resistance (F_rr), Aerodynamic Drag (F_ad), Hill Climbing Force (F_hc), and the Linear Acceleration Force (F_la). The ability of the powertrain to deliver this tractive force determines the vehicle's acceleration capability, which is a primary design specification for HEVs and EVs.* $$\boxed{F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}}$$ The resulting **acceleration** is given by: $$a = \frac{F_t - (F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc})}{m \cdot (1 + \delta)}$$ where $\delta$ is the **rotational inertia factor** (mass factor) that accounts for the equivalent mass of rotating drivetrain components (wheels, gears, motor rotor). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy ‚Äî "Pushing a Stroller Up a Slope"** > > Imagine you are pushing a loaded baby stroller up a slight hill on a windy day: > - **Weight of stroller + baby** ‚Üí analogous to **Vehicle Mass (m)** > - **Friction between wheels and rough road** ‚Üí analogous to **Rolling Resistance (F_rr)** > - **Wind pushing against you** ‚Üí analogous to **Aerodynamic Drag (F_ad)** > - **Height of the hill you must climb** ‚Üí analogous to **Hill Climbing Force (F_hc)** > - **The extra burst of force to make it go faster** ‚Üí analogous to **Acceleration Force (F_la)** > > Just as the force you apply on the handle equals the *sum* of all these opposing effects (plus the force needed to speed it up), the **tractive force from the EV motor must equal the sum of all resistances plus the acceleration force.** This is why EVs use high-torque motors from rest ‚Äî to overcome all four terms simultaneously during launch. --- ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit | Notes | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | $\rho$ | Air density | **1.2 kg/m¬≥** | At sea level, 25¬∞C | | $g$ | Gravitational acceleration | **9.81 m/s¬≤** | Standard earth gravity | | $C_d$ | Coefficient of drag | 0.25 ‚Äì 0.40 | Modern sedans ~ 0.25‚Äì0.30 | | $f$ | Coefficient of rolling resistance | 0.008 ‚Äì 0.015 | Car tire on asphalt ~ 0.01 | | $A_f$ | Frontal area | 1.7 ‚Äì 2.5 m¬≤ | Mid-size sedan ~ 2.0 m¬≤ | | $\delta$ | Rotational inertia factor | 0.05 ‚Äì 0.15 | EV: typically 0.1 | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Decomposition of the Four Opposing Forces The vehicle dynamics model treats the car as a single rigid mass moving along a slope, with all rotational inertia lumped into an effective mass factor. Each resistive force is analyzed independently: ### 2.1.1 Rolling Resistance (F_rr) Rolling resistance arises from the **hysteresis losses** in the tire rubber as it deforms while rolling, plus minor contributions from wheel bearing friction, brake drag, and tire scrub. It is modeled as proportional to the **normal load** on the tire (i.e., the vehicle weight component perpendicular to the road surface). $$F_{rr} = W \cdot f \cdot \cos(\alpha)$$ where: - $W = m \cdot g$ ‚Üí total vehicle weight in Newtons - $f$ ‚Üí coefficient of rolling resistance (dimensionless) - $\alpha$ ‚Üí road grade angle (radians) > **Why it matters in HEVs/EVs:** Electric vehicles are generally heavier than ICE vehicles due to battery mass. Hence rolling resistance is **proportionally higher** ‚Äî a critical design consideration. ### 2.1.2 Aerodynamic Drag (F_ad) Aerodynamic drag is the **viscous + pressure resistance** the vehicle body experiences while moving through still air. The kinetic energy imparted to the air parcels goes into turbulence (pressure drag) and viscous shear (skin friction). $$F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2$$ where $v$ is the vehicle velocity in m/s. Note the **velocity-squared dependence** ‚Äî this is why drag dominates highway fuel economy and limits top speed. > **KTU High-Yield Insight:** For EVs, regenerative braking recovers part of this kinetic energy when decelerating from highway speeds ‚Äî a major efficiency win. ### 2.1.3 Hill Climbing Force (F_hc) The component of vehicle weight acting along the slope that opposes forward motion: $$F_{hc} = W \cdot \sin(\alpha) \approx W \cdot \tan(\alpha) \quad \text{(for small }\alpha\text{)}$$ The **percent grade** is defined as: $$\text{Grade (\%)} = 100 \times \tan(\alpha)$$ A 6% grade means $\tan(\alpha) = 0.06$, giving $F_{hc} \approx 0.06 \cdot W$. ### 2.1.4 Linear Acceleration Force (F_la) By Newton's Second Law, the force to produce a forward acceleration of $a$ (m/s¬≤) on an effective mass $(m + m_i) = m \cdot (1+\delta)$: $$F_{la} = m \cdot (1 + \delta) \cdot a$$ The rotational inertia factor $\delta$ accounts for the kinetic energy stored in rotating driveline components (wheels, motor rotor, transmission gears). A common approximation: $$\delta \approx \frac{J_w + J_m \cdot n^2 \cdot \eta_g}{m \cdot r^2}$$ where $J_w$ = wheel inertia, $J_m$ = motor inertia, $n$ = gear ratio, $\eta_g$ = gear efficiency, $r$ = tire radius. --- ## 2.2 Power Requirement at the Wheels The **tractive power** required to propel the vehicle at a given speed is the scalar product of tractive force and velocity: $$P_t = F_t \cdot v = (F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}) \cdot v$$ Since $F_{ad} \propto v^2$, the aerodynamic power requirement scales as $v^3$ ‚Äî a **cubic dependence** that governs high-speed EV range. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **MASTER REFERENCE TABLE ‚Äî Memorize before the exam.** | # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | $F_{rr} = m \cdot g \cdot f \cdot \cos\alpha$ | Rolling resistance force | N (‚âà $mgf$ for level road) | | 2 | $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^2$ | Aerodynamic drag | N, velocity-squared | | 3 | $F_{hc} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha \approx m \cdot g \cdot G$ | Grade climbing force | N, $G$ = grade (decimal) | | 4 | $F_{la} = m(1+\delta) \cdot a$ | Linear acceleration force | N, effective mass | | 5 | $F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}$ | Master tractive force equation | N | | 6 | $a = \dfrac{F_t - (F_{rr}+F_{ad}+F_{hc})}{m(1+\delta)}$ | Resolved acceleration | m/s¬≤ | | 7 | $P_t = F_t \cdot v$ | Tractive power at wheels | W (J/s) | | 8 | $\eta_{total} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{P_{battery}}$ | Drivetrain efficiency | % (EV: 85‚Äì92%) | | 9 | $t_a = \displaystyle\int_{v_1}^{v_2} \dfrac{m(1+\delta) \cdot v \, dv}{F_t(v) - (F_{rr}+F_{hc})}$ | Acceleration time | seconds | | 10 | $\delta \approx 0.05 + 0.002 \cdot n_{gear}$ | Empirical inertia factor (cars) | dimensionless | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Acceleration Time from v‚ÇÅ to v‚ÇÇ **Objective:** Derive the closed-form expression for the time taken by a vehicle to accelerate from initial speed $v_1$ to final speed $v_2$ on a **level road** (so $F_{hc} = 0$). ### Step 1 ‚Äî Set up Newton's Second Law From the tractive force balance, the net force producing acceleration is: $$F_{net} = F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} = m(1+\delta) \cdot a = m(1+\delta) \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$$ ### Step 2 ‚Äî Isolate dt Rearranging for $dt$: $$dt = \frac{m(1+\delta) \cdot dv}{F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad}}$$ ### Step 3 ‚Äî Substitute F_ad (function of v) Since $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^2$, let $C_0 = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f$, so $F_{ad} = C_0 v^2$: $$dt = \frac{m(1+\delta) \cdot dv}{F_t - m g f - C_0 v^2}$$ ### Step 4 ‚Äî Integrate from v‚ÇÅ to v‚ÇÇ Assuming $F_t$ is constant (idealized maximum motor tractive force, e.g., peak torque region): $$t_a = \int_{v_1}^{v_2} \frac{m(1+\delta) \, dv}{F_t - mgf - C_0 v^2}$$ ### Step 5 ‚Äî Closed-form result (using $\arctanh$ substitution) The integral yields a hyperbolic arctangent function. Let $D(v) = F_t - mgf - C_0 v^2$. Performing the partial fraction style integration: $$t_a = \frac{m(1+\delta)}{2\sqrt{C_0(F_t - mgf)}} \cdot \ln\!\left[ \frac{\sqrt{F_t - mgf} + \sqrt{C_0}\, v_2}{\sqrt{F_t - mgf} + \sqrt{C_0}\, v_1} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{F_t - mgf} - \sqrt{C_0}\, v_1}{\sqrt{F_t - mgf} - \sqrt{C_0}\, v_2} \right]$$ > **Engineering Utility:** This expression is used by EV design engineers to compute **0‚Äì100 km/h time**, a globally standard performance metric. Typical EVs achieve **3‚Äì7 seconds**. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Model Problem) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sample Problem (KTU Board Style):** An electric vehicle with the following specifications is to be analyzed: > - Curb mass $m = 1500$ kg > - Rolling resistance coefficient $f = 0.012$ > - Drag coefficient $C_d = 0.28$ > - Frontal area $A_f = 2.1$ m¬≤ > - Rotational inertia factor $\delta = 0.10$ > - Maximum tractive force $F_t = 4500$ N (from motor at low speed) > - Operating conditions: **level road**, $v = 25$ m/s (‚âà 90 km/h) > > **Find:** (a) Acceleration at 25 m/s, (b) Power required at the wheels, (c) Verify if motor has enough power for 30% grade climbing at 15 m/s. ### Part (a) ‚Äî Acceleration at 25 m/s **Step 1: Calculate Rolling Resistance** $$F_{rr} = m \cdot g \cdot f = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 = 176.58 \text{ N}$$ **Step 2: Calculate Aerodynamic Drag** $$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.28 \times 2.1 \times 25^2$$ Performing the calculation explicitly: $$\begin{aligned} F_{ad} &= 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.28 \times 2.1 \times 625 \\ &= 0.6 \times 0.28 \times 2.1 \times 625 \\ &= 0.6 \times 0.588 \times 625 \\ &= 0.3528 \times 625 \\ &= 220.5 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Hill Climbing Force on Level Road** $$F_{hc} = 0 \text{ N (since grade} = 0\text{)}$$ **Step 4: Solve for Acceleration** The net accelerating force is $F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_{hc}$ divided by the effective mass: $$\begin{aligned} F_{net} &= F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_{hc} \\ &= 4500 - 176.58 - 220.5 - 0 \\ &= 4102.92 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$ Effective mass: $$m_{eff} = m(1 + \delta) = 1500 \times 1.10 = 1650 \text{ kg}$$ Therefore: $$a = \frac{4102.92}{1650} = 2.487 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 2.49 \text{ m/s}^2$$ ### Part (b) ‚Äî Power Required at the Wheels $$P_t = F_t \cdot v = 4500 \times 25 = 112{,}500 \text{ W} = 112.5 \text{ kW}$$ This represents the **wheel-side** power demand. Accounting for a typical EV drivetrain efficiency of 90%, the **battery-side** power draw would be: $$P_{batt} = \frac{112.5}{0.90} \approx 125 \text{ kW}$$ ### Part (c) ‚Äî Grade Climbing at 15 m/s on 30% Slope For 30% grade: $\tan\alpha = 0.30$, so $\sin\alpha \approx 0.287$ and $\cos\alpha \approx 0.954$. **Rolling resistance (corrected for slope):** $$F_{rr} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 \times 0.954 = 168.49 \text{ N}$$ **Aerodynamic drag at 15 m/s:** $$\begin{aligned} F_{ad} &= 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.28 \times 2.1 \times 15^2 \\ &= 0.3528 \times 225 \\ &= 79.38 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$ **Hill climbing force:** $$F_{hc} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.287 = 4223.21 \text{ N}$$ **Total resisting force:** $$F_{resist} = 168.49 + 79.38 + 4223.21 = 4471.08 \text{ N}$$ **Net accelerating force available:** $$F_{net} = 4500 - 4471.08 = 28.92 \text{ N}$$ **Resulting acceleration:** $$a = \frac{28.92}{1650} = 0.01753 \text{ m/s}^2$$ > **Conclusion:** The motor can barely maintain speed (near-zero acceleration) on a 30% grade at 15 m/s. The EV has **insufficient margin** to climb such a steep grade while still accelerating. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix ‚Äî ICE vs Hybrid vs Electric | Parameter | Conventional ICE | Hybrid (HEV) | Battery Electric (BEV) | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | Torque at 0 rpm | **0 (stalls)** | **High (motor assist)** | **Maximum (peak torque)** | | 0‚Äì100 km/h | 8‚Äì12 s | 6‚Äì9 s | **3‚Äì7 s** | | Power at top speed | Limited by ICE redline | Extended via motor | **High (single gear)** | | Acceleration consistency | Drops with gear shifts | Smooth (CVT/e-CVT) | **Smoothest (no shift)** | | Regen braking recovery | None | Partial (10‚Äì25%) | **High (20‚Äì40%)** | | Drivetrain inertia factor $\delta$ | 0.05‚Äì0.10 | 0.07‚Äì0.12 | **0.05‚Äì0.10** | > **Key Takeaway:** BEVs deliver **peak torque at zero rpm**, eliminating the launch delay inherent in ICE vehicles ‚Äî this is the single biggest contributor to superior acceleration performance. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Vehicle Dynamics ‚Äî Force Interaction Block Diagram This block diagram illustrates how the four resistance forces combine at the **wheel-road interface** to balance the **tractive force** delivered by the EV drivetrain. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Battery Pack
