Why is it particularly important in EVs? `[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023]` > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The rotational inertia factor Œ¥ is a dimensionless multiplier that represents the **equivalent mass contribution of rotating drivetrain components** (wheels, motor rotor, gears) to the linear acceleration resistance of the vehicle. > > It modifies Newton's second law from $F = m \cdot a$ to: > > $$F = m(1 + \delta) \cdot a$$ > > **Importance in EVs:** (1) High-speed EV motors carry large rotor inertia; (2) Single-speed transmissions mean the entire drivetrain inertia is always engaged; (3) It directly affects the **0‚Äì100 km/h acceleration time calculation**, which is a key performance metric. Typical EV values lie between 0.05 and 0.15. --- ### **Q2.** State and explain the **Fundamental Tractive Force Equation** of vehicle dynamics. Highlight why aerodynamic drag dominates at high speeds. `[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Jun 2024]` > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The fundamental tractive force equation states that the tractive force $F_t$ developed at the wheel‚Äìroad contact must balance the sum of rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, hill climbing force, and linear acceleration force: > > $$F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}$$ > > **Why drag dominates at high speed:** The aerodynamic drag force is proportional to $v^2$. Consequently, the power required to overcome drag is proportional to $v^3$. At low speeds (< 30 km/h), rolling resistance dominates; above ~ 80 km/h, aerodynamic drag becomes the largest single term, limiting top speed and significantly impacting high-speed EV range. --- ## PART B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice Provided ‚Äî Answer ANY ONE of the following:** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Derive, with clear assumptions, the **complete expression for the tractive force requirement** of a road vehicle moving on a grade Œ±. List the standard values of all coefficients used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An electric vehicle has the following specifications: Mass = 1800 kg, $C_d = 0.30$, $A_f = 2.2$ m¬≤, $f = 0.015$, $\delta = 0.08$, $F_t = 5000$ N. Compute: (i) the maximum acceleration at 20 m/s on a level road, (ii) the gradient it can sustain at constant 15 m/s without acceleration, and (iii) the power required at 20 m/s. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Question A** **Part (a) ‚Äî Derivation (7 marks)** **Assumptions:** 1. Vehicle treated as a point mass with lumped rotational inertia. 2. Wind velocity = 0 (still air); vehicle velocity $v$ is relative wind speed. 3. Road grade is constant; tires do not slip. 4. No headwind or crosswind components. **Step 1:** Apply Newton's Second Law along the direction of motion: $$F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_{hc} = m(1+\delta) \cdot a$$ **Step 2:** Write each term with its physical origin: - $F_{rr} = m g f \cos\alpha$ ‚Äî from tire deformation hysteresis - $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^2$ ‚Äî from air displacement and skin friction - $F_{hc} = m g \sin\alpha$ ‚Äî from gravitational component along slope - $F_{la} = m(1+\delta) \cdot a$ ‚Äî from net accelerating force **Step 3:** Final tractive force equation: $$\boxed{F_t = mgf\cos\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^2 + mg\sin\alpha + m(1+\delta)\,a}$$ **Step 4:** Standard coefficient values: | Symbol | Typical Range | Car Default | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | $\rho$ | 1.1‚Äì1.3 kg/m¬≥ | **1.2 kg/m¬≥** | | $C_d$ | 0.22‚Äì0.40 | **0.30** | | $A_f$ | 1.6‚Äì2.5 m¬≤ | **2.0 m¬≤** | | $f$ | 0.005‚Äì0.020 | **0.012** | | $g$ | ‚Äî | **9.81 m/s¬≤** | | $\delta$ | 0.05‚Äì0.15 | **0.10** | --- **Part (b) ‚Äî Numerical Solution (7 marks)** **Given:** $m = 1800$ kg, $C_d = 0.30$, $A_f = 2.2$ m¬≤, $f = 0.015$, $\delta = 0.08$, $F_t = 5000$ N, $v = 20$ m/s. **Step 1 ‚Äî Effective mass:** $$m_{eff} = 1800 \times 1.08 = 1944 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 2 ‚Äî Resistances on level road at 20 m/s:** $$F_{rr} = 1800 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 = 264.87 \text{ N}$$ $$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 20^2 = 0.396 \times 400 = 158.4 \text{ N}$$ $$F_{hc} = 0 \text{ N (level road)}$$ **(i) Maximum acceleration at 20 m/s:** $$a = \frac{5000 - 264.87 - 158.4 - 0}{1944} = \frac{4576.73}{1944} = 2.354 \text{ m/s}^2$$ **Converting to 0‚Äì100 km/h time** (rough estimate using constant a, actual value is less due to $v^2$ drag term): $$t = \frac{100{,}000/3600}{2.354} = \frac{27.78}{2.354} \approx 11.8 \text{ s}$$ **(ii) Sustainable grade at constant 15 m/s (a = 0):** At $a = 0$, the tractive force must equal total resistance: $$F_t = F_{rr}\cos\alpha + F_{ad} + mg\sin\alpha$$ Neglecting $\cos\alpha \approx 1$ for small angles: $$5000 = 1800 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 + F_{ad} + 1800 \times 9.81 \times \sin\alpha$$ At $v = 15$ m/s, $F_{ad} = 0.396 \times 225 = 89.1$ N. $$5000 = 264.87 + 89.1 + 17658 \sin\alpha$$ $$\sin\alpha = \frac{4646.03}{17658} = 0.2631$$ $$\alpha = \arcsin(0.2631) = 15.25¬∞$$ $$\text{Grade} = 100 \times \tan(15.25¬∞) = 27.27\%$$ **(iii) Power required at 20 m/s:** $$P_t = F_t \times v = 5000 \times 20 = 100{,}000 \text{ W} = 100 \text{ kW}$$ $$\boxed{a = 2.354 \text{ m/s}^2, \quad \text{Grade} \approx 27.3\%, \quad P_t = 100 \text{ kW}}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî Alternative Choice** **(a)** With a suitable sketch, explain the **operating regions of an EV motor's N‚ÄìT‚ÄìV characteristic curve**. Identify the regions of constant torque, constant power, and field weakening. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A HEV with mass 1600 kg, $C_d = 0.29$, $A_f = 2.0$ m¬≤, $f = 0.011$, $\delta = 0.07$ travels at 22 m/s on a 5% upgrade. Determine: (i) total tractive force required (assume $a = 0$), (ii) the power requirement at the wheels, and (iii) the minimum additional tractive force required to maintain an acceleration of 1.5 m/s¬≤. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Question B** **Part (a) ‚Äî Motor N-T-V Characteristics (7 marks)** Refer to the schematic in **Section 4.2** of these notes. The EV motor's torque-speed-power curve is divided into three regions: **Region I ‚Äî Constant Torque (0 to base speed $v_b$):** The motor delivers **maximum torque** $T_{max}$ from standstill. The inverter operates in the **constant current mode**, limited by stator current. Power increases linearly with speed. $P = T_{max} \cdot \omega$. **Region II ‚Äî Constant Power ($v_b$ to $v_{max}$):** Above base speed, the motor voltage saturates (limited by DC bus voltage). Field weakening begins, and torque decreases as $T \propto 1/\omega$. Power remains at **peak rating** $P_{max}$. This is the **typical cruising region**. **Region III ‚Äî Deep Field Weakening (above $v_{max}$):** Both voltage and structural limits constrain operation. Torque and power both drop off. The motor cannot sustain continuous operation; only short bursts are allowed. The vehicle's resistance curve (rolling + drag + grade) superimposed on this N-T-V curve determines: - **Launch acceleration capacity** = $F_{t,max} - F_{resist}(0)$ - **Maximum cruising speed** = where $F_{t,motor}(v) = F_{resist}(v)$ --- **Part (b) ‚Äî Numerical Solution (7 marks)** **Given:** $m = 1600$ kg, $C_d = 0.29$, $A_f = 2.0$ m¬≤, $f = 0.011$, $\delta = 0.07$, $v = 22$ m/s, $G = 0.05$ (5% grade). **Step 1 ‚Äî Pre-compute constants:** $$mg = 1600 \times 9.81 = 15{,}696 \text{ N}$$ $$\frac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.29 \times 2.0 = 0.348$$ **Step 2 ‚Äî Compute each resistance force:** Rolling resistance (cos Œ± ‚âà 1 for 5% grade): $$F_{rr} = 15696 \times 0.011 = 172.66 \text{ N}$$ Aerodynamic drag: $$F_{ad} = 0.348 \times 22^2 = 0.348 \times 484 = 168.43 \text{ N}$$ Hill climbing: $$F_{hc} = 15696 \times 0.05 = 784.80 \text{ N}$$ **(i) Total tractive force (a = 0):** $$F_t = 172.66 + 168.43 + 784.80 = 1125.89 \text{ N}$$ **(ii) Power at the wheels:** $$P_t = F_t \times v = 1125.89 \times 22 = 24{,}769.58 \text{ W} \approx 24.77 \text{ kW}$$ **(iii) Additional force to maintain 1.5 m/s¬≤ acceleration:** Effective mass: $m_{eff} = 1600 \times 1.07 = 1712$ kg $$F_{la} = 1712 \times 1.5 = 2568 \text{ N}$$ Total required force: $$F_{t,new} = 1125.89 + 2568 = 3693.89 \text{ N}$$ Additional force above steady-state: $$\Delta F_t = 2568 \text{ N}$$ $$\boxed{F_t = 1125.89 \text{ N}, \quad P_t = 24.77 \text{ kW}, \quad \Delta F_t = 2568 \text{ N}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Forgetting $\delta$:** Many students write $a = F_{net}/m$ instead of $a = F_{net}/[m(1+\delta)]$. This gives a **higher (incorrect) acceleration** and costs **2 marks**. > 2. **Neglecting $\cos\alpha$ vs $\sin\alpha$:** On steep grades, using $F_{hc} = mg \cdot \text{grade}$ instead of $mg \sin\alpha$ introduces up to **5% error**. > 3. **Unit mismatch in $F_{ad}$:** Forgetting that $v$ must be in m/s (not km/h) is the **#1 mistake** ‚Äî produces drag values that are 1000√ó too small or 1000√ó too large. > 4. **Confusing tractive force with torque:** Tractive force $F_t = T \cdot n \cdot \eta_g / r$ ‚Äî not just $T$ alone. Examiners **frequently test** this conversion. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚Äî Vehicle Acceleration** - **Master Equation:** $F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}$ ‚Äî memorize in this exact form. - **Rolling resistance:** $F_{rr} \approx m g f$ for level road; depends on tire pressure and surface. - **Aerodynamic drag:** $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^2$ ‚Äî velocity-squared, dominates above ~80 km/h. - **Hill climbing:** $F_{hc} = mg \sin\alpha$; for small grades, $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \text{grade (decimal)}$. - **Acceleration force:** $F_{la} = m(1+\delta) \cdot a$ ‚Äî always include the inertia factor $\delta$. - **Tractive power:** $P_t = F_t \cdot v$ ‚Äî watts when $F_t$ in N and $v$ in m/s. - **EV advantage:** Instant peak torque at 0 rpm ‚Üí superior 0‚Äì100 km/h performance vs ICE. - **Power requirement scales as $v^3$ for aerodynamic losses** ‚Äî critical for high-speed range estimation. - **Acceleration time** uses integral $t_a = \int dv/a(v)$ ‚Äî rarely closed-form in practice; numerical integration used. - **Standard values to remember:** $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m¬≥, $g = 9.81$ m/s¬≤, $\delta_{EV} \approx 0.10$. - **KTU exam focus:** Numerical problems on level-road acceleration and grade-climbing force appear almost every semester ‚Äî practice the **6‚Äì7 standard data sets** thoroughly. - **Key performance metrics** derived: (1) Maximum acceleration vs. speed curve, (2) Top speed, (3) Gradeability, (4) 0‚Äì100 km/h time.

topics.title: Calculation of Motor Power from Traction Torque
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Calculation of Motor Power from Traction Torque # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of Hybrid and Electric Vehicles (HEVs/EVs), the **Calculation of Motor Power from Traction Torque** is the fundamental sizing procedure that establishes the required mechanical output of the traction electric machine based on the resistive forces acting on the vehicle at a given operating point. Mathematically, the **Tractive Force ($F_t$)** required at the tire contact patch is converted into a **Wheel Torque ($T_w$)** through the wheel radius ($r_w$), and subsequently transformed into a **Motor Torque ($T_m$)** and **Motor Power ($P_m$)** using the transmission gear ratio ($G_r$) and drivetrain efficiency ($\eta_d$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** > The energy storage, power and torque requirements for EV/HEV traction are derived from the fundamental vehicle dynamics equation: > $$F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{ia}$$ > where each force component represents rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, hill climbing, and inertia respectively. The motor must satisfy the worst-case summation of these forces. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Analogy ‚Äî "The Bicycle in a Storm":** > Imagine you are cycling a loaded bicycle (a 100 kg load plus your 70 kg body) up a 10% incline at 25 km/h, while a strong headwind pushes against you. The **tractive effort** is the total force you must push against the pedals to maintain that speed. Now imagine replacing your legs with an electric motor. The question of "motor power" essentially asks: **"How strong (torque) and how fast (rotational speed) does the electric motor need to spin to replace my legs and overcome the wind, gravity, and friction?"** In simple terms: - **Torque** = How hard the motor can push (the "muscle"). - **Speed** = How fast the motor can spin (the "leg cadence"). - **Power** = The product of both ‚Äî the total rate at which the motor delivers mechanical energy to the wheels. The gear system (single-speed reducer or multi-speed transmission) acts as a **mechanical transformer**: it trades the motor's high-speed/low-torque output for a low-speed/high-torque output at the wheels, exactly the way a bicycle's chainring amplifies your pedaling force. ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics | Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s¬≤** | | $\rho_{air}$ | Air density at sea level (ISA) | **1.225 kg/m¬≥** | | $\eta_d$ | Drivetrain efficiency (typical EV) | **0.90 ‚Äì 0.96** | | $f_r$ | Rolling resistance coefficient | **0.008 ‚Äì 0.015** (passenger car tires) | | $C_d$ | Aerodynamic drag coefficient | **0.22 ‚Äì 0.35** (modern EVs) | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Vehicle Dynamics Chain (Step-by-Step Logic) The calculation of motor power from traction torque follows a strict **causal chain** ‚Äî moving from environmental demands (forces) to component-level requirements (motor rating). This is one of the most frequently tested sequences in KTU Module 1 examinations. ### Step 1 ‚Äî Identify the Resistive Forces The tractive force must overcome four fundamental resistances: 1. **Rolling Resistance ($F_{rr}$)** ‚Äî Tire deformation hysteresis on the road. 2. **Aerodynamic Drag ($F_{ad}$)** ‚Äî Displacement of air by the vehicle body. 3. **Hill Climbing Force ($F_{hc}$)** ‚Äî Gravitational pull along an inclined road. 4. **Inertial / Acceleration Force ($F_{ia}$)** ‚Äî Resistance of mass to changes in velocity. ### Step 2 ‚Äî Compute Total Tractive Force All four components are vectorially added along the direction of motion. For a vehicle climbing a slope at constant speed, the inertial term is zero; for an accelerating vehicle, it must be included. ### Step 3 ‚Äî Convert Tractive Force to Wheel Torque The wheel radius acts as the moment arm: $$T_w = F_t \cdot r_w$$ ### Step 4 ‚Äî Apply Transmission Transformation The gear ratio multiplies torque and divides speed: $$T_m = \frac{T_w}{G_r \cdot \eta_d} \quad ; \quad \omega_m = \omega_w \cdot G_r$$ ### Step 5 ‚Äî Compute Motor Power Mechanical power is the product of torque and angular velocity at the motor shaft: $$P_m = T_m \cdot \omega_m = \frac{F_t \cdot v}{\eta_d}$$ > [!TIP] > **The "Why" Behind the Two Formulas for Power:** > The two expressions $P_m = T_m \cdot \omega_m$ and $P_m = \frac{F_t \cdot v}{\eta_d}$ must give the **same numerical value**. The first operates at the motor shaft, the second at the wheel-road interface. This duality is a classic KTU examiner check ‚Äî students who compute them inconsistently lose marks. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) | # | Quantity | Equation | Units | Boundary / Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Rolling Resistance | $F_{rr} = f_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\alpha)$ | N | $\alpha$ = road slope angle | | 2 | Aerodynamic Drag | $F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2$ | N | $A_f$ = frontal area (m¬≤) | | 3 | Hill Climbing Force | $F_{hc} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha)$ | N | Positive for uphill | | 4 | Inertial Force | $F_{ia} = m \cdot a = m \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$ | N | $a$ = linear acceleration | | 5 | Total Tractive Force | $F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{ia}$ | N | Vector sum (1D longitudinal) | | 6 | Wheel Torque | $T_w = F_t \cdot r_w$ | N¬∑m | $r_w$ = effective wheel radius | | 7 | Motor Torque | $T_m = \dfrac{T_w}{G_r \cdot \eta_d}$ | N¬∑m | $\eta_d < 1$ for losses | | 8 | Wheel Angular Speed | $\omega_w = \dfrac{v}{r_w}$ | rad/s | $v$ in m/s | | 9 | Motor Angular Speed | $\omega_m = \omega_w \cdot G_r$ | rad/s | ‚Äî | | 10 | Motor Power (form A) | $P_m = T_m \cdot \omega_m$ | W | At motor shaft | | 11 | Motor Power (form B) | $P_m = \dfrac{F_t \cdot v}{\eta_d}$ | W | At motor shaft, via traction | | 12 | Wheel Power | $P_w = F_t \cdot v$ | W | At wheel-road interface | | 13 | Slope Percentage | $\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{\text{grade (\%)}}{100}$ | ‚Äî | E.g., 10% $\Rightarrow$ $\alpha \approx 5.71¬∞$ | | 14 | Vehicle Kinetic Energy | $KE = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^2$ | J | For regenerative-braking analysis | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in modern EV design:** > 1. **Component Sizing** ‚Äî Determines the kW rating of the traction inverter and motor, and the kWh capacity of the battery pack. > 2. **Drive Cycle Compliance** ‚Äî Real-world cycles (WLTP, NEDC, EPA) are translated into torque-speed operating points plotted on the **motor efficiency map (efficiency ellipses)**. > 3. **Performance Targets** ‚Äî 0‚Äì100 km/h acceleration, gradeability (%), and top speed are all sub-products of these calculations. > 4. **Regenerative Braking** ‚Äî The same traction equations (in reverse) determine the maximum recoverable power back into the battery. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Newton's Second Law to Motor Power The starting point is Newton's Second Law applied to a vehicle moving up a slope with acceleration $a$: $$m \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} = F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_{hc}$$ ### 3.1.1 Derivation of the Four Resistance Forces **A) Rolling Resistance ($F_{rr}$):** A tire under load deforms as it rolls. Energy lost to viscoelastic deformation appears as a small horizontal resistance. Empirically modeled as a fraction of the normal force ($N = m g \cos\alpha$): $$ \begin{aligned} F_{rr} &= f_r \cdot N \\ N &= m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\alpha) \\ \Rightarrow F_{rr} &= f_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\alpha) \end{aligned} $$ **B) Aerodynamic Drag ($F_{ad}$):** Drag is modeled using standard fluid-dynamics drag equation. The dynamic pressure ($\frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$) acts on the projected frontal area $A_f$, scaled by the dimensionless drag coefficient $C_d$: $$ \begin{aligned} q &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot v^2 \\ \Rightarrow F_{ad} &= C_d \cdot A_f \cdot q = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2 \end{aligned} $$ **C) Hill Climbing Force ($F_{hc}$):** The component of gravitational weight acting along the inclined direction of motion: $$ \begin{aligned} F_{hc} &= m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha) \\ \text{For small slopes (grade} \leq 20\%\text{):} \quad \alpha &\approx \arctan(\text{grade}/100) \\ \Rightarrow \sin(\alpha) &\approx \text{grade}/100 \quad \text{(good approximation)} \end{aligned} $$ **D) Inertial Force ($F_{ia}$):** This is the effective "resistance" the mass offers to changes in motion. An equivalent rotating-mass term ($\delta \cdot m_{eq}$) can be added for the inertia of rotating drivetrain components, but for the basic KTU problem we use translational mass only: $$ F_{ia} = m \cdot a = m \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} $$ ### 3.1.2 Summation into Total Tractive Force $$ \begin{aligned} F_t &= F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{ia} \\ F_t &= f_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\alpha) + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2 + m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha) + m \cdot a \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.1.3 Conversion to Wheel Torque Treating the wheel as a rigid disc rolling without slipping, the contact patch becomes the instantaneous center of rotation. The torque about the wheel center equals force times effective radius: $$ \begin{aligned} T_w &= F_t \cdot r_w \\ \text{(This is the torque the tire must deliver to the road to generate } F_t \text{.)} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.1.4 Conversion to Motor Torque (Gearbox Action) The transmission has a fixed gear ratio $G_r = \frac{\omega_m}{\omega_w}$ and efficiency $\eta_d < 1$. The mechanical power must be conserved (less losses): $$ \begin{aligned} P_w &= T_w \cdot \omega_w \\ P_m \cdot \eta_d &= P_w \\ T_m \cdot \omega_m \cdot \eta_d &= T_w \cdot \omega_w \\ T_m \cdot (\omega_w \cdot G_r) \cdot \eta_d &= T_w \cdot \omega_w \\ \Rightarrow T_m &= \frac{T_w}{G_r \cdot \eta_d} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.1.5 Conversion to Motor Power (Final Step) $$ \begin{aligned} P_m &= T_m \cdot \omega_m \\ P_m &= \left( \frac{T_w}{G_r \cdot \eta_d} \right) \cdot \left( \omega_w \cdot G_r \right) \\ P_m &= \frac{T_w \cdot \omega_w}{\eta_d} \\ \Rightarrow P_m &= \frac{F_t \cdot v}{\eta_d} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "G_r Cancels" Principle:** > Notice how the gear ratio **cancels out** in the final power equation. The motor power required is **independent of gear ratio** ‚Äî the gear ratio only changes the **split** between motor torque and motor speed. This is a frequent KTU conceptual question. ## 3.2 Worked Example: Sample Numerical (Will be Fully Solved in Section 5) A preview of the typical KTU numerical pattern: **Given:** $m = 1500$ kg, $v = 60$ km/h, grade = 10%, $f_r = 0.015$, $C_d = 0.3$, $A_f = 2.2$ m¬≤, $r_w = 0.3$ m, $G_r = 4.5$, $\eta_d = 0.95$. **Find:** $T_m$ and $P_m$. **Pre-computation (for verification):** - $v = 60/3.6 = 16.667$ m/s - $\sin(\alpha) = \sin(\arctan(0.10)) = 0.0995$ - $\cos(\alpha) = \cos(\arctan(0.10)) = 0.9950$ - $F_{rr} = 0.015 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.995 = 219.6$ N - $F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.3 \times 2.2 \times (16.667)^2 = 110.0$ N - $F_{hc} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.0995 = 1463.9$ N - $F_t = 219.6 + 110.0 + 146.9 = 1793.5$ N - $T_w = 1793.5 \times 0.3 = 538.05$ N¬∑m - $T_m = 538.05 / (4.5 \times 0.95) = 125.84$ N¬∑m - $\omega_w = 16.667 / 0.3 = 55.56$ rad/s - $P_m = 1793.5 \times 16.667 / 0.95 = 31,464$ W $\approx 31.46$ kW # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Power Flow from Battery to Wheel ```mermaid flowchart LR A[BATTERY PACK
DC Energy Source] --> B[POWER ELECTRONICS
Traction Inverter] B --> C[ELECTRIC MACHINE
Motor Torque Tm and Speed wm] C --> D[GEARBOX
Gear Ratio Gr, Efficiency nd] D --> E[WHEEL ASSEMBLY
Wheel Torque Tw, Radius rw] E --> F[ROAD CONTACT PATCH
Tractive Force Ft] F --> G[VEHICLE BODY
Resistive Forces Frr, Fad, Fhc, Fia] G -->|Opposing Reaction| F ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** - **Horizontal flow (left ‚Üí right):** Energy conversion from electrical ‚Üí mechanical ‚Üí translational kinetic. - **The "tractive force" $F_t$** at the contact patch is the **boundary condition** that closes the loop ‚Äî it must equal the sum of vehicle resistive forces. - **The "gear ratio"** in the gearbox block is the **key design lever** that trades motor torque for motor speed. ## 4.2 Force Decomposition Diagram (Free-Body Representation) ```mermaid flowchart TD VEH[VEHICLE ON INCLINE
Mass m, Velocity v] VEH --> F1[FRR: Rolling Resistance
= fr x m x g x cos alpha
Acts opposite to motion] VEH --> F2[FAD: Aerodynamic Drag
= 0.5 x rho x Cd x Af x v squared
Acts opposite to motion] VEH --> F3[FHC: Hill Climbing
= m x g x sin alpha
Acts along slope downward] VEH --> F4[FIA: Inertial Force
= m x a
Resists acceleration] F1 --> SUM[FT: Total Tractive Force
Required at Wheels] F2 --> SUM F3 --> SUM F4 --> SUM SUM --> TW[TW = FT x rw
Wheel Torque] TW --> TM[TM = TW divided by Gr x nd
Motor Torque] TM --> PM[PM = TM x wm
Motor Power Output] ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** - All four force components act **along the longitudinal axis** of the vehicle. - They sum vectorially (in 1D, this is algebraic) to yield $F_t$. - The transformation from $F_t$ to $P_m$ is a **2-step process** (force ‚Üí torque ‚Üí power), passing through the wheel radius and gear ratio. ## 4.3 Power Transformation Stages (Energy Loss Visualization) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[VEHICLE DEMAND] P1[FT x v
Required Traction Power] end subgraph STAGE1[STAGE 1: WHEEL] P2[FT x v = TW x ww
Wheel Power] end subgraph STAGE2[STAGE 2: GEARBOX] P3[FT x v divided by nd
Motor Shaft Power] end subgraph STAGE3[STAGE 3: MOTOR] P4[TM x wm
Mechanical Output] end subgraph STAGE4[STAGE 4: INVERTER] P5[DC Input Power
from Battery] end P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** - Power **grows** as we move upstream (from road to battery) due to cumulative losses. - The drivetrain efficiency $\eta_d$ is the **single most important design parameter** for range optimization. - The inverter efficiency $\eta_{inv}$ and motor efficiency $\eta_m$ are additional loss terms in a full powertrain model. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]** **State the equation for tractive force required for a vehicle moving up a gradient, and identify the four resistive force components involved.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The total tractive force required to propel an EV/HEV is given by the longitudinal dynamics equation: $$F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{ia}$$ The four components are: 1. **$F_{rr}$ (Rolling Resistance):** $f_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\alpha)$ ‚Äî friction in tire-road contact. 2. **$F_{ad}$ (Aerodynamic Drag):** $\frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2$ ‚Äî resistance from air displacement. 3. **$F_{hc}$ (Hill Climbing Force):** $m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha)$ ‚Äî gravitational component along slope. 4. **$F_{ia}$ (Inertial Force):** $m \cdot a$ ‚Äî resistance to linear acceleration. *(Award 1 mark for the equation, 2 marks for correctly listing the four components with their physical meaning.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]** **Why is the gear ratio $G_r$ absent in the final expression for motor power $P_m = \frac{F_t \cdot v}{\eta_d}$?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The gear ratio $G_r$ **cancels out algebraically** when motor torque and motor angular velocity are substituted into the power equation: $$P_m = T_m \cdot \omega_m = \left( \frac{T_w}{G_r \cdot \eta_d} \right) \cdot (\omega_w \cdot G_r) = \frac{T_w \cdot \omega_w}{\eta_d} = \frac{F_t \cdot v}{\eta_d}$$ **Physical interpretation:** Gear ratio does **not change the energy required** to propel the vehicle ‚Äî it only redistributes the demand between **torque** and **speed** at the motor shaft. A higher $G_r$ reduces $T_m$ (lower current ‚Üí cheaper inverter) but increases $\omega_m$ (higher speed ‚Üí may exceed motor's max RPM). Therefore, $G_r$ is selected based on **motor characteristic matching**, not on power minimization. *(Award 1.5 marks for the cancellation logic, 1.5 marks for the physical interpretation.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ‚Äî Internal Choice Pattern) ### **Question A (14 Marks) ‚Äî Option 1** > **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2022]** > An electric passenger car has the following specifications: > - Total mass (including passengers): $m = 1500$ kg > - Maximum constant speed on level road: $v = 60$ km/h > - Road gradient: 10% (climbing) > - Rolling resistance coefficient: $f_r = 0.015$ > - Aerodynamic drag coefficient: $C_d = 0.30$ > - Frontal area: $A_f = 2.2$ m¬≤ > - Wheel effective radius: $r_w = 0.30$ m > - Gear ratio (single-speed): $G_r = 4.5$ > - Drivetrain efficiency: $\eta_d = 0.95$ > - Air density: $\rho = 1.20$ kg/m¬≥ > > **(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the total tractive force required at the wheels and the corresponding wheel torque. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the motor torque and motor power required to maintain this constant speed on the gradient. #### **Model Solution to Q-A(a):** **Step 1 ‚Äî Convert units:** $$v = 60 \, \text{km/h} = \frac{60}{3.6} = 16.667 \, \text{m/s}$$ $$\alpha = \arctan(0.10) = 5.711¬∞ \Rightarrow \sin(\alpha) = 0.0995, \; \cos(\alpha) = 0.9950$$ **Step 2 ‚Äî Compute Rolling Resistance:** $$F_{rr} = f_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\alpha) = 0.015 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.9950$$ $$F_{rr} = 219.6 \, \text{N}$$ **Step 3 ‚Äî Compute Aerodynamic Drag:** $$F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2 = 0.5 \times 1.20 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (16.667)^2$$ $$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.20 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 277.78$$ $$F_{ad} = 110.0 \, \text{N}$$ **Step 4 ‚Äî Compute Hill Climbing Force:** $$F_{hc} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha) = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.0995$$ $$F_{hc} = 1463.9 \, \text{N}$$ **Step 5 ‚Äî Total Tractive Force** (constant speed ‚Üí $F_{ia} = 0$): $$F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} = 219.6 + 110.0 + 1463.9$$ $$\boxed{F_t = 1793.5 \, \text{N}}$$ **Step 6 ‚Äî Wheel Torque:** $$T_w = F_t \cdot r_w = 1793.5 \times 0.30$$ $$\boxed{T_w = 538.05 \, \text{N¬∑m}}$$ *(Awarding scheme: 1 mark each for F_rr, F_ad, F_hc; 1 mark for total; 2 marks for T_w; 1 mark for unit conversion.)* #### **Model Solution to Q-A(b):** **Step 1 ‚Äî Motor Torque from Gearbox Relation:** $$T_m = \frac{T_w}{G_r \cdot \eta_d} = \frac{538.05}{4.5 \times 0.95}$$ $$T_m = \frac{538.05}{4.275}$$ $$\boxed{T_m = 125.86 \, \text{N¬∑m}}$$ **Step 2 ‚Äî Wheel Angular Velocity:** $$\omega_w = \frac{v}{r_w} = \frac{16.667}{0.30} = 55.56 \, \text{rad/s}$$ **Step 3 ‚Äî Motor Angular Velocity:** $$\omega_m = \omega_w \cdot G_r = 55.56 \times 4.5$$ $$\omega_m = 250.0 \, \text{rad/s}$$ **Step 4 ‚Äî Motor Power (Method 1: Direct):** $$P_m = T_m \cdot \omega_m = 125.86 \times 250.0$$ $$\boxed{P_m = 31{,}465 \, \text{W} \approx 31.47 \, \text{kW}}$$ **Step 5 ‚Äî Verification (Method 2: Via Traction Power):** $$P_w = F_t \cdot v = 1793.5 \times 16.667 = 29{,}894 \, \text{W} \approx 29.89 \, \text{kW}$$ $$P_m = \frac{P_w}{\eta_d} = \frac{29{,}894}{0.95} = 31{,}467 \, \text{W} \approx 31.47 \, \text{kW} \; \checkmark$$ *(Awarding scheme: 2 marks for T_m; 1 mark each for omega_w and omega_m; 2 marks for P_m; 1 mark for verification.)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)** > **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2023]** > A battery electric delivery van is to be designed with the following parameters: > - Kerb weight: $m_k = 1800$ kg, Payload: $m_p = 600$ kg (so total $m = 2400$ kg) > - Top speed on level road: $v_{max} = 90$ km/h > - Gradeability requirement: 15% slope at $v = 30$ km/h (constant speed) > - Rolling resistance coefficient: $f_r = 0.012$ > - Aerodynamic drag coefficient: $C_d = 0.35$ > - Frontal area: $A_f = 3.5$ m¬≤ > - Wheel radius: $r_w = 0.35$ m > - Gear ratio: $G_r = 5.5$ > - Drivetrain efficiency: $\eta_d = 0.93$ > - Air density: $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m¬≥ > > **(a) [7 Marks]** Determine the tractive force and wheel torque required to climb the 15% slope at 30 km/h. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the corresponding motor torque, motor speed, and motor power. Also comment on whether the motor would be able to cruise at 90 km/h on level road at the same power rating. #### **Model Solution to Q-B(a):** **Step 1 ‚Äî Convert units:** $$v = 30 \, \text{km/h} = 8.333 \, \text{m/s}$$ $$\alpha = \arctan(0.15) = 8.531¬∞ \Rightarrow \sin(\alpha) = 0.1483, \; \cos(\alpha) = 0.9889$$ **Step 2 ‚Äî Rolling Resistance:** $$F_{rr} = f_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\alpha) = 0.012 \times 2400 \times 9.81 \times 0.9889$$ $$F_{rr} = 279.3 \, \text{N}$$ **Step 3 ‚Äî Aerodynamic Drag:** $$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.35 \times 3.5 \times (8.333)^2$$ $$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.35 \times 3.5 \times 69.44$$ $$F_{ad} = 52.10 \, \text{N}$$ **Step 4 ‚Äî Hill Climbing Force:** $$F_{hc} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha) = 2400 \times 9.81 \times 0.1483$$ $$F_{hc} = 3491.4 \, \text{N}$$ **Step 5 ‚Äî Total Tractive Force (constant speed, no inertia):** $$F_t = 279.3 + 52.10 + 3491.4$$ $$\boxed{F_t = 3822.8 \, \text{N}}$$ **Step 6 ‚Äî Wheel Torque:** $$T_w = F_t \cdot r_w = 3822.8 \times 0.35$$ $$\boxed{T_w = 1338.0 \, \text{N¬∑m}}$$ #### **Model Solution to Q-B(b):** **Step 1 ‚Äî Motor Torque:** $$T_m = \frac{T_w}{G_r \cdot \eta_d} = \frac{1338.0}{5.5 \times 0.93} = \frac{1338.0}{5.115}$$ $$T_m = 261.6 \, \text{N¬∑m}$$ **Step 2 ‚Äî Wheel Angular Speed:** $$\omega_w = \frac{v}{r_w} = \frac{8.333}{0.35} = 23.81 \, \text{rad/s}$$ **Step 3 ‚Äî Motor Angular Speed:** $$\omega_m = \omega_w \cdot G_r = 23.81 \times 5.5 = 130.95 \, \text{rad/s}$$ **Step 4 ‚Äî Motor Power:** $$P_m = T_m \cdot \omega_m = 261.6 \times 130.95$$ $$\boxed{P_m = 34{,}256 \, \text{W} \approx 34.26 \, \text{kW}}$$ **Step 5 ‚Äî Check at 90 km/h, level road:** $$v_{max} = 90/3.6 = 25.0 \, \text{m/s}, \; \alpha = 0 \Rightarrow F_{hc} = 0, \; \cos\alpha = 1$$ $$F_{rr} = 0.012 \times 2400 \times 9.81 \times 1 = 282.5 \, \text{N}$$ $$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.35 \times 3.5 \times (25)^2 = 468.9 \, \text{N}$$ $$F_t = 282.5 + 468.9 + 0 = 751.4 \, \text{N}$$ $$P_m = \frac{F_t \cdot v_{max}}{\eta_d} = \frac{751.4 \times 25.0}{0.93} = 20{,}199 \, \text{W} \approx 20.2 \, \text{kW}$$ **Comment:** Since 20.2 kW < 34.26 kW, the motor **can easily cruise** at 90 km/h on level road. In fact, it operates at only ~59% of its rated power at top speed, leaving substantial margin for overtaking acceleration. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout ‚Äî Read Carefully!** > > 1. **Unit Conversion Trap:** Speed must be in **m/s** for $F_{ad}$ and $P$ calculations. Many students write $v = 60$ km/h directly into the drag formula and lose **2 marks** immediately. Always convert: $v_{m/s} = v_{km/h} / 3.6$. > > 2. **Slope Angle vs Grade %:** A "10% grade" is **NOT** $\sin(\alpha) = 0.10$ ‚Äî it is $\tan(\alpha) = 0.10$. For $\alpha = 5.71¬∞$, $\sin(\alpha) = 0.0995$ (slight difference, but the procedure matters). For grades $\leq$ 15%, the small-angle approximation is acceptable; beyond that, use exact trigonometric values. > > 3. **The $F_{ia}$ Term:** Many students forget to include (or wrongly include) the inertia term. **If the problem says "constant speed," $F_{ia} = 0$.** If the problem says "accelerates from $v_1$ to $v_2$ in time $t$," then $a = (v_2 - v_1)/t$ and $F_{ia} = m \cdot a$ **plus** an equivalent rotating-mass term if mentioned. > > 4. **Power Duality Check:** KTU examiners often award 1 bonus mark if a student verifies $P_m$ using both methods ($T_m \cdot \omega_m$ and $F_t \cdot v / \eta_d$). Always show the check. > > 5. **Drivetrain Efficiency Direction:** Power flows **upstream** in losses. So $P_m > P_w$, meaning $P_m = P_w / \eta_d$. A common mistake is $P_m = P_w \cdot \eta_d$ (which would make motor power **smaller** than wheel power ‚Äî physically impossible). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 ‚Äî Motor Power from Traction Torque** - **The Master Equation (memorize this first):** $$\boxed{P_m = \frac{F_t \cdot v}{\eta_d} = T_m \cdot \omega_m}$$ - **The Four Resistive Forces (memorize all four equations with their trig functions):** - $F_{rr} = f_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\alpha)$ ‚Üí rolling - $F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2$ ‚Üí drag - $F_{hc} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha)$ ‚Üí gradient - $F_{ia} = m \cdot a$ ‚Üí inertia (zero at constant speed) - **The Three Conversion Ratios (force ‚Üí torque ‚Üí power):** - Force ‚Üí Wheel Torque: multiply by $r_w$ - Wheel Torque ‚Üí Motor Torque: divide by $(G_r \cdot \eta_d)$ - Wheel Speed ‚Üí Motor Speed: multiply by $G_r$ - Wheel Power ‚Üí Motor Power: divide by $\eta_d$ - **Key Conversions to Remember:** - $1 \text{ km/h} = 1/3.6 \text{ m/s}$ - Grade (%) = $100 \cdot \tan(\alpha)$ - $1 \text{ hp} = 746 \text{ W}$; $1 \text{ kW} = 1.341 \text{ hp}$ - **Critical Conceptual Points (KTU favorite 2-mark questions):** - Gear ratio does **NOT** change motor power ‚Äî only the torque-speed split. - Aerodynamic drag scales with $v^2$ ‚Äî dominant at high speeds. - Hill climbing force scales with mass ‚Äî dominant on steep inclines. - At constant low speed on level road, rolling resistance dominates. - At constant high speed on level road, aerodynamic drag dominates. - **Common Standard Values (for quick reference in exams):** - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ - $\rho_{air} = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (sea level, ISA) - $f_r \in [0.008, 0.015]$ for radial passenger tires - $C_d \in [0.22, 0.35]$ for modern EV body shapes - $\eta_d \in [0.90, 0.96]$ for single-speed EV reducers - **Always Do These Three Sanity Checks Before Submitting:** 1. Are all speeds in **m/s**? (not km/h) 2. Is the **grade** interpreted as $\tan\alpha$, not $\sin\alpha$? 3. Did you **divide** by $\eta_d$ (not multiply) when going from wheel power to motor power?

topics.title: Hybrid Electric Drive-trains: Basic Concept of Hybrid Traction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hybrid Electric Drive-trains: Basic Concept of Hybrid Traction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# üîã Module 1 ‚Äî Hybrid Electric Drive-trains: Basic Concept of Hybrid Traction

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Standard):**
> A **Hybrid Electric Drive-train (HEDT)** is a propulsion system that combines **two or more distinct energy sources and converters** ‚Äî typically an **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** and one or more **Electric Machines (EMs)** with an **Energy Storage System (ESS)** ‚Äî to deliver tractive power to the wheels of a vehicle. The system intelligently arbitrates power flow between these sources to optimize **fuel economy, emissions, and performance**.

In KTU parlance, "Hybrid Traction" refers to the **method by which the wheels of a vehicle are driven by a combination of mechanical (ICE) and electrical (motor/generator) power paths**, governed by an **Energy Management Strategy (EMS)** or **Supervisory Controller**.

### üîë Key Constituents of Any Hybrid Drive-train

| # | Component | Function |
|---|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** | Primary mechanical power source ‚Äî typically gasoline/diesel |
| 2 | **Electric Motor (EM)** | Converts electrical energy ‚Üí mechanical tractive power |
| 3 | **Generator (G)** | Converts mechanical energy ‚Üí electrical energy (in series HEV) |
| 4 | **Energy Storage System (ESS)** | Battery pack / Ultra-capacitor / Hybrid ESS |
| 5 | **Power Electronics Converter (PEC)** | DC-DC converter, DC-AC inverter, rectifier |
| 6 | **Transmission** | Gearbox, planetary gear set, CVT, or fixed reduction |
| 7 | **Supervisory Controller (VCU / HC)** | Energy Management Strategy executor |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview ‚Äî The "Two Engines" Analogy üöó

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:**
> Imagine you are cycling uphill. A **regular bicycle** is like a pure ICE car ‚Äî *only your legs* (one energy source) push you. An **electric bicycle** is like a pure EV ‚Äî *only the motor* pushes you. A **Hybrid vehicle is like riding a bicycle where BOTH your legs and a small electric motor are pushing at the SAME time*. Your brain (the *supervisory controller*) decides: "On flat road, just legs. On uphill, both legs + motor. Going downhill, motor acts as a brake to recharge the battery (regenerative braking)."

This mental model immediately clarifies the **three operating modes** of any hybrid:
1. **Pure ICE mode** ‚Üí Only the engine drives wheels
2. **Pure Electric mode** ‚Üí Only the electric motor drives wheels
3. **Hybrid (Combined) mode** ‚Üí Both ICE and EM drive wheels simultaneously

### Why Hybrids? ‚Äî The Engineering Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Engineering Drivers (must remember for KTU viva/2-markers):**
> - **ICE operates most efficiently (~38‚Äì42 %)** at a narrow sweet-spot RPM/load. Hybrids use the EM to "fill the gaps" and keep the ICE in its efficient zone.
> - **Braking energy is wasted** in pure ICE cars. Hybrids recover it via **Regenerative Braking**.
> - **Idling losses eliminated** ‚Äî ICE can shut off at traffic signals (Stop-Start system).
> - **Emission compliance** ‚Äî Lower CO‚ÇÇ, NO‚Çì, PM; meets BS-VI / Euro-7 norms.
> - **Performance boost** ‚Äî EM provides instant torque, supplementing ICE at low speeds.

### Standard Metrics (bold as required)

- **BSFC (Brake Specific Fuel Consumption)**: g/kWh
- **SOC (State of Charge)**: **% (0‚Äì100)**
- **Degree of Hybridization (DoH)**: Ratio of EM peak power to total peak power
- **All-Electric Range (AER)**: **km**
- **CD (Coefficient of Drag)**: dimensionless
- **Rolling Resistance Coefficient (C·µ£·µ£)**: dimensionless
- **Œ∑ (efficiency)**: typically **0.85‚Äì0.95** for EM, **0.30‚Äì0.40** for ICE at part load

---

## 1.3 Classification of Hybrid Vehicles (KTU 2024 Critical)

> [!NOTE]
> **Based on Degree of Hybridization (DoH):**
>
> $$\text{DoH} = \frac{P_{\text{EM,peak}}}{P_{\text{ICE,peak}} + P_{\text{EM,peak}}}$$
>
> - **Micro-hybrid (DoH < 5 %)** ‚Üí Only Stop-Start + brake energy recovery
> - **Mild Hybrid (5 % ‚â§ DoH < 25 %)** ‚Üí 48 V system, EM assists ICE (e.g., 48V BSG)
> - **Full Hybrid (25 % ‚â§ DoH ‚â§ 50 %)** ‚Üí Both ICE & EM can drive wheels independently (Toyota HSD)
> - **Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV) (DoH > 50 %)** ‚Üí Large battery, external charging, AER 30‚Äì80 km

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Based on Drive-train Architecture ‚Äî THE 3 GOLDEN CONFIGURATIONS:**
> 1. **Series Hybrid**
> 2. **Parallel Hybrid**
> 3. **Series-Parallel Hybrid (Power-Split / PSHEV)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis ‚Äî The Three Hybrid Architectures

## 2.1 Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The ICE is **mechanically decoupled** from the wheels. The ICE drives **only a generator**, which charges the battery or feeds an electric motor that alone propels the vehicle. The ICE NEVER directly drives the wheels.

### Operational Logic ‚Äî Step by Step

1. **ICE ‚Üí Generator ‚Üí Rectifier ‚Üí DC Bus**
   - ICE runs at its optimum point on the **Willans line** (constant optimal BSFC curve)
2. **DC Bus ‚Üí Inverter ‚Üí Traction Motor ‚Üí Wheels**
   - Traction motor (typically a **3-phase AC induction** or **PMSM**) drives the wheels through a fixed-ratio gearbox
3. **Battery** is connected in parallel to the DC bus ‚Üí acts as a **buffer/equalizer**
4. **When ICE output > demand** ‚Üí excess goes to battery
5. **When ICE output < demand** ‚Üí battery supplements
6. **Regenerative braking** ‚Üí motor acts as generator ‚Üí charges battery

### Why / How it works

- **Why?** ICE can be sized smaller (range extender concept) and operated strictly at the most efficient point.
- **How?** Total decoupling allows independent optimization of ICE (efficiency) and EM (transient response).

### ‚úÖ Advantages & ‚ùå Disadvantages

| ‚úÖ Advantages | ‚ùå Disadvantages |
|--------------|-----------------|
| Simple mechanical layout (no transmission/clutch) | **Double energy conversion** ‚Üí efficiency losses |
| ICE always at optimum speed | Large traction motor + large battery required |
| Easy regenerative braking | High cost, high weight |
| Ideal for city buses (constant stop-go) | Not suitable for high-speed highway driving |

### Real-world Examples
- **Chevrolet Volt (1st gen)** ‚Äî extended-range EV
- **BMW i3 with REx**
- **Nissan e-Power (Note/Kicks)** ‚Äî series hybrid in India/Japan

---

## 2.2 Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV-Parallel)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Both the ICE and the EM are **mechanically coupled to the wheels** through a **clutch and/or gearbox**. Either source can drive the vehicle **alone or simultaneously**.

### Operational Logic ‚Äî Step by Step

1. **Mechanical Coupling** via clutch ‚Üí torque additivity
2. **ICE path** ‚Üí Clutch ‚Üí Gearbox ‚Üí Wheels
3. **EM path** ‚Üí Clutch/Gearbox ‚Üí Wheels
4. **Operating modes**:
   - **Mode 1: ICE only** ‚Äî Clutch 1 engaged, EM off (highway cruise)
   - **Mode 2: EM only** ‚Äî Clutch 1 open, Clutch 2 engaged (city driving, "EV mode")
   - **Mode 3: Combined (ICE + EM)** ‚Äî Both clutches engaged ‚Üí maximum torque/acceleration
   - **Mode 4: ICE + Generator charging** ‚Äî ICE drives wheels + charges battery
   - **Mode 5: Regenerative braking** ‚Äî EM acts as generator

### Mathematical Torque Relationship at the Coupling

$$T_{\text{wheel}} = (T_{\text{ICE}} \cdot \eta_g \cdot i_g) + (T_{\text{EM}} \cdot \eta_m \cdot i_m)$$

where  
- $T_{\text{ICE}}$ = ICE torque  
- $T_{\text{EM}}$ = EM torque  
- $\eta_g, \eta_m$ = transmission & motor efficiencies  
- $i_g, i_m$ = gear ratios  

### Why / How

- **Why?** Only **one energy conversion** ‚Üí higher efficiency than series.
- **How?** Both sources share the propulsion load ‚Üí smaller ICE + smaller EM suffices.

### ‚úÖ Advantages & ‚ùå Disadvantages

| ‚úÖ Advantages | ‚ùå Disadvantages |
|--------------|-----------------|
| Higher overall efficiency (no double conversion) | Complex clutch/gearbox control |
| Smaller ICE + smaller EM | Torque coordination is challenging |
| Lower cost than series | Less flexibility in ICE optimization |
| Good for mixed city-highway | Regenerative braking more complex |

### Real-world Examples
- **Honda Insight (1st gen, 1999)** ‚Äî Integrated Motor Assist (IMA)
- **Honda Civic Hybrid**
- **Hyundai Sonata Hybrid** (parallel architecture with TMED)

---

## 2.3 Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid ‚Äî The "Best of Both Worlds"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Combines the **series path** (ICE ‚Üí Generator) and the **parallel path** (ICE ‚Üí Wheels) using a **planetary gear set (PGS)** as a power-split device. Toyota calls this the **Hybrid Synergy Drive (HSD)**.

### Planetary Gear Set ‚Äî The Heart of Power-Split

A planetary gear set has three ports:
- **Sun gear (S)**
- **Planet carrier (C)**
- **Ring gear (R)**

Two of these three ports are connected to ICE, MG1, and MG2; the third becomes the output.

#### Toyota HSD Configuration (Standard, ask in viva)

| Element | Connected to |
|---------|--------------|
| **Sun Gear (S)** | MG1 (acts as Generator + Motor) |
| **Planet Carrier (C)** | ICE |
| **Ring Gear (R)** | Output ‚Üí MG2 (Traction Motor) ‚Üí Wheels |

### Speed Relationship (Fundamental Kinematic Equation)

$$\left(1 + N\right) \cdot \omega_C = \omega_S + N \cdot \omega_R$$

where **N = number of ring gear teeth / number of sun gear teeth** (‚âà 2.6 in Toyota HSD).

### Why / How

- **Why?** Allows **continuous variable transmission (CVT-like)** behavior without a CVT.
- **How?** MG1 acts as a **reactive torque element** ‚Üí controls ICE speed independently of vehicle speed ‚Üí ICE stays at optimum.

### ‚úÖ Advantages & ‚ùå Disadvantages

| ‚úÖ Advantages | ‚ùå Disadvantages |
|--------------|-----------------|
| ICE always at optimum (like series) | Most complex control software |
| High efficiency (like parallel) | Most expensive of all three |
| Smooth, seamless transitions | Planetary gear friction losses |
| Best fuel economy (Toyota Prius ‚âà 50 mpg) | Difficult to manufacture |

### Real-world Examples
- **Toyota Prius (1997‚Äìpresent)** ‚Äî Gold standard
- **Toyota Camry Hybrid, Corolla Altis Hybrid**
- **Ford Escape Hybrid (2nd gen)**

---

## 2.4 üìã KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS ‚Äî Must memorize for KTU Exam**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $F_t = F_r + F_{\text{ad}} + F_{\text{grade}} + F_{\text{acc}}$ | Tractive force equation | N |
| 2 | $F_t = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_D \cdot A \cdot v^2 + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + m \cdot a$ | Expanded tractive force | N |
| 3 | $P_t = F_t \cdot v$ | Tractive power | W |
| 4 | $T_{\text{req}} = F_t \cdot r_{\text{wheel}} / i_g \cdot \eta_g$ | Required wheel torque | N¬∑m |
| 5 | $\text{DoH} = \dfrac{P_{\text{EM,peak}}}{P_{\text{ICE,peak}} + P_{\text{EM,peak}}}$ | Degree of Hybridization | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{series}} = \eta_{\text{ICE}} \cdot \eta_{\text{Gen}} \cdot \eta_{\text{Mot}} \cdot \eta_{\text{Batt}}$ | Series efficiency | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{parallel}} = \alpha \cdot \eta_{\text{ICE}} + (1-\alpha) \cdot \eta_{\text{EM}}$ | Parallel efficiency (load share) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\omega_S + N \cdot \omega_R = (1+N) \cdot \omega_C$ | Planetary gear speed eqn | rad/s |
| 9 | $\text{SOC} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{used}}}{Q_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\%$ | State of Charge | % |
| 10 | $E_{\text{regen}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot (v_1^2 - v_2^2) \cdot \eta_{\text{regen}}$ | Regen braking energy | J |
| 11 | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_{\text{brake}}}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | g/kWh |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Mnemonic for KTU Viva:**
> *"Series is for city, Parallel is for highway, Series-Parallel is for the world."*

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive OEM design** ‚Üí Selecting the right architecture based on duty cycle
- **Public transport** ‚Üí Series for buses (no transmission, smooth stop-go)
- **Performance cars** ‚Üí Parallel (Porsche 918, McLaren P1 use complex parallel)
- **Mass market** ‚Üí Series-Parallel (Toyota's global dominance)
- **Fleet economics** ‚Üí Fuel cost reduction & emission compliance

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚Äî Vehicle Tractive Force Equation (Fundamental)

The vehicle tractive force required at the wheels must overcome **four resistive forces**:

### Step 1: Write Newton's 2nd Law in vehicle longitudinal direction

$$F_t - F_r - F_{\text{ad}} - F_{\text{grade}} = m \cdot a$$

Rearranging:

$$F_t = F_r + F_{\text{ad}} + F_{\text{grade}} + m \cdot a$$

### Step 2: Express Rolling Resistance Force

$$F_r = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr}$$

- $C_{rr}$ ‚âà 0.008‚Äì0.015 for car tires on asphalt

### Step 3: Express Aerodynamic Drag

$$F_{\text{ad}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{\text{air}} \cdot C_D \cdot A_f \cdot v^2$$

- $\rho_{\text{air}}$ = **1.225 kg/m¬≥** (standard)
- $C_D$ ‚âà 0.25‚Äì0.35 for modern cars
- $A_f$ = frontal area (m¬≤)
- $v$ = vehicle speed (m/s)

### Step 4: Express Grade Resistance (Climbing a slope $\theta$)

$$F_{\text{grade}} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$$

### Step 5: Express Linear Acceleration Resistance

$$F_{\text{acc}} = m \cdot a = m \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$$

### Step 6: Final Combined Tractive Force Equation

$$\boxed{F_t = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_D \cdot A_f \cdot v^2 + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + m \cdot a}$$

### Step 7: Tractive Power at Wheels

$$P_t = F_t \cdot v$$

$$\boxed{P_t = \left[m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_D \cdot A_f \cdot v^2 + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + m \cdot a\right] \cdot v}$$

### Step 8: Required Motor Torque

$$\boxed{T_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{F_t \cdot r_{\text{wheel}}}{i_g \cdot \eta_g}}$$

where $i_g$ = total gear ratio, $\eta_g$ = drivetrain efficiency.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚Äî Planetary Gear Speed Relation (Toyota HSD)

### Step 1: Geometric Constraint of Planetary Gear

The relative motion of gears in a planetary set obeys (using the **lever analogy** with N = R/S teeth ratio):

$$\dfrac{\omega_R - \omega_C}{\omega_S - \omega_C} = -\dfrac{N_S}{N_R} = -\dfrac{1}{N}$$

### Step 2: Cross-Multiply

$$N \cdot (\omega_R - \omega_C) = -(\omega_S - \omega_C)$$

### Step 3: Expand Both Sides

$$N \cdot \omega_R - N \cdot \omega_C = -\omega_S + \omega_C$$

### Step 4: Collect $\omega_C$ Terms

$$N \cdot \omega_R + \omega_S = N \cdot \omega_C + \omega_C$$

### Step 5: Factor Out $\omega_C$

$$N \cdot \omega_R + \omega_S = (N + 1) \cdot \omega_C$$

### Step 6: Solve for $\omega_C$ (ICE speed)

$$\boxed{\omega_C = \dfrac{\omega_S + N \cdot \omega_R}{1 + N}}$$

This is the **fundamental kinematic equation of the Toyota HSD planetary gear set**.

### Step 7: Special Case Verification

**Case A: Vehicle at standstill, ICE running**
- $\omega_R = 0$ (wheels still)
- $\omega_C = \dfrac{\omega_S}{1+N}$
- So MG1 must rotate at $(1+N) \cdot \omega_C$ to keep ICE spinning while car is parked ‚Üí MG1 acts as a **starter-generator**!

**Case B: Pure EV mode, ICE off**
- $\omega_C = 0$ ‚Üí MG1 must counter-rotate: $\omega_S = -N \cdot \omega_R$ ‚Üí MG1 acts as motor, ICE locked.

**Case C: Direct mechanical coupling (1:1, no MG1 torque)**
- $\omega_S = 0$ ‚Üí ICE speed = ring speed / (1+N) ‚Äî this is the natural mechanical path.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚Äî Fuel Consumption Comparison (Series vs Parallel)

### Series HEV ‚Äî Energy Path: Fuel ‚Üí ICE ‚Üí Generator ‚Üí Battery ‚Üí Motor ‚Üí Wheels

$$\eta_{\text{series}} = \eta_{\text{ICE}} \cdot \eta_{\text{gen}} \cdot \eta_{\text{batt}} \cdot \eta_{\text{inv}} \cdot \eta_{\text{mot}}$$

Plugging typical values:
- $\eta_{\text{ICE}} = 0.35$
- $\eta_{\text{gen}} = 0.90$
- $\eta_{\text{batt}} = 0.95$
- $\eta_{\text{inv}} = 0.95$
- $\eta_{\text{mot}} = 0.92$

$$\eta_{\text{series}} = 0.35 \times 0.90 \times 0.95 \times 0.95 \times 0.92 \approx 0.261 \;(26.1\%)$$

### Parallel HEV ‚Äî Direct Mechanical Path

$$\eta_{\text{parallel}} = \alpha \cdot \eta_{\text{ICE}} + (1-\alpha) \cdot \eta_{\text{EM}}$$

For $\alpha = 0.6$ (60% load from ICE), $\eta_{\text{ICE,eff}} = 0.38$, $\eta_{\text{EM}} = 0.92$:

$$\eta_{\text{parallel}} = 0.6 \times 0.38 + 0.4 \times 0.92 = 0.228 + 0.368 = 0.596 \;(59.6\%)$$

**Conclusion:** Parallel is **> 2√ó more efficient** in this idealized case. This is exactly why high-performance hybrids use parallel architectures.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ‚Äî Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery

A 1500 kg vehicle decelerates from 100 km/h to 0 km/h.

### Step 1: Convert Units

$$v_1 = 100 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{100}{3.6} = 27.78 \text{ m/s}, \quad v_2 = 0$$

### Step 2: Kinetic Energy

$$E_{\text{kinetic}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v_1^2 = 0.5 \times 1500 \times (27.78)^2$$

$$E_{\text{kinetic}} = 0.5 \times 1500 \times 771.6 = 578{,}700 \text{ J} \approx 0.161 \text{ kWh}$$

### Step 3: Recovered Energy (assuming 65% regen efficiency)

$$E_{\text{recovered}} = E_{\text{kinetic}} \times \eta_{\text{regen}} = 578{,}700 \times 0.65$$

$$E_{\text{recovered}} = 376{,}155 \text{ J} \approx 0.105 \text{ kWh}$$

### Step 4: Equivalent Fuel Saved

Petrol energy density ‚âà 35.8 MJ/L ‚Üí at ICE efficiency 35 %:

$$V_{\text{fuel saved}} = \dfrac{376{,}155}{35.8 \times 10^6 \times 0.35} = 3.00 \times 10^{-5} \text{ L} = 0.03 \text{ mL per brake event}$$

In real city driving with 30 brake events/km, this adds up to **5‚Äì10% fuel savings**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Series Hybrid ‚Äî Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fuelA[Fuel Tank] --> iceA[ICE Engine]
    iceA --> genA[Generator]
    genA --> rectA[Rectifier]
    rectA --> dcbusa[DC Bus]
    batA[Battery Pack] -.->|charge/discharge| dcbusa
    dcbusa --> invA[Inverter]
    invA --> tmA[Traction Motor]
    tmA --> gbA[Gearbox]
    gbA --> whA[Wheels]
    whA -.->|regen| tmA
    vcuA[VCU Supervisory Controller] -.controls.-> iceA
    vcuA -.controls.-> genA
    vcuA -.controls.-> tmA
    vcuA -.monitors.-> batA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** The ICE has **no mechanical path** to the wheels. Power always goes through **electrical conversion** ‚Äî this is the **defining feature** of the series hybrid.

---

## 4.2 Parallel Hybrid ‚Äî Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fuelB[Fuel Tank] --> iceB[ICE Engine]
    iceB --> cl1B[Clutch 1]
    emB[Battery] --> invB[Inverter]
    invB --> motB[Traction Motor]
    motB --> cl2B[Clutch 2]
    cl1B --> gboxB[Gearbox]
    cl2B --> gboxB
    gboxB --> whB[Wheels]
    whB -.->|regen| motB
    vcuB[VCU Controller] -.controls.-> iceB
    vcuB -.controls.-> cl1B
    vcuB -.controls.-> cl2B
    vcuB -.controls.-> motB
    vcuB -.monitors.-> emB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** Both ICE and EM are **mechanically coupled** to the wheels via clutches and a common gearbox. Either source can drive the vehicle independently or together.

---

## 4.3 Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid ‚Äî Functional Block Diagram (Toyota HSD)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fuelC[Fuel Tank] --> iceC[ICE Engine]
    iceC --> pgsC[Planetary Gear Set]
    pgsC --> sunC[Sun Gear]
    pgsC --> carC[Planet Carrier]
    pgsC --> ringC[Ring Gear]
    sunC --> mg1C[MG1 Generator-Motor]
    ringC --> mg2C[MG2 Traction Motor]
    carC -.mechanical link.-> iceC
    mg1C --> inv1C[Inverter 1]
    mg2C --> inv2C[Inverter 2]
    batC[Battery Pack] --> inv1C
    batC --> inv2C
    mg2C --> whC[Wheels]
    whC -.->|regen| mg2C
    vcuC[VCU Power Split Controller] -.controls.-> iceC
    vcuC -.controls.-> mg1C
    vcuC -.controls.-> mg2C
    vcuC -.monitors.-> batC
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key observation:** The **planetary gear set** allows ICE power to split into **two paths**:
> - **Mechanical path** (Carrier ‚Üí Ring ‚Üí Wheels) ‚Äî like a parallel HEV
> - **Electrical path** (Carrier ‚Üí Sun ‚Üí MG1 ‚Üí Battery ‚Üí MG2 ‚Üí Wheels) ‚Äî like a series HEV
>
> The VCU continuously adjusts MG1 speed to control ICE's operating point ‚Äî this is **the magic** of Toyota HSD.

---

## 4.4 Operating Mode Decision Tree (Master Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA{Vehicle Start} -->|Ignition ON| socCheckC
    socCheckC{SOC Level Check} -->|SOC > 30 percent| evmodeA[EV Only Mode]
    socCheckC -->|SOC less than 30 percent| iceStartB[Start ICE MG1 acts as starter]
    evmodeA -->|SOC drops below 20 percent| iceStartB
    iceStartB --> powersplitD[Power-Split Mode]
    powersplitD -->|Light Load| iceOnlyC[ICE Only Mode Highway Cruise]
    powersplitD -->|Heavy Load| boostModeA[Boost Mode ICE + MG2 combined]
    powersplitD -->|Braking Detected| regenModeA[Regenerative Braking Mode]
    powersplitD -->|Vehicle Stopped| idleStopA[Idle Stop ICE OFF]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **This decision tree is a typical KTU 14-mark answer diagram.** Always draw it with full labeling of arrows and conditions.

---

## 4.5 Comparison Matrix ‚Äî The Three Architectures

| Parameter | Series HEV | Parallel HEV | Series-Parallel HEV |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Mechanical ICE-Wheel link** | ‚ùå None | ‚úÖ Yes (via clutch) | ‚úÖ Yes (via PGS) |
| **Number of motors** | 1 traction + 1 generator | 1 traction (or assist) | 2 (MG1 + MG2) |
| **ICE always at optimum?** | ‚úÖ Yes | ‚ùå Not always | ‚úÖ Yes |
| **Complexity** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Cost** | High | Medium | Very High |
| **Best Application** | City buses, delivery vans | Performance cars, light trucks | Sedans, mass-market cars |
| **Regenerative Braking** | Easy | Difficult (clutch control) | Easy |
| **Transmission needed?** | No (fixed ratio) | Yes (clutch + gearbox) | No (PGS replaces it) |
| **Example** | Chevy Volt, BMW i3 REx | Honda Insight, Sonata | Toyota Prius, Camry Hybrid |
| **Efficiency (typical)** | ~26 % | ~60 % | ~45‚Äì50 % |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## üìù PART A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]**
**Define the term "Hybrid Electric Vehicle". List any four advantages of hybrid drive-trains over conventional ICE vehicles.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)** is a vehicle that uses **two or more distinct energy sources**, conventionally an **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** and an **Electric Motor (EM)** powered by an **Energy Storage System (battery)**, to drive the wheels. The two power sources work either alone or in combination under the command of a **supervisory controller** to optimize fuel economy and reduce emissions.

**Four advantages:**
1. **Improved fuel economy** (typically 30‚Äì50 % improvement over conventional ICE)
2. **Reduced emissions** (CO‚ÇÇ, NO‚Çì, HC lower by 20‚Äì90 %)
3. **Regenerative braking** recovers kinetic energy that would otherwise be lost as heat
4. **Engine idle-stop** eliminates idling fuel wastage
5. **Higher torque at low speeds** ‚Äî EM provides instant torque, eliminating turbo-lag
6. **Smaller ICE sizing** possible due to EM supplementation

> [!NOTE]
> *Examiner awards full 3 marks if you write the definition + 4 valid advantages. Always write the definition first.*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Dec 2022]**
**Explain the concept of "Degree of Hybridization" (DoH) and classify HEVs based on it.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Degree of Hybridization (DoH)** is defined as the **ratio of the peak power of the electric machine to the total peak power** of the hybrid drive-train.

$$\text{DoH} = \frac{P_{\text{EM,peak}}}{P_{\text{ICE,peak}} + P_{\text{EM,peak}}}$$

**Classification:**

| Type | DoH Range | Description |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| **Micro-hybrid** | < 5 % | Only Stop-Start + brake energy recovery (12 V system) |
| **Mild Hybrid** | 5 % ‚Äì 25 % | 48 V system, EM assists ICE (cannot drive alone) |
| **Full Hybrid** | 25 % ‚Äì 50 % | Both ICE and EM can drive wheels independently |
| **Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV)** | > 50 % | Large battery with external charging, AER 30‚Äì80 km |

---

## üìù PART B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Module 1 long-answer questions carry 14 marks and offer an INTERNAL CHOICE. Two alternative questions are provided below.**

---

### ‚úèÔ∏è QUESTION A (14 Marks) ‚Äî *On Series-Parallel (Power-Split) HEV*

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **Series-Parallel Hybrid Electric Drive-train** using a **planetary gear set**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Derive the **kinematic speed relationship** of the planetary gear set and explain how the **Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive (HSD)** uses MG1 to control the ICE operating point. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A (a):

**Construction:**

A Series-Parallel (Power-Split) HEV consists of:
1. **ICE** ‚Äî connected to the **planet carrier (C)** of the planetary gear set
2. **MG1 (Generator-Motor)** ‚Äî connected to the **sun gear (S)**
3. **MG2 (Traction Motor)** ‚Äî connected to the **ring gear (R)**, which is also the output to the wheels
4. **Battery pack** ‚Äî connected via power electronics (inverters) to MG1 and MG2
5. **Planetary Gear Set (PGS)** ‚Äî the power-split device
6. **VCU (Vehicle Control Unit)** ‚Äî supervises all energy flow

```
                ICE
                 |
            [Carrier C]
            /            \
       [Sun S]        [Ring R]
          |               |
         MG1             MG2
       Generator       Traction Motor
          |               |
      Inverter 1     Inverter 2
          \              /
            [Battery Pack]
                        |
                     Wheels
```

**Working:**

The **planetary gear set** is the heart of the system. The ICE power splits into two paths:

- **Mechanical path:** Carrier ‚Üí Ring ‚Üí MG2 ‚Üí Wheels (direct mechanical transmission, like a parallel HEV)
- **Electrical path:** Carrier ‚Üí Sun ‚Üí MG1 (acts as generator) ‚Üí Inverter ‚Üí Battery ‚Üí Inverter ‚Üí MG2 ‚Üí Wheels (like a series HEV)

By controlling **MG1's speed**, the VCU can make the **ICE rotate at any speed independent of the vehicle speed**. Thus the ICE can be maintained at its **most efficient BSFC point** (the "sweet spot") regardless of driving conditions.

**Operating modes:**
1. **Starting from rest:** ICE off, MG2 alone drives wheels (EV mode)
2. **Light load cruising:** ICE running, MG1 generates, MG2 assists wheels
3. **Heavy acceleration (Boost):** ICE + MG2 both drive wheels; MG1 may be stationary
4. **Highway cruise:** MG1 rotates freely (no torque), ICE power reaches wheels mechanically
5. **Braking:** MG2 acts as generator, charges battery through inverter
6. **Battery low:** ICE runs to charge battery through MG1

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A (b):

**Derivation of Planetary Gear Speed Equation:**

Consider the planetary gear set with:
- $\omega_S$ = angular speed of sun gear
- $\omega_C$ = angular speed of planet carrier
- $\omega_R$ = angular speed of ring gear
- $N_S, N_R$ = number of teeth on sun and ring
- $N = N_R / N_S$ = gear ratio

**Step 1:** Geometric constraint from gear meshing:

$$\dfrac{\omega_R - \omega_C}{\omega_S - \omega_C} = -\dfrac{N_S}{N_R} = -\dfrac{1}{N}$$

**Step 2:** Cross-multiply:

$$N \cdot (\omega_R - \omega_C) = -(\omega_S - \omega_C)$$

**Step 3:** Expand:

$$N \cdot \omega_R - N \cdot \omega_C = -\omega_S + \omega_C$$

**Step 4:** Group $\omega_C$ terms:

$$N \cdot \omega_R + \omega_S = (N+1) \cdot \omega_C$$

**Step 5:** Final kinematic equation:

$$\boxed{\omega_C = \dfrac{\omega_S + N \cdot \omega_R}{1 + N}}$$

**How Toyota HSD uses this:**

In the Toyota HSD:
- **Sun gear (S)** ‚Üí MG1
- **Planet carrier (C)** ‚Üí ICE
- **Ring gear (R)** ‚Üí MG2 ‚Üí Wheels

Substituting:

$$\omega_{\text{ICE}} = \dfrac{\omega_{\text{MG1}} + N \cdot \omega_{\text{MG2}}}{1 + N}$$

The **VCU controls $\omega_{\text{MG1}}$** continuously:

- To increase ICE speed ‚Üí MG1 spins faster
- To decrease ICE speed ‚Üí MG1 slows or counter-rotates
- This allows ICE to operate at **optimum BSFC point** (~ 2000‚Äì2500 RPM) **regardless of vehicle speed**

**Numerical example:** Suppose N = 2.6, vehicle at 60 km/h requires $\omega_R = 1000$ RPM, and ICE optimum is $\omega_C = 2000$ RPM. Then:

$$2000 = \dfrac{\omega_S + 2.6 \times 1000}{3.6}$$

$$\omega_S + 2600 = 7200 \implies \omega_S = 4600 \text{ RPM}$$

So MG1 must spin at **4600 RPM** to keep ICE at its 2000 RPM sweet spot while the vehicle is at 60 km/h. This is the **core intelligence** of Toyota HSD.

---

### ‚úèÔ∏è QUESTION B (14 Marks) ‚Äî *On Series HEV & Comparative Analysis*

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Series Hybrid Electric Drive-train**. Discuss its **advantages, disadvantages, and typical applications**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Compare **Series, Parallel, and Series-Parallel Hybrid drive-trains** on the basis of: (i) Mechanical complexity, (ii) Energy conversion stages, (iii) ICE operating flexibility, (iv) Fuel efficiency, (v) Cost, and (vi) Suitability for city vs highway driving. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B (a):

**Block Diagram of Series HEV:**

```
Fuel Tank ‚Üí ICE ‚Üí Generator ‚Üí Rectifier ‚Üí DC Bus
                                                ‚Üï
                                            Battery
                                                ‚Üì
                                            Inverter ‚Üí Traction Motor ‚Üí Gearbox ‚Üí Wheels
```

**Description:**

In a **Series HEV**, the **ICE is mechanically decoupled** from the wheels. The ICE drives **only a generator** (typically a 3-phase AC generator + rectifier). The electrical output charges the **battery** (in parallel) and/or feeds the **inverter**, which supplies the **traction motor** (typically PMSM or induction motor). The traction motor is the **sole mechanical driver** of the wheels through a fixed-ratio gearbox.

**Operating modes:**
1. **Pure EV mode** ‚Äî ICE off, battery ‚Üí inverter ‚Üí traction motor ‚Üí wheels
2. **Pure ICE mode (Range Extender)** ‚Äî ICE ‚Üí generator ‚Üí inverter ‚Üí traction motor ‚Üí wheels (battery floating)
3. **Hybrid mode** ‚Äî ICE charges battery + drives motor simultaneously
4. **Regenerative braking** ‚Äî motor ‚Üí generator ‚Üí battery

**Advantages:**
- ICE always operates at optimum BSFC point (constant speed, constant load)
- No transmission/clutch needed ‚Üí simple mechanical layout
- Easy regenerative braking implementation
- Lower emissions at urban speeds
- Smooth, quiet operation
- Ideal for **stop-go city driving**

**Disadvantages:**
- **Three energy conversion stages** (mechanical ‚Üí electrical ‚Üí mechanical) ‚Üí cumulative efficiency loss
- Requires **large traction motor and large battery** (full power must pass through them)
- High cost and weight
- Poor high-speed performance (energy conversion losses dominate)
- Highway cruising less efficient

**Typical Applications:**
- **Chevrolet Volt (gen 1)** ‚Äî extended-range EV
- **BMW i3 REx**
- **City buses** (Volvo, BYD e-bus with range extender)
- **Nissan e-Power** (Kicks, Note)
- **Delivery vans** with frequent stop-go

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B (b):

**Comparative Analysis Table:**

| S.No. | Parameter | Series HEV | Parallel HEV | Series-Parallel HEV |
|-------|-----------|-----------|--------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Mechanical complexity** | Low (no clutch, no gearbox) | Medium (clutch + gearbox required) | High (planetary gear + 2 motors) |
| 2 | **Energy conversion stages** | 3 (mech ‚Üí elec ‚Üí mech) | 1 (direct mechanical) | 1 or 3 (selectable by VCU) |
| 3 | **ICE operating flexibility** | Highest (always at optimum) | Limited (follows vehicle demand) | Highest (MG1 controls ICE speed) |
| 4 | **Fuel efficiency** | Lowest in class (~26%) | High (~60%) | Moderate-High (~50%) |
| 5 | **Cost** | High (large motor + battery) | Medium | Very high (2 motors + PGS) |
| 6 | **City driving suitability** | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ Excellent | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ Good | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ Very Good |
| 7 | **Highway driving suitability** | ‚òÖ‚òÖ Poor | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ Very Good | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ Excellent |
| 8 | **Regen braking** | Easy | Difficult | Easy |
| 9 | **Examples** | Chevy Volt, BMW i3 | Honda Insight | Toyota Prius |

> [!WARNING]
> **üö® KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ‚Äî Read Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "PHEV" (Plug-in) with "Parallel HEV".** PHEV refers to a vehicle with a charging port (battery > 4 kWh). "Parallel" refers to **architecture**. Many students write "PHEV = Parallel" ‚Äî this is **WRONG** and costs 2‚Äì3 marks.
>
> 2. **Do NOT draw series and parallel architectures as the same diagram.** Series has NO mechanical link between ICE and wheels ‚Äî make this crystal clear with **dashed lines vs solid lines** in your sketch.
>
> 3. **In the planetary gear question**, you **MUST derive** the kinematic equation ‚Äî just writing the formula without derivation = **lose 3 marks** in KTU valuation.
>
> 4. **For the comparison table**, fill **all 6 parameters** even if you run out of words. Tables are easiest to score full marks on.
>
> 5. **Always mention** the role of VCU / Energy Management Strategy (EMS) ‚Äî examiners specifically look for this in 14-mark questions on hybrid architectures.
>
> 6. **Standard values to remember** (must drop in answers for partial credit):
>    - $C_{rr} = 0.01$ (typical)
>    - $C_D = 0.3$ (typical sedan)
>    - $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.225$ kg/m¬≥
>    - ICE peak efficiency ‚âà 38‚Äì42 %
>    - EM efficiency ‚âà 90‚Äì95 %

---

## üîÅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block ‚Äî Read 2 hours before exam**

- [x] **Hybrid EV Definition** = Two or more energy sources + converters propelling wheels intelligently
- [x] **Three architectures**: Series, Parallel, Series-Parallel (Power-Split)
- [x] **Series** = ICE drives generator only; motor alone propels wheels
- [x] **Parallel** = ICE and EM both mechanically coupled to wheels
- [x] **Series-Parallel** = Planetary gear set splits ICE power into mech + elec paths
- [x] **DoH Formula**: $\text{DoH} = P_{EM} / (P_{ICE} + P_{EM})$
- [x] **Classifications by DoH**: Micro (<5%) ‚Üí Mild (5‚Äì25%) ‚Üí Full (25‚Äì50%) ‚Üí PHEV (>50%)
- [x] **Planetary Kinematic Eqn**: $\omega_C = (\omega_S + N \cdot \omega_R) / (1 + N)$
- [x] **Toyota HSD** mapping: Sun‚ÜíMG1, Carrier‚ÜíICE, Ring‚ÜíMG2‚ÜíWheels
- [x] **Tractive force equation** (4 components): rolling + drag + grade + inertia
- [x] **Series efficiency** ‚âà product of 5 efficiencies ‚âà 26 % (worst case)
- [x] **Parallel efficiency** ‚âà load-share weighted ‚âà 60 % (best case)
- [x] **Regenerative braking** recovers 30‚Äì70 % of kinetic energy
- [x] **BSFC sweet spot** = where ICE runs at optimum in hybrids
- [x] **Real examples**: Chevy Volt (series), Honda Insight (parallel), Toyota Prius (series-parallel)
- [x] **VCU (Vehicle Control Unit)** = brain of HEV ‚Üí executes Energy Management Strategy
- [x] **Stop-Start system** = entry-level hybrid feature (micro-hybrid)
- [x] **Air density** = **1.225 kg/m¬≥** (always remember)

### üéØ The "One-Liner" Formula Cheat for Last-Minute Reading

> *"Power flows from ICE; MG1 controls ICE; MG2 drives wheels; PGS splits; VCU decides."*

### üßÝ Frequently Asked "Trick" Topics in KTU

1. **"Why is series HEV least efficient despite ICE always at optimum?"** ‚Üí Because of **3-stage energy conversion losses** (ICE ‚Üí Gen ‚Üí Batt ‚Üí Inv ‚Üí Mot).
2. **"What happens if MG1 fails in Toyota HSD?"** ‚Üí Vehicle can still run in **parallel mode only** (limited performance), but ICE cannot be started (MG1 is the starter).
3. **"Why is DoH > 50% required for PHEV classification?"** ‚Üí Because the **all-electric range** must be substantial (>30 km) to justify plug-in infrastructure.
4. **"Why parallel HEV has higher efficiency than series-parallel at high speeds?"** ‚Üí At high speeds, the planetary gear in series-parallel still has **3-stage losses** in the electrical path, while parallel uses **direct mechanical path**.

---

> **END OF MODULE 1 ‚Äî Hybrid Electric Drive-trains: Basic Concept of Hybrid Traction**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Hybrid Drive-train Topologies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme)**
> A **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV) drive-train topology** is the *architectural configuration* of mechanical and electrical power flow paths between the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE), Electric Motor/Generator (EM/EG), Energy Storage System (ESS ‚Äî typically a battery pack), and the vehicle's final drive (wheels). The topology defines **how**, **when**, and **through which path** mechanical and electrical power is delivered to the traction wheels.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEEET722 ‚Äì Module 1)**, hybrid drive-trains are classified primarily into **four canonical topologies**:

| # | Topology | KTU Designation | Key Power Path |
|---|----------|----------------|----------------|
| 1 | Series Hybrid | **SHEV** | ICE ‚Üí Generator ‚Üí Battery/Motor ‚Üí Wheels |
| 2 | Parallel Hybrid | **PHEV** | ICE ‚Äñ Motor ‚Üí Wheels (mechanical sum) |
| 3 | Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid | **SPHEV / PSHEV** | Combines Series + Parallel via Planetary Gearset |
| 4 | Complex (Dual-Clutch / Multi-mode) Hybrid | **CHEV** | Multiple EMs with selectable modes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standardized Terminology (per SAE J1711 / ISO 23274):**
> - **SOC** ‚Äî State of Charge of the battery (dimensionless, 0 to 1)
> - **CD** ‚Äî Charge-Depleting mode (battery dominant)
> - **CS** ‚Äî Charge-Sustaining mode (ICE dominant)
> - **PGS** ‚Äî Planetary Gear Set (used in Toyota Hybrid System - THS)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy ‚Äî The "Kitchen Mixer" of Cars**
> Imagine you are kneading dough. You have **two arms (ICE and Electric Motor)**, and a single rolling pin (the wheel). 
> - In a **Series** topology, *only one arm ever touches the pin at a time* ‚Äî the other arm charges a battery (like resting on the counter).
> - In a **Parallel** topology, *both arms grab the pin at the same time* and push together, sharing the load.
> - In a **Series-Parallel (Power-Split)** topology, *one arm holds a gear-box that lets it switch between working alone, helping, or just charging* ‚Äî like a smart kitchen assistant that decides the best strategy.
> - In a **Complex** topology, *you have three arms* and a clutch ‚Äî you can dynamically reconfigure who does what.

**Why are these topologies studied?**
Each topology represents a different **engineering trade-off** between:
- **Efficiency** (fuel economy in **km/litre** or **mpge**)
- **Complexity** (number of components ‚Üí cost in **USD**)
- **Packaging** (volume in **litres** and mass in **kg**)
- **Driveability** (0‚Äì100 km/h acceleration in **seconds**)
- **Emissions** (CO‚ÇÇ in **g/km**)

The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes that *no single topology is universally optimal* ‚Äî the choice depends on the vehicle mission (city, highway, heavy-duty, etc.).

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following constants and metrics recur in every KTU derivation for HEV topologies:

- **Energy density of gasoline** ‚âà **32 MJ/litre** (‚âà 8.9 kWh/litre)
- **Energy density of Li-ion battery** ‚âà **0.5‚Äì0.8 MJ/kg**
- **Round-trip efficiency of Li-ion ESS** ‚âà **85‚Äì95 %**
- **Peak efficiency of modern ICE** ‚âà **35‚Äì40 %** (at optimal RPM/Load)
- **Peak efficiency of PMSM Electric Motor** ‚âà **92‚Äì97 %**
- **Standard test cycle**: **NEDC**, **WLTP**, **FTP-75**, **JC08**
- **Anthropogenic CO‚ÇÇ limit (Euro 7)** ‚â§ **93 g/km** (passenger cars)
- **Tractive force equation**: $F_t = m \cdot a + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$ where all symbols carry their usual SI units.
