# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Topology-Wise Operational Logic Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Series Hybrid (SHEV) ‚Äî "The Electric Train with a Range Extender"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** In a Series Hybrid, **the ICE is mechanically decoupled from the wheels**. The ICE drives **only** a generator (or a generator-integrated motor), which charges the battery. The traction motor is the **sole** mechanical link to the wheels.

**Operating Modes:**
1. **Pure Electric Mode:** ICE OFF ‚Üí Battery ‚Üí Motor ‚Üí Wheels (zero tailpipe emission).
2. **Range Extender Mode:** ICE ON ‚Üí Generator ‚Üí Battery (sustaining) + Motor ‚Üí Wheels.
3. **Hybrid Boost Mode:** ICE ON ‚Üí Generator + Battery ‚Üí Motor ‚Üí Wheels (peak power demand).
4. **Regenerative Braking Mode:** Wheels ‚Üí Motor (acting as generator) ‚Üí Battery.

**Why Series?**
- The ICE can operate at its **constant optimal efficiency point** (BSFC map "sweet spot"), regardless of vehicle speed ‚Äî this is called **thermodynamic decoupling**.
- Simple mechanical layout (no multi-gear transmission required).
- Excellent for **city buses, delivery vans** (Nissan e-Power, BMW i3 REx, Chevrolet Volt gen-1).

**Why NOT Series?**
- **Three energy conversions** (mechanical ‚Üí electrical ‚Üí mechanical) occur in series ‚Äî cumulative losses ‚âà **15‚Äì20 %**.
- Requires **large, expensive traction motor** (rated for full vehicle power).
- Highway efficiency suffers.

---

### 2.1.2 Parallel Hybrid (PHEV) ‚Äî "Two Engines, One Driveline"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** In a Parallel Hybrid, **the ICE and Electric Motor are mechanically coupled** to the wheels through a torque-additing device (clutch, gear train, or chain). Both can deliver power **simultaneously and directly** to the wheels.

**Operating Modes:**
1. **ICE-Only Mode:** ICE ‚Üí Clutch ‚Üí Transmission ‚Üí Wheels.
2. **Motor-Only Mode (EV mode):** Motor ‚Üí Transmission ‚Üí Wheels.
3. **Combined Mode:** ICE + Motor ‚Üí Torque Sum ‚Üí Wheels (peak torque assist).
4. **Power Split / Generator Mode:** ICE ‚Üí Clutch ‚Üí Wheels + (excess) ‚Üí Motor (acting as generator) ‚Üí Battery.
5. **Regenerative Braking:** Wheels ‚Üí Motor ‚Üí Battery.

**Why Parallel?**
- Only **two energy conversions** in combined mode ‚Üí higher overall efficiency.
- Smaller traction motor (sized for assist only, e.g., 30‚Äì50 % of ICE power).
- Excellent for **highway cruising** where ICE efficiency is naturally good.
- Examples: **Honda Insight (1st gen), Honda Civic Hybrid, Hyundai Sonata Hybrid**.

**Why NOT Parallel?**
- ICE is still mechanically linked ‚Üí **cannot be decoupled** to operate at constant optimum point.
- Clutch control is complex ‚Üí **shift shock** possible.
- Limited EV-only range.

---

### 2.1.3 Series-Parallel / Power-Split Hybrid (SPHEV) ‚Äî "The Best of Both Worlds"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The Series-Parallel topology uses a **Planetary Gear Set (PGS)** as a *continuously variable mechanical power splitter* (analogous to a **differential**). It enables **all four operating modes** ‚Äî pure EV, pure ICE, series, parallel ‚Äî *simultaneously* without discrete gear shifts.

**Components:**
- **Sun gear** ‚Üí connected to Motor-Generator 1 (**MG1**, acts as generator + starter).
- **Planet carrier** ‚Üí connected to ICE.
- **Ring gear** ‚Üí connected to Motor-Generator 2 (**MG2**, traction motor) ‚Üí final drive ‚Üí wheels.
- **Battery** ‚Üí DC bus connecting MG1 and MG2.

**Kinematic Constraint (Willis Equation for PGS):**
$$\omega_s \cdot N_s + \omega_r \cdot N_r = \omega_c \cdot (N_s + N_r)$$

where $\omega_s, \omega_r, \omega_c$ are angular velocities and $N_s, N_r$ are tooth counts of sun and ring gears.

**Operating Modes (Toyota Hybrid System / THS-II):**
1. **EV Mode:** MG1 locked ‚Üí ICE OFF ‚Üí MG2 drives wheels.
2. **ICE Cruise Mode:** MG1 spins freely (zero torque) ‚Üí ICE drives wheels directly through PGS.
3. **Power-Split (Series-like):** ICE ‚Üí MG1 (generator) + MG2 (traction) ‚Üí wheels.
4. **Boost Mode:** ICE + MG2 ‚Üí wheels (parallel-like).
5. **Regenerative Braking:** Wheels ‚Üí MG2 ‚Üí battery.
6. **Battery Charging at Standstill:** ICE ‚Üí MG1 ‚Üí battery (parking charging).

**Real-World Example:** **Toyota Prius (1997‚Äìpresent), Camry Hybrid, Ford Escape Hybrid (1st gen)**. Toyota has sold **> 20 million** THS-equipped vehicles globally as of 2024.

---

### 2.1.4 Complex Hybrid (CHEV) ‚Äî "The Fully Reconfigurable Driveline"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** A Complex Hybrid incorporates **two or more electric machines** and **multiple clutch packs**, allowing *mode-switching* between series, parallel, and EV operation *on-the-fly*. It is the most flexible but most expensive topology.

**Examples:**
- **Chevrolet Volt (gen-2, 2016)** ‚Äî Voltec II system with two MGs and 3 clutches.
- **Toyota Hybrid System-II (THS-II) with rear MG3** ‚Äî adds AWD capability.
- **Hyundai TMED (Transmission-Mounted Electric Device)** ‚Äî 6-speed DCT + EM.

**Why Complex?**
- Optimizes for *every* driving condition (city, highway, climb, off-road).
- High-performance capability (e.g., **Toyota Highlander Hybrid** AWD).
- Modular ‚Äî can be adapted for PHEV/BEV architectures.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master this table ‚Äî at least 30 % of KTU Module-1 marks come from these equations.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units / Boundary |
|---|--------------------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | $P_{wheel} = F_t \cdot v$ | Tractive power at wheels | W (where $F_t$ in N, $v$ in m/s) |
| 2 | $F_t = m \cdot a + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + \mu m g \cos\theta$ | Total tractive force | N (with $\rho$ = 1.225 kg/m¬≥ air density) |
| 3 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{batt} \cdot \eta_{mot} \cdot \eta_{trans}$ | Series drivetrain chain efficiency | dimensionless; each term $\leq 1$ |
| 4 | $P_{ICE,opt} = P_{wheel} / \eta_{series}$ | Series ICE sizing (for range-extender) | W |
| 5 | $P_{ICE} + P_{mot} = P_{wheel} / \eta_{trans}$ | Parallel torque/power summation | W (for combined mode) |
| 6 | $\omega_s N_s + \omega_r N_r = \omega_c (N_s + N_r)$ | Planetary gear kinematic (Willis) | rad/s; valid for PGS |
| 7 | $R_{PGS} = N_r / N_s$ | PGS speed ratio | dimensionless (typically $R \approx 2.0$‚Äì$2.6$) |
| 8 | $\dot{m}_{fuel} = P_{ICE} / (Q_{LHV} \cdot \eta_{ICE})$ | Fuel mass flow rate | kg/s; $Q_{LHV}$ ‚âà 43 MJ/kg for gasoline |
| 9 | $CO_2 = \dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot 3.16$ | Specific CO‚ÇÇ emission | kg (per kg fuel) |
| 10 | $E_{batt} = \int V_{oc}(SOC) \cdot I \, dt$ | Battery energy throughput | Wh or J |
| 11 | $SOC(t) = SOC_0 - \tfrac{1}{Q_{batt}} \int_0^t I \, dt$ | Coulomb-counting SOC | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\eta_{regen} = \tfrac{E_{recov}}{E_{kinetic}}$ | Regenerative braking efficiency | dimensionless (typically 0.3‚Äì0.7) |
| 13 | $P_{MG1} = P_{ICE} \cdot (1 + R_{PGS})$ | MG1 power (in power-split) | W (sign convention: gen = +ve) |
| 14 | $v_{max,EV} = \omega_{mot,max} \cdot r_{wheel} / G$ | Pure EV top speed | m/s (with $G$ = gear ratio) |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Power-Split Speed Relationship in a Planetary Gear Set

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Derive the relationship between ICE speed, MG1 speed, and MG2 (wheel) speed in a Toyota Hybrid System using a planetary gear set."*

### Step 1 ‚Äî Identify the Three Members of the PGS
The planetary gear set (PGS) has three rotating members:
- **Sun gear** (S) ‚Äî connected to MG1.
- **Planet carrier** (C) ‚Äî connected to ICE.
- **Ring gear** (R) ‚Äî connected to MG2 (traction motor) and final drive.

Let the sun gear have $N_s$ teeth and the ring gear have $N_r$ teeth. The number of planet gear teeth $N_p$ satisfies:
$$N_r = N_s + 2 N_p$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Apply the Willis Equation (Fundamental Kinematic Constraint)
For a planetary gear set, the Willis equation is the **kinematic identity** that must always hold:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\omega_s - \omega_c}{\omega_r - \omega_c} &= -\frac{N_r}{N_s} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Rearranging (multiply both sides by $(\omega_r - \omega_c)$):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_s - \omega_c &= -\frac{N_r}{N_s} (\omega_r - \omega_c) \\
\omega_s - \omega_c &= -\frac{N_r}{N_s} \omega_r + \frac{N_r}{N_s} \omega_c \\
\omega_s &= \omega_c \left(1 + \frac{N_r}{N_s}\right) - \frac{N_r}{N_s} \omega_r \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Define the PGS Speed Ratio
Let $R = N_r / N_s$ (positive, typically 2.0‚Äì2.6 for THS). Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_s = \omega_c (1 + R) - R \, \omega_r
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 ‚Äî Substitute Physical Quantities
- $\omega_s = \omega_{MG1}$ (sun gear = MG1 speed)
- $\omega_c = \omega_{ICE}$ (carrier = ICE speed)
- $\omega_r = \omega_{MG2} = \omega_{wheels} \cdot G_{FD}$ (ring = MG2 speed = wheel speed √ó final drive ratio)

Therefore:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_{MG1} = (1 + R) \cdot \omega_{ICE} - R \cdot \omega_{wheels} \cdot G_{FD}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 ‚Äî Solve for Each Operating Mode

> **Mode A: Pure EV (ICE OFF, vehicle stationary then accelerating)**

In pure EV mode, MG1 is held **stationary** (it acts as a reaction element). The ICE is OFF, so $\omega_{ICE} = 0$.

Set $\omega_{MG1} = 0$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= (1 + R) \cdot 0 - R \cdot \omega_{wheels} \cdot G_{FD} \\
0 &= -R \cdot \omega_{wheels} \cdot G_{FD} \\
\Rightarrow \omega_{wheels} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

This means **the vehicle cannot move in pure EV mode with the simplest PGS configuration** ‚Äî the practical solution is that **MG1 is held fixed (not rotated) and MG2 spins the ring gear** while MG1 acts as a static reaction element. In a more detailed analysis, MG1 is allowed to spin in the opposite direction with a controlled torque, and ICE is disconnected by a lockup clutch.

> **Mode B: Steady ICE Cruise (MG1 spins freely, zero torque)**

In steady cruise, MG1 produces **zero torque** (free spin) and the ICE drives the ring gear (and thus the wheels) directly. From the power balance:
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{ICE} &= P_{wheels} + P_{MG1} \quad \text{with } P_{MG1} = 0 \\
\Rightarrow P_{ICE} &= P_{wheels}
\end{aligned}
$$

The speed ratio in this case is **fixed** by the PGS geometry:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\omega_{wheels}}{\omega_{ICE}} &= \frac{1}{G_{FD}} \cdot \frac{1}{(1+R)} \cdot \text{(in some arrangements)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

(Exact expression depends on whether MG1 is locked or free-spun.)

> **Mode C: Power-Split (Series-like, ICE at constant optimum)**

The ICE operates at $\omega_{ICE,opt} = \text{const}$ (say 2500 rpm). Part of the ICE power goes to the wheels via the ring gear (mechanical path), and the rest goes to MG1 (electrical path ‚Üí MG2 ‚Üí wheels).

Power balance:
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{ICE} &= P_{mech,to\,wheels} + P_{MG1} \\
P_{MG1} &= P_{ICE} - P_{mech,to\,wheels}
\end{aligned}
$$

Torque balance on the carrier (assuming reaction torque on MG1):
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{ICE} &= T_{wheels,mech} \cdot \frac{1}{(1+R)} + T_{MG1} \cdot \frac{R}{(1+R)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of units:** All terms in the PGS speed and torque equations are **dimensionless ratios** ‚Äî this confirms the equations are pure kinematics, consistent with the KTU Module-1 learning outcome CO1: *"Understand the various hybrid drive-train topologies."*

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Series Hybrid Sizing)

> [!TIP]
> **Typical KTU 14-mark sub-question:** *"A series hybrid city bus has a total mass of 12,000 kg, a frontal area of 7.5 m¬≤, $C_d$ = 0.6, and a maximum speed of 80 km/h. The traction motor efficiency is 92 %, the generator efficiency is 90 %, the battery round-trip efficiency is 88 %, and the ICE efficiency is 38 % (LHV of diesel = 42.5 MJ/kg). Compute the fuel consumption in litres/100 km at constant 50 km/h on a flat road."*

**Given:**
- $m = 12{,}000$ kg
- $A = 7.5$ m¬≤, $C_d = 0.6$
- $v = 50$ km/h = $13.89$ m/s
- $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m¬≥
- $\eta_{mot} = 0.92$, $\eta_{gen} = 0.90$, $\eta_{batt} = 0.88$, $\eta_{ICE} = 0.38$
- $Q_{LHV} = 42.5$ MJ/kg, $\rho_{diesel} = 0.835$ kg/litre

**Step 1 ‚Äî Compute the tractive force at 50 km/h on flat road:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 \quad \text{(no slope, no acceleration)} \\
&= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.6 \times 7.5 \times (13.89)^2 \\
&= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.6 \times 7.5 \times 192.93 \\
&= 531.6 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute wheel power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{wheel} &= F_t \cdot v = 531.6 \times 13.89 = 7383 \text{ W} \approx 7.38 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Back-calculate the ICE power (series chain):**

The energy path is: ICE ‚Üí Generator ‚Üí Battery ‚Üí Motor ‚Üí Wheels.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{chain} &= \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{batt} \cdot \eta_{mot} \\
&= 0.38 \times 0.90 \times 0.88 \times 0.92 \\
&= 0.2768
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{ICE} &= P_{wheel} / \eta_{chain} = 7.38 / 0.2768 = 26.66 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Compute fuel mass flow rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{fuel} &= P_{ICE} / (Q_{LHV} \cdot \eta_{ICE}) \\
&= 26.66 \times 10^3 / (42.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.38) \\
&= 26.66 \times 10^3 / 16.15 \times 10^6 \\
&= 1.651 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Compute fuel consumption in litres/100 km:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}_{fuel} &= \dot{m}_{fuel} / \rho_{diesel} = 1.651 \times 10^{-3} / 0.835 = 1.978 \times 10^{-3} \text{ L/s} \\
\text{Per 100 km:} \quad \dot{V}_{100} &= \dot{V}_{fuel} \times (100{,}000 / v) \\
&= 1.978 \times 10^{-3} \times (100{,}000 / 13.89) \\
&= 14.24 \text{ L/100 km}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** A purely series hybrid city bus at 50 km/h flat consumes **~14.2 L/100 km**. A conventional diesel bus of similar mass would consume **~22‚Äì25 L/100 km** ‚Äî confirming the **~35 % fuel economy benefit** of the series topology at constant speed.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Diagram: Series Hybrid Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ICE Engine] -->|mechanical shaft| B[Generator G]
    B -->|AC electrical| C[Inverter]
    C -->|DC bus| D[Battery Pack]
    D -->|DC bus| C
    C -->|3-phase AC| E[Traction Motor M]
    E -->|mechanical shaft| F[Final Drive and Wheels]
    W[Wheels] -->|kinetic energy| E
    E -.->|regen mode| D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** In the series topology, the ICE **never** mechanically touches the wheels. The **only mechanical path** from the engine to the road is ICE ‚Üí Generator ‚Üí (electrical) ‚Üí Motor ‚Üí Wheels.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block-Diagram: Parallel Hybrid Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ICE Engine] -->|shaft 1| C[Torque Coupler / Clutch]
    B[Traction Motor M] -->|shaft 2| C
    C -->|combined shaft| D[Multi-speed Transmission]
    D -->|output shaft| E[Final Drive and Wheels]
    F[Battery Pack] -->|DC| G[Inverter]
    G -->|3-phase AC| B
    W[Wheels] -->|recapture| B
    B -.->|regen / charge| F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The **torque coupler** is the heart of the parallel topology. Both ICE and Motor feed mechanical power simultaneously to the wheels in combined mode.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block-Diagram: Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid ‚Äî Toyota THS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[ICE Engine] -->|carrier shaft| H[Planetary Gear Set PGS]
    B[MG1 Generator Starter] -->|sun gear| H
    H -->|ring gear| C[MG2 Traction Motor]
    C -->|shaft| D[Final Drive and Wheels]
    D -.->|reverse power| C
    E[Battery Pack HV] -->|DC bus| F[Inverter 1 MG1]
    F -->|3-phase AC| B
    E -->|DC bus| G[Inverter 2 MG2]
    G -->|3-phase AC| C
    C -.->|regen| E
    B -.->|charge mode| E
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The **Planetary Gear Set (H)** acts as a *mechanical differentiator* ‚Äî it splits the ICE power continuously between a *mechanical path* (to the ring gear ‚Üí wheels) and an *electrical path* (to MG1 ‚Üí battery ‚Üí MG2 ‚Üí wheels). This is what gives the THS its "e-CVT" (electronic continuously variable transmission) behavior.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block-Diagram: Complex Hybrid (Voltec II / THS-II AWD)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ICE Engine] -->|clutch 1| B[Input Splitter]
    B -->|path A| C[MG1]
    B -->|path B| D[MG2]
    C -->|sun gear| E[PGS]
    D -->|ring gear| E
    E -->|carrier output| F[Clutch 2]
    F --> G[Final Drive Front]
    H[MG3 Rear Motor] -->|rear shaft| I[Final Drive Rear]
    J[Battery Pack] -->|DC bus| K[Power Electronics Unit]
    K --> C
    K --> D
    K --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Three clutches (1, 2, plus an optional brake clutch) and **three** electric machines (MG1, MG2, MG3) enable **AWD, EV, Series, Parallel, and Power-Split** modes all in a single driveline. This is the **most flexible** topology.

---

## 4.5 Comparative Mermaid: All Four Topologies Side-by-Side

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1[Series SHEV]
        A1[ICE] --> B1[GEN]
        B1 --> D1[BAT]
        D1 --> E1[MOT]
        E1 --> F1[WHEELS]
    end
    subgraph S2[Parallel PHEV]
        A2[ICE] --> C2[COUPLER]
        B2[MOT] --> C2
        C2 --> F2[WHEELS]
    end
    subgraph S3[Series Parallel SPHEV]
        A3[ICE] --> H3[PGS]
        B3[MG1] --> H3
        H3 --> C3[MG2]
        C3 --> F3[WHEELS]
    end
    subgraph S4[Complex CHEV]
        A4[ICE] --> C4[CLUTCH1]
        C4 --> H4[SPLITTER]
        H4 --> B4[MG1]
        H4 --> D4[MG2]
        B4 --> F4[WHEELS]
        D4 --> F4
        E4[MG3] --> F4
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A ‚Äî 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define a Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV) and list its two main disadvantages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle is one in which the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) is **mechanically decoupled** from the drive wheels. The ICE drives a generator only; the electrical energy produced is used to charge the battery and/or feed the traction motor, which alone propels the vehicle.
*(1 mark for definition)*

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Triple energy conversion** (mechanical ‚Üí electrical ‚Üí mechanical) results in cumulative losses of 15‚Äì20 %, reducing highway efficiency. *(1 mark)*
2. The traction motor must be **sized for the full vehicle power** (e.g., 100 kW for a compact car), increasing cost and mass. *(1 mark)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2024, Supplementary]**
**What is the role of a Planetary Gear Set (PGS) in a Toyota Hybrid System (THS)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Planetary Gear Set acts as a **continuously variable mechanical power splitter** between the ICE, MG1 (generator), and the wheels (via MG2). *(1 mark)*

It allows the ICE to operate at its **most efficient speed‚Äìload point** independent of the vehicle's instantaneous speed, by splitting the ICE's mechanical power into two paths:
- A **mechanical path** directly to the wheels (via the ring gear), and
- An **electrical path** through MG1 ‚Üí battery ‚Üí MG2 ‚Üí wheels. *(2 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B ‚Äî 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A ‚Äî 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2022, Main Exam]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Series-Parallel (Power-Split) hybrid drive-train topology**. Discuss its **four** major operating modes. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **kinematic relationship** between the ICE, MG1, and wheel speeds in a planetary gear set-based power-split hybrid. Show that the ICE speed can be held constant while the wheel speed varies. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (a) [7 marks]

**Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.3):**
The THS consists of: ICE, MG1, MG2, Planetary Gear Set (PGS), Battery, Inverters, and Final Drive. *(1 mark)*

**Operating Modes:**

1. **Pure EV Mode** *(1.5 marks)*: ICE OFF. MG1 is held as a reaction (or spun in reverse via battery). MG2 receives battery power and drives the ring gear ‚Üí wheels. Zero tailpipe emission.

2. **ICE Cruise Mode** *(1.5 marks)*: ICE ON, MG1 spins freely (zero torque). Power flows mechanically from ICE ‚Üí carrier ‚Üí ring gear ‚Üí wheels. MG2 may add assist or regen.

3. **Power-Split Mode (Series-like)** *(1.5 marks)*: ICE is held at $\omega_{ICE,opt}$. Part of the ICE power goes mechanically to wheels; the rest is converted by MG1 to electrical, fed through the battery to MG2, and delivered to wheels.

4. **Boost Mode (Parallel-like)** *(1.5 marks)*: ICE + MG2 deliver power simultaneously to wheels (e.g., during hard acceleration). MG1 manages the power balance.

---

#### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 ‚Äî Identify PGS Members:** Sun (MG1), Carrier (ICE), Ring (MG2 ‚Üí wheels). *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 ‚Äî Apply Willis Equation:** *(1 mark)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\omega_{sun} - \omega_{carrier}}{\omega_{ring} - \omega_{carrier}} &= -\frac{N_r}{N_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Substitute and Rearrange:** *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_{MG1} &= (1 + R) \cdot \omega_{ICE} - R \cdot \omega_{wheels} \cdot G_{FD}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $R = N_r / N_s$.

**Step 4 ‚Äî Show ICE Speed Constancy:** *(3 marks)*

Solving for $\omega_{ICE}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_{ICE} &= \frac{\omega_{MG1} + R \cdot \omega_{wheels} \cdot G_{FD}}{1 + R}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the control system regulates $\omega_{MG1}$ as a function of $\omega_{wheels}$ according to:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_{MG1} = (1 + R) \cdot \omega_{ICE,opt} - R \cdot \omega_{wheels} \cdot G_{FD}
\end{aligned}
$$

then $\omega_{ICE} = \omega_{ICE,opt} = \text{constant}$, **independent of $\omega_{wheels}$**. This is the **fundamental advantage** of the power-split topology ‚Äî the ICE can be operated at its best BSFC point for *all* vehicle speeds. ‚àé

---

### QUESTION B ‚Äî 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Compare the **Series** and **Parallel** hybrid drive-train topologies under the following heads: (i) mechanical coupling, (ii) ICE efficiency, (iii) component count, (iv) suitable application. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A parallel hybrid car has a total mass of 1500 kg, ICE power of 75 kW, motor power of 30 kW, and a combined driveline efficiency of 88 %. The car accelerates from 0 to 100 km/h in 9.5 seconds on a flat road. Assuming rolling resistance coefficient $\mu = 0.012$ and $C_d A = 0.7$ m¬≤, compute the **energy consumed in kWh** during the 0‚Äì100 km/h acceleration event. Neglect driveline inertia and wind speed. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (a) [7 marks]

| Head | Series (SHEV) | Parallel (PHEV) |
|------|----------------|------------------|
| **(i) Mechanical coupling** *(2 marks)* | ICE is **not** mechanically coupled to wheels; only via generator ‚Üí motor | ICE and motor are **both** mechanically coupled to wheels via a torque coupler |
| **(ii) ICE efficiency** *(2 marks)* | ICE can run at **constant optimal point** (decoupled) ‚Üí 38‚Äì40 % | ICE is **load-following** ‚Üí 25‚Äì35 % average |
| **(iii) Component count** *(1.5 marks)* | 1 EM, 1 GEN, 1 battery, 1 inverter | 1 EM, 1 battery, 1 clutch, 1 multi-gear transmission |
| **(iv) Suitable application** *(1.5 marks)* | **City buses, delivery vans, range-extender EVs** (constant speed) | **Sedans, highway cruisers** (e.g., Honda Civic Hybrid) |

---

#### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $m = 1500$ kg
- $v_f = 100$ km/h = 27.78 m/s
- $t = 9.5$ s
- $\mu = 0.012$, $C_d A = 0.7$ m¬≤, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m¬≥
- $\eta_{driveline} = 0.88$

**Step 1 ‚Äî Compute average velocity:** *(1 mark)*
$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{avg} = 27.78 / 2 = 13.89 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Distance traveled:** *(0.5 marks)*
$$
\begin{aligned}
d = v_{avg} \cdot t = 13.89 \times 9.5 = 131.96 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Average acceleration:** *(0.5 marks)*
$$
\begin{aligned}
a = v_f / t = 27.78 / 9.5 = 2.924 \text{ m/s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Average tractive force:** *(1 mark)*
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{t,avg} &= m \cdot a + \mu m g + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v_{avg}^2 \\
&= 1500 \times 2.924 + 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 + 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.7 \times (13.89)^2 \\
&= 4386 + 176.6 + 82.7 \\
&= 4645.3 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Average tractive power at wheels:** *(1 mark)*
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{wheel,avg} &= F_{t,avg} \cdot v_{avg} = 4645.3 \times 13.89 = 64{,}524 \text{ W} \approx 64.5 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Energy at wheels:** *(1 mark)*
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{wheel} &= P_{wheel,avg} \cdot t = 64{,}524 \times 9.5 = 613{,}000 \text{ J} = 0.170 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 ‚Äî Energy at source (battery + ICE):** *(1 mark)*
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{source} &= E_{wheel} / \eta_{driveline} = 0.170 / 0.88 = 0.193 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $E_{source} \approx \mathbf{0.19 \text{ kWh}}$ for the 0‚Äì100 km/h acceleration event. This is why EVs with a **~50 kWh** battery can perform hundreds of such accelerations.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Series vs Series-Parallel:** A common mistake is calling a Series-Parallel system a "Series system with two motors." Always emphasize the **planetary gear set** as the differentiator.
> 2. **Forgetting units in the Willis equation:** Many students write $\omega_{sun} - \omega_{ring} = \omega_{carrier}$ ‚Äî this is **dimensionally inconsistent** in general. Always carry the **tooth-count ratio** $N_r / N_s$.
> 3. **Ignoring rolling resistance:** In 0‚Äì100 km/h problems, $\mu m g$ is often **20‚Äì30 %** of the total tractive force ‚Äî omitting it loses 1‚Äì2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing MG1 and MG2:** MG1 is the **generator** (sun gear); MG2 is the **tractor** (ring gear). Inverting these in the diagram costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Not specifying driveline efficiency:** A numerical answer without $\eta_{driveline}$ applied is **incomplete** ‚Äî the examiner deducts 1 mark for "missing efficiency chain."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print & Carry):**

- ‚úÖ A **hybrid drive-train topology** = the *architecture* connecting ICE, EM(s), battery, and wheels.
- ‚úÖ **Series**: ICE decoupled ‚Üí ICE ‚Üí GEN ‚Üí BATT ‚Üí MOT ‚Üí WHEELS. Best for **city** use.
- ‚úÖ **Parallel**: ICE and EM both mechanical to wheels via a **clutch/coupler**. Best for **highway** use.
- ‚úÖ **Series-Parallel (Power-Split)**: Uses a **Planetary Gear Set (PGS)**; combines both topologies; ICE at constant optimum.
- ‚úÖ **Complex**: Multiple EMs + clutches ‚Üí reconfigurable modes; highest flexibility and cost.
- ‚úÖ **Willis Equation (PGS)**: $\omega_s N_s + \omega_r N_r = \omega_c (N_s + N_r)$.
- ‚úÖ **MG1** = Sun gear = **Generator/Starter**; **MG2** = Ring gear = **Traction Motor**.
- ‚úÖ **Toyota Prius** is the **canonical example** of Power-Split; **Honda Insight** is the **canonical example** of Parallel.
- ‚úÖ **Tractive force** has **four** components: inertia, slope, aerodynamic drag, rolling resistance.
- ‚úÖ **Regenerative braking efficiency** typically 30‚Äì70 %; never 100 % (losses in motor, inverter, battery).
- ‚úÖ **Series chain efficiency** $\eta_{chain} = \eta_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{batt} \cdot \eta_{mot}$ ‚Äî usually **< 0.30** overall.
- ‚úÖ **PGS speed ratio** $R = N_r / N_s$ ‚âà 2.0‚Äì2.6 for THS.
- ‚úÖ **Best thermal efficiency of mass-production gasoline ICE** = 40 % (Toyota 2ZR-FXE), achieved *only* with the power-split topology.
- ‚úÖ **EV-only range**: Series > Parallel > Power-Split (HEV mode).
- ‚úÖ **Cost index (rel.)**: Series (1.0) < Parallel (1.2) < Series-Parallel (1.5) < Complex (2.0).
- ‚úÖ **CO‚ÇÇ (g/km) ranking** (typical): Complex (60‚Äì90) < Series-Parallel (70‚Äì95) < Series (80‚Äì100) < Parallel (90‚Äì120).
- ‚úÖ **FAME-II (India)** subsidises HEV/PHEV/BEV under categories M1 and N1.
- ‚úÖ **SOC window** for HEV batteries: typically **30 %‚Äì80 %** (charge-sustaining) to maximise cycle life.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Flow Control in Various Hybrid Drive-train Topologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Power Flow Control in Various Hybrid Drive-train Topologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (OEEET722 - Hybrid and Electric Vehicles), **Power Flow Control in Hybrid Drive-train Topologies** refers to the systematic regulation, distribution, and management of mechanical and electrical power among multiple energy sources and conversion devices within a hybrid electric vehicle (HEV). These sources typically include the **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)**, one or more **Electric Machines (M/G ‚Äî Motor/Generator)**, the **Energy Storage System (ESS ‚Äî Battery or Ultracapacitor)**, and the mechanical transmission leading to the **Driving Wheels**.

Power flow control is the supervisory brain of any hybrid drive-train. It dictates *how much* power each component contributes, *when* it contributes, and *under what operating conditions* ‚Äî all while honouring the driver's torque demand, the State of Charge (SOC) of the battery, the efficiency maps of each component, and the emissions targets of the ICE.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The student must be able to analyse and compare the **Series, Parallel, Series-Parallel (Power-Split), and Complex Hybrid** drive-train topologies with respect to their **power flow paths, control objectives, and energy management strategies**. This is a high-weightage topic for Part B (14-mark) questions in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview ‚Äî The "Orchestra Conductor" Analogy

Imagine a hybrid vehicle as a **musical orchestra** performing a symphony. The orchestra has several talented musicians:

- The **ICE** is a powerful but slightly sluggish and "noisy" brass section ‚Äî excellent for sustained, heavy passages but inefficient for soft, delicate moments.
- The **Electric Motor (EM)** is a nimble, quick-response violin section ‚Äî perfect for instant attacks and quiet passages, but limited in endurance if asked to play too loudly for too long.
- The **Battery (ESS)** is the music stand holding the sheet music ‚Äî it stores the energy (sheet music/charge) and dispenses it as needed.
- The **Generator** is a backstage assistant who can recharge the music stand using the brass section's leftover sound energy.
- The **Wheels** are the audience demanding a particular volume (torque/speed) at every instant.

The **Power Flow Controller (PFC) / Hybrid Control Unit (HCU)** is the **Conductor** standing at the podium. The conductor decides:
1. Which musicians play (which power source is active).
2. How loudly each one plays (how much power each delivers).
3. When to switch between solo and ensemble (operating mode transitions).
4. How to keep the music stand (battery) charged within healthy limits.

Without a conductor, the orchestra would produce chaos. Similarly, without intelligent power flow control, a hybrid vehicle would waste fuel, drain its battery, and fail to meet performance or emissions targets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics used in this Module:**
> - **Power (P)** ‚Äî measured in **kW** (kilowatts).
> - **Torque (T)** ‚Äî measured in **Nm** (Newton-metres).
> - **Speed (œâ)** ‚Äî measured in **rad/s** (radians/second).
> - **State of Charge (SOC)** ‚Äî dimensionless ratio, typically maintained between **0.3 and 0.8**.
> - **Mechanical Power Equation:** $P = T \cdot \omega$ (fundamental relationship used everywhere).

## 1.3 Why is Power Flow Control Necessary?

A hybrid vehicle has **multiple energy sources** that can supply power to the wheels in **many combinations**. For example, in a parallel hybrid, the wheels can be driven by:

1. ICE alone.
2. EM alone.
3. ICE + EM together.
4. ICE charging the battery while also driving the wheels.
5. Regenerative braking ‚Äî wheels driving the EM as a generator to charge the battery.

Each of these modes has a different fuel efficiency, emissions profile, and component-loading implication. Power flow control selects the **optimal mode in real time** based on the driver's demand and the system's state.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Power Flow Control Objectives (The "Five Goals")

The Hybrid Control Unit (HCU) executes power flow control to achieve **five simultaneous engineering objectives**. KTU examiners frequently ask students to *list and explain* these objectives:

1. **Fuel Minimisation** ‚Äî Operate the ICE at its Best Operating Line (BOL) / Optimal Operating Point (OOP) where Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) is lowest.
2. **Emissions Reduction** ‚Äî Avoid ICE operation in low-efficiency, high-emission zones (cold start, idling, high load transients).
3. **Battery SOC Sustenance** ‚Äî Keep SOC within a healthy band (e.g., 0.3 ‚Äì 0.8) using a charge-sustaining (CS) strategy, preventing over-discharge or over-charge.
4. **Drivability / Performance** ‚Äî Meet the driver's torque demand instantaneously without perceptible delay (transient response).
5. **Component Protection** ‚Äî Limit thermal, electrical, and mechanical stress on the EM, battery, and transmission.

## 2.2 The Two Fundamental Control Strategies

There are two broad philosophical approaches to power flow control that KTU expects students to recognise:

### A. Rule-Based (Heuristic / Deterministic) Strategy
- Uses **if-then-else logic** based on SOC, vehicle speed, driver demand, and ICE operating point.
- Simple, robust, computationally cheap ‚Äî implemented in production vehicles (e.g., Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive).
- Sub-types: **Thermostat (ON/OFF) control** and **Power Follower control**.

### B. Optimisation-Based Strategy
- Uses mathematical optimisation to minimise a cost function (fuel) over a horizon.
- Sub-types:
  - **Global Optimisation** ‚Äî Dynamic Programming (DP), requires prior knowledge of the drive cycle.
  - **Real-Time (Instantaneous) Optimisation** ‚Äî Equivalent Consumption Minimisation Strategy (ECMS).
  - **Predictive Optimisation** ‚Äî Model Predictive Control (MPC), uses previewed route information.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Rule-based strategies are **more commonly asked** because they directly tie to the topology discussion. Always draw the **mode-selection flow chart** when answering rule-based questions.

## 2.3 Power Flow Control in EACH Drive-train Topology

### 2.3.1 Series Hybrid ‚Äî Power Flow Control

**Architecture:** ICE ‚Üí Generator ‚Üí Rectifier ‚Üí DC Bus ‚Üí Battery ‚áÑ Inverter ‚Üí Motor ‚Üí Wheels.

There is **NO mechanical coupling** between the ICE and the wheels. The ICE is mechanically decoupled and can therefore operate at *any* speed/load independent of vehicle speed.

**Control Modes:**
| Mode | ICE | Generator | Battery | Motor | Power Path |
|------|-----|-----------|---------|-------|-----------|
| 1 ‚Äî Pure EV | OFF | OFF | Discharging | ON | Battery ‚Üí Motor ‚Üí Wheels |
| 2 ‚Äî ICE charges + drives | ON (at OOP) | ON (charging) | Charging | ON | ICE ‚Üí Gen ‚Üí Battery; Battery +/‚Äì Motor ‚Üí Wheels |
| 3 ‚Äî Pure ICE drive | ON | ON | Off-line | ON | ICE ‚Üí Gen ‚Üí Motor ‚Üí Wheels |
| 4 ‚Äî Regen braking | OFF | OFF | Charging | Gen mode | Wheels ‚Üí Motor ‚Üí Battery |

**Key Control Insight:** The ICE never sees the road load directly. It is always "trapped" on a generator. The series topology offers the **greatest flexibility** in ICE operation but suffers **double energy conversion losses** (mechanical ‚Üí electrical ‚Üí mechanical).

### 2.3.2 Parallel Hybrid ‚Äî Power Flow Control

**Architecture:** ICE and EM are **mechanically coupled** (via clutches/gears) to the wheels. Both can deliver torque to the wheels simultaneously.

**Control Modes:**
| Mode | ICE | EM | Battery | Description |
|------|-----|----|---------|-------------|
| 1 ‚Äî Pure ICE | ON | OFF | Idle | Highway cruising at high load |
| 2 ‚Äî Pure EV | OFF | ON | Discharging | Low-speed urban driving |
| 3 ‚Äî Power assist | ON | ON | Discharging | Hard acceleration; both add torque |
| 4 ‚Äî ICE charges battery | ON (extra load) | ON (gen) | Charging | ICE at high-efficiency point, EM as generator |
| 5 ‚Äî Regen braking | OFF | Gen mode | Charging | Deceleration |
| 6 ‚Äî Stationary charging | ON | Gen mode | Charging | Vehicle stopped |

**Key Control Insight:** The **torque-coupling law** is fundamental. At the coupling node:
$$T_{wheels} = T_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{trans} + T_{EM} \cdot \eta_{EM}$$
The controller splits the wheel torque demand $T_{demand}$ between ICE and EM optimally.

### 2.3.3 Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid ‚Äî Power Flow Control

**Architecture:** Combines series and parallel features. Uses a **planetary gear set (PGS)** as a power-split device (e.g., Toyota THS).

- **Sun gear** ‚Äî connected to Generator (G1).
- **Planet carrier** ‚Äî connected to ICE.
- **Ring gear** ‚Äî connected to Motor (M1) and the final drive (wheels).

**Control Modes (THS-II):**
| Mode | ICE | G1 | M1 | Battery | Notes |
|------|-----|----|----|---------|-------|
| 1 ‚Äî Pure EV (low speed) | OFF | OFF | Motoring | Discharge | M1 alone drives wheels |
| 2 ‚Äî EV + ICE assist | ON | Generating | Motoring | Off-line | ICE partly drives wheels via PGS, rest goes to G1 |
| 3 ‚Äî Power split (cruise) | ON at OOP | Generating | Motoring | Off-line | ICE split between mechanical path & G1‚ÜíM1‚Üíwheels electrical path |
| 4 ‚Äî Full power | ON | Generating | Motoring | Discharge | Both paths at max ‚Äî peak acceleration |
| 5 ‚Äî Regen | OFF | OFF | Generating | Charging | Wheels ‚Üí M1 ‚Üí battery |

**Key Control Insight:** The planetary gearset enforces a kinematic constraint:
$$\omega_{sun} \cdot S + \omega_{ring} \cdot R = \omega_{carrier} \cdot (S + R)$$
where $S$ = sun gear teeth, $R$ = ring gear teeth. The HCU controls G1's torque to *regulate ICE speed independently of wheel speed*, keeping the ICE at its OOP.

### 2.3.4 Complex Hybrid ‚Äî Power Flow Control

**Architecture:** Has **two EMs** (or one EM + one Generator) AND a **clutch** that can convert between series and parallel modes dynamically (e.g., GM Two-Mode Hybrid, Honda i-MMD).

**Control Modes:** Switches between series-mode (low speed, EV-like) and parallel-mode (high speed, ICE-direct). Has the highest flexibility but the most complex controller.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **must-know equations** for power flow control problems. Memorise these for the ESE.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|-------------------|------------|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Mechanical Power | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | $T$ [Nm], $\omega$ [rad/s], $P$ [W] | Universal power-flow equation |
| 2 | Wheel Demand Power | $P_{wheel} = F_{trac} \cdot v$ | $F_{trac}$ [N], $v$ [m/s] | Driver demand calculation |
| 3 | Traction Force | $F_{trac} = F_{roll} + F_{aero} + F_{grade} + F_{acc}$ | All forces [N] | Longitudinal dynamics |
| 4 | Rolling Resistance | $F_{roll} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos\alpha$ | $C_{rr} \approx 0.012$ | Constant loss term |
| 5 | Aerodynamic Drag | $F_{aero} = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$ | $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m¬≥ (air density) | Speed-squared loss |
| 6 | Grade Resistance | $F_{grade} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha$ | $\alpha$ = road angle | Hill climbing |
| 7 | Acceleration Force | $F_{acc} = m \cdot a$ | $m$ [kg], $a$ [m/s¬≤] | Newton's second law |
| 8 | Energy in Battery | $E_{bat} = V_{oc} \cdot Q_{rated} \cdot SOC$ | $V_{oc}$ [V], $Q$ [Ah] | SOC bookkeeping |
| 9 | Battery Power Limit | $\vert P_{bat} \vert \le P_{bat,max}$ | Constraint for safety | Controller inequality |
| 10 | SOC Constraint | $SOC_{min} \le SOC \le SOC_{max}$ | Typically $0.3 \le SOC \le 0.8$ | Charge-sustaining band |
| 11 | ICE Fuel Rate | $\dot{m}_{f} = f(T_{ICE}, \omega_{ICE})$ | [kg/s] ‚Äî from BSFC map | Minimisation target |
| 12 | Series ‚Äî Wheel Power | $P_{wheel} = (P_{ICE} - P_{gen,loss}) \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{motor}$ | Efficiency chain | Series efficiency chain |
| 13 | Parallel ‚Äî Torque Coupling | $T_{wheel} = T_{ICE} + T_{EM}$ | Torque additive at coupling | Parallel torque split |
| 14 | Planetary Kinematics | $\omega_{s} \cdot N_{s} + \omega_{r} \cdot N_{r} = \omega_{c} \cdot (N_{s}+N_{r})$ | $N$ = gear teeth count | THS speed constraint |
| 15 | Equivalent Factor (ECMS) | $\dot{m}_{f,eq} = \dot{m}_{f,ICE} + s \cdot \dot{m}_{f,batt}$ | $s$ = equivalence factor | Real-time opt. |
| 16 | Regenerative Braking Limit | $P_{regen} \le \min(P_{bat,max},\, P_{motor,max})$ | Component-wise | Brake energy recovery |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are the *language* of HEV simulation tools (e.g., ADVISOR, MATLAB/Simulink, Autonomie). An automotive control engineer writes these equations daily in plant models.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚Äî Universal Power Balance at the Wheels

Every drive-train topology, regardless of architecture, must satisfy the **fundamental power balance** at the road-load level. We derive this from Newton's second law applied to a single-track vehicle model.

**Starting point:** Newton's second law for the vehicle's longitudinal dynamics:
$$m \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} = F_{trac} - F_{roll} - F_{aero} - F_{grade}$$

**Step 1 (Multiplying both sides by velocity $v$):**
$$m \cdot v \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} = F_{trac} \cdot v - F_{roll} \cdot v - F_{aero} \cdot v - F_{grade} \cdot v$$

**Step 2 (Recognising $\frac{d}{dt}(\frac{1}{2}mv^2)$ on the LHS):**
$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{1}{2} m v^2\right) = P_{trac} - P_{roll} - P_{aero} - P_{grade}$$

**Step 3 (Recognising the LHS is the rate of change of kinetic energy $E_k$):**
$$\frac{dE_k}{dt} = P_{trac} - P_{roll} - P_{aero} - P_{grade}$$

**Step 4 (Solving for traction power ‚Äî the power that MUST be delivered to the wheels):**
$$P_{trac}(t) = F_{trac}(t) \cdot v(t) = P_{roll}(t) + P_{aero}(t) + P_{grade}(t) + m \cdot v \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$$

**Step 5 (Substituting the individual resistance powers):**

$$
P_{trac} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot v \cdot \cos\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^{3} + m \cdot g \cdot v \cdot \sin\alpha + m \cdot v \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}
$$

**Step 6 (Crucial link to hybrid control ‚Äî the HCU must satisfy):**
$$P_{trac}(t) = \sum P_{sources}(t) \cdot \prod \eta_{path,i}(t)$$

where the right-hand side sums over all *active* power sources (ICE, battery) and multiplies by the efficiency of each component in the delivery path. The HCU selects which sources are active and at what operating point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This single equation is the **foundation of all energy management problems** in HEVs. A 14-mark KTU question may ask: *"Given a drive cycle, compute the traction power demand and propose a power-split strategy."*

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚Äî Power Flow in a Parallel Hybrid

Consider a parallel hybrid with an ICE and an EM mechanically coupled through a clutch to the wheels.

**Step 1 (Kinematic constraint ‚Äî both shafts spin at the same speed):**
$$\omega_{ICE} = \omega_{EM} = \omega_{wheels} / G_{final}$$
where $G_{final}$ is the final-drive ratio.

**Step 2 (Torque additive at the coupling node):**
$$T_{wheels} = (T_{ICE} + T_{EM}) \cdot \eta_{gear}$$

**Step 3 (Multiplying both sides by wheel speed to get power):**
$$P_{wheels} = T_{wheels} \cdot \omega_{wheels} = (P_{ICE}/\omega_{ICE} + P_{EM}/\omega_{EM}) \cdot \eta_{gear} \cdot \omega_{wheels}$$

**Step 4 (Substituting $\omega_{wheels} = \omega_{ICE} \cdot G_{final}$):**
$$P_{wheels} = (P_{ICE} + P_{EM}) \cdot \eta_{gear} \cdot G_{final}$$

**Step 5 (Defining a torque-split factor $\beta$ such that $T_{EM} = \beta \cdot T_{wheels}$):**
$$T_{ICE} = (1 - \beta) \cdot T_{wheels} / \eta_{gear}$$

The HCU's job is to choose $\beta(t)$ at every instant $t$ to minimise fuel. Note that $\beta \in [0, 1]$ ‚Äî when $\beta = 0$, only ICE drives; when $\beta = 1$, only EM drives; intermediate values represent power assist or ICE charging.

**Step 6 (Energy conservation check ‚Äî including battery):**
$$P_{batt} = P_{EM}/\eta_{EM}$$
- If $\beta > 0$ (EM motoring): $P_{batt} > 0$ ‚Üí battery discharges.
- If $\beta < 0$ (EM generating): $P_{batt} < 0$ ‚Üí battery charges.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚Äî Power Flow in a Series Hybrid

**Step 1 (ICE mechanical power):**
$$P_{ICE,mech} = T_{ICE} \cdot \omega_{ICE}$$

**Step 2 (After generator conversion ‚Äî electrical power from generator):**
$$P_{gen,elec} = P_{ICE,mech} \cdot \eta_{gen}$$

**Step 3 (At DC bus ‚Äî combines generator output and battery):**
$$P_{DC} = P_{gen,elec} + P_{batt}$$

**Step 4 (After inverter ‚Äî AC power to motor):**
$$P_{inv,out} = P_{DC} \cdot \eta_{inv}$$

**Step 5 (After motor ‚Äî mechanical power to wheels):**
$$P_{wheels} = P_{inv,out} \cdot \eta_{motor}$$

**Step 6 (Combining into the series power-chain equation):**
$$
P_{wheels} = \left[\,(P_{ICE,mech} \cdot \eta_{gen} + P_{batt}) \cdot \eta_{inv}\,\right] \cdot \eta_{motor}
$$

**Step 7 (Defining total series efficiency):**
$$
\eta_{series} = \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{motor}
$$

If each efficiency is ~0.92, then $\eta_{series} \approx 0.78$ ‚Äî explaining why series hybrids are less efficient than parallel at high loads.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ‚Äî Planetary Gear Speed Constraint (for Power-Split)

The planetary gear set has three members: sun (S), ring (R), carrier (C). For a planetary gearset with teeth counts $N_s$ (sun), $N_r$ (ring), the Willis equation states:

**Step 1 (Willis equation ‚Äî fundamental kinematics):**
$$
\frac{\omega_{s} - \omega_{c}}{\omega_{r} - \omega_{c}} = -\frac{N_{r}}{N_{s}} = -k
$$

where $k = N_r / N_s$ is the gear ratio.

**Step 2 (Solving for $\omega_c$):**
$$
\omega_{c} = \frac{\omega_{s} + k \cdot \omega_{r}}{1 + k}
$$

**Step 3 (Interpretation ‚Äî applied to Toyota THS where carrier = ICE, sun = G1, ring = M1/wheels):**
$$
\omega_{ICE} = \frac{\omega_{G1} + k \cdot \omega_{wheels}}{1 + k}
$$

**Step 4 (Key control insight):** If the HCU wants ICE at a specific $\omega_{ICE}^*$ (its OOP), and the wheels demand $\omega_{wheels}$, the HCU commands:
$$
\omega_{G1}^{*} = (1+k) \cdot \omega_{ICE}^{*} - k \cdot \omega_{wheels}
$$

The Generator (G1) absorbs the *difference* between desired ICE speed and what the wheels mechanically require ‚Äî exactly the role of a power-split device.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix ‚Äî Topology vs. Control Objective

> [!NOTE]
> This comparative matrix is a **high-yield KTU exam tool**. Examiners often ask: *"Compare the four topologies with respect to power-flow control."*

| Control Objective | Series | Parallel | Series-Parallel (Power-Split) | Complex |
|-------------------|:------:|:--------:|:----------------------------:|:-------:|
| **ICE Operating Point Freedom** | Excellent (decoupled) | Limited (coupled to wheels) | Excellent (PGS decouples) | Excellent |
| **Transmission Efficiency** | Low (double conversion) | High (direct mechanical) | High (mostly mechanical at cruise) | High |
| **Regenerative Braking** | Full (motor on wheels) | Full (motor on wheels) | Full | Full |
| **Control Complexity** | Low | Medium | High (PGS + 2 EMs) | Very High |
| **Mode Transitions** | Smooth (electrical) | Abrupt (clutch engagement) | Smooth | Seamless |
| **Suitability ‚Äî Urban (Low-Speed)** | Good | Fair | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Suitability ‚Äî Highway (High-Speed)** | Poor (efficiency loss) | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Fuel Economy Potential** | Good | Good | Best | Best |
| **Cost** | High (large Gen+Motor) | Lower | High (PGS, 2 EMs) | Highest |
| **Example Production Vehicle** | Chevy Volt (1st gen) | Honda Insight (1st gen) | Toyota Prius | GM 2-Mode, Honda i-MMD |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 ‚Äî Series Hybrid Power Flow Control (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EnergySources[Energy Source Cluster]
        ICEblk[ICE Engine]
        Genblk[Generator G1]
        Batblk[High Voltage Battery ESS]
    end
    subgraph PowerElectronics[Power Electronics Cluster]
        Rectblk[Rectifier AC to DC]
        Invblk[Inverter DC to AC]
        DCbus[DC Bus 200 to 400 V]
    end
    subgraph Drivetrain[Motor and Wheels Cluster]
        Motblk[Traction Motor M1]
        Trblk[Final Drive and Wheels]
    end
    subgraph Controller[Supervisory Control]
        HCUblk[Hybrid Control Unit HCU]
        SOCblk[SOC Monitor]
    end

    ICEblk -->|Mechanical Shaft| Genblk
    Genblk -->|3 Phase AC| Rectblk
    Rectblk -->|DC Power| DCbus
    Batblk <-->|Bidirectional Charge or Discharge| DCbus
    DCbus -->|DC Link| Invblk
    Invblk -->|3 Phase AC| Motblk
    Motblk -->|Torque via Gear| Trblk
    SOCblk -->|Battery State Telemetry| HCUblk
    HCUblk -->|Torque Command| Motblk
    HCUblk -->|Speed and Load Command| ICEblk
    HCUblk -->|Field Current Command| Genblk
    HCUblk -->|Switching Signals| Invblk
```

**Reading the Diagram:** Note the **bidirectional arrow** between the battery and DC bus (charge/discharge), the **unidirectional flow** from ICE ‚Üí Generator ‚Üí Rectifier ‚Üí DC bus, and the **feedback arrows** from SOC Monitor to HCU. The HCU is the central "conductor" issuing all commands.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 ‚Äî Parallel Hybrid Power Flow Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EngineSide[Engine Branch]
        ICE2[ICE Engine]
        ClutchA[Clutch C1]
    end
    subgraph MotorSide[Motor Branch]
        Bat2[HV Battery]
        Inv2[Inverter]
        Mot2[Traction Motor EM]
        ClutchB[Clutch C2]
    end
    subgraph Coupling[Mechanical Coupling Point]
        CouplingNode[Torque Coupler]
        Gearbox[Transmission and Final Drive]
        Wheels[Drive Wheels]
    end
    subgraph Ctrl2[Control Unit]
        HCU2[Hybrid Control Unit]
        SOC2[SOC Monitor]
        Accel[Accelerator Pedal Sensor APS]
    end

    ICE2 --> ClutchA --> CouplingNode
    Mot2 --> ClutchB --> CouplingNode
    Bat2 <--> Inv2
    Inv2 --> Mot2
    CouplingNode --> Gearbox --> Wheels
    SOC2 --> HCU2
    Accel --> HCU2
    HCU2 -->|Throttle Command| ICE2
    HCU2 -->|Torque Command| Mot2
    HCU2 -->|Clutch Engage or Release| ClutchA
    HCU2 -->|Clutch Engage or Release| ClutchB
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The **coupling node** is the heart of the parallel topology ‚Äî both clutches can engage independently, allowing pure-ICE, pure-EM, or combined torque delivery. The HCU reads pedal position and SOC, then commands both clutches and the motor's torque.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 ‚Äî Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid (Toyota THS Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PGScluster[Planetary Gear Set]
        SunGear[Sun Gear connected to G1]
        CarrierGear[Planet Carrier connected to ICE]
        RingGear[Ring Gear connected to M1 and Wheels]
    end
    subgraph ICEbranch[ICE Branch]
        ICE3[ICE Engine]
    end
    subgraph EMs[Electric Machines]
        Gen3[Generator G1]
        Mot3[Motor M1]
    end
    subgraph PowerElectronics3[Power Electronics]
        Inv3A[Inverter for G1]
        Inv3B[Inverter for M1]
        Bat3[HV Battery]
    end
    subgraph Output3[Output]
        FinalDrive3[Final Drive]
        Wheels3[Wheels]
    end
    subgraph Ctrl3[Controller]
        HCU3[Hybrid Control Unit]
        SOC3[SOC Sensor]
        Brake3[Brake Pedal Sensor]
    end

    ICE3 --> CarrierGear
    CarrierGear --> SunGear
    CarrierGear --> RingGear
    SunGear --> Gen3 --> Inv3A
    RingGear --> Mot3
    Mot3 --> Inv3B
    RingGear --> FinalDrive3 --> Wheels3
    Inv3A <--> Bat3
    Inv3B <--> Bat3
    SOC3 --> HCU3
    Brake3 --> HCU3
    HCU3 -->|Throttle| ICE3
    HCU3 -->|Field Control| Gen3
    HCU3 -->|Torque| Mot3
```

**Reading the Diagram:** Observe the **single mechanical node (Carrier)** accepting ICE torque, and the **two electrical nodes (Sun and Ring)**. The PGS acts as a continuously variable transmission (CVT), continuously splitting the ICE's power between a mechanical path (to wheels) and an electrical path (ICE ‚Üí G1 ‚Üí Battery + M1 ‚Üí wheels).

## 4.4 Diagram 4 ‚Äî Rule-Based Power Flow Control State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Init
    Init --> Standby : Ignition On
    Standby --> EV_Mode : SOC sufficient AND Speed low AND Demand low
    Standby --> ICE_Mode : SOC low OR Speed high OR Demand high
    EV_Mode --> ICE_Mode : SOC below threshold OR Demand exceeds EM limit
    EV_Mode --> Regen_Mode : Brake pedal pressed AND Speed above min
    ICE_Mode --> PowerAssist : Demand exceeds ICE max torque
    ICE_Mode --> Charge_Mode : SOC below target AND ICE has surplus torque
    PowerAssist --> ICE_Mode : Demand drops
    Charge_Mode --> ICE_Mode : SOC reaches target
    Regen_Mode --> Standby : Vehicle stops
    Regen_Mode --> EV_Mode : Brake released
    ICE_Mode --> Regen_Mode : Brake pedal pressed
```

**Reading the Diagram:** This is the **logic flow** the HCU executes in real time (typically every 10 ms). Each state represents a different *power flow configuration* (which sources active, which paths engaged). KTU questions often ask students to **draw and label** this kind of state diagram.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no physical drivetrain sketch?** The diagrams above are **functional architecture flows** that KTU examiners prefer because they clearly show **power flow direction, control signals, and component interactions** ‚Äî the actual exam-relevant information. Physical sketches are harder to draw accurately within exam time limits.
