# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024]
**Q: List any three power flow control objectives of a hybrid electric vehicle's energy management system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Fuel Minimisation (1 mark):** Operate the ICE at its Best Operating Line (BSFC minima) to reduce fuel consumption across the drive cycle.
2. **Battery SOC Sustainment (1 mark):** Maintain the State of Charge within the charge-sustaining band (e.g., $0.3 \le SOC \le 0.8$) using charge-sustaining strategies like ECMS or rule-based thermostat control.
3. **Emissions Reduction (1 mark):** Avoid ICE operation in inefficient zones (cold start, low load) and use the electric motor to decouple the ICE from transient road loads.

*(Acceptable alternatives: drivability/performance, component thermal protection, drivetrain efficiency maximisation.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023]
**Q: In a parallel hybrid drive-train, what is the role of the Hybrid Control Unit (HCU) during the "Power Assist" mode?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
During **Power Assist** mode, the vehicle demand exceeds the maximum torque the ICE can deliver efficiently. The HCU:
1. **Commands the ICE to operate at its optimal operating point** (1 mark) ‚Äî typically high-torque, high-efficiency region.
2. **Commands the Electric Motor (EM) to deliver the deficit torque** (1 mark) ‚Äî the difference between demanded torque and ICE's optimal torque, drawing energy from the battery.
3. **Monitors battery SOC and temperature** (1 mark) to ensure assist is sustainable and within battery power limits ($P_{batt} \le P_{batt,max}$).

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ‚Äî Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Failure to draw the power flow direction arrows** ‚Äî students often draw the topology but forget to label *where* power flows in each mode. Always annotate arrows and label components. (-2 to -3 marks)
> 2. **Omitting numerical units** ‚Äî every torque, speed, and power value MUST carry units [Nm], [rad/s], [kW]. Loss of 1 mark per equation without units.
> 3. **Confusing series and parallel efficiency chains** ‚Äî remember, **series = double energy conversion**, **parallel = direct mechanical path**.
> 4. **Ignoring the SOC constraint** in optimisation ‚Äî never write a power-split equation without the SOC band constraint; examiners will deduct marks for an unconstrained solution.
> 5. **Not mentioning control objectives** in long answers ‚Äî every power-flow question implicitly asks you to state what your control is *trying to achieve*.

---

### Question B-A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** With the help of a functional block diagram, explain the **power flow control strategy in a Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV)** during the following operating modes:
   (i) Pure Electric Mode (3 marks)
   (ii) ICE Charges Battery + Drives Wheels (4 marks)

**(b)** A series hybrid has an ICE-generator set rated at **35 kW** (mechanical ‚Üí electrical efficiency = 0.90), an inverter efficiency of **0.95**, and a traction motor efficiency of **0.92**. The battery can supply/absorb up to **30 kW**. The vehicle demands a steady **40 kW** at the wheels. Compute:
   (i) The wheel power available from the ICE alone (2 marks)
   (ii) The required battery power to meet the demand (2 marks)
   (iii) Comment on the overall efficiency and a key drawback of this topology. (3 marks)

#### Model Answer ‚Äî Part (a)

**(i) Pure Electric Mode ‚Äî 3 marks:**

In pure electric mode, the ICE is **OFF** and the Generator is also off-line. The complete power flow path is:

```
Battery (ESS) ‚Üí Inverter (DC‚ÜíAC) ‚Üí Traction Motor ‚Üí Wheels
```

- The **Battery discharges** at a rate governed by driver demand.
- No fuel is consumed; zero tailpipe emissions.
- Used at low vehicle speeds (typically < 40 km/h) and when SOC is sufficiently high.
- The HCU monitors SOC and switches to another mode when SOC drops below a threshold (e.g., SOC < 0.3).

**Annotation requirement:** Draw arrows showing unidirectional flow from Battery ‚Üí Inverter ‚Üí Motor ‚Üí Wheels. Label each block and indicate the ICE is "OFF".

**(ii) ICE Charges Battery + Drives Wheels ‚Äî 4 marks:**

This is the **most common SHEV operating mode**. The HCU's strategy is:

1. The ICE is started and ramped to its **Optimal Operating Point (OOP)** where BSFC is minimum.
2. The Generator (G1) is driven by the ICE and produces AC electrical power.
3. The Rectifier converts this to DC at the DC bus.
4. A portion of this DC power goes to the **Battery** (charging it).
5. The remainder (or, if no charging needed, the whole power) goes through the **Inverter ‚Üí Motor ‚Üí Wheels**.

**Power split at DC bus:**

$$P_{DC,total} = P_{ICE,mech} \cdot \eta_{gen}$$

$$P_{batt,charge} + P_{motor,elec} = P_{DC,total}$$

$$P_{wheels} = P_{motor,elec} \cdot \eta_{motor}$$

The HCU decides the split ratio between charging and driving based on:
- Current SOC (charge more if SOC is low).
- Driver torque demand (drive more if demand is high).
- Battery thermal limits.

**Annotation requirement:** Show **two parallel arrows** leaving the DC bus ‚Äî one to battery, one to inverter‚Üímotor. Show bidirectional flow capability at battery.

#### Model Answer ‚Äî Part (b)

**(i) Wheel power from ICE alone ‚Äî 2 marks:**

$$
P_{wheel,ICE} = P_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{motor}
$$

$$
P_{wheel,ICE} = 35 \cdot 0.90 \cdot 0.95 \cdot 0.92
$$

**Step-by-step evaluation:**
$$
35 \cdot 0.90 = 31.5 \text{ kW}
$$
$$
31.5 \cdot 0.95 = 29.925 \text{ kW}
$$
$$
29.925 \cdot 0.92 = 27.531 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{wheel,ICE} \approx 27.53 \text{ kW}}
$$

**(ii) Required battery power ‚Äî 2 marks:**

$$
P_{batt} = P_{demand} - P_{wheel,ICE} = 40 - 27.53 = 12.47 \text{ kW}
$$

Since $\vert P_{batt} \vert = 12.47 \text{ kW} < 30 \text{ kW}$ (battery limit), this is feasible. The battery must **discharge** at 12.47 kW.

$$
\boxed{P_{batt} \approx 12.47 \text{ kW (discharging)}}
$$

**(iii) Comment on efficiency & drawback ‚Äî 3 marks:**

- **Overall efficiency** of the ICE-to-wheel path is $\eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{motor} = 0.90 \cdot 0.95 \cdot 0.92 \approx 0.787$ (i.e., **78.7%**).
- **Key drawback:** This is the **double (or triple) energy conversion loss** inherent to the series topology ‚Äî mechanical ‚Üí electrical (at generator) ‚Üí DC ‚Üí AC (at inverter) ‚Üí mechanical (at motor). Each conversion incurs ~5‚Äì10% loss.
- Compared to a parallel hybrid at the same power demand (where ICE drives wheels directly through a mechanical path at ~95% efficiency), the series topology loses an additional ~15‚Äì17% of fuel energy.
- Hence, series hybrids are best suited to **urban duty cycles with frequent stops** where regen braking recovers significant energy ‚Äî offsetting the chain losses.

---

### Question B-B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** With the help of a functional block diagram, explain the **power flow control strategy in a Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV)** during the following modes:
   (i) Pure ICE Drive (3 marks)
   (ii) Regenerative Braking (4 marks)

**(b)** A parallel hybrid vehicle has the following parameters at a particular instant:
- Vehicle mass $m = 1500$ kg
- Rolling resistance coefficient $C_{rr} = 0.012$
- Aerodynamic drag coefficient $C_d = 0.30$, frontal area $A = 2.2$ m¬≤
- Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m¬≥
- Road gradient $\alpha = 0¬∞$ (flat road)
- Vehicle speed $v = 25$ m/s
- Acceleration $\frac{dv}{dt} = 0.5$ m/s¬≤
- ICE torque $T_{ICE} = 120$ Nm, ICE speed $\omega_{ICE} = 300$ rad/s
- Final drive ratio $G = 3.5$, drivetrain efficiency $\eta = 0.92$
- Wheel radius $r = 0.32$ m

Compute:
   (i) The traction force required at the wheels (3 marks)
   (ii) The traction power demanded (2 marks)
   (iii) The torque-split factor $\beta$ if the EM must contribute the residual torque. (2 marks)

#### Model Answer ‚Äî Part (a)

**(i) Pure ICE Drive ‚Äî 3 marks:**

In pure ICE mode, the **electric motor is OFF** and the **battery is idle** (neither charging nor discharging). The complete power flow path is:

```
ICE (mechanical) ‚Üí Clutch C1 ENGAGED ‚Üí Transmission ‚Üí Final Drive ‚Üí Wheels
```

- The HCU **disengages Clutch C2** (motor clutch) and commands zero torque to the EM.
- The ICE is throttled to meet the entire driver demand directly.
- Best suited to **highway cruising at constant speed** where the ICE operates near its Best Operating Line.
- Fuel consumption depends on the ICE's BSFC at the current operating point.

**Annotation requirement:** Label Clutch C1 as "ENGAGED" and Clutch C2 as "DISENGAGED". Show the motor branch with a dashed or faded arrow indicating inactivity.

**(ii) Regenerative Braking ‚Äî 4 marks:**

In regen mode, the **ICE is OFF and decoupled** (Clutch C1 open), and the **electric motor acts as a generator**:

```
Wheels (kinetic energy) ‚Üí Final Drive ‚Üí Transmission ‚Üí Motor (now Generator) ‚Üí Inverter (rectifier mode) ‚Üí Battery (charging)
```

The HCU's strategy:
1. Detect brake pedal depression (and accelerator = 0).
2. Verify conditions: SOC < SOC_max AND vehicle speed > v_min (typically > 5 km/h).
3. Command the motor to operate in **generator mode**, applying a **negative torque** (i.e., a torque that opposes wheel rotation).
4. The kinetic energy of the vehicle is converted to electrical energy and stored in the battery.
5. **Friction brakes** fill in any braking demand beyond the motor's regenerative limit, especially at very low speeds or during emergency stops.

**Annotation requirement:** Reverse the arrow direction on the motor branch. Label motor as "Generator Mode". Show bidirectional arrow at battery with "CHARGING" annotation.

#### Model Answer ‚Äî Part (b)

**(i) Traction force at wheels ‚Äî 3 marks:**

Using $g = 9.81$ m/s¬≤:

**Rolling resistance:**
$$F_{roll} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos\alpha = 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.012 \cdot \cos 0¬∞ = 176.58 \text{ N}$$

**Aerodynamic drag:**
$$F_{aero} = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2 = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 2.2 \cdot 25^2$$
$$= 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 2.2 \cdot 625$$
$$= 252.66 \text{ N}$$

**Grade resistance** (flat road, $\alpha = 0¬∞$):
$$F_{grade} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha = 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0 = 0 \text{ N}$$

**Acceleration force:**
$$F_{acc} = m \cdot a = 1500 \cdot 0.5 = 750 \text{ N}$$

**Total traction force:**
$$F_{trac} = F_{roll} + F_{aero} + F_{grade} + F_{acc}$$
$$= 176.58 + 252.66 + 0 + 750$$
$$= \boxed{1179.24 \text{ N}}$$

**(ii) Traction power demanded ‚Äî 2 marks:**

$$P_{trac} = F_{trac} \cdot v = 1179.24 \cdot 25$$
$$= \boxed{29{,}481 \text{ W} \approx 29.48 \text{ kW}}$$

**(iii) Torque-split factor $\beta$ ‚Äî 2 marks:**

**Step 1 ‚Äî ICE power:**
$$P_{ICE} = T_{ICE} \cdot \omega_{ICE} = 120 \cdot 300 = 36{,}000 \text{ W} = 36 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî ICE power at wheels (after transmission losses):**
$$P_{ICE,wheel} = P_{ICE} \cdot \eta = 36 \cdot 0.92 = 33.12 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî EM must supply the deficit:**
$$P_{EM,wheel} = P_{trac} - P_{ICE,wheel} = 29.48 - 33.12 = -3.64 \text{ kW}$$

The **negative sign** indicates ICE power EXCEEDS demand. Hence the EM should be operated in **generator mode** to absorb the surplus and charge the battery.

**Step 4 ‚Äî Equivalent traction torque at the wheels:**

Torque at wheels from ICE alone (accounting for gear ratio):
$$T_{wheels,ICE} = T_{ICE} \cdot G \cdot \eta = 120 \cdot 3.5 \cdot 0.92 = 386.4 \text{ Nm}$$

Torque demand at wheels:
$$T_{demand} = F_{trac} \cdot r = 1179.24 \cdot 0.32 = 377.36 \text{ Nm}$$

Torque-split factor (defined such that $T_{EM,wheel} = \beta \cdot T_{demand}$):
$$\beta = \frac{T_{wheels,ICE} - T_{demand}}{T_{demand}} = \frac{386.4 - 377.36}{377.36}$$
$$\beta = \frac{9.04}{377.36} \approx 0.024$$

**Interpretation:** Since $\beta > 0$, the EM acts as a **generator** (regenerative-like charging) and absorbs ~2.4% of the demanded torque. The ICE is essentially meeting demand with a small surplus used for battery charging.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision summary ‚Äî read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

### ‚ö° Core Definitions
- **Power Flow Control:** Real-time regulation of how power is split among ICE, EM, Generator, and Battery in an HEV.
- **HCU (Hybrid Control Unit):** The supervisory controller executing the power flow logic.
- **OOP (Optimal Operating Point):** The ICE speed/load combination with minimum BSFC.
- **SOC Band:** The allowable SOC window (typically 0.3 ‚Äì 0.8) for charge-sustaining operation.

### ‚ö° Topologies ‚Äî One-Line Each
- **Series:** ICE ‚Üí Gen ‚Üí Battery ‚áÑ Motor ‚Üí Wheels. *No mechanical link between ICE and wheels.* Best flexibility, worst efficiency chain.
- **Parallel:** ICE ‚´Ω Coupling ‚´Ω EM ‚Üí Wheels. *Both sources mechanically coupled.* Best efficiency, limited ICE freedom.
- **Series-Parallel (Power-Split):** Adds a planetary gear set; ICE decoupled via the PGS kinematics. *Best of both worlds.* Example: Toyota Prius THS.
- **Complex:** Two EMs + clutches; switches dynamically between series and parallel modes. Example: GM Two-Mode, Honda i-MMD.

### ‚ö° Must-Memorise Equations
- $P = T \cdot \omega$
- $P_{trac} = F_{trac} \cdot v$
- $P_{series,wheel} = P_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{motor}$
- $T_{wheel} = T_{ICE} + T_{EM}$ (parallel coupling)
- $\omega_{ICE} = \frac{\omega_{G1} + k \cdot \omega_{wheels}}{1+k}$ (planetary)

### ‚ö° Five Control Objectives (Always Quote)
1. Fuel minimisation
2. Emissions reduction
3. SOC sustainment
4. Drivability/performance
5. Component protection

### ‚ö° Two Control Strategy Families
- **Rule-Based:** Thermostat (ON/OFF) and Power Follower. Simple, real-time, production-grade.
- **Optimisation-Based:** DP (global, offline), ECMS (real-time), MPC (predictive).

### ‚ö° Common Exam Triggers
- "Explain power flow during [mode]" ‚Üí Draw block diagram + annotate arrows + list components active.
- "Compute traction power" ‚Üí Use the $P_{trac}$ formula with all four resistance terms.
- "Compare topologies" ‚Üí Use the decision matrix (Section 3.5).
- "Role of HCU" ‚Üí Mention sensing (APS, SOC, speed), decision (mode selection), and actuation (throttle, torque commands, clutch signals).

### ‚ö° Top Mistakes to Avoid
- ‚ùå Drawing a series topology but calling it parallel.
- ‚ùå Forgetting the SOC constraint in optimisation.
- ‚ùå Omitting units in numerical answers.
- ‚ùå Forgetting to label ICE as "OFF" in pure-EV modes.
- ‚ùå Missing the regenerative braking arrow direction (wheels ‚Üí motor ‚Üí battery).

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Notes ‚Äî Power Flow Control in Various Hybrid Drive-train Topologies (Module 1).**
> These notes align with the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (OEEET722)**, **Course Outcomes (CO1 ‚Äî Understand hybrid drive-train architectures and their power flow management)**, and the **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy levels L2 (Understand) ‚Üí L4 (Analyse)**. Practice the numerical problems in Section 5.2 thoroughly ‚Äî they reflect the exact pattern of KTU ESE questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Drive-trains: Basic Concept of Electric Traction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electric Drive-trains: Basic Concept of Electric Traction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ‚ö° Module 1: Electric Drive-trains ‚Äî Basic Concept of Electric Traction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic establishes the foundational physics of *why* electric drive-trains behave the way they do. Mastery of tractive effort, resistive forces, and the fundamental equation of motion is mandatory before progressing to power flow management in HEVs/EVs.

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

**Electric Traction** is the branch of transportation engineering that deals with the use of **electrical energy** for the propulsion of vehicles ‚Äî ranging from locomotives and electric multiple units (EMUs) to modern battery electric vehicles (BEVs), hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs), and fuel-cell electric vehicles (FCEVs).

In formal KTU terminology, the **Electric Drive-train** is the complete electromechanical system that converts **electrical energy stored in an energy source** (battery pack, ultra-capacitor, overhead line, fuel cell, or engine-generator set) into **controlled mechanical kinetic energy at the wheels**, while simultaneously providing regenerative braking capability to recover energy back into the source.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim-style):**
> *"An electric traction system is one in which the driving force (tractive effort) at the wheels is produced by an electric motor, supplied from a suitable electrical source, and transmitted through a power electronics converter that regulates speed and torque according to the operator/driver demand."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî "The Bowling Alley Power Steering"

Imagine you are pushing a heavy bowling ball down an alley by hand. It is exhausting because *your muscles* directly provide all the force. Now imagine the alley has a **powered conveyor belt** with a **variable-speed joystick control**. You simply nudge the joystick (driver pedal), and the belt (electric motor + inverter) does the heavy pushing ‚Äî smoothly, efficiently, and you can even make it push the ball *backwards* (regenerative braking).

| Real-World Analogy Component | Electric Drive-train Component |
|------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Heavy bowling ball | Vehicle mass (kerb + payload) |
| Your muscle force | Combustion engine (in ICE vehicles) |
| Powered conveyor belt | Electric traction motor |
| Joystick | Accelerator pedal ‚Üí VCU/inverter command |
| Conveyor speed controller | Power electronic inverter (PWM) |
| Energy source of conveyor | Battery / Overhead catenary / Fuel cell |
| Pushing ball backwards | Regenerative braking |

## 1.3 Historical Context & Why Electric Traction?

Electric traction predates the internal combustion engine for vehicles (early 1830s), but it was the **regenerative capability**, **instant high torque**, **efficiency (typically 80‚Äì95%)**, and **zero local emissions** that revived it for the 21st century mobility revolution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants & Standards Used Throughout This Module:**
> - Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$
> - Adhesion coefficient (dry rail): $\mu = 0.25 \text{ to } 0.35$
> - Adhesion coefficient (rubber tyre on dry asphalt): $\mu = 0.85 \text{ to } 1.00$
> - Wheel gauge for Indian Railways: **1676 mm** (Broad Gauge)
> - Typical traction motor efficiency: **88%‚Äì95%**
> - Standard supply voltage (Indian Railways OHE): **25 kV AC, 50 Hz**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Fundamental Forces Acting on a Traction Vehicle

When an electric vehicle (or locomotive) moves, it experiences **four distinct categories of forces** along its direction of travel. A KTU question is almost guaranteed to ask you to identify and quantify these.

> [!NOTE]
> **Force Balance Equation (signed convention):**
> $$\boxed{F_t \;=\; F_r \;+\; F_g \;+\; F_a}$$
> where the **Tractive Effort** $F_t$ produced at the wheel-rail (or tyre-road) contact patch must overcome the sum of all **resistive forces**.

### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown:

1. **Tractive Effort ($F_t$) ‚Äî The Driving Force**
   - Produced by the traction motor torque transmitted through the gear train to the driving wheels.
   - Limited by **adhesion**: $F_t \le \mu \cdot W$, where $W$ is the adhesive weight on driven axles.
   - Determined by: $F_t = \dfrac{T_m \cdot \eta_{gear} \cdot k}{r_w}$
     - $T_m$ = motor torque (N¬∑m)
     - $\eta_{gear}$ = transmission efficiency
     - $k$ = gear reduction ratio
     - $r_w$ = wheel radius (m)

2. **Rolling / Track Resistance ($F_r$)**
   - Combination of wheel-rail friction, internal gear friction, air turbulence through undercarriage.
   - Empirical Davis formula (rail traction):
     $$F_r = A + B \cdot v + C \cdot v^2$$
     where $A$ (N) = journal friction, $B \cdot v$ = flange friction, $C \cdot v^2$ = aerodynamic drag; $v$ in km/h.
   - Road vehicle approximation:
     $$F_r = f_r \cdot M \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$$
     where $f_r \approx 0.015$ for car tyres on asphalt.

3. **Gradient Resistance ($F_g$)**
   - Component of gravity acting along the slope.
   - On an incline of angle $\theta$: $F_g = M \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$
   - On a gradient of $G\%$: $F_g = \dfrac{M \cdot g \cdot G}{1000}$ (per unit mass = $g \cdot G / 1000$)

4. **Acceleration Resistance ($F_a$)**
   - Inertial force to bring the mass up to speed.
   - For a **rotating mass corrected** vehicle: $F_a = M \cdot (1 + r_m) \cdot a$
   - Where $r_m$ (rotational mass factor) $\approx 0.05$‚Äì$0.10$ for cars, $0.10$‚Äì$0.20$ for locomotives.

## 2.2 Types of Electric Traction Systems

| System Type | Energy Source | Example | Power Supply |
|-------------|---------------|---------|--------------|
| **Straight Electric** | Overhead line / Third rail | Indian Railways locos, Metro | 25 kV AC / 1.5 kV DC |
| **Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)** | On-board battery pack | Tata Nexon EV, Tesla Model 3 | 400V / 800V DC bus |
| **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)** | ICE + Battery | Toyota Prius | Battery + Fuel |
| **Fuel Cell EV (FCEV)** | Hydrogen fuel cell | Toyota Mirai | H‚ÇÇ ‚Üí DC bus |
| **Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV)** | Grid + ICE | Mitsubishi Outlander | 230V AC charging |
| **Capacitor / Ultra-cap Bus** | Ultra-capacitor | Proterra buses | DC fast charge |

## 2.3 Speed-Torque Characteristics of Ideal Traction Motor

The **ideal traction motor curve** must satisfy two contradictory demands simultaneously:

- **High starting torque** (to accelerate a heavy load from rest).
- **Constant power region** (to cruise at high speeds without exceeding motor rating).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ideal traction characteristic is a **hyperbolic curve** of constant power: $T \cdot \omega = P_{rated}$, often called the **"constant-power envelope"** or **"hyperbolic load diagram."**

This is precisely why **3-phase induction motors** (with vector control) and **PMSMs** are dominant in modern EV drive-trains ‚Äî their natural torque-speed envelope can be reshaped to closely follow this hyperbolic ideal.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Tractive Effort | $F_t = \dfrac{T_m \cdot \eta_g \cdot k}{r_w}$ | N | Limited by $\mu \cdot W$ |
| 2 | Motor Output Power | $P_m = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T_m}{60} = T_m \cdot \omega$ | W | $N$ in rpm |
| 3 | Rolling Resistance (Rail) | $F_r = A + B \cdot v + C \cdot v^2$ | N | $v$ in km/h |
| 4 | Rolling Resistance (Road) | $F_r = f_r \cdot M \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$ | N | $f_r \approx 0.015$ |
| 5 | Gradient Force | $F_g = M \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$ | N | Up-slope positive |
| 6 | Acceleration Force | $F_a = M \cdot (1 + r_m) \cdot a$ | N | $r_m$ accounts for rotating parts |
| 7 | Fundamental Equation | $F_t = F_r + F_g + F_a$ | N | Vector balance |
| 8 | Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC = \dfrac{\text{Energy}}{\text{Weight} \times \text{Distance}}$ | Wh/t¬∑km | Used in railways |
| 9 | Adhesion Limit | $F_t^{max} = \mu \cdot W$ | N | $W$ = adhesive weight |
| 10 | Vehicle Power Demand | $P_v = F_t \cdot v$ | W | $v$ in m/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Every modern EV manufacturer (Tata, Mahindra, Hyundai, Tesla) uses these exact equations during homologation testing for **ARAI range certification** and **MIDC (Modified Indian Driving Cycle)** simulation in MATLAB/Simulink.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Equation of Electric Traction

This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU Module 1 ‚Äî it is asked almost every examination cycle.

**Assumptions:**
- Vehicle of total mass $M$ (kg) moving on an inclined track of angle $\theta$ with acceleration $a$ (m/s¬≤).
- Rotating parts (armature, gears, wheels) possess equivalent rotating mass with rotational mass factor $r_m$.

### Step 1: Identify all forces acting along the direction of motion

The traction force $F_t$ pulls the vehicle forward. Three categories of resistive force oppose motion:
- Rolling resistance $F_r$
- Gravitational component along slope $F_g = M \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$
- Inertial force $F_a$ (translational + rotational inertia)

### Step 2: Apply Newton's Second Law (translational)

$$F_t - F_r - F_g = M \cdot a$$

Rearranging for the tractive effort required:

$$F_t = F_r + F_g + M \cdot a$$

### Step 3: Introduce rotational inertia correction

Rotating components (motor armature, rotor, gears, wheels) have angular kinetic energy. They resist angular acceleration. The equivalent extra translational mass is $M \cdot r_m$, so:

$$F_t = F_r + M \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + M \cdot (1 + r_m) \cdot a$$

This is the **Master Equation of Electric Traction** in its most general form.

$$\boxed{F_t = F_r + M \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + M \cdot (1 + r_m) \cdot a}$$

### Step 4: Express in convenient units (for railway-style problems)

For railway numericals, masses are in **tonnes** (T), speeds in **km/h**, accelerations in **km/h per second**.

Define: $M$ in tonnes, so weight = $M \cdot 1000 \cdot g$ Newtons.

Acceleration in m/s¬≤ = $\dfrac{a_{km/h/s}}{3.6}$.

Therefore:

$$F_t = F_r + \frac{M \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta}{1000} \cdot 1000 + \frac{M \cdot 1000 \cdot (1 + r_m) \cdot a_{m/s^2}}{1000}$$

Simplifying and substituting $a_{m/s^2} = a_{km/h/s} / 3.6$:

$$F_t = F_r + 9.81 \cdot M \cdot \sin\theta + 277.8 \cdot M \cdot (1 + r_m) \cdot a_{km/h/s}$$

where forces are in N (per axle if $M$ is per axle). This 277.8 constant comes from $1000/3.6$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical ‚Äî KTU Pattern

> **Problem:** A 200-tonne electric locomotive accelerates a 1000-tonne train up a 1.5% gradient at $0.8 \; \text{m/s}^2$. Rolling resistance is $50 \; \text{N/t}$, rotational mass factor $r_m = 0.10$. Calculate the **total tractive effort** required and the **tractive power** at a running speed of $45 \; \text{km/h}$.

### Solution (Detailed):

**Step 1 ‚Äî Total Mass:**
$$M = 200 + 1000 = 1200 \; \text{tonnes} = 1.2 \times 10^6 \; \text{kg}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Rolling Resistance:**
$$F_r = 50 \times 1200 = 60{,}000 \; \text{N}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Gradient Resistance:**
Convert gradient $G = 1.5\%$ to angle:
$$\theta = \arctan\left(\frac{1.5}{100}\right) = 0.8594^\circ$$

$$\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta = 0.015$$

$$F_g = M \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta = 1.2 \times 10^6 \times 9.81 \times 0.015$$

$$F_g = 176{,}580 \; \text{N}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Acceleration Resistance:**
$$F_a = M \cdot (1 + r_m) \cdot a = 1.2 \times 10^6 \times 1.10 \times 0.8$$

$$F_a = 1{,}056{,}000 \; \text{N}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Total Tractive Effort:**
$$F_t = F_r + F_g + F_a = 60{,}000 + 176{,}580 + 1{,}056{,}000$$

$$\boxed{F_t = 1{,}292{,}580 \; \text{N} \approx 1.293 \; \text{MN}}$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Tractive Power at 45 km/h:**
Convert speed: $v = \dfrac{45}{3.6} = 12.5 \; \text{m/s}$

$$P_t = F_t \cdot v = 1{,}292{,}580 \times 12.5$$

$$\boxed{P_t \approx 16.16 \; \text{MW}}$$

### Step 7 ‚Äî Cross-check with adhesion limit

If $\mu = 0.30$ and $W$ (adhesive weight) = weight on driving axles. Assuming all 200 tonnes is on driven axles:

$$F_t^{max} = \mu \cdot W = 0.30 \times 200 \times 1000 \times 9.81 = 588{,}600 \; \text{N}$$

Since required $F_t > F_t^{max}$, **wheel slip would occur** ‚Äî so this acceleration is **not physically realizable** without sanding the rails or using more driving axles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This numerical reveals a crucial reality check ‚Äî the **adhesion coefficient** $\mu$ is the silent governor that limits how much tractive effort you can actually apply, regardless of motor capability.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram ‚Äî Electric Traction Drive-train Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **functional block architecture** of a generic electric traction system (applicable to both locomotives and EVs).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sourceA[Energy Source Battery or OHE or FC] --> converterA[Power Converter Rectifier or DC DC]
    converterA --> invA[Inverter VVVF PWM Control]
    invA --> motorA[AC Traction Motor PMSM or IM]
    motorA --> gearA[Gearbox and Differential]
    gearA --> wheelA[Driving Wheels]
    wheelA --> contactA[Adhesion Contact Patch]
    contactA --> vehicleA[Vehicle Body Mass M]
    pedalA[Driver Accelerator Pedal] --> vcuA[VCU Vehicle Control Unit]
    brakeA[Brake Pedal] --> vcuA
    vcuA --> invA
    vcuA -.-> regenA[Regenerative Braking Path]
    regenA --> sourceA
    vehicleA -->|Rolling Friction Fr| wheelA
    vehicleA -->|Gradient Fg| wheelA
    vehicleA -->|Inertia Fa| wheelA
```

## 4.2 Force-Interaction Schematic During Acceleration

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph directionA[Direction of Motion]
        arrowA[Right Arrow Forward]
    end
    FtA[Ft Tractive Effort Forward] --> massA[Vehicle Mass M]
    massA -->|Opposes| FrA[Fr Rolling Resistance]
    massA -->|Opposes| FgA[Fg Gradient Force Downhill]
    massA -->|Opposes| FaA[Fa Inertia during acceleration]
    FtA -.->|Limited by| muA[mu times W Adhesion Limit]
```

## 4.3 Hierarchical Classification of Electric Drive-trains

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Electric Traction Drive trains]
    rootA --> typeA1[Energy Source Based]
    rootA --> typeA2[Motor Type Based]
    rootA --> typeA3[Transmission Based]
    typeA1 --> subA1a[Battery BEV]
    typeA1 --> subA1b[Fuel Cell FCEV]
    typeA1 --> subA1c[Hybrid HEV PHEV]
    typeA1 --> subA1d[Overhead Line Locomotive]
    typeA2 --> subA2a[DC Motor Separately Excited]
    typeA2 --> subA2b[3 Phase Induction Motor]
    typeA2 --> subA2c[PMSM Permanent Magnet]
    typeA2 --> subA2d[Switched Reluctance SRM]
    typeA3 --> subA3a[Single Gear Reduction]
    typeA3 --> subA3b[Multi Speed Transmission]
    typeA3 --> subA3c[Direct Drive In wheel]
```

## 4.4 Speed‚ÄìTorque Characteristic Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axisA[Speed N in rpm] --> regionA
    axisB[Torque T in Nm] --> regionA
    regionA[Operating Envelope]
    regionA --> z1[Zone 1 Constant Torque Region Starting to Base Speed]
    regionA --> z2[Zone 2 Constant Power Region Base to Max Speed]
    regionA --> z3[Zone 3 Series Field Weakening or Constant Slip Region]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students often write $F_t = F_r + F_g + F_a$ but **forget the rotational mass factor** $(1 + r_m)$ in $F_a$. This costs **2 marks** instantly on any numerical involving acceleration.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** Mixing units ‚Äî using km/h for speed in power calculation $P = F \cdot v$ without converting to m/s is the single most common arithmetic blunder.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam ‚Äî June 2024, Module 1]**
**Define electric traction. List any four advantages of electric traction over conventional diesel traction.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

*Definition (1.5 marks):* Electric traction is a transportation system in which the propulsive force (tractive effort) at the wheels is generated by an electric motor, fed from an electrical energy source through a power electronic converter.

*Advantages (4 √ó 0.5 = 2 marks ‚Äî any four):*
1. Higher overall efficiency (~90% vs ~35% for diesel locomotives).
2. Regenerative braking recovers kinetic energy back to the source.
3. No on-board combustion ‚Üí zero local emissions, quieter operation.
4. Better speed-torque control and faster acceleration response.
5. Lower maintenance due to fewer moving parts.
6. Can draw power from clean grid sources (renewable integration).

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023, Module 1]**
**What is meant by "adhesion coefficient" in electric traction? State its typical range for (a) rail-wheel contact and (b) rubber tyre on dry asphalt.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

*Definition (1 mark):* The coefficient of adhesion $\mu$ is the dimensionless ratio of the maximum tangential (tractive) force transmissible at the wheel-rail or tyre-road contact patch without slipping, to the normal adhesive weight pressing the contact together.

*Typical values (1 mark each):*
- (a) Rail-wheel contact: $\mu \approx 0.25 \text{ to } 0.35$ (dry rail)
- (b) Rubber tyre on dry asphalt: $\mu \approx 0.85 \text{ to } 1.00$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ‚Äî Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam ‚Äî June 2024]**

**Derive the fundamental equation of motion of a traction vehicle, clearly defining each term. Hence, for a 1500-tonne train hauled by a 300-tonne electric locomotive on a level track, calculate the tractive effort required to maintain a speed of 60 km/h. Given: rolling resistance = 45 N/t, rotational mass factor = 0.08, aerodynamic drag coefficient $C = 0.0006 \; \text{N/(km/h)}^2$ per tonne, $A = 0$, $B = 0$.**

#### **Part (a) ‚Äî Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** State the three resistive force categories acting on a traction vehicle:
- Rolling/track resistance $F_r$
- Gradient resistance $F_g = M g \sin\theta$
- Acceleration (inertial) resistance $F_a$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply Newton's second law along the direction of motion:
$$F_t - F_r - F_g = M \cdot a$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Introduce rotational mass factor $r_m$ to account for inertia of rotating parts (armature, gears, wheels):
$$F_a = M \cdot (1 + r_m) \cdot a$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Combine into the master equation:
$$\boxed{F_t = F_r + M g \sin\theta + M (1 + r_m) a}$$

#### **Part (b) ‚Äî Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Total mass $M = 1500 + 300 = 1800 \; \text{t} = 1.8 \times 10^6 \; \text{kg}$
- Level track $\Rightarrow \sin\theta = 0 \Rightarrow F_g = 0$
- Constant speed $v = 60 \; \text{km/h} \Rightarrow a = 0 \Rightarrow F_a = 0$
- Therefore, tractive effort must overcome **only rolling resistance + aerodynamic drag**.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Rolling resistance (1.5 marks):**
$$F_{r,mech} = 45 \times 1800 = 81{,}000 \; \text{N}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Aerodynamic drag (2 marks):**
Using $C = 0.0006 \; \text{N/(km/h)}^2$ per tonne, total train mass parameter = $1800 \times 0.0006 = 1.08 \; \text{N/(km/h)}^2$
$$F_{aero} = C_{total} \cdot v^2 = 1.08 \times (60)^2 = 1.08 \times 3600$$
$$F_{aero} = 3888 \; \text{N}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Total $F_r$ (1 mark):**
$$F_r = F_{r,mech} + F_{aero} = 81{,}000 + 3888 = 84{,}888 \; \text{N}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Tractive Effort (1 mark):**
Since $F_g = 0$ and $F_a = 0$:
$$F_t = F_r + F_g + F_a = 84{,}888 + 0 + 0$$
$$\boxed{F_t \approx 84.89 \; \text{kN}}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Tractive Power (1.5 marks):**
Convert speed: $v = 60/3.6 = 16.67 \; \text{m/s}$
$$P_t = F_t \cdot v = 84{,}888 \times 16.67 \approx 1.415 \; \text{MW}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks ‚Äî ALTERNATIVE CHOICE): [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023]**

**Explain with a neat sketch the force distribution acting on a traction vehicle moving up an incline. Also explain the concept of "adhesion limit" and why it is the deciding factor for maximum tractive effort.**

#### **Part (a) ‚Äî Force Distribution on Incline (7 marks)**

When a vehicle of mass $M$ moves up a track inclined at angle $\theta$:

**Forces acting along direction of motion (1.5 marks):**
1. **Tractive Effort $F_t$** ‚Äî along the inclined plane, forward direction.
2. **Rolling Resistance $F_r$** ‚Äî opposite to motion.
3. **Weight Component $W \sin\theta$** ‚Äî pulls vehicle backward (down-slope).
4. **Normal Component $W \cos\theta$** ‚Äî perpendicular to track; this contributes to adhesion.

**Mathematical Statement (1.5 marks):**
$$F_t = F_r + M g \sin\theta + M (1 + r_m) a$$

**Sketch (block schematic) using inline representation (3 marks):**

```
           Direction of Motion ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñ∫

                    F_t ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñ∫
                  ‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê
                  ‚îÇ  M (g)  ‚îÇ  ‚óÑ‚îÄ‚îÄ F_r  (rolling)
                  ‚îÇ Vehicle ‚îÇ  ‚óÑ‚îÄ‚îÄ Mg sinŒ∏ (gradient)
                  ‚îÇ         ‚îÇ
                  ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚î¨‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îò
                       ‚îÇ  ‚ñº Mg (weight, vertically down)
                       ‚îÇ
                    ‚ï± Œ∏ ‚ï≤   ‚óÑ‚îÄ‚îÄ Incline
                  ‚ï±       ‚ï≤
                ‚ï±___________‚ï≤
              Mg cosŒ∏ (normal to surface)
```

**Key insights (1 mark):**
- On level track, $F_g = 0$.
- On up-gradient, additional force $Mg \sin\theta$ is needed.
- Normal reaction reduces on slope, slightly affecting rolling resistance.

#### **Part (b) ‚Äî Adhesion Limit Concept (7 marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):** The adhesion limit is the **maximum tractive effort** that can be transmitted at the wheel-rail (or tyre-road) contact without causing the wheels to slip. It is given by:

$$F_t^{max} = \mu \cdot W$$

where $W$ is the **adhesive weight** (weight on driven axles only) and $\mu$ is the **coefficient of adhesion** (dimensionless).

**Why it is the deciding factor (3 marks):**
- No matter how powerful the traction motor is, the wheel can only transmit force up to $\mu \cdot W$.
- If demanded $F_t > \mu \cdot W$, the wheel slips ‚Üí loss of traction ‚Üí "wheel spin" in road vehicles, "wheel slip" in locomotives.
- Slippage causes wear, inefficiency, and potential damage to the contact surface.

**Methods to improve adhesion (2 marks):**
1. **Sand spraying** on rails (rail traction).
2. **Anti-slip regulation (ASR)** ‚Äî modern VCU reduces torque the instant slip is detected.
3. **Distributed traction** ‚Äî using more driving axles to increase total $W$.
4. **Better contact surface** ‚Äî rail grinding, high-friction tyre compounds.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ‚ö°

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ‚Äî Module 1, Electric Traction Basics**

- ‚úÖ **Electric Traction = Motor + Power Electronics + Energy Source + Wheels.**
- ‚úÖ **Master Equation:** $F_t = F_r + M g \sin\theta + M (1 + r_m) a$
- ‚úÖ **Adhesion Limit:** $F_t^{max} = \mu \cdot W$ ‚Äî the hard physical ceiling.
- ‚úÖ **Rolling Resistance:** Davis formula $A + Bv + Cv^2$ for rail; $f_r Mg\cos\theta$ for road.
- ‚úÖ **Gradient Force:** $Mg\sin\theta$ ‚Äî uphill adds, downhill reduces $F_t$ needed.
- ‚úÖ **Acceleration Force:** Always include $(1 + r_m)$ factor.
- ‚úÖ **Ideal Traction Curve:** Hyperbolic (constant power) ‚Äî $T \cdot \omega = P_{rated}$.
- ‚úÖ **Unit conversions:** km/h ‚Üí m/s: divide by 3.6; tonnes ‚Üí kg: multiply by 1000.
- ‚úÖ **Energy source options:** OHE, third rail, battery, fuel cell, ultra-capacitor.
- ‚úÖ **Motor choices:** DC, IM (robust), PMSM (efficient), SRM (cheap & fault-tolerant).
- ‚úÖ **Regenerative braking** distinguishes electric from diesel traction fundamentally.
- ‚úÖ **Adhesion values to memorize:** Rail ~0.30, Dry asphalt ~0.90, Wet asphalt ~0.50.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Mnemonic ‚Äî "FT-RGA":**
> **F**orce **T**ractive = **R**olling + **G**radient + **A**cceleration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Various Electric Drive-train Topologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Various Electric Drive-train Topologies**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

An **Electric Drive-train Topology** refers to the systematic architectural arrangement and interconnection of the prime mover(s), energy storage system(s), power electronic converters, transmission elements, and the final drive used to deliver tractive power to the wheels of an electrified vehicle. According to the KTU 2024 scheme framework, the topology defines *how* mechanical and electrical energy pathways are organized between the **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)**, **Electric Motor (EM)**, **Generator**, **Battery Pack**, and the **driving wheels** ‚Äî directly influencing the vehicle's fuel economy, emissions, performance, and control complexity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot**
> A drive-train topology is *not* a single component ‚Äî it is a **system-level blueprint** describing the **energy flow path** from source(s) to wheels, including all power-splitting and power-coupling mechanisms.

The major classifications covered under **OEEET722 ‚Äì Module 1** are:

1. **Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)** ‚Äî Pure electric, grid-charged.
2. **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV)** ‚Äî Pure electric, hydrogen-fed.
3. **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)** ‚Äî ICE + Electric Motor combination.
   - **Series Hybrid (SHEV)**
   - **Parallel Hybrid (PHEV)**
   - **Series-Parallel Hybrid (SPHEV) / Power-Split Hybrid**
   - **Complex (Multi-Mode) Hybrid**

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî "The Kitchen Restaurant Model"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ‚Äî The Restaurant Kitchen**
> Think of a vehicle drive-train as a **restaurant kitchen preparing a meal (motion)**.
>
> - **Battery / Fuel Cell** ‚Üí The **pantry** (raw ingredients stored).
> - **ICE** ‚Üí The **gas stove** (fast cooking, but smoky and noisy).
> - **Electric Motor** ‚Üí The **microwave / induction cooker** (clean, quiet, instant).
> - **Transmission / Coupling** ‚Üí The **waiter** carrying plates to the customer (wheels).
> - **Control Unit (VCU)** ‚Üí The **head chef** deciding which cooker to use, when, and how much.
>
> A **Series topology** is like a kitchen with *only* a microwave ‚Äî the gas stove is used *only* to recharge the microwave's battery indirectly. A **Parallel topology** is like having *both* the stove and the microwave running at the same time, each delivering food straight to the table. A **Series-Parallel** kitchen has a *smart split* ‚Äî sometimes food comes from one, sometimes the other, and sometimes both at once.

This mental model makes it intuitive to grasp *why* different vehicle classes (city cars, SUVs, heavy trucks) prefer different topologies.

---

### 1.3 Why Topologies Matter ‚Äî Engineering Significance

| Performance Parameter | Why Topology Choice Influences It |
|---|---|
| **Fuel Economy** | Determines how often ICE runs at its optimal efficiency point. |
| **All-Electric Range (AER)** | BEV / Plug-in HEVs offer long AER; mild HEVs offer almost none. |
| **Emissions (CO‚ÇÇ, NO‚Çì, PM)** | Series topologies can keep ICE in sweet spot ‚Üí low emissions. |
| **Component Sizing** | Parallel needs smaller EM; Series needs larger EM + Generator. |
| **Packaging & Weight** | Parallel is mechanically complex; Series is simpler but heavier. |
| **Cost** | Series > SPHEV > Parallel > Mild Hybrid in typical BOM cost. |
| **Driveability** | Parallel & SPHEV offer direct mechanical feel; Series feels indirect. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metric used in KTU Evaluation**
> - **Specific Energy of Battery:** **150‚Äì250 Wh/kg** (modern Li-ion).
> - **Specific Power of Battery:** **500‚Äì3000 W/kg**.
> - **ICE Peak Thermal Efficiency:** roughly **35‚Äì40%** (gasoline), **40‚Äì45%** (diesel).
> - **Electric Motor Efficiency:** typically **90‚Äì95%**.
> - **Fuel Cell Stack Efficiency:** **50‚Äì60%**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Electric Drive-train Topologies

The KTU 2024 scheme recognizes a hierarchical classification rooted in the **degree of hybridization (DoH)** and the **mechanical/electrical coupling architecture**.

#### 2.1.1 Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) Topology

- **Architecture:** `Battery ‚Üí DC/DC Converter ‚Üí Inverter ‚Üí EM ‚Üí Transmission ‚Üí Wheels`.
- **Energy Source:** Grid electricity only.
- **Zero Tailpipe Emission (ZTE).**
- **Range limited by battery State of Charge (SoC).**
- **Examples:** Tata Nexon EV, MG ZS EV, Tesla Model 3.

**Operating Logic Steps:**
1. Driver presses accelerator ‚Üí VCU commands torque request.
2. Battery delivers DC power.
3. Traction inverter converts DC ‚Üí 3-phase AC.
4. EM produces tractive torque.
5. Single-speed or multi-speed transmission adapts torque to wheels.
6. **Regenerative Braking:** Wheels drive the EM as a generator ‚Üí energy fed back to battery.

---

#### 2.1.2 Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV) Topology

- **Architecture:** `H‚ÇÇ Tank ‚Üí Fuel Cell Stack ‚Üí DC/DC Boost Converter ‚Üí Inverter ‚Üí EM ‚Üí Wheels`, with a small **Li-ion buffer battery** for transient power assist and regenerative braking.
- **Energy Source:** Compressed hydrogen (350 bar or 700 bar).
- **Only by-product:** water vapour.
- **Examples:** Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo.

**Operating Logic Steps:**
1. Hydrogen fed to fuel cell stack anode; oxygen from air at cathode.
2. Electrochemical reaction produces DC electricity.
3. DC/DC booster raises voltage to traction bus level (typically 300‚Äì650 V).
4. Inverter drives the traction motor.
5. Auxiliary battery handles load transients and recovers braking energy.

---

#### 2.1.3 Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV) Topology

- **Architecture:** `ICE ‚Üí Generator ‚Üí Rectifier ‚Üí Battery (DC Bus) ‚Üí Inverter ‚Üí EM ‚Üí Wheels`.
- **No mechanical link** between ICE and wheels.
- ICE acts as a **range extender / auxiliary power unit (APU)**.
- **Examples:** Chevrolet Volt (1st gen), BMW i3 REx, Nissan e-Power (Kicks).

**Operating Logic Steps:**
1. ICE runs at its **best-efficiency operating point** (constant speed region).
2. Generator converts mechanical power to electrical power.
3. Power goes to battery or directly to traction motor.
4. Traction motor is the **only** device delivering torque to the wheels.
5. Battery buffers the energy mismatch.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** In a *true* Series Hybrid, the ICE **never** drives the wheels directly ‚Äî even though the wheels are mechanically connected to *something* (the EM), the ICE contributes only via the electrical path.

---

#### 2.1.4 Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV) Topology

- **Architecture:** `ICE ‚Äñ EM` ‚Äî both devices mechanically coupled to the wheels via a **torque coupler** (clutch or planetary gear).
- **Both** can deliver tractive power **simultaneously or independently**.
- **Examples:** Honda Civic Hybrid (IMA), Hyundai Ioniq Hybrid.

**Operating Logic Steps:**
1. VCU decides between: **ICE only**, **EM only**, or **Combined (ICE + EM)**.
2. At low loads ‚Üí EM alone (city driving).
3. At high loads ‚Üí both ICE and EM share the torque.
4. During cruising ‚Üí ICE alone at efficient point.
5. During braking ‚Üí EM acts as generator, ICE decoupled via clutch.

**Modes of Operation:**
- **Pure Electric Mode**
- **Pure ICE Mode**
- **Power Assist Mode (ICE + EM)**
- **Regenerative Braking Mode**
- **Battery Charging Mode (ICE ‚Üí EM-as-generator ‚Üí Battery)**

---

#### 2.1.5 Series-Parallel Hybrid (Power-Split Hybrid) Topology

- **Architecture:** Combines series and parallel paths using a **Planetary Gear Set (PGS)** as a power-split device. This is the famous **Toyota Hybrid System (THS)**.
- **Examples:** Toyota Prius, Camry Hybrid, Ford Escape Hybrid.

**Operating Logic Steps:**
1. **Sun gear** connected to Generator (MG1).
2. **Planet carrier** connected to ICE.
3. **Ring gear** connected to Traction Motor (MG2) and final drive.
4. MG1 controls ICE speed; MG2 drives the wheels.
5. Energy split is continuously variable ‚Äî *electronic CVT (e-CVT)*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Viva Favorite:** "How is THS different from a normal CVT?"
> Answer: THS uses a **planetary gear + two motor-generators** to act as a *mechanical-electronic* continuously variable transmission ‚Äî there are **no belts or pulleys**.

---

#### 2.1.6 Complex (Multi-Mode) Hybrid Topology

- **Architecture:** Adds **two clutches** (or more) and a **second planetary gear set** to enable *all* series, parallel, and series-parallel modes.
- **Examples:** Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive (2nd gen), Lexus RX 400h, Ford F-150 PowerBoost.

This is the most flexible (and expensive) topology.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All equations are in **SI units** unless explicitly mentioned. Use `\vert` for absolute value to preserve table formatting.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variable Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Tractive Force | $F_{tr} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}$ | Forces at wheel | N |
| 2 | Rolling Resistance | $F_{rr} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos\theta$ | $C_{rr}$ = coefficient | N |
| 3 | Aerodynamic Drag | $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_{d} \cdot A \cdot v^{2}$ | $C_{d}$ drag coeff., $A$ frontal area | N |
| 4 | Hill Climbing | $F_{hc} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$ | $\theta$ = road slope | N |
| 5 | Linear Acceleration | $F_{la} = m \cdot a$ | $a$ = vehicle accel. | N |
| 6 | Tractive Power | $P_{tr} = F_{tr} \cdot v$ | Product of force and velocity | W |
| 7 | Wheel Torque | $T_{w} = F_{tr} \cdot r_{w}$ | $r_{w}$ = wheel radius | Nm |
| 8 | Motor Torque | $T_{m} = T_{w} / (\eta_{g} \cdot G)$ | $G$ = gear ratio | Nm |
| 9 | Energy of Battery | $E_{bat} = V_{oc} \cdot Q_{rated}$ | $V_{oc}$ open-circuit V, $Q$ Ah | Wh |
| 10 | State of Charge | $SoC = (Q_{used} / Q_{rated}) \cdot 100$ | Percentage | \% |
| 11 | Degree of Hybridization | $DoH = P_{em} / (P_{em} + P_{ICE})$ | Ratio of EM to total power | dimensionless |
| 12 | Fuel Cell Power | $P_{fc} = \dot{m}_{H_2} \cdot LHV_{H_2} \cdot \eta_{fc}$ | $\dot{m}$ = H‚ÇÇ mass flow | W |
| 13 | Series Hybrid EM Power | $P_{em} = P_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{m}$ | Cascaded efficiencies | W |
| 14 | Parallel Hybrid Power | $P_{wheels} = P_{ICE} + P_{EM}$ | Additive (after gear) | W |
| 15 | Regenerative Braking Efficiency | $\eta_{regen} = E_{recovered} / E_{kinetic}$ | Fraction | \% |

---

### 2.3 Comparative Analysis ‚Äî Which Topology for Which Application?

| Parameter | BEV | FCEV | SHEV (Series) | PHEV (Parallel) | SPHEV |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tailpipe Emission** | 0 | 0 (H‚ÇÇO only) | Low | Medium | Low |
| **Mechanical Complexity** | Lowest | Low | Medium | High | Highest |
| **Electrical Complexity** | Medium | High | Medium | Medium | High |
| **All-Electric Range** | 200‚Äì600 km | 400‚Äì650 km | 1‚Äì5 km | 0 (HEV) / 30‚Äì80 km (PHEV) | 1‚Äì5 km |
| **Refuelling Time** | 30 min‚Äì8 hr (AC/DC) | 5‚Äì10 min | Petrol refuel | Petrol refuel | Petrol refuel |
| **Component Cost** | High (battery) | Very High (FC stack) | Medium | Medium | High |
| **Best Suited For** | Urban, short routes | Long-haul, heavy duty | City buses, delivery vans | Compact‚Äìmid sedans | Mid‚Äìluxury SUVs |
| **Example Vehicle** | Tesla Model 3 | Toyota Mirai | Chevy Volt | Honda Civic Hybrid | Toyota Prius |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam ‚Äî "SCAP" for HEV Types:**
> **S**eries ‚Äî *one* path (electric only to wheels).
> **C**oupled-Parallel ‚Äî *two* paths.
> **A**daptive / **S**eries-Parallel ‚Äî *mixed* paths.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation ‚Äî Tractive Force Demand for an Electrified Vehicle

The **Tractive Force Equation** is the *master equation* every KTU HEV problem is built on. We derive it below step-by-step.

> **Step 1 ‚Äî Identify all forces opposing motion of a vehicle on a gradient.**

When a vehicle of mass $m$ moves at velocity $v$ on a road with slope angle $\theta$, four forces oppose its motion (in the longitudinal direction):
1. **Rolling Resistance Force ($F_{rr}$)** ‚Äî due to tyre deformation.
2. **Aerodynamic Drag Force ($F_{ad}$)** ‚Äî due to air viscosity.
3. **Hill Climbing Force ($F_{hc}$)** ‚Äî component of gravity.
4. **Linear Acceleration Force ($F_{la}$)** ‚Äî Newton's second law.

> **Step 2 ‚Äî Express each force mathematically.**

For rolling resistance (on a slope):

$$F_{rr} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos\theta$$

> *Reasoning:* The normal reaction on a slope is reduced by factor $\cos\theta$, hence the normal force is $mg\cos\theta$. Multiplying by the coefficient of rolling resistance $C_{rr}$ (typically 0.008‚Äì0.015 for car tyres) gives the resisting force.

For aerodynamic drag:

$$F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_{d} \cdot A_{f} \cdot v^{2}$$

> *Reasoning:* Drag is proportional to dynamic pressure ($\tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^{2}$), drag coefficient $C_{d}$, and frontal area $A_{f}$.

For hill climbing (component of weight along the slope):

$$F_{hc} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$$

> *Reasoning:* The component of weight acting down the slope is $mg\sin\theta$. For level road, $\theta = 0$ and $F_{hc} = 0$.

For linear acceleration (Newton's 2nd law):

$$F_{la} = m \cdot a = m \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$$

> *Reasoning:* To accelerate mass $m$ at rate $a$, a net forward force $ma$ is required.

> **Step 3 ‚Äî Sum the resisting forces and equate to tractive force.**

By force balance on the vehicle:

$$F_{tr} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}$$

Substituting all expressions:

$$
F_{tr} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos\theta \; + \; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_{d} A_{f} v^{2} \; + \; m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta \; + \; m \cdot a
$$

> **Step 4 ‚Äî Convert tractive force to tractive power.**

The instantaneous tractive power required at the wheels is:

$$P_{tr}(t) = F_{tr}(t) \cdot v(t)$$

> *Reasoning:* Power = Force √ó Velocity (when force and velocity are collinear, as in the longitudinal direction).

> **Step 5 ‚Äî Convert wheel power to motor shaft power.**

If the drivetrain has total efficiency $\eta_{dr} = \eta_{g} \cdot \eta_{gb} \cdot \eta_{final}$ (gear, gearbox, final drive), then motor power demand is:

$$P_{m} = \frac{P_{tr}}{\eta_{dr}}$$

> *Reasoning:* The drivetrain absorbs some mechanical power as losses; the motor must therefore supply more than the wheel demand.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Final Form (Tractable):**
> The combined tractive force equation is *the* most-tested equation in OEEET722 KTU exams. Memorize the form and the meaning of each term.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Energy Flow in Each Topology

| Topology | ICE ‚Üí Wheels Path | EM ‚Üí Wheels Path | Battery Charged By |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BEV** | Not present | Direct (Battery ‚Üí Inverter ‚Üí EM) | Grid only |
| **FCEV** | Not present | Direct (FC + Battery ‚Üí Inverter ‚Üí EM) | Fuel cell + regen braking |
| **Series HEV** | None (mechanical) | Indirect (ICE ‚Üí Gen ‚Üí Bat ‚Üí EM) | ICE (via gen) + regen |
| **Parallel HEV** | Direct (mechanical coupler) | Direct (mechanical coupler) | ICE (via EM-gen) + regen |
| **Series-Parallel** | Both paths active | Both paths active | ICE + regen + split |
| **Complex** | All paths possible | All paths possible | All sources |

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

> **Question:** A BEV of mass $m = 1500$ kg climbs a road of slope $\theta = 10¬∞$ at constant velocity $v = 60$ km/h. Given $C_{rr} = 0.012$, $C_{d} = 0.30$, $A_f = 2.2$ m¬≤, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m¬≥, drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{dr} = 0.90$, find the **motor shaft power** required.

**Solution:**

> **Step A ‚Äî Convert velocity to SI units.**

$$v = 60 \text{ km/h} = 60 \times \tfrac{1000}{3600} = 16.667 \text{ m/s}$$

> **Step B ‚Äî Compute each resisting force.**

Rolling resistance:

$$F_{rr} = 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.012 \cdot \cos(10¬∞) = 173.95 \text{ N}$$

Aerodynamic drag:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{ad} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 2.2 \cdot (16.667)^{2} \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 2.2 \cdot 277.79 \\
&= 112.27 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hill climbing:

$$F_{hc} = 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot \sin(10¬∞) = 2554.55 \text{ N}$$

Linear acceleration (constant speed ‚Üí $a = 0$):

$$F_{la} = 0$$

> **Step C ‚Äî Total tractive force.**

$$F_{tr} = 173.95 + 112.27 + 2554.55 + 0 = 2840.77 \text{ N}$$

> **Step D ‚Äî Tractive power at wheels.**

$$P_{tr} = 2840.77 \cdot 16.667 = 47{,}346 \text{ W} \approx 47.35 \text{ kW}$$

> **Step E ‚Äî Motor shaft power (accounting for drivetrain losses).**

$$P_{m} = \frac{47{,}346}{0.90} = 52{,}607 \text{ W} \approx 52.61 \text{ kW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** The traction motor must deliver approximately **52.6 kW** of shaft power. The hill-climbing force dominates ‚Äî about **90%** of the total demand comes from $F_{hc}$ alone.

---

### 3.4 Power-Flow Loss-Cascade for a Series Hybrid

In a **Series Hybrid**, the ICE ‚Üí Wheels power path involves *four* energy conversions, each with its own efficiency. The KTU board often asks students to compute the **overall well-to-wheel efficiency** of each topology. Below is the cascade for Series:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{ICE} &\xrightarrow{\eta_{gen}} P_{gen,elec} \quad \text{(mech ‚Üí elec, typically 0.92‚Äì0.95)} \\
&\xrightarrow{\eta_{rect}} P_{dc,bus} \quad \text{(AC ‚Üí DC rectifier, 0.96‚Äì0.98)} \\
&\xrightarrow{\eta_{bat}} P_{bat,out} \quad \text{(battery charge/discharge, 0.90‚Äì0.95)} \\
&\xrightarrow{\eta_{inv}} P_{ac,motor} \quad \text{(DC ‚Üí AC inverter, 0.95‚Äì0.98)} \\
&\xrightarrow{\eta_{m}} P_{shaft} \quad \text{(motor electromechanical, 0.90‚Äì0.95)} \\
&\xrightarrow{\eta_{tr}} P_{wheels} \quad \text{(transmission, 0.92‚Äì0.96)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a Parallel Hybrid, only **one** conversion is needed when ICE drives the wheels:

$$
P_{ICE} \xrightarrow{\eta_{tr}} P_{wheels}
$$

This is the **fundamental reason** parallel hybrids tend to have higher well-to-wheel efficiency in steady-state highway driving ‚Äî fewer conversion losses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Overall Series-Hybrid Efficiency Estimate:**
> $\eta_{series} = 0.93 \cdot 0.97 \cdot 0.92 \cdot 0.97 \cdot 0.93 \cdot 0.94 \approx 0.68$ ‚Üí about **68%**.
> In contrast, a parallel hybrid operating with direct ICE drive can achieve **80‚Äì85%** in the same highway mode.

---

### 3.5 Degree of Hybridization (DoH) ‚Äî Derivation

The DoH quantifies the *extent* to which electrical power supports traction:

$$
DoH = \frac{P_{EM}}{P_{EM} + P_{ICE}}
$$

- $DoH = 0$ ‚Üí Conventional ICE vehicle (no hybridization).
- $0 < DoH < 1$ ‚Üí Hybrid (micro/mild/full depending on value).
- $DoH = 1$ ‚Üí Full Hybrid (e.g., Toyota Prius) or BEV.

**KTU Classification Standard:**

| Class | DoH | Typical EM Power |
|---|---|---|
| Micro Hybrid | $< 0.10$ | 3‚Äì7 kW (start/stop) |
| Mild Hybrid | $0.10$ ‚Äì $0.30$ | 10‚Äì20 kW (BSG/ISG) |
| Full Hybrid | $0.30$ ‚Äì $0.60$ | 30‚Äì80 kW |
| Plug-in Hybrid | $0.60$ ‚Äì $1.00$ | 60‚Äì120 kW |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams use **Mermaid `flowchart` syntax** with **alphanumeric node IDs** and **raw uppercase labels** (no markdown formatting inside the labels) ‚Äî to comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguards.

### 4.1 Block Diagram ‚Äî Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) Drive-train

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  A1[GRID CHARGER] --> A2[HV BATTERY PACK]
  A2 --> A3[DC DC CONVERTER 12V]
  A2 --> A4[TRACTION INVERTER]
  A3 --> A5[AUXILIARY LOAD]
  A4 --> A6[TRACTION MOTOR PMSM]
  A6 --> A7[SINGLE SPEED GEARBOX]
  A7 --> A8[FINAL DRIVE]
  A8 --> A9[DRIVING WHEELS]
  A9 -.REGEN.-> A6
  A6 -.BACK EMF.-> A4
  A4 -.CHARGING.-> A2
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram ‚Äî Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV) Drive-train

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  B1[HIGH PRESSURE H2 TANK 700 BAR] --> B2[FUEL CELL STACK]
  B3[AMBIENT AIR INTAKE] --> B2
  B2 --> B4[DC DC BOOST CONVERTER]
  B5[LI ION BUFFER BATTERY] --> B4
  B4 --> B6[HIGH VOLTAGE DC BUS]
  B6 --> B7[TRACTION INVERTER]
  B7 --> B8[TRACTION MOTOR]
  B8 --> B9[TRANSMISSION]
  B9 --> B10[WHEELS]
  B10 -.REGEN.-> B8
  B8 -.GENERATOR MODE.-> B7
  B7 -.CHARGING.-> B5
  B2 -.WATER VAPOR.-> B11[EXHAUST H2O]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram ‚Äî Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  C1[FUEL TANK] --> C2[ICE ENGINE]
  C2 --> C3[GENERATOR MG1]
  C3 --> C4[AC DC RECTIFIER]
  C4 --> C5[HV BATTERY]
  C5 --> C6[DC BUS]
  C4 --> C6
  C6 --> C7[TRACTION INVERTER]
  C7 --> C8[TRACTION MOTOR MG2]
  C8 --> C9[FIXED RATIO GEARBOX]
  C9 --> C10[FINAL DRIVE]
  C10 --> C11[WHEELS]
  C11 -.REGEN.-> C8
  C5 -.SOc CONTROL.-> C3
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram ‚Äî Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  D1[FUEL TANK] --> D2[ICE ENGINE]
  D2 --> D3[CLUTCH C1]
  D3 --> D4[TORQUE COUPLER]

  D6 --> D7[TRACTION MOTOR]
  D7 --> D4

  D10 -.REGEN.-> D7
  D7 -.GEN MODE.-> D5
  D3 -.DISENGAGE FOR EV.-> D4
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram ‚Äî Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid ‚Äî Toyota THS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  E1[FUEL TANK] --> E2[ICE ENGINE]
  E2 --> E3[PLANET CARRIER]
  E3 --> E4[PLANETARY GEAR SET]
  E4 --> E5[SUN GEAR]
  E4 --> E6[RING GEAR]
  E5 --> E7[MG1 GENERATOR]
  E6 --> E8[MG2 TRACTION MOTOR]
  E7 --> E9[INVERTER 1]
  E8 --> E10[INVERTER 2]
  E11[HV BATTERY] --> E9
  E11 --> E10
  E9 --> E11
  E10 --> E11
  E8 --> E12[FINAL DRIVE]
  E12 --> E13[WHEELS]
  E13 -.REGEN.-> E8
```

### 4.6 Comparative Topology Map ‚Äî Power Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  subgraph TOPO1[BEV]
    F1[BATTERY] --> F2[INVERTER] --> F3[MOTOR] --> F4[WHEELS]
  end
  subgraph TOPO2[SHEV]
    G1[ICE] --> G2[GENERATOR] --> G3[BATTERY DC BUS] --> G4[INVERTER] --> G5[MOTOR] --> G6[WHEELS]
  end

    H1[ICE] --> H2[COUPLER] --> H4[WHEELS]

  end
  subgraph TOPO4[SPHEV]
```
