> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Pattern:** KTU 2024 board papers usually include a **draw-the-block-diagram** sub-question (7 marks) for one topology + **compare two topologies** as another sub-question (7 marks). Master the six diagrams above and you are well-prepared.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]** Define an **Electric Drive-train Topology**. List the **four main hybrid drive-train topologies** recognised in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An electric drive-train topology is the **systematic arrangement of energy sources, power converters, transmission, and traction devices** that defines the energy flow path from the source(s) to the driving wheels in an electrified vehicle.

The four main hybrid topologies are:
1. **Series Hybrid (SHEV)**
2. **Parallel Hybrid (PHEV)**
3. **Series-Parallel / Power-Split Hybrid (SPHEV)**
4. **Complex / Multi-Mode Hybrid**

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing with brief role.)*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]** Distinguish between a **Series** and a **Parallel** hybrid electric vehicle based on the **mechanical coupling** between the ICE and the wheels.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Series Hybrid | Parallel Hybrid |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanical link ICE ‚Üî Wheels | **No** direct link | **Yes**, via clutch/coupler |
| Device driving wheels | Only the **traction motor** | Both ICE and EM (separately or together) |
| Number of energy conversions | Multiple (mech ‚Üí elec ‚Üí mech) | Fewer (mechanical direct) |
| Best efficiency regime | Steady city driving | Highway cruising |

*(1 mark each for any 3 of the above points.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Instructions:** Answer **any one** of the two choices. Each choice has two sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** **[7 marks]** With a **neat block diagram**, explain the **Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV)** drive-train topology. Discuss its **operating modes** and the **role of the traction motor and generator**.

**Model Solution:**

> **Block Diagram (3 marks):**
> *(Refer to Section 4.3 Mermaid block diagram; reproduce on paper as a clean flow: Fuel Tank ‚Üí ICE ‚Üí Generator ‚Üí Rectifier ‚Üí Battery (DC Bus) ‚Üí Inverter ‚Üí Traction Motor ‚Üí Gearbox ‚Üí Wheels.)*

> **Operating Modes (4 marks):**
>
> 1. **Pure Electric Mode:** ICE off; battery alone powers the EM. Used during low-speed city driving.
> 2. **ICE Charging Mode (APU Mode):** ICE drives the generator to charge the battery while the EM propels the vehicle. The ICE operates at its best-efficiency point (e.g., BSFC minimum).
> 3. **Hybrid Traction Mode:** ICE and battery both supply power to the EM simultaneously (peak demand, e.g., overtaking).
> 4. **Regenerative Braking Mode:** Wheels drive the EM as a generator; energy stored in battery; ICE is off (clutch disengaged or idle).

> **Role of Traction Motor and Generator (‚âà lines for completeness):**
> - **Traction Motor (MG2):** Sole provider of tractive torque to the wheels; sized for peak power requirement of the vehicle.
> - **Generator (MG1):** Converts ICE mechanical power to electrical power; also acts as a motor to crank-start the ICE; in some designs (e.g., Chevrolet Volt), the generator is reversible.

**(b)** **[7 marks]** Derive the **Tractive Force Equation** for a vehicle moving on a sloped road. A BEV of mass **1800 kg** climbs a **5¬∞ slope at 50 km/h**. Given $C_{rr}=0.015$, $C_d=0.28$, $A_f=2.3$ m¬≤, $\rho=1.2$ kg/m¬≥, $\eta_{dr}=0.92$, find the **motor shaft power** in kW. Take $a=0$.

**Model Solution:**

> **Derivation (3 marks):**
>
> The total tractive force equals the sum of rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, hill-climbing force, and linear acceleration force:
>
> $$F_{tr} = m g C_{rr} \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^2 + m g \sin\theta + m a$$
>
> Tractive power at wheels is $P_{tr} = F_{tr} \cdot v$, and motor shaft power is $P_m = P_{tr} / \eta_{dr}$.

> **Numerical Solution (4 marks):**
>
> Velocity: $v = 50 \times \tfrac{1000}{3600} = 13.889$ m/s.
>
> $F_{rr} = 1800 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.015 \cdot \cos(5¬∞) = 263.78$ N
>
> $F_{ad} = 0.5 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 0.28 \cdot 2.3 \cdot (13.889)^2 = 93.21$ N
>
> $F_{hc} = 1800 \cdot 9.81 \cdot \sin(5¬∞) = 1538.99$ N
>
> $F_{la} = 0$ (constant speed)
>
> $F_{tr} = 263.78 + 93.21 + 1538.99 + 0 = 1895.98$ N
>
> $P_{tr} = 1895.98 \cdot 13.889 = 26{,}333$ W = **26.33 kW**
>
> $P_m = 26.33 / 0.92 = \mathbf{28.62 \; kW}$

> **Final Answer (7 marks total):** $P_m \approx 28.62$ kW.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî Alternative Choice**

**(a)** **[7 marks]** Explain the **Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle** drive-train topology with a **neat block diagram**. List and briefly describe its **five operating modes**.

**Model Solution:**

> **Block Diagram (3 marks):**
> *(Refer to Section 4.4 Mermaid block. Reproduce on paper: Fuel Tank ‚Üí ICE ‚Üí Clutch ‚Üí Coupler ‚Üí Gearbox ‚Üí Wheels, paralleled by Battery ‚Üí Inverter ‚Üí Traction Motor ‚Üí Coupler.)*

> **Five Operating Modes (4 marks):**
>
> 1. **Engine-Only Mode:** ICE alone drives the wheels (highway cruising).
> 2. **Motor-Only (EV) Mode:** Clutch open; EM alone drives the vehicle (low-speed, low-load city driving).
> 3. **Combined Traction Mode:** Both ICE and EM drive the wheels via the coupler (peak acceleration / hill climbing).
> 4. **Power-Split Battery Charging Mode:** ICE provides tractive power *and* drives the EM as a generator to charge the battery.
> 5. **Regenerative Braking Mode:** ICE clutch disengaged; EM acts as a generator; braking energy stored in battery.

**(b)** **[7 marks]** Compare **Series, Parallel, and Series-Parallel hybrid drive-trains** in terms of (i) mechanical complexity, (ii) energy conversion losses, (iii) all-electric range, and (iv) best-suited vehicle application. State **two real-world examples** of vehicles using each topology.

**Model Solution:**

| Criterion | Series | Parallel | Series-Parallel |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Mechanical Complexity | Low (no mech link ICE-wheels) | High (clutch + coupler + gearbox) | Highest (planetary gear + 2 clutches) |
| (ii) Energy Conversion Losses | High (multiple conversions) | Low (direct ICE drive) | Medium (split + dual MG) |
| (iii) All-Electric Range | 1‚Äì5 km (mild) / 30‚Äì80 km (PHEV) | 0 km (HEV) | 0‚Äì5 km (HEV) |
| (iv) Best Application | City buses, delivery vans, range-extenders | Compact‚Äìmid sedans, urban hatchbacks | Mid-luxury SUVs, premium sedans |

**Real-world examples (2 marks):**
- **Series:** Chevrolet Volt (Gen 1), BMW i3 REx, Nissan e-Power.
- **Parallel:** Honda Civic Hybrid (IMA), Hyundai Ioniq Hybrid, Mercedes-Benz ML450.
- **Series-Parallel:** Toyota Prius, Lexus ES 300h, Ford Escape Hybrid.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Series-Parallel" with "Complex" Topology.** Series-Parallel uses *one* planetary gear set; Complex uses *two* planetary sets or *two clutches* enabling more modes. Many students treat them as identical ‚Äî KTU 2024 valuation keys deduct **2 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting to include the DC Bus symbol** in series and complex hybrid diagrams. The DC bus is a *mandatory* junction ‚Äî the examiner checks for it.
> 3. **Stating FCEV produces "zero emissions."** Technically correct as a *tailpipe* statement, but the examiner expects "**only water vapour at tailpipe; well-to-wheel emissions depend on H‚ÇÇ production method**." A loose answer loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Tractive Force Equation errors:** forgetting the $\cos\theta$ factor in rolling resistance on slopes ‚Äî a classic 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Degree of Hybridization misquoted:** students often write $DoH = P_{ICE}/(P_{ICE}+P_{EM})$ ‚Äî the **correct** KTU 2024 definition is $DoH = P_{EM}/(P_{EM}+P_{ICE})$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist ‚Äî Module 1**

- **Drive-train topology** = the *blueprint of energy flow* from source to wheels.
- **Five electrified topologies:** BEV, FCEV, Series HEV, Parallel HEV, Series-Parallel HEV.
- **BEV** = battery-only; **FCEV** = H‚ÇÇ fuel cell + buffer battery; **HEV** = ICE + EM.
- **Series:** ICE ‚Üí Generator ‚Üí Battery ‚Üí Motor ‚Üí Wheels. ICE **never** mechanically drives wheels.
- **Parallel:** ICE and EM *both* mechanically coupled to wheels; can operate alone or together.
- **Series-Parallel (THS):** uses **planetary gear set** ‚Äî sun gear to MG1, carrier to ICE, ring to MG2.
- **Degree of Hybridization:** $DoH = P_{EM}/(P_{EM}+P_{ICE})$; ranges 0 (ICE) to 1 (BEV).
- **Master Equation:** $F_{tr} = mgC_{rr}\cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^2 + mg\sin\theta + ma$.
- **Power balance:** $P_{tr} = F_{tr} \cdot v$ ; $P_m = P_{tr}/\eta_{dr}$.
- **Well-to-wheel efficiency ranking (highway):** Parallel > Series-Parallel > Series > BEV (depending on grid mix).
- **Best city topology:** Series (ICE in sweet spot, smooth EV drive).
- **Best highway topology:** Parallel (direct mechanical drive, low losses).
- **Best overall:** Series-Parallel (combines advantages; used in Toyota Prius, Camry Hybrid).
- **Regenerative braking** is the *single most important* efficiency-boosting feature of all electrified vehicles.
- **FCEV by-product:** water vapour only at tailpipe ‚Äî but **hydrogen production** may involve emissions.
- **DC Bus** is the electrical junction in all hybrids; missing it in a diagram = marks lost.
- **Planetary gear** in THS acts as an *electronic CVT* ‚Äî speed ratio is continuously variable.
- **Mild Hybrid:** DoH = 0.10‚Äì0.30; **Full Hybrid:** DoH = 0.30‚Äì0.60; **Plug-in Hybrid:** DoH = 0.60‚Äì1.00.
- **KTU Viva Trivia:** Toyota Prius is **NOT** the world's first hybrid ‚Äî that honour goes to the **Honda Insight (1999)**, but the Prius (1997 in Japan) is the first *mass-produced* hybrid.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line takeaway:**
> *"No single topology is 'best' ‚Äî each is a deliberate trade-off between efficiency, complexity, cost, and driving cycle."* ‚Äî Use this line as a concluding statement in any KTU descriptive answer for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Flow Control in Electric Drive-train Topologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Power Flow Control in Electric Drive-train Topologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Power Flow Control in Electric Drive-train Topologies** refers to the systematic management, regulation, and distribution of mechanical and electrical energy among multiple power sources (Internal Combustion Engine, battery, ultra-capacitor, fuel cell) and energy sinks (wheels, accessories, regenerative braking loads) within the propulsion architecture of a Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV) or Electric Vehicle (EV).

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (OEEET722 ‚Äì Module 1), power flow control is implemented through an **Energy Management Strategy (EMS)** that:

- Determines the **optimal operating point** of each energy source.
- Splits the driver's torque demand among the **Traction Motor (TM)**, **Engine (ICE)**, and **Generator (G)**.
- Maintains the **State of Charge (SOC)** of the energy storage system within a safe window (typically **0.3 to 0.7**).
- Coordinates **mode transitions** to ensure drivability, efficiency, and component longevity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The four canonical drive-train topologies ‚Äî **Series, Parallel, Series-Parallel (Power-Split), and Complex (Plug-in)** ‚Äî differ fundamentally in *how* the power flow is mechanically/electrically routed, and therefore demand distinct control philosophies.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a household water supply system:

- **The Engine (ICE)** is like a large, slow-to-start water pump (efficient only at high flow).
- **The Battery + Motor** is like an instant-on electric water heater (fast, efficient at low flow).
- **The Transmission** is the network of pipes connecting pumps to taps (wheels).
- **The EMS (Energy Management Strategy)** is the **smart valve controller** that decides ‚Äî in real time ‚Äî how much water to draw from the pump, how much to preheat instantly, and whether to store excess in a tank (regenerative braking).

A **Series topology** = one big pipe (electric path) carrying water from either source to the tap. A **Parallel topology** = two separate pipes that can both deliver water directly. A **Power-Split topology** = a clever mixing valve (planetary gear set) that blends mechanical and hydraulic (electrical) flow continuously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Question papers repeatedly test whether a student understands that the *physical coupling* of components dictates the *control variables available* to the EMS.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Battery SOC window | $SOC$ | **0.3 ‚Äì 0.7** |
| Vehicle mass | $m$ | **1200 ‚Äì 2500 kg** |
| Rolling resistance coeff. | $\mu_{rr}$ | **0.008 ‚Äì 0.015** |
| Aerodynamic drag coeff. | $C_d$ | **0.25 ‚Äì 0.35** |
| Gravitational constant | $g$ | **9.81 m/s¬≤** |
| Air density | $\rho_a$ | **1.225 kg/m¬≥** |
| ICE best efficiency | $\eta_{ICE,max}$ | **~38%** (Otto), **~43%** (Diesel) |
| Electric motor efficiency | $\eta_{EM}$ | **85% ‚Äì 95%** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Canonical Drive-train Topologies

### A. Series Hybrid (SHEV)
- **Architecture:** ICE ‚Üí Generator ‚Üí Battery/DC Bus ‚Üí Inverter ‚Üí Traction Motor ‚Üí Wheels.
- **No mechanical link** between ICE and wheels.
- ICE operates **only at its optimal efficiency island** (steady-state sweet spot).

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a **diesel-electric locomotive** ‚Äî the engine never turns the wheels directly.

**Control Variables:**
- ICE power $P_{ICE}$
- Generator torque $T_G$
- Battery power $P_{bat}$ (positive = discharge, negative = charge)

**Power Balance Equation:**

$$P_{ICE} \cdot \eta_G = P_{bat} + P_{TM}/\eta_{inv}$$

where $P_{TM}$ is the traction motor power demand, $\eta_G$ is the generator efficiency, and $\eta_{inv}$ is the inverter efficiency.

### B. Parallel Hybrid (PHEV)
- **Architecture:** ICE and TM are **mechanically coupled** (via clutch/gearbox) to the wheels.
- Both can propel the vehicle **simultaneously or individually**.
- More efficient at **highway speeds**.

**Control Variables:**
- ICE torque $T_{ICE}$
- TM torque $T_{TM}$
- Clutch engagement state $C_s \in \{0, 1\}$

**Torque Balance at the Coupling Point:**

$$T_{wheel} = (T_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{gear} + T_{TM} \cdot \eta_{TM}) \cdot r_{fd}$$

where $r_{fd}$ is the final drive ratio and $\eta_{gear}$ is the gearbox efficiency.

### C. Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid
- Combines **both paths**: a planetary gear set (Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive) blends series and parallel modes continuously.
- **Operating modes:** EV, ICE-only, Series, Parallel, Power-Split (blended).

**Control Variables:**
- ICE speed $\omega_{ICE}$ and torque $T_{ICE}$
- MG1 (generator) torque $T_{MG1}$ ‚Äî controls ICE speed
- MG2 (tractor) torque $T_{MG2}$ ‚Äî contributes to wheel torque
- Locking clutch state

**Planetary Gear Kinematics (Willis Equation):**

$$(\omega_s - \omega_r) \cdot R_s = (\omega_c - \omega_r) \cdot R_c$$

For a simple planetary: $\omega_s + k \cdot \omega_c = (1+k) \cdot \omega_r$ where $k = R_c/R_s$ is the gear ratio.

### D. Complex (Plug-in / Multi-mode) Hybrid
- Features **two clutches** (e.g., P2, P3, P4 configurations) enabling **multiple distinct operating modes** selectable by the EMS.
- Found in modern PHEVs like the **Hyundai TMED**, **VW Hybrid**, and **Mercedes-Benz modular hybrid**.

## 2.2 Vehicle Dynamics ‚Äî The Foundational Force Equation

The tractive force required at the wheels is governed by Newton's second law projected onto the road:

$$F_{tr} = m \cdot a + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2 + m g \sin\theta + \mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta$$

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $m \cdot a$ | Inertial / acceleration force |
| $\frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2$ | Aerodynamic drag |
| $m g \sin\theta$ | Hill-climbing force |
| $\mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta$ | Rolling resistance |

The required wheel torque is:

$$T_{wheel} = F_{tr} \cdot r_w$$

where $r_w$ is the effective wheel radius (typically **0.3 m**).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | Rotational power | **W** |
| 2 | $F_{tr} = m a + F_{aero} + F_{grade} + F_{rr}$ | Tractive force | **N** |
| 3 | $T_{wheel} = F_{tr} \cdot r_w$ | Wheel torque | **N¬∑m** |
| 4 | $P_{bat} = V_{oc} I_{bat} - I_{bat}^2 R_{int}$ | Battery power | **W** |
| 5 | $SOC(t) = SOC_0 - \frac{1}{Q_{bat}} \int_0^t I_{bat}(\tau) d\tau$ | Coulomb counting | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\eta_{sys} = P_{wheel} / P_{fuel}$ | Well-to-wheel efficiency | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\omega_s + k \cdot \omega_c = (1+k) \omega_r$ | Planetary gear constraint | **rad/s** |
| 8 | $P_{req} = v \cdot F_{tr}$ | Road-load power | **W** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations form the core of every EMS simulation in MATLAB/Simulink (ADVISOR, Autonomie, Cruise) ‚Äî which is exactly what KTU 2024 Scheme lab/practical questions test.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Power flow control is the **brain** of modern electrified vehicles. Without it, a hybrid would be less efficient than a pure ICE car. Applications include:

1. **Toyota Prius / Camry Hybrid** ‚Äì Power-Split EMS with planetary gear.
2. **Tesla Model 3** ‚Äì EV with dual-motor torque vectoring (a form of instantaneous power flow control).
3. **F1 KERS / MGU-K** ‚Äì Ultra-fast (millisecond) power flow control for hybrid recovery.
4. **Honda i-MMD** ‚Äì Series-parallel with intelligent mode switching.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Driver Pedal Input to Wheel Torque Split

**Problem Setup:** A parallel hybrid vehicle of mass $m = 1500$ kg is cruising at $v = 50$ km/h on a flat road. The driver demands a mild acceleration of $a = 0.5$ m/s¬≤. The ICE operates most efficiently at $T_{ICE,opt} = 120$ N¬∑m, $\omega_{ICE,opt} = 2500$ rpm. The traction motor can supply $T_{TM,max} = 250$ N¬∑m continuously. Determine the optimal torque split using the **thermostatic (on/off) control strategy**.

### Step 1 ‚Äî Compute the Total Tractive Force Required

First, convert velocity: $v = 50$ km/h $= 50/3.6 = 13.89$ m/s.

Using the force equation (level road, so $\theta = 0$):

$$F_{tr} = m a + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2 + \mu_{rr} m g$$

Assume $C_d A_f = 0.7$ m¬≤ (typical mid-size sedan) and $\mu_{rr} = 0.012$.

$$F_{tr} = 1500(0.5) + \frac{1}{2}(1.225)(0.7)(13.89)^2 + 0.012(1500)(9.81)$$

Computing each term individually:

$$F_{inertia} = 750.0 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.7 \times 192.9 = 82.7 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{rr} = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 = 176.6 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{tr} = 750.0 + 82.7 + 176.6 = 1009.3 \text{ N}$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Convert to Required Wheel Torque

For $r_w = 0.3$ m:

$$T_{wheel,req} = F_{tr} \cdot r_w = 1009.3 \times 0.3 = 302.8 \text{ N¬∑m}$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Account for Transmission and Final Drive

Let the final drive ratio $r_{fd} = 3.5$ and gearbox efficiency $\eta_{gb} = 0.95$. The torque *at the gearbox input shaft* (after the coupling) is:

$$T_{coupling} = \frac{T_{wheel,req}}{r_{fd} \cdot \eta_{gb}} = \frac{302.8}{3.5 \times 0.95} = 91.0 \text{ N¬∑m}$$

### Step 4 ‚Äî Apply Thermostatic Control Strategy

**Thermostatic Rule:** The ICE is **OFF** whenever $SOC > SOC_{high}$ (e.g., 0.7), and **ON** whenever $SOC < SOC_{low}$ (e.g., 0.3). Once ON, ICE delivers its *optimum* torque $T_{ICE,opt} = 120$ N¬∑m (and any excess charges the battery).

**Step 4a ‚Äî Hypothesize ICE-OFF mode:** The TM provides *all* 91.0 N¬∑m.

Since $T_{TM,max} = 250$ N¬∑m $\gg 91.0$ N¬∑m, the TM can handle it. If $SOC > 0.7$ ‚Üí **EV mode confirmed**.

**Step 4b ‚Äî Hypothesize ICE-ON mode (charge-sustaining):** The ICE delivers 120 N¬∑m. The TM must therefore *absorb* the surplus:

$$T_{TM} = T_{coupling} - T_{ICE,opt} = 91.0 - 120 = -29.0 \text{ N¬∑m}$$

The negative sign means the TM acts as a **generator**, charging the battery with 29.0 N¬∑m of braking torque.

### Step 5 ‚Äî Compute Power Flows and Efficiencies

ICE power delivered:

$$P_{ICE} = T_{ICE,opt} \cdot \omega_{ICE,opt} = 120 \times (2500 \times 2\pi/60) = 120 \times 261.8 = 31416 \text{ W} \approx 31.4 \text{ kW}$$

Power going to wheels (after losses):

$$P_{wheel} = T_{wheel,req} \cdot \omega_{wheel} = 302.8 \times \frac{13.89}{0.3} = 14018 \text{ W} \approx 14.0 \text{ kW}$$

Power diverted to battery (from TM in generator mode):

$$P_{bat} = 29.0 \times 261.8 = 7592 \text{ W} \approx 7.6 \text{ kW}$$

Energy balance check (accounting for $\eta$):

$$\text{Verifies: } P_{ICE} \cdot \eta_{gen} \approx P_{wheel}/\eta_{TM} + P_{bat\,losses}$$

### Step 6 ‚Äî Conclude the Torque Split

| Source | Torque | Power | Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| ICE | **+120.0 N¬∑m** | 31.4 kW | ON (charge-sustaining) |
| Traction Motor | **‚àí29.0 N¬∑m** | ‚àí7.6 kW | Generating |
| Net at coupling | **+91.0 N¬∑m** | 14.0 kW at wheel | Thermostatic ON |

## 3.2 Derivation: Power-Split Mode Using Planetary Gear

**Problem Setup:** A Toyota-style power-split HEV uses a planetary gear with sun = MG1 (generator), carrier = ICE, ring = MG2 + wheels. The gear ratio is $k = R_c/R_s = 2.6$. Given:
- $T_{wheel,req} = 200$ N¬∑m
- Vehicle speed $v = 60$ km/h
- $r_w = 0.31$ m, $r_{fd} = 3.7$
- ICE optimum: $T_{ICE,opt} = 130$ N¬∑m, $\omega_{ICE,opt} = 2200$ rpm

**Find:** $T_{MG1}$ and $T_{MG2}$.

### Step 1 ‚Äî Compute Wheel Speed

$$\omega_{wheel} = \frac{v}{r_w} = \frac{60/3.6}{0.31} = 53.76 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Compute Ring Speed (which equals the wheel-side speed after final drive)

$$\omega_{ring} = \omega_{wheel} \cdot r_{fd} = 53.76 \times 3.7 = 198.9 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Apply the Planetary Constraint

$$\omega_{sun} + k \cdot \omega_{carrier} = (1+k) \cdot \omega_{ring}$$

With $\omega_{carrier} = \omega_{ICE} = 2200 \times 2\pi/60 = 230.4$ rad/s:

$$\omega_{sun} = (1+2.6) \times 198.9 - 2.6 \times 230.4$$

$$\omega_{sun} = 716.0 - 599.0 = 117.0 \text{ rad/s}$$

This is the MG1 (generator) speed ‚Äî well within typical MG1 limits (~6000 rpm ‚âà 628 rad/s).

### Step 4 ‚Äî Torque Relations in a Planetary Gear

For an ideal (lossless) planetary gear, torques satisfy:

$$\frac{T_{sun}}{R_s} = \frac{T_{carrier}}{R_s + R_c} = \frac{T_{ring}}{R_c}$$

Therefore:

$$T_{carrier} = T_{ICE,opt} = 130 \text{ N¬∑m}$$

$$T_{sun} = T_{ICE,opt} \cdot \frac{R_s}{R_s + R_c} = T_{ICE,opt} \cdot \frac{1}{1+k} = \frac{130}{3.6} = 36.1 \text{ N¬∑m}$$

This is the **MG1 reaction torque** ‚Äî the generator must absorb/produce 36.1 N¬∑m to maintain ICE speed.

### Step 5 ‚Äî Compute MG2 Torque

Total wheel torque needed *at the ring*:

$$T_{ring,req} = T_{wheel,req} / (r_{fd} \cdot \eta_{fd}) = 200 / (3.7 \times 0.95) = 56.9 \text{ N¬∑m}$$

ICE contribution to ring:

$$T_{ring,from\,ICE} = T_{ICE,opt} \cdot \frac{R_c}{R_s + R_c} = 130 \cdot \frac{2.6}{3.6} = 93.9 \text{ N¬∑m}$$

Since the ICE alone provides **more** torque than required (93.9 > 56.9 N¬∑m), MG2 must **absorb** the surplus as regenerative charging:

$$T_{MG2} = T_{ring,req} - T_{ring,from\,ICE} = 56.9 - 93.9 = -37.0 \text{ N¬∑m}$$

### Step 6 ‚Äî Power Flow Summary

| Path | Torque | Speed | Power | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ICE (Carrier) | 130 N¬∑m | 230.4 rad/s | 29.95 kW | Input |
| MG1 (Sun) | 36.1 N¬∑m | 117.0 rad/s | 4.22 kW | To battery |
| MG2 (Ring) | ‚àí37.0 N¬∑m | 198.9 rad/s | ‚àí7.36 kW | From ring |
| Net to ring | 56.9 N¬∑m | 198.9 rad/s | 11.32 kW | To wheels |

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **classic power-split mode** ‚Äî part of the ICE power goes to the road *mechanically* (via ring), and part goes *electrically* (via MG1 ‚Üí battery ‚Üí MG2). The split ratio is continuously variable.
