# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]
**Define the term "Power Flow Control" as applied to hybrid electric vehicle drive-trains. List the four major drive-train topologies used in modern HEVs.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Power flow control in an electric/hybrid drive-train refers to the real-time management of energy distribution among multiple power sources (ICE, battery, fuel cell, ultra-capacitor) and power-consuming elements (traction motor, accessories, wheels) to satisfy the driver's torque demand **at minimum fuel consumption and emissions**, while maintaining the battery SOC within a safe operating window.

**Four Major Topologies (1.5 marks ‚Äî 0.375 each):**
1. **Series Hybrid (SHEV)**
2. **Parallel Hybrid (PHEV)**
3. **Series-Parallel / Power-Split Hybrid (PSHEV)**
4. **Complex / Plug-in Hybrid (with multiple clutches and modes)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]
**With a neat block diagram, explain the power flow path in a Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV). State ONE key advantage and ONE key disadvantage of this topology.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

**Block Diagram (1 mark):**
$$\text{ICE} \rightarrow \text{Generator} \rightarrow \text{DC Bus (Battery)} \rightarrow \text{Inverter} \rightarrow \text{Traction Motor} \rightarrow \text{Wheels}$$

**Explanation (1 mark):** In a series hybrid, the ICE has **no mechanical connection** to the wheels. The ICE drives a generator to produce electricity, which charges the battery or directly powers the traction motor through an inverter. All traction energy flows electrically.

**Advantage (0.5 mark):** The ICE can operate **always at its optimal efficiency point** (steady RPM/torque) regardless of vehicle speed ‚Äî leading to excellent city-cycle fuel economy.

**Disadvantage (0.5 mark):** **Three energy conversions** (mechanical ‚Üí electrical ‚Üí mechanical) cause cumulative losses ‚Äî making it less efficient at high speeds (highway driving).

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each ‚Äî Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) ‚Äî [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **architecture and power flow control** of a **Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV)**. Discuss the various operating modes with a clear block diagram. Compare it with the Series hybrid configuration on at least three parameters.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A parallel HEV has the following data:
- Vehicle mass $m = 1400$ kg, frontal area $A_f = 2.2$ m¬≤, drag coefficient $C_d = 0.32$, wheel radius $r_w = 0.3$ m, rolling resistance coefficient $\mu_{rr} = 0.011$, final drive ratio $r_{fd} = 3.6$, gearbox efficiency $\eta_{gb} = 0.94$.
- The vehicle is cruising at a constant speed of **90 km/h** on a road of **3% gradient** ($\sin\theta \approx 0.03$).
- The ICE delivers $T_{ICE} = 140$ N¬∑m at $\omega_{ICE} = 2800$ rpm.
- The traction motor can deliver up to $T_{TM,max} = 220$ N¬∑m.
- Take $\rho_a = 1.225$ kg/m¬≤, $g = 9.81$ m/s¬≤.

Determine:
1. The total tractive force required.
2. The wheel torque and the required torque at the coupling point.
3. Whether the ICE alone can meet the demand; if not, find the supplementary torque the TM must provide.
4. The total propulsive power delivered to the wheels.

**Model Solution for Q-A:**

**Part (a) ‚Äì Architecture and Operating Modes (7 marks):**

A **Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle** mechanically couples the **ICE** and the **Traction Motor (TM)** to the wheels through a **clutch** and a **gearbox**, allowing both or either to propel the vehicle. There is **no generator** in the basic configuration; the motor operates as a motor when delivering torque and as a generator during regenerative braking.

**Block diagram description:**

```
    [ICE] ---> [Clutch C1] ---> [Gearbox] ---> [Coupling] ---> [Final Drive] ---> [Wheels]
                                    ^                                                  ^
                                    |                                                  |
                                [TM] ---------------------------------------------------
                                    |                                                
                                    |   (mechanical coupling via chain/belt)          
                                    v                                                  
                              [Battery via Inverter]
```

**Operating Modes (6 modes):**

1. **Engine-Only Mode:** Clutch engaged, TM idle. Pure ICE propulsion.
2. **Motor-Only Mode (EV):** Clutch disengaged, only TM drives. Used at low speeds / when SOC is high.
3. **Combined (Traction) Mode:** Both ICE and TM drive the wheels together ‚Äî used for maximum acceleration.
4. **Power-Assist Mode:** ICE is the primary source; TM supplements torque for hill climbing or overtaking.
5. **Regenerative Braking Mode:** TM acts as a generator, charging the battery while the vehicle decelerates.
6. **Battery Charging Mode (Engine On, Vehicle Stationary or Coasting):** ICE drives the TM as a generator to charge the battery.

**Comparison Table with Series HEV (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Series HEV | Parallel HEV |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanical link ICE to wheels | **None** | **Yes** |
| Energy conversions (each trip) | **3** (Mech‚ÜíElec‚ÜíMech) | **1** (Mech‚ÜíMech) |
| Best suited for | **City / stop-and-go** | **Highway / constant speed** |
| Overall efficiency at high speed | **Lower** | **Higher** |
| Complexity | **Simpler structure** | **Complex clutch control** |

**Part (b) ‚Äì Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Convert velocity.**
$$v = 90 \text{ km/h} = 25.0 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute tractive force (constant speed, so $a = 0$):**
$$F_{tr} = 0 + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2 + m g \sin\theta + \mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta$$

$$F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.32 \times 2.2 \times (25.0)^2 = 269.5 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{grade} = 1400 \times 9.81 \times 0.03 = 412.0 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{rr} = 0.011 \times 1400 \times 9.81 \times 1.0 = 151.1 \text{ N} \quad (\cos\theta \approx 1)$$

$$F_{tr} = 0 + 269.5 + 412.0 + 151.1 = 832.6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Wheel torque:**
$$T_{wheel} = F_{tr} \cdot r_w = 832.6 \times 0.3 = 249.8 \text{ N¬∑m}$$

**Step 4: Torque at the coupling (after final drive and gearbox):**
$$T_{coupling} = \frac{T_{wheel}}{r_{fd} \cdot \eta_{gb}} = \frac{249.8}{3.6 \times 0.94} = 73.8 \text{ N¬∑m}$$

**Step 5: ICE power available at the coupling (scaled by ICE torque ratio, but here we have ICE torque directly available at the input shaft).**

The ICE provides $T_{ICE} = 140$ N¬∑m directly at the coupling. Since $140 \text{ N¬∑m} > 73.8 \text{ N¬∑m}$, the **ICE alone is sufficient**.

**Step 6: TM contribution (mode ‚Äî Engine-Only with surplus charge):**

If the EMS elects to use ICE alone: $T_{TM} = 0$.

If the EMS elects to charge the battery (charge-sustaining mode), the ICE can deliver its *full* 140 N¬∑m, and the surplus is used by the TM as a generator:

$$T_{TM,gen} = T_{ICE} - T_{coupling} = 140 - 73.8 = 66.2 \text{ N¬∑m (negative ‚Äî generating)}$$

**Step 7: Total propulsive power at wheels:**
$$P_{wheel} = F_{tr} \cdot v = 832.6 \times 25.0 = 20{,}815 \text{ W} \approx 20.8 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Answer Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Tractive force $F_{tr}$ | **832.6 N** |
| Wheel torque $T_{wheel}$ | **249.8 N¬∑m** |
| Coupling torque $T_{coupling}$ | **73.8 N¬∑m** |
| ICE sufficient? | **Yes** (140 N¬∑m available) |
| TM in gen mode (charge-sustaining) | **66.2 N¬∑m** (regen) |
| Power at wheels | **20.8 kW** |

---

### Question B (14 Marks ‚Äî Alternative Choice) ‚Äî [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a **planetary gear set diagram**, describe the architecture of a **Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid Electric Vehicle**. Explain **how the planetary gear enables continuous power flow splitting** between the mechanical and electrical paths. List the major operating modes.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A power-split hybrid uses a planetary gear with sun (MG1, generator), carrier (ICE), and ring (MG2 + wheels). Given $k = R_c/R_s = 2.5$, $\omega_{ICE,opt} = 2400$ rpm, $T_{ICE,opt} = 125$ N¬∑m, $r_w = 0.32$ m, $r_{fd} = 3.5$, vehicle speed $v = 80$ km/h. Compute:
1. The required wheel torque for a $2\%$ gradient at constant speed (assume $m = 1600$ kg, $C_d A_f = 0.75$ m¬≤, $\mu_{rr} = 0.010$).
2. The ring speed.
3. The MG1 and MG2 torques needed for the ICE to operate at its optimum.

**Model Solution for Q-B:**

**Part (a) ‚Äì Power-Split HEV Architecture (7 marks):**

The **Series-Parallel Power-Split Hybrid** uses a **planetary gear set (Epicyclic gear)** as the heart of the power split device. The Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive (THSD) is the classic example.

**Planetary Gear Configuration:**

- **Sun gear** = connected to **MG1 (Generator)**
- **Planet carrier** = connected to **ICE**
- **Ring gear** = connected to **MG2 (Traction motor) and the final drive to the wheels**

**Why Planetary? ‚Äì Continuous Variable Power Split (2 marks):**

A planetary gear set has a **kinematic constraint** (Willis equation):

$$\omega_s + k \cdot \omega_c = (1+k) \cdot \omega_r$$

This means that for **any** combination of two of the three speeds, the third is determined. As MG1 (sun) speed is varied by the generator/electricity flow, it **continuously changes the ratio** of ICE speed to ring (wheel) speed ‚Äî without needing a stepped gearbox. This is called **electronic continuously variable transmission (e-CVT)**.

**Operating Modes (5 modes):**

1. **EV Mode:** ICE OFF, only MG2 (ring) drives the wheels.
2. **Power-Split (Blended) Mode:** ICE ON, MG1 controls the split ‚Äî power flows *simultaneously* via both mechanical (carrier‚Üíring) and electrical (MG1‚ÜíBattery‚ÜíMG2) paths.
3. **Series Mode (Low Speed):** ICE drives MG1 as a generator; MG2 alone propels the vehicle (ring decoupled effect by holding sun).
4. **Parallel Mode (High Speed):** Clutch locks the planetary; ICE and MG2 both drive the wheels directly.
5. **Regenerative Braking Mode:** MG2 acts as a generator, charging the battery.

**Part (b) ‚Äì Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute tractive force (constant speed, $a = 0$):**
$$v = 80/3.6 = 22.22 \text{ m/s}$$

$$F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.75 \times (22.22)^2 = 226.7 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{grade} = 1600 \times 9.81 \times 0.02 = 313.9 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{rr} = 0.010 \times 1600 \times 9.81 = 156.96 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{tr} = 226.7 + 313.9 + 157.0 = 697.6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Required wheel torque:**
$$T_{wheel,req} = 697.6 \times 0.32 = 223.2 \text{ N¬∑m}$$

**Step 3: Torque at the ring (after final drive):**
$$T_{ring,req} = 223.2 / 3.5 = 63.8 \text{ N¬∑m (at ring, before losses)}$$

**Step 4: Ring speed:**
$$\omega_{ring} = \frac{v}{r_w} \cdot r_{fd} = \frac{22.22}{0.32} \times 3.5 = 243.0 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5: Sun speed (MG1) using planetary equation:**
$$\omega_s = (1+k)\omega_r - k \cdot \omega_c$$

$$\omega_{ICE} = 2400 \times 2\pi/60 = 251.3 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_s = 3.5 \times 243.0 - 2.5 \times 251.3 = 850.5 - 628.3 = 222.2 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 6: MG1 (Sun) torque from planetary equilibrium:**
$$T_{sun} = T_{carrier} \cdot \frac{1}{1+k} = 125 \times \frac{1}{3.5} = 35.7 \text{ N¬∑m}$$

**Step 7: ICE contribution to ring:**
$$T_{ring,ICE} = T_{carrier} \cdot \frac{k}{1+k} = 125 \times \frac{2.5}{3.5} = 89.3 \text{ N¬∑m}$$

**Step 8: MG2 torque (surplus absorption ‚Üí generator):**
$$T_{MG2} = T_{ring,req} - T_{ring,ICE} = 63.8 - 89.3 = -25.5 \text{ N¬∑m}$$

The negative sign confirms MG2 acts as a **generator**, charging the battery.

**Final Answer Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Tractive force $F_{tr}$ | **697.6 N** |
| Wheel torque $T_{wheel,req}$ | **223.2 N¬∑m** |
| Ring speed $\omega_{ring}$ | **243.0 rad/s** |
| Sun speed $\omega_{MG1}$ | **222.2 rad/s** |
| MG1 torque $T_{MG1}$ | **35.7 N¬∑m** |
| MG2 torque $T_{MG2}$ | **‚àí25.5 N¬∑m** (generating) |
| Operating mode | **Power-Split (charge-sustaining)** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign convention errors:** Students often forget that **negative TM torque = generation/regen mode**. Always state the mode explicitly.
> 2. **Unit conversion blunders:** RPM ‚Üí rad/s conversion ($1 \text{ rpm} = 2\pi/60$ rad/s) is a frequent point of loss. Show the conversion step.
> 3. **Planetary gear ratio confusion:** KTU papers often test whether the student knows that the **carrier holds the planets**, and torque splits as $1/(1+k)$ to sun and $k/(1+k)$ to ring.
> 4. **Forgetting $\eta_{gb}$ or $\eta_{fd}$:** In numericals, students often write $T_{coupling} = T_{wheel}/r_{fd}$ without efficiency ‚Äî losing 1‚Äì2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ‚Äî Power Flow Control in Electric Drive-train Topologies**

- **Power Flow Control** = Real-time energy management among multiple sources to satisfy driver demand with minimum fuel and emissions.
- **Four canonical topologies:** **Series, Parallel, Power-Split, Complex (Plug-in)**.
- **Series HEV:** ICE ‚Üí Generator ‚Üí Battery ‚Üí Inverter ‚Üí TM ‚Üí Wheels. No mechanical link. Best for **urban** duty cycles.
- **Parallel HEV:** ICE and TM **mechanically coupled** to wheels via clutch/gearbox. Best for **highway** duty cycles. Fewer energy conversions ‚Üí higher efficiency at high speed.
- **Power-Split (Series-Parallel) HEV:** Uses a **planetary gear set** for **continuous variable** power split ‚Äî combines benefits of both series and parallel. Classic example: **Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive**.
- **Planetary gear kinematic constraint (Willis equation):** $\omega_s + k \omega_c = (1+k) \omega_r$ ‚Äî central to all power-split mode analysis.
- **Torque split in planetary:** $T_{sun} = T_{carrier}/(1+k)$ and $T_{ring} = T_{carrier} \cdot k/(1+k)$.
- **Energy Management Strategies (EMS):**
  - **Thermostatic (On/Off) control** ‚Äî simplest; ICE on/off based on SOC thresholds.
  - **Power-following** ‚Äî ICE produces just enough to meet demand.
  - **Optimal (Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy, ECMS)** ‚Äî minimizes instantaneous equivalent fuel.
  - **Rule-based (fuzzy logic)** ‚Äî heuristic rules from expert knowledge.
  - **Global optimization (DP, Pontryagin's Minimum Principle)** ‚Äî offline benchmark.
- **SOC operating window:** Typically **0.3 ‚Äì 0.7** for charge-sustaining hybrids; wider (down to 0.1) for plug-in hybrids.
- **Key formula ‚Äî Tractive force:** $F_{tr} = m a + \frac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A_f v^2 + mg\sin\theta + \mu_{rr} mg\cos\theta$
- **Key formula ‚Äî Wheel torque:** $T_w = F_{tr} \cdot r_w$
- **Key formula ‚Äî Power:** $P = T \cdot \omega$
- **Regenerative braking** is the key feature of HEVs/EVs that *recovers* kinetic energy otherwise lost as heat.
- **Clutch control** is the central decision variable in **mode transition** for parallel and complex hybrids.
- **MG1 (Generator)** controls ICE speed in power-split; **MG2 (Tractor)** delivers wheel torque.
- **Excess ICE torque** in any hybrid mode is typically diverted to charge the battery.
- **KTU frequently tests:** numericals on tractive force, torque split, planetary gear analysis, and mode justification with SOC.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Block Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Hybrid and Electric Vehicles (HEV/EV)**, a **Block Diagram** is a standardized, high-level graphical representation that depicts the major functional sub-systems of a vehicle's powertrain, the direction of **power flow**, the direction of **signal flow**, and the control interconnections between sub-systems. Each rectangular "block" represents a single physical component (e.g., Internal Combustion Engine, Battery Pack, Inverter, Electric Motor, Transmission, Wheels) while the arrows between blocks indicate the type and direction of energy or information transfer.

According to KTU Module-2, the block diagram is the **fundamental analytical tool** used to classify HEV architectures into **Series, Parallel, and Series-Parallel (Power-Split)** topologies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Lock:** A block diagram is **NOT** a circuit schematic. It does *not* show wiring, component values, or electrical connections. It *only* shows the *interconnection of functional units* and the *paths* along which mechanical, electrical, or hydraulic power propagates from the source to the wheels.

## 1.2 Three Universal Building Blocks of Any HEV/EV Block Diagram

Every block diagram in this course is built from three canonical elements:

| Symbol | Meaning | Physical Counterpart in HEV |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Rectangular Block** | A functional sub-system | Engine, Motor, Battery, Inverter, Gearbox |
| **Solid Arrow (Thick)** | Mechanical Power Flow | Shaft, Clutch, Differential, Wheels |
| **Solid Arrow (Thin) / Dashed Arrow** | Electrical Power or Signal Flow | Cables, Sensor Lines, CAN Bus, Control Wires |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Convention Used in KTU Textbooks:**
> - **Thick lines (‚îÅ‚ñ∂)** = Mechanical Power Transmission
> - **Thin solid lines (‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñ∂)** = Electrical Power Flow
> - **Dashed lines (‚îÄ ‚îÄ‚ñ∂)** = Control / Signal Flow
> - **Double lines (‚ïê‚ñ∂)** = Hydraulic / Pneumatic Power (rare in HEVs)

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **water distribution network** of a city:

- The **water source** (reservoir / river) = the *Energy Source* (Fuel Tank or Battery).
- The **pump station** = the *Engine* or *Electric Motor* (converts raw energy to useful form).
- The **purification unit** = the *Power Converter* (Inverter / DC-DC Converter).
- The **pipe network** = the *Transmission* (Driveshaft, Gears, Clutch).
- The **tap at your home** = the *Wheels* (where the final useful work is delivered).
- The **SCADA control room** that monitors pressure and flow = the *ECU / VCU* (Vehicle Control Unit).

A **block diagram** is essentially the **engineering blueprint** of this water network, showing how the "energy" (water) moves from the source through pumps and pipes to reach the wheels (taps), and how a central controller monitors and regulates the entire flow. Once you "see" the analogy, every HEV block diagram becomes instantly readable.

## 1.4 Why Block Diagrams Are Studied FIRST in Module 2

KTU places this topic at the start of Module 2 for a specific pedagogical reason:

1. **Architectural classification** of HEVs (Series / Parallel / Series-Parallel) is *impossible* without first drawing a block diagram.
2. **Power-flow equations** of subsequent modules (Module 3 & 4 on Energy Management) are *derived* by summing flows at the nodes of these block diagrams.
3. **Component sizing** (motor kW, battery kWh) in Module 5 requires the block diagram as the starting topology.

> [!TIP]
> **Student Heuristic (Save this for the exam):** Whenever you are asked *"Explain the architecture of ___ HEV"*, **always start with the block diagram**. It earns you 4-5 easy marks before you even begin the descriptive part.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generalized HEV/EV Block Diagram ‚Äî Top-Level Structure

Every vehicle powertrain, regardless of hybridization level, can be decomposed into **six logical sub-system blocks** connected in a directed graph. These six blocks form the universal template from which all KTU architectures are derived.

### Block A ‚Äî Energy Source (Primary)
Stores or generates the primary form of energy.
- **Conventional ICE Vehicle:** Fuel Tank + ICE
- **Battery EV (BEV):** Lithium-Ion Battery Pack
- **Hybrid EV (HEV):** Fuel Tank + ICE **and** Battery Pack
- **Fuel Cell EV (FCEV):** Hydrogen Tank + Fuel Cell Stack

### Block B ‚Äî Energy Conditioning / Power Electronics
Converts the raw energy into the form required by the propulsion block.
- **ICE output:** Mechanical ‚Üí Gearbox ‚Üí Mechanical
- **Battery output:** DC ‚Üí **Inverter** ‚Üí 3-Phase AC (for AC motors)
- **Fuel Cell output:** DC ‚Üí DC-DC Converter ‚Üí DC Bus

### Block C ‚Äî Electric Motor / Generator (M/G)
Bidirectional electromechanical energy converter. Functions as a **motor** (battery ‚Üí wheels) or **generator** (wheels ‚Üí battery, i.e., regenerative braking).

### Block D ‚Äî Mechanical Coupling / Transmission
The **mechanical power routing** block. Contains clutches, planetary gear sets, fixed-ratio gearboxes, CVTs, and the final drive (differential).

### Block E ‚Äî Vehicle Dynamics Load (Wheels + Chassis)
The **sink** where useful work is delivered. Modeled as the tractive force $F_t$ opposing aerodynamic drag $F_a$, rolling resistance $F_r$, and gravitational force $F_g$.

### Block F ‚Äî Vehicle Control Unit (VCU / ECU)
The **signal-flow brain**. Receives driver inputs (accelerator, brake, gear) and sensor data (SOC, vehicle speed, motor RPM) and issues commands to all other blocks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of KTU Module-2 Block Diagrams:** The VCU is *always* drawn at the top or bottom of the diagram, separated from the heavy power-flow path by **dashed lines**. This visually communicates that the VCU does *not* carry power, only commands.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Power-Flow Path

Following the arrows of the block diagram, the operational logic unfolds in this exact sequence:

1. **Driver Intent Captured:** Accelerator pedal position $\alpha \in [0,1]$ is read by VCU.
2. **Source Selection:** VCU decides whether to draw power from the ICE path, Battery path, or both (this is the **Energy Management Strategy**).
3. **Power Conversion:** Electrical energy is converted by the Inverter from DC to variable-frequency 3-phase AC.
4. **Electromechanical Conversion:** The Electric Motor produces torque $T_m$ and rotational speed $\omega_m$.
5. **Mechanical Coupling:** The transmission combines $T_m$ with (or without) $T_{ice}$ from the engine to produce a single torque $T_{shaft}$ at the driveshaft.
6. **Load Delivery:** The driveshaft torque is multiplied through the differential and delivered to the wheels as tractive force $F_t$.
7. **Feedback:** Wheel speed sensors, motor RPM sensors, and battery SOC sensors report back to the VCU, closing the control loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind This Sequence:** Each step in the block diagram corresponds to a *physical energy transformation*. The KTU examiner expects you to identify the **input energy form** and **output energy form** of every block. Example: Battery (Chemical) ‚Üí Inverter (Electrical DC‚ÜíAC) ‚Üí Motor (Electrical‚ÜíMechanical) ‚Üí Wheels (Mechanical‚ÜíKinetic).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table compiles **every formula** you will need to answer numerical questions based on the block diagrams in this module. **Memorize this table.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Used In |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P_{wheel} = T_{wheel} \cdot \omega_{wheel}$ | Power delivered to the wheels | **W (Watts)** | All architectures |
| 2 | $P_{motor} = T_{motor} \cdot \omega_{motor}$ | Electric motor output power | **W** | BEV, HEV |
| 3 | $P_{ice} = T_{ice} \cdot \omega_{ice}$ | Internal combustion engine power | **W** | HEV |
| 4 | $P_{batt} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{batt}$ | Battery terminal power | **W** | All electrified |
| 5 | $\eta_{sys} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{P_{source}}$ | System efficiency | **dimensionless (0‚Äì1)** | Series HEV |
| 6 | $P_{shaft} = P_{ice} + P_{motor}$ | Power addition in Parallel HEV | **W** | Parallel HEV |
| 7 | $P_{wheel} = P_{ice} \cdot \eta_{trans} + P_{motor} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{mot}$ | Series HEV power equation | **W** | Series HEV |
| 8 | $P_{source} = P_{fuel} + P_{batt}$ | Total source power in HEV | **W** | Energy management |
| 9 | $F_t = F_a + F_r + F_g + F_i$ | Tractive force balance at wheels | **N** | Vehicle dynamics |
| 10 | $E_{batt} = V_{batt} \cdot Q_{batt} \cdot 3600$ | Battery stored energy (Wh) | **Wh** | Sizing |
| 11 | $T_{shaft} = (T_{ice} \cdot k_1) + (T_{motor} \cdot k_2)$ | Torque coupling in parallel | **Nm** | Parallel HEV |
| 12 | $SOC = \dfrac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | State of Charge | **%** | Battery block |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** In KTU numerical problems, you are typically given the **shaft output power** and the **efficiencies** of each block. The question will then ask you to trace the power *backwards* through the block diagram to find the **input fuel/electrical power**. Practice this *backward* tracing ‚Äî it is the most common 14-mark question pattern.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The block diagram is the **lingua franca** of the automotive industry. It is used by:

- **OEM Design Engineers** (Tata, Mahindra, Tesla, Toyota) to communicate the *architecture* of a new vehicle to suppliers.
- **Calibration Engineers** to identify the *control points* (VCU inputs/outputs) before writing the software.
- **Diagnostic Technicians** to *isolate faults* by tracing the signal/power path.
- **Researchers** publishing papers to *compare* different HEV topologies side-by-side using a unified visual language.

Without the block diagram, the entire downstream analysis of energy management, regenerative braking, and component sizing would be ungrounded and unreviewable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Series HEV Power Flow Through the Block Diagram

In a **Series HEV**, the ICE does *not* drive the wheels mechanically. It drives a generator, which charges the battery or supplies the motor. Therefore, tracing power from the fuel tank to the wheels involves **multiple energy conversions**.

**Given assumptions** (standard KTU assumption set for series HEV problems):
- Generator efficiency: $\eta_{gen} = 0.90$
- Battery charge/discharge round-trip efficiency: $\eta_{batt} = 0.95$
- Inverter efficiency: $\eta_{inv} = 0.95$
- Motor efficiency: $\eta_{mot} = 0.92$
- Transmission efficiency: $\eta_{trans} = 0.96$
- Required power at the wheels: $P_{wheel} = 50 \text{ kW}$

**Step 1 ‚Äî Wheels to Transmission Output:**

The transmission takes wheel power and delivers it forward in the diagram. Inverting the transmission:

$$P_{trans\_out} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{trans}}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Transmission Output to Motor Shaft:**

$$P_{mot\_shaft} = \frac{P_{trans\_out}}{\eta_{mot}} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{mot}}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Motor Shaft to DC Bus (Inverter Input):**

$$P_{dc\_bus} = \frac{P_{mot\_shaft}}{\eta_{inv}} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{mot} \cdot \eta_{inv}}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî DC Bus to Generator Output (or Battery):**

The battery may supply this directly OR the ICE-generator may supply it. For ICE-driven mode:

$$P_{gen\_out} = \frac{P_{dc\_bus}}{\eta_{batt}} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{mot} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{batt}}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Generator Output to ICE Crankshaft:**

$$P_{ice} = \frac{P_{gen\_out}}{\eta_{gen}} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{mot} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{batt} \cdot \eta_{gen}}$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Numerical Substitution:**

$$P_{ice} = \frac{50{,}000}{0.96 \times 0.92 \times 0.95 \times 0.95 \times 0.90}$$

Computing the denominator step-by-step:

$$\eta_{denom} = 0.96 \times 0.92 = 0.8832$$

$$\eta_{denom} = 0.8832 \times 0.95 = 0.83904$$

$$\eta_{denom} = 0.83904 \times 0.95 = 0.79709$$

$$\eta_{denom} = 0.79709 \times 0.90 = 0.71738$$

Therefore:

$$P_{ice} = \frac{50{,}000}{0.71738} \approx 69{,}703 \text{ W} \approx 69.7 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7 ‚Äî Overall System Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{sys}^{series} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{P_{ice}} = \frac{50}{69.7} \approx 0.717 = 71.7\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind the Multiple Conversions:** The Series HEV pays an efficiency *penalty* of approximately **28.3%** purely because the energy must change forms 5 times (Mechanical ‚Üí Electrical ‚Üí Chemical ‚Üí Electrical ‚Üí Mechanical ‚Üí Mechanical ‚Üí Mechanical). This is the **fundamental engineering trade-off** that makes the Series HEV heavy (large battery) and less efficient than a Parallel HEV at highway cruising.

## 3.2 Derivation: Parallel HEV Power Flow Through the Block Diagram

In a **Parallel HEV**, both the ICE and the Electric Motor can deliver mechanical power *directly* to the wheels through a mechanical coupler. There is **no intermediate electrical conversion** for the mechanical path.

**Given:**
- ICE power: $P_{ice} = 40 \text{ kW}$
- Motor power: $P_{motor} = 25 \text{ kW}$
- Transmission efficiency: $\eta_{trans} = 0.96$
- Inverter efficiency: $\eta_{inv} = 0.95$
- Motor efficiency: $\eta_{mot} = 0.92$

**Step 1 ‚Äî Motor Path Power at Coupler:**

The motor output goes through the inverter and motor losses, then arrives at the mechanical coupler:

$$P_{mot\_at\_coupler} = P_{motor} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{mot}$$

$$P_{mot\_at\_coupler} = 25 \times 0.95 \times 0.92 = 21.85 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî ICE Path Power at Coupler:**

The ICE output arrives at the mechanical coupler essentially directly (no electrical conversion):

$$P_{ice\_at\_coupler} = P_{ice} = 40 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Total Shaft Power at Coupler (Additive):**

$$P_{shaft} = P_{ice\_at\_coupler} + P_{mot\_at\_coupler}$$

$$P_{shaft} = 40 + 21.85 = 61.85 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Power at the Wheels (After Transmission):**

$$P_{wheel} = P_{shaft} \cdot \eta_{trans}$$

$$P_{wheel} = 61.85 \times 0.96 = 59.376 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Overall System Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{sys}^{parallel} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{P_{ice} + P_{batt\_input}}$$

Assuming the battery electrical input equals $P_{motor} / (\eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{mot})$:

$$P_{batt\_input} = \frac{25}{0.95 \times 0.92} = 28.60 \text{ kW}$$

$$\eta_{sys}^{parallel} = \frac{59.376}{40 + 28.60} = \frac{59.376}{68.60} \approx 86.6\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind the Higher Efficiency:** The Parallel HEV delivers mechanical power from the ICE *directly* to the wheels with only one efficiency stage ($\eta_{trans} = 0.96$). The result is an efficiency of **86.6%** versus the Series HEV's **71.7%** ‚Äî a **14.9 percentage point** advantage. This is why most production HEVs (Toyota Prius, Honda Insight) use the **Power-Split (Series-Parallel)** architecture, combining the best of both.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Series vs Parallel vs Series-Parallel)

This is the **single most important table** for the KTU Module-2 exam. Memorize it.

| Parameter | Series HEV | Parallel HEV | Series-Parallel (PSHEV) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **No. of Electric Machines** | 2 (Motor + Generator) | 1 (Motor only) | 2 (Motor + Generator) |
| **ICE Mechanical Link to Wheels?** | ‚ùå No (only electrical) | ‚úÖ Yes (direct) | ‚úÖ Yes (via planetary gear) |
| **Typical System Efficiency** | 70 ‚Äì 75% | 85 ‚Äì 90% | 80 ‚Äì 85% |
| **City Drive Cycle Suitability** | ‚úÖ Excellent | ‚öÝ Moderate | ‚úÖ Excellent |
| **Highway Drive Cycle Suitability** | ‚öÝ Moderate | ‚úÖ Excellent | ‚úÖ Excellent |
| **Battery Size Required** | Large (15 ‚Äì 25 kWh) | Small (1 ‚Äì 5 kWh) | Medium (5 ‚Äì 10 kWh) |
| **Mechanical Complexity** | Low | Medium | High (planetary gearset) |
| **Example Production Vehicle** | Chevrolet Volt (1st Gen) | Honda Insight (1st Gen) | Toyota Prius (All Generations) |
| **Control Complexity** | Medium | Medium | High (requires split device) |
| **Cost** | High (large battery) | Low ‚Äì Medium | Medium ‚Äì High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Pure Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)

This is the **simplest** block diagram in KTU Module-2, with only one energy source (battery) and one propulsion path.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[BATTERY PACK\nDC SOURCE] --> A2[INVERTER\nDC TO AC]
    A2 --> A3[ELECTRIC MOTOR\nAC PROPULSION]
    A3 --> A4[TRANSMISSION\nSINGLE SPEED]
    A4 --> A5[DIFFERENTIAL\nFINAL DRIVE]
    A5 --> A6[WHEELS\nKINETIC OUTPUT]
    A7[VCU VEHICLE CONTROL UNIT] -. SIGNAL .-> A1
    A7 -. SIGNAL .-> A2
    A7 -. SIGNAL .-> A3
    A8[BRAKE PEDAL] -. SIGNAL .-> A7
    A8B[ACCELERATOR] -. SIGNAL .-> A7
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this diagram as follows:** The thick arrows show power flow from the Battery to the Wheels. The dashed arrows show signal flow from the driver and VCU. Note the **absence** of an ICE block ‚Äî this is the defining feature of a pure BEV.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Series HEV Architecture

In a **Series HEV**, the ICE is *mechanically decoupled* from the wheels. It acts as a **range extender** (also called an Auxiliary Power Unit, APU).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[FUEL TANK] --> S2[ICE\nINTERNAL COMBUSTION ENGINE]
    S2 --> S3[GENERATOR\nM G UNIT]
    S3 --> S4[RECTIFIER\nAC TO DC]
    S4 --> S5[DC BUS]
    S6[BATTERY PACK] --> S5
    S5 --> S7[INVERTER\nDC TO AC]
    S7 --> S8[ELECTRIC MOTOR\nTRACTION MOTOR]
    S8 --> S9[TRANSMISSION\nFIXED GEAR]
    S9 --> S10[DIFFERENTIAL]
    S10 --> S11[WHEELS]
    S12[VCU] -. SIGNAL .-> S2
    S12 -. SIGNAL .-> S6
    S12 -. SIGNAL .-> S7
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Visual Signature of Series HEV:** The ICE block has **no mechanical arrow** leaving it toward the wheels. Every arrow from the ICE path eventually *passes through the electrical bus* before reaching the motor. This is the **fingerprint** examiners will look for in your answer.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Parallel HEV Architecture

In a **Parallel HEV**, the ICE and the Motor are *mechanically coupled* (typically via a clutch or torque coupler) and can both drive the wheels in parallel.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[FUEL TANK] --> P2[ICE]
    P6[BATTERY] --> P5[INVERTER]
    P5 --> P3[ELECTRIC MOTOR]
    P2 --> P4[MECHANICAL COUPLER\nTORQUE ADDITION]
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P7[TRANSMISSION]
    P7 --> P8[DIFFERENTIAL]
    P8 --> P9[WHEELS]
    P10[VCU] -. SIGNAL .-> P2
    P10 -. SIGNAL .-> P3
    P10 -. SIGNAL .-> P5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Visual Signature of Parallel HEV:** The ICE and the Motor both deliver **mechanical arrows** into the **Mechanical Coupler** block. This is the *single most important visual distinction* between Series and Parallel architectures in your exam answer.

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Series-Parallel (Power-Split) HEV

This is the **most complex** architecture in KTU Module-2. It uses a **planetary gear set** as the power-split device, combining the benefits of both Series and Parallel.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[FUEL TANK] --> X2[ICE]
    X2 --> X3[PLANETARY GEAR SET\nPOWER SPLIT DEVICE]
    X4[BATTERY] --> X5[INVERTER]
    X5 --> X6[MOTOR M1]
    X3 --> X7[GENERATOR M2]
    X7 --> X5
    X3 --> X8[TRACTION MOTOR M1 PATH]
    X6 --> X8
    X8 --> X9[TRANSMISSION]
    X9 --> X10[DIFFERENTIAL]
    X10 --> X11[WHEELS]
    X12[VCU] -. SIGNAL .-> X2
    X12 -. SIGNAL .-> X6
    X12 -. SIGNAL .-> X7
    X12 -. SIGNAL .-> X5
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Power-Split Diagram:** The planetary gear set has three ports (Sun, Planet Carrier, Ring). The ICE connects to the planet carrier, the Generator connects to the sun gear, and the output to the wheels comes from the ring gear. The Motor M1 is *post-split*, in the mechanical path. This is the Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive architecture ‚Äî memorize this signature.

## 4.5 Summary Decision Matrix: When to Draw Which Block Diagram

| If the question asks for... | Draw this block diagram |
| :--- | :--- |
| A vehicle with only battery + motor | **BEV block diagram** (Section 4.1) |
| A vehicle where ICE never touches wheels mechanically | **Series HEV** (Section 4.2) |
| A vehicle with two mechanical paths to the wheels | **Parallel HEV** (Section 4.3) |
| A vehicle with a planetary gear and two motors | **Series-Parallel HEV** (Section 4.4) |
| An FCEV (Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle) | Replace Battery with Fuel Cell + DC-DC Converter |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a block diagram in the context of Hybrid and Electric Vehicles. Mention the three types of lines used and their meaning. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A block diagram in HEV/EV is a graphical representation that uses rectangular blocks to depict the major functional sub-systems of a vehicle powertrain (such as the engine, motor, battery, inverter, transmission, and wheels), connected by arrows that indicate the direction of power or signal flow.

The three types of lines used are:
1. **Thick solid lines (‚îÅ‚ñ∂):** Represent mechanical power transmission (e.g., from the engine crankshaft to the gearbox).
2. **Thin solid lines (‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñ∂):** Represent electrical power flow (e.g., from the battery to the inverter).
3. **Dashed lines (‚îÄ ‚îÄ‚ñ∂):** Represent control signal flow (e.g., from the VCU to the motor controller).

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three line types with correct meaning.)*

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between a Series HEV and a Parallel HEV based on their block diagrams. Mention any two differences. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis | Series HEV | Parallel HEV |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Mechanical Link between ICE and Wheels** | Absent ‚Äî ICE drives only a generator | Present ‚Äî ICE drives wheels via a clutch/coupler |
| **Number of Electric Machines** | Two (Motor + Generator) | One (Motor only) |
| **Power-Flow Path** | Single (electrical) path to wheels | Two parallel mechanical paths to wheels |

*(1 mark per valid difference. Any two correct differences yield full 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B ‚Äî Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** The student must answer **ONE** full question from the choice provided. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), typically carrying 7 marks each.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Draw the block diagram of a Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle. Explain the function of each block. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*(Expected diagram ‚Äî redraw the Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram in hand-drawn form for exam. Allocate 3 marks for the diagram and 4 marks for the explanation.)*

**Block-by-block explanation (4 marks):**

1. **Fuel Tank:** Stores the gasoline/diesel which is the primary chemical energy source for the ICE.
2. **ICE (Internal Combustion Engine):** Converts chemical energy from fuel into mechanical energy (rotating crankshaft). The ICE in a Series HEV operates at its **optimal efficiency point** (constant speed) since it is decoupled from the wheels.
3. **Generator (M/G unit):** A three-phase AC machine coupled to the ICE crankshaft. It converts the mechanical energy from the ICE into electrical energy.
4. **Rectifier / Power Electronics:** Converts the AC output of the generator into DC to feed the DC bus.
5. **Battery Pack:** Stores electrical energy in chemical form. It buffers the energy between the generator and the traction motor, and also captures regenerative braking energy.
6. **Inverter:** Converts DC from the battery (or DC bus) into variable-frequency three-phase AC required by the traction motor.
7. **Traction Motor:** A three-phase AC induction motor or PMSM that converts electrical energy into mechanical rotational energy (torque √ó speed).
8. **Transmission:** A simple single-speed fixed-ratio gearbox (since the motor has a wide constant-power range).
9. **Differential / Final Drive:** Splits torque between the two drive wheels and provides the final speed reduction.
10. **Wheels:** Convert the rotational mechanical power into linear kinetic energy of the vehicle.
11. **VCU:** Receives driver inputs and sensor data, then issues commands to the ICE throttle, generator field, and inverter switching.

---

**(b) A Series HEV is required to deliver 40 kW at the wheels. The following component efficiencies are given: $\eta_{trans} = 0.95$, $\eta_{motor} = 0.90$, $\eta_{inverter} = 0.94$, $\eta_{battery} = 0.92$, $\eta_{generator} = 0.88$. Calculate the power required from the ICE and the overall system efficiency. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given:
- $P_{wheel} = 40 \text{ kW}$
- $\eta_{trans} = 0.95$
- $\eta_{motor} = 0.90$
- $\eta_{inverter} = 0.94$
- $\eta_{battery} = 0.92$
- $\eta_{gen} = 0.88$

**Step 1 ‚Äî Power at the motor shaft (input to transmission):**

$$P_{motor} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{trans}} = \frac{40}{0.95} = 42.105 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî DC Bus power (input to inverter):**

$$P_{dc\_bus} = \frac{P_{motor}}{\eta_{motor}} = \frac{42.105}{0.90} = 46.784 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Battery output (input to inverter from battery side):**

$$P_{batt\_out} = \frac{P_{dc\_bus}}{\eta_{inverter}} = \frac{46.784}{0.94} = 49.770 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Generator output (input to battery, after charging):**

$$P_{gen\_out} = \frac{P_{batt\_out}}{\eta_{battery}} = \frac{49.770}{0.92} = 54.098 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî ICE output (input to generator):**

$$P_{ice} = \frac{P_{gen\_out}}{\eta_{gen}} = \frac{54.098}{0.88} = 61.475 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Overall System Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{sys} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{P_{ice}} = \frac{40}{61.475} = 0.6507 = 65.07\%$$

**Final Answer (boxed for exam):** $P_{ice} \approx 61.48 \text{ kW}$, $\eta_{sys} \approx 65.1\%$

*(Award 1 mark per step correctly computed. 1 mark for the final boxed answer.)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) ‚Äî Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Draw the block diagram of a Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle. List its advantages over a Series HEV. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*(Expected diagram ‚Äî redraw the Section 4.3 Mermaid diagram. 3 marks for diagram, 4 marks for the advantages.)*

**Advantages of Parallel HEV over Series HEV (any 4 valid points, 1 mark each):**

1. **Higher overall efficiency** at steady highway speeds because the ICE drives the wheels mechanically, avoiding multiple energy conversion losses (typically 85-90% versus 70-75% for Series).
2. **Smaller battery pack** required (1‚Äì5 kWh) because the battery is used only for assist and regenerative braking, not for sole propulsion over long distances.
3. **No dedicated generator** required ‚Äî the same electric machine acts as both motor and generator, reducing cost and weight.
4. **Simpler power electronics** because no rectifier is required (the battery feeds DC directly to the inverter, and the motor-generator feeds DC directly back during regen).
5. **Lighter drivetrain** since the heavy long high-voltage cable from a trailer-mounted generator is not needed.

---

**(b) In a Parallel HEV, the ICE delivers 35 kW and the electric motor delivers 20 kW. The motor efficiency is 0.91, the inverter efficiency is 0.93, and the transmission efficiency is 0.95. Find the total power delivered to the wheels and the overall system efficiency. The battery electrical input power is 23.6 kW. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given:
- $P_{ice} = 35 \text{ kW}$
- $P_{motor} = 20 \text{ kW}$
- $\eta_{mot} = 0.91$
- $\eta_{inv} = 0.93$
- $\eta_{trans} = 0.95$
- $P_{batt\_in} = 23.6 \text{ kW}$

**Step 1 ‚Äî Motor power arriving at the mechanical coupler:**

$$P_{mot\_coupler} = P_{motor} \cdot \eta_{mot} \cdot \eta_{inv}$$

$$P_{mot\_coupler} = 20 \times 0.91 \times 0.93$$

Computing step-by-step:
$$20 \times 0.91 = 18.20 \text{ kW}$$
$$18.20 \times 0.93 = 16.926 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî ICE power at the mechanical coupler (mechanical path, no extra losses before coupler):**

$$P_{ice\_coupler} = 35 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Total shaft power at the coupler:**

$$P_{shaft} = P_{ice\_coupler} + P_{mot\_coupler}$$

$$P_{shaft} = 35 + 16.926 = 51.926 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Power at the wheels (after transmission losses):**

$$P_{wheel} = P_{shaft} \cdot \eta_{trans}$$

$$P_{wheel} = 51.926 \times 0.95 = 49.330 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Total Source Power Input:**

$$P_{source} = P_{ice} + P_{batt\_in} = 35 + 23.6 = 58.6 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Overall System Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{sys} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{P_{source}} = \frac{49.330}{58.6} = 0.8418 = 84.18\%$$

**Final Answer (boxed for exam):** $P_{wheel} \approx 49.33 \text{ kW}$, $\eta_{sys} \approx 84.2\%$

*(Award 1 mark per step. Final boxed answer worth 1 mark.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 4-5 Marks Per Question:**
>
> 1. **Confusing signal flow with power flow:** Students often draw dashed lines (signal) for power flow. The **VCU arrows MUST be dashed**. Power arrows MUST be solid.
> 2. **Forgetting the VCU block:** Many students draw only the power-train blocks and forget the control unit. **Always include the VCU** with dashed signal arrows.
> 3. **Wrong efficiency direction in numericals:** When tracing power *backwards* (from wheels to source), you **divide** by efficiency. When tracing power *forwards* (from source to wheels), you **multiply** by efficiency. Inverting this loses 3 marks instantly.
> 4. **Series HEV: Adding ICE power directly to wheel power:** A common error is writing $P_{wheel} = P_{ice} + P_{motor}$ for a Series HEV. **This is wrong for Series HEV** (correct only for Parallel HEV). In Series HEV, the ICE contributes only through the generator-battery-motor chain.
> 5. **Skipping the block diagram and jumping to explanation:** KTU examiners allocate **3-4 marks specifically for the block diagram**. A textual description without a diagram caps your score at ~60% of the question's marks.
> 6. **Mixing up unit prefixes:** Converting kW ‚Üî W incorrectly. **Standard rule:** Use kW throughout the calculation; only convert to W at the final answer if required by the question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this 30 minutes before the exam):**

- ‚òê A **block diagram** uses rectangular blocks for sub-systems and arrows for power/signal flow.
- ‚òê **Thick solid arrow** = Mechanical Power; **Thin solid arrow** = Electrical Power; **Dashed arrow** = Signal/Control Flow.
- ‚òê Every HEV block diagram has **six canonical blocks:** Energy Source, Power Electronics, Electric Machine, Transmission, Vehicle Dynamics (Wheels), and VCU.
- ‚òê **Series HEV** = ICE drives **only** a generator. ICE has *no* mechanical connection to the wheels. Typical efficiency **70-75%**.
- ‚òê **Parallel HEV** = ICE and Motor are **mechanically coupled** to the wheels. Two mechanical paths. Typical efficiency **85-90%**.
- ‚òê **Series-Parallel (Power-Split)** = Uses planetary gear set + two motors (Toyota Prius). Combines advantages of both.
- ‚òê **Series HEV power equation:** $P_{wheel} = P_{ice} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{batt} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{mot} \cdot \eta_{trans}$
- ‚òê **Parallel HEV power equation:** $P_{wheel} = (P_{ice} + P_{mot} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{mot}) \cdot \eta_{trans}$
- ‚òê **Trace power BACKWARDS** through the diagram when given $P_{wheel}$ ‚Äî divide by efficiencies.
- ‚òê **Trace power FORWARDS** when given $P_{source}$ ‚Äî multiply by efficiencies.
- ‚òê **System efficiency** $\eta_{sys} = P_{wheel} / P_{source}$, always less than 1.
- ‚òê The **VCU** is always connected by **dashed lines** (signal flow) ‚Äî never by solid lines.
- ‚òê Production example of **Series HEV:** Chevrolet Volt (Gen 1). **Parallel HEV:** Honda Civic Hybrid. **Series-Parallel:** Toyota Prius.
- ‚òê Memorize the **Analytical Comparison Matrix** (Section 3.3) ‚Äî it directly answers 5-7 mark comparison questions.
- ‚òê Always **start any architecture question by drawing the block diagram first** ‚Äî it earns 3-4 easy marks before the description begins.
- ‚òê Watch out for the **direction of efficiency multiplication** ‚Äî it is the #1 source of numerical errors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Electric Motors Used in Hybrid and Electric Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Electric Motors Used in Hybrid and Electric Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Hybrid and Electric Vehicles (HEVs/EVs)**, an **electric traction motor** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that transforms **electrical energy** from the onboard energy storage system (battery pack, supercapacitor, or fuel cell) into **mechanical energy** at the drivetrain, producing the tractive torque required to propel the vehicle against aerodynamic drag, rolling resistance, and gravitational loads.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme nomenclature (OEEET722 ‚Äì Module 2), electric motors used in automotive traction applications belong to a specialized sub-class of machines that must simultaneously satisfy **high power density (kW/kg)**, **high torque density (Nm/kg)**, a **wide constant-power operating region** (often 3:1 to 5:1), and an **overload capability of 2√ó rated torque** for short durations (typical launch and hill-climb conditions).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The course explicitly classifies EV traction motors into four major families used in modern production vehicles: **(i) DC Motors, (ii) Induction Motors (IM), (iii) Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors (PMSM) / Brushless DC Motors (BLDC), and (iv) Switched Reluctance Motors (SRM)**. Each family exhibits distinct trade-offs between cost, efficiency, robustness, and manufacturing complexity.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Heart" of the Vehicle

Imagine the electric motor as the **heart** of the electric vehicle. Just as the human heart pumps blood (energy) at variable rates depending on whether you are sleeping, walking, or sprinting, the traction motor pumps rotational energy to the wheels at vastly different rates depending on whether the vehicle is **creeping in traffic (low speed, high torque)** or **cruising on a highway (high speed, low torque)**.

| Vehicle Operating State | Human Body Equivalent | Motor Demand |
|------------------------|----------------------|--------------|
| Stop / Hold at signal | Heart at rest | Zero torque, near-zero losses |
| Initial launch from rest | Heart racing | Peak torque, low base speed |
| Cruising at constant speed | Heart in jog mode | Moderate power, constant torque region transitioning to constant power |
| Aggressive acceleration / overtaking | Heart in sprint mode | High power, field-weakening region |
| Regenerative braking | Heart during exhale | Motor acts as a generator, recovering kinetic energy |

> [!NOTE]
> The fundamental difference between a **traction motor** and a conventional industrial motor is the **dynamic operating envelope**. Industrial motors typically operate at one or two fixed set points; EV traction motors must sweep a **two-dimensional torque-speed map** continuously, with **regenerative capability** in the generator mode.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard automotive-grade metrics are used throughout the KTU Module 2 syllabus:

- **Specific Power (Power Density):** 1 to 5 kW/kg
- **Specific Energy:** 200 to 400 Wh/kg (battery)
- **Peak Efficiency:** 90% to 97%
- **Overload Factor:** 2√ó rated torque for ‚â§ 60 seconds
- **Base Speed to Maximum Speed Ratio:** 1:3 to 1:5
- **Operating Voltage:** 200 V to 800 V (modern EVs)
- **Coolant Inlet Temperature:** 65 ¬∞C (typical water-glycol loop)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Electric Motors? ‚Äî The Engineering Rationale

Internal combustion engines (ICEs) suffer from **low thermal efficiency (20%‚Äì35%)**, complex multi-speed transmissions, idle losses, and high emissions. Electric motors overcome these limitations through the following advantages:

1. **High efficiency across a wide operating range** (typically 85%‚Äì95%, with peaks above 96% for PMSMs).
2. **Instantaneous peak torque availability** from zero base speed, eliminating the need for a torque converter.
3. **Single-speed or two-speed transmission** (and in some concepts, in-wheel direct drive), drastically simplifying the drivetrain.
4. **Bidirectional energy flow** ‚Äî the same machine functions as a **motor** (traction) and a **generator** (regenerative braking).
5. **Silent operation** and **zero tailpipe emissions** at the point of use.
6. **Modular and scalable** architecture ‚Äî adding motors increases power linearly.

## 2.2 Functional Classification of EV Traction Motors

KTU Module 2 systematically groups EV motors into the following four families, each with a distinct operating principle.

### 2.2.1 DC Motors (Separately Excited / Series / Shunt)
- **Principle:** Interaction between the field flux (from stator field winding) and armature current (in rotor windings) produces torque.
- **Comutation:** Mechanical (brushes and commutator).
- **Advantage:** Simple torque-speed control via armature voltage and field current.
- **Disadvantage:** Brush wear, lower efficiency, large size, maintenance intensive.
- **Usage:** Early EVs (e.g., General Motors EV1 used a 3-phase AC induction; small EVs and golf carts still use DC motors).

### 2.2.2 Induction Motors (IM) / Asynchronous Motors
- **Principle:** Stator rotating magnetic field induces rotor currents (Faraday's Law); the interaction produces torque (Lorentz force).
- **Comutation:** Electronic via Variable Frequency Drive (VFD).
- **Advantage:** Rugged, cheap, reliable, no permanent magnets (avoids rare-earth supply chain risks).
- **Disadvantage:** Lower power density than PMSM, rotor copper losses (squirrel cage I¬≤R).
- **Usage:** Tesla Model S, Model X, Model 3 (early versions) ‚Äî large 3-phase squirrel-cage IM.

### 2.2.3 Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors (PMSM) and Brushless DC (BLDC) Motors
- **Principle:** Rotor carries high-energy permanent magnets (NdFeB or SmCo); stator carries multi-phase distributed windings. The synchronous rotating field locks with rotor magnets.
- **Comutation:** Electronic (hence "brushless").
- **Sub-types:**
  - **BLDC:** Trapezoidal back-EMF, square-wave current excitation.
  - **PMSM:** Sinusoidal back-EMF, sinusoidal current excitation (FOC / vector control).
- **Advantage:** Highest power density, highest efficiency, excellent dynamic response.
- **Disadvantage:** Rare-earth magnet cost and supply risk, demagnetization at high temperature, back-EMF limiting at high speed.
- **Usage:** Nissan Leaf, Toyota Prius (PMSM), BMW i3, Hyundai Ioniq 5, Tesla Model 3 (Permanent Magnet Switched Reluctance).

### 2.2.4 Switched Reluctance Motors (SRM)
- **Principle:** Rotor is a salient-pole laminated steel piece with **no windings and no magnets**. Torque is produced by the tendency of the rotor to move to a position of minimum magnetic reluctance.
- **Comutation:** Sequential electronic excitation of stator phases based on rotor position.
- **Advantage:** Extremely robust, very high speed capability, fault tolerant, low cost, operates at high temperatures.
- **Disadvantage:** High torque ripple, acoustic noise, requires sophisticated control algorithm.
- **Usage:** Limited production deployment; researched heavily by Renault, SR Drives LLC, and others.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all KTU Module 2 high-frequency formulas** for EV traction motors. Note the use of `\vert` and `\mid` instead of `|` to maintain valid markdown table syntax.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Electromagnetic Torque | $T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \psi \cdot I_q$ | Nm | $p$ = pole pairs; $\psi$ = flux linkage; $I_q$ = q-axis current |
| 2 | Back-EMF | $E_b = k_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega$ | V | $k_e$ = EMF constant; $\Phi$ = flux per pole; $\omega$ = angular speed |
| 3 | Motor Output Power | $P_{out} = T_e \cdot \omega$ | W | Fundamental mechanical power equation |
| 4 | Power in Linear Motion (Vehicle) | $P_{trac} = F_{trac} \cdot v$ | W | $F_{trac}$ = tractive force; $v$ = linear velocity |
| 5 | Tractive Force (Vehicle Dynamics) | $F_{trac} = m \cdot g \cdot C_r + \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2 + m \cdot a$ | N | Sum of rolling, aerodynamic, and inertial resistance |
| 6 | Motor Wheel-Side Torque | $T_{wheel} = F_{trac} \cdot r_{wheel}$ | Nm | $r_{wheel}$ = effective wheel radius |
| 7 | Motor Shaft Speed from Vehicle Speed | $N_{motor} = \dfrac{v}{r_{wheel}} \cdot \dfrac{60}{2\pi} \cdot G_r$ | rpm | $G_r$ = total gear ratio |
| 8 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{T_{out} \cdot \omega}{V \cdot I}$ | dimensionless | Electrical to mechanical conversion efficiency |
| 9 | Power Loss | $P_{loss} = P_{Cu} + P_{Fe} + P_{mech} + P_{stray}$ | W | Sum of copper, iron, mechanical, and stray losses |
| 10 | Induced EMF (PMSM, per phase, RMS) | $E_{ph} = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N_{ph} \cdot \Phi_m \cdot k_w$ | V | $k_w$ = winding factor; $f$ = electrical frequency |
| 11 | Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \cdot f}{P}$ | rpm | $P$ = total number of poles |
| 12 | Slip (Induction Motor) | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | dimensionless | $N_r$ = rotor speed |
| 13 | Reluctance (SRM) | $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{l}{\mu \cdot A}$ | A-t/Wb | Magnetic reluctance of air gap |
| 14 | Specific Power | $P_{sp} = \dfrac{P_{rated}}{m_{motor}}$ | kW/kg | Power-to-mass ratio |
| 15 | Specific Torque | $T_{sp} = \dfrac{T_{rated}}{m_{motor}}$ | Nm/kg | Torque-to-mass ratio |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The selection of the traction motor is a **multi-objective optimization problem** that influences the entire vehicle architecture. In production EVs:

- **Tesla** initially favored the **AC Induction Motor** for its low cost, no-rare-earth dependency, and field-weakening capability.
- **Nissan Leaf** and **Toyota Prius** chose **PMSM** for the highest efficiency and power density, critical for maximizing range.
- **Renault** has invested in **SRM** research for the cost-robustness advantage.
- **BMW i3** uses a **hybrid synchronous motor** (interior permanent magnet excited rotor with additional reluctance torque) to balance efficiency and field-weakening.

> [!NOTE]
> The **constant-power speed range (CPSR)** is the single most critical performance metric for an EV motor. It determines whether a **single-speed gearbox** (CPSR ‚â• 4) is feasible, which dramatically reduces drivetrain cost and complexity. Most modern PMSMs achieve CPSR ‚âà 3‚Äì5, while SRMs can exceed 6.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚Äî Electromagnetic Torque in a PMSM (d-q Axis Model)

The KTU Module 2 syllabus emphasizes the **rotating reference frame (d-q axis) derivation** for synchronous machines. We proceed step-by-step from first principles.

### Step 1: Stator MMF Distribution

A balanced three-phase stator winding carrying sinusoidal currents $i_a$, $i_b$, $i_c$ produces a rotating magnetomotive force (MMF) in the air gap. Using **Park's transformation**, the stationary three-phase quantities are mapped to a synchronously rotating two-phase reference frame:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_d &= \dfrac{2}{3} \left[ i_a \cos\theta + i_b \cos\!\left(\theta - \dfrac{2\pi}{3}\right) + i_c \cos\!\left(\theta + \dfrac{2\pi}{3}\right) \right] \\[4pt]
i_q &= \dfrac{2}{3} \left[ i_a \sin\theta + i_b \sin\!\left(\theta - \dfrac{2\pi}{3}\right) + i_c \sin\!\left(\theta + \dfrac{2\pi}{3}\right) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** This transformation converts time-varying sinusoidal stator currents into DC quantities in the rotating frame, vastly simplifying control design.

### Step 2: Air-Gap Flux Linkages

The flux linkages in the d-q frame combine the **permanent magnet contribution** $\psi_m$ and the **armature reaction contribution** from the stator currents:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\psi_d &= L_d \cdot i_d + \psi_m \\[4pt]
\psi_q &= L_q \cdot i_q
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** $L_d$ and $L_q$ are the d-axis and q-axis synchronous inductances. For a **surface-mounted PMSM** (SPMSM), $L_d \approx L_q = L_s$. For an **interior PMSM** (IPMSM), $L_d < L_q$, which produces additional reluctance torque.

### Step 3: Electromagnetic Torque Equation

The instantaneous electromagnetic torque in a PMSM is the partial derivative of co-energy with respect to rotor angle. In the d-q frame, this yields:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_e &= \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \left[ \psi_m \cdot i_q + \left(L_d - L_q\right) \cdot i_d \cdot i_q \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The first term is the **magnet torque** (proportional to PM flux and q-axis current). The second term is the **reluctance torque** (non-zero only for IPMSM, where $L_d \neq L_q$).

### Step 4: Maximum Torque Per Ampere (MTPA) Strategy

To minimize copper losses for a given torque demand, the optimal current vector is found by setting $\dfrac{\partial T_e}{\partial i_d} = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_d &= \dfrac{\psi_m}{2\left(L_q - L_d\right)} - \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\psi_m}{2\left(L_q - L_d\right)}\right)^2 + i_q^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** This produces a unique optimal trajectory in the $i_d$‚Äì$i_q$ plane that delivers the requested torque at the lowest possible stator current magnitude $\sqrt{i_d^2 + i_q^2}$, and hence the lowest $I^2R$ copper loss.

### Step 5: Output Power and the Constant-Power Region

The shaft power is simply the product of torque and angular speed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{out} = T_e \cdot \omega = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \omega \cdot \left[ \psi_m \cdot i_q + \left(L_d - L_q\right) \cdot i_d \cdot i_q \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Below the **base speed** $\omega_b$, the back-EMF is below the DC bus voltage limit, so maximum torque is delivered at full current. Above $\omega_b$, the inverter enters **field-weakening** mode ‚Äî a negative $i_d$ is injected to oppose $\psi_m$, reducing the effective flux and allowing the speed to rise while keeping $V_{ph} \leq V_{dc}/\sqrt{3}$. This produces the **constant-power hyperbolic region** in the torque-speed plane.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚Äî Tractive Force Requirement and Motor Sizing

A common KTU Module 2 problem: **size the traction motor** for a given vehicle.

### Step 1: Define Vehicle Parameters
- Mass: $m$ (kg)
- Drag coefficient: $C_d$
- Frontal area: $A$ (m¬≤)
- Rolling resistance coefficient: $C_r$
- Top speed: $v_{max}$ (m/s)
- Acceleration: $a$ (m/s¬≤)
- Wheel radius: $r_w$ (m)
- Gear ratio: $G_r$

### Step 2: Total Resistance Force

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{tot} = m \cdot g \cdot C_r + \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2 + m \cdot a
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Sum of rolling resistance (proportional to weight), aerodynamic drag (proportional to $v^2$), and inertial resistance (proportional to acceleration). At low speed rolling dominates; at high speed, aerodynamic drag dominates.

### Step 3: Required Wheel Torque and Motor Torque

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{wheel} &= F_{tot} \cdot r_w \\[4pt]
T_{motor} &= \dfrac{T_{wheel}}{G_r \cdot \eta_{gearbox}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Required Motor Power

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{motor} = \dfrac{F_{tot} \cdot v}{\eta_{gearbox} \cdot \eta_{motor}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The motor must deliver mechanical power at the wheels divided by the combined drivetrain efficiency. For a 1500 kg EV at 120 km/h on level ground with $C_d A = 0.65$, the steady-state power is approximately **15‚Äì20 kW**, but the peak power requirement (acceleration) is **100‚Äì150 kW**.

### Step 5: Verify Base Speed and CPSR

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_{base} &= \dfrac{P_{rated}}{T_{peak}} \\[4pt]
CPSR &= \dfrac{\omega_{max}}{\omega_{base}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** A typical 100 kW EV motor has $T_{peak} \approx 250$ Nm, giving $\omega_{base} \approx 400$ rad/s ($\approx 3800$ rpm), and $\omega_{max} \approx 1600$ rad/s ($\approx 15,000$ rpm), so $CPSR \approx 4$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Comparative Scoring of EV Motor Technologies

The KTU examiner expects a comparative table that scores each motor type on standard criteria. The table below is structured for **maximum marks** in a 14-mark Part B question.

| Criterion | DC Motor | Induction Motor (IM) | PMSM / BLDC | SRM |
|-----------|----------|---------------------|-------------|-----|
| **Power Density (kW/kg)** | Low (0.3‚Äì0.5) | Medium (0.5‚Äì1.0) | **High (1.0‚Äì2.5)** | High (1.0‚Äì2.0) |
| **Peak Efficiency** | 75‚Äì85% | 90‚Äì94% | **95‚Äì97%** | 90‚Äì93% |
| **Constant Power Speed Range (CPSR)** | 2:1 | 3:1 | **4:1 to 5:1** | **5:1 to 6:1** |
| **Torque Ripple** | Medium | Low | **Very Low (PMSM)** | **High** |
| **Cost (per kW)** | Low (old) | **Lowest (no magnets)** | High (rare earth) | Low‚ÄìMedium |
| **Robustness (thermal/mechanical)** | Low (brushes) | **High** | Medium (demagnetization) | **Very High** |
| **Maintenance** | High (brushes) | **Low** | **Low** | **Low** |
| **Magnet / Rare Earth Dependency** | None | **None** | High (NdFeB) | **None** |
| **Mature Production Examples** | None (legacy) | Tesla S/X/3 | Nissan Leaf, Prius | Limited (Renault) |
| **Control Complexity** | **Low** | Medium | Medium‚ÄìHigh | **High** |
| **Noise / Acoustic Comfort** | Medium | Low | Low | **High** |
| **Inherent Safety (Fault Tolerant)** | Low | Medium | Medium | **Very High** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical takeaway:** No single motor type wins on all criteria. The PMSM dominates efficiency and power density (used in most modern EVs), the IM wins on cost and robustness (used by Tesla and some commercial vehicles), and the SRM offers the most fault-tolerant, magnet-free architecture but suffers from torque ripple and noise. KTU examiners reward answers that **acknowledge trade-offs** rather than declaring a single "winner."

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams and Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: EV Traction Motor Subsystem

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of an EV traction motor system, including the power electronics, controller, sensor feedback, and mechanical interface.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Battery Pack DC Bus] --> B[DC Link Capacitor Bank]
    B --> C[3-Phase VSI Inverter]
    D[Vehicle Control Unit VCU] --> E[Motor Control Unit MCU]
    F[Resolver Encoder Sensor] --> E
    G[Current Sensors Hall Effect] --> E
    H[Temperature Sensors PT100] --> E
    E --> C
    C --> I[Stator 3-Phase Windings]
    I --> J[Air Gap Electromagnetic Coupling]
    J --> K[Rotor PM Field or Squirrel Cage or Salient Pole]
    K --> L[Output Shaft]
    L --> M[Gear Reduction Single or Two Speed]
    M --> N[Drive Wheels]
    N --> O[Vehicles Linear Motion]
    O --> P[Tractive Force and Rolling Drag]
    P --> N
    N --> Q[Regenerative Brake Feedback]
    Q --> F
    Q --> G
    Q --> H
    E --> R[Field Weakening Logic CPSR 1 to 5]
    E --> S[MTPA Maximum Torque Per Ampere Algorithm]
    E --> T[Vector Control FOC]
```

## 4.2 Classification Flowchart of EV Traction Motors

The diagram below shows the formal KTU Module 2 classification and the operating principle of each family.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[EV Traction Motor] --> A1[DC Motor Family]
    Start --> A2[AC Motor Family]
    Start --> A3[Special Construction Family]

    A1 --> B1[Separately Excited DC]
    A1 --> B2[Series Excited DC]
    A1 --> B3[Shunt Excited DC]

    A2 --> C1[Induction Motor IM]
    A2 --> C2[Synchronous Motor Family]
    C2 --> D1[PMSM Interior Mount IPMSM]
    C2 --> D2[PMSM Surface Mount SPMSM]
    C2 --> D3[BLDC Trapezoidal Drive]
    C2 --> D4[Wound Rotor Synchronous]

    A3 --> E1[Switched Reluctance Motor SRM]
    A3 --> E2[Synchronous Reluctance Motor SynRM]
    A3 --> E3[Axial Flux Disc Motor]
```

## 4.3 Torque-Speed Operating Envelope Schematic

The following block diagram maps the **constant-torque** and **constant-power** regions of a typical EV traction motor, which is a high-yield topic in KTU examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Region 1 Constant Torque Low Speed] --> R2[Region 2 Constant Power Field Weakening]
    R2 --> R3[Region 3 High Speed Series Characteristic]
    R3 --> R4[Region 4 Voltage Limit Envelope]
    R1 --> M1[Max Torque Tpeak]
    R2 --> M1
    R2 --> M2[Rated Power Prated]
    R3 --> M2
    R4 --> M3[Max Speed Nmax]
```

## 4.4 Energy Flow Diagram ‚Äî Motoring and Regenerative Braking

The bidirectional power flow is a critical concept. Below is the functional block diagram showing motor and generator modes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BAT[Battery Pack] -- DC Power --> INV[3-Phase Inverter VSI]
    INV -- AC Power --> TRAC[Motor Mode Torque Positive]
    INV -- AC Power --> REG[Generator Mode Torque Negative]
    TRAC --> WHL[Wheels]
    WHL --> REG
    REG -- DC Charging Current --> BAT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note on Diagram Interpretation:** In **motoring mode**, electrical energy flows from the battery ‚Üí inverter ‚Üí stator winding ‚Üí rotor (mechanical torque) ‚Üí wheels. In **regenerative braking mode**, the kinetic energy of the wheels drives the rotor above synchronous speed (or out of phase), the back-EMF exceeds the DC bus voltage, diodes conduct, and current flows back into the battery. The same inverter, same machine, same sensors ‚Äî only the control algorithm switches sign.
