# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]
**Q: List any three performance requirements that an electric motor for EV traction application must satisfy. Justify each in one sentence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks ‚Äî 1 mark per requirement):**
1. **High specific power (kW/kg):** EV traction motors must deliver large power from a compact, lightweight package to maximize payload and range. (1 mark)
2. **Wide constant-power speed range (CPSR ‚â• 3:1):** This allows a single-speed gearbox, eliminating the cost and complexity of a multi-speed transmission. (1 mark)
3. **High peak efficiency (‚â• 90%):** Maximizes driving range per kWh of battery capacity, directly addressing the consumer's primary concern. (1 mark)

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between PMSM and BLDC motors based on back-EMF waveform and current excitation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | PMSM | BLDC |
|---------|------|------|
| **Back-EMF Waveform** | Sinusoidal (1 mark) | Trapezoidal (1 mark) |
| **Current Excitation** | Sinusoidal (FOC / vector control) | Square-wave (6-step commutation) (1 mark) |
| **Torque Ripple** | Lower | Higher |
| **Control Complexity** | Higher (requires d-q transformation) | Lower |

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A [14 Marks] ‚Äî Motor Selection and Sizing**

**A(a) [7 Marks]: Compare the four major types of electric motors used in HEVs and EVs (DC, IM, PMSM, SRM) in terms of power density, efficiency, cost, and ruggedness. Identify which type is used in the Toyota Prius and justify.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **DC Motors:** Low power density (0.3‚Äì0.5 kW/kg), efficiency 75‚Äì85%, low cost, low ruggedness due to brushes. Used in early EVs. (1 mark)
2. **Induction Motors (IM):** Medium power density (0.5‚Äì1.0 kW/kg), efficiency 90‚Äì94%, lowest cost (no magnets), high ruggedness (squirrel-cage rotor). Used by Tesla. (1.5 marks)
3. **PMSM:** High power density (1.0‚Äì2.5 kW/kg), peak efficiency 95‚Äì97%, medium-high cost (rare-earth magnets), medium ruggedness (demagnetization risk). (1.5 marks)
4. **SRM:** High power density, efficiency 90‚Äì93%, low-medium cost, very high ruggedness (no magnets, no windings on rotor). High torque ripple and noise. (1.5 marks)

**Toyota Prius uses PMSM** (specifically, an interior permanent magnet synchronous motor). Justification: The Prius design requires **maximum fuel economy** and **compact packaging** in a hybrid architecture where the motor must frequently start the ICE. The PMSM's high efficiency directly improves fuel economy, and its high power density allows packaging under the rear seat. (1.5 marks)

**A(b) [7 Marks]: A 1500 kg electric vehicle is to be designed for a top speed of 150 km/h, a peak acceleration of 0.4 g, rolling resistance coefficient $C_r = 0.012$, drag coefficient $C_d = 0.30$, frontal area $A = 2.2$ m¬≤, wheel radius $r_w = 0.33$ m, single gear ratio $G_r = 9.0$, and combined drivetrain efficiency $\eta = 0.92$. Compute the required peak motor torque and peak motor power. Take $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m¬≥, $g = 9.81$ m/s¬≤.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Total resistance force at top speed (steady state, a = 0):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{roll} &= m \cdot g \cdot C_r = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 = 176.58 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
F_{aero} &= \dfrac{1}{2} \rho_{air} C_d A v^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (41.67)^2 \\[4pt]
&= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 1736.4 = 702.5 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
F_{tot} &= 176.58 + 702.5 = 879.1 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 2: Power at wheels:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{wheel} = F_{tot} \times v = 879.1 \times 41.67 = 36{,}634 \text{ W} \approx 36.6 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Motor power at top speed (steady cruise):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{\eta} = \dfrac{36.6}{0.92} = 39.8 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 4: Force required during 0.4 g peak acceleration at low speed (take v ‚âà 5 m/s for sizing):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{acc} &= m \cdot a = 1500 \times 0.4 \times 9.81 = 5886 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
F_{tot,peak} &\approx F_{roll} + F_{aero,low} + F_{acc} = 176.58 + 5.4 + 5886 \approx 6068 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Wheel torque at peak:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{wheel,peak} = F_{tot,peak} \times r_w = 6068 \times 0.33 = 2002.4 \text{ Nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Motor torque after gear reduction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{motor,peak} = \dfrac{T_{wheel,peak}}{G_r \times \eta} = \dfrac{2002.4}{9.0 \times 0.92} = 241.8 \text{ Nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

(1.5 marks)

**Step 7: Peak motor power during launch:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{peak} = T_{motor,peak} \times \omega_{motor}
\end{aligned}
$$

At launch ($\omega \approx 0$), the peak power is dominated by the cruise requirement. The motor must be **rated for at least 40 kW continuous** with a **peak torque capability of ‚âà 250 Nm**. Sizing typically rounds up to **75 kW peak / 40 kW continuous** for a 1500 kg vehicle. (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall ‚Äî Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Do not confuse $F_{trac}$ with $F_{tot}$ during acceleration.** $F_{trac}$ at the wheels is the total resistive force PLUS the inertial term $m \cdot a$. Forgetting $m \cdot a$ will give a peak torque 30√ó too low.
> 2. **Do not forget to divide the wheel-side torque by the gear ratio** to get the motor-side torque. A common error is to report the wheel torque directly as the motor torque, giving a value 9√ó too high.
> 3. **Always include drivetrain efficiency in the motor sizing** ‚Äî the motor must deliver MORE power than the wheels receive. Reporting the wheel power as the motor rating is a 1‚Äì2 mark deduction.
> 4. **Use $v$ in m/s, not km/h**, in the aerodynamic and power equations. The conversion $v_{m/s} = v_{km/h} / 3.6$ is a frequent source of numerical error.

---

### **Question B [14 Marks] ‚Äî Operating Principles and Vector Control**

**B(a) [7 Marks]: With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the d-q axis model of a Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM) used in EVs. Derive the electromagnetic torque expression.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Diagram (block representation, 2 marks):** The d-q reference frame is fixed to the rotor, with the d-axis aligned with the permanent magnet flux vector $\psi_m$, and the q-axis perpendicular (90¬∞ electrical ahead) in the direction of rotation. The stator currents $i_a, i_b, i_c$ are transformed via **Park's transformation** into $i_d$ (flux-producing component) and $i_q$ (torque-producing component).

**Transformation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_d &= i_a \cos\theta + i_b \cos(\theta - 2\pi/3) + i_c \cos(\theta + 2\pi/3) \\[4pt]
i_q &= -i_a \sin\theta - i_b \sin(\theta - 2\pi/3) - i_c \sin(\theta + 2\pi/3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Flux linkages and torque (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\psi_d &= L_d \cdot i_d + \psi_m \\[4pt]
\psi_q &= L_q \cdot i_q \\[4pt]
T_e &= \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \left[ \psi_m \cdot i_q + (L_d - L_q) \cdot i_d \cdot i_q \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Physical interpretation (1 mark):** The first term $\psi_m \cdot i_q$ is the **magnet torque**; the second is the **reluctance torque** (non-zero only for IPMSM with $L_d \neq L_q$).

**B(b) [7 Marks]: Explain the field-weakening operation of a PMSM. Why is it required for EV applications? Show the constant-torque and constant-power regions in a torque-speed diagram.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Field-weakening principle (3 marks):** The back-EMF $E_b = k_e \cdot \psi_m \cdot \omega$ increases linearly with speed. When $\omega$ reaches the **base speed** $\omega_b$, the back-EMF equals the maximum available phase voltage from the inverter. Beyond $\omega_b$, the speed can only be increased if the effective flux is reduced. This is achieved by injecting a **negative d-axis current** $i_d < 0$ that opposes $\psi_m$, weakening the net air-gap flux and allowing $\omega$ to rise while keeping the phase voltage within the inverter limit.

**Why required for EVs (2 marks):** Highway cruising and high-speed overtaking require motor speeds of 12,000‚Äì18,000 rpm, well above base speed. Without field-weakening, the motor would saturate the inverter voltage and could not reach the required speed. Field-weakening also enables **regenerative braking at high speeds**, which is critical for energy recovery during deceleration from highway speeds.

**Torque-speed diagram (2 marks):**

| Region | Speed Range | Torque | Power |
|--------|-------------|--------|-------|
| Region 1 ‚Äî Constant Torque | 0 to $\omega_b$ | $T_{peak}$ (constant) | Rises linearly with $\omega$ |
| Region 2 ‚Äî Constant Power | $\omega_b$ to $\omega_{max}$ | Falls as $1/\omega$ (hyperbolic) | $P_{rated}$ (constant) |
| Region 3 ‚Äî Voltage / Series Limit | Above $\omega_{max}$ | Falls as $1/\omega^2$ | Falls as $1/\omega$ |

The **CPSR = $\omega_{max} / \omega_b$**, and a high CPSR is the design goal of EV traction motors.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall ‚Äî Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Do not write "field-weakening uses less current."** Field-weakening actually **increases** total current and copper loss ‚Äî its purpose is to trade efficiency for speed.
> 2. **Do not confuse base speed with rated speed.** Base speed is the speed at which back-EMF saturates the inverter voltage; rated speed is often higher.
> 3. **Always draw the torque-speed diagram with the constant-power hyperbolic curve**, not a straight line. A straight-line decline is a 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Reluctance torque sign convention:** The term $(L_d - L_q)$ is **negative** for IPMSM (since $L_d < L_q$). Students often drop the sign and lose a mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 ‚Äî Introduction to Electric Motors**

- [ ] **Four motor families:** DC, IM, PMSM/BLDC, SRM. Know one production EV example for each.
- [ ] **PMSM vs BLDC:** Sinusoidal vs trapezoidal back-EMF; FOC vs 6-step commutation.
- [ ] **PMSM torque equation:** $T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} p \left[ \psi_m i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q \right]$ ‚Äî memorize the form, not just the symbols.
- [ ] **MTPA strategy:** Minimizes $I^2R$ loss; produces optimal $i_d$ trajectory.
- [ ] **Field weakening:** Negative $i_d$ to reduce $\psi_{eff}$ above base speed.
- [ ] **CPSR = $\omega_{max} / \omega_b$**; target ‚â• 3:1 for single-speed gearbox.
- [ ] **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = 120 f / P$.
- [ ] **Slip in IM:** $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$; small positive value at rated load (typically 2‚Äì5%).
- [ ] **SRM has no magnets and no rotor windings** ‚Äî only stator windings and a salient steel rotor.
- [ ] **Vehicle tractive force equation:** $F_{trac} = mgC_r + 0.5 \rho C_d A v^2 + ma$ ‚Äî all three terms required.
- [ ] **Motor sizing rule of thumb:** 60‚Äì100 kW peak per 1000 kg of vehicle mass.
- [ ] **Specific power benchmark:** ‚â• 1 kW/kg for modern EV traction motors.
- [ ] **Peak efficiency benchmark:** ‚â• 95% for PMSM; 90‚Äì94% for IM; 90‚Äì93% for SRM.
- [ ] **Tesla uses IM, Nissan uses PMSM, Renault experiments with SRM.**
- [ ] **Rare-earth magnet supply risk** ‚Äî a key driver of SRM and IM research.
- [ ] **Regenerative braking** ‚Äî the same traction machine acts as a generator; no separate hardware.
- [ ] **Inverter topology:** 3-phase Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) with IGBT or SiC MOSFET switches.
- [ ] **Sensor requirements:** Resolver (rotor position), Hall-effect current sensors, PT100 / PT1000 temperature sensors.
- [ ] **Cooling:** Most EV motors are water-glycol jacket cooled; some use oil spray cooling for high-performance applications.
- [ ] **Acoustic noise:** SRM is the noisiest due to radial force variations; PMSM is the quietest.
- [ ] **Fault tolerance:** SRM phases are independent ‚Üí highest fault tolerance; PMSM has no inherent redundancy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Examination Tip:** KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 questions frequently combine a **derivation** (7 marks) with a **comparative analysis** (7 marks). Practice deriving the PMSM torque equation and the SRM torque equation from co-energy, and always back up comparative claims with **numerical values** (e.g., "PMSM achieves 96% peak efficiency versus 91% for IM at rated load"). Numerical justification is rewarded more heavily than qualitative statements.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DC Motor Drives: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** DC Motor Drives: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **DC Motor Drive** is an integrated electromechanical power conversion system comprising a Direct Current (DC) machine, a power electronic converter (DC-DC chopper or rectifier), and a closed-loop controller, used to deliver variable speed and variable torque to a mechanical load. In the context of **Hybrid and Electric Vehicles (HEVs/EVs)**, a DC motor drive converts stored electrical energy from the battery pack into precisely controlled mechanical traction power at the wheels.

The mathematical operating region of any DC motor drive is governed by three foundational equations:

$$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$$

$$E_b = K_e \cdot \phi \cdot \omega$$

$$T = K_t \cdot \phi \cdot I_a$$

Where:
- $V_a$ = Armature terminal voltage (Volts)
- $E_b$ = Back-EMF (Volts)
- $I_a$ = Armature current (Amperes)
- $R_a$ = Armature resistance (Ohms)
- $K_e$ = Voltage constant (V¬∑s/rad)
- $K_t$ = Torque constant (N¬∑m/A)
- $\phi$ = Flux per pole (Weber)
- $\omega$ = Angular speed (rad/s)
- $T$ = Electromagnetic torque (N¬∑m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** In SI units, $K_e = K_t$. Examiners frequently award or deduct **2 marks** solely on this dimensional equivalence.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The Bicycle Dynamo in Reverse

Imagine a **bicycle dynamo** (a small DC generator). When you spin its wheel, it produces voltage ‚Äî that is *back-EMF opposing rotation*. Now reverse the process: feed it DC current from a battery, and it spins a wheel. This is a DC motor.

A **DC motor drive** is essentially the same setup, but with a *throttle*. The accelerator pedal in an EV is not a mechanical cable; it is a **potentiometer** that sends a 0‚Äì5 V reference signal to a controller. The controller then regulates the battery voltage reaching the motor ‚Äî just like opening or closing a water tap. More voltage ‚Üí more speed; more current ‚Üí more torque.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** A DC motor drive is the "throttle-to-wheel translator" of an electric vehicle. The battery supplies raw DC, the chopper/controller modulates it, and the motor converts it into rotary motion.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Performance Metrics

| Metric | Typical EV Traction Value |
|---|---|
| Specific Power (Power-to-weight) | **0.1 ‚Äì 0.5 kW/kg** |
| Peak Efficiency | **85 ‚Äì 92 %** |
| Base Speed | **1500 ‚Äì 3000 rpm** |
| Constant Power Speed Range | **3 : 1 to 4 : 1** |
| Maximum Torque (per unit) | **2 ‚Äì 3 √ó Rated Torque** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of DC Motors Used in EV/HEV Applications

DC motors used in traction are classified by **field winding connection**, which directly determines the torque-speed characteristic ‚Äî the single most important property for vehicle propulsion.

### A. Separately Excited DC Motor (SEDM)
- Field winding is supplied from an **independent DC source**.
- Flux $\phi$ is controlled independently of armature current.
- **Application:** Historically used in electric forklifts, modern small EVs, and laboratory drives. Excellent for **regenerative braking** because $\phi$ can be reduced to push speed above base.

### B. Series DC Motor (SDM)
- Field winding connected in **series** with the armature.
- $\phi \propto I_a$ (until magnetic saturation).
- **Application:** Traditional choice for EVs due to enormous starting torque ‚Äî exactly what a vehicle needs at standstill / hill climb.

### C. Shunt DC Motor
- Field winding in **parallel** with the armature.
- $\phi$ is approximately constant.
- **Application:** Rare in modern EVs (poor starting torque), but appears in auxiliary drives (cooling fans, pumps).

### D. Compound DC Motor
- Hybrid of series and shunt field.
- Used in **heavy traction locomotives** (diesel-electric locomotives like Indian Railways WAP-5, WAG-9 historically used compound DC traction motors before the transition to three-phase AC).

### E. Permanent Magnet DC Motor (PMDC)
- Field produced by **rare-earth permanent magnets** (NdFeB).
- Compact, high efficiency, no field copper losses.
- **Application:** Modern small EVs, e-bikes, golf carts, two-wheelers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** When asked *"Why are series DC motors preferred for traction?"*, the model answer must contain three keywords: **(1) High starting torque (‚àù I¬≤)**, **(2) Self-protection against overload**, **(3) Naturally suited for variable-speed traction loads.**

## 2.2 Why DC Motors in HEVs/EVs? The Engineering Trade-off

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Reality Check:** Most 2024+ production EVs (Tesla Model 3, Hyundai Ioniq 5, Tata Nexon EV) use **3-phase AC induction** or **PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor)** drives ‚Äî *not* DC. However, DC drives remain in the KTU syllabus because (a) many low-speed EVs and two-wheelers in India still use BLDC/PMDC, (b) the pedagogical foundation of motor drives begins with DC machines, and (c) regenerative braking logic is conceptually identical.

| Feature | DC Motor Drive | AC Motor Drive |
|---|---|---|
| Controller complexity | Simple (1 quadrant chopper) | Complex (SVPWM, FOC) |
| Starting torque | Very high (series) | High (with vector control) |
| Maintenance | High (brushes, commutator) | Very low (brushless) |
| Efficiency at rated load | 85‚Äì90 % | 92‚Äì97 % |
| Cost (controller) | Low | Moderate‚ÄìHigh |
| Suitability for HEV | Limited (small vehicles) | Industry standard |

## 2.3 Operating Quadrants of a DC Motor Drive

A complete drive must operate in **four quadrants** to support real driving:

| Quadrant | Speed $\omega$ | Torque $T$ | Driving Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| I | + (Forward) | + (Forward) | Forward motoring (acceleration) |
| II | + (Forward) | ‚àí (Braking) | Forward regenerative braking |
| III | ‚àí (Reverse) | ‚àí (Reverse) | Reverse motoring |
| IV | ‚àí (Reverse) | + (Braking) | Reverse regenerative braking |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Favourite:** Questions on *"Explain the four-quadrant operation of a DC motor drive with chopper circuits"* are a **guaranteed appearance** in End Semester Examinations.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Armature voltage equation (KVL) | All KTU numericals |
| 2 | $E_b = K_e \phi \omega$ | Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction | Speed calculation |
| 3 | $T = K_t \phi I_a$ | Lorentz force-based torque production | Torque/current calculation |
| 4 | $N = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_e \phi} \cdot \dfrac{60}{2\pi}$ | Speed in RPM | Speed regulation problems |
| 5 | $\omega = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_e \phi}$ | Speed in rad/s | Derivation base |
| 6 | $P_{developed} = T \cdot \omega = E_b \cdot I_a$ | Internal electromagnetic power | Power flow diagram |
| 7 | $P_{input} = V_a \cdot I_a$ | Electrical input power | Efficiency calculation |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{E_b I_a}{V_a I_a} = 1 - \dfrac{I_a R_a}{V_a}$ | Efficiency expression | Efficiency problems |
| 9 | $\phi_{series} \propto I_a \Rightarrow T \propto I_a^2$ | Series motor torque law | Quadrant problems |
| 10 | $\dfrac{d\omega}{dT} = -\dfrac{R_a}{(K_e \phi)^2}$ | Slope of N-T characteristics | Stability analysis |

> [!WARNING]
> **Equation #10** is rarely taught in class but appears in KTU **Part C (14-mark) derivations**. Memorize it ‚Äî it comes from differentiating $\omega = (V - I_a R_a) / K_e \phi$ and substituting $T = K_t \phi I_a$.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in HEV/EV Engineering

1. **Traction Control**: A DC drive with closed-loop PI controller maintains exact wheel torque demanded by the driver.
2. **Regenerative Braking**: When the chopper is operated in boost mode, the motor acts as a generator, feeding current back to the battery ‚Äî recovering **10‚Äì30 %** of kinetic energy.
3. **Battery Management Compatibility**: DC drives draw variable current but at varying voltage, allowing Battery Management Systems (BMS) to operate within the State-of-Charge (SOC) sweet spot.
4. **Auxiliary Loads**: Power windows, HVAC blowers, coolant pumps, and electronic power steering in many HEVs use small PMDC or BLDC drives.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I ‚Äî Speed Equation of a Separately Excited DC Motor (Starting from First Principles)

**Objective:** Derive the expression for angular speed $\omega$ in terms of $V_a$, $I_a$, $R_a$, $\phi$, and $K_e$.

### Step 1 ‚Äî Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) to the armature loop
The armature circuit consists of the supply voltage $V_a$, the armature resistance $R_a$, the armature inductance $L_a$, and the induced back-EMF $E_b$.

$$V_a = I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} + E_b$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Assume steady-state operation
Under steady-state conditions, the current $I_a$ is no longer changing with time, hence $\dfrac{dI_a}{dt} = 0$. The transient inductance term vanishes.

$$V_a = I_a R_a + E_b$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Express back-EMF using Faraday's law
For a DC machine with $P$ poles, $Z$ total conductors, and $A$ parallel paths, the average back-EMF induced per conductor is the rate of cutting of flux. For a wave-wound armature ($A = 2$):

$$E_b = \frac{\phi Z N P}{60 A}$$

The combined machine constant $K_e$ is defined as $K_e = \dfrac{ZP}{60A}$, yielding:

$$E_b = K_e \cdot \phi \cdot N$$

where $N$ is in revolutions per minute (RPM).

### Step 4 ‚Äî Convert RPM to angular speed in rad/s
By definition, $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$. Rearranging, $N = \dfrac{60 \omega}{2\pi}$. Substituting:

$$E_b = K_e \cdot \phi \cdot \frac{60 \omega}{2\pi} = \left(\frac{60 K_e}{2\pi}\right) \phi \omega$$

### Step 5 ‚Äî Define a new constant $K_e'$ such that $E_b = K_e' \phi \omega$
Combining:

$$K_e' = \frac{60 K_e}{2\pi} = \frac{30 K_e}{\pi}$$

Therefore in SI units, $E_b = K_e' \phi \omega$. (For simplicity, most textbooks and KTU papers absorb this $30/\pi$ factor into the constant and write $E_b = K \phi \omega$.)

### Step 6 ‚Äî Substitute $E_b$ back into the KVL equation

$$V_a = I_a R_a + K_e' \phi \omega$$

### Step 7 ‚Äî Solve for $\omega$

$$K_e' \phi \omega = V_a - I_a R_a$$

$$\boxed{\omega = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_e' \phi}}$$

### Step 8 ‚Äî Express in RPM form (preferred for KTU board answers)

$$\boxed{N = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_e \phi} \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = \frac{(V_a - I_a R_a) \times 60}{2 \pi K_e \phi}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The speed depends on **(a) applied voltage $V_a$**, **(b) armature current $I_a$ (i.e., load torque)**, **(c) flux $\phi$**, and **(d) machine constant $K_e$**. Two control strategies emerge from this single equation: **Armature Voltage Control** and **Flux Control** ‚Äî both are KTU 14-mark favourites.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II ‚Äî Torque Equation of a DC Motor

**Objective:** Derive $T = K_t \phi I_a$.

### Step 1 ‚Äî Force on a single current-carrying conductor
A conductor of length $l$ carrying current $I$ in a magnetic field of flux density $B$ experiences a force:

$$F = B \cdot I \cdot l$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Express $B$ in terms of flux per pole
For a pole shoe of area $A_p$, the average flux density is $B = \phi / A_p$. Thus:

$$F = \frac{\phi}{A_p} \cdot I \cdot l$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Torque produced by a single conductor
If the conductor is at radius $r$ from the shaft:

$$F_{per\ cond} \cdot r = \frac{\phi \cdot I \cdot l \cdot r}{A_p}$$

### Step 4 ‚Äî Total torque from all $Z$ conductors
Each conductor carries current $I_a / A$ (where $A$ is the number of parallel paths). Summing over all $Z$ conductors:

$$T = \frac{Z}{A} \cdot \frac{\phi \cdot I_a \cdot l \cdot r}{A_p}$$

### Step 5 ‚Äî Define torque constant
The geometric terms $Z, l, r, A, A_p$ are constants of the machine. Group them as $K_t$:

$$K_t = \frac{Z \cdot l \cdot r}{A \cdot A_p}$$

### Step 6 ‚Äî Final torque equation

$$\boxed{T = K_t \cdot \phi \cdot I_a}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **SI units**, the numerical value of $K_t$ equals that of $K_e'$ (the back-EMF constant in V¬∑s/rad). This is not a coincidence ‚Äî it follows directly from energy conservation ($P_{electrical} = P_{mechanical}$).

---

## 3.3 Derivation III ‚Äî Torque-Speed Characteristic of a Separately Excited DC Motor

**Objective:** Derive the linear relationship $N$ vs $T$ that defines the motor's load line.

### Step 1 ‚Äî Start from the speed equation
From Derivation I:

$$\omega = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_e' \phi}$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Express $I_a$ in terms of $T$ from Derivation II

$$I_a = \frac{T}{K_t \phi}$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Substitute into speed equation

$$\omega = \frac{V_a}{K_e' \phi} - \frac{R_a}{K_e' \phi} \cdot \frac{T}{K_t \phi}$$

### Step 4 ‚Äî Use the SI identity $K_e' = K_t = K$

$$\omega = \frac{V_a}{K \phi} - \frac{R_a}{(K \phi)^2} \cdot T$$

### Step 5 ‚Äî No-load speed ($T = 0$)

$$\omega_0 = \frac{V_a}{K \phi}$$

### Step 6 ‚Äî Stall torque ($\omega = 0$)

$$T_{stall} = \frac{V_a \cdot K \phi}{R_a}$$

### Step 7 ‚Äî Final linear characteristic

$$\boxed{\omega = \omega_0 \left( 1 - \frac{T}{T_{stall}} \right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This is the equation of a **straight line** in the $\omega$‚Äì$T$ plane. It intercepts the $\omega$-axis at $\omega_0$ and the $T$-axis at $T_{stall}$. The slope $\dfrac{d\omega}{dT} = -\dfrac{R_a}{(K\phi)^2}$ is *negative* and very steep (high $K\phi$ ‚Üí flatter curve, better speed regulation).

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV ‚Äî Efficiency of a DC Motor Drive

### Step 1 ‚Äî Electrical input power

$$P_{in} = V_a I_a$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Mechanical output power
Mechanical output = developed power ‚àí rotational losses (friction, windage, stray). At shaft:

$$P_{out} = T_{shaft} \cdot \omega$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Developed (air-gap) power
Air-gap power is the electromechanical conversion point:

$$P_{gap} = E_b I_a = T \cdot \omega$$

### Step 4 ‚Äî Armature copper loss

$$P_{cu} = I_a^2 R_a$$

### Step 5 ‚Äî Efficiency expression

$$\eta = \frac{E_b I_a}{V_a I_a} = \frac{E_b}{V_a} = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{V_a} = 1 - \frac{I_a R_a}{V_a}$$

### Step 6 ‚Äî Condition for maximum efficiency
Differentiating with respect to $I_a$ and setting to zero gives:

$$\frac{d\eta}{dI_a} = -\frac{R_a}{V_a} = 0 \quad (\text{cannot be zero})$$

A more rigorous derivation considering variable armature voltage gives the **maximum efficiency condition**:

$$\boxed{E_b = \frac{V_a}{2} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad I_a R_a = \frac{V_a}{2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** A DC motor (or any DC-DC converter) achieves **maximum efficiency at 50 % voltage drop across internal resistance** ‚Äî a consequence of the square-law copper loss.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram ‚Äî DC Motor Drive System in an EV

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    batt[Battery Pack DC Source] --> conv[Power Electronic Converter Chopper or DC-DC]
    conv --> ctrl[Controller ECU Level Signal]
    ctrl --> conv
    conv --> motor[DC Motor Armature + Field]
    motor --> shaft[Mechanical Shaft to Wheels]
    shaft --> load[Vehicle Load Variable]
    load --> fb[Tachogenerator Speed Feedback]
    fb --> ctrl
    motor --> fb2[Current Sensor Armature]
    fb2 --> ctrl
    motor -.-> regen[Regenerative Braking Path]
    regen -.-> batt
```

**Interpretation of the block diagram:**
- The **battery** (typically 48 V ‚Äì 400 V Li-ion pack) supplies raw DC.
- The **chopper** is a high-frequency IGBT/MOSFET switch (e.g., 5‚Äì20 kHz) that modulates the average voltage.
- The **controller** is a microcontroller / DSP implementing a PI or fuzzy logic algorithm.
- **Tachogenerator** provides speed feedback (closed-loop).
- **Current sensor** provides torque-feedback (inner loop).
- **Regenerative path** returns energy to the battery during braking.

## 4.2 Schematic ‚Äî Power Flow and Loss Diagram of a DC Motor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pin[Electrical Input Power V_a times I_a] --> pcu[Armature Copper Loss I_a squared R_a]
    pin --> pcore[Core Loss Hysteresis and Eddy Current]
    pcu --> pgap[Air Gap Power E_b times I_a]
    pcore --> pgap
    pgap --> pmec[Mechanical Power Developed T times omega]
    pmec --> pfric[Friction and Windage Loss]
    pmec --> pstray[Stray Load Loss]
    pfric --> pshaft[Shaft Output Power T_shaft times omega]
    pstray --> pshaft
```

## 4.3 Schematic ‚Äî Four-Quadrant Operation of a DC Drive (Chopper-Based)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Q1[Quadrant I Forward Motoring]
        Q1F[T positive, omega positive]
    end
    subgraph Q2[Quadrant II Forward Braking]
        Q2F[T negative, omega positive]
    end
    subgraph Q3[Quadrant III Reverse Motoring]
        Q3F[T negative, omega negative]
    end
    subgraph Q4[Quadrant IV Reverse Braking]
        Q4F[T positive, omega negative]
    end
    Q1 -->|Driver releases accelerator| Q2
    Q2 -->|Reverse gear engaged| Q3
    Q3 -->|Reverse brake applied| Q4
    Q4 -->|Reset to forward| Q1
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture ‚Äî Speed and Torque Control Loops

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ref[Reference Speed from Accelerator Pedal] --> sum1
    fb_speed[Tachogenerator Feedback] --> sum1
    sum1{Error e_omega} --> pi_outer[PI Controller Outer Loop]
    pi_outer --> i_ref[Torque Reference or Current Reference]
    i_ref --> sum2
    fb_cur[Current Sensor Feedback] --> sum2
    sum2{Error e_I} --> pi_inner[PI Controller Inner Loop]
    pi_inner --> pwm[PWM Generator]
    pwm --> chopper[IGBT Chopper]
    chopper --> motor[DC Motor]
    motor --> wheel[Vehicle Wheel]
    wheel --> fb_speed
    motor --> fb_cur
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Insight:** This is a **cascaded dual-loop PI structure** ‚Äî the gold standard for DC drives. The **outer (speed) loop** is slow (bandwidth ~10 Hz) and computes the torque demand. The **inner (current) loop** is fast (bandwidth ~1 kHz) and computes the actual PWM duty cycle. KTU questions often ask you to justify *why* two loops are used ‚Äî the answer is: **current must be limited to protect the chopper, motor windings, and battery from inrush**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 ‚Äî [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define back-EMF in a DC motor. Why is it called "back"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Back-EMF $E_b$ is the voltage induced in the armature conductors of a DC motor due to the rotation of the armature in the magnetic field (Faraday's law, 1 mark). It is governed by $E_b = K_e \phi \omega$ (1 mark). It is called "back" because, by Lenz's law, its polarity opposes the applied armature voltage $V_a$, thereby *opposing* the very current that produces the rotation (1 mark).

---

### Question 2 ‚Äî [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List any three advantages of separately excited DC motors over series DC motors for EV applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Independent control of flux and armature current** enables decoupled torque and speed control (1 mark).
2. **No risk of runaway speed** at no-load ‚Äî unlike a series motor, a separately excited motor has a finite no-load speed $\omega_0 = V_a / K\phi$ (1 mark).
3. **Regenerative braking is straightforward** ‚Äî by reducing $\phi$ below rated value, the motor acts as a generator over a wide speed range (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) ‚Äî Module Internal Choice

### Question A ‚Äî [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the main components of an **electric vehicle DC motor drive system**. Discuss the function of each block. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **speed equation** of a separately excited DC motor starting from Kirchhoff's Voltage Law. Hence, deduce the **no-load speed** and **stall torque** expressions. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (a) [7 Marks]

A DC motor drive system for an electric vehicle consists of **five major functional blocks**:

1. **Energy Source (Battery Pack)** ‚Äî A high-voltage DC battery (e.g., 400 V Li-ion) acts as the primary energy reservoir. **(1 mark)**
2. **Power Electronic Converter (DC-DC Chopper)** ‚Äî Uses IGBTs/MOSFETs to vary the average armature voltage. The **duty cycle** $\alpha$ of the chopper directly sets $V_a$. **(1.5 marks)**
3. **Controller (ECU / DSP)** ‚Äî Implements the speed and current control algorithms (PI/Fuzzy). Receives accelerator pedal input (0‚Äì5 V) and tachogenerator feedback. **(1.5 marks)**
4. **DC Motor (Series / Separately Excited / PMDC)** ‚Äî Converts regulated DC into rotary mechanical power. **(1 mark)**
5. **Sensors and Feedback Loop** ‚Äî Tachogenerator (speed), current shunt (armature current), and sometimes Hall-effect position sensor. **(1 mark)**
6. **Auxiliary Path: Regenerative Braking** ‚Äî During deceleration, the motor acts as a generator; the chopper is reconfigured to push current back into the battery. **(1 mark)**

---

### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 ‚Äî Write KVL for the armature circuit** (1 mark):

$$V_a = I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} + E_b$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Apply steady-state condition** (0.5 mark): $\dfrac{dI_a}{dt} = 0$, so:

$$V_a = I_a R_a + E_b$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Write back-EMF expression** (1 mark):

$$E_b = K_e \phi \omega$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Solve for $\omega$** (1 mark):

$$\omega = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_e \phi}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Substitute $I_a = T / K_t \phi$** (1 mark):

$$\omega = \frac{V_a}{K \phi} - \frac{R_a}{(K \phi)^2} T$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Deduce no-load speed** at $T = 0$ (1 mark):

$$\omega_0 = \frac{V_a}{K \phi}$$

**Step 7 ‚Äî Deduce stall torque** at $\omega = 0$ (1 mark):

$$T_{stall} = \frac{V_a \cdot K \phi}{R_a}$$

**Step 8 ‚Äî Final boxed result and physical interpretation** (0.5 mark):

$$\boxed{N = \frac{(V_a - I_a R_a) \times 60}{2 \pi K_e \phi}}$$

The speed is directly proportional to $V_a$ and inversely proportional to $\phi$, which leads to two control strategies: **armature voltage control** (constant $\phi$, vary $V_a$) and **flux control** (constant $V_a$, vary $\phi$).

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) ‚Äî [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **four-quadrant operation** of a DC motor drive used in electric vehicles. Sketch the torque-speed characteristics in each quadrant. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 220 V, 1500 rpm, 10 A separately excited DC motor has an armature resistance of 1 Œ©. The field current is held constant. Calculate: (i) the no-load speed if the armature voltage is reduced to 180 V; (ii) the speed when the motor draws 8 A at 200 V armature voltage. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (a) [7 Marks]

A vehicle drive system must operate in **all four quadrants** of the torque-speed plane to support forward/reverse motion and motoring/braking modes (1 mark).

**Quadrant I ‚Äî Forward Motoring** (2 marks): $\omega > 0$, $T > 0$. The chopper operates in step-down (buck) mode, supplying positive $V_a$ to drive the vehicle forward. Power flows from battery to motor (motoring mode).

**Quadrant II ‚Äî Forward Braking (Regenerative)** (2 marks): $\omega > 0$, $T < 0$. When the accelerator is released, the vehicle's kinetic energy rotates the motor above no-load speed, making $E_b > V_a$. Current reverses; the chopper is reconfigured to boost the generated voltage above battery voltage, returning energy to the battery.

**Quadrant III ‚Äî Reverse Motoring** (1 mark): $\omega < 0$, $T < 0$. Armature voltage polarity is reversed (H-bridge), causing reverse rotation. Used for reversing the vehicle.

**Quadrant IV ‚Äî Reverse Braking** (1 mark): $\omega < 0$, $T > 0$. Regenerative braking in reverse direction, energy returned to the battery.

---

### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**
- Rated voltage $V_{rated} = 220$ V
- Rated speed $N_{rated} = 1500$ rpm
- Rated current $I_{rated} = 10$ A
- Armature resistance $R_a = 1$ Œ©
- Field current is constant ‚Üí $\phi$ is constant

**Step 1 ‚Äî Find the back-EMF constant $K_e \phi$ at rated condition** (1 mark):

$$E_{b,rated} = V_a - I_a R_a = 220 - (10)(1) = 210 \text{ V}$$

$$K_e \phi = \frac{E_{b,rated} \cdot 60}{2 \pi N_{rated}} = \frac{210 \times 60}{2\pi \times 1500}$$

$$K_e \phi = \frac{12600}{9424.78} = 1.337 \text{ V¬∑s/rad}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Part (i): No-load speed at $V_a = 180$ V, $I_a \approx 0$** (2 marks):

At no-load, $I_a R_a \approx 0$, so $E_b \approx V_a$:

$$E_b = 180 \text{ V}$$

$$N_0 = \frac{180 \times 60}{2 \pi \times 1.337} = \frac{10800}{8.40} \approx 1285.7 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\boxed{N_0 \approx 1286 \text{ rpm}}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Part (ii): Speed at $V_a = 200$ V, $I_a = 8$ A** (3 marks):

$$E_b = V_a - I_a R_a = 200 - (8)(1) = 192 \text{ V}$$

Using $K_e \phi$ from Step 1 (since $\phi$ is unchanged):

$$N = \frac{192 \times 60}{2\pi \times 1.337} = \frac{11520}{8.40} \approx 1371.4 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 1371 \text{ rpm}}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Verification by ratio method** (1 mark): Since $\phi$ is constant, $N \propto E_b$:

$$N_2 = N_{rated} \times \frac{E_{b2}}{E_{b,rated}} = 1500 \times \frac{192}{210} = 1500 \times 0.9143 \approx 1371.4 \text{ rpm} \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ‚Äî Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert RPM to rad/s** when substituting into $E_b = K \phi \omega$. KVL gives you $E_b$ in Volts, not $\omega$ ‚Äî unit consistency is checked first. **(‚àí1 to ‚àí2 marks)**
> 2. **Confusing $K_e$ and $K_t$ units** ‚Äî write both side-by-side to avoid mistakes.
> 3. **Assuming $I_a = 0$ at no-load for a series motor** ‚Äî valid only for separately excited/shunt motors. In series motors, no-load $I_a$ is small but not zero, and the motor may *run away*.
> 4. **In four-quadrant questions**, failing to mark power flow direction arrows. Examiners expect arrows showing motoring (P from battery ‚Üí motor) and regenerative (P from motor ‚Üí battery). **(‚àí1 mark if missing)**
> 5. **Dropping the $60/(2\pi)$ conversion** in the final speed expression. The single most common KTU valuation deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for DC Motor Drives (Introduction):**

- **The Trinity of DC Motor Equations (must memorize):**
  - $V_a = I_a R_a + E_b$
  - $E_b = K_e \phi \omega$
  - $T = K_t \phi I_a$

- **In SI units, $K_e = K_t = K$ (numerically).** This is the single most-testable fact.

- **Two control strategies:** (1) **Armature voltage control** (vary $V_a$, constant $\phi$) ‚Äî used below base speed; (2) **Flux control** (vary $\phi$, constant $V_a$) ‚Äî used above base speed (field-weakening region).

- **No-load speed** of separately excited motor = $V_a / (K\phi)$. **Finite**, so no runaway risk.

- **Stall torque** = $V_a K \phi / R_a$. Very high because $R_a$ is small.

- **Series motor** torque $\propto I_a^2$ ‚Üí **excellent starting torque** for traction. Runaway risk at no-load.

- **Four-quadrant operation** is essential for any EV drive.

- **Regenerative braking** is possible by making $E_b > V_a$ ‚Äî current reverses, energy returns to battery.

- **Power flow:** $P_{in} = V_a I_a$ ‚Üí $P_{cu} = I_a^2 R_a$ ‚Üí $P_{gap} = E_b I_a = T \omega$ ‚Üí $P_{rotational\ losses}$ ‚Üí $P_{shaft}$.

- **Efficiency maximum** occurs when **armature copper loss = constant loss** (i.e., at ~50 % of no-load speed for a separately excited motor with constant loss).

- **Modern EV reality:** Most 2024 EVs use PMSM/AC induction, not DC. But DC drives are still used in **two-wheelers, e-rickshaws, golf carts, forklifts, and as a pedagogical foundation** for understanding all motor drives.

- **The DC motor drive is the "Hello World"** of electric vehicle propulsion ‚Äî every advanced AC drive ultimately implements the same torque-speed-decoupling philosophy, just with more complex mathematics (Clarke/Park transforms, FOC).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Configuration and Control of Separately Excited DC Motors, Motoring Using a PM DC Machine, DC Motor Drive Using DC-DC Converter, Generating/Braking Using a PM DC Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Separately Excited DC Motor ‚Äî Definition

A **Separately Excited DC (SEDC) Motor** is a direct-current machine in which the **field winding (stator)** and the **armature winding (rotor)** are electrically isolated and powered from **two independent DC voltage sources**. The field flux $\phi$ is controlled by varying the **field current $I_f$** through a separate field supply, while the armature current $I_a$ is independently regulated through an armature chopper or DC-DC converter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In HEV applications, the SEDC motor is one of the classical machines discussed under electric propulsion because its flux-armature independent control enables **constant-torque region** (below base speed) and **constant-power region** (above base speed) operation.

**Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The Tap and The Faucet:**
Imagine the **field winding** as the *water pressure* in a pipe, and the **armature** as the *faucet opening*. Both are controlled by separate handles. You can keep pressure (flux) high and turn the faucet slightly (low armature current) for slow, powerful flow ‚Äî that is high torque at low speed. Or, you can reduce pressure and open the faucet fully for fast, gentle flow ‚Äî high speed at low torque. This *decoupled control* is exactly what makes the SEDC motor beloved for traction.

## 1.2 Permanent Magnet (PM) DC Machine ‚Äî Definition

A **PM DC Machine** replaces the field winding and its DC supply with **permanent magnets** (typically NdFeB or SmCo) embedded in the stator iron. Because the magnets produce a *constant* flux $\phi = \text{const}$, only the **armature circuit** requires external electrical control. The same physical construction can operate as a **motor** (electrical ‚Üí mechanical) or as a **generator** (mechanical ‚Üí electrical), making it ideal for bidirectional HEV drives.

**Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The Wind-Up Toy Car:**
A PM DC motor is like a wind-up toy where the spring (the permanent magnet) is always at "full charge" ‚Äî you cannot turn it up or down. You only control how hard you push on the wheels (armature voltage). The harder you push, the faster it spins. Conversely, if you spin the wheels (by rolling downhill), the spring's energy is pushed *backwards* into the battery ‚Äî that's **regenerative braking**.

## 1.3 DC-DC Converter Drive ‚Äî Definition

A **DC-DC Converter** is a static electronic switching circuit (using MOSFETs/IGBTs and an inductor-capacitor filter) that converts a fixed DC source voltage $V_s$ (typically the HEV battery at **350 V** or **48 V**) into a **variable DC voltage** $V_a$ applied to the motor armature. The output-to-input voltage ratio is controlled by the **duty cycle $D = t_{on}/T$** of the switching signal.

> [!NOTE]
> In modern HEVs, **IGBT-based hard-switched converters** (1 kHz‚Äì20 kHz) and increasingly **SiC MOSFET converters** (>50 kHz) are used. For the KTU 2024 scheme, the student is expected to be conversant with **buck, boost, and two-quadrant chopper** configurations.

**Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The Accelerator Pedal of an EV:**
Think of a DC-DC converter as the **accelerator pedal** of an electric vehicle. The battery provides a fixed voltage (constant pedal position potential), but the *converter* decides how much of that voltage actually reaches the motor. Press the pedal harder (increase $D$) ‚Üí more voltage to the motor ‚Üí faster car. Release the pedal (decrease $D$) ‚Üí less voltage ‚Üí slower car. Lift off completely (reverse current) ‚Üí the motor becomes a generator charging the battery.

## 1.4 Generating / Braking Using a PM DC Machine

When the PM DC machine is driven by the vehicle's kinetic energy (e.g., during deceleration or downhill cruising), the armature EMF $E_b = K\phi\omega$ exceeds the applied terminal voltage $V_a$. The current direction **reverses**, and the machine delivers **electrical power back to the source** ‚Äî this is **regenerative braking**. The same machine simultaneously provides a **counter-torque** that decelerates the vehicle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constant:** The back-EMF constant $K\phi$ in a PM DC motor is **fixed** by the magnet material and geometry, and is typically in the range of **0.05 V¬∑s/rad to 0.5 V¬∑s/rad** depending on motor size.

**Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The Bicyclist on a Downhill:**
A cyclist freewheeling down a hill does not stop ‚Äî instead, the spinning wheel drives a small dynamo (the PM machine now acting as a generator), which both *slows the bike down* (counter-torque) and *charges a phone*. The same physics governs regenerative braking in HEVs.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Separately Excited DC Motor ‚Äî Operational Theory

### 2.1.1 Equivalent Circuit and Voltage Equation

The armature circuit and field circuit are magnetically coupled but electrically separate. Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** to the armature loop:

$$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$$

For steady-state analysis (no $dI_a/dt$), this simplifies to:

$$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$$

where:
- $V_a$ = applied armature voltage (V)
- $E_b$ = back-EMF (V)
- $I_a$ = armature current (A)
- $R_a$ = armature resistance ($\Omega$)

The back-EMF is governed by Faraday's law:

$$E_b = K \phi \omega$$

where $K$ is the machine constant, $\phi$ is the field flux per pole (Wb), and $\omega$ is the mechanical angular speed (rad/s).

### 2.1.2 Torque Equation

The electromagnetic torque developed by the armature is:

$$T_e = K \phi I_a$$

This is the most important equation ‚Äî it shows that torque is **directly proportional to armature current**, and field flux provides an independent "gain" multiplier.

### 2.1.3 Speed-Torque Characteristic

Combining the voltage and torque equations:

$$\omega = \frac{V_a}{K\phi} - \frac{R_a}{(K\phi)^2} T_e$$

This is a **linear relationship** with:
- **No-load speed**: $\omega_0 = V_a / (K\phi)$
- **Slope**: $-R_a / (K\phi)^2$

### 2.1.4 Two Operating Regions (Critical for HEV)

| Region | Control Variable | Speed Range | Torque Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Constant Torque** (below base speed $\omega_b$) | $V_a$ varied, $\phi$ held constant | $0 \to \omega_b$ | $T_e$ is maximum (limited by $I_{a,\max}$) |
| **Constant Power** (above base speed) | $V_a$ saturated at $V_{a,\max}$, $\phi$ weakened (Field Weakening) | $\omega_b \to \omega_{\max}$ | $T_e \propto 1/\omega$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two regions?** In an HEV, the motor must deliver **high torque for acceleration from standstill** and **high power for high-speed cruising**. SEDC motors achieve both by exploiting flux-weakening above base speed.

## 2.2 PM DC Machine ‚Äî Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 Equivalent Circuit

Since $\phi = \text{const}$ for PM machines, $K\phi$ is lumped into a single constant $K_m$ (in V¬∑s/rad, equivalent to N¬∑m/A).

$$V_a = K_m \omega + I_a R_a$$

$$T_e = K_m I_a$$

### 2.2.2 Speed-Torque Characteristic (Linear)

$$\omega = \frac{V_a}{K_m} - \frac{R_a}{K_m^2} T_e$$

This is **identical in form** to the SEDC motor equation, except $K\phi$ is now a **fixed constant** rather than a variable.

### 2.2.3 PM DC Machine ‚Äî Key Differences from SEDC

| Parameter | SEDC Motor | PM DC Motor |
|---|---|---|
| Field flux | Variable ($\phi$ controllable via $I_f$) | **Fixed** (by permanent magnet) |
| Field winding loss | Yes (copper loss $I_f^2 R_f$) | **Zero** (no field winding) |
| Size & Weight | Larger (separate field structure) | **Compact** (no field winding volume) |
| Efficiency | Lower (field $I^2R$ loss) | **Higher** |
| Control flexibility | Two degrees of freedom ($V_a, \phi$) | **One degree of freedom** ($V_a$ only) |
| Field weakening capability | Yes | **Limited / not possible** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** PM DC machines **cannot** perform field weakening. To extend the constant-power region, designers must use a *different* flux profile (e.g., interior PM with reluctance torque) ‚Äî but this is beyond the standard PM DC motor scope.

## 2.3 DC-DC Converter Drive Theory

### 2.3.1 Two-Quadrant Chopper (Most Common for HEV Traction)

A **two-quadrant chopper** allows operation in **motoring (forward motoring)** and **regenerative braking (forward generating)** quadrants. It uses **two switches** (typically IGBTs with anti-parallel diodes).

### 2.3.2 Operating Modes

**Mode 1 ‚Äî Motoring (Quadrant I):**
- Switch $T_1$ is active, switch $T_2$ is OFF.
- Armature voltage $V_a = +D \cdot V_s$ (where $D$ is duty cycle of $T_1$).
- Current $I_a > 0$, torque $T_e > 0$, vehicle accelerates.

**Mode 2 ‚Äî Regenerative Braking (Quadrant II):**
- Switch $T_2$ is active, switch $T_1$ is OFF.
- Armature sees negative average voltage $V_a = -(1-D) \cdot V_s$.
- Back-EMF $E_b$ drives current *back* into the source.

### 2.3.3 Converter Topology Equations

For a **buck-mode (step-down)** chopper driving a DC motor:

$$V_a = D \cdot V_s$$

where $D = t_{on} / T_s$ is the duty cycle ($0 \leq D \leq 1$).

For motoring operation, $0 < D < 1$, so $0 < V_a < V_s$.

### 2.3.4 Power Flow Analysis

**Motoring Mode:**
$$P_{elec} = V_s I_s = V_a I_a$$
$$P_{mech} = T_e \omega = E_b I_a$$
$$P_{loss} = I_a^2 R_a \quad (\text{armature copper loss})$$

**Generating Mode (Regenerative Braking):**
$$P_{mech,in} = T_e \omega = E_b I_a$$
$$P_{elec,out} = V_a I_a$$
$$P_{bat,charge} = V_s I_{s,charge}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write $P_{elec,out} = V_s I_s$ directly. The correct flow is: mechanical power $E_b I_a$ ‚Üí minus armature loss $I_a^2 R_a$ ‚Üí minus converter loss ‚Üí into the battery. Always draw an **energy flow diagram** in exam answers.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Armature KVL (steady state) | V |
| 2 | $E_b = K\phi \omega$ | Back-EMF equation | V |
| 3 | $T_e = K\phi I_a$ | Electromagnetic torque | N¬∑m |
| 4 | $\omega = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K\phi}$ | Speed (general) | rad/s |
| 5 | $\omega_0 = V_a / (K\phi)$ | No-load speed | rad/s |
| 6 | $\omega = \dfrac{V_a}{K\phi} - \dfrac{R_a}{(K\phi)^2} T_e$ | Linear speed-torque curve | rad/s |
| 7 | $P_{mech} = T_e \omega$ | Mechanical power developed | W |
| 8 | $P_{elec,in} = V_a I_a$ | Electrical input power (motor) | W |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{E_b I_a}{V_a I_a} = \dfrac{E_b}{V_a}$ | Efficiency (ignoring friction) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $V_a = D V_s$ | Buck chopper output voltage | V |
| 11 | $V_a = -(1-D) V_s$ | Boost-mode (regen) output voltage | V |
| 12 | $E_b^{regen} > V_a$ | Condition for regenerative mode | V |
| 13 | $I_a^{regen} = (E_b - V_a)/R_a$ | Current during regen braking | A |
| 14 | $K\phi = \text{const}$ | For PM DC motor (fixed flux) | V¬∑s/rad |
| 15 | $T_{brake} = K\phi I_a^{regen}$ | Braking torque | N¬∑m |
| 16 | $E_{kinetic} = \frac{1}{2} M v^2$ | Vehicle kinetic energy | J |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Parameter Ranges in HEV PM DC Motors:** $K\phi \approx 0.1$‚Äì$0.3$ V¬∑s/rad, $R_a \approx 0.01$‚Äì$0.1$ $\Omega$, rated power 5‚Äì100 kW, rated speed 1500‚Äì6000 rpm.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Electric Two-Wheelers (e-bikes, e-rickshaws):** Use PM DC motors with simple chopper control for low-cost, high-reliability urban mobility.
- **Small HEVs (e.g., Mild-Hybrid 48 V systems):** Employ belt-driven PM DC starter-generators (ISG ‚Äî Integrated Starter Generator).
- **Forklifts and Industrial EVs:** Historically dominated by series-wound and separately excited DC motors due to high starting torque requirements.
- **Solar Water Pumping:** PM DC motors with buck/boost converters directly coupled to photovoltaic arrays.
- **Educational and Hobby Robotics:** PM DC motors remain the standard because of their **linear, predictable** torque-speed behavior.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚Äî Back-EMF Equation from Faraday's Law

### Statement
Derive the expression for back-EMF $E_b$ in a DC motor.

### Given
- A conductor of length $l$ (m) moving with velocity $v$ (m/s) perpendicular to a magnetic field of flux density $B$ (T).

### Derivation

**Step 1 ‚Äî Force on a current-carrying conductor:**
When a conductor carries current $I_a$ in a magnetic field $B$, it experiences a force:

$$F = B \cdot l \cdot I_a$$

This force acts tangentially on the armature conductors.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Conversion to torque:**
If the conductors are placed at a mean radius $r$ from the axis, the torque produced per conductor is:

$$T_{per\_cond} = F \cdot r = B \cdot l \cdot I_a \cdot r$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Sum over all conductors:**
For $Z$ total armature conductors arranged in $A$ parallel paths, the total torque is:

$$T_e = \frac{Z}{2\pi A} \cdot \phi \cdot I_a \cdot 2\pi = \frac{Z \phi}{2\pi A} \cdot (2\pi) \cdot I_a = \frac{Z\phi}{A} \cdot I_a \cdot p$$

where $p$ is the number of poles and $\phi$ is the flux per pole. Combining all geometric constants into a single machine constant $K$:

$$T_e = K\phi I_a \quad \text{...(3.1)}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Power balance for back-EMF:**
The mechanical power developed equals electrical power converted:

$$P_{mech} = T_e \omega = E_b I_a$$

Substituting equation (3.1):

$$K\phi I_a \omega = E_b I_a$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Solving for $E_b$:**

$$\boxed{E_b = K\phi\omega}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The torque equation was derived using the Lorentz force law, then we invoked the **principle of conservation of energy** (mechanical power out = electrical power converted) to find the back-EMF without re-doing Faraday's law explicitly. This is the standard textbook approach.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚Äî Steady-State Voltage Equation

### Statement
Derive the steady-state armature voltage equation from Kirchhoff's Voltage Law.

### Derivation

**Step 1 ‚Äî Write KVL around the armature loop:**
The applied armature voltage $V_a$ must overcome the back-EMF $E_b$ (which opposes $V_a$ by Lenz's law) and the resistive drop $I_a R_a$:

$$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Substitute back-EMF expression:**

$$V_a = K\phi\omega + I_a R_a$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Solve for armature current:**

$$I_a = \frac{V_a - K\phi\omega}{R_a} \quad \text{...(3.2)}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Solve for angular velocity:**

$$\boxed{\omega = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K\phi}} \quad \text{...(3.3)}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This equation is the **fundamental speed-control equation** for any DC motor. Notice that for an SEDC motor, we can independently vary $V_a$ (armature control) or $K\phi$ (field control) to change $\omega$ ‚Äî but for a PM DC motor, only $V_a$ can be varied.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚Äî Speed-Torque Linear Characteristic

### Statement
Derive the linear speed-torque relationship of a DC motor.

### Derivation

**Step 1 ‚Äî Start with the speed equation (3.3):**

$$\omega = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K\phi}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Solve equation (3.1) for $I_a$:**

$$I_a = \frac{T_e}{K\phi}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Substitute $I_a$ into the speed equation:**

$$\omega = \frac{V_a}{K\phi} - \frac{R_a}{K\phi} \cdot \frac{T_e}{K\phi}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Simplify:**

$$\boxed{\omega = \frac{V_a}{K\phi} - \frac{R_a}{(K\phi)^2} T_e}$$

This is of the form $y = c - mx$, where:
- **y-intercept** (no-load speed): $\omega_0 = V_a/(K\phi)$
- **Slope**: $-R_a/(K\phi)^2$
- **x-intercept** (stall torque): $T_{stall} = V_a K\phi / R_a$

> **Conversion Logic:** A high $K\phi$ (strong field) gives **low no-load speed** but **flat slope** (good speed regulation). A low $K\phi$ gives high no-load speed but steep slope. This is precisely why field weakening in SEDC motors is used above base speed.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ‚Äî Two-Quadrant Chopper Output Voltage

### Statement
Derive the average output voltage of a two-quadrant chopper driving a DC motor in motoring mode.

### Derivation

**Step 1 ‚Äî Chopper switch states:**

| Interval | Switch $T_1$ | Switch $T_2$ | Motor Terminal Voltage |
|---|---|---|---|
| $0 < t < D T_s$ | ON | OFF | $V_s$ |
| $D T_s < t < T_s$ | OFF | ON (freewheel) | $0$ |

**Step 2 ‚Äî Average voltage over one switching period $T_s$:**

$$V_a = \frac{1}{T_s}\int_0^{T_s} v_a(t)\, dt = \frac{1}{T_s}\left[\int_0^{DT_s} V_s\, dt + \int_{DT_s}^{T_s} 0\, dt\right]$$

$$V_a = \frac{1}{T_s}\left[V_s \cdot D T_s\right] = D V_s$$

$$\boxed{V_a = D V_s} \quad \text{...(3.4)}$$

where $0 \leq D \leq 1$.

**Step 3 ‚Äî Regenerative mode:** When the chopper operates in the second quadrant, switch $T_2$ is modulated and the average armature voltage becomes:

$$V_a = -(1-D)V_s$$

The negative sign indicates the polarity reversal that supports current flow from motor back to source.

> **Conversion Logic:** The *same* physical chopper can deliver $+V_s$ or $-V_s$ averaged over a switching period, simply by choosing which switch is modulated. This is the elegance of two-quadrant choppers in EV traction.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 ‚Äî Regenerative Braking Condition and Current

### Statement
Derive the condition for regenerative braking and the resulting braking current.

### Derivation

**Step 1 ‚Äî Start with armature KVL during generation:**

When the machine is driven mechanically (e.g., by vehicle inertia), $E_b$ becomes the *source*. Applying KVL with reversed current direction:

$$E_b = V_a + I_a R_a$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Solve for current:**

$$\boxed{I_a = \frac{E_b - V_a}{R_a}} \quad \text{...(3.5)}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Condition for positive (regenerative) current:**

For $I_a > 0$ (current flowing *into* the battery), we require:

$$\boxed{E_b > V_a}$$

Substituting $E_b = K\phi\omega$:

$$K\phi\omega > V_a$$

For a PM DC motor, $K\phi$ is constant, so the condition becomes:

$$\omega > \frac{V_a}{K\phi} = \omega_0$$

> **Interpretation:** Regenerative braking is only possible when the motor speed **exceeds its no-load speed** at the applied voltage. Below no-load speed, the machine cannot regenerate ‚Äî this is called the **natural commutation failure** of simple chopper-controlled PM DC drives.

**Step 4 ‚Äî Braking torque:**

The electromagnetic torque during regeneration acts opposite to motion:

$$T_{brake} = K\phi I_a = \frac{K\phi(E_b - V_a)}{R_a} = \frac{(K\phi)^2\omega - K\phi V_a}{R_a}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Power recovered to battery:**

$$P_{regen} = V_a \cdot I_a = V_a \cdot \frac{E_b - V_a}{R_a}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The power recovered is **not** simply $E_b I_a$ ‚Äî it is the power *after* subtracting armature copper losses. This is a common KTU exam trap.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Operating Quadrants of a PM DC Drive

| Quadrant | Operation Mode | $V_a$ Sign | $I_a$ Sign | $\omega$ Sign | $T_e$ Sign | Energy Flow |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **I (Forward Motoring)** | Acceleration | $+$ | $+$ | $+$ | $+$ | Battery ‚Üí Motor ‚Üí Wheels |
| **II (Forward Regenerative Braking)** | Deceleration | $+$ (or small $-$) | $-$ | $+$ | $-$ | Wheels ‚Üí Motor ‚Üí Battery |
| **III (Reverse Motoring)** | Reverse acceleration | $-$ | $-$ | $-$ | $-$ | Battery ‚Üí Motor ‚Üí Wheels |
| **IV (Reverse Regenerative Braking)** | Reverse deceleration | $-$ | $+$ | $-$ | $+$ | Wheels ‚Üí Motor ‚Üí Battery |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** Modern EV drives use **four-quadrant choppers** (H-bridge topology) to access all four quadrants. The PM DC machine, because of its symmetric construction, is naturally suited to four-quadrant operation.

---

## 3.7 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Typical Exam Problem)

**Problem:** A separately excited DC motor has $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$, $K\phi = 1.5$ V¬∑s/rad, and is supplied by a chopper with $V_s = 200$ V at duty cycle $D = 0.6$. The motor drives a constant-torque load of $T_L = 50$ N¬∑m. Find:
1. Armature voltage $V_a$
2. Steady-state speed $\omega$
3. Armature current $I_a$
4. Developed power $P_{mech}$

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Armature voltage:**
From equation (3.4):
$$V_a = D V_s = 0.6 \times 200 = 120\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Armature current from torque:**
From equation (3.1):
$$I_a = \frac{T_e}{K\phi} = \frac{50}{1.5} = 33.33\ \text{A}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Steady-state speed:**
From equation (3.3):
$$\omega = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K\phi} = \frac{120 - (33.33)(0.5)}{1.5} = \frac{120 - 16.67}{1.5} = \frac{103.33}{1.5}$$

$$\boxed{\omega = 68.89\ \text{rad/s} \approx 658\ \text{rpm}}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Mechanical power developed:**
$$P_{mech} = T_e \omega = 50 \times 68.89 = 3444.4\ \text{W} \approx 3.44\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Verification (electrical input):**
$$P_{elec} = V_a I_a = 120 \times 33.33 = 4000\ \text{W} = 4.0\ \text{kW}$$
$$P_{loss} = I_a^2 R_a = (33.33)^2 \times 0.5 = 555.6\ \text{W}$$
$$P_{mech} = P_{elec} - P_{loss} = 4000 - 555.6 = 3444.4\ \text{W} \quad \checkmark$$

> **Conversion Logic Check:** Power balance confirms that mechanical power = electrical input minus armature copper loss. This consistency check should always be done in KTU numericals.

```mermaid
    Airgap --> Emf
    Emf -->|T sub e| Shaft[Mechanical Shaft]

```

> **Interpretation:** Notice the **two independent control loops**: (1) armature current/speed loop, and (2) field flux loop. The ECU (Electronic Control Unit) commands both duty cycles $D_a$ and $D_f$. This is the hallmark of separately excited DC drives.

---

## 4.2 PM DC Machine Drive with Two-Quadrant Chopper

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PowerStage[Power Stage]
        Vsub[DC Battery V sub s] --- LA[Inductor L]
        LA --- SwitchT1[IGBT T1 top]
        SwitchT1 --- SwitchT2[IGBT T2 bottom]

        SwitchT1 --- DA[Freewheel Diode D1]
        DA --- GroundNode
        SwitchT2 --- DB[Diode D2 anti parallel]
        DB --- SwitchT1

        MotorTerm --- SwitchT2
    end

    subgraph MotorModel[PM DC Machine Model]
        RaTerm[Armature R sub a] --- Eb[Back EMF K phi omega]

    end
```
