
> **Interpretation:** The two IGBTs $T_1$ (top) and $T_2$ (bottom) form a **half-bridge leg**. When $T_1$ is ON, the motor is connected to $+V_s$ (motoring). When $T_2$ is ON, the motor is short-circuited through $T_2$ (freewheel) or via $D_2$ during regen (current returns to source).

---

## 4.3 Operating Mode State Diagram (PM DC Drive)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Stopped : Initial State
    Stopped --> Motoring : Throttle Applied V sub a greater than zero
    Motoring --> Motoring : D in 0 to 1 Increasing Speed
    Motoring --> Freewheel : Throttle Released T1 OFF T2 ON
    Freewheel --> Motoring : Throttle Reapplied
    Motoring --> RegenBraking : Brake Applied OR Downhill
    RegenBraking --> Stopped : Vehicle Halts omega less than min regen
    RegenBraking --> Freewheel : Brake Released
    Freewheel --> Stopped : Brake Applied to Zero Speed
    Stopped --> ReverseMotoring : Reverse Gear Selected V sub a negative
    ReverseMotoring --> RegenReverse : Reverse Brake
    RegenReverse --> Stopped

    note right of Motoring : Quadrant I  Forward Acceleration\nV sub a and I sub a positive
    note right of RegenBraking : Quadrant II  Forward Braking\nI sub a negative  E sub b greater than V sub a
    note right of Freewheel : Coasting No torque\nSwitch T2 ON shorts motor
    note right of ReverseMotoring : Quadrant III  Reverse Drive\nV sub a and I sub a negative
```

---

## 4.4 Energy Flow Block Diagram ‚Äî Motoring vs. Regenerative Braking

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Motoring[Quadrant I  Motoring]
        BatM[Battery V sub s I sub s] -- V sub a I sub a --> ArmM[Armature R sub a]
        ArmM -- I squared R sub a Loss --> LossM[Heat Sink]
        ArmM -- E sub b I sub a --> ConvM[Converter]
        ConvM -- T sub e omega --> ShaftM[Mechanical Shaft to Wheels]
        ShaftM -- Vehicle Motion --> KEM[K E 0.5 M v squared]
    end

    subgraph Braking[Quadrant II  Regenerative Braking]
        KEB[K E 0.5 M v squared] -- Vehicle Decel --> ShaftB[Mechanical Shaft from Wheels]
        ShaftB -- T sub e omega --> ConvB[Generator Action]
        ConvB -- E sub b I sub a --> ArmB[Armature R sub a]
        ArmB -- I squared R sub a Loss --> LossB[Heat]
        ArmB -- V sub a I sub a --> BatB[Battery Receiving Charge]
    end

    classDef energy fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef conv fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class BatM,ArmM,ShaftM,KEM,KEB,ShaftB,ArmB,BatB energy
    class LossM,LossB loss
    class ConvM,ConvB conv
```

> **Interpretation:** This diagram emphasizes that during regenerative braking, the **vehicle's kinetic energy** is the source ‚Äî the road wheels act as the prime mover. The motor operates as a generator, but the armature still dissipates $I_a^2 R_a$ as heat (this loss is unavoidable).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 ‚Äî `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"Differentiate between a Separately Excited DC Motor and a Permanent Magnet DC Motor. List two advantages of PM DC motors in HEV applications."**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Separately Excited DC Motor | PM DC Motor |
|---|---|---|
| Field flux source | Electromagnetic winding (requires separate DC source) | Permanent magnets (NdFeB / SmCo) |
| Flux control | Variable ($I_f$ adjustable) | Fixed (non-controllable) |
| Field copper loss | Present ($I_f^2 R_f$) | Absent |
| Construction | Bulky (separate field structure) | Compact and lightweight |
| Control flexibility | Two degrees of freedom ($V_a$ + flux) | One degree of freedom ($V_a$ only) |
| Cost | Higher (extra field supply and chopper) | Lower (simpler drive electronics) |

**Two advantages of PM DC motors in HEVs:**
1. **Higher efficiency** ‚Äî absence of field copper loss improves overall drive efficiency, extending vehicle range.
2. **Higher power density** ‚Äî absence of field winding volume reduces motor weight and size, critical for vehicle packaging.

*(1 mark for differentiation table, 2 marks for advantages with reasoning.)*

---

### Question A2 ‚Äî `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"State the condition for regenerative braking in a PM DC motor drive. Why is regenerative braking not possible below a certain motor speed?"**

**Model Answer:**

**Condition for regenerative braking:**
The back-EMF must exceed the applied armature voltage:
$$E_b > V_a \implies K\phi\omega > V_a$$

**Reason below a certain speed regen is not possible:**
At low motor speeds, $E_b = K\phi\omega$ is small. To push current *back* into the battery, $E_b$ must be greater than $V_a$. If $\omega < V_a / K\phi = \omega_0$ (no-load speed), the back-EMF cannot overcome the applied voltage, and current cannot reverse. Hence, **regenerative braking has a minimum speed threshold** $\omega_{min} = V_a / (K\phi)$.

In modern EVs, this minimum speed is typically 10‚Äì15 km/h. Below this, **mechanical friction brakes** take over.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ‚Äî Internal Choice

### Question B-A (14 Marks) ‚Äî `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**"Explain the configuration and control of a Separately Excited DC Motor used as an HEV traction motor. Discuss (a) the constant-torque and constant-power operating regions, and (b) the role of armature and field choppers in speed control. Derive the steady-state speed equation and justify the field-weakening requirement above base speed."**

#### Part (a) ‚Äî Configuration and Two Operating Regions (7 Marks)

**Configuration:**
The separately excited DC motor consists of two electrically isolated windings:
- **Field winding (stator):** Receives $V_f$ from a field chopper, producing flux $\phi \propto I_f$.
- **Armature winding (rotor):** Receives $V_a$ from an armature chopper, producing torque $T_e = K\phi I_a$.

Both windings are supplied from the **same DC bus** (HEVs typically use 350 V or 48 V), but through **independent choppers** controlled by the ECU via PWM.

**Two Operating Regions:**

**Region 1 ‚Äî Constant Torque Region ($0 \leq \omega \leq \omega_b$):**
- Flux $\phi$ is held at rated value $\phi_{rated}$ (i.e., $I_f$ at rated value).
- Armature voltage $V_a$ is varied from 0 to $V_{a,max}$ to control speed.
- Armature current $I_a$ is limited to $I_{a,max}$ (by current controller) ‚Üí maximum torque $T_{max} = K\phi_{rated} I_{a,max}$ is held constant.
- **Use case:** Launch, hill climbing, low-speed acceleration.

**Region 2 ‚Äî Constant Power Region ($\omega_b \leq \omega \leq \omega_{max}$):**
- Armature voltage is saturated at $V_{a,max}$.
- Flux $\phi$ is *reduced* by reducing $I_f$ below rated value ‚Äî this is **field weakening**.
- Since $T_e = K\phi I_a$ and $I_a$ is also limited, reducing $\phi$ reduces torque.
- Mechanical power $P = T_e \omega \approx \text{constant}$ because $T_e \propto 1/\omega$.
- **Use case:** High-speed cruising on highways.

#### Part (b) ‚Äî Speed Equation Derivation and Field Weakening Justification (7 Marks)

**Derivation of Steady-State Speed Equation:**
Applying KVL to the armature circuit:

$$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a = K\phi\omega + I_a R_a$$

Solving for $\omega$:

$$\boxed{\omega = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K\phi}}$$

**Justification for Field Weakening:**

At **base speed** $\omega_b$, the armature voltage reaches $V_{a,max}$. Beyond this point, $V_a$ cannot be increased further (limited by battery voltage and chopper rating). From the speed equation:

$$\omega = \frac{V_{a,max} - I_a R_a}{K\phi}$$

If $\phi$ is held constant, the only way to increase $\omega$ further is by reducing $I_a$. But in traction, $I_a$ corresponds to torque demand. Reducing $I_a$ arbitrarily is not possible because the driver demands a certain torque.

**Solution ‚Äî Field Weakening:**
By reducing $\phi$ (via lower $I_f$), the denominator $K\phi$ decreases, allowing $\omega$ to increase above $\omega_b$ even at the same $V_a$ and $I_a$.

The base speed is:
$$\omega_b = \frac{V_{a,max} - I_a R_a}{K\phi_{rated}}$$

The maximum speed under field weakening (with $\phi \to \phi_{min}$):
$$\omega_{max} = \frac{V_{a,max} - I_a R_a}{K\phi_{min}}$$

**Speed ratio:**
$$\frac{\omega_{max}}{\omega_b} = \frac{\phi_{rated}}{\phi_{min}} \quad (\text{typically } 2:1 \text{ to } 4:1)$$

This field-weakening capability is the **principal advantage** of SEDC motors over PM DC motors in HEV traction, and the reason SEDC drives dominated early electric vehicles (e.g., Toyota RAV4 EV first generation, General Motors EV1).

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) ‚Äî `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` *(ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)*

**"With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a PM DC motor drive using a DC-DC converter for HEV applications. Discuss (a) the motoring mode operation with armature voltage control, and (b) the regenerative braking mode with detailed power flow analysis. Derive expressions for the average armature voltage in both modes."**

#### Part (a) ‚Äî Motoring Mode with Armature Voltage Control (7 Marks)

**Circuit Description:**
The PM DC motor drive consists of:
- **DC source** (battery $V_s$).
- **Two-quadrant chopper** with two switches $T_1$ (IGBT/MOSFET) and $T_2$, plus two freewheeling diodes $D_1$ and $D_2$.
- **PM DC motor** with armature resistance $R_a$ and back-EMF $E_b = K\phi\omega$.

**Operation in Motoring Mode (Quadrant I):**
- ECU modulates $T_1$ with duty cycle $D$.
- During $0 < t < DT_s$: $T_1$ is ON, motor terminal is connected to $+V_s$. Current $i_a$ rises through $L_a$ and $R_a$.
- During $DT_s < t < T_s$: $T_1$ is OFF. The inductive current freewheels through $D_2$ and $T_2$ (or $D_2$ alone). Motor terminal is short-circuited (effectively $V_a = 0$ during this interval).
- **Average armature voltage:**

$$V_a = D \cdot V_s \quad \text{...(B.1)}$$

- The motor develops torque $T_e = K\phi I_a$, and the mechanical power is $P_{mech} = T_e \omega = K\phi\omega I_a$.
- **Power flow:** Battery $\to$ Chopper $\to$ Armature $\to$ Mechanical Shaft $\to$ Wheels.

**Derivation of $V_a = DV_s$:**

During $DT_s$, $v_a(t) = V_s$. During $(1-D)T_s$, $v_a(t) = 0$.

$$V_a = \frac{1}{T_s} \int_0^{T_s} v_a(t)\,dt = \frac{1}{T_s} \left[ V_s \cdot DT_s + 0 \cdot (1-D)T_s \right] = DV_s$$

#### Part (b) ‚Äî Regenerative Braking Mode with Power Flow Analysis (7 Marks)

**Condition for Regeneration:**
The motor must spin fast enough that $E_b > V_a$. In a moving vehicle decelerating from speed, $\omega$ is naturally high, satisfying this condition.

**Operation:**
- ECU turns OFF $T_1$ and modulates $T_2$ with duty cycle $D_{reg}$.
- During $DT_s$: $T_2$ is ON. Motor terminal is connected to ground (0 V). The back-EMF drives current *upwards* through the motor and into the inductor, storing energy.
- During $(1-D)T_s$: $T_2$ is OFF. The inductor releases current through $D_1$ into the *positive* battery terminal ‚Äî current flows back to the battery.

**Average armature voltage during regen:**
$$V_a = -(1-D) V_s \quad \text{...(B.2)}$$

The negative sign indicates that the effective voltage applied to the motor is opposite to the motoring case. The **magnitude** $|V_a| = (1-D)V_s$ is controlled to limit the regenerative current and braking torque.

**Regenerative Current:**
$$I_{a,regen} = \frac{E_b - V_a}{R_a} = \frac{K\phi\omega - V_a}{R_a}$$

**Braking Torque:**
$$T_{brake} = K\phi I_{a,regen} = \frac{K\phi(E_b - V_a)}{R_a}$$

**Power Flow Analysis:**

| Power Term | Expression | Value (Typical Example) |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanical power input | $P_{mech} = T_e \omega = E_b I_a$ | 10 kW |
| Armature copper loss | $P_{cu} = I_a^2 R_a$ | 500 W |
| Electrical power generated | $P_{gen} = P_{mech} - P_{cu} = V_a I_a$ | 9.5 kW |
| Chopper switching loss | $P_{sw}$ | 100 W |
| Net power to battery | $P_{bat} = P_{gen} - P_{sw}$ | 9.4 kW |
| **Regeneration efficiency** | $\eta_{regen} = P_{bat}/P_{mech}$ | **94%** |

**Derivation of Equation (B.2):**

During the *ON* time of $T_2$ (interval $DT_s$), the motor terminal is at $0$ V (ground). During the *OFF* time of $T_2$ (interval $(1-D)T_s$), the inductor current freewheels through $D_1$, applying $+V_s$ to the motor terminal *in the reverse polarity*. The average armature voltage:

$$V_a = \frac{1}{T_s}\left[0 \cdot DT_s + (-V_s)(1-D)T_s\right] = -(1-D)V_s$$

**Conclusion:** The same physical chopper can deliver $+DV_s$ (motoring) or $-(1-D)V_s$ (regen) simply by switching which IGBT is modulated. This bidirectional power flow is essential for HEV regenerative braking systems.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing motoring and generating KVL signs:** Many students write $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$ for *both* motoring and generating. In regen, it must be $E_b = V_a + I_a R_a$ (i.e., $E_b$ is the source). Marks lost: **2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $I_a^2 R_a$ loss in regen power calculation:** Saying "regenerated power = $E_b I_a$" ignores armature copper loss. Correct: $P_{bat} = V_a I_a$ (after the armature loss is subtracted). Marks lost: **2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Assuming PM motors can do field weakening:** PM DC motors have **fixed flux**. Stating that flux can be reduced in a PM DC motor is a fundamental conceptual error. Marks lost: **2‚Äì3 marks**.
>
> 4. **Not stating the regen condition $E_b > V_a$:** Any regen-mode answer without this condition is incomplete. Marks lost: **1‚Äì2 marks**.
>
> 5. **Drawing the chopper circuit incorrectly:** The two IGBTs in a half-bridge must be drawn with their **anti-parallel diodes** clearly shown, and the freewheeling path during each interval must be mentioned. Marks lost: **2 marks**.
>
> 6. **Skipping the energy/power balance verification:** KTU examiners *love* asking students to verify $P_{mech} + P_{loss} = P_{elec}$. Omitting this consistency check costs 1 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ‚Äî Module 2 (DC Motor Drives)**

### Core Equations (Must Memorize)
- ‚òê $V_a = K\phi\omega + I_a R_a$
- ‚òê $E_b = K\phi\omega$
- ‚òê $T_e = K\phi I_a$
- ‚òê $\omega = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K\phi}$
- ‚òê $V_a = DV_s$ (motoring) and $V_a = -(1-D)V_s$ (regen)
- ‚òê $I_{regen} = (E_b - V_a)/R_a$
- ‚òê $\omega_{no-load} = V_a/(K\phi)$

### Conceptual Differentiators
- ‚òê **SEDC**: Two independent control inputs ($V_a$ and $\phi$) ‚Üí two operating regions (constant torque + constant power via field weakening).
- ‚òê **PM DC**: Single control input ($V_a$ only) ‚Üí single linear torque-speed region, no field weakening, but higher efficiency.
- ‚òê **Two-quadrant chopper**: Single IGBT leg ($T_1$, $T_2$, $D_1$, $D_2$) ‚Üí handles both motoring and regen.
- ‚òê **Four-quadrant chopper** (H-bridge): Required for forward/reverse motoring + regen.

### Critical Conditions
- ‚òê **Regen possible only if** $E_b > V_a$, i.e., $\omega > V_a/(K\phi)$.
- ‚òê **Below base speed** $\omega_b$: armature control ($V_a$ varied, $\phi$ fixed).
- ‚òê **Above base speed**: field weakening ($\phi$ reduced, $V_a$ saturated).

### Power Flow Facts
- ‚òê Motor: $P_{in} = V_a I_a$, $P_{out} = T_e \omega = E_b I_a$, $P_{loss} = I_a^2 R_a$.
- ‚òê Generator (regen): $P_{mech,in} = E_b I_a$, $P_{out} = V_a I_a$, $P_{loss} = I_a^2 R_a$.
- ‚òê **Efficiency of DC motor** (idealized): $\eta = E_b / V_a$.

### HEV-Specific Notes
- ‚òê PM DC motors are preferred for **low-voltage 48 V mild-hybrid** systems due to compact size and high efficiency.
- ‚òê SEDC motors dominated **early HEV traction** (e.g., EV1) due to wide constant-power range via field weakening.
- ‚òê Modern HEVs use **PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor)** or **induction motors**, but PM DC remains relevant for **low-cost, low-power** applications (e-bikes, e-rickshaws, ISG).
- ‚òê **Buck-mode chopper** is standard for traction: $V_a = DV_s < V_s$.
- ‚òê **Boost-mode chopper** is used when motor rated voltage > battery voltage (rare in HEVs but common in solar EVs).

### Numerical Problem Solving Hints
- ‚òê Always write down the KVL equation first.
- ‚òê Identify the operating quadrant (motoring vs. regen) before solving.
- ‚òê Use $\omega$ in rad/s (not rpm) in equations. Convert at the end: $N_{rpm} = \omega \times 60/(2\pi)$.
- ‚òê Verify power balance as a sanity check.
- ‚òê Standard SI units: V, A, $\Omega$, Wb, rad/s, N¬∑m, W.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Induction Motor Drives: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Induction Motor Drives: Introduction # MODULE 2 ‚Äî INDUCTION MOTOR DRIVES: INTRODUCTION ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition ‚Äî Induction Motor (IM) Drive for EV Application:** > An **Induction Motor Drive** is an electromechanical propulsion system in which a three-phase (or single-phase) AC induction machine‚Äîtypically the **squirrel-cage rotor** type‚Äîis electronically commutated and torque-controlled by a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) / Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) to deliver the variable torque‚Äìspeed characteristics demanded by the road-load profile of an Electric or Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV). The induction machine operates on the principle of **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** and **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. A rotating stator MMF (magnetomotive force) cuts the short-circuited rotor conductors and induces currents in them (Lenz's law). The interaction between the induced rotor currents and the stator RMF produces the **Lorentz force ($F = BIL$)**, which manifests as electromagnetic torque ($T_e$) on the rotor. In an EV, the induction motor is *never* started direct-on-line (DOL). Instead, it is fed from a **DC-link inverter** that synthesises a sinusoidal PWM (SPWM) or SVPWM voltage whose **frequency ($f_s$) and voltage ($V_s$) are jointly varied** to maintain a constant air-gap flux ($\Phi$). This combined $V/f$ control scheme makes the IM a true "Variable Speed Drive" (VSD). **Key Standard Metrics used in EV-grade IM Drives:** - **Synchronous speed ($N_s$)** in **rpm** - **Slip ($s$)** ‚Äî dimensionless, typically **0.01 to 0.05** for EV operation - **Per-phase supply frequency ($f_s$)** in **Hz (50/60 Hz base, varied 0‚Äì200 Hz in EV)** - **Air-gap flux density ($B_g$) ‚âà 0.7 T to 0.9 T** in modern EV motors - **Number of poles ($P$)** ‚Äî always an **even integer (2, 4, 6, 8)** in traction IM ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ‚Äî "The Spinning Tornado of a Washing Machine" > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî "The Invisible Whirlpool":** > Imagine a giant, invisible "tornado" of magnetism spinning inside the motor housing (this is the **stator's rotating magnetic field**). The rotor is like a small metal squirrel cage sitting in the middle of this tornado. The tornado *drags* the squirrel cage along with it ‚Äî not because the wind pushes the bars directly, but because the changing magnetic field "induces" swirling currents in the closed-cage bars (like eddy currents), and these currents, trying to *oppose* the change (Lenz's law), get pulled along by the tornado. The rotor can never quite catch up with the tornado ‚Äî this small "lag" is called **slip ($s$)**, and *without slip, there is no induced current, and hence no torque*. That is the essence of an induction motor! For an EV, imagine you are the *driver* (the VFD/inverter). You can change the speed of the tornado (by changing the frequency) and its strength (by changing the voltage). By adjusting both together, you keep the tornado's strength per unit area (the air-gap flux) **constant**, so the squirrel-cage rotor never loses its grip. This is the **$V/f$ control** strategy used in every Tesla Model S, X, 3 and Y. ### 1.3 Why Induction Motors in Electric Vehicles? > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight ‚Äî Tesla's Traction Motor Choice:** > Tesla Motors (under the technical leadership of **JB Straubel** and **Elon Musk**) famously chose **3-Phase AC Squirrel-Cage Induction Motors** for the **Tesla Roadster (2008), Model S (2012), Model X (2015)** and **Model 3 (2017)**. The deciding factors were: > 1. **No rare-earth permanent magnets** (unlike PMSM) ‚Äî cheaper, geopolitically safer. > 2. **Rotor robustness** ‚Äî rotor can withstand **> 18,000 RPM** without structural failure (no magnets to fly apart). > 3. **Excellent field-weakening** ‚Äî natural constant-power region for high-speed cruising. > 4. **Liquid-cooling friendly** ‚Äî uniform copper cage dissipates heat isotropically. > 5. **Lower cost per kW** than PMSM or SRM. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Block Decomposition The complete EV traction drive chain consists of **four** interacting subsystems. Understanding their handshakes is critical for KTU Module-2 answers: - **Step 1 ‚Äî Battery Pack (DC Source):** A high-voltage Li-ion pack (typically **400 V** in EVs like Tata Nexon EV, **800 V** in Hyundai E-GMP, Porsche Taycan) provides the **DC link voltage ($V_{DC}$)**. - **Step 2 ‚Äî DC-Link Capacitor Bank:** A large electrolytic/film capacitor ($C \approx 1$‚Äì$2$ mF) smooths the inverter-input current ripple and acts as an energy buffer during regenerative braking. - **Step 3 ‚Äî Voltage Source Inverter (VSI):** Six IGBTs/MOSFETs arranged in three half-bridge legs produce a 3-phase PWM output. The **modulation index ($m_a$)** controls the fundamental RMS voltage, and the **modulation frequency ($f_{PWM}$)**, typically **5‚Äì20 kHz**, controls the stator electrical frequency. - **Step 4 ‚Äî Three-Phase Induction Motor:** The stator windings receive variable-V, variable-f supply. The rotor (copper/aluminium squirrel cage) converts electromagnetic energy to mechanical torque ($T_e$) on the shaft. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why DC link capacitor?** The inverter draws *pulsating* current from the battery. Without the capacitor, the battery would see AC ripples ‚Üí premature degradation. - **Why vary V and f together?** The air-gap flux $\Phi \propto V / f$. If you increase $f$ alone, $\Phi$ drops ‚Üí torque collapses. If you increase $V$ alone, $\Phi$ saturates the iron core ‚Üí excessive magnetising current ‚Üí overheating. Hence the constant $V/f$ rule in the *constant-torque region*. - **Why PWM at 5‚Äì20 kHz?** Above the audible range (20 Hz‚Äì20 kHz) it avoids acoustic noise; below ~20 kHz the switching losses in IGBTs are acceptable. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **The following table is a CHEAT SHEET for Module 2 questions. Memorise each entry ‚Äî it appears in almost every past KTU exam.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Operating Condition | |---|----------|---------|-------|---------------------| | 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f_s}{P}$ | **rpm** | Stator electrical frequency $f_s$ in Hz, $P$ = number of poles | | 2 | Synchronous angular speed | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi f_s}{P/2} = \dfrac{4\pi f_s}{P}$ | **rad/s** | Equivalent mechanical rad/s of the rotating field | | 3 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | **dimensionless** | $s = 0$ at synchronous, $s = 1$ at standstill | | 4 | Rotor speed | $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$ | **rpm** | Actual mechanical shaft speed | | 5 | Rotor frequency | $f_r = s \cdot f_s$ | **Hz** | Frequency of induced rotor currents | | 6 | Air-gap flux (constant V/f) | $\Phi \propto \dfrac{V_s}{f_s}$ | **Wb** | Constant-torque region (base speed & below) | | 7 | Developed electromagnetic torque | $T_e = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_s^2 \cdot (R_r / s)}{(R_s + R_r / s)^2 + (X_s + X_r)^2}$ | **N¬∑m** | The exact per-phase equivalent-circuit torque | | 8 | Starting torque (s = 1) | $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_s^2 \cdot R_r}{(R_s + R_r)^2 + (X_s + X_r)^2}$ | **N¬∑m** | Plugging/launch condition of EV | | 9 | Maximum (pull-out) torque | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{2\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_s^2}{R_s + \sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_r)^2}}$ | **N¬∑m** | Slip at $T_{max}$: $s_{max} = \dfrac{R_r}{\sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_r)^2}}$ | | 10 | Mechanical output power | $P_{out} = T_e \cdot \omega_m$ | **W** | $\omega_m = (1-s)\omega_s$ is the rotor mechanical speed | | 11 | Air-gap power split | $P_{ag} : P_{cu,r} : P_{mech} = 1 : s : (1-s)$ | **W** | Fundamental power-flow identity (must memorise) | | 12 | Rotor copper loss | $P_{cu,r} = s \cdot P_{ag}$ | **W** | Slip √ó air-gap power | | 13 | Developed mechanical power | $P_{mech} = (1-s) P_{ag}$ | **W** | Air-gap power minus rotor copper loss | | 14 | EV traction force | $F_t = \dfrac{T_e \cdot \eta_{tr}}{r_w}$ | **N** | $\eta_{tr}$ = drivetrain eff., $r_w$ = wheel radius | | 15 | Vehicle dynamics (cruise) | $F_t = m g f_r + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$ | **N** | $f_r$ = rolling resistance coeff., $C_d$ = drag coeff. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The identity $P_{ag} : P_{cu,r} : P_{mech} = 1 : s : (1-s)$** is the single most-asked relation in KTU ESE. It states that *for every 1 W of air-gap power crossing the air-gap, $s$ watts are dissipated as rotor copper heat, and $(1-s)$ watts emerge as useful mechanical shaft power*. Hence, an EV cruising at low slip ($s \approx 0.02$) wastes only **2 %** of air-gap power in the rotor ‚Äî a key reason induction motors are highly efficient. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Formula 14 ‚Üí 15** is used by every OEM (Tesla, Tata Motors, Mahindra Electric) to size the motor for a vehicle target (0‚Äì100 km/h time, gradeability). - **Formula 9** ($T_{max}$) determines the "overload capability" ‚Äî an EV IM can deliver **2‚Äì3√ó rated torque for 30 seconds** for overtaking bursts. - **Formula 11** justifies why EVs prefer multi-pole (P = 4 or 6) motors at **low base speeds** (~1500‚Äì3000 rpm), then use **reduction gears** (single or two-speed, like Porsche Taycan) to match wheel speed. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚Äî Synchronous Speed of Rotating Magnetic Field A three-phase winding distributed over $120¬∞$ electrical in the stator slots, energised by a balanced 3-phase supply, produces a sinusoidal **MMF wave** that physically rotates in space. **Step A ‚Äî Per-phase MMF expressions:** The three phase MMFs (assumed sinusoidally distributed in space with $P/2$ pole-pairs) are: $$\begin{aligned} F_A(\theta, t) &= F_{max} \cos(\theta) \cdot \cos(\omega_s t) \\[2mm] F_B(\theta, t) &= F_{max} \cos(\theta - 120¬∞) \cdot \cos(\omega_s t - 120¬∞) \\[2mm] F_C(\theta, t) &= F_{max} \cos(\theta - 240¬∞) \cdot \cos(\omega_s t - 240¬∞) \end{aligned}$$ **Step B ‚Äî Apply the trigonometric product-to-sum identity** $\cos A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$: $$\begin{aligned} F_A(\theta, t) &= \tfrac{F_{max}}{2} [\cos(\theta - \omega_s t) + \cos(\theta + \omega_s t)] \\[2mm] F_B(\theta, t) &= \tfrac{F_{max}}{2} [\cos(\theta - \omega_s t) + \cos(\theta + \omega_s t - 240¬∞)] \\[2mm] F_C(\theta, t) &= \tfrac{F_{max}}{2} [\cos(\theta - \omega_s t) + \cos(\theta + \omega_s t - 480¬∞)] \end{aligned}$$ **Step C ‚Äî Sum the three MMFs.** The forward-traveling waves (containing $\theta - \omega_s t$) **add in phase**, while the backward waves ($\theta + \omega_s t$) are **120¬∞ apart** and sum to zero: $$\begin{aligned} F_{total}(\theta, t) &= F_A + F_B + F_C \\[2mm] &= \tfrac{3 F_{max}}{2} \cos(\theta - \omega_s t) \end{aligned}$$ **Step D ‚Äî Interpret the result.** The resultant is a single sinusoidal wave with constant amplitude $\tfrac{3 F_{max}}{2}$, whose peak occurs when $\theta - \omega_s t = 0$, i.e., at space-angle: $$\begin{aligned} \theta(t) = \omega_s t \end{aligned}$$ This means the peak of the MMF wave **rotates** in space at electrical angular speed $\omega_s$. Converting to mechanical RPM via $\omega_m = \omega_s / (P/2)$: $$\begin{aligned} \omega_m = \dfrac{2 \omega_s}{P} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 2\pi f_s}{P} = \dfrac{4\pi f_s}{P} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_s = \dfrac{60 \cdot \omega_m}{2\pi} = \dfrac{120 f_s}{P} \end{aligned}$$ This is the **synchronous speed** ‚Äî it is independent of load and depends *only* on supply frequency and pole count. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚Äî Electromagnetic Torque from the Per-Phase Equivalent Circuit The per-phase equivalent circuit of a 3-phase induction motor referred to the stator side yields the **air-gap power**: $$\begin{aligned} I_r' = \dfrac{V_s}{\sqrt{\left(R_s + \dfrac{R_r'}{s}\right)^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step A ‚Äî Air-gap power per phase** is the power dissipated in the rotor branch resistance $R_r'/s$: $$\begin{aligned} P_{ag,\phi} = (I_r')^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_r'}{s} \end{aligned}$$ **Step B ‚Äî Total 3-phase air-gap power:** $$\begin{aligned} P_{ag} = 3 (I_r')^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_r'}{s} \end{aligned}$$ **Step C ‚Äî Substitute $I_r'$ and multiply by 3:** $$\begin{aligned} P_{ag} = \dfrac{3 V_s^2 \, (R_r'/s)}{\left(R_s + \dfrac{R_r'}{s}\right)^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2} \end{aligned}$$ **Step D ‚Äî Electromagnetic torque is air-gap power divided by synchronous mechanical angular speed** $\omega_s$: $$\begin{aligned} T_e = \dfrac{P_{ag}}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_s^2 \, (R_r'/s)}{\left(R_s + \dfrac{R_r'}{s}\right)^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2} \end{aligned}$$ **Step E ‚Äî Slip at maximum torque.** Differentiate $T_e$ with respect to $s$ and set $dT_e/ds = 0$. After algebraic reduction (chain-rule applied to numerator and denominator), the result simplifies elegantly: $$\begin{aligned} s_{max} = \dfrac{R_r'}{\sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step F ‚Äî Substitute $s_{max}$ back into the torque equation** and simplify using $R_s \ll \sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2}$ (valid for typical EV motors where $R_s < 0.1 \, \vert X_s + X_r' \vert$): $$\begin{aligned} T_{max} \approx \dfrac{3}{2 \omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_s^2}{R_s + \sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2}} \end{aligned}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** $T_{max}$ is **independent of rotor resistance $R_r'$**! This is why an EV designer cannot change $T_{max}$ by altering rotor bar material ‚Äî but CAN change the **slip** $s_{max}$ at which it occurs. This is exploited in **deep-bar / double-cage rotors** to tailor the torque curve for traction. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚Äî The Power-Flow Identity $P_{ag} : P_{cu,r} : P_{mech} = 1 : s : (1-s)$ **Step A ‚Äî Rotor copper loss** is by Joule heating in $R_r'$ at the *slip frequency* $f_r = s f_s$: $$\begin{aligned} P_{cu,r} = 3 (I_r')^2 R_r' \end{aligned}$$ **Step B ‚Äî Air-gap power** (from Step C of Derivation 2): $$\begin{aligned} P_{ag} = 3 (I_r')^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_r'}{s} \end{aligned}$$ **Step C ‚Äî Take the ratio** $P_{cu,r} / P_{ag}$: $$\begin{aligned} \dfrac{P_{cu,r}}{P_{ag}} = \dfrac{3 (I_r')^2 R_r'}{3 (I_r')^2 (R_r'/s)} = s \end{aligned}$$ **Step D ‚Äî Hence** $P_{cu,r} = s \cdot P_{ag}$ ‚Äî QED for one identity. **Step E ‚Äî Mechanical power developed** is air-gap power minus rotor copper loss: $$\begin{aligned} P_{mech} = P_{ag} - P_{cu,r} = P_{ag} - s P_{ag} = (1 - s) P_{ag} \end{aligned}$$ **Step F ‚Äî Final normalised identity:** $$\begin{aligned} \dfrac{P_{ag} : P_{cu,r} : P_{mech}}{P_{ag}} = 1 : s : (1-s) \end{aligned}$$ --- ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Problem) > **Problem:** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor drives an EV. Full-load slip is 4 %. Stator copper loss = 300 W, rotor copper loss = 200 W, iron loss = 150 W, mechanical (friction + windage) loss = 100 W. Compute: (a) Air-gap power, (b) Shaft power, (c) Efficiency. **Solution:** Given $P_{cu,r} = 200$ W and $s = 0.04$. From the identity $P_{cu,r} = s \, P_{ag}$: $$\begin{aligned} P_{ag} = \dfrac{P_{cu,r}}{s} = \dfrac{200}{0.04} = 5000 \text{ W} = 5 \text{ kW} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} P_{mech} = (1 - s) P_{ag} = (1 - 0.04)(5000) = 4800 \text{ W} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} P_{shaft} = P_{mech} - P_{fw} = 4800 - 100 = 4700 \text{ W} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} P_{input} = P_{shaft} + P_{cu,r} + P_{cu,s} + P_{iron} + P_{fw} = 4700 + 200 + 300 + 150 + 100 = 5450 \text{ W} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \eta = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{P_{input}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{4700}{5450} \times 100\% \approx 86.24\% \end{aligned}$$ --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram ‚Äî EV Induction Motor Drive Power Flow ```mermaid graph LR BAT[Battery Pack
400 V or 800 V DC] DC[DC Link Capacitor
1 to 2 mF] INV[Voltage Source Inverter
6 IGBT 3 Phase] CTRL[VFD Controller
V by f Strategy] IM[3 Phase Induction Motor
Squirrel Cage Rotor] GEAR[Reduction Gearbox
Single or 2 Speed] WH[EV Wheels] BAT --- DC DC --- INV CTRL -.fPWM.-> INV INV ==> IM IM --- GEAR GEAR --- WH INV -. Regenerative Braking .-> DC DC -. Charge Current .-> BAT style BAT fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#c1121f,color:#000 style DC fill:#fff1b8,stroke:#b08900,color:#000 style INV fill:#cddafd,stroke:#3a86ff,color:#000 style CTRL fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#2d6a4f,color:#000 style IM fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#e85d04,color:#000 style GEAR fill:#caffbf,stroke:#40916c,color:#000 style WH fill:#bdb2ff,stroke:#5a189a,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows = forward motoring power flow. Dashed arrows = control signals (low-power) and reverse regenerative-braking energy. The **double-arrow `==>`** marks the electromechanical energy conversion at the air-gap. --- ## 4.2 Torque‚ÄìSpeed Characteristic of EV Induction Motor Drive ```mermaid graph TB subgraph CONST_TORQUE["CONSTANT TORQUE REGION 0 to Base Speed"] direction LR T0[Starting Torque Tst] --> TR[Rated Torque Trated] end subgraph CONST_POWER["CONSTANT POWER REGION Base to Max Speed"] direction LR TR2[Rated Torque] --> TW[Weakened Field Torque] end N0[Nr = 0 Standstill] --> NB[Base Speed Nbase] --> NMAX[Max Speed Nmax] T0 -. s = 1 .- N0 TR -. rated slip .- NB TW -. field weakening .- NMAX style CONST_TORQUE fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#e85d04,color:#000 style CONST_POWER fill:#caffbf,stroke:#40916c,color:#000 style T0 fill:#fff style TR fill:#fff style TW fill:#fff ``` **Interpretation:** - **0 ‚Üí Base speed:** $V/f$ is held constant; flux is constant; torque is constant ‚âà $T_{rated}$. This is the **launch & low-speed cruising region**. - **Base ‚Üí Max speed:** $V$ is clamped at max (battery voltage limit); only $f$ increases; flux weakens as $\Phi \propto 1/f$; power $P = T \omega$ remains constant ‚Äî hence the name **constant-power region**. This is the **highway cruising & overtaking region**. --- ## 4.3 Power-Flow Sankey-Style Architecture ```mermaid graph LR PIN[Input Electrical Power Pin] --> CSL[Stator Cu Loss] PIN --> IR[Iron Loss] CSL --> PAG[Air Gap Power Pag] IR --> PAG PAG --> CRL[Rotor Cu Loss s times Pag] PAG --> PMECH[Mechanical Power Developed] CRL --> THERMAL[Heat Dissipation] PMECH --> FWL[Friction and Windage] PMECH --> PSHAFT[Shaft Output Power] FWL --> THERMAL style PIN fill:#a2d2ff,stroke:#023e8a,color:#000 style PAG fill:#ffb703,stroke:#fb8500,color:#000 style PSHAFT fill:#80ed99,stroke:#38a169,color:#000 style THERMAL fill:#ffadad,stroke:#c1121f,color:#000 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024, Series-1]` **Why are three-phase squirrel-cage induction motors preferred over DC motors and PMSM for many modern Electric Vehicle applications? Mention any four reasons.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **No permanent magnets** ‚Üí avoids rare-earth (NdFeB) supply-chain risks; reduces cost by ~30 % compared to PMSM. 2. **Rugged rotor construction** (cast aluminium / copper bars in solid iron laminations) ‚Üí can sustain centrifugal stresses at **> 18,000 rpm**, far beyond what surface-mounted magnets can tolerate. 3. **Excellent field-weakening range** ‚Üí natural constant-power region from base speed to 3‚Äì4√ó base speed enables single-gear highway cruising. 4. **Liquid-cooling friendly** ‚Üí uniform isotropic copper cage dissipates heat; stator end-windings are easily jacket-cooled. *(1 mark for any 2 reasons, full marks for 4 well-explained reasons.)* ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023]` **Define "slip" in an induction motor. Why is slip essential for torque production?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Slip ($s$)** is defined as the relative difference between synchronous speed $N_s$ and actual rotor speed $N_r$, expressed as a fraction of $N_s$: $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$. Slip is **essential** because: - **At $s = 0$** the rotor rotates in synchronism with the stator RMF ‚Üí no relative motion ‚Üí no flux-cutting ‚Üí no induced EMF (Faraday's law requires $d\Phi/dt \neq 0$) ‚Üí no rotor current ‚Üí **no torque**. - **At any $s > 0$**, the rotor conductors *cut* the RMF ‚Üí induced EMF ‚Üí induced current ‚Üí force on conductors ‚Üí **torque**. - Therefore torque is a function of $s$: $T_e \propto s$ at low slip (linear region) and $T_e \propto 1/s$ at high slip. *(2 marks for definition; 1 mark for the "why" explanation.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks ‚Äî Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024, Module 2, Set A]` **(a)** With a neat per-phase equivalent circuit, derive the expression for the electromagnetic torque developed by a 3-phase induction motor. Show that the slip at maximum torque is independent of stator voltage. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has a full-load slip of 4 %. The rotor copper loss at full load is 600 W and the stator copper loss is 800 W. The friction and windage losses total 200 W. Compute: (i) Air-gap power, (ii) Shaft power, (iii) Mechanical power developed, (iv) Efficiency. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q-A(a) **Step 1:** Write the per-phase equivalent circuit referred to the stator (show $R_s$, $X_s$, magnetising branch $jX_m$, rotor branch $R_r'/s + jX_r'$). **(1 mark)** **Step 2:** Express the rotor-referred current from circuit: $$\begin{aligned} I_r' = \dfrac{V_s}{\left[\left(R_s + \dfrac{R_r'}{s}\right)^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2\right]^{1/2}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3:** Air-gap power per phase, $P_{ag,\phi} = (I_r')^2 \cdot R_r'/s$; total $P_{ag} = 3 (I_r')^2 R_r'/s$. **(1 mark)** **Step 4:** Electromagnetic torque, $T_e = P_{ag}/\omega_s$: $$\begin{aligned} T_e = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_s^2 \cdot (R_r'/s)}{\left(R_s + \dfrac{R_r'}{s}\right)^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2} \end{aligned}$$ **(2 marks for arriving at this final equation)** **Step 5:** Differentiate $T_e$ with respect to $s$, set $dT_e/ds = 0$. Using the quotient rule on numerator $N(s) = R_r'/s$ and denominator $D(s) = (R_s + R_r'/s)^2 + (X_s+X_r')^2$, after algebraic simplification: $$\begin{aligned} s_{max} = \dfrac{R_r'}{\sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2}} \end{aligned}$$ **(2 marks for derivation; $s_{max}$ is independent of $V_s$ ‚Äî completing the proof.)** **Step 6:** The corresponding $T_{max}$ expression: $$\begin{aligned} T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{2\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_s^2}{R_s + \sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2}} \end{aligned}$$ **(1 mark for stating the conclusion that $T_{max} \propto V_s^2$ but $s_{max}$ is $V_s$-independent.)** --- #### Model Solution to Q-A(b) Given: $P = 6$, $f = 50$ Hz, $s = 0.04$, $P_{cu,r} = 600$ W, $P_{cu,s} = 800$ W, $P_{fw} = 200$ W. **(i) Air-gap power** ‚Äî using the identity $P_{cu,r} = s \cdot P_{ag}$: **(2 marks)** $$\begin{aligned} P_{ag} = \dfrac{P_{cu,r}}{s} = \dfrac{600}{0.04} = 15000 \text{ W} = 15 \text{ kW} \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Shaft power** ‚Äî $P_{shaft} = P_{mech} - P_{fw}$: $$\begin{aligned} P_{mech} &= (1 - s) P_{ag} = (1 - 0.04)(15000) = 14400 \text{ W} \\[2mm] P_{shaft} &= 14400 - 200 = 14200 \text{ W} = 14.2 \text{ kW} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)} \end{aligned}$$ **(iii) Mechanical power developed** = $P_{mech} = (1-s) P_{ag} = 14.4$ kW. **(1 mark)** **(iv) Efficiency** ‚Äî Input power = $P_{shaft} + P_{cu,s} + P_{cu,r} + P_{fw}$: $$\begin{aligned} P_{in} &= 14200 + 800 + 600 + 200 = 15800 \text{ W} \\[2mm] \eta &= \dfrac{14200}{15800} \times 100\% \approx 89.87\% \quad \textbf{(2 marks)} \end{aligned}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023, Module 2, Set B]` **(a)** Explain with block diagram the role of a **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)** in controlling a 3-phase induction motor used in an EV. Discuss the **$V/f$ control** strategy in detail. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare **Squirrel-Cage IM, PMSM and SRM** for EV application on the basis of (i) Power density, (ii) Efficiency, (iii) Cost, (iv) Cooling complexity, (v) Field-weakening capability, (vi) Reliability, (vii) Regenerative braking ability. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q-B(a) **Step 1 ‚Äî Block Diagram (3 marks):** The VFD chain for an EV IM drive is: **Battery (DC)** ‚Üí **DC-link Capacitor** ‚Üí **Rectifier** (only if AC charging considered; otherwise direct DC link) ‚Üí **VSI (3-phase IGBT inverter)** ‚Üí **Induction Motor**. The **controller** senses rotor position (or estimates it via sensorless FOC), reads accelerator-pedal torque demand, and modulates inverter $f_{PWM}$ and $m_a$. **Step 2 ‚Äî V/f Strategy (2 marks):** Below **base speed** ($\omega_{base}$), maintain $V/f = \text{constant}$ so that air-gap flux $\Phi$ stays at rated value. This yields **constant torque** capability. Typical V/f ratio for a 400 V, 100 Hz base is **4 V/Hz** (per-phase RMS line-neutral ‚âà 230 V at 50 Hz). **Step 3 ‚Äî Field Weakening (2 marks):** Above base speed, $V$ saturates at the battery maximum. Only $f$ rises ‚Üí $\Phi \propto 1/f$ drops ‚Üí torque falls as $1/f$, but power $T\omega \approx$ constant. **Key Equation (1 mark):** $$\begin{aligned} \Phi_{air-gap} = \dfrac{V_s}{4.44 \, f_s \, N_s \, k_w} \end{aligned}$$ where $k_w$ is the winding factor and $N_s$ is turns per phase. To keep $\Phi$ constant, $V_s / f_s$ must remain constant. --- #### Model Solution to Q-B(b) | Criterion | Squirrel-Cage IM | PMSM | SRM | |-----------|------------------|------|-----| | Power density | Medium (3‚Äì5 kW/kg) | High (5‚Äì7 kW/kg) | High (5‚Äì6 kW/kg) | | Peak efficiency | 92‚Äì95 % | 95‚Äì97 % | 92‚Äì94 % | | Cost per kW | **Low** (no magnets) | High (rare-earth magnets) | Medium | | Cooling complexity | **Easy** (uniform copper) | Hard (localised magnet heating) | Hard (rotor hot-spots) | | Field-weakening | **Excellent** (natural) | Limited by magnet flux linkage | Excellent (variable excitation) | | Reliability | **Very high** (rugged rotor) | Medium (demag at high T) | High | | Regen braking | Excellent (inverter-driven) | Excellent | Excellent | **(Award 1 mark per row for correct comparison with justification ‚Äî sample 5‚Äì6 rows suffice for full 7 marks.)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing air-gap power with shaft power** ‚Äî many students write $P_{shaft} = P_{ag} - P_{cu,r}$ and forget friction/windage. Deduct 1 mark. > 2. **Using $N_s = 120 f / P$ incorrectly** ‚Äî remember $N_s$ is in **rpm**, not rad/s. For mechanical angular speed use $\omega_s = 2\pi N_s / 60$. > 3. **Forgetting to convert per-phase voltage** when using the torque equation ‚Äî $V_s$ in the formula is the **phase (line-neutral) RMS voltage**, NOT the line-to-line. Many students substitute $V_{LL}$ directly. **Penalty: ‚àí1 mark.** > 4. **Slip must always be in DECIMAL (0.04)**, not percentage (4 %). The identity $P_{cu,r} = s P_{ag}$ uses dimensionless slip. > 5. **In $V/f$ control** ‚Äî clearly state **TWO regions** (constant torque, constant power). Generic statements like "voltage and frequency are varied" fetch only partial marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 2 ‚Äî Induction Motor Drives (Introduction)** - [ ] **Slip** $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$; at start $s = 1$; at synchronous $s = 0$; at EV cruise $s \approx 0.01$‚Äì$0.05$. - [ ] **Synchronous speed** $N_s = 120 f_s / P$ ‚Äî depends ONLY on $f$ and pole count, not load. - [ ] **The golden identity** $P_{ag} : P_{cu,r} : P_{mech} = 1 : s : (1-s)$ ‚Äî must be memorised verbatim. - [ ] **Rotor copper loss** $P_{cu,r} = s \cdot P_{ag}$ ‚Äî low slip ‚Üí low rotor heat ‚Üí high efficiency. - [ ] **Air-gap flux** $\Phi \propto V/f$ ‚Äî keep this constant in the constant-torque region (V/f control). - [ ] **Torque equation** $T_e = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_s^2 (R_r'/s)}{(R_s + R_r'/s)^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2}$. - [ ] **Slip at max torque** $s_{max} = R_r' / \sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2}$ ‚Äî independent of $V_s$. - [ ] **Max torque** $T_{max} \propto V_s^2$ ‚Äî but $s_{max}$ does not depend on $V_s$. - [ ] **Two operating regions in EV:** (i) Constant-torque ($V/f$ constant, 0 ‚Üí base speed) for launch/grade; (ii) Constant-power (field-weakened, base ‚Üí max speed) for cruise. - [ ] **Why IM in EVs:** no magnets, rugged rotor, low cost, high field-weakening range, excellent regen. - [ ] **VFD components:** DC link capacitor + VSI (6-IGBT) + PWM controller using $V/f$ or **Field-Oriented Control (FOC)**. - [ ] **Tesla application:** Model S/X/3/Y all use 3-phase AC squirrel-cage IMs (later versions moving to PMSM with carbon-wrapped rotor for higher specific power). - [ ] **Rotor frequencies** $f_r = s f_s$ ‚Äî for an EV at $s = 0.03$ and $f_s = 100$ Hz, rotor currents oscillate at only **3 Hz** ‚Äî enabling deep-bar rotor design. - [ ] **Permanent-magnet-free** advantage gives IM a strategic supply-chain edge as the world moves away from Chinese rare-earth monopoly. ---

topics.title: Speed Control of Induction Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Speed Control of Induction Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Three-Phase Induction Motor (IM)** is a self-starting, robust, brushless AC machine whose rotor speed $N_r$ is fundamentally tied to the synchronous speed $N_s$ of the rotating stator magnetic field through the **slip (s)**. Because the rotor cannot physically reach synchronous speed (which would require zero induced rotor current and thus zero torque), **Speed Control of an Induction Motor** is the systematic manipulation of input electrical parameters ‚Äî namely **voltage (V), frequency (f), and pole count (P)** ‚Äî to deliberately alter the synchronous speed and operating slip, thereby commanding the mechanical output speed of the motor across a desired range.

Mathematically, this relationship is anchored by the **fundamental speed equation**:

$$N_s = \frac{120 \cdot f}{P} \quad \text{[in RPM]}$$

and the **slip relation**:

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Hybrid and Electric Vehicle applications, induction motors are favored for their ruggedness, high power density, and absence of rare-earth magnets (unlike PMSMs). The dominant speed-control strategy in production EVs (e.g., Tesla Model S, Tata Nexon EV) is **Variable Voltage Variable Frequency (VVVF)** control, often combined with **Field-Oriented Control (FOC)**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview ‚Äî The Bicycle Analogy

Imagine you are cycling on a stationary bicycle trainer connected to a generator:

- The **pedaling rate** = Stator supply frequency ($f$)
- The **gear ratio** = Number of magnetic poles ($P$)
- The **actual wheel rotation** = Rotor speed ($N_r$)
- The **lag of the wheel** = Slip ($s$)

To go faster, you have three choices: pedal quicker (**increase frequency**), shift to a smaller gear (**reduce poles**), or change the terrain resistance. Induction motor speed control works on identical principles ‚Äî we modulate *how fast* the magnetic field rotates and *how strongly* the rotor chases it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Units to Memorize:**
> - Synchronous speed $N_s$ ‚Üí **RPM**
> - Supply frequency $f$ ‚Üí **Hz**
> - Slip $s$ ‚Üí **per unit (p.u.) or fraction**
> - Pole count $P$ ‚Üí **integer (always even)**
> - Mechanical rotor speed $N_r$ ‚Üí **RPM**

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Speed Control is Inherently Difficult in Induction Motors

Unlike DC motors where speed is a linear function of armature voltage, the AC induction motor presents a **non-linear, coupled electromagnetic system**. The torque produced depends on:

1. Stator flux $\Phi$
2. Slip frequency $sf$
3. Rotor time constant $\tau_r = L_r / R_r$

Because flux $\Phi \propto V/f$, any change in frequency must be accompanied by a proportional change in voltage to maintain **constant flux** and prevent magnetic saturation (over-fluxing) or torque collapse (under-fluxing).

### 2.2 Classification of Speed Control Methods (KTU-Exam Essential Taxonomy)

| Sl. No. | Category | Sub-Methods |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | **From Stator Side** | (i) V/F Control (VVVF) |
| | | (ii) Variable Voltage Control |
| | | (iii) Pole Changing (PAM) |
| **B** | **From Rotor Side** | (iv) Rotor Resistance Control |
| | | (v) Slip Power Recovery (Kramer/Scherbius) |
| **C** | **Advanced Vector Methods** | (vi) Field-Oriented Control (FOC) |
| | | (vii) Direct Torque Control (DTC) |

### 2.3 Method 1 ‚Äî V/F Control (VVVF) ‚Äî **The Dominant EV Method**

**Operating Principle:** Maintain a constant **Volts-per-Hertz ratio** so that air-gap flux remains nominal across the entire speed range.

$$\frac{V}{f} = \text{constant}$$

**Operating Regions of V/F Drive:**

| Region | Speed Range | Control Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Constant Torque Region** (0 ‚Üí Base Speed) | $0 < N < N_{base}$ | $V$ rises linearly with $f$; flux $\Phi$ held constant |
| **Constant Power Region** (Base ‚Üí Critical Speed) | $N_{base} < N < N_{max}$ | $V$ held constant at rated; $f$ increased; flux weakens |
| **Constant Voltage / Series Region** | $N > N_{max}$ | Both limited by inverter ceiling; torque drops as $1/N^2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is V/F control critical for HEV/EV?**
> - The inverter (DC-AC) in an EV feeds the motor with synthesized sine waves whose amplitude and frequency are software-controlled.
> - During **city driving (low speed, high torque)**, the inverter operates in the **constant torque region**.
> - During **highway cruising (high speed, lower torque)**, the inverter pushes into the **field-weakening (constant power) region**.
> - This mimics an IC engine's torque curve, allowing **single-pedal driving feel** and **regenerative braking** integration.

### 2.4 Method 2 ‚Äî Vector / Field-Oriented Control (FOC)

FOC transforms the 3-phase stator currents ($i_a, i_b, i_c$) into a **rotating 2-axis reference frame (d-q)** aligned with the rotor flux:

- $i_d$ (direct-axis current) ‚Üí controls **flux**
- $i_q$ (quadrature-axis current) ‚Üí controls **torque**

This **decouples** torque and flux, allowing the induction motor to be controlled with the same precision as a separately excited DC motor.

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{L_m^2}{L_r} \cdot i_d \cdot i_q$$

### 2.5 Method 3 ‚Äî Direct Torque Control (DTC)

DTC uses **hysteresis comparators** on stator flux and torque estimates to directly select inverter switching states from an **optimum switching table**. It offers:
- Faster torque response (~1 ms)
- No PWM modulator or coordinate transformations needed
- Higher torque ripple (a key EV trade-off)

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Relation | Meaning & Units |
|---|---|---|
| Synchronous Speed | $N_s = 120 f / P$ | Rotating field speed, **RPM** |
| Slip | $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ | Fraction, dimensionless |
| Rotor Frequency | $f_r = s \cdot f$ | Frequency of rotor current, **Hz** |
| V/F Ratio | $V/f = k$ (constant) | Maintains rated flux |
| Air-Gap Flux | $\Phi \approx V / (4.44 \cdot f \cdot N \cdot k_w)$ | **Weber (Wb)** |
| Electromagnetic Torque | $T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s \cdot E_2^2 \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + (s \cdot X_2)^2}$ | **N¬∑m** |
| Slip at Max Torque | $s_{max} = R_2 / X_2$ | Independent of load |
| Maximum Torque | $T_{max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^2}{2 X_2}$ | **N¬∑m**, constant in V/F mode |
| FOC Torque | $T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{L_m^2}{L_r} \cdot i_d \cdot i_q$ | **N¬∑m** |
| EV Power Balance | $P_{mech} = T_e \cdot \omega_m$ | **Watts (W)** |
| EV Drive Power | $P_{batt} = P_{mech} / (\eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{inv})$ | Includes inverter + motor losses |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Pitfall ‚Äî Escape Velocity of V/F Method:**
> Notice in the torque formula that if you keep $V/f$ constant, then $E_2/f$ is constant and therefore $T_{max}$ becomes **independent of frequency** ‚Äî this is precisely why V/F drives deliver **constant rated torque below base speed**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Torque-Slip Characteristic of Induction Motor

Starting from the **per-phase equivalent circuit** (approximate, with rotor referred to stator), the air-gap power transferred to the rotor is:

$$P_{ag} = 3 \cdot I_2^{\prime 2} \cdot \frac{R_2^{\prime}}{s}$$

The mechanical power developed (air-gap power minus rotor copper loss) is:

$$P_m = 3 \cdot I_2^{\prime 2} \cdot R_2^{\prime} \cdot \frac{(1-s)}{s}$$

Since $P_m = T_e \cdot \omega_m$ and $\omega_m = \omega_s (1 - s)$ where $\omega_s = 2\pi f / (P/2)$:

$$T_e = \frac{P_m}{\omega_m} = \frac{3 \cdot I_2^{\prime 2} \cdot R_2^{\prime} / s}{\omega_s}$$

Substituting the rotor current from the equivalent circuit:

$$I_2^{\prime} = \frac{s \cdot V}{\sqrt{R_2^{\prime 2} + (s \cdot X_2^{\prime})^2}}$$

we obtain the **complete torque equation**:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s \cdot V^2 \cdot R_2^{\prime}}{R_2^{\prime 2} + (s \cdot X_2^{\prime})^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Transition:** The numerator contains $s \cdot V^2 \cdot R_2^{\prime}$ ‚Äî this explains *why voltage control works* (torque scales as $V^2$ at low slip). The denominator $(R_2^{\prime 2} + (s \cdot X_2^{\prime})^2)$ grows rapidly at high slip, causing torque to drop after $s_{max}$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Slip at Maximum Torque ($s_{max}$)

To find the slip where $T_e$ is maximum, we apply $dT_e/ds = 0$.

Let $K = \dfrac{3 V^2 R_2^{\prime}}{\omega_s}$ (a constant for a given V/f pair), so:

$$T_e = K \cdot \frac{s}{R_2^{\prime 2} + s^2 X_2^{\prime 2}}$$

Differentiating with respect to $s$:

$$\frac{dT_e}{ds} = K \cdot \frac{(R_2^{\prime 2} + s^2 X_2^{\prime 2}) - s \cdot (2 s X_2^{\prime 2})}{(R_2^{\prime 2} + s^2 X_2^{\prime 2})^2}$$

Setting the numerator to zero:

$$R_2^{\prime 2} + s^2 X_2^{\prime 2} - 2 s^2 X_2^{\prime 2} = 0$$

$$R_2^{\prime 2} = s^2 X_2^{\prime 2}$$

$$\boxed{s_{max} = \frac{R_2^{\prime}}{X_2^{\prime}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Engineering Insight:** $s_{max}$ depends **only on the rotor circuit parameters** ‚Äî not on supply voltage. This means a V/F drive can shift the operating slip freely without altering the *location* of peak torque on the slip axis.

### 3.3 Derivation: Condition for Constant Torque in V/F Control

We want to show that when $V/f$ = constant, $T_{max}$ is independent of $f$.

The maximum torque expression (substituting $s_{max}$ back into $T_e$):

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^{\prime 2}}{2 X_2^{\prime}}$$

Since $X_2^{\prime} = 2\pi f L_2^{\prime}$, dividing both $E_2^{\prime 2}$ and $X_2^{\prime}$ by $f$:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{(2\pi f / (P/2))} \cdot \frac{E_2^{\prime 2}}{2 \cdot 2\pi f L_2^{\prime}} = \text{constant when } \frac{E_2^{\prime}}{f} = \text{constant}$$

In the linear magnetic region, $E_2^{\prime} \approx V$, hence:

$$\boxed{\frac{V}{f} = \text{constant} \implies T_{max} = \text{constant (below base speed)}}$$

### 3.4 Derivation: FOC Torque Equation from d-q Model

The induction motor stator voltage equations in the synchronous reference frame are:

$$v_{ds} = R_s i_{ds} + \frac{d\lambda_{ds}}{dt} - \omega_e \lambda_{qs}$$

$$v_{qs} = R_s i_{qs} + \frac{d\lambda_{qs}}{dt} + \omega_e \lambda_{ds}$$

with flux linkages:

$$\lambda_{ds} = L_s i_{ds} + L_m i_{dr}, \quad \lambda_{qs} = L_s i_{qs} + L_m i_{qr}$$

$$\lambda_{dr} = L_m i_{ds} + L_r i_{dr}, \quad \lambda_{qr} = L_m i_{qs} + L_r i_{qr}$$

In the **rotor-flux-oriented frame**, by definition $\lambda_{qr} = 0$ and $\lambda_{dr} = \lambda_r$. Solving:

$$i_{dr} = \frac{\lambda_r - L_m i_{ds}}{L_r}, \quad i_{qr} = - \frac{L_m}{L_r} i_{qs}$$

The electromagnetic torque in the d-q frame is:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot L_m (i_{qs} i_{dr} - i_{ds} i_{qr})$$

Substituting $i_{dr}$ and $i_{qr}$:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{L_m}{L_r} (i_{qs} \lambda_r - i_{ds} \cdot 0) \cdot \text{(rearrangement gives)}$$

$$\boxed{T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{L_m^2}{L_r} \cdot i_{ds} \cdot i_{qs}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of Decoupling:** With $i_{ds}$ controlling flux and $i_{qs}$ controlling torque independently, the AC induction motor is reduced ‚Äî *in software* ‚Äî to a linear DC-machine-like control system.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Numerical Style)

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor runs at 1440 RPM on full load. Calculate (a) slip, (b) rotor frequency, (c) rotor copper loss as a percentage of input if mechanical losses are 5% of input.

**Solution:**

(a) Synchronous speed:

$$N_s = \frac{120 \cdot 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ RPM}$$

Slip:

$$s = \frac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = \frac{60}{1500} = 0.04 \text{ p.u. (or 4\%)}$$

(b) Rotor frequency:

$$f_r = s \cdot f = 0.04 \cdot 50 = 2 \text{ Hz}$$

(c) Mechanical power developed: $P_m = (1 - s) P_{ag}$. Let input $P_{in} = 1.0$ p.u.

Rotor copper loss: $P_{rcu} = s \cdot P_{ag}$. Mechanical output: $P_{out} = P_m - P_{mech,loss}$.

Using $P_{ag}/P_{in} = 1/(1 - s_{total})$ and mechanical losses = 0.05 p.u., solving yields **rotor copper loss ‚âà 4.2% of input**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse $P_m$ (mechanical power developed) with $P_{out}$ (shaft output). Remember: $P_m = (1-s) P_{ag}$ and $P_{out} = P_m - P_{rotational\ losses}$.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]**
*State the fundamental relationship between synchronous speed, supply frequency, and number of poles in a three-phase induction motor. Why is speed control of an induction motor considered more complex than that of a DC motor?*

**Model Answer:**
The synchronous speed of the rotating magnetic field is given by $N_s = 120 f / P$. Speed is directly proportional to supply frequency $f$ and inversely proportional to pole count $P$. Induction motor speed control is inherently complex because the rotor speed depends on slip, and torque is a **non-linear function** of both voltage and frequency. Unlike a DC motor where flux and torque are independently controllable via separate field and armature circuits, in an AC induction motor these quantities are **magnetically coupled**, requiring sophisticated electronic control (V/F, FOC, or DTC) to achieve decoupled control. **[3 marks]**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2024]**
*What is meant by "constant V/F ratio" in induction motor speed control? State its significance in electric vehicle applications.*

**Model Answer:**
The constant V/F ratio refers to maintaining $V/f = \text{constant}$ so that the air-gap flux $\Phi$ remains at its rated value. Since $\Phi \propto V/f$, holding this ratio constant prevents **flux weakening** (which reduces torque) and **flux strengthening** (which saturates the core). In EV applications, V/F control is essential because the inverter synthesizes variable voltage and frequency from the DC bus; maintaining constant flux delivers **constant rated torque from standstill to base speed**, which matches the EV's high torque requirement for city acceleration. **[3 marks]**

### 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks ‚Äî Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ‚Äî V/F Control for EV Traction**

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2024]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain with a block diagram the **Variable Voltage Variable Frequency (VVVF)** speed control method of a three-phase induction motor used in an electric vehicle. Discuss the **three operating regions** of the V/F drive and their relevance to EV driving cycles.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A 4-pole, 50 Hz, 3-phase induction motor has a rotor resistance of $0.2\ \Omega$ per phase and standstill rotor reactance of $1.0\ \Omega$ per phase. Calculate the slip at maximum torque and the speed at maximum torque. If the supply frequency is reduced to 25 Hz while maintaining V/f ratio constant, what is the new synchronous speed and the new slip at maximum torque?

**Model Solution for (a):**
The VVVF control uses a **three-phase voltage source inverter (VSI)** fed from the EV's DC battery bus. The VCU (Vehicle Control Unit) commands reference frequency $f^*$ and voltage $V^* = k \cdot f^*$, which are converted to PWM gate signals (using **SPWM or SVPWM**) for the inverter IGBTs.

**Three Operating Regions:**
1. **Constant Torque Region (0 ‚Üí 1500 RPM):** $V \propto f$, $\Phi = $ rated, $T_{max} = $ constant. Used during **launch and low-speed city driving**.
2. **Constant Power Region (1500 ‚Üí ~3000 RPM):** $V = V_{rated}$ (held at inverter ceiling), $f$ continues to rise, $\Phi$ weakens $\propto 1/f$. Used during **highway cruising and overtaking**.
3. **Series Region (>3000 RPM):** Both $V$ and the slope of $f$ are limited; torque falls $\propto 1/N^2$. Used sparingly at **top speed**.

Block Diagram (text form):
`DC Bus ‚Üí Inverter (IGBTs) ‚Üí 3-phase IM ‚Üí Resolver ‚Üí Controller ‚Üí PWM generator ‚Üí Inverter`

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: $P = 4$, $f_1 = 50$ Hz, $R_2 = 0.2\ \Omega$, $X_2 = 1.0\ \Omega$.

**Step 1:** Slip at maximum torque (original):
$$s_{max} = \frac{R_2}{X_2} = \frac{0.2}{1.0} = 0.2 \text{ p.u.}$$

**Step 2:** Synchronous speed at 50 Hz:
$$N_{s1} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 3:** Speed at maximum torque:
$$N_{r1} = N_{s1}(1 - s_{max}) = 1500(1 - 0.2) = 1200 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 4:** New synchronous speed at 25 Hz:
$$N_{s2} = \frac{120 \times 25}{4} = 750 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 5:** New standstill reactance at 25 Hz (scales linearly with frequency):
$$X_2' = X_2 \cdot \frac{f_2}{f_1} = 1.0 \times \frac{25}{50} = 0.5\ \Omega$$

**Step 6:** New slip at maximum torque:
$$s_{max}' = \frac{R_2}{X_2'} = \frac{0.2}{0.5} = 0.4 \text{ p.u.}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation:** Even though the slip at max torque changed from 0.2 to 0.4, the *magnitude* of maximum torque remains the **same** because V/f is held constant. This is the magic of VVVF control ‚Äî torque envelope is preserved across the speed range. **[14 marks total]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî Vector Control Theory**

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2024]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the principle of **Field-Oriented Control (FOC)** of an induction motor. Derive the expression for electromagnetic torque in the d-q reference frame and show how torque and flux are decoupled.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compare the **V/F control**, **Direct Torque Control (DTC)**, and **FOC** methods in terms of dynamic response, complexity, parameter sensitivity, and suitability for EV traction. State which method is most commonly used in production EVs and justify.

**Model Solution for (a):**

FOC transforms the 3-phase stator currents $(i_a, i_b, i_c)$ into a 2-phase rotating reference frame $(d, q)$ aligned with the rotor flux vector $\vec{\lambda_r}$. This is achieved in two steps:
1. **Clarke Transform:** $abc \rightarrow \alpha\beta$ (stationary 2-phase)
2. **Park Transform:** $\alpha\beta \rightarrow dq$ (rotating with $\vec{\lambda_r}$)

After alignment, by definition the q-axis rotor flux $\lambda_{qr} = 0$, leaving only $\lambda_{dr} = \lambda_r$.

**Derivation of torque expression** (recap from Section 3.4):

The general d-q torque equation is:
$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot L_m (i_{qs} i_{dr} - i_{ds} i_{qr})$$

With $\lambda_{qr} = 0$, solving the rotor circuit gives:
$$i_{dr} = \frac{\lambda_r - L_m i_{ds}}{L_r}, \quad i_{qr} = - \frac{L_m}{L_r} i_{qs}$$

Substituting and simplifying:
$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{L_m}{L_r} \cdot i_{qs} \cdot \lambda_r = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{L_m^2}{L_r} \cdot i_{ds} \cdot i_{qs}$$

**Decoupling achieved:** $i_{ds}$ solely controls flux $\lambda_r$; $i_{qs}$ solely controls torque $T_e$. They are orthogonal ‚Äî the motor behaves like a DC motor under software control.

**Model Solution for (b):**

| Criterion | V/F Control | DTC | FOC |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dynamic Response** | Slow (open-loop) | Fastest (~1 ms) | Fast (~2‚Äì5 ms) |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Very simple | Moderate | High (needs Park/Clarke) |
| **Parameter Sensitivity** | Low | Low | High (needs $R_r$, $L_m$) |
| **Torque Ripple** | High at low speed | Highest | Lowest (smooth) |
| **Current Quality** | Distorted | Distorted | Near-sinusoidal |
| **EV Suitability** | Low (2-wheeler, small EVs) | Good (commercial EVs) | **Excellent (most modern EVs)** |

**Conclusion:** **FOC is the industry standard** for production EVs (Tesla Model S/X/3, Tata Nexon EV, Hyundai Kona) because it offers the best **trade-off between smooth torque delivery (low ripple for NVH comfort), wide constant-power operating range (for highway driving), and regenerative braking precision**. V/F is used in budget 2-wheelers; DTC is occasionally used in industrial EVs where noise is less critical. **[14 marks total]**
