### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones (Examiner Notes):**
> 1. **Forgetting to scale rotor reactance with frequency** ‚Äî when frequency changes, $X_2 = 2\pi f L_2$ changes too! Students often use the 50 Hz value, losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $P_m$ (developed power) with $P_{out}$ (shaft power)** ‚Äî always subtract mechanical losses (friction + windage).
> 3. **Skipping the Clarke + Park transform steps** in FOC derivations ‚Äî these are *expected* steps, not optional commentary.
> 4. **Writing "V/F is constant" without specifying the operating region** ‚Äî examiners expect explicit mention of constant-torque vs. field-weakening regions.
> 5. **Using wrong pole pairs** ‚Äî remember $P$ in formulas refers to *number of poles*, but $\omega_s = 2\pi f / (P/2)$ uses *pole pairs*. Mixing these costs marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Synchronous speed** $N_s = 120 f / P$ is the upper bound; rotor always lags by slip $s$.
- **Slip** $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ is the operational lever for torque control.
- **V/F control** maintains constant flux below base speed ‚Äî the *workhorse* of EV traction.
- **Three operating regions**: Constant Torque ‚Üí Constant Power (field weakening) ‚Üí Series.
- **Field-weakening** allows the motor to run above base speed (essential for highway EVs).
- **FOC** decouples flux ($i_d$) and torque ($i_q$) ‚Äî gives DC-motor-like control to an AC machine.
- **DTC** offers fastest response at the cost of higher torque ripple and noise.
- **For EV battery sizing:** $P_{batt} = T_e \cdot \omega_m / (\eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{inverter})$.
- **Regenerative braking** uses the same inverter in reverse ‚Äî motor acts as generator, charging the DC bus.
- **Tesla's choice of induction motors** (over PMSMs in early models) was driven by **lower cost (no rare-earth magnets)** and **robustness** ‚Äî proving IM with FOC is a credible EV technology.
- **Most production EVs use FOC + Space Vector PWM (SVPWM)** on **SiC MOSFET inverters** for >95% efficiency.
- **Remember** the magic result: $s_{max} = R_2/X_2$ ‚Äî *independent* of voltage, a favorite KTU short-answer question.
- **Remember** the V/F result: constant $V/f$ ‚üπ constant $T_{max}$ ‚Äî the foundation of every EV inverter control law.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: V/f Control of Induction Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** V/f Control of Induction Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **V/f Control (Volts per Hertz Control)** is a scalar speed-control strategy for three-phase AC induction motors (IMs) in which the **ratio of stator RMS line voltage ($V_s$) to the supply frequency ($f_s$) is held constant** across the entire operating speed range. Also known as *constant volts-per-hertz control* or *linear V/f law*, it is the industry-standard, low-complexity torque-curve shaping method used in electric vehicle (EV) traction inverters, industrial drives, and HVAC compressors.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$\frac{V_s}{f_s} = \text{constant} = k$$

This single constraint, when properly implemented, allows the air-gap flux ($\Phi$) of the machine to remain approximately **constant at its rated value**, thereby preserving the motor's natural torque-producing capability across the speed envelope.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU OEEET722 Module 2):** V/f control belongs to the family of *scalar control* methods, as opposed to *vector (FOC)* or *direct torque control (DTC)*. It is the baseline traction-motor control algorithm expected to be thoroughly understood before progressing to advanced EV drive strategies.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Water-Pressure vs. Tap-Flow" Intuition

Imagine a garden hose connected to a municipal water pipeline:

- **Voltage ($V_s$)** is analogous to **water pressure** at the tap.
- **Frequency ($f_s$)** is analogous to **how fast you open and close the tap (oscillation rate of flow)**.
- **Magnetic Flux ($\Phi$)** is analogous to the **strength of the water stream** actually delivered.

If you open and close the tap very quickly (high $f_s$) but apply only a trickle of pressure (low $V_s$), the water stream is weak ‚Äî equivalent to **flux weakening** in the motor. Conversely, if you slam the tap open hard at high pressure but only nudge it (low $f_s$), the pressure builds up uselessly ‚Äî the motor saturates and overheats.

**V/f control is the art of keeping the water stream (flux) at the *Goldilocks* strength ‚Äî just right ‚Äî by proportionally scaling the pressure to the tap-oscillation rate.** That is the entire engineering philosophy of V/f control in a single sentence.

## 1.3 Why V/f Control Matters in EVs and HEVs

| Application Context | Role of V/f |
|---|---|
| Entry-level EV traction (e.g., two/three-wheelers) | Provides smooth torque during low-speed creep |
| Auxiliary motors (water pump, oil pump, HVAC blower) | Replaces mechanical belt drives with efficient variable-speed units |
| Hybrid vehicle generator mode (ICE ‚Üí ISG/Generator) | Maintains rated flux for maximum power extraction across RPM |
| Industrial conveyor/elevator drives | Energy savings by reducing voltage at low speeds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Industry Metric:** Modern automotive-grade IGBT inverters implementing V/f control typically achieve drive efficiencies of **92%‚Äì96%** in the constant-torque region, with switching frequencies in the range of **5 kHz ‚Äì 20 kHz**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Reference Values

- **Rated stator voltage** ($V_{rated}$): typically **400 V** (line-to-line, three-phase) or **230 V** for low-voltage EV auxiliary systems.
- **Rated frequency** ($f_{rated}$): **50 Hz** (India/Europe) or **60 Hz** (USA).
- **Base/turnover speed** ($N_{base}$): motor speed at rated $V$ and rated $f$.
- **Number of poles** ($P$): 2, 4, 6, or 8 for traction motors (4-pole most common).
- **Air-gap flux reference** ($\Phi_{rated}$): typically **0.7 ‚Äì 0.9 Wb** for a 4-pole, 50 Hz industrial-grade machine.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Governing Physics ‚Äî Why Constant V/f Preserves Flux

The induced EMF per phase in the stator winding of an induction motor is given by Faraday's law applied to a rotating machine:

$$E_s = 4.44 \, f_s \, N_s \, \Phi_m \, k_w$$

Where:
- $E_s$ = RMS induced EMF per phase (V)
- $f_s$ = supply frequency (Hz)
- $N_s$ = number of turns per phase
- $\Phi_m$ = peak air-gap flux per pole (Wb)
- $k_w$ = winding factor (typically 0.85 ‚Äì 0.95)

Under steady-state operation, the stator voltage equation is:

$$V_s \approx E_s + I_s (R_s + jX_s)$$

At light load (typical during speed sweeps), the stator current $I_s$ is small, and the resistive/impedance drops are negligible. Therefore:

$$V_s \approx E_s = 4.44 \, f_s \, N_s \, \Phi_m \, k_w$$

Rearranging for flux:

$$\Phi_m = \frac{V_s}{4.44 \, f_s \, N_s \, k_w}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Inference:** Since $N_s$ and $k_w$ are fixed machine parameters, $\Phi_m \propto \dfrac{V_s}{f_s}$. Hence, *holding $V_s / f_s$ constant is mathematically equivalent to holding $\Phi_m$ constant*.

## 2.2 Torque Behavior Under Constant V/f

The electromagnetic torque developed by an induction machine in the linear (low-slip) region is:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_s^2 \cdot (R_r / s)}{(R_s + R_r / s)^2 + (X_s + X_r)^2}$$

If $V_s / f_s$ = constant, then at any synchronous speed $\omega_s = 2\pi f_s / P$, the torque-vs-slip curve *translates* along the speed axis without losing its peak amplitude. The result is the characteristic **constant-torque region** from zero speed up to base speed.

## 2.3 The Three Operating Regions of an EV/Industrial Drive

| Region | Speed Range | Voltage Behavior | Frequency Behavior | Flux Behavior | Torque Capability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Constant Torque (Region I)** | $0 \to N_{base}$ | $V_s \propto f_s$ | Increases linearly | $\Phi = \Phi_{rated}$ (constant) | Full rated torque available |
| **Constant Power (Region II)** | $N_{base} \to N_{max}$ | $V_s = V_{rated}$ (saturated) | Continues to increase | $\Phi < \Phi_{rated}$ (weakened) | Torque falls as $1/N$ |
| **Series / Natural (Region III)** | $N > N_{max}$ | $V_s = V_{rated}$ | $f_s > f_{rated}$ | Heavily weakened | Pull-out limited |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EV-Specific Note:** The constant-torque region corresponds to **launch and low-speed hill climbing**, while the constant-power region corresponds to **cruising and high-speed overtaking**. The crossover speed $N_{base}$ is the design point that EV engineers carefully optimize for each vehicle class.

## 2.4 The Low-Frequency Voltage Boost Problem

Below roughly **5 Hz**, the stator copper drop $I_s R_s$ becomes comparable to the back-EMF $E_s$, so simply maintaining $V_s/f_s$ linear leaves the flux **depressed** (since $V_s \approx E_s$ is no longer valid). The mitigation is to add a **voltage boost** $\Delta V$:

$$V_s = k \cdot f_s + \Delta V$$

Where:
- $k = V_{rated} / f_{rated}$
- $\Delta V \approx (0.05 \text{ to } 0.10) \cdot V_{rated}$, depending on $R_s$ and desired low-speed starting torque

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_s / f_s = k$ | Linear V/f law | V/Hz |
| 2 | $\Phi_m = V_s / (4.44 \, f_s \, N_s \, k_w)$ | Flux from EMF equation | Wb |
| 3 | $N_s = 120 \, f_s / P$ | Synchronous speed | RPM |
| 4 | $\omega_s = 2\pi f_s / P$ | Synchronous speed (electrical rad/s) | rad/s |
| 5 | $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ | Per-unit slip | dimensionless |
| 6 | $T_e = (3/\omega_s) \cdot V_s^2 \cdot (R_r/s) / [(R_s + R_r/s)^2 + (X_s + X_r)^2]$ | Electromagnetic torque | N¬∑m |
| 7 | $T_{max} = (3/2\omega_s) \cdot V_s^2 / (X_s + X_r)$ | Pull-out (maximum) torque | N¬∑m |
| 8 | $s_{max} = R_r / \sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_r)^2}$ | Slip at maximum torque | dimensionless |
| 9 | $V_s = k f_s + \Delta V$ | Low-frequency boost equation | V |
| 10 | $\eta = P_{out} / (P_{out} + P_{losses})$ | Drive efficiency | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU Notation Rule:** The vertical pipe symbol `$\vert$` is reserved for tables only. When writing absolute value or "divided by" in your exam answer, use `$\mid$` or `$\big/$` in LaTeX, or simply use the phrase "magnitude of" in plain text to avoid markdown table breakage in your answer sheets.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Electric Two-Wheelers (Hero Electric, Ola S1, Ather):** Use V/f-controlled BLDC/induction hub motors for simplicity and inverter cost reduction.
2. **Regenerative Braking:** V/f controller can be commanded to operate in the **plugging/regenerative region** ($s < 0$) by overspeeding the rotor beyond synchronous speed.
3. **Battery Voltage Sag Compensation:** V/f ratio can be dynamically adjusted to compensate for **DC-link droop** during high-torque transients, preserving flux during acceleration.
4. **Traction Motor Soft-Start:** Prevents inrush currents that would otherwise weld relay contacts and trip protection circuits.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Constant V/f Yields Constant Flux

**Starting Point ‚Äî Faraday's Law for a Rotating Stator Winding:**

$$E_s = 4.44 \, f_s \, N_s \, \Phi_m \, k_w$$

**Step 1: Assume the Stator Copper and Leakage Drops Are Negligible**

In the no-load and low-load regime, the stator current is small, hence $I_s R_s \ll E_s$ and $I_s X_s \ll E_s$. The KVL around the stator loop reduces to:

$$V_s \approx E_s$$

**Step 2: Substitute the EMF Expression into the Approximation**

$$V_s = 4.44 \, f_s \, N_s \, \Phi_m \, k_w$$

**Step 3: Solve Explicitly for $\Phi_m$**

$$\Phi_m = \frac{V_s}{4.44 \, f_s \, N_s \, k_w}$$

**Step 4: Define the V/f Constant**

Let the constant of proportionality be $k$, such that $V_s = k f_s$. Substituting:

$$\Phi_m = \frac{k \, f_s}{4.44 \, f_s \, N_s \, k_w} = \frac{k}{4.44 \, N_s \, k_w}$$

**Step 5: Final Result**

The frequency $f_s$ cancels out entirely, leaving $\Phi_m$ as a function **only of machine constants and the V/f slope $k$**:

$$\boxed{\Phi_m = \frac{V_s / f_s}{4.44 \, N_s \, k_w} = \text{constant}}$$

This is the mathematical cornerstone of V/f control.

## 3.2 Derivation: Synchronous Speed as a Function of V/f

**Given:** $N_s = 120 \, f_s / P$ (mechanical RPM)

**Substituting $f_s = V_s / k$:**

$$N_s = \frac{120}{P} \cdot \frac{V_s}{k} = \left(\frac{120}{k P}\right) V_s$$

**Conclusion:** Synchronous speed is a **linear function of stator voltage** under constant V/f, providing direct, predictable, open-loop speed command ‚Äî a major benefit for EV traction control loops.

## 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Torque Invariance Under V/f

Starting from the well-known induction-machine torque expression:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_s^2 \cdot (R_r / s)}{(R_s + R_r / s)^2 + (X_s + X_r)^2}$$

**Step 1: Take the derivative** $\dfrac{dT_e}{ds} = 0$ **and solve for the slip at peak torque:**

$$s_{max} = \frac{R_r}{\sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_r)^2}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute $s_{max}$ back into the torque equation to obtain peak torque:**

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{2\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_s^2}{R_s + \sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_r)^2}}$$

**Step 3: Apply the V/f constraint** $V_s = k f_s$ **and** $\omega_s = 2\pi f_s / P$:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{2 \cdot (2\pi f_s / P)} \cdot \frac{(k f_s)^2}{R_s + \sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_r)^2}}$$

$$T_{max} = \frac{3P}{4\pi} \cdot \frac{k^2 f_s}{R_s + \sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_r)^2}}$$

**Step 4: Recognize that** $(X_s + X_r) = 2\pi f_s (L_s + L_r)$**, so the denominator scales with** $f_s$**. The exact cancellation gives:**

$$T_{max} \approx \text{constant} \quad \text{(independent of } f_s \text{)}$$

**This proves mathematically that under constant V/f, the maximum available torque remains invariant across the entire speed range ‚Äî the defining property of the constant-torque region.**

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

> A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz, 400 V induction motor has the following parameters per phase: $R_s = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $R_r = 0.6 \, \Omega$, $X_s = X_r = 1.2 \, \Omega$. Determine the synchronous speed, the slip for maximum torque, and the maximum torque at (a) rated 50 Hz and (b) reduced 25 Hz under constant V/f.

### Solution

**Given:** $P = 4$, $V_{LL} = 400 \text{ V}$, $f_{rated} = 50 \text{ Hz}$.
Per-phase voltage: $V_{ph} = 400 / \sqrt{3} = 230.94 \text{ V}$.

**Part (a): At 50 Hz**

**Step 1: Synchronous speed**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ RPM}$$

$$\omega_s = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2: Slip at maximum torque**

$$s_{max} = \frac{0.6}{\sqrt{0.5^2 + (1.2 + 1.2)^2}} = \frac{0.6}{\sqrt{0.25 + 5.76}} = \frac{0.6}{\sqrt{6.01}} = \frac{0.6}{2.452} = 0.2447$$

**Step 3: Maximum torque**

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{2 \times 157.08} \cdot \frac{230.94^2}{0.5 + 2.452}$$

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{314.16} \cdot \frac{53332.8}{2.952} = 0.009549 \times 18066.5 = 172.5 \text{ N¬∑m}$$

**Part (b): At 25 Hz under constant V/f**

**Step 1: New voltage** ($V_s = k f_s$, with $k = 230.94 / 50 = 4.619$)

$$V_{ph,\,new} = 4.619 \times 25 = 115.47 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: New synchronous speed**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 25}{4} = 750 \text{ RPM}, \quad \omega_s = 78.54 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Reactances scale with frequency.** At 25 Hz, the leakage reactances halve:

$$X_s = X_r = 1.2 \times (25/50) = 0.6 \, \Omega$$

**Step 4: New slip for maximum torque**

$$s_{max} = \frac{0.6}{\sqrt{0.5^2 + (0.6 + 0.6)^2}} = \frac{0.6}{\sqrt{0.25 + 1.44}} = \frac{0.6}{1.30} = 0.4615$$

**Step 5: New maximum torque**

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{2 \times 78.54} \cdot \frac{115.47^2}{0.5 + 1.30}$$

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{13333.2}{1.80} = 0.01910 \times 7407.3 = 141.5 \text{ N¬∑m}$$

**Step 6: Without voltage boost, the maximum torque has dropped by** $\approx 18\%$**, illustrating the need for low-frequency voltage boost** $\Delta V$ **to recover full torque capability.**

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix: V/f vs. Other Control Schemes

| Parameter | V/f (Scalar) | Field-Oriented Control (FOC) | Direct Torque Control (DTC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Control variable | $V_s$ and $f_s$ (magnitude only) | $i_d$ and $i_q$ (vector components) | Torque and flux (hysteresis) |
| Speed range | Up to 1:20 typical | Up to 1:10,000+ | Up to 1:1000+ |
| Torque accuracy | Moderate (5‚Äì10%) | High (<2%) | High (<3%) |
| Dynamic response | Slow (~50‚Äì100 ms) | Fast (~1‚Äì5 ms) | Very fast (~0.5‚Äì2 ms) |
| DSP/MCU demand | Low (8-bit MCU sufficient) | High (DSP/FPGA required) | High (DSP/FPGA required) |
| Cost in EV inverter | Lowest | High | Medium |
| Use in HEV/EV | Auxiliary drives, low-cost 2W | Main traction (Tesla Model S, Tata Nexon EV) | Mid-segment EV (Renault Zoe variants) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - June 2023]**

**Q:** Define V/f control of a three-phase induction motor. Why is it essential to maintain a constant V/f ratio in variable-frequency drives?

**Model Answer:**

V/f control is a scalar speed-control technique for three-phase AC induction motors in which the **ratio of stator RMS voltage ($V_s$) to supply frequency ($f_s$) is held constant** throughout the operating range, i.e., $V_s / f_s = k$ (constant).

It is essential because the air-gap flux $\Phi_m$ is directly proportional to $V_s / f_s$ (from $V_s \approx 4.44 \, f_s \, N_s \, \Phi_m \, k_w$). Maintaining this ratio constant ensures that the air-gap flux remains at its rated value, which in turn preserves the **torque-producing capability** of the motor across the entire speed range. If $V/f$ is not maintained (e.g., $V$ is reduced without reducing $f$), flux weakens and torque drops sharply; conversely, increasing $V$ without increasing $f$ causes magnetic saturation, excessive current, and motor overheating.

**(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for flux/torque justification)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - December 2022]**

**Q:** What is voltage boost in V/f control? Under what condition is it required, and what is its typical magnitude?

**Model Answer:**

Voltage boost (also called *torque boost* or *low-frequency compensation*) is the technique of adding a small offset voltage $\Delta V$ to the linear V/f law at very low operating frequencies (typically below 5 Hz):

$$V_s = k \cdot f_s + \Delta V$$

It is required because, at low frequencies, the stator copper drop $I_s R_s$ becomes a significant fraction of the back-EMF $E_s$, causing the flux to weaken despite maintaining $V/f$ constant. Without boost, the motor exhibits poor starting torque and sluggish low-speed response.

The typical magnitude of $\Delta V$ is **5% to 10% of rated voltage**, adjusted experimentally based on the machine's stator resistance and the desired low-speed starting torque.

**(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for condition + 1 mark for magnitude)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ‚Äî Internal Choice)

### Choice Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**Q:** (a) Derive the relationship between stator voltage, frequency, and air-gap flux in a three-phase induction motor. Show mathematically how constant V/f control maintains constant flux. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz, 400 V induction motor has the following per-phase parameters referred to the stator: $R_s = 0.4 \, \Omega$, $R_r = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $X_s = 1.0 \, \Omega$, $X_r = 1.0 \, \Omega$. Calculate the slip at maximum torque and the maximum torque at (i) 50 Hz and (ii) 30 Hz under constant V/f operation. Comment on the need for low-frequency voltage boost. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1: The EMF equation of an induction motor stator winding is:**

$$E_s = 4.44 \, f_s \, N_s \, \Phi_m \, k_w$$

where $E_s$ is the RMS EMF per phase, $f_s$ is the supply frequency, $N_s$ is the turns per phase, $\Phi_m$ is the peak air-gap flux per pole, and $k_w$ is the winding factor.

**Step 2: Applying KVL to the stator phase circuit:**

$$V_s = E_s + I_s (R_s + jX_s)$$

**Step 3: At light load, $I_s$ is small and the impedance drop is negligible, so:**

$$V_s \approx E_s = 4.44 \, f_s \, N_s \, \Phi_m \, k_w$$

**Step 4: Solving for flux:**

$$\Phi_m = \frac{V_s}{4.44 \, f_s \, N_s \, k_w}$$

**Step 5: Substituting the V/f constraint** $V_s = k f_s$:

$$\Phi_m = \frac{k f_s}{4.44 \, f_s \, N_s \, k_w} = \frac{k}{4.44 \, N_s \, k_w} = \text{constant}$$

**Step 6: Conclusion:** Since $N_s$ and $k_w$ are fixed machine parameters, $\Phi_m$ becomes a function of only the V/f slope $k$, which is held constant. Therefore, **constant V/f control guarantees constant air-gap flux**, which in turn ensures the motor delivers its **rated torque capability** at any operating frequency in the constant-torque region.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Given:**
- $P = 4$, $V_{LL} = 400$ V, $f_{rated} = 50$ Hz
- $R_s = 0.4 \, \Omega$, $R_r = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $X_s = X_r = 1.0 \, \Omega$ (at 50 Hz)
- $V_{ph} = 400 / \sqrt{3} = 230.94$ V

**Formula for slip at maximum torque:**

$$s_{max} = \frac{R_r}{\sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_r)^2}}$$

**Formula for maximum torque:**

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{2\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_s^2}{R_s + \sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_r)^2}}$$

---

**(i) At 50 Hz:**

$$s_{max} = \frac{0.5}{\sqrt{0.4^2 + (1.0 + 1.0)^2}} = \frac{0.5}{\sqrt{0.16 + 4.0}} = \frac{0.5}{\sqrt{4.16}} = \frac{0.5}{2.0396} = 0.2451$$

$$\omega_s = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{2 \times 157.08} \cdot \frac{230.94^2}{0.4 + 2.0396} = \frac{3}{314.16} \cdot \frac{53332.8}{2.4396} = 0.009549 \times 21861 = 208.7 \text{ N¬∑m}$$

---

**(ii) At 30 Hz under constant V/f:**

New per-phase voltage: $V_{ph} = 230.94 \times (30/50) = 138.56$ V
New synchronous speed: $N_s = (120 \times 30)/4 = 900$ RPM, $\omega_s = 94.25$ rad/s
Reactances at 30 Hz: $X_s = X_r = 1.0 \times (30/50) = 0.6 \, \Omega$

$$s_{max} = \frac{0.5}{\sqrt{0.4^2 + (0.6 + 0.6)^2}} = \frac{0.5}{\sqrt{0.16 + 1.44}} = \frac{0.5}{1.262} = 0.3962$$

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{2 \times 94.25} \cdot \frac{138.56^2}{0.4 + 1.262} = \frac{3}{188.5} \cdot \frac{19199.0}{1.662} = 0.01591 \times 11551.7 = 183.7 \text{ N¬∑m}$$

**Comment on voltage boost:** The maximum torque has decreased from 208.7 N¬∑m at 50 Hz to 183.7 N¬∑m at 30 Hz (a drop of ~12%) despite maintaining linear V/f. This is because the stator copper drop $I_s R_s$ is no longer negligible at low frequencies, causing the actual flux to weaken. **To compensate, a low-frequency voltage boost $\Delta V$ is added to the V/f law**, restoring the flux to its rated value and recovering full torque capability.

---

### Choice Question B (14 Marks ‚Äî Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**Q:** (a) With the help of a block diagram, explain the closed-loop V/f control scheme for a three-phase induction motor drive used in an electric vehicle application. Identify the function of each block. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare V/f control, Field-Oriented Control (FOC), and Direct Torque Control (DTC) for EV traction applications. Justify why V/f control is preferred for low-cost auxiliary drives while FOC is preferred for main traction. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Block Diagram Description (corresponds to Section 4.4 of these notes):**

1. **Speed Command Reference (Nref):** Driver accelerator pedal or EV torque command ECU provides the desired speed reference.

2. **Reference Frequency Generator:** Converts the speed command into a frequency reference $f_{ref} = P \cdot N_{ref} / 120$ based on the motor's pole count.

3. **V/f Profile Lookup Table:** Generates the voltage reference $V_{ref} = k \cdot f_{ref} + \Delta V$ (with low-frequency boost for $f < 5$ Hz). This block encapsulates the **constant V/f law**.

4. **Sine PWM Modulator:** Generates three-phase SPWM gate signals by comparing the reference sinusoids with a high-frequency triangular carrier.

5. **Three-Phase VSI Inverter (IGBT/SiC):** Converts the DC battery voltage into variable-voltage, variable-frequency three-phase AC.

6. **Induction Motor:** Converts electrical power into mechanical torque on the rotor shaft.

7. **Rotor Speed Sensor (Encoder/Tachogenerator):** Provides actual speed feedback.

8. **Speed Error Generator:** Computes $e = N_{ref} - N_{actual}$ and feeds it to a PI controller, which adjusts $f_{ref}$ to null the error.

9. **Current Sensor and Protection Block:** Monitors inverter output current; implements overcurrent and short-circuit protection by gating OFF the IGBTs.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | V/f Control | FOC (Vector Control) | DTC |
|---|---|---|---|
| Control philosophy | Scalar (magnitude only) | Vector (decoupled flux & torque) | Hysteresis-based torque/flux |
| Computational complexity | Low | High | Medium |
| Dynamic response | Slow | Fast | Very fast |
| Torque accuracy | ¬±5 to ¬±10% | ¬±1 to ¬±2% | ¬±2 to ¬±3% |
| Speed range | 1:20 | 1:10,000+ | 1:1000+ |
| Sensor requirement | Optional (open-loop) | Mandatory (encoder, current sensors) | Speed sensor optional |
| Cost | Lowest | Highest | Medium |
| Inverter cost in EV | ‚Çπ3,000‚Äì‚Çπ5,000 (low power) | ‚Çπ25,000‚Äì‚Çπ1,50,000+ (traction) | ‚Çπ15,000‚Äì‚Çπ60,000 (mid) |

**Justification:**

- **V/f control is preferred for low-cost auxiliary EV drives** (water pump, oil pump, HVAC blower, radiator fan) because these applications do not require precise torque or fast dynamic response. They run at quasi-steady speeds, prioritize energy efficiency and BOM cost reduction, and can tolerate the open-loop speed variation of V/f drives. A simple 8-bit or 32-bit MCU is sufficient for implementation.

- **FOC is preferred for main EV traction** because the propulsion motor demands:
  - Precise torque control at zero speed (launching on inclines)
  - Fast dynamic response during acceleration/braking
  - High efficiency across a wide speed range (city + highway driving)
  - Regenerative braking with smooth torque reversal

  V/f control cannot meet these demands, especially at low speeds where its open-loop nature leads to torque pulsations and poor starting behavior.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Using line voltage instead of phase voltage in torque calculations (and vice versa).** Always convert $V_{LL}$ to $V_{ph} = V_{LL} / \sqrt{3}$ before substituting into per-phase equations like $T_{max}$.
> 2. **Forgetting to scale reactances with frequency.** In Part (b) numericals, leakage reactances $X_s$ and $X_r$ are frequency-dependent: $X(f) = X_{rated} \times (f / f_{rated})$. Many students keep them constant and lose 2‚Äì3 marks.
> 3. **Confusing synchronous speed $N_s$ with rotor speed $N_r$.** $N_s = 120 f / P$ is the speed of the rotating magnetic field; the actual rotor turns at $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$.
> 4. **Omitting the units in the final answer.** KTU examiners often deduct 0.5 marks for missing N¬∑m, RPM, Hz, Wb, etc.
> 5. **Writing $V_s / f_s$ without stating that it is the *RMS line-to-line* or *phase* quantity.** Be explicit: "per-phase RMS voltage" is the safe phrase.
> 6. **Not mentioning the low-frequency boost in long answers.** Even if the question is on the V/f law in general, always add a concluding sentence on $\Delta V$ to demonstrate deeper understanding ‚Äî examiners reward this.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for V/f Control of Induction Motor**

- [x] **Definition:** V/f control maintains $V_s / f_s$ = constant to preserve air-gap flux $\Phi_m$ at its rated value.
- [x] **Core equation:** $\Phi_m = V_s / (4.44 \, f_s \, N_s \, k_w)$.
- [x] **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = 120 f_s / P$; rotor speed $N_r = N_s(1 - s)$.
- [x] **Three operating regions:** Constant torque (0 ‚Üí N_base), constant power (N_base ‚Üí N_max), field weakening (N > N_max).
- [x] **Low-frequency boost:** Add $\Delta V$ for $f < 5$ Hz to compensate for stator $I_s R_s$ drop.
- [x] **Maximum torque invariance:** $T_{max}$ remains approximately constant across speed range under ideal V/f.
- [x] **Slip at maximum torque:** $s_{max} = R_r / \sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_r)^2}$.
- [x] **In EV/HEV context:** V/f control is used in **auxiliary drives** (low cost) and **two/three-wheeler traction** (cost-sensitive); main EV traction prefers FOC.
- [x] **Regenerative capability:** Achievable in V/f drive by operating at $s < 0$ (overspeed region).
- [x] **Implementation:** Sine-PWM or Space-Vector PWM (SVPWM) with IGBT/SiC MOSFET inverter, 5‚Äì20 kHz switching.
- [x] **Efficiency range:** 92%‚Äì96% in constant-torque region; drops in field-weakening region.
- [x] **Disadvantages:** Open-loop speed inaccuracy, no torque ripple minimization, poor low-speed performance without boost.
- [x] **Key KTU keywords to use in answers:** "constant V/f ratio," "constant air-gap flux," "constant-torque region," "field weakening," "voltage boost," "scalar control."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Battery, Battery Parameters, Units of Battery Energy Storage, Capacity Rate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Battery, Battery Parameters, Units of Battery Energy Storage, Capacity Rate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Battery ‚Äî Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Battery (Electrochemical Energy Storage Device):** A battery is a **galvanic (voltaic) cell** or an assembly of such cells that converts stored **chemical energy** into **electrical energy** through spontaneous redox (reduction‚Äìoxidation) reactions. In the context of **Hybrid and Electric Vehicles (HEVs/EVs)**, a battery functions as the **primary on-board Energy Storage System (ESS)**, supplying electrical power to the traction motor, auxiliary loads, and regenerative braking recovery circuits.

A battery is rated by its **nominal voltage (V)**, **capacity (Ah)**, **energy content (Wh or kWh)**, and **C-rate**, all of which must be tightly matched to the vehicle's power-train demand profile.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ‚Äî "The Water Tank Model"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a water tank connected to a tap.
> - The **size of the tank** = **Capacity (Ah)** ‚Äî how much charge it can hold.
> - The **water pressure** = **Voltage (V)** ‚Äî how forcefully the charge is delivered.
> - The **total water you can spray** = **Energy (Wh = V √ó Ah)** ‚Äî the actual useful work.
> - **How fast you open the tap** = **C-rate** ‚Äî the speed of discharge.
> - **Sediment buildup over time** = **Internal resistance rise / State of Health (SOH) loss** with ageing.
> - **Scale inside the tank** = **State of Charge (SOC) indicator**.

A bigger tank with higher pressure = more energy. Opening the tap wider (higher C-rate) drains it faster and stresses the system more. This is exactly how a **Traction Battery Pack** behaves in an EV.

---

### 1.3 Types of Batteries (HEV/EV Relevant)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Module 3 mandates study of the following battery chemistries used in modern vehicles:

| Category | Examples | Key Trait |
|----------|----------|-----------|
| **Lead-Acid (Pb-acid)** | SLI, VRLA, AGM | Mature, cheap, low energy density |
| **Nickel-Metal Hydride (NiMH)** | Toyota Prius Gen 1‚Äì3 packs | Reliable, moderate energy density |
| **Lithium-Ion (Li-ion) family** | LFP, NMC, NCA, LTO | High energy & power density |
| **Solid-State Batteries** | Sulfide / Oxide / Polymer electrolytes | Next-gen, non-flammable |
| **Sodium-Ion / Zinc-Air** | Emerging chemistries | Low-cost alternatives |

The **Lithium-ion family** dominates contemporary EV traction packs because of superior **specific energy (Wh/kg)** and **cycle life**.

---

### 1.4 Core Battery Parameters ‚Äî Quick Glossary

> [!NOTE]
> **Essential Parameter Vocabulary (KTU Board-Exam Favourite):**

- **Nominal Voltage (V‚Çô):** The reference operating voltage of a single cell.
- **Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV):** Terminal voltage when no load is connected.
- **Terminal Voltage (V‚Çú):** Voltage under load ‚Äî always less than OCV due to internal resistance drop.
- **Capacity (C, Ah):** Total charge a battery can deliver; 1 Ah = 3600 Coulombs.
- **Energy (E, Wh):** Total work extractable = V‚Çô √ó C.
- **C-rate:** Discharge/charge current normalised to rated capacity.
- **State of Charge (SOC):** Present charge level as a percentage of full charge.
- **Depth of Discharge (DOD):** Complement of SOC; charge used so far.
- **State of Health (SOH):** Current maximum capacity √∑ rated capacity, expressed as a percentage.
- **Internal Resistance (R·µ¢):** Opposition to current flow inside the cell (mŒ© level).
- **Cycle Life:** Number of complete charge‚Äìdischarge cycles before capacity fades to 80 % of rated value.
- **Specific Energy (Wh/kg):** Energy per unit mass.
- **Energy Density (Wh/L):** Energy per unit volume.
- **Specific Power (W/kg):** Power-delivery capability per unit mass.

---

### 1.5 Units of Battery Energy Storage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard SI & Practical Units used in EV Industry:**

- **Charge:** Coulomb (C), Ampere-hour (Ah), where **1 Ah = 3600 C**.
- **Energy:** Joule (J), Watt-hour (Wh), kilowatt-hour (kWh), where **1 kWh = 3.6 √ó 10‚Å∂ J = 3.6 MJ**.
- **Power:** Watt (W), kilowatt (kW).
- **Energy Density (Gravimetric):** Wh/kg.
- **Energy Density (Volumetric):** Wh/L.
- **Specific Power:** W/kg.
- **Operating Temperature:** ¬∞C or K.
- **Self-Discharge Rate:** % per month.

A typical EV traction pack stores between **30 kWh (small EV)** to **100+ kWh (long-range EV)**. The 1 kWh benchmark is sometimes used as a **rule-of-thumb unit cost reference**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Different Battery Types Exist ‚Äî The Engineering Trade-off

Batteries are not "one-size-fits-all." Each chemistry represents a compromise along four engineering axes:

1. **Energy Density** ‚Äî How far can the vehicle go on a full charge?
2. **Power Density** ‚Äî How fast can it accelerate / accept regen braking?
3. **Cycle Life** ‚Äî How long before replacement?
4. **Safety / Cost** ‚Äî Thermal runaway risk and $ per kWh.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the EV industry moved from Pb-acid ‚Üí NiMH ‚Üí Li-ion:**
> Energy density increased from ~**35 Wh/kg (Pb-acid)** ‚Üí ~**80 Wh/kg (NiMH)** ‚Üí ~**150‚Äì250 Wh/kg (Li-ion NMC)**. This directly translated to **longer driving range** without increasing pack weight.

---

### 2.2 Comparison of Major EV Battery Chemistries

| Chemistry | Nominal V (V) | Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | Cycle Life | EV Application |
|-----------|---------------|--------------------------|------------|----------------|
| Lead-Acid (VRLA) | 2.0 | 30‚Äì40 | 300‚Äì500 | 12 V aux, mild-hybrid |
| NiMH | 1.2 | 60‚Äì90 | 1000+ | Toyota Prius (Gen 1‚Äì3) |
| Li-ion LFP | 3.2 | 90‚Äì160 | 2000‚Äì5000 | BYD, e-buses, entry EVs |
| Li-ion NMC | 3.7 | 150‚Äì220 | 1000‚Äì2000 | Hyundai, Kia, VW, MG |
| Li-ion NCA | 3.6 | 200‚Äì260 | 500‚Äì1000 | Tesla (legacy) |
| Li-Titanate LTO | 2.4 | 60‚Äì80 | 10000+ | Ultra-fast charging EVs |
| Solid-State (emerging) | ~3.7 | 300‚Äì500 (target) | TBD | 2027+ production EVs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick ‚Äî "LFP is Safe, NMC is Normal, NCA is Niche, LTO is Long-life, Solid is the Star."**

---

### 2.3 Detailed Battery Parameter Analysis

#### A. Voltage Parameters
- **Electromotive Force (EMF / OCV):** Determined by Nernst equation based on electrolyte concentration and electrode chemistry.
- **Terminal Voltage:** V‚Çú = OCV ‚àí I √ó R·µ¢ (during discharge).
- **Cut-off Voltage:** Manufacturer-defined minimum allowed V‚Çú; discharging below it permanently damages the cell.

#### B. Capacity
- **Theoretical Capacity** = (n √ó F) / (3600 √ó M), where n = electrons transferred, F = Faraday constant, M = molar mass of active material.
- **Practical Capacity < Theoretical** because of side reactions, inactive mass, and electrolyte depletion.

#### C. Internal Resistance
- **Ohmic part (RŒ©):** Electrode + electrolyte + contact resistance.
- **Charge-transfer part (Rct):** From electrochemical reaction kinetics.
- **Diffusion part (Wd):** From mass-transport limitations.
- **Total R·µ¢ = RŒ© + Rct + Wd** ‚Äî rises as SOC drops and as the cell ages.

#### D. Self-Discharge
All batteries lose charge when idle. Rate: ~**3‚Äì20 % per month** depending on chemistry and temperature.

#### E. Efficiency
- **Coulombic Efficiency (Œ∑C)** = (Charge out √∑ Charge in) √ó 100 %.
- **Energy Efficiency (Œ∑E)** = (Energy out √∑ Energy in) √ó 100 %.
- Li-ion: Œ∑C > 99 %, Œ∑E ‚âà 90‚Äì95 %.

---

### 2.4 Capacity Rate (C-Rate) ‚Äî The Heart of EV Powertrain Sizing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **C-Rate Definition (KTU Board Definition):**
> The **C-rate** is the **discharge or charge current** of a battery expressed as a **multiple of its rated capacity**. A **1C rate** means the current that would **fully discharge the battery in 1 hour**. An **nC rate** means the current that would **fully discharge the battery in (1/n) hours**.

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$C\text{-rate} = \dfrac{I_{\text{discharge}}}{C_{\text{rated}}}$$

where $I_{\text{discharge}}$ is in Amperes and $C_{\text{rated}}$ is in Ampere-hours (Ah).

**Inverse relationship with discharge time:**

$$t_{\text{discharge}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{rated}}}{I} = \dfrac{1}{C\text{-rate}} \text{ (hours)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real EV Example ‚Äî Tesla Model 3 LR Pack:**
> Capacity ‚âà **75 kWh** at ~**350 V nominal** ‚Üí pack Ah ‚âà 214 Ah.
> Peak discharge current during launch ‚âà **1500 A** ‚Üí C-rate ‚âà 1500 / 214 ‚âà **7C**.
> This means the pack is designed to deliver its full energy in just **~8.5 minutes** at peak power. A normal driving C-rate is around **0.3C‚Äì0.5C**.

---

### 2.5 Units of Battery Energy Storage ‚Äî Conversion Hierarchy

$$1 \text{ kWh} = 1000 \text{ Wh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$$

$$1 \text{ Ah} \times V_{\text{nominal}} = 1 \text{ Wh (at that voltage)}$$

$$E_{\text{stored}} = V_{\text{nominal}} \times C_{\text{Ah}} \quad [\text{Wh}]$$

$$E_{\text{stored}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{nominal}} \times C_{\text{Ah}}}{1000} \quad [\text{kWh}]$$

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All equations you must memorise for Module-3 KTU Board Exam:**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $E = V \times C$ | Energy stored | Wh |
| 2 | $E = V \times I \times t$ | Energy from power √ó time | Wh |
| 3 | $P = V \times I$ | Instantaneous power | W |
| 4 | $E = P \times t$ | Energy = power √ó time | Wh |
| 5 | $C\text{-rate} = I / C_{\text{rated}}$ | C-rate definition | dimensionless |
| 6 | $t = 1 / C\text{-rate}$ | Discharge time at constant C-rate | hours |
| 7 | $I = C_{\text{rated}} \times C\text{-rate}$ | Current from C-rate | A |
| 8 | $SOC = (C_{\text{remaining}} / C_{\text{rated}}) \times 100$ | State of Charge | % |
| 9 | $DOD = 100 - SOC$ | Depth of Discharge | % |
| 10 | $SOH = (C_{\text{max,present}} / C_{\text{rated,new}}) \times 100$ | State of Health | % |
| 11 | $Q = I \times t$ | Charge delivered | Ah |
| 12 | $E_{\text{specific}} = E / m$ | Specific energy | Wh/kg |
| 13 | $P_{\text{specific}} = P / m$ | Specific power | W/kg |
| 14 | $V_t = OCV - I \cdot R_i$ | Terminal voltage under load | V |
| 15 | $\eta_E = (E_{out} / E_{in}) \times 100$ | Energy efficiency | % |
| 16 | $E_{\text{kinetic}} = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Regen braking recoverable energy | J |
| 17 | $R_{\text{total}} = R_\Omega + R_{ct} + W_d$ | Internal resistance components | Œ© |
| 18 | $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$ | Energy unit conversion | ‚Äî |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In EV design, equations 1‚Äì7 are used during **battery pack sizing**; equations 8‚Äì10 drive the **BMS (Battery Management System)** firmware; equations 12‚Äì13 dictate **vehicle range and acceleration performance**; equation 16 links **regenerative braking energy recovery** to motor-controller design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚Äî Relationship Between C-Rate and Discharge Time

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Premise:** C-rate is the current that would discharge a fully charged cell in exactly one hour.

**Step 1:** By definition, the **rated capacity** of a battery is the charge it can deliver over 1 hour at its rated discharge current.

$$C_{\text{rated}} = I_{\text{1C}} \times 1 \text{ hour}$$

This is a direct application of the fundamental charge equation:

$$Q = I \times t \quad \Rightarrow \quad I = \dfrac{Q}{t}$$

**Step 2:** Substituting the rated capacity in Ampere-hours and the discharge time of 1 hour:

$$I_{\text{1C}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{rated}}}{1 \text{ h}} = C_{\text{rated}} \quad \text{[in Amperes, when C is in Ah]}$$

**Step 3:** For a generic C-rate of value $n$ (e.g., 2C, 0.5C, 5C), the discharge current is:

$$I_{nC} = n \times I_{\text{1C}} = n \times C_{\text{rated}}$$

**Step 4:** The discharge time $t$ at this current equals the rated capacity divided by the discharge current:

$$t = \dfrac{C_{\text{rated}}}{I_{nC}}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $I_{nC} = n \times C_{\text{rated}}$:

$$t = \dfrac{C_{\text{rated}}}{n \times C_{\text{rated}}}$$

**Step 6:** Cancel $C_{\text{rated}}$ from numerator and denominator:

$$\boxed{\,t = \dfrac{1}{n} \text{ hours}\,}$$

**Interpretation Table:**

| C-rate (n) | Discharge Current (I = nC) | Discharge Time (t) |
|------------|----------------------------|--------------------|
| 0.2C | 0.2 √ó C_rated | 5 h |
| 0.5C | 0.5 √ó C_rated | 2 h |
| 1C | 1 √ó C_rated | 1 h |
| 2C | 2 √ó C_rated | 30 min |
| 5C | 5 √ó C_rated | 12 min |
| 10C | 10 √ó C_rated | 6 min |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always show Step 3‚ÄìStep 6 explicitly. Marks are awarded for **substitution and cancellation** steps, not just the final box.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚Äî Energy Stored vs Capacity vs C-rate

**Step 1:** The total energy stored in a battery is the integral of instantaneous power over the discharge time:

$$E = \int_{0}^{t} P(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2:** For a battery operating at (approximately) constant terminal voltage $V$ and constant current $I$:

$$E = V \times I \times t$$

**Step 3:** But $I \times t = Q$, the charge delivered. If the battery is fully discharged:

$$Q = C_{\text{rated}} \quad \text{[in Ah]}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting:

$$E = V \times C_{\text{rated}}$$

**Step 5:** If the discharge is only partial (DOD = x, where $0 \le x \le 1$):

$$E_{\text{usable}} = x \times V \times C_{\text{rated}}$$

**Step 6:** In SI units (Joules), recognising $1 \text{ V} \times 1 \text{ Ah} = 3600 \text{ J}$:

$$E_{\text{Joules}} = V \times C_{\text{rated}} \times 3600$$

**Step 7:** To convert to kWh, divide by 1000:

$$\boxed{\,E_{\text{kWh}} = \dfrac{V \times C_{\text{rated}}}{1000}\,}$$

**Verification Example (Numerical):**
- A 400 V, 100 Ah pack ‚Üí E = 400 √ó 100 / 1000 = **40 kWh**. ‚úì
- A 350 V, 214 Ah pack (Tesla Model 3) ‚Üí E = 350 √ó 214 / 1000 ‚âà **74.9 kWh**. ‚úì

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚Äî Power Delivered at a Given C-rate

**Step 1:** Instantaneous power delivered by the battery:

$$P = V_{\text{terminal}} \times I$$

**Step 2:** Substituting $I = n \times C_{\text{rated}}$:

$$P = V_{\text{terminal}} \times n \times C_{\text{rated}}$$

**Step 3:** Using $V \times C_{\text{rated}} = E$ (rated energy in Wh):

$$P = n \times E_{\text{rated,Wh}}$$

**Step 4:** To get power in kW:

$$\boxed{\,P_{\text{kW}} = n \times E_{\text{rated,kWh}}\,}$$

**Interpretation:** A battery with **75 kWh** rated energy discharged at **2C** delivers:
- $P = 2 \times 75 = 150$ kW of peak power.

This is the equation used by EV manufacturers to **advertise peak motor power** from the battery pack.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 ‚Äî Peukert's Law (Capacity Fading at High C-rate)

> [!NOTE]
> **Background:** A real battery delivers **less usable capacity** at high discharge currents than its rated value. This is captured empirically by **Peukert's Law (1897)**.

**Step 1:** Empirical statement of Peukert's Law:

$$t = H \times \left( \dfrac{C_{\text{rated}}}{I \times H} \right)^{n}$$

where:
- $t$ = actual discharge time (hours)
- $H$ = rated discharge time (hours, typically 20 h for lead-acid)
- $C_{\text{rated}}$ = rated capacity (Ah)
- $I$ = actual discharge current (A)
- $n$ = Peukert's exponent (1.0 < n < 1.5; typically ~1.2 for lead-acid, ~1.05 for Li-ion)

**Step 2:** Simplify by setting $H = 1$ (i.e., rate relative to 1C):

$$t = \left( \dfrac{C_{\text{rated}}}{I} \right)^{n}$$

**Step 3:** The effective capacity delivered is $C_{\text{eff}} = I \times t$:

$$C_{\text{eff}} = I \times \left( \dfrac{C_{\text{rated}}}{I} \right)^{n} = I^{1-n} \times C_{\text{rated}}^{n}$$

**Step 4:** Take logarithms to linearise (useful for experimental data fitting):

$$\log(t) = n \cdot \log(C_{\text{rated}}) - n \cdot \log(I) + \log(H)$$

**Step 5:** Example ‚Äî a 100 Ah lead-acid battery with n = 1.25:
- At 5 A discharge (0.05C) ‚Üí t = (100/5)^1.25 = 20^1.25 ‚âà 47.7 h ‚Üí C_eff ‚âà 238 Ah (effective).
- At 50 A discharge (0.5C) ‚Üí t = (100/50)^1.25 = 2^1.25 ‚âà 2.38 h ‚Üí C_eff ‚âà 119 Ah.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Significance:** Peukert's Law is why EV packs specify a **maximum continuous C-rate** (typically 1C‚Äì2C) and a **peak C-rate** (3C‚Äì10C) separately. Operating at very high C-rates continuously causes irreversible capacity loss.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 ‚Äî Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery Efficiency

**Step 1:** Kinetic energy of a moving vehicle of mass $m$ at speed $v$:

$$E_{\text{kinetic}} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2$$

**Step 2:** Convert speed from km/h to m/s: $v_{\text{m/s}} = v_{\text{km/h}} \times \dfrac{5}{18}$

$$E_{\text{kinetic}} = \dfrac{1}{2} m \left( v_{\text{km/h}} \times \dfrac{5}{18} \right)^2$$

**Step 3:** Regenerative system recovers only a fraction $\eta_{\text{regen}}$ (typically 0.6‚Äì0.8) and battery charge acceptance is $\eta_{\text{chg}}$ (~0.9 for Li-ion):

$$E_{\text{recovered}} = \eta_{\text{regen}} \times \eta_{\text{chg}} \times E_{\text{kinetic}}$$

**Step 4:** Add the **C-rate check** to ensure battery can absorb the regen current:

$$I_{\text{regen}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{regen}}}{V_{\text{bus}}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{brake}} \times v}{V_{\text{bus}}}$$

The regen C-rate is $I_{\text{regen}} / C_{\text{rated}}$, which must be within the battery's **maximum charge C-rate limit** (typically 1C‚Äì2C for Li-ion NMC, up to 5C for LTO).

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
- $m = 1500$ kg, $v = 60$ km/h = 16.67 m/s, $\eta_{\text{regen}} = 0.7$, $\eta_{\text{chg}} = 0.9$.
- $E_{\text{kinetic}} = 0.5 \times 1500 \times (16.67)^2 = 0.5 \times 1500 \times 277.9 = 208{,}425$ J ‚âà **57.9 Wh**.
- $E_{\text{recovered}} = 0.7 \times 0.9 \times 57.9 = 36.5$ Wh per braking event.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 ‚Äî C-Rate Visualisation Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q0[BATTERY RATED CAPACITY C_rated Ah] --> Q1[CHOOSE C RATE n]
    Q1 --> Q2A[n = 0.2C 5 hours DISCHARGE]
    Q1 --> Q2B[n = 1C 1 hour DISCHARGE]
    Q1 --> Q2C[n = 5C 12 minutes DISCHARGE]

    Q2C --> R1
    Q2D --> R1
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 ‚Äî Battery Pack Energy Flow Architecture (HEV/EV)

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC1[GRID AC DC CHARGER] --> CHG[BMS CHARGE CONTROLLER]
    REGEN[REGEN BRAKING MOTOR GENERATOR MODE] --> CHG
    CHG --> CELL[TRACTION BATTERY PACK]
    CELL --> DISCHG[BMS DISCHARGE CONTROLLER]
    DISCHG --> INV[MOTOR INVERTER VSI]
    INV --> MTR[TRACTION MOTOR PMSM or INDUCTION]
    MTR --> WH[VEHICLE WHEELS]
    CELL --> AUX[AUX 12V DC DC CONVERTER]
    AUX --> LOAD[LIGHTS HVAC INFOTAINMENT]
    CELL -.SOC SOH V T SENSING.-> BMS[BMS ECU]
    BMS -.PROTECTION CELL BALANCING.-> CELL
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The **Battery Pack** is the central node. All charging paths (grid + regen) and all discharging paths (traction inverter + aux DC-DC) are routed through the **BMS (Battery Management System)**, which constantly monitors **Voltage, Current, Temperature, SOC, and SOH** to prevent thermal runaway and ensure cycle longevity.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024 Style]**

---

**Q1. Define the term C-rate with a suitable example.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The **C-rate** of a battery is the **discharge or charge current** expressed as a **multiple of its rated capacity** (in Ah). Mathematically, $\text{C-rate} = I / C_{\text{rated}}$, where $I$ is the current in Amperes and $C_{\text{rated}}$ is the rated capacity in Ampere-hours. It is **dimensionless**.
>
> **Example:** A battery rated at **50 Ah** discharged at **25 A** is operating at a C-rate of $25 / 50 = 0.5\text{C}$. The discharge time at this rate is $t = 1 / 0.5 = 2$ hours. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for numerical example.)*

---

**Q2. List any six important battery parameters used to characterise EV traction batteries.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> Six important battery parameters for EV traction batteries are:
> 1. **Nominal Voltage (V‚Çô)** ‚Äî Reference operating voltage per cell.
> 2. **Capacity (C, Ah)** ‚Äî Total charge deliverable.
> 3. **Energy (E, Wh or kWh)** ‚Äî Total work extractable = V √ó C.
> 4. **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** ‚Äî Energy per unit mass, governs range.
> 5. **C-rate** ‚Äî Normalised charge/discharge current.
> 6. **State of Charge (SOC, %)** ‚Äî Present charge level as a percentage of full charge.
>
> *(Other acceptable parameters: Internal Resistance, Cycle Life, SOH, DOD, Self-Discharge Rate, Power Density.)* *(3 marks: 0.5 mark per parameter.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Classify and explain the **different types of batteries** used in Electric and Hybrid Electric Vehicles, with a focus on **Lead-Acid, NiMH, and Li-ion** chemistries. Compare their **specific energy, cycle life, and cost**. `[7 Marks]`

**(b)** Define the following parameters in detail: **C-rate, State of Charge (SOC), Depth of Discharge (DOD), State of Health (SOH), and Internal Resistance**. Show how SOC and DOD are mathematically related. `[7 Marks]`

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION ‚Äî Question A

**(a) Battery Types for EV/HEV:**

> [!NOTE]
> **1. Lead-Acid Batteries (Pb-acid):** The oldest rechargeable battery technology. Two varieties ‚Äî **SLI (Starting, Lighting, Ignition)** and **VRLA (Valve-Regulated Lead-Acid, including AGM)**. Used in conventional 12 V auxiliary systems and in some mild-hybrid 48 V architectures. Specific energy ~**30‚Äì40 Wh/kg**, cycle life ~**300‚Äì500 cycles**, cost ~**$100‚Äì150/kWh**. Pros: cheap, recyclable, robust. Cons: low energy density, sulphation if left discharged, contains toxic lead. *(2 marks)*

> [!NOTE]
> **2. Nickel-Metal Hydride (NiMH):** Dominated first-generation HEVs, notably the **Toyota Prius (1997‚Äì2015)**. Specific energy ~**60‚Äì90 Wh/kg**, cycle life ~**1000+ cycles**, cost ~**$250‚Äì300/kWh**. Pros: safe, tolerant to overcharge, good power density. Cons: memory effect (mild), self-discharge higher than Li-ion, contains nickel (expensive). *(2 marks)*

> [!NOTE]
> **3. Lithium-Ion Family (Li-ion):** Current standard for all modern EVs. Sub-variants:
> - **LFP (LiFePO‚ÇÑ):** ~3.2 V nominal, 90‚Äì160 Wh/kg, safest, 2000‚Äì5000 cycles, used in BYD and entry-level EVs.
> - **NMC (Li-Ni-Mn-Co-O‚ÇÇ):** ~3.7 V nominal, 150‚Äì220 Wh/kg, 1000‚Äì2000 cycles, used in Hyundai, Kia, VW.
> - **NCA (Li-Ni-Co-Al-O‚ÇÇ):** ~3.6 V nominal, 200‚Äì260 Wh/kg, used in Tesla legacy packs.
> - **LTO (Li‚ÇÑTi‚ÇÖO‚ÇÅ‚ÇÇ):** ~2.4 V nominal, 60‚Äì80 Wh/kg, 10000+ cycles, ultra-fast charging.
>
> Cost: ~**$120‚Äì140/kWh** (2024 industry benchmark). Pros: highest energy density, low self-discharge, no memory effect. Cons: requires BMS, thermal runaway risk if abused. *(3 marks)*

**Comparison Table (within the 7-mark sub-part):**

| Chemistry | Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | Cycle Life | Relative Cost |
|-----------|--------------------------|------------|---------------|
| Pb-acid | 30‚Äì40 | 300‚Äì500 | Lowest |
| NiMH | 60‚Äì90 | 1000+ | Medium |
| Li-ion (LFP) | 90‚Äì160 | 2000‚Äì5000 | Low‚ÄìMedium |
| Li-ion (NMC) | 150‚Äì220 | 1000‚Äì2000 | Medium‚ÄìHigh |
| Li-ion (NCA) | 200‚Äì260 | 500‚Äì1000 | High |

---

**(b) Battery Parameter Definitions:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1. C-rate:** Defined as the discharge (or charge) current divided by the rated capacity: $\text{C-rate} = I / C_{\text{rated}}$. A 1C rate discharges the battery in 1 hour; a 2C rate in 30 minutes; a 0.5C rate in 2 hours. Dimensionless. *(1.5 marks)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **2. State of Charge (SOC):** The ratio of **remaining capacity** to **rated capacity**, expressed as a percentage:
> $$SOC = \dfrac{C_{\text{remaining}}}{C_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\%$$
> SOC = 100 % when fully charged; SOC = 0 % at cut-off voltage. *(1.5 marks)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **3. Depth of Discharge (DOD):** The percentage of rated capacity that has been **discharged**:
> $$DOD = \dfrac{C_{\text{discharged}}}{C_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\%$$
> *(1 mark)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **4. State of Health (SOH):** Ratio of **present maximum capacity** to **rated new capacity**:
> $$SOH = \dfrac{C_{\text{max,present}}}{C_{\text{rated,new}}} \times 100\%$$
> A battery is considered end-of-life when SOH ‚â§ 80 %. *(1.5 marks)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **5. Internal Resistance (R·µ¢):** Opposition to current flow inside the cell; composed of **ohmic (RŒ©)**, **charge-transfer (Rct)**, and **diffusion (Wd)** components. Rises with age, low SOC, and low temperature. Affects terminal voltage: $V_t = OCV - I \cdot R_i$. *(1.5 marks)*

**SOC‚ÄìDOD Relationship:**
$$SOC + DOD = 100\%$$
$$\boxed{\,SOC = 100\% - DOD\,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse SOH with SOC.** SOH measures ageing (capacity fade over years); SOC measures instantaneous charge level.
> 2. **Internal resistance is temperature-dependent** ‚Äî stating only the formula $V_t = OCV - IR_i$ without mentioning the temperature variation is considered incomplete and loses 1 mark.
> 3. **C-rate is dimensionless**, not in "per hour." Writing "C-rate = 1/h" is a common wrong answer that costs 0.5‚Äì1 mark.
> 4. In the comparison table, ensure you mention **cycle life** alongside specific energy and cost ‚Äî examiners specifically check for it.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) ‚Äî *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a)** A **Li-ion traction battery** is rated at **200 Ah** and **3.7 V nominal voltage**. Calculate:
- (i) The **total rated energy** in kWh and MJ. `[2 Marks]`
- (ii) The **discharge current and time** if discharged at a **C/5 rate**. `[2 Marks]`
- (iii) The **peak power** delivered if the battery is momentarily discharged at **5C rate**. `[3 Marks]`

**(b)** Explain the **units of battery energy storage** (Wh, kWh, Ah, MJ) and show how they interconvert. Discuss why **specific energy (Wh/kg)** and **energy density (Wh/L)** are the most important metrics for EV designers. `[7 Marks]`

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION ‚Äî Question B

**(a) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $C_{\text{rated}} = 200 \text{ Ah}$, $V_n = 3.7 \text{ V}$.

**(i) Rated Energy in kWh and MJ:**

$$E_{\text{Wh}} = V_n \times C_{\text{rated}} = 3.7 \times 200 = 740 \text{ Wh}$$

$$E_{\text{kWh}} = \dfrac{740}{1000} = 0.74 \text{ kWh}$$

$$E_{\text{MJ}} = 0.74 \times 3.6 = 2.664 \text{ MJ}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** $E = 0.74$ kWh = $2.664$ MJ. *(2 marks: 1 for kWh, 1 for MJ, with explicit conversion shown.)*

**(ii) Discharge Current and Time at C/5 rate:**

$$n = \dfrac{1}{5} = 0.2\text{C}$$

$$I = n \times C_{\text{rated}} = 0.2 \times 200 = 40 \text{ A}$$

$$t = \dfrac{1}{n} = \dfrac{1}{0.2} = 5 \text{ hours}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** $I = 40$ A, $t = 5$ h. *(2 marks: 1 for current, 1 for time, both with units.)*

**(iii) Peak Power at 5C rate:**

$$I_{\text{peak}} = 5 \times 200 = 1000 \text{ A}$$

$$P_{\text{peak}} = V_n \times I_{\text{peak}} = 3.7 \times 1000 = 3700 \text{ W} = 3.7 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** $P_{\text{peak}} = 3.7$ kW. *(3 marks: 1 for current calculation, 1 for power formula, 1 for final numerical answer with unit.)*

---

**(b) Units of Battery Energy Storage:**

> [!NOTE]
> **1. Coulomb (C):** SI unit of electric charge. $1 \text{ C} = 1 \text{ A} \times 1 \text{ s}$. Not commonly used in EV context because batteries store thousands of Coulombs. *(1 mark)*

> [!NOTE]
> **2. Ampere-hour (Ah):** $1 \text{ Ah} = 3600 \text{ C}$. Practical unit for battery **capacity**, because it directly tells us how long the battery can deliver a known current. A 200 Ah battery can supply 200 A for 1 hour, or 50 A for 4 hours, etc. *(1 mark)*

> [!NOTE]
> **3. Watt-hour (Wh):** $1 \text{ Wh} = 3600 \text{ J}$. The product of voltage and capacity: $E = V \times C$. This is the **true measure of energy** because it accounts for the voltage at which charge is delivered. Two batteries of the same Ah rating but different voltages store different energy. *(1.5 marks)*

> [!NOTE]
> **4. Kilowatt-hour (kWh):** $1 \text{ kWh} = 1000 \text{ Wh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$. Industry standard for EV pack sizing. Typical EV: 30 kWh (small) to 100+ kWh (long range). *(1 mark)*

> [!NOTE]
> **5. Megajoule (MJ):** $1 \text{ MJ} = 10^6 \text{ J} = 0.2778 \text{ kWh}$. Used in scientific literature and for comparing regenerative braking energy recovery. *(0.5 mark)*

**Conversion chain:**
$$1 \text{ Ah} \times V[\text{V}] = 1 \text{ Wh} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1000 \text{ Wh} = 1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **6. Why Specific Energy and Energy Density Matter for EVs:**
> - **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** governs the **driving range** of the EV. A higher specific energy means **lighter battery for the same range**, which further reduces the vehicle mass, creating a virtuous cycle.
> - **Energy Density (Wh/L)** governs the **pack packaging** ‚Äî how much physical space the battery occupies. Critical for vehicle design because a bulky pack reduces passenger/cargo space.
> - Together, they determine whether an EV can practically achieve 400+ km range without exceeding weight and volume budgets. *(2 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Part B Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors are the #1 cause of lost marks.** Always write $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$ explicitly before substituting. Do not assume the examiner will infer it.
> 2. In part (a)(iii), students often forget to **convert W to kW** at the end ‚Äî losing 0.5 mark.
> 3. In part (b), do not list units without **explanations**. Listing "Wh, kWh, Ah, MJ" without converting or justifying why they are used is a 0‚Äì1 mark answer.
> 4. The relationship $1 \text{ Ah} \times V = 1 \text{ Wh}$ is a **must-state**; examiners specifically check this.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 3 ‚Äî Types of Battery, Battery Parameters, Units, C-rate**

- ‚òê **Battery** = electrochemical device converting chemical energy to electrical energy; rated by **V √ó Ah = Wh**.
- ‚òê **Five key EV battery chemistries** to remember: **Pb-acid, NiMH, LFP, NMC, NCA**. Add LTO and Solid-State for advanced answers.
- ‚òê **Li-ion NMC** is the current industry standard for traction packs due to high specific energy (**150‚Äì220 Wh/kg**) and reasonable cycle life.
- ‚òê **Specific energy** of Pb-acid ‚âà **30‚Äì40 Wh/kg**; NiMH ‚âà **60‚Äì90 Wh/kg**; Li-ion NMC ‚âà **150‚Äì220 Wh/kg**.
- ‚òê **Battery parameters** (must-know list): **Nominal Voltage, OCV, Terminal Voltage, Capacity, Energy, C-rate, SOC, DOD, SOH, Internal Resistance, Cycle Life, Specific Energy, Energy Density, Specific Power, Self-Discharge, Efficiency**.
- ‚òê **C-rate formula:** $n = I / C_{\text{rated}}$ (dimensionless). Discharge time $t = 1/n$ hours.
- ‚òê **At 1C**, a fully charged battery is exhausted in **exactly 1 hour**.
- ‚òê **At 5C**, discharge time = **12 minutes** (high stress).
- ‚òê **At 0.2C (C/5)**, discharge time = **5 hours** (gentle, longer life).
- ‚òê **Energy** = $V_n \times C_{\text{rated}}$ in Wh; divide by 1000 for kWh; multiply by 3.6 for MJ.
- ‚òê **Power** at C-rate $n$ = $n \times E_{\text{rated,kWh}}$ kW.
- ‚òê **Peak regen C-rate** of typical Li-ion = **1C‚Äì2C continuous, 5C peak**; LTO can accept up to **10C**.
- ‚òê **SOC + DOD = 100 %**; **SOH ‚â§ 80 %** = end-of-life.
- ‚òê **Internal resistance** $R_i$ causes voltage drop: $V_t = OCV - I R_i$.
- ‚òê **Peukert's Law** (advanced): $t = H (C / I H)^n$; high C-rate reduces effective capacity.
- ‚òê **EV pack sizing** example: 400 V √ó 100 Ah = 40 kWh; 350 V √ó 214 Ah ‚âà 75 kWh (Tesla Model 3 LR).
- ‚òê **Rule of thumb:** 1 kWh ‚âà 5‚Äì7 km of EV range (varies with vehicle efficiency).
- ‚òê **Conversion constants:** $1 \text{ Ah} = 3600 \text{ C}$; $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$.
- ‚òê **Energy in regen braking:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ ‚Äî link this to C-rate to verify BMS acceptance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ‚Äî Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cell Voltage (V)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Cell Voltage is the electric potential difference developed between the positive and negative terminals of an electrochemical cell due to the difference in electrochemical potential of the two electrodes. It is expressed in **Volts (V)** and is a fundamental performance metric for any battery used in Electric Vehicles (EVs) and Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs).

Three distinct voltage levels are defined in KTU board examinations:

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|------|--------|------------|
| **Nominal Voltage** | $V_{nom}$ | The "rated" or reference voltage assigned by the manufacturer (e.g., 3.7 V for Li-ion NMC) |
| **Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV)** | $V_{oc}$ | The terminal voltage when no load is connected (steady-state) |
| **Terminal Voltage** | $V_t$ | The voltage actually delivered to the load under current draw |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** In a discharging cell under load, $V_t < V_{oc}$ because of internal voltage drops across internal resistance.

### Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Water Tap" Model
Imagine a water tank placed on a rooftop. The **height of the tank** represents the **cell voltage** (pressure pushing electrons out). A taller tank pushes water (current) out with greater force. But if the connecting pipe is narrow (high internal resistance), the actual water flow at the tap (terminal voltage) will be less than the tank's potential pressure (OCV). The tap water pressure is the **terminal voltage**, the tank pressure is the **OCV**, and the narrow pipe is the **internal resistance**.

---

## 1.2 Specific Energy (Wh/kg)

**Formal Definition:**
Specific Energy is the amount of electrical energy a battery can deliver per unit mass. It is measured in **Watt-hours per kilogram (Wh/kg)** and is the single most important metric that determines the **driving range** of an electric vehicle.

$$E_{sp} = \frac{E_{stored}}{m_{cell}} = \frac{V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}}{m_{cell}} \quad \text{[Wh/kg]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Specific Energy matters in EVs:** A higher specific energy means a lighter battery pack for the same range. Tesla's 4680 cells (~300 Wh/kg) give far more range than older LFP packs (~160 Wh/kg) of the same mass.

### Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Fuel Tank" Analogy
Think of specific energy as the **energy content of fuel per liter**. Diesel has more energy per liter than petrol (‚âà 35.8 MJ/L vs 32 MJ/L) ‚Äî similarly, Li-S batteries (~500 Wh/kg theoretical) have more "fuel" per kilogram than lead-acid (~40 Wh/kg). A Tesla carrying Li-ion cells carries less "dead weight" than an old e-rickshaw carrying lead-acid.

---

## 1.3 Cycle Life

**Formal Definition:**
Cycle Life is the **number of complete charge-discharge cycles** a battery can deliver before its usable capacity drops to a defined end-of-life threshold, conventionally **80% of its initial rated capacity** (KTU 2024 reference standard).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Definition:** A "cycle" = one full charge + one full discharge = one Depth-of-Discharge (DoD) of 100%.

### Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Shoe Sole" Analogy
A shoe's sole wears out with every step (cycle). Light usage (shallow DoD, low current) gives many steps before the sole tears. Heavy running (deep DoD, high C-rate) wears it out fast. Different chemistries have different "sole thicknesses" ‚Äî LiFePO‚ÇÑ is like a thick rubber hiking boot (3000+ cycles), while NMC is like a thin racing shoe (~1000 cycles).

---

## 1.4 Self-Discharge

**Formal Definition:**
Self-Discharge is the **irreversible loss of stored charge** in a battery when it is left idle (no external load connected), caused by internal parasitic chemical reactions and internal leakage currents. It is typically expressed as a **percentage of capacity lost per month (%/month)** at a reference temperature of **25 ¬∞C**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Self-discharge is NOT the same as coulombic inefficiency. Self-discharge occurs even at rest, while coulombic inefficiency occurs only during active cycling.

### Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Leaking Water Bottle"
Imagine a sealed water bottle left in a cupboard. Even closed, tiny amounts evaporate through microscopic pores. A NiMH cell "leaks" ~30% of its charge per month (high self-discharge), while a Li-ion cell loses only ~2‚Äì3% per month. Storing at cool temperatures (refrigerator) slows the "evaporation."

---

## 1.5 Static Battery Equivalent Circuit Model

**Formal Definition:**
A Static Battery Equivalent Circuit Model is a **lumped-parameter electrical network** (composed of voltage sources, resistors, and capacitors) that mathematically reproduces the **steady-state I‚ÄìV behavior** of a battery. The most widely used static models in KTU 2024 syllabus are:

1. **Ideal Voltage Source Model** ‚Äî $V_t = V_{oc}$
2. **R-Internal (R-int) Model** ‚Äî $V_t = V_{oc} - I \cdot R_{int}$
3. **Thevenin Model** ‚Äî $V_{oc}$ in series with $R_{int}$ and one parallel RC network ($R_{ct} \parallel C_{dl}$) representing transient diffusion and charge-transfer effects.
4. **N-th Order RC Model** ‚Äî multiple RC branches for higher accuracy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Static" means the model parameters are assumed constant** for a given State-of-Charge (SoC) and temperature ‚Äî no time-varying ODEs are solved in the static case. (Dynamic models add Warburg diffusion elements.)

### Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Battery as a Plumbing System"
The OCV is the water tank pressure. $R_{int}$ is the narrow pipe (ohmic drop). The RC network (parallel capacitor + resistor) represents the **elastic bladder inside the tank** ‚Äî when current suddenly flows, the bladder flexes (capacitor charges), causing a transient voltage dip that recovers slowly (resistor bleeding off the charge).

---

## 1.6 Series-Parallel Battery Pack Equivalent Circuits

**Formal Definition:**
An EV battery pack is constructed by connecting many individual cells in **series** (to raise voltage) and **parallel** (to raise capacity). The series-parallel equivalent circuit is the electrical network that represents this combination using an aggregated OCV, equivalent resistance, and equivalent capacitance.

- **S cells in series** ‚Üí pack voltage = $S \times V_{cell}$, capacity unchanged.
- **P cells in parallel** ‚Üí capacity = $P \times C_{cell}$, voltage unchanged.
- **S-P hybrid** ‚Üí pack rated as **S-series √ó P-parallel (SxP)** configuration.

### Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Battery as a Building"
Imagine stacking batteries like floors of a building. Floors in **series** raise the total "height" (voltage). Within each floor, multiple batteries placed **side-by-side in parallel** widen the floor (capacity). A typical Tesla Model 3 pack is **96s46p** (96 series, 46 parallel) ‚Äî 96 floors tall, with 46 batteries per floor.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ‚Äî Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cell Voltage ‚Äî Deep Analysis

The thermodynamic equilibrium voltage of a cell is governed by the **Nernst Equation**:

$$V_{oc} = V^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(\frac{a_{\text{anode}}}{a_{\text{cathode}}}\right)$$

where:
- $V^{\circ}$ = standard electrode potential
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol¬∑K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred per reaction
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- $a$ = activity of the chemical species

**Under load (terminal voltage):**

$$V_t = V_{oc} - I \cdot R_{int}$$

**Why is the voltage not constant during discharge?**
- **Activation overvoltage** ($V_{act}$) ‚Äî needed to drive charge-transfer kinetics at electrodes.
- **Ohmic overvoltage** ($V_{ohm} = IR_{int}$) ‚Äî drop across electrolyte, current collectors, electrodes.
- **Concentration overvoltage** ($V_{conc}$) ‚Äî due to mass-transport limitations of ions.

$$V_t = V_{oc} - V_{act} - V_{ohm} - V_{conc}$$

---

## 2.2 Specific Energy ‚Äî Deep Analysis

Specific energy can be expressed volumetrically or gravimetrically:

| Metric | Formula | Unit | Used For |
|--------|---------|------|----------|
| **Gravimetric Specific Energy** | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{V_{nom} \cdot C_{Ah}}{m}$ | Wh/kg | Vehicle range per mass |
| **Volumetric Energy Density** | $E_{vol} = \dfrac{V_{nom} \cdot C_{Ah}}{V_{cell}}$ | Wh/L | Pack packaging |

**Relationship to Specific Power:**
- **Specific Power** $P_{sp} = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{m}$ in W/kg
- **Energy-to-Power Ratio** (Ragone plot) determines application:
  - **High $E_{sp}$, low $P_{sp}$** ‚Üí long-range EVs (e.g., NMC, LFP)
  - **Low $E_{sp}$, high $P_{sp}$** ‚Üí acceleration, hybrid mode (e.g., NMC + supercapacitor hybrids)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common question: "Why can't we use lead-acid batteries in modern EVs?" Answer: low specific energy (~40 Wh/kg) ‚Äî too heavy for range.

---

## 2.3 Cycle Life ‚Äî Deep Analysis

**Mathematical definition:**
$$N_{cycles} = \text{Number of cycles until } C_{actual}(N) = 0.8 \times C_{rated}$$

**Empirical degradation model (KTU 2024):**
$$Q(N) = Q_0 \cdot \left(1 - k \cdot N^{0.5}\right)$$

where $k$ is a degradation constant dependent on chemistry, DoD, temperature, and C-rate.

**Factors affecting cycle life:**
1. **Depth of Discharge (DoD)** ‚Äî Shallower cycles ‚áí exponentially more cycles (Peukert effect)
2. **Temperature** ‚Äî High T accelerates SEI growth; Low T causes Li-plating
3. **C-rate** ‚Äî Higher discharge/charge currents ‚Üí faster degradation
4. **State-of-Charge (SoC) window** ‚Äî Cycling between 20‚Äì80% SoC doubles cycle life
5. **Calendar aging** ‚Äî Even unused, batteries age due to SEI layer growth

**Typical Cycle Life (KTU Reference Table):**

| Chemistry | Cycle Life (to 80% cap) | Specific Energy |
|-----------|--------------------------|------------------|
| Lead-Acid | 300‚Äì500 | 30‚Äì50 Wh/kg |
| NiMH | 500‚Äì1000 | 60‚Äì120 Wh/kg |
| LFP (LiFePO‚ÇÑ) | 2000‚Äì5000 | 90‚Äì160 Wh/kg |
| NMC | 1000‚Äì2000 | 150‚Äì220 Wh/kg |
| NCA | 500‚Äì1000 | 200‚Äì260 Wh/kg |
| Li-S (theoretical) | 200‚Äì500 | 400‚Äì500 Wh/kg |

---

## 2.4 Self-Discharge ‚Äî Deep Analysis

**Self-Discharge Rate (% per month):**
$$R_{sd} = \frac{C_{initial} - C_{after\,1\,month}}{C_{initial}} \times 100\%$$

**Arrhenius temperature dependence:**
$$R_{sd}(T) = R_{sd,ref} \cdot e^{\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_{ref}}\right)}$$

where $E_a$ is the activation energy of the parasitic reaction (typically 60‚Äì100 kJ/mol for Li-ion).

**Mechanisms of self-discharge:**
1. **Electrochemical side-reactions** ‚Äî e.g., electrolyte decomposition at electrodes
2. **Internal micro-short circuits** ‚Äî dendrite formation causing current leakage
3. **Shuttle reactions** ‚Äî polysulfide migration in Li-S cells (major issue)
4. **Thermal decomposition** ‚Äî accelerated at high temperature

**Self-discharge comparison:**
- Li-ion: 2‚Äì3% / month
- NiMH: 20‚Äì30% / month
- NiCd: 10‚Äì15% / month
- Lead-acid: 4‚Äì20% / month
- LFP: 1‚Äì3% / month (best)

---

## 2.5 Static Battery Equivalent Circuit Models ‚Äî Deep Analysis

### 2.5.1 Ideal Source Model
$$V_t = V_{oc}$$
*No internal resistance, no transients.* Useful only for textbook baseline.

### 2.5.2 R-Internal (R-int) Model
$$V_t = V_{oc} - I \cdot R_{int}$$
*Most widely used in KTU board questions.* Includes steady-state ohmic drop.

### 2.5.3 Thevenin (1st-Order RC) Model
The model consists of:
- $V_{oc}$ ‚Äî ideal voltage source
- $R_0$ ‚Äî ohmic resistance (electrolyte, contact)
- $R_{ct}$ ‚Äî charge-transfer resistance
- $C_{dl}$ ‚Äî double-layer capacitance

**Governing equations:**
$$V_t = V_{oc} - I R_0 - V_{RC}$$
$$I = \frac{V_{RC}}{R_{ct}} + C_{dl} \frac{dV_{RC}}{dt}$$

Solving the ODE for a step-current $I$ input:
$$V_{RC}(t) = I \cdot R_{ct} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) \quad \text{where } \tau = R_{ct} C_{dl}$$

### 2.5.4 PNGV / Multi-RC Model
For higher accuracy, multiple RC branches are added in series, each representing different time-constants of diffusion processes.

---

## 2.6 Series-Parallel Pack Equivalents ‚Äî Deep Analysis

For a pack with **S cells in series, P in parallel per branch (S√óP configuration)**:

| Parameter | Series (S) | Parallel (P) | S√óP Pack |
|-----------|------------|--------------|----------|
| Voltage | $S \cdot V_{cell}$ | $V_{cell}$ | $S \cdot V_{cell}$ |
| Capacity (Ah) | $C_{cell}$ | $P \cdot C_{cell}$ | $P \cdot C_{cell}$ |
| Energy (Wh) | $S \cdot V_{cell} \cdot C_{cell}$ | $V_{cell} \cdot P \cdot C_{cell}$ | $S \cdot P \cdot V_{cell} \cdot C_{cell}$ |
| Internal Resistance | $S \cdot R_{cell}$ | $R_{cell}/P$ | $S \cdot R_{cell} / P$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** When calculating pack resistance, the series factor **S** multiplies and the parallel factor **P divides**. Don't forget both effects simultaneously.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---------|---------|------|
| 1 | $V_t = V_{oc} - I R_{int}$ | Terminal voltage under load | V |
| 2 | $V_{oc} = V^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(\frac{a_{an}}{ca}\right)$ | Nernst equation | V |
| 3 | $E_{sp} = V_{nom} C_{Ah} / m$ | Specific energy | Wh/kg |

| 6 | $Q(N) = Q_0 (1 - k N^{0.5})$ | Empirical degradation | Ah |
| 7 | $R_{sd}(T) = R_{ref} \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{R}(\tfrac{1}{T} - \tfrac{1}{T_{ref}})\right]$ | Arrhenius self-discharge | %/month |
| 8 | $V_{RC}(t) = I R_{ct}(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | Thevenin transient | V |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** These equations are used daily in **Battery Management System (BMS) firmware** in every EV (Tata Nexon EV, MG ZS EV, Tesla Model 3) to estimate SoC, predict range, and protect the pack.
