# SECTION 3 ‚Äî Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation ‚Äî Pack Equivalent Resistance for S√óP Configuration

**Problem Statement:** Derive the Thevenin-equivalent internal resistance of a battery pack with $S$ cells in series and $P$ cells in parallel per series string.

### Step 1 ‚Äî Single Cell Resistance Baseline
Each cell has an internal resistance $R_{cell}$. By definition, the internal resistance is the lumped sum of:
- electrolyte ionic resistance
- electrode/electrolyte interfacial resistance
- tab and current-collector resistance

$$R_{cell} = R_{electrolyte} + R_{contact} + R_{tabs}$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Resistance of a Series String of S Cells
When $S$ cells are connected in series, the same current $I$ flows through every cell. By Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL), the total ohmic drop across the string is the sum of drops across each cell:

$$V_{drop,series} = \sum_{i=1}^{S} I \cdot R_{cell,i} = S \cdot I \cdot R_{cell}$$

(assuming all $R_{cell,i}$ are equal ‚Äî a standard KTU assumption)

Therefore, the equivalent resistance of a series string is:

$$\boxed{R_{series} = S \cdot R_{cell}}$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Resistance of P Parallel Strings
When $P$ cells are placed in parallel, they share the same voltage $V$ but split the current. The total current is the sum of individual branch currents:

$$I_{total} = \sum_{j=1}^{P} \frac{V}{R_{cell}} = P \cdot \frac{V}{R_{cell}}$$

The equivalent resistance seen at the terminals is:

$$R_{parallel} = \frac{V}{I_{total}} = \frac{V}{P \cdot V / R_{cell}} = \frac{R_{cell}}{P}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{R_{parallel} = \frac{R_{cell}}{P}}$$

### Step 4 ‚Äî Combining Both: S-series √ó P-parallel
The pack is built by placing $P$ parallel strings, each containing $S$ cells in series. We can model it as **one big cell of resistance $R_{series}$ repeated P times in parallel**:

$$R_{pack} = \frac{R_{series}}{P} = \frac{S \cdot R_{cell}}{P}$$

**Final result:**

$$\boxed{R_{pack} = \frac{S \cdot R_{cell}}{P}}$$

### Step 5 ‚Äî Numerical Example (KTU-style)
A 96s46p Tesla pack with each 21700 cell having $R_{cell} = 25 \text{ m}\Omega$:

$$R_{pack} = \frac{96 \times 0.025}{46} = \frac{2.4}{46} = 0.05217 \, \Omega \approx 52 \text{ m}\Omega$$

This matches published Tesla Model 3 pack impedance data (~50 mŒ©).

---

## 3.2 Derivation ‚Äî Specific Energy vs Energy Density Conversion

**Given:** A battery cell of mass $m$, volume $\mathcal{V}$, nominal voltage $V_{nom}$, and capacity $C_{Ah}$.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Stored energy (Wh):**
$$E = V_{nom} \cdot C_{Ah} \quad [\text{Wh}]$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Specific energy (gravimetric):**
$$E_{sp} = \frac{E}{m} = \frac{V_{nom} \cdot C_{Ah}}{m} \quad [\text{Wh/kg}]$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Energy density (volumetric):**
$$E_{vol} = \frac{E}{\mathcal{V}} = \frac{V_{nom} \cdot C_{Ah}}{\mathcal{V}} \quad [\text{Wh/L}]$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Cross-relation using density $\rho = m/\mathcal{V}$:**
$$E_{sp} = E_{vol} \cdot \rho$$

**Numerical Example:** An LFP cell with $E_{vol} = 350$ Wh/L and $\rho = 2100$ kg/m¬≥:
$$E_{sp} = 350 \times 2.1 = 735 \text{ Wh/kg} \quad (\text{ideal, real} \approx 160 \text{ Wh/kg})$$

The huge gap between ideal and real arises because the calculation above ignores packaging, current collectors, and electrolyte mass.

---

## 3.3 Derivation ‚Äî Thevenin Model Step Response

**Setup:** Battery at rest, OCV = $V_{oc}$, no current flows. At $t = 0$, a constant discharge current $I$ is applied. Determine $V_t(t)$.

**Step 1 ‚Äî State equation:** The RC branch voltage $V_{RC}$ follows:
$$C_{dl} \frac{dV_{RC}}{dt} + \frac{V_{RC}}{R_{ct}} = I$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Standard first-order linear ODE.** The homogeneous solution is:
$$V_{RC,h}(t) = A \cdot e^{-t/\tau}, \quad \tau = R_{ct} C_{dl}$$

The particular solution (constant current) is:
$$V_{RC,p} = I \cdot R_{ct}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Apply initial condition** $V_{RC}(0) = 0$ (capacitor initially uncharged):
$$0 = A + I \cdot R_{ct} \implies A = -I \cdot R_{ct}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Final expression:**
$$V_{RC}(t) = I \cdot R_{ct} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Terminal voltage:**
$$V_t(t) = V_{oc} - I \cdot R_0 - I \cdot R_{ct} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Steady-state value** ($t \to \infty$):
$$V_{t,ss} = V_{oc} - I(R_0 + R_{ct}) = V_{oc} - I \cdot R_{int}$$

which is consistent with the R-int model. **This confirms the Thevenin model reduces to the R-int model at steady state** ‚Äî a key KTU board concept.

---

## 3.4 Derivation ‚Äî Maximum Power Transfer (Battery Internal Limit)

**Theorem:** A real battery with EMF $V_{oc}$ and internal resistance $R_{int}$ can deliver **maximum power to a load** $R_L$ when $R_L = R_{int}$ (impedance matching).

**Step 1 ‚Äî Current through load:**
$$I = \frac{V_{oc}}{R_{int} + R_L}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Power delivered to load:**
$$P_L = I^2 R_L = \frac{V_{oc}^2 R_L}{(R_{int} + R_L)^2}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Differentiate w.r.t. $R_L$ and set to zero:**
$$\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = \frac{V_{oc}^2(R_{int} + R_L)^2 - V_{oc}^2 R_L \cdot 2(R_{int} + R_L)}{(R_{int} + R_L)^4} = 0$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Numerator simplification:**
$$(R_{int} + R_L) - 2 R_L = 0 \implies R_L = R_{int}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Maximum power:**
$$\boxed{P_{max} = \frac{V_{oc}^2}{4 R_{int}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** EV drivetrains never operate at $R_L = R_{int}$ because the efficiency at this point is only 50% (half the energy is wasted in $R_{int}$). Real EVs operate at much lower $R_L$ for high efficiency (typically 80‚Äì95%).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Comparison of Static Battery Models

| Feature | Ideal Source | R-int | Thevenin (1st order) | Multi-RC (2nd order) |
|---------|--------------|-------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Components** | $V_{oc}$ only | $V_{oc}, R_{int}$ | $V_{oc}, R_0, R_{ct}, C_{dl}$ | $V_{oc}, R_0, 2 \times (R_i, C_i)$ |
| **Captures ohmic drop** | ‚ùå | ‚úÖ | ‚úÖ | ‚úÖ |
| **Captures transient** | ‚ùå | ‚ùå | ‚úÖ | ‚úÖ‚úÖ |
| **Captures diffusion** | ‚ùå | ‚ùå | ‚ùå | ‚úÖ |
| **Accuracy** | Lowest | Medium | High | Very High |
| **Parameters to identify** | 1 | 2 | 4 | 6 |
| **KTU use-case** | Theoretical baseline | KTU Part A answers | KTU Part B (full) | Research / advanced BMS |

# SECTION 4 ‚Äî Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Thevenin (1st-Order RC) Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1["+ Terminal"] --> R0["R0 ohmic"]
    R0 --> node2["Node A"]
    node2 --> Rct["Rct charge transfer"]
    node2 --> Cdl["Cdl double layer"]

    Voc -.parallel to Rct+Cdl.-> node3

```

**Component roles in the diagram:**
- `R0` ‚Äî Ohmic resistance (instantaneous voltage drop)
- `Rct` ‚Äî Charge-transfer resistance (activation loss)
- `Cdl` ‚Äî Double-layer capacitance (transient charging)

## 4.2 Series Battery Pack (SxP with P=1)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1["Cell 1: V1, R1"] --> A2["Cell 2: V2, R2"]
    A2 --> A3["Cell 3: V3, R3"]
    A3 --> A4["Cell S: VS, RS"]
    A4 --> Load["Pack +"]
    Load --- B["Pack -"]
    B --> A1
```

**Aggregated result:** $V_{pack} = \sum V_i$, $R_{pack} = \sum R_i$, $C_{pack} = C_{cell}$ (capacity unchanged).

---

## 4.3 Parallel Battery Pack (SxP with S=1)

```mermaid
graph TB
    P1["Cell 1: V, R1"] --- N["Common -"]
    P2["Cell 2: V, R2"] --- N
    P3["Cell P: V, RP"] --- N
    P1 --- Bus["Common +"]
    P2 --- Bus
    P3 --- Bus
    Bus --> Load["External Load"]
    Load --> N
```

**Aggregated result:** $V_{pack} = V_{cell}$, $R_{pack} = R_{cell}/P$, $C_{pack} = P \cdot C_{cell}$.

## 4.4 S√óP Hybrid Pack (e.g., 4s2p) ‚Äî Full Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB

```

**Block-level functional interpretation:**
- **Each "String"** = one series chain raising voltage.
- **Multiple strings in parallel** = raising capacity and reducing equivalent impedance.
- **Bus bars** (Pack + and Pack ‚àí) carry the high current to the **EV inverter** which drives the motor.

## 4.5 Battery Degradation vs Cycle Number (Ragone-style curve concept)

```mermaid
graph LR

```

**Interpretation:** The "End of Life" (EoL) for KTU automotive applications is reached when capacity drops to 80% of initial. After this point, the cell is unsuitable for EV traction but may be re-purposed for stationary energy storage (second-life batteries).

## 4.6 Self-Discharge vs Storage Time

```mermaid
graph LR

```

**Practical implication:** NiMH cells in older Toyota Prius hybrids lose about 1/3 charge per month when parked. Li-ion packs lose only 2‚Äì3%, making them far better for infrequently-driven EVs.

# SECTION 5 ‚Äî KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A ‚Äî 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ‚Äî June 2024]
**Define the term "specific energy" of a battery. Why is it a critical parameter for EV battery selection? Mention typical specific energy values for Li-ion and Lead-Acid chemistries.**

**Model Answer:**
Specific energy is the energy stored per unit mass of a battery, expressed in **Wh/kg**. It is calculated as:

$$E_{sp} = \frac{V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}}{m_{cell}}$$

It is critical for EVs because it directly determines the **driving range per kilogram** of battery mass ‚Äî higher specific energy means more range for less weight. Typical values: **Li-ion (NMC) ‚âà 150‚Äì220 Wh/kg**, **Lead-Acid ‚âà 30‚Äì50 Wh/kg**. Li-ion offers ~4‚Äì5√ó the range for the same battery mass, which is why lead-acid is now obsolete in modern EVs.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023]
**What is self-discharge in a battery? List two mechanisms that cause self-discharge and state the typical self-discharge rate of Li-ion cells at 25 ¬∞C.**

**Model Answer:**
Self-discharge is the **irreversible loss of stored charge** in a battery during idle (open-circuit) conditions, expressed in **%/month**. It occurs without any external load.

**Two mechanisms:**
1. **Electrochemical side-reactions** at the electrode/electrolyte interface (e.g., SEI growth in Li-ion).
2. **Internal micro-shorts** caused by dendrite growth or separator defects.

**Typical rate:** Li-ion cells lose only **2‚Äì3% of capacity per month** at 25 ¬∞C, which is among the lowest of all chemistries. NiMH loses 20‚Äì30%/month, while LFP loses only 1‚Äì3%/month.

---

## PART B ‚Äî 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the **Thevenin equivalent circuit model** of a battery. Derive the expression for the terminal voltage during a step-discharge current pulse. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A battery pack has configuration **14s10p**, with each cell having $V_{oc} = 3.6$ V, $C_{cell} = 3.0$ Ah, and $R_{cell} = 30$ mŒ©. Calculate:
1. Pack nominal voltage
2. Pack capacity
3. Pack stored energy in Wh
4. Pack internal resistance **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a):

**Thevenin Equivalent Circuit:**

The Thevenin model consists of:
- $V_{oc}$ ‚Äî open-circuit voltage source
- $R_0$ ‚Äî ohmic series resistance
- $R_{ct} \parallel C_{dl}$ ‚Äî parallel RC branch representing the charge-transfer/double-layer effect

(See Section 4.1 for the circuit diagram.)

**Derivation of step response:**

When a step current $I$ is applied at $t=0$, the parallel RC branch voltage follows the ODE:

$$C_{dl} \frac{dV_{RC}}{dt} + \frac{V_{RC}}{R_{ct}} = I$$

Solving with $V_{RC}(0)=0$ and $\tau = R_{ct} C_{dl}$:

$$V_{RC}(t) = I R_{ct}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

**Terminal voltage:**

$$V_t(t) = V_{oc} - I R_0 - I R_{ct}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

**Physical interpretation:**
- At $t=0$ (instantaneous): $V_t(0^+) = V_{oc} - I R_0$ (purely ohmic drop, capacitor still uncharged).
- At $t \to \infty$ (steady state): $V_t(\infty) = V_{oc} - I(R_0 + R_{ct}) = V_{oc} - I R_{int}$.

This proves that the **R-int model is the steady-state limit** of the Thevenin model.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b):

**Given:** $S = 14$ series, $P = 10$ parallel, $V_{oc,cell} = 3.6$ V, $C_{cell} = 3.0$ Ah, $R_{cell} = 30$ mŒ© = 0.030 Œ©

**Step 1 ‚Äî Pack nominal voltage:**
$$V_{pack} = S \cdot V_{cell} = 14 \times 3.6 = \mathbf{50.4 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Pack capacity (parallel adds capacity):**
$$C_{pack} = P \cdot C_{cell} = 10 \times 3.0 = \mathbf{30 \text{ Ah}}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Pack energy:**
$$E_{pack} = V_{pack} \times C_{pack} = 50.4 \times 30 = \mathbf{1512 \text{ Wh} \approx 1.51 \text{ kWh}}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Pack internal resistance:**
$$R_{pack} = \frac{S \cdot R_{cell}}{P} = \frac{14 \times 0.030}{10} = \frac{0.420}{10} = \mathbf{0.042 \text{ Œ©} = 42 \text{ mŒ©}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ‚Äî Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Mixing up S and P roles** ‚Äî many students calculate $R = S/P$ instead of $S \times R / P$. Remember: series multiplies R; parallel divides it.
> 2. **Forgetting units** ‚Äî Write "42 mŒ©" or "0.042 Œ©", not just "42".
> 3. **Energy unit confusion** ‚Äî Convert Wh to kWh correctly: 1 kWh = 1000 Wh.
> 4. **Initial condition** ‚Äî For Thevenin transient, students often forget $V_{RC}(0)=0$; this gives wrong sign in exponential.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the terms **cycle life** and **self-discharge** of a battery. Discuss the factors affecting cycle life in Li-ion batteries. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Li-ion cell has the following Thevenin parameters at SoC = 50%: $V_{oc} = 3.7$ V, $R_0 = 25$ mŒ©, $R_{ct} = 40$ mŒ©, $C_{dl} = 1500$ F. A constant discharge current of 20 A is applied. Calculate:
1. Initial instantaneous terminal voltage $V_t(0^+)$
2. The RC time-constant $\tau$
3. Steady-state terminal voltage $V_t(\infty)$
4. The terminal voltage at $t = \tau$ **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a):

**Cycle Life:**
Cycle life is the **number of complete charge-discharge cycles** a battery undergoes before its capacity drops to **80% of the initial rated capacity**. For Li-ion NMC, this is typically 1000‚Äì2000 cycles. For LFP, it can exceed 3000‚Äì5000 cycles.

**Self-Discharge:**
Self-discharge is the **loss of stored charge** over time when the battery sits idle at open circuit, expressed as **%/month**.

**Factors affecting Li-ion cycle life:**
1. **Depth of Discharge (DoD)** ‚Äî Cycling between 20‚Äì80% SoC doubles cycle life vs. 0‚Äì100% DoD.
2. **Operating temperature** ‚Äî Above 35 ¬∞C accelerates SEI growth; below 0 ¬∞C causes Li-plating.
3. **C-rate** ‚Äî Higher charge/discharge currents (e.g., 2C vs 0.5C) accelerate degradation.
4. **State-of-Charge window** ‚Äî Storing at 100% SoC stresses the cathode; 50% SoC storage is optimal.
5. **Calendar aging** ‚Äî Even without cycling, SEI growth consumes cyclable Li, reducing capacity over time.

**Mathematical degradation model:**
$$Q(N) = Q_0 (1 - k \sqrt{N})$$

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**Given:** $V_{oc} = 3.7$ V, $R_0 = 0.025$ Œ©, $R_{ct} = 0.040$ Œ©, $C_{dl} = 1500$ F, $I = 20$ A.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Initial instantaneous voltage** (capacitor acts as short-circuit, $V_{RC}(0^+) = 0$):
$$V_t(0^+) = V_{oc} - I R_0 - 0 = 3.7 - 20 \times 0.025 = 3.7 - 0.5 = \mathbf{3.2 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Time constant:**
$$\tau = R_{ct} \cdot C_{dl} = 0.040 \times 1500 = \mathbf{60 \text{ s}}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Steady-state voltage** ($t \to \infty$, capacitor fully charged, no current in $C_{dl}$):
$$V_t(\infty) = V_{oc} - I(R_0 + R_{ct}) = 3.7 - 20 \times (0.025 + 0.040) = 3.7 - 20 \times 0.065 = 3.7 - 1.3 = \mathbf{2.4 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Voltage at $t = \tau$:**
$$V_{RC}(\tau) = I R_{ct}(1 - e^{-1}) = 20 \times 0.040 \times (1 - 0.3679) = 0.8 \times 0.6321 = 0.5057 \text{ V}$$

$$V_t(\tau) = V_{oc} - I R_0 - V_{RC}(\tau) = 3.7 - 0.5 - 0.5057 = \mathbf{2.694 \text{ V}}$$

**Verification:** $V_t(\tau) = 2.694$ V lies between $V_t(0^+) = 3.2$ V and $V_t(\infty) = 2.4$ V ‚úì (correct since 63.2% of the way to steady state at $t=\tau$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ‚Äî Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Confusing $R_0$ with $R_{ct}$** ‚Äî The ohmic drop at $t=0^+$ uses $R_0$ only; the long-term drop uses $(R_0 + R_{ct})$.
> 2. **Wrong sign in exponent** ‚Äî Use $e^{-t/\tau}$, NOT $e^{t/\tau}$ (capacitor charges, not discharges).
> 3. **Unit mismatch** ‚Äî Capacitance in Farads, resistance in Ohms ‚Üí time-constant in seconds. Don't mix mF and mŒ© without conversion.
> 4. **Numerical rounding** ‚Äî Use at least 4 decimal places for $e^{-1} = 0.3679$ to avoid cumulative errors.

---

## üìã Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ‚úÖ **Cell Voltage** = $V_t = V_{oc} - I R_{int}$ under load. Three flavors: $V_{nom}$, $V_{oc}$, $V_t$ ‚Äî never confuse them.
- ‚úÖ **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** = $V_{nom} C_{Ah} / m$. Determines EV range. Li-ion ~150‚Äì220; Lead-acid ~30‚Äì50.
- ‚úÖ **Energy Density (Wh/L)** = $V_{nom} C_{Ah} / \mathcal{V}$. Determines pack packaging.
- ‚úÖ **Cycle Life** = number of cycles to reach 80% capacity. Affected by DoD, T, C-rate, SoC window.
- ‚úÖ **Self-Discharge** = % charge lost per month at rest. Li-ion 2‚Äì3%/mo; NiMH 20‚Äì30%/mo. Arrhenius temperature dependence.
- ‚úÖ **R-int model** = static, 2 parameters ($V_{oc}, R_{int}$). Most common in KTU Part A.
- ‚úÖ **Thevenin model** = static + transient. Adds $R_{ct} \parallel C_{dl}$. Step response: $V_{RC}(t) = I R_{ct}(1-e^{-t/\tau})$.
- ‚úÖ **Time constant** $\tau = R_{ct} C_{dl}$. At $t = \tau$, response is 63.2% complete.
- ‚úÖ **Series (S)** raises voltage: $V_{pack} = S V_{cell}$, $R_{pack} = S R_{cell}$.
- ‚úÖ **Parallel (P)** raises capacity: $C_{pack} = P C_{cell}$, $R_{pack} = R_{cell}/P$.
- ‚úÖ **S√óP Pack** resistance: $R_{pack} = S R_{cell} / P$. Energy: $E_{pack} = S \cdot P \cdot V_{cell} C_{cell}$.
- ‚úÖ **Max power transfer** at $R_L = R_{int}$ gives $P_{max} = V_{oc}^2 / (4R_{int})$ ‚Äî but only 50% efficient.
- ‚úÖ **Static** model parameters assumed constant w.r.t. SoC and time ‚Äî distinguish from **dynamic** (Warburg, hysteresis) models.
- ‚úÖ **KTU Standard EoL** = 80% of initial capacity for automotive Li-ion cells.
- ‚úÖ **Faraday's constant** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol and **gas constant** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol¬∑K) are exam constants.
- ‚úÖ Always convert **mŒ© to Œ©** or **mF to F** before substituting into equations to avoid unit mismatches.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Other Storage Topologies: Fuel Cell Based Energy Storage Systems, Supercapacitors, Flywheel, Hybridization of Different Energy Storage Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Other Storage Topologies: Fuel Cell Based Energy Storage Systems, Supercapacitors, Flywheel, Hybridization of Different Energy Storage Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Alternative Energy Storage Topologies

In the context of Hybrid and Electric Vehicles (HEVs/EVs), the energy storage system (ESS) is the "heart" of the drivetrain. While the lithium-ion battery is the dominant workhorse, KTU Module 3 explores three other high-impact storage topologies ‚Äî **Fuel Cell Systems (FCS)**, **Supercapacitors (Ultracapacitors)**, and **Flywheel Energy Storage Systems (FESS)** ‚Äî and then explains how **hybridization** of these devices is essential to overcome the inherent limitations of any single technology.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> "Other Storage Topologies include Fuel Cell based energy storage, supercapacitors, flywheels and the hybridization of these energy storage devices to meet the combined demands of high energy density (for range) and high power density (for acceleration/regeneration) in modern electric and hybrid vehicles."

---

### 1.2 Fuel Cell Based Energy Storage System (FCESS)

#### Formal Definition
A **Fuel Cell** is an **electrochemical energy conversion device** that continuously converts the chemical energy of a fuel (typically **Hydrogen, H‚ÇÇ**) and an oxidant (typically **Oxygen, O‚ÇÇ** from air) directly into electrical energy, with **water (H‚ÇÇO)** and **heat** as the only by-products. Unlike a battery, it does not store energy internally; instead, it is an *open system* that generates electricity as long as fuel is supplied.

#### Intuitive Analogy ‚Äî "The Slow-Burning Generator That Drinks Hydrogen"
Imagine a tiny power plant sitting under the hood of a car. Instead of burning petrol, it gently sips hydrogen gas and exhales water vapor. There is no flame, no explosion, no piston ‚Äî just a quiet chemical "handshake" between H‚ÇÇ and O‚ÇÇ across a special membrane. The fuel cell is essentially a **"refillable battery"** ‚Äî the tank (hydrogen cylinder) is the energy reservoir, and the fuel cell stack is the **engine** that converts it to electricity on demand. Range anxiety disappears because refueling takes minutes, not hours.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The most automotive-relevant type is the **PEMFC (Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell)**, operating at a low temperature of **60‚Äì80 ¬∞C**, which is ideal for vehicle packaging. The standard thermodynamic cell potential under standard conditions is **E¬∞ = 1.229 V**.

#### Working Principle (Hydrogen Oxidation Half-Reactions)
At the **Anode (negative electrode):**
$$H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$$
At the **Cathode (positive electrode):**
$$\frac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$$
**Overall reaction:**
$$H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$$

---

### 1.3 Supercapacitors (Electrochemical Double-Layer Capacitors ‚Äî EDLCs)

#### Formal Definition
A **Supercapacitor** (also called an **Ultracapacitor** or **EDLC**) is an electrochemical energy storage device that stores energy **electrostatically** by separating charge at the interface between an **activated carbon electrode** (with an extremely high surface area, **1500‚Äì3000 m¬≤/g**) and an **electrolyte**. It bridges the gap between conventional capacitors (high power, low energy) and batteries (high energy, low power).

#### Intuitive Analogy ‚Äî "The Energy Sponge"
Think of a supercapacitor as a **microscopic sponge** for electrons. Instead of using chemistry to store charge (as batteries do), it uses physics ‚Äî millions of tiny holes on carbon surfaces act like millions of tiny "electron parking lots." When the car brakes, the supercapacitor soaks up huge bursts of current in a fraction of a second. When the car accelerates, it releases that current equally fast. The trade-off? It cannot hold as much **total energy** as a battery, so its range contribution is small ‚Äî but its **power contribution is enormous**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** A single supercapacitor cell operates at a low voltage (**2.5‚Äì2.85 V**), so for a 400 V EV bus, **~150 cells must be connected in series** with active cell-balancing circuitry. The capacitance ranges from **100 F to 3400 F** in commercial automotive modules.

---

### 1.4 Flywheel Energy Storage System (FESS)

#### Formal Definition
A **Flywheel Energy Storage System (FESS)** stores energy in the form of **rotational kinetic energy** in a high-speed rotating mass (rotor). Energy is transferred to/from the flywheel via an integrated **motor-generator (M/G)** unit. Modern automotive FESS uses a **carbon-fiber composite rotor** spinning in a **sealed vacuum housing** on **magnetic levitation (maglev) bearings** to eliminate friction.

#### Intuitive Analogy ‚Äî "The Mechanical Battery"
Picture a spinning top that never stops. The faster it spins, the more energy it stores. When the EV brakes, a motor-generator converts the car's kinetic energy into rotational energy, spinning the flywheel *faster* (up to **60,000‚Äì100,000 RPM**). When the car accelerates, the flywheel's rotation drives the motor-generator, which powers the wheels. It is purely mechanical ‚Äî no chemical wear, no heat loss, and the rotor can last **20+ years** with near-instantaneous charge/discharge cycles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Must-Know:** FESS is rarely used in modern road EVs but has historical importance in **Formula 1 KERS (Kinetic Energy Recovery Systems)** and niche applications like **buses and grid-tied UPS systems**. The energy stored is given by $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$, where $I$ is the moment of inertia and $\omega$ is the angular velocity.

---

### 1.5 Hybridization of Energy Storage Devices

#### Formal Definition
**Hybridization** is the engineering strategy of combining **two or more dissimilar energy storage devices** (e.g., **Battery + Supercapacitor**, or **Fuel Cell + Battery + Supercapacitor**) into a single coordinated ESS such that the **weaknesses of one device are compensated by the strengths of another**. The combined system is typically managed by a **bidirectional DC-DC converter** and a supervisory energy management strategy (EMS).

#### Intuitive Analogy ‚Äî "Marathon Runner + Sprinter"
Imagine a **marathon runner** (the battery/fuel cell) who carries you for hours at a steady pace, paired with a **sprinter** (the supercapacitor) who gives you a lightning-fast burst of energy at the start, during overtaking, or for regenerative braking. Neither alone wins the race, but together, they are unbeatable. The same logic applies to EVs: batteries provide **range**, supercapacitors provide **power**, and the DC-DC converter acts as the **"coach"** deciding who runs when.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Hybridization is visualized on the **Ragone Plot** (Energy Density vs. Power Density). No single device sits in the top-right corner ‚Äî only a *hybrid combination* can approach that ideal region.

---

### 1.6 Comparative Overview Table

| Parameter | Fuel Cell (PEMFC) | Supercapacitor (EDLC) | Flywheel (FESS) | Li-ion Battery (Reference) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy Storage Principle** | Electrochemical conversion | Electrostatic (double-layer) | Mechanical (kinetic) | Electrochemical (Faradaic) |
| **Energy Density (Wh/kg)** | 500‚Äì1000 (system) | 4‚Äì10 | 10‚Äì30 | 100‚Äì265 |
| **Power Density (W/kg)** | 200‚Äì500 | 1000‚Äì10,000 | 1000‚Äì5000 | 250‚Äì1000 |
| **Cycle Life** | N/A (stack wear) | 500,000‚Äì1,000,000 | 20+ years | 1000‚Äì3000 |
| **Charge Time** | Minutes (refuel) | Seconds | Seconds | Minutes‚ÄìHours |
| **Operating Temp** | 60‚Äì80 ¬∞C | ‚àí40 to +65 ¬∞C | Ambient | 15‚Äì40 ¬∞C |
| **Recharge Method** | H‚ÇÇ refueling | Electric | Mechanical spin-up | Electric |
| **Primary Use in EV** | Range (primary) | Power (peak) | Power (peak) | Range + Power |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fuel Cell ‚Äî Thermodynamic and Electrochemical Analysis

A fuel cell's theoretical performance is governed by **Gibbs Free Energy (ŒîG)**, which converts directly into electrical work. The **Nernst Equation** gives the actual cell voltage as a function of operating conditions.

#### Step-by-Step Logic for Fuel Cell Operation:
1. **Hydrogen gas** is fed to the anode side, where a platinum catalyst splits it into **protons (H‚Å∫)** and **electrons (e‚Åª)**.
2. Protons migrate through the **proton exchange membrane (Nafion‚Ñ¢)**; electrons are forced through the external circuit (this is the **useful current**).
3. At the cathode, **oxygen (from air)** combines with the arriving protons and electrons to form **water**, which is exhausted as vapor.
4. The cell voltage per layer is **~0.6‚Äì0.7 V** under load; to reach traction-level voltages (300‚Äì650 V), **300‚Äì500 cells are stacked in series** forming a *fuel cell stack*.
5. **Water management** is critical: too dry ‚Üí membrane dehydrates; too wet ‚Üí "flooding" blocks reactant access.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Learning Outcome Alignment (CO3):** Students must be able to derive the Nernst voltage and explain the difference between the **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** and **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** efficiency of a fuel cell system.

#### Critical Fuel Cell Formulas

| # | Formula / Parameter | Description | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta G = -nFE$ | Gibbs free energy linked to cell voltage | J/mol |
| 2 | $E_{Nernst} = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln(P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5})$ | Nernst open-circuit voltage (PEMFC) | V |
| 3 | $E^0 = 1.229$ V | Standard reversible cell potential (25 ¬∞C, 1 atm) | V |
| 4 | $\eta_{LHV} = \frac{1.482}{E_{cell} \cdot n}$ | LHV-based fuel cell efficiency | % |
| 5 | $V_{stack} = N_{cells} \cdot V_{cell}$ | Total stack voltage | V |
| 6 | $P_{stack} = V_{stack} \cdot I_{stack}$ | Stack electrical power output | W |
| 7 | $\lambda_{H_2} = \frac{\dot{m}_{H_2, supplied}}{\dot{m}_{H_2, reacted}}$ | Hydrogen stoichiometric ratio (typically 1.1‚Äì1.2) | dimensionless |

Where:
- $E^0$ = standard cell potential = **1.229 V**
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol¬∑K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature = **298.15 K** (standard)
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred = **2** (for H‚ÇÇ)
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**

---

### 2.2 Supercapacitor ‚Äî Electrostatic Analysis

A supercapacitor's capacitance arises from the formation of two charge layers (the **Helmholtz double layer**) at the electrode-electrolyte interface, separated by only **~1 nm** of dielectric. This microscopic distance, combined with the massive surface area of activated carbon, yields capacitances **thousands of times greater** than conventional capacitors.

#### Step-by-Step Logic for Supercapacitor Operation:
1. During **charging**, electrons accumulate on one carbon electrode; positive ions from the electrolyte form a matching layer on the other.
2. No chemical reaction occurs ‚Äî this is **non-Faradaic** storage, so the process is **highly reversible**.
3. During **discharge**, the charge layers collapse, releasing current almost instantaneously.
4. Because the dielectric is a thin electrolyte layer, the cell voltage is limited to **~2.85 V** (above which the electrolyte decomposes).
5. The **equivalent series resistance (ESR)** is what causes internal heating and limits peak power delivery.

#### Critical Supercapacitor Formulas

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Parallel-plate capacitance model (EDLC) | F |
| 2 | $E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$ | Total energy stored | J |
| 3 | $E_{specific} = \frac{E_{stored}}{m}$ | Gravimetric energy density | Wh/kg |
| 4 | $P_{max} = \frac{V^2}{4 \cdot ESR}$ | Maximum deliverable power | W |
| 5 | $P_{specific} = \frac{P_{max}}{m}$ | Gravimetric power density | W/kg |
| 6 | $V_{pack} = N_s \cdot V_{cell}$ | Pack voltage (series connection) | V |
| 7 | $C_{pack} = \frac{C_{cell}}{N_s}$ | Pack capacitance (series) | F |
| 8 | $\eta_C = \frac{Q_{discharge}}{Q_{charge}} \times 100\%$ | Coulombic efficiency (typically **~95‚Äì99%**) | % |

Where:
- $A$ = effective electrode surface area (can exceed **3000 m¬≤/g** of activated carbon)
- $d$ = effective charge separation distance (**~0.3‚Äì1 nm**)
- $V$ = cell voltage (typically **2.5‚Äì2.85 V**)
- $ESR$ = equivalent series resistance (typically **0.1‚Äì1 mŒ©** for large cells)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** The squared-voltage dependency ($V^2$) means that supercapacitor energy is **extremely sensitive to voltage**. If a 2.7 V cell drops to 1.35 V (50% SoC), it has already given up **75% of its stored energy** ‚Äî this is the key reason supercapacitors are paired with batteries in hybrid ESS.

---

### 2.3 Flywheel ‚Äî Mechanical Analysis

A flywheel's energy is purely **kinetic** and depends on the rotor's **moment of inertia ($I$)** and **angular velocity ($\omega$)**. Modern high-speed flywheels push $\omega$ to extremes to maximize energy density in a compact volume.

#### Step-by-Step Logic for Flywheel Operation:
1. The rotor is a **carbon-fiber/epoxy composite disc** (or steel in older designs) machined to a high safety factor.
2. It is supported by **passive magnetic bearings** (PMB) or **active electromagnetic bearings (AMB)** inside a **vacuum chamber** to eliminate aerodynamic drag.
3. A **permanent magnet synchronous motor-generator (PMSG)** integrated into the same shaft acts as both the **charge motor** and **discharge generator**.
4. During **regenerative braking**, the generator spins the flywheel up; during **acceleration**, the motor-generator converts flywheel momentum into electrical output.
5. A **containment housing** (carbon-fiber overwrap) is mandatory ‚Äî the rotor stores enormous kinetic energy and a failure is catastrophic.

#### Critical Flywheel Formulas

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_k = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ | Rotational kinetic energy (general) | J |
| 2 | $I = \frac{1}{2} m r^2$ | Moment of inertia (solid disc) | kg¬∑m¬≤ |
| 3 | $I = \frac{1}{2} m (r_o^2 + r_i^2)$ | Moment of inertia (hollow disc) | kg¬∑m¬≤ |
| 4 | $E_k = \frac{1}{4} m r^2 \omega^2$ | Kinetic energy (solid disc substitution) | J |
| 5 | $\sigma_{max} = \rho \omega^2 r^2$ | Tensile hoop stress at rim | Pa |
| 6 | $P = \tau \cdot \omega$ | Instantaneous mechanical power | W |
| 7 | $E_{specific} = \frac{E_k}{m}$ | Gravimetric energy density | Wh/kg |
| 8 | $\omega_{max} = \frac{1}{r}\sqrt{\frac{\sigma_{allow}}{\rho}}$ | Maximum safe angular velocity | rad/s |

Where:
- $m$ = mass of the rotor (typically **5‚Äì100 kg** for EV-scale FESS)
- $r$ = outer radius of rotor (typically **0.1‚Äì0.3 m**)
- $\rho$ = density of rotor material
- $\sigma_{allow}$ = allowable tensile stress (carbon fiber: **~1.5 GPa**; steel: **~700 MPa**)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Engineering Insight:** For a given volume, increasing $\omega$ is **8√ó more effective** than increasing $m$, because energy scales with $\omega^2$ but only linearly with $m$. This is the design rationale for ultra-high-speed FESS (60,000+ RPM) used in F1 KERS.

---

### 2.4 Hybridization of Energy Storage Devices

The fundamental driver for hybridization is the **Ragone Trade-off**: a single device cannot simultaneously achieve **high energy density** AND **high power density**. Hybridization is the engineering solution.

#### Hybridization Logic (Ragone Plot Interpretation)
- **Batteries** sit in the **high energy density / moderate power density** region.
- **Supercapacitors** sit in the **high power density / low energy density** region.
- **Fuel cells** sit in the **very high energy density / moderate power density** region.
- **Flywheels** sit in the **moderate energy / very high power** region.
- A **hybrid (Battery + Supercapacitor)** system shifts the operating envelope *upward and to the right* on the Ragone plot, approaching the ideal "top-right corner."

#### Hybridization Sizing Formulas

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{total} = E_{batt} + E_{cap}$ | Total system energy (parallel hybrid) |
| 2 | $P_{peak} = P_{batt,max} + P_{cap,max}$ | Total peak power capability |
| 3 | $S_oC_{cap} \in [0.5, 1.0]$ | Optimal supercapacitor SoC window (charge-sustaining) |
| 4 | $\eta_{sys} = \frac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} = \eta_{batt} \cdot \eta_{DC-DC} \cdot \eta_{cap}$ | System round-trip efficiency |
| 5 | $I_{peak} = \frac{P_{peak}}{V_{bus}}$ | Required DC-link current during peak demand |
| 6 | $C_{req} = \frac{\Delta E}{V^2}$ | Capacitance required for an energy buffer $\Delta E$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** The Toyota Mirai (Fuel Cell EV) uses a **Fuel Cell + Li-ion Battery + Supercapacitor** triple-hybrid ESS. During acceleration, the FC ramps up slowly, but the supercapacitor instantly delivers the peak current. During braking, the supercapacitor absorbs the regenerative energy, protecting the battery from high-C-rate stress.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Nernst Equation for a Hydrogen PEM Fuel Cell

**Starting Point:** The Gibbs free energy change per mole of H‚ÇÇ consumed is $\Delta G = -nFE$. At standard conditions ($T = 298.15$ K, $P = 1$ atm), the maximum reversible voltage is $E^0 = 1.229$ V.

**Step 1:** Under non-standard reactant partial pressures, the cell potential deviates from $E^0$. By the Nernst relation:

$$E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln(Q)$$

where $Q$ is the **reaction quotient**. For the overall reaction $H_2 + \frac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$:

$$Q = \frac{a_{H_2O}}{a_{H_2} \cdot a_{O_2}^{0.5}} = \frac{1}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}$$

(assuming pure liquid water product, so $a_{H_2O} = 1$).

**Step 2:** Substituting $Q$ into the Nernst equation:

$$E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(\frac{1}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Using the logarithmic identity $\ln(1/x) = -\ln(x)$:

$$E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln(P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5})$$

**Step 4:** Final KTU-ready form (for a single PEMFC cell at temperature $T$):

$$E_{cell} = 1.229 + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln(P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5})$$

**Step 5 (Numerical Example):** Assume $T = 353$ K (80 ¬∞C), $P_{H_2} = 1.5$ atm, $P_{O_2} = 0.21$ atm. Plug in:

$$E_{cell} = 1.229 + \frac{(8.314)(353)}{2(96485)} \ln(1.5 \cdot 0.21^{0.5})$$

$$E_{cell} = 1.229 + \frac{2935.84}{192970} \ln(0.6874)$$

$$E_{cell} = 1.229 + 0.01522 \times (-0.3750)$$

$$E_{cell} = 1.229 - 0.00571 = 1.2233 \text{ V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The reversible cell voltage at 80 ¬∞C and ambient O‚ÇÇ pressure is slightly **less** than the 25 ¬∞C value. This is because the cell reaction becomes less thermodynamically favorable at higher temperatures ‚Äî a critical KTU concept.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Theoretical Efficiency of a PEMFC

**Starting Point:** The electrical work output per mole of H‚ÇÇ is $W_{el} = nFE$. The chemical (Heating) energy content per mole of H‚ÇÇ is the **Higher Heating Value (HHV) = 285.8 kJ/mol** (water produced is liquid) or **Lower Heating Value (LHV) = 241.8 kJ/mol** (water produced is vapor ‚Äî the actual case in a PEMFC).

**Step 1:** Define efficiency as the ratio of electrical work output to chemical energy input:

$$\eta = \frac{W_{el}}{HHV \text{ or } LHV} = \frac{nFE}{\Delta H}$$

**Step 2:** For PEMFC using LHV (most automotive references use LHV because the exhaust water is vapor):

$$\eta_{LHV} = \frac{2 \times 96485 \times E_{cell}}{241800}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the constant:

$$\eta_{LHV} = \frac{192970 \times E_{cell}}{241800} = 0.798 \times E_{cell}$$

**Step 4:** At standard conditions, $E^0 = 1.229$ V, so:

$$\eta_{LHV, max} = 0.798 \times 1.229 = 0.981 = 98.1\%$$

**Step 5:** This is the *reversible* (thermodynamic) efficiency. In practice, due to **activation, ohmic, and concentration losses** (the polarization curve), the operating efficiency is **40‚Äì60%** in real automotive PEMFC stacks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** Compare this with an **internal combustion engine**, whose theoretical (Carnot) efficiency is capped at ~30‚Äì40% and actual efficiency is 20‚Äì35%. This is the *fundamental thermodynamic advantage* of fuel cells over ICEs in vehicles.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Stored in a Supercapacitor

**Starting Point:** For a capacitor with capacitance $C$ charged to voltage $V$, the differential energy stored at voltage $v$ when adding charge $dq = C \, dv$ is:

$$dE = v \cdot dq = v \cdot C \, dv$$

**Step 1:** Integrate from $0$ to $V$:

$$E = \int_0^V v \cdot C \, dv = C \int_0^V v \, dv$$

$$E = C \left[ \frac{v^2}{2} \right]_0^V = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$$

**Step 2:** Convert to Watt-hours (1 Wh = 3600 J):

$$E_{Wh} = \frac{C V^2}{2 \times 3600} = \frac{C V^2}{7200}$$

**Step 3 (Numerical Example):** Maxwell BCAP3400 P270 supercapacitor: $C = 3400$ F, $V_{rated} = 2.7$ V.

$$E_{Wh} = \frac{3400 \times (2.7)^2}{7200} = \frac{3400 \times 7.29}{7200} = \frac{24786}{7200} = 3.44 \text{ Wh}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Conclusion:** A massive 3400 F, 2.7 V supercapacitor stores only **~3.44 Wh** of energy ‚Äî enough to power a laptop for ~30 minutes, but in an EV, this is used for **seconds-long peak power events**, not range.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Kinetic Energy of a Solid-Disc Flywheel

**Starting Point:** The kinetic energy of a rotating rigid body is $E_k = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$, where $I$ is the moment of inertia about the spin axis.

**Step 1:** For a **uniform solid disc** of mass $m$ and outer radius $r$ rotating about its central axis, the moment of inertia is:

$$I = \frac{1}{2} m r^2$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the kinetic energy equation:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{1}{2} m r^2 \right) \omega^2 = \frac{1}{4} m r^2 \omega^2$$

**Step 3:** For a **hollow cylinder (rim flywheel)** of mass $m$, inner radius $r_i$, outer radius $r_o$:

$$I = \frac{1}{2} m (r_o^2 + r_i^2)$$

$$E_k = \frac{1}{4} m (r_o^2 + r_i^2) \omega^2$$

**Step 4 (Numerical Example):** Carbon-fiber flywheel, $m = 20$ kg, $r = 0.15$ m, $\omega = 60000$ RPM = $6283$ rad/s.

$$E_k = \frac{1}{4} \times 20 \times (0.15)^2 \times (6283)^2$$

$$E_k = 5 \times 0.0225 \times 39,476,089 = 4,441,060 \text{ J} = 4.44 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to kWh:

$$E_k = \frac{4.44 \times 10^6}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 1.23 \text{ kWh}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Practical Note:** A **20 kg flywheel spinning at 60,000 RPM stores ~1.23 kWh**, comparable to the energy of a small EV battery pack. However, the energy cannot be fully extracted (minimum safe speed is typically 50% of max), giving a *usable* energy of $\frac{1}{2} \times (1 - 0.5^2) \times 1.23 = 0.92$ kWh. The high specific power (>5 kW/kg) and 20+ year life are the real engineering advantages.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Capacitance Required in a Battery‚ÄìSupercapacitor Hybrid

**Problem Statement:** An EV draws a peak power of $P_{peak} = 100$ kW for $t_{peak} = 10$ seconds during acceleration. The DC bus voltage is $V_{bus} = 400$ V. The supercapacitor must absorb the *transient* power beyond the battery's continuous rating of $P_{batt,cont} = 60$ kW. Find the minimum supercapacitor pack capacitance.

**Step 1:** Determine the supercapacitor's peak power share:

$$P_{cap} = P_{peak} - P_{batt,cont} = 100 - 60 = 40 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the energy required from the supercapacitor during the 10 s event:

$$\Delta E = P_{cap} \times t_{peak} = 40000 \times 10 = 400000 \text{ J} = 0.111 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 3:** To allow the supercapacitor to operate in a safe SoC window (say, $V_{min} = 200$ V, $V_{max} = 400$ V ‚Äî a 2:1 voltage swing), the usable energy from a capacitor of total capacitance $C_{pack}$ is:

$$\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} C_{pack} (V_{max}^2 - V_{min}^2)$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $C_{pack}$:

$$C_{pack} = \frac{2 \Delta E}{V_{max}^2 - V_{min}^2}$$

$$C_{pack} = \frac{2 \times 400000}{(400)^2 - (200)^2} = \frac{800000}{160000 - 40000}$$

$$C_{pack} = \frac{800000}{120000} = 6.67 \text{ F}$$

**Step 5:** If each supercapacitor cell is 2.7 V, 3000 F, the number of cells in series is:

$$N_s = \frac{V_{max}}{V_{cell}} = \frac{400}{2.7} \approx 149 \text{ cells}$$

**Step 6:** Total capacitance of the pack (series combination reduces capacitance):

$$C_{pack,actual} = \frac{C_{cell}}{N_s} = \frac{3000}{149} = 20.13 \text{ F}$$

**Step 7:** Verifying that 20.13 F is greater than the required 6.67 F ‚Äî **Yes**, this design has ~3√ó safety margin. In practice, designers also derate for ESR, thermal limits, and aging.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Perspective:** This type of calculation is a **favourite 14-mark question**. Practice showing all algebraic steps, the unit consistency (J, V, F), and a final engineering sanity check.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Energy Storage Comparison

| Property | Fuel Cell | Supercapacitor | Flywheel | Battery (Reference) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy form stored** | Chemical (H‚ÇÇ tank) | Electrostatic (charge) | Kinetic (rotation) | Chemical (Faradaic) |
| **Energy density (Wh/kg)** | **500‚Äì1000** (system) | **4‚Äì10** | **10‚Äì30** | **100‚Äì265** |
| **Power density (W/kg)** | **200‚Äì500** | **1000‚Äì10,000** | **1000‚Äì5000** | **250‚Äì1000** |
| **Round-trip efficiency** | **40‚Äì60%** (LHV) | **90‚Äì98%** | **85‚Äì95%** | **80‚Äì95%** |
| **Cycle life** | Stack wear ~5000 h | **1,000,000+** | **20+ years** | **1000‚Äì3000** |
| **Self-discharge** | Very low (when fueled) | High (days‚Äìweeks) | Very low (vacuum) | Low (months) |
| **Refuel/recharge time** | **3‚Äì5 min** | **1‚Äì10 s** | **1‚Äì10 s** | **30 min ‚Äì 8 h** |
| **Cold start** | Difficult (sub-zero) | Excellent | Excellent | Degraded |
| **Environmental impact** | Zero (water exhaust) | Low (carbon, electrolyte) | Low (composite) | Mining concerns |
| **Maturity for EV use** | High (commercial) | High (commercial) | Niche (F1/bus) | Very high |
| **Primary KTU use-case** | Range extender | Peak power assist | Peak power (historical) | Main traction energy |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram A ‚Äî Fuel Cell EV Powertrain Architecture

This block diagram shows how a PEMFC system integrates with the electric drivetrain, including the mandatory **BoP (Balance of Plant)** subsystems.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Hydrogen Storage Tank H2] --> nodeB[Pressure Regulator 350 to 10 bar]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Anode Inlet Humidifier]
    nodeC --> nodeD[PEMFC Stack 300 to 500 cells]
    nodeE[Air Compressor and Filter] --> nodeF[Cathode Inlet Humidifier]
    nodeF --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeG[Water and Heat Exchanger BoP]
    nodeD --> nodeH[DC DC Boost Converter 350V to 650V]
    nodeH --> nodeI[DC Link Capacitor Bank]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Traction Inverter VFD]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Three Phase PMSM Traction Motor]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Final Drive and Wheels]
    nodeI --> nodeM[Li ion Battery Pack Peak Power Buffer]
    nodeI --> nodeN[Supercapacitor Module Transient Buffer]
    nodeO[Vehicle Control Unit VCU and EMS] --> nodeD
    nodeO --> nodeH
    nodeO --> nodeM
    nodeO --> nodeN
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The fuel cell stack is the **primary energy source**, but it cannot respond fast enough to acceleration demand. The **Li-ion battery** and **supercapacitor** modules act as the *dynamic power buffer*, absorbing regenerative braking energy and supplying peak current during acceleration. The VCU/EMS is the brain that coordinates all three sources through the DC-DC converters.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram B ‚Äî Battery + Supercapacitor Hybrid ESS (Parallel Topology)

This diagram shows the most common ESS hybridization architecture used in mild-hybrid EVs and F1 KERS.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[High Voltage DC Bus 400V] --> nodeB[Traction Inverter]
    nodeB --> nodeC[PMSM Traction Motor]
    nodeD[Li ion Battery Pack 30 to 50 kWh] --> nodeE[Battery DC DC Converter 1]
    nodeE --> nodeA
    nodeF[Supercapacitor Module 150s x 2.7V] --> nodeG[Capacitor DC DC Converter 2]
    nodeG --> nodeA
    nodeH[Regenerative Braking System] --> nodeA
    nodeA --> nodeH
    nodeI[Energy Management Controller EMC] --> nodeE
    nodeI --> nodeG
    nodeI --> nodeB
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Topology Insight:** The **dual-converter architecture** allows each storage device to operate at its **native voltage** while the DC bus is held at the inverter's required level. This is the most flexible and widely adopted topology. The alternative is a *direct parallel* connection (no DC-DC), which is cheaper but forces the battery and capacitor to share the same voltage, severely limiting utilization of the supercapacitor's wide SoC window.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram C ‚Äî Ragone Plot Concept (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

Since a true Ragone plot is a 2-D logarithmic chart, we represent it as a block-level positioning of each storage technology in the energy/power landscape.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[LOW ENERGY LOW POWER] --> nodeB[Conventional Capacitor]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Supercapacitor High Power Low Energy]
    nodeD[Flywheel High Power Moderate Energy] --> nodeE[IDEAL REGION High Energy and High Power]
    nodeF[Battery Moderate Power High Energy] --> nodeE
    nodeG[Fuel Cell System High Energy Moderate Power] --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeH[HYBRID ESS Real Engineering Solution]
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeD --> nodeH
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Reading:** No single technology occupies the **ideal top-right corner** of the Ragone plot. Hybridization ‚Äî combining a high-energy source (battery or fuel cell) with a high-power source (supercapacitor or flywheel) ‚Äî is the only way to *approach* the ideal region. This is the central engineering motivation for the entire Module 3 topic.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram D ‚Äî Flywheel Energy Storage System (FESS) Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Vacuum Chamber Housing] --> nodeB[Carbon Fiber Composite Rotor]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Magnetic Levitation Bearings PMB]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Permanent Magnet Synchronous M G Unit]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Power Electronics Converter Inverter and Rectifier]
    nodeE --> nodeF[DC Link Bus]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Vehicle DC Bus or Grid]
    nodeH[Containment Housing CFRP Overwrap] --> nodeA
    nodeI[Vacuum Pump] --> nodeA
    nodeJ[Control Unit FESS ECU] --> nodeD
    nodeJ --> nodeE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic Reading:** The rotor spins in a vacuum to eliminate aerodynamic drag, suspended by magnetic bearings to eliminate mechanical contact friction, and housed in a CFRP overwrap to contain catastrophic failure. The M/G unit acts as both motor (charging the flywheel by speeding it up) and generator (discharging by extracting rotational energy). The FESS ECU monitors rotor speed, temperature, and vacuum integrity in real time.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Nernst Equation Mistake:** Students often forget to convert $T$ to Kelvin, confuse HHV with LHV, or use the wrong sign of the logarithm. KTU key answers deduct up to 2 marks for these.
> 2. **Supercapacitor Energy:** A common error is writing $E = CV^2$ instead of $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$. **Always include the ¬Ω factor.**
> 3. **Flywheel Formula:** Some students confuse the moment of inertia of a *solid disc* ($\frac{1}{2}mr^2$) with that of a *thin ring* ($mr^2$). Read the question carefully.
> 4. **Hybrid ESS:** A favourite valuation trap: students describe a hybrid system but **fail to justify** *why* it is needed using the Ragone trade-off. A 14-mark question expects an explicit mention of energy density vs. power density limitations.
> 5. **Units:** Always use SI base units. Mixing Wh with J, or RPM with rad/s without conversion, costs marks.

---

### PART A ‚Äî Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC). List its main components and state the standard reversible cell potential at 25 ¬∞C.**

**Model Answer:**
A PEMFC is a low-temperature fuel cell (60‚Äì80 ¬∞C) that uses a **proton-conducting polymer membrane** (typically Nafion‚Ñ¢) as the electrolyte. It electrochemically combines **H‚ÇÇ** and **O‚ÇÇ** to produce electricity, water, and heat.

**Main Components:**
1. **Anode** with platinum catalyst
2. **Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM)** ‚Äî electrolyte
3. **Cathode** with platinum catalyst
4. **Bipolar plates** ‚Äî distribute gases and collect current
5. **Gas diffusion layers (GDL)** ‚Äî reactant distribution

**Standard reversible cell potential at 25 ¬∞C and 1 atm:** $E^0 = \mathbf{1.229\ V}$

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2023]`
**Compare a supercapacitor and a battery in terms of (i) energy storage mechanism, (ii) cycle life, and (iii) suitable application in an EV.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Supercapacitor | Battery (Li-ion) |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Energy storage mechanism | **Electrostatic** (Helmholtz double-layer, non-Faradaic) | **Electrochemical** (Faradaic redox reactions) |
| (ii) Cycle life | **500,000 ‚Äì 1,000,000 cycles** | **1000 ‚Äì 3000 cycles** |
| (iii) Suitable EV application | **Peak power assist** (acceleration, regenerative braking) | **Primary traction energy** (range) |

---

### PART B ‚Äî 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a PEM fuel cell-based powertrain for a Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV). List the major Balance-of-Plant (BoP) subsystems and state two key challenges of fuel cell vehicles.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the Nernst equation for a single PEMFC cell and calculate the open-circuit voltage at $T = 353$ K (80 ¬∞C) when the partial pressures are $P_{H_2} = 1.5$ atm and $P_{O_2} = 0.21$ atm. State the universal constants used.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PEMFC Powertrain for FCEV:**

The Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle powertrain consists of the following key blocks:

1. **Hydrogen Storage Tank** ‚Äî High-pressure (350 or 700 bar) Type IV composite cylinder.
2. **Pressure Regulator & Recirculation Pump** ‚Äî Reduces H‚ÇÇ pressure and recirculates unused H‚ÇÇ.
3. **Air Supply System** ‚Äî Air filter, compressor, and humidifier.
4. **PEMFC Stack** ‚Äî 300‚Äì500 cells in series producing 250‚Äì400 V.
5. **Thermal Management System (TMS)** ‚Äî Cooling loop (typically deionized water + glycol) to maintain 60‚Äì80 ¬∞C.
6. **Water Management** ‚Äî Humidification of reactant gases and removal of product water.
7. **DC-DC Boost Converter** ‚Äî Steps up the variable stack voltage to a stable DC bus voltage (e.g., 650 V).
8. **Traction Inverter (VFD)** ‚Äî Converts DC to 3-phase AC for the PMSM traction motor.
9. **Energy Storage Buffer** ‚Äî Li-ion battery and/or supercapacitor pack for peak power.
10. **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) / Energy Management System (EMS)** ‚Äî Supervises the entire powertrain.

**Two Key Challenges:**
- **Hydrogen Storage & Infrastructure:** Lack of refueling stations; high-pressure tanks have low volumetric density.
- **Cold Start & Durability:** Sub-zero cold starts require special membrane designs; Pt catalyst degradation over time.

**(b) Nernst Equation Derivation & Numerical:**

As derived in **Section 3.1** of these notes:

$$E_{cell} = 1.229 + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln(P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5})$$

**Constants Used:**
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol¬∑K)
- $F = 96485$ C/mol
- $n = 2$ (electrons per H‚ÇÇ molecule)
- $T = 353$ K

**Substitution:**

$$E_{cell} = 1.229 + \frac{(8.314)(353)}{2(96485)} \ln(1.5 \times 0.21^{0.5})$$

**Intermediate Step:**

$$\frac{(8.314)(353)}{2(96485)} = \frac{2935.84}{192970} = 0.01522 \text{ V}$$

$$\ln(1.5 \times 0.21^{0.5}) = \ln(1.5 \times 0.4583) = \ln(0.6874) = -0.3750$$

$$E_{cell} = 1.229 + 0.01522 \times (-0.3750) = 1.229 - 0.00571$$

$$\boxed{E_{cell} = 1.2233 \text{ V}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction, working, and energy storage mechanism of an Electric Double-Layer Capacitor (EDLC). Derive the expression for the energy stored and calculate the specific energy (Wh/kg) of a 3400 F, 2.7 V supercapacitor of mass 0.5 kg.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A flywheel energy storage system uses a solid steel disc of mass 30 kg and radius 0.2 m, spinning at a maximum speed of 20,000 RPM. Calculate (i) the maximum kinetic energy stored, (ii) the usable energy if the minimum safe speed is 50% of maximum, and (iii) the average power that can be delivered over 5 seconds of full discharge from usable energy.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) EDLC Construction, Working & Energy Derivation:**

**Construction:** An EDLC consists of two **activated carbon electrodes** (surface area 1500‚Äì3000 m¬≤/g) immersed in an **organic or aqueous electrolyte**, separated by an **ion-permeable separator** and housed in a sealed container. Current collectors (aluminum) connect to the external circuit.

**Working:** During charging, electrons accumulate on one electrode; oppositely charged ions from the electrolyte form a thin (~1 nm) **Helmholtz double layer** at each electrode-electrolyte interface. This creates two capacitors in series. The energy is stored **electrostatically**, with **no chemical reaction**, ensuring high reversibility and long cycle life.

**Energy Derivation:** As derived in **Section 3.3**:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 3400 \times (2.7)^2 = 12,393 \text{ J}$$

**Specific Energy Calculation:**

$$E_{specific} = \frac{12393}{0.5 \times 3600} = \frac{12393}{1800} = 6.885 \text{ Wh/kg}$$

$$\boxed{E_{specific} = 6.89 \text{ Wh/kg}}$$

**(b) Flywheel Numerical:**

**Given:** $m = 30$ kg, $r = 0.2$ m, $N_{max} = 20000$ RPM

**Convert RPM to rad/s:**

$$\omega_{max} = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 20000}{60} = 2094.4 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Moment of inertia of solid disc:**

$$I = \frac{1}{2} m r^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 30 \times (0.2)^2 = 0.6 \text{ kg¬∑m}^2$$

**(i) Maximum kinetic energy:**

$$E_{max} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_{max}^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.6 \times (2094.4)^2$$

$$E_{max} = 0.3 \times 4,386,490 = 1,315,947 \text{ J} \approx 1.316 \text{ MJ}$$

**(ii) Usable energy (50% minimum speed):**
$\omega_{min} = 0.5 \times \omega_{max} = 1047.2$ rad/s

$$E_{usable} = \frac{1}{2} I (\omega_{max}^2 - \omega_{min}^2) = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.6 \times (2094.4^2 - 1047.2^2)$$

$$E_{usable} = 0.3 \times (4,386,490 - 1,096,623) = 0.3 \times 3,289,867$$

$$E_{usable} = 986,960 \text{ J} \approx 0.987 \text{ MJ}$$

**(iii) Average power over 5 seconds:**

$$P_{avg} = \frac{E_{usable}}{t} = \frac{986,960}{5} = 197,392 \text{ W} \approx 197.4 \text{ kW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** A modest 30 kg steel flywheel can deliver ~200 kW for 5 seconds ‚Äî sufficient for a strong EV launch boost. The flywheel's mass-energy trade-off is governed by $r^2$ and $\omega^2$, both of which are pushed to extremes in modern FESS design.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Fuel Cell (PEMFC):** Electrochemical conversion of H‚ÇÇ + O‚ÇÇ ‚Üí electricity + water. Nernst equation: $E = 1.229 + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln(P_{H_2}P_{O_2}^{0.5})$. Standard potential **1.229 V** at 25 ¬∞C.
- **Fuel Cell Efficiency:** Reversible LHV efficiency ‚âà 98% theoretically; **40‚Äì60%** practically due to polarization losses.
- **Supercapacitor (EDLC):** Electrostatic storage at the Helmholtz double layer. $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$, $C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$. Energy density **4‚Äì10 Wh/kg**, power density **up to 10,000 W/kg**, cycle life **~1 million**.
- **Voltage Sensitivity of Supercapacitors:** Energy scales with $V^2$, so 50% voltage drop = 75% energy loss.
- **Flywheel (FESS):** $E_k = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$. For a solid disc, $E_k = \frac{1}{4}mr^2\omega^2$. Speed (œâ) is the dominant design lever.
- **Maximum safe speed** is limited by the **tensile hoop stress**: $\sigma_{max} = \rho \omega^2 r^2$.
- **Hybridization (Ragone Trade-off):** No single device has both high energy AND high power density. Hybrid ESS (e.g., Battery + Supercapacitor) approaches the ideal region of the Ragone plot.
- **Hybrid ESS Control:** A **bidirectional DC-DC converter** is the power-electronic interface that decouples the voltages of the two storage devices and enables independent energy management.
- **FCEV Real-world example:** Toyota Mirai (FC + Battery + Supercapacitor triple-hybrid); Honda Clarity; Hyundai NEXO.
- **KTU-Favourite Formulas to Memorize:** Nernst equation, $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$, $E_k = \frac{1}{4}mr^2\omega^2$, and the LHV efficiency factor $0.798 \times V_{cell}$.
- **Common Examiner Traps:** Forgetting the ¬Ω in capacitor energy, mixing HHV with LHV, using ¬∞C instead of K in Nernst, and confusing solid disc vs. thin ring moment of inertia.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Charging Stations: AC Level 1 & 2
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Charging Stations: AC Level 1 & 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

According to the **SAE J1772** standard (also adopted by **IEC 62196** for international harmonization), AC Level 1 and AC Level 2 charging stations refer to two distinct categories of **Alternating Current (AC) conductive charging infrastructure** that deliver electrical energy from the utility grid to the battery of a Plug-in Electric Vehicle (PEV) through the vehicle's **On-Board Charger (OBC)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Syllabus Term):**
> *AC Level 1 and Level 2 charging are conductive AC supply methods in which the EV's on-board charger (OBC) converts grid-supplied AC into regulated DC to charge the traction battery. The charging "level" differentiates based on supply voltage, current rating, and resulting power throughput.*

| Parameter | AC Level 1 | AC Level 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Standard Reference | SAE J1772 / IEC 61851-1 Mode 1 & 2 | SAE J1772 / IEC 61851-1 Mode 3 |
| Supply Voltage (India) | **230 V AC, Single Phase** | **400 V AC, Three Phase** (or 230 V single phase high current) |
| Maximum Current | **12‚Äì16 A** (typical) | **16‚Äì80 A** (typical) |
| Power Output | **1.4 kW ‚Äì 1.9 kW** (up to 2 kW) | **3.3 kW ‚Äì 19.2 kW** (up to 22 kW) |
| Charging Time (BEV) | **8‚Äì14 hours** | **4‚Äì8 hours** |
| Connector Type (India/Europe) | Type 2 (Mennekes) / Bharat AC-001 | Type 2 (Mennekes) / Bharat AC-001 |
| Primary Use Case | Residential (Home) | Residential, Workplace, Public |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ‚Äî The Water Tap & Bucket System:**
> Think of charging an EV battery like filling a large water tank (the traction battery) using a tap (the charging station).
> - **AC Level 1** is like using a **small kitchen tap** connected directly to your house's main line. The water flows slowly because the pipe is narrow, but it works overnight while you sleep ‚Äî you wake up to a full tank. No special installation is required; you just plug into the existing household socket.
> - **AC Level 2** is like installing a **bigger garden tap with a wider pipe** and a dedicated water line from the municipal supply. The flow rate is much higher, so the tank fills in a few hours. However, you need a plumber to install the dedicated line and the bigger tap.
>
> The actual "pump" that converts the AC water (alternating current) into a controlled DC stream (direct current) suitable for the battery is housed *inside* the vehicle itself ‚Äî this is the **On-Board Charger (OBC)**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Standard AC Supply in India:** **230 V, 50 Hz, Single Phase**; **400 V, 50 Hz, Three Phase**
> - **Connector Standard (India):** **Bharat AC-001** (light-duty, up to 3.3 kW) per **AIS-138 Part 1**; **Type 2 (Mennekes)** for high-power AC.
> - **Communication Protocol:** **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) signalling** over the Control Pilot pin (per SAE J1772) to negotiate safe current between EV and EVSE.
> - **Indian Standard Body:** **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** under **IS 17017** series for EV conductive charging.
> - **Efficiency Benchmark:** OBC efficiency typically **90‚Äì95%** at rated load.
> - **Power Factor:** Modern OBCs operate at **PFC > 0.95** to comply with **IEC 61000-3-2** harmonic standards.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of AC Charging

In **all AC Level 1 and AC Level 2 charging**, the power flow follows a fixed three-stage architecture:

1. **Grid AC Supply** ‚Üí Utility grid delivers single-phase 230 V or three-phase 400 V AC.
2. **EVSE (Electric Vehicle Supply Equipment)** ‚Üí The "charging station" ‚Äî its role is *only* to provide a safe, metered AC outlet with control signalling. It does **not** convert AC to DC.
3. **On-Board Charger (OBC)** ‚Üí Located *inside* the vehicle. Performs:
   - **Rectification** (AC ‚Üí DC using a diode bridge or active PFC rectifier)
   - **Power Factor Correction (PFC)**
   - **DC-DC Step-Down / Regulation** to match battery voltage
   - **Battery Management System (BMS) Communication** via CAN bus

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "AC Charging" is inherently slower than DC Charging:**
> Because the conversion hardware (rectifier + DC-DC converter) is installed **inside the vehicle**, its size and power rating are constrained by:
> - Vehicle packaging space
> - Weight constraints
> - Thermal management limits
>
> This is why AC charging is **capped typically at 22 kW** in most passenger EVs, whereas DC fast charging (Level 3) uses an off-board converter and can deliver 50‚Äì350 kW.

## 2.2 AC Level 1 ‚Äî Detailed Analysis

### 2.2.1 Technical Characteristics

AC Level 1 uses the **standard domestic electrical socket** without any dedicated infrastructure upgrade. In India, this is a **230 V, 50 Hz, single-phase** 5-pin or 15A socket (IS 1293).

**Why does Level 1 work on a regular socket?**
- The EVSE supplied with the vehicle (or a "portable EVSE") contains basic **over-current protection**, **ground fault detection (RCD/ELCB)**, and a **PWM control pilot** circuit.
- It limits the current draw to **‚â§ 16 A** (‚âà 3.5 kW theoretical, but practically limited to 1.4‚Äì1.9 kW by the OBC rating and thermal derating).

### 2.2.2 Application Scenarios

> [!NOTE]
> - **Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (PHEVs)** with small batteries (8‚Äì18 kWh) ‚Äî overnight charging is fully sufficient.
> - **Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs)** in households where the car is parked for **> 10 hours** daily.
> - **Emergency top-up charging** when no dedicated AC Level 2 station is available.
> - **Rental / fleet vehicles** in long-dwell parking lots.

### 2.2.3 Limitations

- **Very slow charging speed** ‚Äî for a 30 kWh BEV battery, full charge takes **15‚Äì20 hours**.
- **Strain on domestic wiring** if the socket is old or undersized; can cause **thermal runaway** in poor-quality wiring.
- **No real-time billing/energy metering** in most basic Level 1 setups.
- Not suitable for high-utilization commercial settings.

## 2.3 AC Level 2 ‚Äî Detailed Analysis

### 2.3.1 Technical Characteristics

AC Level 2 requires a **dedicated branch circuit** from the distribution board with:
- **230 V single-phase or 400 V three-phase** supply
- **Higher current rating**: 16 A, 32 A, 40 A, or 80 A
- **Dedicated MCB/RCD protection** (typically 30 mA Type B RCD for EV)
- **Proper earthing** per IS 3043

The **EVSE unit** is a wall-mounted or pedestal-mounted enclosure containing:
- Contactor / relay for switching
- **RCD (Residual Current Device)** with **6 mA DC fault detection** (per IEC 62955)
- **PWM Control Pilot generator**
- **Energy meter** (for billing)
- **Communication module** (optional: Wi-Fi, OCPP, RFID)

### 2.3.2 Three-Phase AC Level 2 (India/Europe)

In India and Europe, three-phase 400 V AC Level 2 is common because it allows **higher power delivery without increasing current**:

$$P_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$$

Where:
- $V_L$ = Line voltage (400 V in India)
- $I$ = Line current
- $\cos\phi$ = Power factor (‚âà 0.95‚Äì1.0 for modern OBCs)

For a **22 kW OBC** on three-phase at 32 A:
$$P = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 32 \times 1.0 = 22.18 \text{ kW}$$

This delivers a full charge (60 kWh BEV) in approximately **2.5‚Äì3 hours**.

### 2.3.3 Application Scenarios

> [!NOTE]
> - **Residential homes** with dedicated EV circuit and wall-box charger.
> - **Workplace charging** ‚Äî employees charge during 8-hour work shifts.
> - **Public parking lots**, malls, hotels, hospitals.
> - **Commercial fleet depots** for overnight fleet charging.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AC Power (Single Phase) | $P = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ | Watts (W) | Level 1 power calculation |
| 2 | AC Power (Three Phase) | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ | Watts (W) | Level 2 three-phase |
| 3 | Energy Delivered | $E = P \cdot t$ | kWh | Total energy transferred |
| 4 | Charging Time | $t = \dfrac{C_{bat}}{P \cdot \eta_{OBC}}$ | hours | Time to full charge |
| 5 | OBC Efficiency | $\eta_{OBC} = \dfrac{P_{DC,out}}{P_{AC,in}} \times 100\%$ | % | Energy loss in conversion |
| 6 | Line Current (1-œÜ) | $I = \dfrac{P}{V \cdot \cos\phi}$ | Amperes (A) | Cable sizing |
| 7 | Line Current (3-œÜ) | $I = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi}$ | Amperes (A) | Cable sizing |
| 8 | Charging Cost | $C = E \cdot r_{unit}$ | ‚Çπ/Rs | Electricity bill |
| 9 | Battery State of Charge | $SOC_{new} = SOC_{old} + \dfrac{E}{C_{bat}} \times 100$ | % | Charge added |
| 10 | Coulombic Capacity | $Q = I \cdot t$ | Ah | Energy in battery |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - $\cos\phi$ for a well-designed OBC with active PFC = **0.95 to 1.0**
> - OBC efficiency at full load = **90‚Äì95%**
> - Derate OBC to **80%** of rated power at ambient temperatures > 40 ¬∞C
> - Cable voltage drop must be **< 3%** per IS 732 / IEC 60364

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> AC Level 1 and Level 2 charging dominate the **residential and workplace** charging segment globally. As of 2024, **over 80% of EV charging sessions** in India are AC Level 1 or 2 because:
> - **Capital cost is low** (‚Çπ5,000‚Äì‚Çπ50,000 for a Level 1/2 EVSE vs. ‚Çπ5‚Äì15 lakh for DC fast charger).
> - **Grid impact is minimal** ‚Äî slow charging allows better load balancing.
> - **Battery longevity is preserved** ‚Äî slower charging produces less heat and lower C-rate stress on Li-ion cells, extending cycle life by 15‚Äì30%.
> - **Bidirectional V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid)** pilots are increasingly using AC Level 2 hardware (CHAdeMO 3.0 and ISO 15118-20).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Charging Time as a Function of Battery Capacity and OBC Power

**Problem Statement:**
A Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) has a traction battery with a usable capacity of $C_{bat} = 60$ kWh. The on-board charger (OBC) has a maximum AC input power of $P_{OBC} = 7.4$ kW (single-phase 230 V, 32 A) and an efficiency of $\eta_{OBC} = 0.93$. Calculate the time required to charge the battery from 20% SOC to 80% SOC using:
(a) AC Level 1 (1.4 kW OBC)
(b) AC Level 2 (7.4 kW OBC)

### Step 1: Determine the Energy to be Delivered

The energy required to charge from 20% to 80% SOC is:

$$\Delta SOC = 80\% - 20\% = 60\%$$

The energy required from the grid is:

$$E_{required} = C_{bat} \times \Delta SOC = 60 \text{ kWh} \times 0.60 = 36 \text{ kWh}$$

This is the **DC energy** that must ultimately reach the battery.

### Step 2: Convert DC Energy to Required AC Input Energy

Because the OBC has efficiency losses, the AC energy drawn from the grid must be:

$$E_{AC} = \frac{E_{required}}{\eta_{OBC}}$$

### Step 3: Case (a) ‚Äî AC Level 1 with 1.4 kW OBC

Using $\eta_{OBC} = 0.92$ (typical for low-power OBCs):

$$E_{AC,a} = \frac{36}{0.92} = 39.13 \text{ kWh}$$

The charging time is:

$$t_a = \frac{E_{AC,a}}{P_{Level1}} = \frac{39.13 \text{ kWh}}{1.4 \text{ kW}}$$

$$t_a = 27.95 \text{ hours} \approx 28 \text{ hours}$$

### Step 4: Case (b) ‚Äî AC Level 2 with 7.4 kW OBC

Using $\eta_{OBC} = 0.93$:

$$E_{AC,b} = \frac{36}{0.93} = 38.71 \text{ kWh}$$

The charging time is:

$$t_b = \frac{E_{AC,b}}{P_{Level2}} = \frac{38.71 \text{ kWh}}{7.4 \text{ kW}}$$

$$t_b = 5.23 \text{ hours}$$

### Step 5: Comparison Summary

| Parameter | AC Level 1 | AC Level 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Power | 1.4 kW | 7.4 kW |
| OBC Efficiency | 92% | 93% |
| AC Energy Drawn | 39.13 kWh | 38.71 kWh |
| Charging Time | **27.95 h** | **5.23 h** |
| Time Reduction | ‚Äî | **~5.3√ó faster** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Even though the OBC efficiency improves by only 1% when moving from Level 1 to Level 2, the **charging time reduces by a factor of 5.3√ó**. This is a classic case where **power density matters more than efficiency** for user convenience.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cable and Breaker Sizing for a Level 2 EVSE

**Problem Statement:**
Design the cable cross-section and MCB rating for a three-phase 400 V, 22 kW AC Level 2 EVSE installed in a residential garage. Assume $\cos\phi = 0.98$, ambient temperature 40 ¬∞C, and cable runs in PVC conduit.

### Step 1: Calculate the Full-Load Line Current

Using the three-phase power formula:

$$I_{FL} = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi}$$

$$I_{FL} = \frac{22000}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 0.98}$$

$$I_{FL} = \frac{22000}{678.97} = 32.4 \text{ A}$$

### Step 2: Apply Derating Factors (IS 3961 / IEC 60364)

- **Temperature derating factor** ($k_1$) for 40 ¬∞C ambient, PVC insulated: $k_1 = 0.91$
- **Grouping derating factor** ($k_2$) for 1 circuit in conduit: $k_2 = 1.0$
- **Overall derating factor**: $k = k_1 \times k_2 = 0.91$

### Step 3: Calculate the Required Cable Current Carrying Capacity

$$I_{z,required} = \frac{I_{FL}}{k} = \frac{32.4}{0.91} = 35.6 \text{ A}$$

### Step 4: Select the Cable

From IS 3961, a **6 sq. mm copper cable** has a current carrying capacity of **41 A** (in conduit, 40 ¬∞C). This satisfies $41 \text{ A} \geq 35.6 \text{ A}$. ‚úì

### Step 5: Select the MCB Rating

The MCB rating must be:
$$I_{MCB} \leq I_z \text{ and } I_{MCB} \geq I_{FL}$$

Select a **40 A, C-curve MCB** (suitable for inductive EV loads with inrush).

### Step 6: Verify Voltage Drop

For a 15 m cable run, the voltage drop is:

$$\Delta V = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot I \cdot L \cdot (R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)}{1000}$$

Using $R = 3.39 \;\Omega/\text{km}$ and $X = 0.085 \;\Omega/\text{km}$ for 6 sq. mm Cu:

$$\Delta V = \frac{\sqrt{3} \times 32.4 \times 0.015 \times (3.39 \times 0.98 + 0.085 \times 0.20)}{1000}$$

$$\Delta V = 0.0279 \text{ V} \approx 0.028 \text{ V}$$

The percentage voltage drop is:

$$\%\Delta V = \frac{0.028}{400} \times 100 = 0.007\%$$

This is well below the **3% limit** specified by IS 732. ‚úì

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Design Output:** Use **6 sq. mm Cu 4-core armoured cable**, **40 A C-curve MCB**, and **30 mA Type B RCD** (6 mA DC sensitive) for a 22 kW AC Level 2 EVSE.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: AC Level 1 vs Level 2 vs Level 3 (Reference Comparison)

| Parameter | AC Level 1 | AC Level 2 | DC Level 3 (Reference) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Power | 1.4‚Äì1.9 kW | 3.3‚Äì22 kW | 50‚Äì350 kW |
| Voltage | 230 V AC | 230/400 V AC | 200‚Äì1000 V DC |
| Current | 12‚Äì16 A | 16‚Äì80 A | 100‚Äì500 A |
| AC/DC Conversion | Inside vehicle (OBC) | Inside vehicle (OBC) | Inside charging station |
| Connector | Type 2, Bharat AC-001 | Type 2, Bharat AC-001 | CCS2, CHAdeMO, Bharat DC-001 |
| Time to 80% (60 kWh) | ~30 h | 3‚Äì8 h | 20‚Äì60 min |
| Cost (EVSE) | ‚Çπ5K‚Äì‚Çπ20K | ‚Çπ30K‚Äì‚Çπ2.5L | ‚Çπ5L‚Äì‚Çπ15L |
| Battery Stress | Lowest | Low | Moderate to High |
| Use Case | Home (overnight) | Home/Work/Public | Highway / Public Fast Charge |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: AC Level 1 / Level 2 Charging Power Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Utility Grid 230V/400V AC] --> B1[MCB and RCD Protection Panel]
    B1 --> C1[EVSE Control Unit with PWM Pilot]
    C1 --> D1[Charging Cable and Connector Type 2]
    D1 --> E1[Vehicle Inlet Port]
    E1 --> F1[On Board Charger Rectifier and PFC]
    F1 --> G1[DC DC Converter and Isolation]
    G1 --> H1[Battery Management System BMS]
    H1 --> I1[Li ion Traction Battery Pack]
    I1 --> J1[State of Charge SOC Display]
    
    K1[Ground Fault Detection 6mA DC] -.-> C1
    L1[OCPP Backend Server Billing] -.-> C1
    M1[CAN Bus Communication] --> F1
    M1 --> G1
    M1 --> H1
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: On-Board Charger (OBC) Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A2[AC Mains Input 230V/400V] --> B2[EMI Filter]
    B2 --> C2[Bridge Rectifier with Active PFC]
    C2 --> D2[DC Link Capacitor High Voltage Bus 400-450V DC]
    D2 --> E2[High Frequency Isolated DC DC Converter]
    E2 --> F2[Output Filter Inductor and Capacitor]
    F2 --> G2[Battery Pack 200-800V DC]
    
    H2[CAN Bus from BMS] --> I2[OBC Digital Controller DSP/MCU]
    I2 --> C2
    I2 --> E2
    
    J2[Temperature Sensors NTC] --> I2
    K2[Input Voltage and Current Sensors] --> I2
    L2[Output Voltage and Current Sensors] --> I2
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart: Choosing Between AC Level 1 and AC Level 2

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A3[Start: User Needs EV Charging] --> B3{Daily Driving Distance}
    B3 -->|Less than 30 km/day| C3{Dwell Time at Charge Location}
    B3 -->|30 to 100 km/day| C3
    B3 -->|More than 100 km/day| D3[Recommend DC Level 3 Fast Charging]
    
    C3 -->|More than 8 hours overnight| E3[AC Level 1: Standard 15A Socket]
    C3 -->|4 to 8 hours workplace or home| F3{Budget and Infrastructure Available}
    C3 -->|Less than 4 hours| D3
    
    F3 -->|Low budget no rewiring| E3
    F3 -->|Moderate budget dedicated circuit| G3[AC Level 2: 3.3 to 7.4 kW Wallbox]
    F3 -->|High budget three phase available| H3[AC Level 2: 11 to 22 kW Wallbox]
    
    E3 --> I3[Note: Slow but battery friendly]
    G3 --> I3
    H3 --> I3
    I3 --> J3[End: AC Charging Selected]
    D3 --> K3[End: DC Fast Charging Selected]
```

## 4.4 System Architecture: Communication Protocol Stack (SAE J1772 / IEC 61851)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A4[EVSE Hardware Layer] --> B4[PWM Pilot Signal Generator 1 kHz 12V]
    B4 --> C4[Control Pilot Pin CP]
    C4 --> D4[Proximity Pilot Pin PP]
    C4 --> E4[Vehicle OBC and BMS]
    
    E4 --> F4[CAN Bus ISO 11898]
    F4 --> G4[Battery State SOC Voltage Current Temperature]
    
    H4[Backend Layer] --> I4[OCPP 1.6 or 2.0.1 Protocol]
    I4 --> J4[Central Management System CMS]
    J4 --> K4[Billing and Authentication]
    J4 --> L4[Load Management and Scheduling]
    J4 --> M4[Remote Diagnostics and Firmware OTA]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

### **Q1. Differentiate between AC Level 1 and AC Level 2 EV charging with respect to supply voltage, power, charging time, and typical application.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | AC Level 1 | AC Level 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Supply Voltage | 230 V AC, single phase | 230 V / 400 V AC, single/three phase |
| Power Output | 1.4 ‚Äì 1.9 kW | 3.3 ‚Äì 22 kW |
| Charging Time (BEV) | 8 ‚Äì 14 hours | 4 ‚Äì 8 hours |
| Typical Application | Residential (home, overnight) | Residential, workplace, public parking |
| Infrastructure | Standard domestic socket | Dedicated circuit + wallbox EVSE |

**Key Distinction:** *AC Level 1 uses the existing household socket with a portable EVSE; AC Level 2 requires a dedicated branch circuit and a permanently installed EVSE wallbox with higher current capacity.* **(3 Marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark technical difference + 1 mark application)**

---

### **Q2. What is the role of the On-Board Charger (OBC) in AC Level 1 and Level 2 charging? Why is it a limiting factor for charging power?** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The **On-Board Charger (OBC)** is the AC-to-DC converter located *inside the EV*. It performs:
1. **Rectification** of grid AC into DC,
2. **Power Factor Correction (PFC)** to maintain grid-side power quality,
3. **DC-DC regulation** to match the battery's voltage and current requirements,
4. **Communication with the BMS** via CAN bus to ensure safe charging.
