**Why it is a limiting factor:** The OBC is constrained by **vehicle packaging space, weight, and thermal management**. Therefore, its power rating is typically capped at **3.3‚Äì22 kW** for passenger EVs. This is the fundamental reason AC charging is **slower than DC fast charging**, which uses a much larger off-board converter. **(3 Marks: 1.5 marks function + 1.5 marks limitation)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks ‚Äî Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style ‚Äî Module 3 Choice Question]**

### **Question A: 14 Marks**

**(a) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture and operation of an AC Level 2 EV charging system. Discuss the role of the EVSE, the OBC, and the PWM control pilot. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Block Diagram Explanation (3 Marks):**

A typical AC Level 2 charging system consists of:
- **Grid Supply:** 230 V single-phase or 400 V three-phase AC.
- **Distribution Protection:** MCB + RCD (Type B, 30 mA) at the distribution board.
- **EVSE (Electric Vehicle Supply Equipment):** The wallbox containing a contactor, control pilot circuit, energy meter, and communication module.
- **Charging Cable & Connector:** Type 2 (Mennekes) per IEC 62196-2, or Bharat AC-001 connector.
- **Vehicle Inlet:** Receives AC power into the car.
- **On-Board Charger (OBC):** Performs AC‚ÜíDC conversion, PFC, and battery voltage matching.
- **BMS:** Communicates with OBC to control charging current and prevent overcharge/over-temperature.

**2. Role of EVSE (2 Marks):**

The EVSE is **not a charger** in the electrical sense ‚Äî it is a **smart switch and safety device**. It performs:
- Safe energization/de-energization of the connector
- Ground fault detection
- PWM pilot generation for current signalling
- Energy metering for billing
- Optional: OCPP communication for backend network

**3. Role of OBC (1 Mark):**

The OBC is the **actual power conversion device**, converting grid AC to battery-compatible DC. Its power rating determines the maximum charging speed.

**4. PWM Control Pilot (1 Mark):**

A **1 kHz, ¬±12 V square wave** signal is generated by the EVSE on the Control Pilot (CP) pin. The **duty cycle** of this signal (per SAE J1772) communicates the **maximum allowable current** to the vehicle:

| Duty Cycle | Max Current |
|---|---|
| 50% | 6 A |
| 40% | 12 A |
| 30% | 18 A |
| 25% | 24 A |
| 10% | 51 A (and above) |

The OBC reads this duty cycle and limits its input current accordingly, ensuring **safe, negotiated charging**.

---

**(b) A 60 kWh BEV is charged from a 3-phase, 400 V, 22 kW AC Level 2 charger. The OBC efficiency is 94% and the power factor is 0.98. Calculate: (i) the line current drawn, (ii) the AC energy consumed for a full charge from 10% to 100% SOC, and (iii) the time taken. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $C_{bat} = 60$ kWh
- $V_L = 400$ V (3-phase)
- $P_{max} = 22$ kW
- $\eta_{OBC} = 0.94$
- $\cos\phi = 0.98$
- $SOC_{initial} = 10\%$, $SOC_{final} = 100\%$

**Step (i): Line Current**

$$I_L = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi} = \frac{22000}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 0.98}$$

$$I_L = \frac{22000}{678.97} = 32.4 \text{ A}$$

**Step (ii): DC Energy to be Stored**

$$E_{DC} = C_{bat} \times (SOC_{final} - SOC_{initial}) = 60 \times 0.90 = 54 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step (iii): AC Energy from Grid**

$$E_{AC} = \frac{E_{DC}}{\eta_{OBC}} = \frac{54}{0.94} = 57.45 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step (iv): Time Taken**

Assuming charging at rated power (deriving taper not considered for simplicity):

$$t = \frac{E_{AC}}{P_{AC}} = \frac{57.45 \text{ kWh}}{22 \text{ kW}} = 2.61 \text{ hours}$$

**Results:**
- (i) $I_L = 32.4$ A
- (ii) $E_{AC} = 57.45$ kWh
- (iii) $t = 2.61$ hours **(7 Marks: 1 + 2 + 2 + 2 for step-wise calculation)**

---

### **Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a) Compare AC Level 1, AC Level 2, and DC Level 3 (fast) charging in terms of power, charging time, infrastructure cost, and impact on battery life. State the standards governing each. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | AC Level 1 | AC Level 2 | DC Level 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Power | 1.4‚Äì1.9 kW | 3.3‚Äì22 kW | 50‚Äì350 kW |
| Charging Time (60 kWh) | ~30 h | 3‚Äì8 h | 20‚Äì60 min |
| Conversion Location | Inside vehicle (OBC) | Inside vehicle (OBC) | Inside charging station |
| Infrastructure Cost | Negligible | ‚Çπ30K‚Äì‚Çπ2.5L | ‚Çπ5L‚Äì‚Çπ15L |
| Battery Stress | Lowest | Low | Moderate to High |
| Standards | SAE J1772, IEC 61851 Mode 1/2 | SAE J1772, IEC 61851 Mode 3 | IEC 61851-23, CCS2, CHAdeMO |

**Standards:**
- **AC Level 1:** SAE J1772 (USA), IEC 61851-1 Mode 1, IS 17017 (India)
- **AC Level 2:** SAE J1772, IEC 61851-1 Mode 3, IS 17017
- **DC Level 3:** IEC 61851-23, CCS2 (IEC 62196-3), CHAdeMO, Bharat DC-001 (IS 17017 Part 2)

**Conclusion:** AC Level 1 is best for *overnight home charging of PHEVs/small BEVs*; AC Level 2 suits *daily workplace or home BEV charging*; DC Level 3 is reserved for *highway corridors and long-distance travel*. **(7 Marks: 4 marks comparison table + 2 marks standards + 1 mark conclusion)**

---

**(b) An EV with a 40 kWh battery is charged using a single-phase 230 V, 32 A AC Level 2 charger. The OBC efficiency is 92% and power factor is 0.95. Calculate: (i) the real power drawn from the grid, (ii) the DC power delivered to the battery, (iii) the time to charge from 20% to 90% SOC, and (iv) the cost of charging at ‚Çπ8 per kWh. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $C_{bat} = 40$ kWh
- $V = 230$ V, $I = 32$ A (single phase)
- $\eta_{OBC} = 0.92$, $\cos\phi = 0.95$
- Tariff = ‚Çπ8/kWh

**Step (i): Real AC Power from Grid**

$$P_{AC} = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi = 230 \times 32 \times 0.95 = 6992 \text{ W} \approx 6.99 \text{ kW}$$

**Step (ii): DC Power Delivered to Battery**

$$P_{DC} = P_{AC} \times \eta_{OBC} = 6.99 \times 0.92 = 6.43 \text{ kW}$$

**Step (iii): Energy and Time**

$$E_{DC} = 40 \times (0.90 - 0.20) = 40 \times 0.70 = 28 \text{ kWh}$$

$$E_{AC} = \frac{28}{0.92} = 30.43 \text{ kWh}$$

$$t = \frac{E_{AC}}{P_{AC}} = \frac{30.43}{6.99} = 4.35 \text{ hours}$$

**Step (iv): Cost of Charging**

$$C = E_{AC} \times r = 30.43 \times 8 = ‚Çπ243.47$$

**Results:**
- (i) $P_{AC} = 6.99$ kW
- (ii) $P_{DC} = 6.43$ kW
- (iii) $t = 4.35$ hours
- (iv) $C = ‚Çπ243.47$ **(7 Marks: 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 for stepwise calculation and final answer)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing EVSE with "Charger":** The EVSE is *not* the charger ‚Äî the OBC inside the vehicle is. Students who call the EVSE a "charger" lose 1‚Äì2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting Power Factor ($\cos\phi$):** When calculating AC power, students often write $P = V \times I$ instead of $P = V \times I \times \cos\phi$. This yields a ~5% error and costs full marks.
> 3. **Ignoring OBC Efficiency:** Always account for $\eta_{OBC}$ when computing AC energy from DC battery energy. Skipping this loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing 1-œÜ and 3-œÜ formulas:** For three-phase, use $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$, **not** $P = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$. Examiners specifically test this.
> 5. **Wrong Standard Names:** Use the **correct Indian standard** (IS 17017, AIS-138) and the **correct connector names** (Bharat AC-001, Type 2). Calling them "normal plugs" loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Not stating boundary conditions:** Always mention $\eta_{OBC} = 90\text{‚Äì}95\%$ and $\cos\phi = 0.95\text{‚Äì}1.0$ as standard assumptions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚Äî AC Level 1 & 2 Charging Stations**

- **AC Level 1:** Uses **standard 230 V / 15 A household socket**; delivers **1.4‚Äì1.9 kW**; full charge takes **8‚Äì14 hours**; ideal for **PHEVs and overnight home charging**; uses a **portable EVSE** with basic protection.
- **AC Level 2:** Uses **dedicated 230 V (1-œÜ) or 400 V (3-œÜ) circuit**; delivers **3.3‚Äì22 kW**; full charge takes **3‚Äì8 hours**; ideal for **workplace, public, and home wallbox**; requires **RCD Type B (6 mA DC sensitive)**.
- **Key Components:** Grid ‚Üí MCB+RCD ‚Üí **EVSE (Control Pilot, Contactor, Meter)** ‚Üí Cable+Connector (Type 2 / Bharat AC-001) ‚Üí Vehicle Inlet ‚Üí **OBC (Rectifier + PFC + DC-DC)** ‚Üí BMS ‚Üí Battery.
- **AC vs DC Charging:** In AC, the **OBC inside the car** converts AC to DC; in DC fast charging, the **converter is in the station** ‚Äî this is why DC is faster.
- **Key Standards:** **SAE J1772, IEC 61851-1 (Modes 1, 2, 3), IEC 62196-2 (Type 2), AIS-138 / IS 17017 (India), Bharat AC-001 (India).**
- **PWM Control Pilot:** 1 kHz square wave; **duty cycle encodes max permissible current** (e.g., 50% ‚Üí 6 A, 10% ‚Üí 51 A).
- **Key Formulas (memorize):**
  - 1-œÜ: $P = V I \cos\phi$
  - 3-œÜ: $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I \cos\phi$
  - Time: $t = \dfrac{C_{bat} \cdot \Delta SOC}{P_{AC} \cdot \eta_{OBC}}$
- **Power Factor of OBC:** **‚â• 0.95** (mandatory by IEC 61000-3-2).
- **OBC Efficiency:** **90‚Äì95%** (full load).
- **Current Limit:** 32 A single-phase gives ~7.4 kW; 32 A three-phase gives ~22 kW.
- **Connector in India (2024):** **Bharat AC-001** for ‚â§ 3.3 kW; **Type 2 (Mennekes)** for > 3.3 kW AC.
- **Engineering Insight:** AC slow charging is **battery-friendly** (low C-rate, low heat), extending cycle life; ideal for daily overnight use.
- **Bidirectional Future:** ISO 15118-20 enables **V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid)** over AC Level 2 ‚Äî the EV can feed power back to the grid.
- **Real-world numbers to remember:** Tata Nexon EV (30.2 kWh, 3.3 kW OBC ‚Üí ~9.5 h full charge); MG ZS EV (50.3 kWh, 7.4 kW OBC ‚Üí ~7 h full charge); Hyundai Kona (39.2 kWh, 7.2 kW OBC ‚Üí ~6 h).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DC Level 3, Types of Connectors, CHAdeMO
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** DC Level 3, Types of Connectors, CHAdeMO

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 DC Level 3 Charging ‚Äî Formal Definition

**DC Level 3 Charging** (also known as **High Power Charging ‚Äî HPC**) is the highest tier of the Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) **J1772** combined with the **IEC 61851-23** standard, defined for direct-current (DC) energy transfer into an electric vehicle's high-voltage battery through an off-board charger. The key boundaries of this standard are tabulated below.

| Parameter | Specification |
| :--- | :--- |
| Output Power | **150 kW to 400+ kW** (extending up to 900 kW in research prototypes) |
| DC Voltage Range | **200 V to 1000 V** |
| DC Current Range | **200 A to 400 A** (continuous); up to **500 A** in boost mode |
| Charging Time (10‚Äì80% SOC) | **~10 to 15 minutes** |
| Communication Protocol | **ISO 15118 / DIN 70121** (Power Line Communication ‚Äî PLC) |
| Cable Requirement | **Liquid-cooled** (forced coolant circulation) |
| Typical Use Case | Highway corridors, commercial fleets, intercity travel |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Highlight: A "Level 3" label in DC context **does not** mean three-phase. It denotes the *highest* power classification. Earlier versions (Level 1 and Level 2 DC) are limited to 36 kW and 90 kW respectively, and are now considered legacy.

### 1.2 Types of EV Charging Connectors ‚Äî Overview

EV charging connectors can be broadly classified based on the current type (AC or DC) and the geographic standard adopted. The major global standards are:

1. **Type 1 (SAE J1772)** ‚Äî AC, single-phase, used in North America/Japan.
2. **Type 2 (IEC 62196-2, "Mennekes")** ‚Äî AC, three-phase, used in Europe, India (proposed for AC).
3. **CCS1 (Combo 1)** ‚Äî AC Type 1 + DC pins, North American standard.
4. **CCS2 (Combo 2)** ‚Äî AC Type 2 + DC pins, European/KTU-relevant standard.
5. **CHAdeMO** ‚Äî Japanese-origin DC fast-charging standard.
6. **Tesla Connector** ‚Äî Proprietary (NACS ‚Äî North American Charging Standard, now opened).
7. **GB/T** ‚Äî Chinese national standard (20234 series).

### 1.3 CHAdeMO ‚Äî Formal Definition

**CHAdeMO** is a DC fast-charging protocol and connector specification originally developed in **2009‚Äì2010** by the **CHAdeMO Association**, a consortium founded by the **Tokyo Electric Power Company (TEPCO)**, **Nissan**, **Mitsubishi**, **Subaru**, and **Toyota**. The name is a contraction of **"CHArge de MOve"** (French: "charge for moving"), with a Japanese wordplay on **"„Å°„ÇÉ„Åß„ÇÇ"** (literally "let's have tea while charging") ‚Äî referencing the short time required to top up a battery.

| CHAdeMO Version | Maximum Power | Max Voltage | Max Current |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1.0 (2010) | **62.5 kW** | **500 V DC** | **125 A** |
| 1.2 (2017) | **200 kW** | **500 V DC** | **400 A** |
| 2.0 (2018) | **400 kW** | **1000 V DC** | **400 A** |
| 3.0 (2020) | **>400 kW** (with ChaoJi) | **1500 V DC** | **600 A** |

> [!NOTE]
> CHAdeMO is the **only** widely-deployed DC standard that inherently supports **Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G)** power flow, codified in **IEC 61850-23-3** and the **CHAdeMO 2.0** revision.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy ‚Äî "The Fuel Station Spectrum"

Imagine a hierarchy of "fuel stations" for an electric vehicle:

- **AC Level 1 & 2 (Slow Charging):** A small *kitchen-tap drip* ‚Äî fills a bucket overnight (1.4 kW to 19.2 kW).
- **DC Level 1 & 2 (Fast Charging):** A *garden hose* ‚Äî fills in 1‚Äì2 hours (up to 90 kW).
- **DC Level 3 (High Power Charging):** A **fire hydrant on a tanker truck** ‚Äî fills in 10‚Äì15 minutes (150 kW to 400+ kW). Requires a *liquid-cooled hose* because of the heat generated.

**CHAdeMO** is the *Japanese-designed fire hydrant nozzle* ‚Äî a round, chunky connector originally optimised for 50 kW but progressively scaled to HPC. **CCS** is the *European-designed combined nozzle* (AC + DC in one), and **Tesla** is the *integrated in-house nozzle* that later opened its design to other manufacturers.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world deployment reference:** A 350 kW DC Level 3 charger (e.g., **Ionity**, **Electrify America**) can add roughly **300 km of range in 15 minutes** for a modern 800-V architecture EV (Lucid Air, Hyundai E-GMP, Porsche Taycan).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 DC Level 3 ‚Äî Operational Principle

A **Level 3 DC charger** is *off-board* ‚Äî i.e., the AC-to-DC rectification, power factor correction (PFC), and high-frequency isolation all happen *inside the charging station*, not inside the vehicle. The vehicle simply receives **regulated DC power** through a thick, liquid-cooled cable directly into the battery management system (BMS).

**The "Why" behind off-board conversion:**

- A 400 kW on-board charger would weigh >100 kg and occupy the entire frunk of an EV.
- Off-boarding allows **shared infrastructure** (one charger serves many vehicles).
- The station can be tied to a **medium-voltage grid** (11 kV / 33 kV) with a step-down transformer and active PFC, achieving **>96% efficiency**.

**System-level block flow (textual):**

> Grid (HT 11 kV / 33 kV) ‚Üí Step-down Transformer ‚Üí Active Rectifier (PFC) ‚Üí DC-Link Capacitor Bank ‚Üí High-Frequency Isolated DC-DC Converter ‚Üí Output Filter ‚Üí Liquid-Cooled Cable ‚Üí EV Inlet ‚Üí BMS ‚Üí Battery Pack.

### 2.2 The Physics of Power Transfer

The fundamental governing equation is the standard DC power law:

$$P_{DC} = V_{batt} \times I_{cable}$$

Where the *cable losses* are quadratically dependent on current:

$$P_{loss} = I_{cable}^{2} \times R_{cable}$$

This quadratic penalty is **why liquid cooling is mandatory** for Level 3 ‚Äî at 400 A through a 5 m cable of even $25 \, \text{m}\Omega$ resistance, the dissipation is:

$$P_{loss} = (400)^{2} \times 0.025 = 4000 \, \text{W}$$

Without active cooling, the cable insulation would melt within minutes.

### 2.3 Connector Communication ‚Äî "The Conversation"

For a charging session to initiate, the EV and the charger must exchange a "handshake":

1. **Proximity Detection (PP):** Mechanical switch inside the connector indicates insertion.
2. **Control Pilot (CP):** PWM signal establishes the *allowed current* and *charging state*.
3. **Locking Motor:** A 12 V actuator physically locks the connector into the inlet.
4. **Digital Handshake:** Higher-layer protocol (ISO 15118 for CCS, CAN for CHAdeMO) negotiates **SOC limits, voltage, current, time-of-use tariffs**.
5. **Contactors Close:** HV contactors in the battery pack energise the bus.
6. **Power Transfer Begins** with closed-loop current regulation.

### 2.4 CHAdeMO ‚Äî Detailed Specification

The CHAdeMO connector is a **round, large-diameter (‚âà70 mm) plastic assembly** with a distinctive **notched keyway**. The pin map is:

| Pin # | Name | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1, 4 | **DC + / DC ‚àí** | Main power transfer (up to 400 A continuous) |
| 2, 5 | **GND (Signal ground)** | Reference for CAN bus |
| 3 | **CAN-H** | Controller Area Network High |
| 6 | **CAN-L** | Controller Area Network Low |
| 7 | **CP (Control Pilot)** | PWM-based pilot signal |
| 8 | **PP (Proximity)** | Detect plug insertion |
| 9, 10, 11, 12 | **V2X / V2G** | Bi-directional power flow signals |
| 13 | **PE** | Protective Earth |
| ‚Äî | **Locking Motor (12 V)** | Mechanical retention during charging |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $P_{DC} = V \cdot I$ | DC charging power | W (kW) | Power delivered to battery |
| $P_{loss} = I^{2} R$ | Cable copper loss | W | Heat dissipated in cable |
| $\eta = \dfrac{P_{batt}}{P_{grid}}$ | Charger efficiency | dimensionless | Energy bill calculation |
| $t = \dfrac{E_{req}}{P_{DC} \cdot \eta}$ | Charging time | hours (or s) | SOC estimation |
| $E_{req} = \text{SOC}_{target} - \text{SOC}_{init} \cdot C_{batt}$ | Energy required | kWh | Range-add calculation |
| $V_{max} \cdot I_{max} \geq P_{rated}$ | Sizing check | V, A | Connector / station sizing |
| $\Delta T = \dfrac{I^{2} R}{h \cdot A}$ | Cable temperature rise | K (¬∞C) | Cooling design |

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Hallucination Note:** The CHAdeMO connector pinout is **10 functional pins** plus 2 large DC power contacts. Total contact count is 10 (signal) + 2 (DC) = 12 (excluding the 12 V locking actuator which is wired separately). Do not confuse with the **GB/T 20234.3** which has a *different* pinout but similar philosophy.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

DC Level 3 infrastructure is critical for:

- **Highway corridors** (IONITY in Europe, Electrify America in USA, CHARGE:ZONE in India).
- **Fleet depots** for last-mile delivery (Amazon Rivian vans charge at 200 kW).
- **Bus terminals** (opportunity charging at 450 kW via pantograph ‚Äî though this uses a *different* pantograph-down/up mechanism, not a plug).

CHAdeMO's **V2G** capability is being trialled in **Denmark, UK, and Japan** where parked EVs feed energy back to the grid during peak hours.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Charging Time for a Given State-of-Charge (SOC) Window

**Problem Statement:** A **60 kWh** lithium-ion battery in an EV is currently at **SOC = 20%** and must be charged to **SOC = 80%** using a DC Level 3 charger rated at **150 kW** with an end-to-end efficiency of **92%**. Estimate the charging time, ignoring taper (constant power assumption).

**Step 1 ‚Äî Define the energy required:**

$$E_{req} = (\text{SOC}_{target} - \text{SOC}_{init}) \cdot C_{batt}$$

Substituting values:

$$E_{req} = (0.80 - 0.20) \cdot 60 = 0.60 \cdot 60 = 36 \, \text{kWh}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Define the effective DC power delivered to the battery:**

$$P_{batt} = P_{rated} \cdot \eta_{chrg}$$

$$P_{batt} = 150 \cdot 0.92 = 138 \, \text{kW}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute the charging time:**

$$t = \dfrac{E_{req}}{P_{batt}}$$

$$t = \dfrac{36}{138} = 0.2609 \, \text{hours}$$

Converting to minutes:

$$t = 0.2609 \cdot 60 \approx 15.65 \, \text{minutes}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Account for the taper effect (correction factor):**

In reality, a battery cannot accept full power for the entire 60% window. Above 50% SOC, the BMS *tapers* current to avoid lithium plating. A practical *availability factor* $k_{taper} \approx 0.75$ for fast-charging windows:

$$t_{real} = \dfrac{t_{ideal}}{k_{taper}} = \dfrac{15.65}{0.75} \approx 20.87 \, \text{minutes}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Cable Sizing and Voltage Drop

**Problem:** A Level 3 charger delivers **400 A** to an 800-V architecture EV through a **5 m** copper cable of cross-section **95 mm¬≤** (resistivity $\rho = 1.72 \times 10^{-8} \, \Omega\cdot\text{m}$). Compute the cable resistance and the steady-state copper loss.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Cable resistance:**

$$R = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{A}$$

$$R = \dfrac{1.72 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 5}{95 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R = \dfrac{8.6 \times 10^{-8}}{9.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 9.05 \times 10^{-4} \, \Omega \approx 0.905 \, \text{m}\Omega$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Copper loss:**

$$P_{loss} = I^{2} R = (400)^{2} \cdot 9.05 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$P_{loss} = 160000 \cdot 9.05 \times 10^{-4} = 144.8 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Voltage drop (informative):**

$$\Delta V = I \cdot R = 400 \cdot 9.05 \times 10^{-4} = 0.362 \, \text{V}$$

This is negligible compared to 800 V (<0.05%), but the **thermal** aspect is the limiting constraint.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Connector Standards

| Attribute | CHAdeMO | CCS2 | Tesla (NACS) | GB/T (DC) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Origin** | Japan | EU + USA | USA (now open) | China |
| **Max Power** | 400 kW (v2.0) | 350 kW (HPC) | 250 kW (V3) | 250 kW+ |
| **Max Voltage** | 1000 V | 1000 V | 500 V (V3) | 1000 V |
| **Max Current** | 400 A | 400 A | 631 A peak | 250 A |
| **Communication** | CAN 2.0B | ISO 15118 (PLC) | Proprietary CAN | CAN |
| **V2G Support** | ‚úÖ Yes (native) | ‚úÖ Yes (ISO 15118-20) | ‚ùå Limited | ‚öÝÔ∏è Planned |
| **Plug Size** | Large (round) | Medium (combined) | Small (combined) | Large |
| **Cooling** | Optional (water) | Liquid (mandatory HPC) | Liquid | Liquid |
| **Adoption** | Japan, EU, declining in EU | EU, India (proposed) | North America | China only |
| **Auto Unlock** | Manual / Motor | Motor (ISO) | Motor | Motor |
| **Cost** | Medium | Medium-High | Low (Tesla scale) | Low |

### 3.4 Decision Algorithm ‚Äî Choosing a Connector

A student / engineer can decide on a connector type by following this logic:

> **Step A:** Vehicle architecture? ‚Üí 800 V system ‚Üí Must support >500 V (all three qualify).
> **Step B:** Region? ‚Üí EU/India ‚Üí CCS2 ; Japan/legacy ‚Üí CHAdeMO ; North America ‚Üí NACS ; China ‚Üí GB/T.
> **Step C:** V2G required? ‚Üí Yes ‚Üí CHAdeMO or ISO 15118-20 compliant CCS2.
> **Step D:** Power >200 kW? ‚Üí Yes ‚Üí Mandatory liquid cooling, then any of CCS2/CHAdeMO 2.0/Tesla V3.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a DC Level 3 Charging Station

The following Mermaid diagram maps the power and signal flow from the high-voltage grid to the EV battery.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G[HV Grid 11kV or 33kV AC] --> T[Step-Down Transformer 11kV to 400V]
    T --> A[Active Rectifier with PFC]
    A --> D[DC Link Capacitor Bank 1000V]
    D --> I[Isolated DC-DC Converter HF]
    I --> F[Output LC Filter]
    F --> LC[Liquid Cooled Cable Assembly]
    LC --> EV[EV Inlet and BMS]
    EV --> B[High Voltage Battery Pack 400V to 800V]
    CP[Control Pilot and ISO 15118 PLC] -.-> EV
    CP -.-> A
    COOL[Coolant Circulation Unit] --> LC
```

### 4.2 CHAdeMO Connector ‚Äî Pinout Schematic (Front View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CHAdeMO_Connector_Pinout
        P1[Pin 1 DC Positive Large] --> P4[Pin 4 DC Negative Large]
        P3[Pin 3 CAN High] --> P6[Pin 6 CAN Low]
        P2[Pin 2 Signal GND] --> P5[Pin 5 Signal GND]
        P7[Pin 7 Control Pilot] --> P8[Pin 8 Proximity Detect]
        P9[Pin 9 V2G Signal A] --> P10[Pin 10 V2G Signal B]
        P11[Pin 11 V2G Enable] --> P12[Pin 12 V2G Status]
        P13[Pin 13 Protective Earth] --> M[Mechanical Lock 12V Motor]
    end
```

### 4.3 EV Charging Connector Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Vehicle Arrives at Charger] --> Q1{Region of Operation}
    Q1 -->|Europe or India| CCS2[Use CCS2 Connector]
    Q1 -->|Japan or Legacy Fleet| CHA[Use CHAdeMO Connector]
    Q1 -->|North America| NACS[Use NACS Tesla Connector]
    Q1 -->|China| GBT[Use GB/T 20234 Connector]
    CCS2 --> Q2{Power Required}
    CHA --> Q2
    NACS --> Q2
    GBT --> Q2
    Q2 -->|Up to 50 kW| AC[AC Slow or AC Fast]
    Q2 -->|50 to 150 kW| DC2[DC Level 2 Air Cooled]
    Q2 -->|Above 150 kW| DC3[DC Level 3 Liquid Cooled HPC]
```

### 4.4 CHAdeMO Communication Sequence (Handshake)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant EV as Electric Vehicle
    participant CP as Control Pilot
    participant CHG as CHAdeMO Charger
    participant CAN as CAN Bus Network
    EV->>CP: Plug inserted PP contact closes
    CP->>CHG: Pilot PWM establishes available current
    CHG->>EV: Connector locking motor energised
    EV->>CAN: Init charging session via CAN 2.0B
    CHG->>EV: Insulation test and parameter negotiation
    EV->>CHG: Close HV contactors acknowledge
    CHG->>EV: Begin current ramp up
    loop During Charging
        EV->>CHG: Battery SOC voltage current request
        CHG->>EV: Adjust current and voltage
    end
    EV->>CHG: Target SOC reached stop request
    CHG->>EV: Ramp down to zero current
    EV->>CHG: Open contactors
    CHG->>EV: Unlock connector
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a flowchart and not a physical photograph?** The KTU board examinations require *schematic clarity* over photographic realism. The above block diagrams convey both **electrical topology** and **control hierarchy** ‚Äî the two most examinable aspects of this topic.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Model Paper 2024]**
**Define DC Level 3 charging. List any two of its salient features.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

DC Level 3 charging, also called **High Power Charging (HPC)**, is the highest classification of DC fast charging under **SAE J1772** and **IEC 61851-23**, delivering power above **150 kW** at voltages up to **1000 V DC** and currents up to **400 A**.

**Salient features (any two, 1.5 marks each):**

1. **Liquid-cooled cables** are mandatory to dissipate $I^2 R$ heat at high current levels.
2. Communication is via **ISO 15118 / DIN 70121** (Power Line Communication), enabling *Plug & Charge* and *bidirectional* (V2G) operation.
3. Typical charging time is **10‚Äì15 minutes** for a 10‚Äì80% SOC window.
4. Power factor corrected active rectifiers on the AC side achieve **>96% efficiency**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023]**
**What does CHAdeMO stand for? Mention the country of origin and its maximum power rating (v2.0).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

CHAdeMO stands for **"CHArge de MOve"** ‚Äî derived from the French phrase meaning *"charge for moving"*. It is also a play on the Japanese phrase *"„ÅäËå∂„Åß„ÇÇ"* (ocha demo) meaning *"let's have a cup of tea"*, alluding to the short charging duration.

- **Country of origin:** Japan (developed by TEPCO, Nissan, Mitsubishi, Subaru, Toyota in 2009‚Äì2010).
- **Maximum power rating (CHAdeMO v2.0):** **400 kW** at 1000 V DC and 400 A continuous current.

---

### 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by (1) confusing **AC Level 3** (proposed 480 V three-phase, not widely implemented) with **DC Level 3** (the high-power standard), and (2) omitting the **liquid cooling** justification in the cable design. Always state the *reason* for design choices ‚Äî KTU examiners reward justification, not just definition.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) ‚Äî [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **DC Level 3 fast-charging station**, including the role of the **liquid-cooled cable assembly** and the **ISO 15118 communication protocol**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw the **CHAdeMO connector pinout** and explain the function of each pin. Discuss the **V2G capability** of CHAdeMO. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture of DC Level 3 Fast-Charging Station (7 marks)**

The DC Level 3 station is an **off-board charger** with the following major blocks (1 mark per block ‚Äî full marks for a labelled diagram):

1. **Medium-Voltage Grid Input (11 kV / 33 kV AC):** Power is drawn from the utility substation.
2. **Step-Down Transformer (1.5 marks):** Reduces the MV to a usable 400 V three-phase AC. Often integrated with the rectifier as a *Solid-State Transformer (SST)* in advanced designs.
3. **Active Rectifier with PFC (1.5 marks):** Converts AC to a regulated DC link (~1000 V) using IGBTs or SiC MOSFETs. PFC maintains a power factor >0.99, satisfying IEEE 519 harmonic limits.
4. **Isolated DC-DC Converter (1.5 marks):** A high-frequency (typically 50‚Äì100 kHz) isolated stage (DAB ‚Äî Dual Active Bridge) provides galvanic isolation (required for safety) and steps the DC link to the EV's required voltage.
5. **Liquid-Cooled Cable Assembly (1 mark):** Mandatory at this power level. A closed-loop coolant (water-glycol mixture) circulates through the cable's hollow conductors, carrying heat to a chiller at the dispenser. Without this, cable temperature can exceed 90 ¬∞C in minutes.
6. **ISO 15118 Communication (1 mark):** PLC over the Control Pilot pin enables *Plug & Charge* (automatic billing via certificate exchange), smart-charge scheduling, and **bidirectional V2G** control. Replaces older PWM-only signalling.

**(b) CHAdeMO Connector Pinout and V2G (7 marks)**

**Pinout diagram (5 marks):**

| Pin # | Name | Function (1 mark total) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1, 4 | DC+, DC‚àí | Main power transfer (up to 400 A) |
| 2, 5 | Signal GND | Reference for communication signals |
| 3, 6 | CAN-H, CAN-L | CAN 2.0B communication bus |
| 7 | CP (Control Pilot) | PWM-based plug insertion and current request |
| 8 | PP (Proximity) | Detects plug insertion via mechanical switch |
| 9‚Äì12 | V2X signals | V2G handshake, enable, status, fault |
| 13 | PE (Protective Earth) | Safety grounding |

**V2G Discussion (2 marks):**

CHAdeMO is the **first widely deployed standard to natively support V2G**, codified in **CHAdeMO 2.0** and harmonised with **IEC 61850-23-3**. The EV can *export* power back to the grid at up to **400 kW** during peak demand. A bidirectional CHAdeMO charger uses a *back-to-back* IGBT converter topology. Real-world deployments include:

- **Denmark (Frederiksberg Forsyning project):** 10 Nissan Leafs in a fleet providing grid services.
- **UK (Octopus Energy V2G trial):** Renault Zoe and Nissan Leaf supplying homes during peak hours.
- **Japan (Tepco V2G hubs):** Pre-cursor project for disaster-resilient energy.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî [KTU University Exam ‚Äî June 2024]**

**(a)** Compare **CHAdeMO, CCS2, and Tesla NACS** connectors in terms of power, communication, V2G support, and regional adoption. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **step-by-step charging handshake** between an EV and a DC Level 3 charger. Why is the *lock motor* a critical safety element? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison of Connectors (7 marks)**

A tabular answer (1 mark per row, plus 1 mark for summary / concluding remarks):

| Parameter | CHAdeMO | CCS2 | Tesla NACS |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Max Power** | 400 kW (v2.0) | 350 kW (HPC) | 250 kW (V3) |
| **Max Voltage** | 1000 V | 1000 V | 500 V (V3) |
| **Max Current** | 400 A | 400 A | 631 A peak |
| **Communication** | CAN 2.0B | ISO 15118 (PLC) | Proprietary CAN |
| **V2G** | ‚úÖ Native | ‚úÖ (ISO 15118-20) | ‚ùå Limited |
| **Cooling** | Optional | Liquid (HPC) | Liquid |
| **Region** | Japan, declining EU | EU, India (proposed) | North America |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** CCS2 is the de-facto global standard for *new* HPC deployments outside North America and China; CHAdeMO retains a niche as the **only V2G-mature** standard; Tesla's NACS is gaining adoption rapidly after being opened to other OEMs in 2023.

**(b) EV‚ÄìCharger Handshake (7 marks)**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Mechanical Insertion (1 mark):** The user aligns the connector and pushes it in. A mechanical *proximity switch* (PP pin on CHAdeMO; resistance coding on CCS2) closes, signalling that the plug is seated.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Pilot Signal (1 mark):** A 1 kHz PWM signal on the **Control Pilot (CP)** pin establishes the *maximum allowed current* and indicates charger readiness. The EV's onboard controller reads this PWM duty cycle to determine if charging may proceed.

**Step 3 ‚Äî Locking (1 mark):** A 12 V DC actuator physically locks the connector into the inlet. This is critical because at 400 A and 1000 V, *any* unintended disconnection would create a **sustained DC arc** that cannot self-extinguish (unlike AC) ‚Äî risking fire and electrocution. **(1 mark for the arc explanation)**

**Step 4 ‚Äî Digital Handshake (1 mark):** High-level protocol (CAN for CHAdeMO; PLC for CCS2) negotiates *battery voltage window, target SOC, current ramp profile, time-of-use tariff*. For CHAdeMO 2.0, this also negotiates V2G direction.

**Step 5 ‚Äî Insulation Test & Pre-charge (1 mark):** The charger performs an *isolation check* (leakage current < 1 mA per kV) and pre-charges the EV's internal DC-link capacitor to match the station voltage, preventing inrush surges.

**Step 6 ‚Äî HV Contactors Close & Power Flow (1 mark):** The EV's main contactors close, and current ramps up linearly. Closed-loop control adjusts current every 100 ms based on BMS feedback.

**Step 7 ‚Äî End of Charge (1 mark):** When target SOC is reached, current ramps down to zero, contactors open, the connector unlocks, and the user can remove it. Any anomaly trips the *emergency stop* via a hardware interlock, bypassing software.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist ‚Äî KTU 2024 Style**

- ‚úÖ **DC Level 3** = power > 150 kW, voltage up to 1000 V, current up to 400 A, *liquid-cooled cable mandatory*.
- ‚úÖ **Off-board rectification** ‚Äî the *charger* converts AC to DC, not the vehicle.
- ‚úÖ **Cable loss** is $I^{2} R$ ‚Äî quadratic in current, which is why cooling is essential.
- ‚úÖ **CHAdeMO** originates in **Japan (2010)**, max 400 kW (v2.0), uses **CAN 2.0B** for communication.
- ‚úÖ **CHAdeMO** is **inherently V2G-capable** ‚Äî the only widely deployed DC standard with native bidirectional power flow.
- ‚úÖ **CCS2** uses **ISO 15118** (PLC), while **CHAdeMO** uses **CAN bus** ‚Äî this is a frequently tested differentiator.
- ‚úÖ Connector pinout for CHAdeMO: 2 DC power pins, 2 ground, 2 CAN, 1 CP, 1 PP, 4 V2X, 1 PE = **13 functional pins** (excluding mechanical lock).
- ‚úÖ Charging time formula: $t = \dfrac{(\text{SOC}_{f} - \text{SOC}_{i}) \cdot C_{batt}}{P_{DC} \cdot \eta}$.
- ‚úÖ **SAE J1772** is the AC Level 1/2 standard; **IEC 61851-23** defines DC charging including Level 3.
- ‚úÖ Tesla's **NACS** was *proprietary* until 2023; it is now an open standard and is rapidly replacing CCS1 in North America.
- ‚úÖ **GB/T** is China-only; *not* compatible with CHAdeMO, CCS, or NACS mechanically.
- ‚úÖ **Liquid cooling** is required when continuous current exceeds ~200 A to keep cable surface temperature below 50 ¬∞C.
- ‚úÖ **Bidirectional V2G** is essential for future grid stability ‚Äî CHAdeMO leads, CCS2 follows (ISO 15118-20).

> **End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes ‚Äî Module 3 ‚Äî DC Level 3, Connectors, CHAdeMO**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CCS Type 1 and 2
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** CCS Type 1 and 2 # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is CCS? **Combined Charging System (CCS)** is a global standard for AC and DC fast charging of electric vehicles (EVs), governed by **IEC 62196-3** and **SAE J1772** specifications. It consolidates slow AC charging and rapid DC charging into a single, compact vehicle inlet using a "combo" architecture. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3):** *CCS is an integrated connector standard that superimposes two large DC power pins beneath a standard AC connector, allowing a single port on the vehicle to accept both slow AC (Level 1/2) and rapid DC (Level 3) charging using either Type 1 (SAE) or Type 2 (IEC) communication protocols.* ## 1.2 The Two Variants ‚Äî Type 1 vs Type 2 | Variant | Region | Standard | AC Pin Count | AC/DC Combo | |---|---|---|---|---| | **CCS Type 1 (Combo 1)** | North America, South Korea, Japan | SAE J1772 (AC) + SAE J1772 Combo (DC) | 5 pins | 5 + 2 DC pins | | **CCS Type 2 (Combo 2)** | Europe, India, Australia, UK, Middle East | IEC 62196-2 (AC) + IEC 62196-3 (DC) | 7 pins | 7 + 2 DC pins | > [!NOTE] > **The "Combo" Principle:** Imagine a single power socket at your home that can accept both a regular 3-pin laptop charger and a high-current industrial welder plug ‚Äî that's the philosophical idea behind CCS. The two extra large DC pins are physically placed *below* the AC pin cluster, so the vehicle has **one inlet** but accepts **two families of plugs**. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The Universal EV Power Strip Think of CCS as the **"USB-C of electric vehicles"**: - Your laptop (the EV) has **one USB-C port** (the vehicle inlet). - You can plug in a **slow 5V phone charger** (AC Level 1/2) for overnight top-up. - Or plug in a **240W fast charger** (DCFC) for a 0‚Äì80% boost in 20 minutes. - The connector *type* you pick depends on the **country's electrical grid standard** (120V/240V/415V). Similarly, the **vehicle manufacturer decides whether to install a CCS1 or CCS2 inlet** based on the target market's grid infrastructure and regional regulation. ## 1.4 Key Standardized Metrics - **Maximum DC Charging Current (Type 1):** **200 A continuous, 500 A peak (with liquid cooling)** - **Maximum DC Charging Current (Type 2):** **500 A continuous (with liquid cooling)** - **Maximum DC Voltage:** **1000 V DC** (both types) - **AC Power (Type 1):** up to **19.2 kW** (240 V, 80 A single-phase) - **AC Power (Type 2):** up to **43 kW** (400 V, 63 A three-phase) - **Communication Protocol (DC):** **PLC over CP/PP using ISO 15118** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why two large DC pins only?** A DC fast charger pushes hundreds of amps at ~400 V. The two oversized pins (positive & negative) minimize resistive (I¬≤R) losses. The smaller AC pins cannot physically carry this current ‚Äî that is the engineering reason a "combo" design was necessary. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Pin Architecture Breakdown ### 2.1.1 CCS Type 1 (SAE J1772 Combo 1) ‚Äî 7-Pin Architecture The Type 1 connector is a **single-phase oriented** design with 5 AC pins + 2 large DC pins (placed at the bottom of the connector body). | Pin # | Designation | Function | Conductor Size (typ.) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | L1 (Line) | AC Phase / Live conductor | 5.3 mm¬≤ (8 AWG) | | 2 | N (Neutral) | AC Neutral return | 5.3 mm¬≤ (8 AWG) | | 3 | PE (Protective Earth) | Safety ground | 5.3 mm¬≤ (8 AWG) | | 4 | CP (Control Pilot) | Communication + signaling | 0.75 mm¬≤ (18 AWG) | | 5 | PP (Proximity Pilot) | Plug presence + current rating | 0.75 mm¬≤ (18 AWG) | | 6 | DC+ | DC Positive (large) | 80‚Äì120 mm¬≤ | | 7 | DC‚àí | DC Negative (large) | 80‚Äì120 mm¬≤ | ### 2.1.2 CCS Type 2 (IEC 62196-3 Combo 2) ‚Äî 9-Pin Architecture The Type 2 connector is a **three-phase oriented** design (Mennekes style for AC) + 2 large DC pins. | Pin # | Designation | Function | |---|---|---| | 1 | L1 | AC Phase 1 | | 2 | L2 | AC Phase 2 (absent in single-phase installs) | | 3 | L3 | AC Phase 3 (absent in single-phase installs) | | 4 | N | AC Neutral | | 5 | PE | Protective Earth | | 6 | CP | Control Pilot | | 7 | PP | Proximity Pilot | | 8 | DC+ | DC Positive (large) | | 9 | DC‚àí | DC Negative (large) | > [!NOTE] > **The Locking Actuator:** Both CCS variants use a **motorized locking pin** that engages a hole near the top of the connector. For DC charging, the EV electronically locks the connector to the inlet (a regulatory safety requirement). For AC, locking is optional and depends on EV manufacturer policy. ## 2.2 Operating Principles ‚Äî Step-by-Step ### Step 1 ‚Äî Physical Engagement The user inserts the charging plug. **Proximity Pilot (PP)** resistor value (typically 150 Œ©, 480 Œ©, or 680 Œ©) tells the EV the **maximum current** the cable assembly can carry. ### Step 2 ‚Äî Handshake ‚Äî Control Pilot (CP) The CP pin uses a **1 kHz PWM signal** (12 V nominal, ¬±12 V swing) to communicate: - **State A (12 V, no diode):** Standby, no EV connected - **State B (9 V, no diode):** EV connected, ready - **State C (6 V, no diode):** EV requesting charging (ventilation not required) - **State D (3 V, no diode):** EV requesting charging with ventilation requirement The **duty cycle** of this PWM (5%‚Äì95%) encodes the **maximum available current** from the EVSE. ### Step 3 ‚Äî Digital Handshake (DC Mode) For DC fast charging, after the basic pilot handshake, communication switches to **Power Line Communication (PLC)** over the Control Pilot pin pair, using the **ISO 15118** stack (TCP/IP, SLAC, V2G messages). This enables: - Battery state-of-charge reporting - Smart charging (V1G) - Bidirectional V2G (V2X) - Plug & Charge (PnC) authentication ### Step 4 ‚Äî Lock Confirmation EV confirms locking actuator is engaged ‚Üí proceeds to high-current DC contactor closure. ### Step 5 ‚Äî Energy Transfer DC current flows through pins 6/7 (Type 1) or 8/9 (Type 2). The EV's BMS (Battery Management System) dynamically commands the EVSE to ramp up/down current based on **State of Charge (SOC), temperature, and cell voltage limits**. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | DC Charging Power | $P_{DC} = V_{batt} \times I_{chg}$ | kW | Single-line, no phase factor | | AC 1-Phase Charging Power | $P_{AC,1\phi} = V \times I \times \cos\phi$ | kW | $\cos\phi \approx 0.95$‚Äì1.0 for EV chargers | | AC 3-Phase Charging Power | $P_{AC,3\phi} = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_L \times \cos\phi$ | kW | $V_L$ = line-to-line voltage (400 V EU) | | Maximum DC Current (CCS2) | $I_{max} = 500 \text{ A (liquid-cooled)}$ | A | IEC 62196-3 limit | | Charging Time (estimated) | $t = \dfrac{E_{batt} \times \Delta SOC}{P_{chg} \times \eta_{chg}}$ | hours | $\eta_{chg} \approx 0.90$‚Äì0.95 | | Energy per Pin Cross-Section | $E = I^2 R t$ (losses) | J | I¬≤R losses grow quadratically with current | | CCS2 3-Phase Max Power | $P = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 63 \times 1.0$ | kW | = **43.65 kW** theoretical AC limit | | CCS1 1-Phase Max Power | $P = 240 \times 80 \times 1.0$ | kW | = **19.2 kW** theoretical AC limit | | Plug to Charge Voltage Drop | $V_{drop} = I \times R_{cable}$ | V | 100 mV/A target for DCFC cables | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL ‚Äî Avoid pipe `|` in tables:** All absolute-value notations use the `\\vert` LaTeX delimiter (e.g., $\\vert V_L \\vert$) to prevent breaking the markdown table parser. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **DCFC Network Deployment (India):** Bharat Petroleum, Tata Power, Statiq, and Ather Grid deploy **CCS2** stations following **Bharat EV AC-001 / DC-001** updates aligning with **IEC 62196-3 Type 2 combo** at 50‚Äì150 kW. - **Tesla Compatibility:** Tesla vehicles in Europe use a **modified Type 2 inlet** (without the lower DC pins). They access CCS2 stations via an adapter or via the native dual-mode (Tesla calls it "Magic Dock") inlet. - **North America (2025+ shift):** NACS (Tesla's connector) is being adopted by Ford, GM, Rivian ‚Äî but **CCS1 remains the standard** for all non-Tesla OEMs in the US. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Theoretical DC Power for a CCS2 Connector **Given:** IEC 62196-3 Type 2 combo, liquid-cooled variant. **Find:** Maximum DC power deliverable. **Step 1 ‚Äî Identify voltage class.** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{DC, max} &= 1000 \text{ V} \quad \text{(per IEC 62196-3 Table 5)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 ‚Äî Identify maximum continuous current.** $$ \begin{aligned} I_{DC, max} &= 500 \text{ A} \quad \text{(liquid-cooled variant, IEC 62196-3)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 ‚Äî Apply the DC power equation.** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{DC, max} &= V_{DC, max} \times I_{DC, max} \\ &= 1000 \text{ V} \times 500 \text{ A} \\ &= 500{,}000 \text{ W} \\ &= \mathbf{500 \text{ kW}} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** DC has no reactive component, so the simple $P = V \times I$ (no $\cos\phi$) is used. The 500 kW is the *theoretical* peak; most modern CCS2 stations operate at **150‚Äì350 kW**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Charging Time for a 60 kWh Battery at 150 kW DC **Given:** Battery capacity $E = 60 \text{ kWh}$, EVSE power $P_{EVSE} = 150 \text{ kW}$, charging efficiency $\eta = 0.92$, $\Delta SOC = 0.20$ to $0.80 = 0.60$. **Step 1 ‚Äî Compute effective power delivered to battery.** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{batt} &= P_{EVSE} \times \eta \\ &= 150 \text{ kW} \times 0.92 \\ &= 138 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 ‚Äî Compute energy required for a 0 ‚Üí 60% SOC change.** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{req} &= E \times \Delta SOC \\ &= 60 \text{ kWh} \times 0.60 \\ &= 36 \text{ kWh} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 ‚Äî Compute charging time.** $$ \begin{aligned} t &= \frac{E_{req}}{P_{batt}} \\ &= \frac{36 \text{ kWh}}{138 \text{ kW}} \\ &= 0.2608 \text{ hours} \\ &\approx \mathbf{15.65 \text{ minutes}} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** Notice that charging time *does not* linearly scale with EVSE power because of (a) tapering current at high SOC and (b) thermal limits on the battery. KTU problems often ignore the taper and use the linear formula above. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: 3-Phase AC Power Limit for Type 2 **Given:** $V_L = 400 \text{ V}$ (line-to-line), $I_L = 63 \text{ A}$, $\cos\phi = 1.0$ (resistive load assumption for Level 2 EV charger). **Step 1 ‚Äî Apply the balanced 3-phase power formula.** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{3\phi} &= \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_L \times \cos\phi \\ &= 1.732 \times 400 \text{ V} \times 63 \text{ A} \times 1.0 \\ &= 1.732 \times 25{,}200 \\ &= 43{,}646.4 \text{ W} \\ &\approx \mathbf{43.65 \text{ kW}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 ‚Äî Validate against Type 2 connector rating.** The Type 2 inlet is rated **63 A per phase, 480 V** (max), giving a theoretical ceiling of: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{3\phi, max} &= \sqrt{3} \times 480 \times 63 \times 1.0 \\ &= 1.732 \times 30{,}240 \\ &= 52{,}375.7 \text{ W} \\ &\approx \mathbf{52.4 \text{ kW}} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** This matches the **IEC 62196-2 Section 6.4** specification ‚Äî Type 2 AC-only max is ~52 kW, with the **43.65 kW** being the practical 400 V grid limit in Europe and India. --- ## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Derivative Proof of Variant Choice) | Property | CCS Type 1 | CCS Type 2 | Mathematical/Logic Basis | |---|---|---|---| | Phase Count (AC) | 1-$\phi$ only | 3-$\phi$ native (also 1-$\phi$) | Pin count: 5 AC vs 7 AC | | AC Peak Power | 19.2 kW | 43.65 kW | $240 \times 80$ vs $\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 63$ | | DC Peak Power | 100 kW (uncooled), 200 kW (cooled) | 350 kW (uncooled), 500 kW (cooled) | Standard headroom from US vs EU grids | | DC Voltage Class | 200‚Äì500 V (typ.), 1000 V max | 200‚Äì920 V (typ.), 1000 V max | EV battery pack architectures | | Communication (DC) | SAE J2847/2 + ISO 15118 | ISO 15118 mandatory | EU regulation favors open ISO standard | | Pin Locking | Latch + motorized (Tesla prior) | Latch + motorized (standard) | IEC 62196-3 ¬ß6.6 | | IP Rating (mated) | IP54 | IP44 (improved to IP54 in v2) | IEC 60529 | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a CCS Charging System The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional flow** of a CCS DC fast charging session ‚Äî the energy path, the control path, and the handshake logic. It represents the *engineering abstraction* of the physical connector and cables. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Grid Utility Supply 11 kV or 415 V] --> B[AC/DC Rectifier Cabinet 50 kW to 350 kW] B --> C[CCS Connector Type 1 or Type 2] C --> D[Vehicle Inlet Combo Port] D --> E[Onboard BMS Battery Management System] E --> F[High Voltage Battery Pack 400 V to 800 V] G[EVSE Controller] -->|PWM 1 kHz Pilot Signal| C C -->|Proximity PP Current Code| G C -->|PLC over CP ISO 15118| G G -->|OCPP 1.6 or 2.0.1| H[Charging Network Backend Cloud] H -->|Authorization and Billing| G C -->|Locking Actuator Engage| D D -->|Contactor Close Permit| F A -. Power path .-> F G -. Control path .-> F ``` > **Interpretation:** The diagram distinguishes two signal channels: > - **Power path** (solid arrows) ‚Äî energy flow from grid to battery. > - **Control path** (dashed arrows) ‚Äî communication and authorization. ## 4.2 CCS Connector Pin Identification Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ConnectorA[CCS Type 1 Combo 1] A1[Pin 1 L1 Live] A2[Pin 2 N Neutral] A3[Pin 3 PE Ground] A4[Pin 4 CP Control Pilot] A5[Pin 5 PP Proximity Pilot] A6[Pin 6 DC Plus] A7[Pin 7 DC Minus] end subgraph ConnectorB[CCS Type 2 Combo 2] B1[Pin 1 L1 Phase 1] B2[Pin 2 L2 Phase 2] B3[Pin 3 L3 Phase 3] B4[Pin 4 N Neutral] B5[Pin 5 PE Ground] B6[Pin 6 CP Control Pilot] B7[Pin 7 PP Proximity Pilot] B8[Pin 8 DC Plus] B9[Pin 9 DC Minus] end ``` ## 4.3 AC vs DC Charging Mode Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[User Plugs In Connector] --> PilotCheck{CP Pilot
State C or D?} PilotCheck -->|No| Stop1[Standby No Charging] PilotCheck -->|Yes| PPRead{PP Resistor
Encoding} PPRead -->|13 A Cable| AC1[AC Slow Level 1] PPRead -->|32 A or 80 A| AC2[AC Fast Level 2] PPRead -->|DC Cable Detected| PLCStart[Initiate PLC Handshake
ISO 15118] PLCStart --> AuthCheck{Authorization
RFID or PnC} AuthCheck -->|Fail| Stop2[Reject Unauthorized] AuthCheck -->|Pass| LockAct[Locking Actuator Engages] LockAct --> BattPermit{BMS Allows
Contactor Close} BattPermit -->|Yes| DCRamp[DC Current Ramp Up
50 A per second] BattPermit -->|No| Stop3[Wait for Battery Ready] DCRamp --> Charge[Charging in Progress] Charge --> SOC{Target SOC
Reached} SOC -->|No| Charge SOC -->|Yes| RampDown[Current Ramp Down
Contactor Open] RampDown --> Unlock[Unlock Connector] Unlock --> End[End of Session] ``` ## 4.4 Physical Form-Factor Sketch (Textual) **CCS Type 1 (Combo 1) ‚Äî Round Inlet:** - Circular outer body, **5-pin top section** (AC cluster) arranged in a "Mickey Mouse ears" pattern (1 small ear = PP, large ear = CP) above a **2-pin lower oval** (DC+ and DC‚àí). - Diameter of inlet: **~70 mm**. **CCS Type 2 (Combo 2) ‚Äî Flat-top Inlet:** - Trapezoidal/oval body with a **flat upper edge** and curved lower edge, **7-pin top section** (AC + comms) and **2-pin lower oval** (DC+ and DC‚àí). - Inlet width: **~70 mm**, height **~85 mm**. > [!NOTE] > A useful mnemonic: **"Type 1 is round (North America = round pegs in round holes), Type 2 is flat-top (Europe = efficiency and symmetry)."** The flat-top design of Type 2 saves vertical space on the vehicle's rear quarter panel. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define the Combined Charging System (CCS). List the two main variants and the international standard governing each. `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** **Combined Charging System (CCS)** is an internationally standardized EV charging interface that integrates AC slow/medium charging and DC fast charging into a **single vehicle inlet** using a "combo" connector design. The two DC power pins are physically combined with the AC pin cluster so that the same inlet accepts both charging modes. The two main variants are: 1. **CCS Type 1 (Combo 1):** Governed by **SAE J1772 (AC)** and **SAE J1772 Combo (DC)**, with a 5-pin AC cluster (L1, N, PE, CP, PP) + 2 DC pins. Adopted primarily in **North America, South Korea, and Japan**. 2. **CCS Type 2 (Combo 2):** Governed by **IEC 62196-2 (AC)** and **IEC 62196-3 (DC)**, with a 7-pin AC cluster (L1, L2, L3, N, PE, CP, PP) + 2 DC pins. Adopted primarily in **Europe, India, UK, Australia, and the Middle East**. --- ### Question 2: What is the function of the **Proximity Pilot (PP)** and **Control Pilot (CP)** pins in a CCS connector? `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]` **Model Answer:** **Proximity Pilot (PP)** ‚Äî This pin uses a **resistor network** between PP and PE to signal the maximum current-carrying capacity of the cable assembly to the EV. Common resistor values are **150 Œ© (13 A)**, **480 Œ© (32 A)**, and **680 Œ© (63 A)**. It also detects whether the plug is **physically inserted** before any current flows, acting as a safety interlock. **Control Pilot (CP)** ‚Äî This pin carries a **1 kHz, ¬±12 V PWM signal** generated by the EVSE. The **duty cycle (5%‚Äì95%)** tells the EV the maximum available current, while the **DC voltage level (12/9/6/3 V)** indicates the **charging state** (Standby, Connected, Charging, or Ventilation Required). For DC mode, CP switches to **PLC communication (ISO 15118)** to exchange battery parameters and authorization data. --- ## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 ESE Module 3 Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following. --- ### üîπ Question A (14 Marks): Explain the CCS Type 1 (SAE J1772 Combo 1) connector in detail, with reference to its pin configuration, power ratings, communication protocol, and locking mechanism. `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]` #### Part (a) ‚Äî Pin Configuration and Power Ratings (7 Marks) **Pin Configuration (7 pins total):** | Pin | Function | Description | |---|---|---| | L1 | AC Live | Carries single-phase AC line current (max 80 A) | | N | AC Neutral | Returns AC current to the EVSE | | PE | Protective Earth | Chassis safety ground | | CP | Control Pilot | PWM signaling + PLC for DC | | PP | Proximity Pilot | Plug presence + cable current rating | | DC+ | DC Positive | High-current positive (200 A uncooled) | | DC‚àí | DC Negative | High-current return (200 A uncooled) | **Power Ratings:** - **AC Level 1:** 120 V √ó 16 A = **1.92 kW** (typical home outlet) - **AC Level 2:** 240 V √ó 80 A = **19.2 kW** (workplace / public) - **DC Fast Charging (uncooled cable):** 500 V √ó 200 A = **100 kW** - **DC Fast Charging (liquid-cooled cable):** 1000 V √ó 500 A = **500 kW** theoretical max (200 kW practical in most US stations) #### Part (b) ‚Äî Communication Protocol and Locking Mechanism (7 Marks) **Communication Protocol (AC mode):** - **SAE J1772** ¬ß5.4 specifies the 1 kHz CP PWM pilot. - Duty cycle 5%‚Äì10% indicates EVSE error/digital comms available; 10%‚Äì85% linearly maps to 6 A‚Äì80 A. - Diode detection: EV places a diode in the CP loop (or in DC mode, the EVSE uses a different detection). **Communication Protocol (DC mode):** - After CP-based hand-shake, the EVSE and EV switch to **PLC (HomePlug GreenPHY)** on the **CP/PE pair** at ~2‚Äì10 MHz. - Higher layer stack is **ISO 15118** (TCP/IP, SLAC, V2G messages). - Used for **Plug & Charge (PnC)**, **V2G (bidirectional)**, and **dynamic load management**. **Locking Mechanism:** - Per **SAE J1772 Combo ¬ß6.2**, the **locking actuator** is on the **vehicle inlet**, not the connector. - A spring-loaded latch engages a **locking pin hole** in the connector body. - For DC charging, the EV **shall not energize the DC contactor** until the lock is confirmed. - For AC charging, locking is mandatory at currents > 30 A per **NEC 625**. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often write "the connector locks the cable." **Wrong.** The **vehicle's inlet** contains the motorized locking actuator; the connector has a **locking hole/pin receiver**. This is a frequent 2-mark loss in KTU evaluations. --- ### üîπ Question B (14 Marks): Compare CCS Type 1 and CCS Type 2 with reference to their regional adoption, pin architecture, electrical specifications, communication standards, and application in the Indian EV ecosystem. `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]` #### Part (a) ‚Äî Regional Adoption and Pin Architecture (7 Marks) | Aspect | CCS Type 1 | CCS Type 2 | |---|---|---| | **Origin Standard** | SAE J1772 (USA) | IEC 62196-2 / 62196-3 (EU) | | **Regional Adoption** | USA, Canada, Mexico, South Korea, Japan, Taiwan | Europe, UK, India, Australia, NZ, Middle East, South America | | **Pin Count (AC)** | 5 pins (L1, N, PE, CP, PP) | 7 pins (L1, L2, L3, N, PE, CP, PP) | | **Phase Support** | Single-phase only | Three-phase (and single-phase fallback) | | **Form Factor** | Round inlet, ~70 mm diameter | Flat-top oval, ~70 √ó 85 mm | | **DC Pin Position** | Below AC cluster (combo) | Below AC cluster (combo) | **Why India uses CCS2:** - The Government of India (MoRTH) issued a **notification in 2017** and reaffirmed in the **PM E-Drive scheme (2024)** mandating **CCS2** for all 4W EVs ‚â• 4W category sold in India. - The Indian grid is **415 V three-phase** industrial-standard, making Type 2's three-phase pins natively usable. - The **Bharat DC-001** standard (15 kW, GB/T-style) is a parallel legacy standard for low-power 4W charging; **Bharat AC-001** uses a **Type 2 variant** (with L1, N, PE, CP, PP) for ‚â§ 7 kW. #### Part (b) ‚Äî Electrical Specs, Communication, and Indian Application (7 Marks) | Aspect | CCS Type 1 | CCS Type 2 | |---|---|---| | **AC Max Power (theoretical)** | 19.2 kW (1-œÜ, 240V/80A) | 43.65 kW (3-œÜ, 400V/63A) | | **DC Max Power (practical)** | 100‚Äì200 kW (350 kW emerging) | 150‚Äì350 kW (500 kW emerging) | | **DC Voltage Range** | 200‚Äì500 V (1000 V max) | 200‚Äì920 V (1000 V max) | | **DC Communication** | SAE J2847/2 + ISO 15118 | ISO 15118 (mandatory) | | **Liquid Cooling** | Optional | Common in EU/India 350 kW stations | | **Cable Cooling (Type)** | Liquid or air | Liquid (predominant above 150 kW) | | **IP Rating (mated)** | IP54 | IP54 (improved from IP44) | | **Smart Charging Features** | Limited (US grid) | V2G, PnC, OCPP 2.0.1 (mandated) | **Indian EV Ecosystem Application:** - **Tata Motors (Nexon EV, Tiago EV, Punch EV):** Uses **CCS2** inlet (after 2022 model year); supports up to 50 kW DC fast charging. - **MG ZS EV, Hyundai Kona, BYD Atto 3, Kia EV6:** All CCS2; up to 233 kW peak (Kia EV6 with 800 V architecture). - **Charging Infrastructure:** Tata Power, Statiq, Ather Grid, BPCL ‚Äî installing 60 kW, 120 kW, and 240 kW **CCS2 DC fast chargers** along NH-48, Mumbai-Pune Expressway, Bangalore-Chennai corridor. - **Bharat Public Charge Specs (2024 update):** DC chargers from 30 kW to 240 kW must support **CCS2 + CHAdeMO** dual connector OR pure CCS2. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **DO NOT confuse CHAdeMO with CCS.** CHAdeMO is a Japanese standard (JEVS G105) using a *different* connector with its own CAN bus protocol. **CCS uses PLC (not CAN) on the CP/PE pair.** > 2. **DC current ratings differ between the uncooled and cooled variants.** Students often write "CCS2 = 200 A max" ‚Äî this is wrong; the cooled variant is **500 A**, and 350 kW stations achieve 500 A via liquid-cooled cables. > 3. **CCS1 supports 3-phase AC only as a fallback** ‚Äî it is *designed* for single-phase. Do not write "CCS1 is 3-phase." > 4. **For Indian exams specifically,** always cite **Bharat DC-001 / AC-001** alongside CCS2 to show awareness of national regulation. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield 60-Second Revision Checklist:** - [x] **CCS = Combined Charging System**, governed by **SAE J1772 (Type 1)** and **IEC 62196-3 (Type 2)**. - [x] **CCS Type 1:** 5 AC pins + 2 DC pins ‚Üí single-phase ‚Üí **North America, Japan, South Korea**. - [x] **CCS Type 2:** 7 AC pins + 2 DC pins ‚Üí three-phase capable ‚Üí **Europe, India, Australia**. - [x] **Max DC voltage:** 1000 V for both; **Max DC current:** 200 A (uncooled Type 1) / 500 A (cooled Type 2). - [x] **AC peak:** Type 1 = 19.2 kW; Type 2 = 43.65 kW. - [x] **CP pin** = 1 kHz PWM for AC; switches to **PLC + ISO 15118** for DC. - [x] **PP pin** = resistor-encoded current rating (150/480/680 Œ©). - [x] **Locking actuator** lives on the **vehicle inlet**, not the connector. - [x] **India** adopted **CCS2** for all 4W EVs; **Bharat DC-001 (15 kW)** is a parallel low-power standard. - [x] **Key formulas:** $P_{DC} = V \times I$; $P_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$; $t = E \cdot \Delta SOC / (P \cdot \eta)$. - [x] **Differentiation traps:** CCS ‚âÝ CHAdeMO; CCS1 ‚âÝ 3-phase capable; Type 1 ‚âÝ Type 2 in either form or function. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Quick Tip for KTU 2024 ESE:** When a 14-mark question asks about CCS, structure the answer as **(i) Pin architecture with table ‚Üí (ii) Power ratings with formula ‚Üí (iii) Communication protocol with stack diagram ‚Üí (iv) Locking + safety + Indian/global context**. A labeled table is worth **2‚Äì3 extra marks** versus prose-only answers.

topics.title: GB/T: Pin Diagrams and Differences
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** GB/T: Pin Diagrams and Differences

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is GB/T?

**GB/T** stands for **Guobiao Standard (ÂõΩÊÝá)** ‚Äî the mandatory and recommended national standards of the **People's Republic of China**. In the context of Electric Vehicles (EVs), the most relevant standards for charging infrastructure are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GB/T 20234 Series** governs the conductive charging couplers (plugs and sockets) used to connect Electric Vehicles to the AC and DC charging supply. It is the **Chinese counterpart** to the IEC 62196 (Type 1, Type 2) and SAE J1772 standards used internationally.

The relevant sub-standards are:

| Standard | Scope |
|---|---|
| **GB/T 20234.1** | General requirements (safety, marking, tests) |
| **GB/T 20234.2** | **AC charging coupler** (single-phase & three-phase) |
| **GB/T 20234.3** | **DC charging coupler** (high-power fast charging) |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ‚Äî The "Universal Power Plug" of Chinese EVs**
> Think of GB/T as the "**charging language**" that every EV in China must "speak" to a charging station. Just like Indian homes use a 3-pin 5A plug for small appliances and a 3-pin 15A plug for air-conditioners (because the appliance has different power needs), GB/T defines two distinct "plugs" ‚Äî a **small AC plug for slow home charging** and a **large DC plug for fast highway charging**. The pins inside each plug are like the "wires" of a telephone cord, each carrying a specific piece of information or electricity: power, ground, control signals, or communication. If a single pin is missing or wired wrong, the car and charger cannot safely "talk" ‚Äî so the standard strictly defines what each pin does.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **AC charging voltage**: **Single-phase 250 V AC**, **Three-phase 440 V AC**
- **AC rated current**: **16 A** (slow) and **32 A** (medium)
- **DC charging voltage**: up to **750 V DC** (latest revisions extend to **1000 V DC**)
- **DC rated current**: **80 A, 125 A, 200 A, 250 A** depending on variant
- **Maximum DC power**: **up to 250 kW** (newer 2015+ revisions)
- **Control pilot signal voltage**: **¬±12 V DC** PWM communication
- **Communication protocol for DC**: **CAN 2.0B** at **250 kbps**

---

### 1.4 Real-World Use Case

Every EV sold or operated in mainland China ‚Äî including BYD, NIO, Xpeng, Geely, and even foreign brands like Tesla (which uses a GB/T adapter in China) ‚Äî must comply with GB/T 20234. This makes it the **largest single charging-standard ecosystem in the world** by vehicle count, surpassing CCS2 and CHAdeMO combined.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 GB/T 20234.2 ‚Äî AC Charging Coupler (Pin-by-Pin)

The **AC coupler** is a **7-pin** configuration used for both **single-phase and three-phase** AC charging. The connector is round with a flat keying notch to ensure correct orientation.

| Pin # | Symbol | Function | Wire Color (China) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **L1** | AC Phase 1 (Line) | Brown |
| **2** | **L2** | AC Phase 2 (for three-phase) | Black |
| **3** | **L3** | AC Phase 3 (for three-phase) | Grey |
| **4** | **N** | Neutral | Blue |
| **5** | **PE** | Protective Earth | Yellow-Green |
| **6** | **CC** | Connection Confirmation (plug detection switch) | ‚Äî |
| **7** | **CP** | Control Pilot (PWM signalling for current limit) | ‚Äî |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does AC only need 7 pins?** Because in AC charging, the **on-board charger (OBC)** inside the EV converts AC ‚Üí DC. The connector only needs to deliver raw AC power + a control pilot wire to negotiate current. Heavy DC power conversion happens **inside the vehicle**, not at the charger.

---

### 2.2 GB/T 20234.3 ‚Äî DC Charging Coupler (Pin-by-Pin)

The **DC coupler** is a **9-pin** configuration used for high-power DC fast charging. Here, the **off-board charger (the EVSE itself)** converts AC ‚Üí DC and feeds DC directly to the battery, bypassing the on-board charger.

| Pin # | Symbol | Function | Wire Specification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **DC+** | DC Power Positive | High-current (125‚Äì250 A capable) |
| **2** | **DC‚àí** | DC Power Negative | High-current return |
| **3** | **PE** | Protective Earth | Earth ground bonding |
| **4** | **S+** | CAN Communication High | CAN-H (shielded twisted pair) |
| **5** | **S‚àí** | CAN Communication Low | CAN-L (shielded twisted pair) |
| **6** | **CC1** | Connection Confirmation 1 | Detection switch on EV side |
| **7** | **CC2** | Connection Confirmation 2 | Detection switch on charger side |
| **8** | **A+** | Auxiliary Power Positive (low-voltage 12 V/24 V) | Supplies EV BMS during handshake |
| **9** | **A‚àí** | Auxiliary Power Negative | Return for A+ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 9 pins for DC?** Because DC fast charging requires:
> 1. Two **large DC power pins** (DC+, DC‚àí)
> 2. A **dedicated CAN bus** (S+, S‚àí) for the EV's BMS to communicate with the charger ‚Äî a feature unique to GB/T and CHAdeMO (CCS uses PLC over the CP pin instead)
> 3. **Auxiliary low-voltage power** (A+, A‚àí) to wake up the car's electronics before main DC power flows
> 4. **Two confirmation pins** (CC1, CC2) ‚Äî one on each side ‚Äî for **redundant safety latching**

---

### 2.3 Key Differences vs Other Global Charging Standards

> [!WARNING]
> This is a **highly recurring KTU exam question** ‚Äî typically asked as a **"Compare GB/T with Type 2 / CCS / CHAdeMO"** 14-mark question. Memorize this comparison matrix.

| Parameter | **GB/T (China)** | **Type 1 / J1772 (USA/Japan)** | **Type 2 / Mennekes (EU)** | **CCS2 Combo 2 (EU DC)** | **CHAdeMO (Japan)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AC Connector Standard** | GB/T 20234.2 | SAE J1772 / IEC 62196-2 Type 1 | IEC 62196-2 Type 2 | (Uses Type 2 for AC + 2 DC pins) | (No AC ‚Äî DC only) |
| **AC Pins** | 7 | 5 | 7 | 7 | ‚Äî |
| **DC Pins** | 9 | ‚Äî | ‚Äî | 9 (incl. 2 DC) | 10 |
| **DC Communication** | **CAN Bus** (S+/S‚àí) | ‚Äî | ‚Äî | **PLC (Power Line Communication)** over CP | **CAN Bus** |
| **Auxiliary Power (12V/24V)** | Yes (A+, A‚àí pins) | No | No | No | No |
| **Max AC Current** | 32 A (3-phase) | 32 A (single-phase) | 63 A (3-phase) | 63 A | ‚Äî |
| **Max DC Power** | **250 kW** (latest) | ‚Äî | ‚Äî | **350 kW** | **400 kW** (CHAdeMO 2.0) |
| **Locking Mechanism** | **Electromagnetic latch** (CC pin driven) | Mechanical latch | Mechanical + motorized | Motorized | Motorized |
| **Control Pilot** | CP (PWM, ¬±12V) | CP (PWM, ¬±12V) | CP (PWM, ¬±12V) | CP (PWM + PLC) | CP + dedicated CAN |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Charging Power Calculations)

For any EV charging system (GB/T included), the power equations are:

$$
P_{AC,\text{1-phase}} = V_{\text{phase}} \times I \times \cos\phi
$$

$$
P_{AC,\text{3-phase}} = \sqrt{3} \times V_{\text{line}} \times I \times \cos\phi
$$

$$
P_{DC} = V_{\text{DC}} \times I_{\text{DC}}
$$

$$
E_{\text{battery}} = \int_{0}^{t} V_{\text{batt}}(t) \cdot I(t) \, dt \approx \overline{V_{\text{batt}}} \cdot Q_{\text{transferred}}
$$

$$
\text{Charging Time} \, T_c = \frac{C_{\text{battery}} \times \text{SoC}_{\text{needed}}}{I_{\text{charging}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For GB/T 20234.3 at **250 kW** DC, with a typical EV battery of **75 kWh**, charging from **20 % ‚Üí 80 % SoC** requires only **18 minutes** ‚Äî illustrating why DC fast charging is critical for highway corridors.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

GB/T's reliance on **CAN bus for DC communication** (instead of PLC like CCS) makes it:
1. **Easier to diagnose** with a standard automotive OBD-II CAN scanner
2. **More deterministic** ‚Äî no PLC signal-quality issues on long cables
3. **Simpler to integrate** with existing EV architectures (CAN is already on-board)
4. But **more expensive** to manufacture (extra shielded twisted pair required)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivations (Worked Numerical Examples)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 1 ‚Äî Three-Phase AC Charging (GB/T 20234.2)**

**Given:** A BYD Han EV is charging at a GB/T three-phase AC station.
- Line-to-line voltage $V_L = 380 \text{ V}$ (Chinese three-phase standard)
- Rated current $I = 32 \text{ A}$
- Power factor $\cos\phi = 0.95$
- Charging duration $t = 4 \text{ hours}$

**Find:** (a) Active power delivered, (b) Energy stored (assuming 92 % charger efficiency).

**Step 1 ‚Äî Recall the 3-phase AC power formula:**
$$
P_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I \times \cos\phi
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Substitute known values:**
$$
P_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} \times 380 \times 32 \times 0.95
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Evaluate intermediate product:**
$$
\sqrt{3} \approx 1.732
$$
$$
380 \times 32 = 12160
$$
$$
12160 \times 1.732 = 21061.12
$$
$$
21061.12 \times 0.95 = 20008.06 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Final active power:**
$$
\boxed{P_{3\phi} \approx 20.0 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Energy delivered to battery (after charger efficiency $\eta = 0.92$):**
$$
E_{\text{out}} = P_{3\phi} \times \eta \times t
$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Substitute:**
$$
E_{\text{out}} = 20008.06 \times 0.92 \times 4
$$

**Step 7 ‚Äî Compute:**
$$
20008.06 \times 0.92 = 18407.42 \text{ W}
$$
$$
18407.42 \times 4 = 73629.66 \text{ Wh}
$$

**Step 8 ‚Äî Convert to kWh:**
$$
\boxed{E_{\text{out}} \approx 73.6 \text{ kWh}}
$$

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 2 ‚Äî DC Fast Charging (GB/T 20234.3)**

**Given:** A NIO ET7 charging at a GB/T DC fast charger.
- DC voltage $V_{\text{DC}} = 500 \text{ V}$
- DC current $I_{\text{DC}} = 250 \text{ A}$
- Initial SoC = 20 %, Target SoC = 80 %
- Battery capacity = 100 kWh

**Find:** (a) DC charging power, (b) Approximate charging time to reach 80 % SoC.

**Step 1 ‚Äî DC Power formula:**
$$
P_{\text{DC}} = V_{\text{DC}} \times I_{\text{DC}}
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Substitute:**
$$
P_{\text{DC}} = 500 \times 250 = 125000 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Convert to kW:**
$$
\boxed{P_{\text{DC}} = 125 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Energy required for 60 % SoC jump:**
$$
E_{\text{req}} = C_{\text{batt}} \times (\text{SoC}_f - \text{SoC}_i) = 100 \times (0.80 - 0.20)
$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Compute:**
$$
\boxed{E_{\text{req}} = 60 \text{ kWh}}
$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Charging time (ideal case, no tapering):**
$$
t_c = \frac{E_{\text{req}}}{P_{\text{DC}}}
$$

**Step 7 ‚Äî Convert units:**
$$
t_c = \frac{60 \times 1000}{125000} = \frac{60000}{125000}
$$

**Step 8 ‚Äî Final:**
$$
\boxed{t_c = 0.48 \text{ hours} = 28.8 \text{ minutes}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, GB/T fast chargers **taper** the current after ~70 % SoC to protect battery cells, so real-world time is **35‚Äì40 minutes**.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Exam-Ready)

| Feature | GB/T 20234.3 (DC) | CCS Combo 2 (DC) | CHAdeMO (DC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Comm. Method** | CAN (250 kbps) | PLC (HomePlug Green PHY) | CAN (500 kbps) |
| **Max Voltage** | 1000 V (newer) | 1000 V | 1000 V (CHAdeMO 2.0) |
| **Max Current** | 250 A | 500 A | 400 A |
| **Handshake** | A+/A‚àí wakes EV BMS | PLC over CP pin | CP + CAN |
| **Vehicle Compatibility** | China only | EU + USA + Korea | Japan + global |
| **Locking** | Electromagnetic (CC pin-controlled) | Motorized pin | Motorized latch |
| **Cooling** | Liquid-cooled (optional) | Liquid-cooled standard | Liquid-cooled optional |
| **Adoption 2024** | **70 % of global EVs** (by volume) | 25 % | 5 % |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid cannot perfectly render physical circular pin layouts, so the following block diagrams describe the **functional architecture** and **pin-to-signal mapping** of GB/T connectors.

### 4.1 GB/T 20234.2 ‚Äî AC Charging Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GRID[AC Grid Supply] --> EVSE[EVSE Wallbox / AC Charger]
    EVSE --> L1[Pin 1 L1 Phase A]
    EVSE --> L2[Pin 2 L2 Phase B]
    EVSE --> L3[Pin 3 L3 Phase C]
    EVSE --> N[Pin 4 Neutral]
    EVSE --> PE[Pin 5 Protective Earth]
    EVSE --> CC[Pin 6 Connection Confirmation]
    EVSE --> CP[Pin 7 Control Pilot PWM]
    
    L1 --> OBC[On-Board Charger in EV]
    L2 --> OBC
    L3 --> OBC
    N --> OBC
    PE --> OBC
    CC --> EVCTRL[EV Charge Controller]
    CP --> EVCTRL
    OBC --> BATT[HV Battery Pack]
```

### 4.2 GB/T 20234.3 ‚Äî DC Charging Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GRID3[AC Grid] --> RECT[Off-Board Rectifier Charger]
    RECT -->|DC+| DCP[Pin 1 DC Positive]
    RECT -->|DC-| DCN[Pin 2 DC Negative]
    RECT --> PE[Pin 3 Protective Earth]
    
    RECT --> CAN[CAN Controller]
    CAN -->|CAN-H| SP[Pin 4 S Plus]
    CAN -->|CAN-L| SN[Pin 5 S Minus]
    
    RECT --> AUXLV[Low Voltage Aux Supply 12V]
    AUXLV --> AP[Pin 8 A Plus]
    AUXLV --> AN[Pin 9 A Minus]
    
    RECT --> CC1CTRL[CC1 Switch Driver]
    CC1CTRL --> CC1[Pin 6 CC1]
    
    RECT --> CC2CTRL[CC2 Switch Driver]
    CC2CTRL --> CC2[Pin 7 CC2]
    
    DCP --> BMS[Vehicle BMS]
    DCN --> BMS
    SP --> BMS
    SN --> BMS
    AP --> BMS
    AN --> BMS
    CC1 --> BMS
    CC2 --> BMS
    PE --> BMS
    BMS --> PACK[HV Battery Pack]
```

### 4.3 GB/T Connector Pin Layout (Top-Down View) ‚Äî Schematic ASCII

**GB/T 20234.2 (AC) ‚Äî 7-pin Circular Layout:**

```
        CC (6)
              CP (7)
   L3 (3)              L1 (1)
        N (4)
              PE (5)
   L2 (2)              
   (key notch at bottom)
```

**GB/T 20234.3 (DC) ‚Äî 9-pin Circular Layout:**

```
           CC1 (6)
                       A+ (8)
   S+ (4)                       DC+ (1)
           CC2 (7)        A- (9)
   S- (5)                       DC- (2)
                       PE (3)
           (key notch at bottom)
```

### 4.4 GB/T vs CCS Communication Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GB_T[GB/T 20234.3 Communication]
        GB1[EV BMS] -->|CAN 250 kbps on S+ S-| GB2[Charger]
        GB1 -->|Wake via A+ A-| GB2
    end
    
    subgraph CCS[CCS Combo 2 Communication]
        C1[EV BMS] -->|PLC over CP pin| C2[Charger]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the 9 pins of GB/T 20234.3 DC charging connector and state the function of any three.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The 9 pins of GB/T 20234.3 DC connector are:
> 1. **DC+** ‚Äî DC power positive (high current)
> 2. **DC‚àí** ‚Äî DC power negative (high current)
> 3. **PE** ‚Äî Protective Earth
> 4. **S+** ‚Äî CAN-H communication
> 5. **S‚àí** ‚Äî CAN-L communication
> 6. **CC1** ‚Äî Connection Confirmation (vehicle side)
> 7. **CC2** ‚Äî Connection Confirmation (charger side)
> 8. **A+** ‚Äî Auxiliary +12 V/+24 V supply
> 9. **A‚àí** ‚Äî Auxiliary return
> *(Any 3 functions explained = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of the CP pin in GB/T AC charging?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **CP (Control Pilot)** pin in GB/T 20234.2 is used to:
> 1. Communicate the **maximum allowable charging current** from the EVSE to the vehicle using a **¬±12 V PWM signal** with variable duty cycle (e.g., 10 % ‚Üí 6 A, 25 % ‚Üí 32 A).
> 2. Verify that the **EV is properly connected** and the **cable is locked** before enabling power flow.
> 3. Provide a **pilot tone** so the EV's on-board controller can detect EVSE presence and absence of ground faults.

---

### 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU ESE convention, Module 3 long questions carry internal choice. **Solve EITHER Question A OR Question B.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **pin configuration of GB/T 20234.2 AC charging connector**. List all 7 pins with their functions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **GB/T 20234.3 DC charging connector** pin layout and state **any 4 differences** between GB/T and CCS Combo 2. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) GB/T 20234.2 AC Connector (7 marks):**
The GB/T 20234.2 AC connector has **7 pins** arranged in a circular layout:

| Pin | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | L1 | Phase A conductor |
| 2 | L2 | Phase B conductor (3-phase) |
| 3 | L3 | Phase C conductor (3-phase) |
| 4 | N | Neutral return |
| 5 | PE | Protective Earth ‚Äî chassis bonding |
| 6 | CC | Connection Confirmation ‚Äî detects plug insertion |
| 7 | CP | Control Pilot ‚Äî PWM-based current limit signaling |

Refer to ASCII layout in Section 4.3 for the spatial arrangement. The connector features a **keying notch** to prevent incorrect insertion, and uses **electromagnetic latching** driven by the CC pin for safety.

---

**(b) GB/T 20234.3 + Differences from CCS (7 marks):**
GB/T 20234.3 has **9 pins** (DC+, DC‚àí, PE, S+, S‚àí, CC1, CC2, A+, A‚àí). Differences from CCS Combo 2:

1. **Communication**: GB/T uses **dedicated CAN bus (S+/S‚àí) at 250 kbps**, while CCS uses **PLC (HomePlug Green PHY)** over the CP pin.
2. **Auxiliary Power**: GB/T provides **A+/A‚àí (12V/24V)** to wake the EV's BMS before main DC power; CCS does **not** have this ‚Äî it relies on PLC always-on.
3. **Locking**: GB/T uses **electromagnetic latch** controlled by CC pin signal; CCS uses **motorized latch**.
4. **Pin Count**: GB/T DC is **9 pins only**; CCS Combo 2 has **9 pins (AC Type 2 base + 2 large DC pins below)** ‚Äî physical geometry is different.
5. **Cooling**: GB/T uses **liquid cooling optional**; CCS2 mandates **liquid-cooled cables** for high power (350 kW).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Draw the **pin diagram of GB/T 20234.3 DC charging coupler** and explain the role of **S+/S‚àí, A+/A‚àí, and CC1/CC2** pins in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **GB/T DC charging with CHAdeMO** in terms of communication, maximum power, locking mechanism, and adoption. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) GB/T 20234.3 DC Pin Diagram & Critical Pins (7 marks):**

Diagram as in Section 4.2 / 4.3.
