- **S+/S‚àí (CAN Bus pins)**: Implement **CAN 2.0B at 250 kbps**. The vehicle's BMS and the off-board charger exchange parameters ‚Äî battery voltage, max current, max voltage, SoC, temperature ‚Äî before and during charging. CAN-H = S+ (dominant ~3.5 V), CAN-L = S‚àí (recessive ~1.5 V).

- **A+/A‚àí (Auxiliary Power)**: Provide **12 V or 24 V low-voltage DC** (typically up to 5 A) from charger to EV. This powers the **BMS controller, contactor coils, and DC-DC converter** during the handshake phase, **before** main DC power is applied. Without A+, the BMS cannot "wake up" to authorize DC contactor closure.

- **CC1/CC2 (Connection Confirmation)**: **CC1** is a switch loop on the **EV side**; **CC2** is a switch loop on the **charger side**. Both must show **closed (low-impedance)** when the connector is properly latched. The charger refuses to enable HV output unless both CC1 and CC2 are confirmed ‚Äî providing **redundant safety**.

---

**(b) GB/T vs CHAdeMO Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | GB/T 20234.3 | CHAdeMO |
|---|---|---|
| **Communication** | CAN 250 kbps (S+/S‚àí) | CAN 500 kbps (dedicated CAN) |
| **Max Power** | **250 kW** (latest spec) | **400 kW** (CHAdeMO 2.0 / ChaoJi) |
| **Locking** | Electromagnetic (CC pin) | Motorized latch |
| **Aux Power** | Yes (A+/A‚àí, 12/24 V) | No (uses separate +12 V signal wire) |
| **Adoption** | **China mandatory** ‚Äî used by 70 % of global EVs | Japan, EU retrofit, declining share |
| **Standard Body** | SAC (China) | JEVS / CHAdeMO Association |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing GB/T 20234.2 (AC) with GB/T 20234.3 (DC)** ‚Äî students often mix up the pin counts (7 vs 9). Always specify which sub-standard you are discussing.
> 2. **Forgetting PE pin** ‚Äî it is pin 5 in AC and pin 3 in DC. Examiners deduct marks if PE is omitted.
> 3. **Saying GB/T uses PLC** ‚Äî this is WRONG. GB/T uses **CAN bus**, not PLC. PLC is CCS. Mixing these up loses 2 marks minimum.
> 4. **Ignoring the A+/A‚àí auxiliary pins** ‚Äî these are **unique to GB/T** and a favourite exam point.
> 5. **Not mentioning the keying notch / electromagnetic latch** when describing the physical connector ‚Äî adds depth.
> 6. **Writing "GB/T" without expanding** ‚Äî always write "**GB/T 20234.2/20234.3**" for full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Revision Checklist (Pin Diagrams and Differences):**
> - ‚úÖ GB/T = Chinese national standard; 20234.2 = AC, 20234.3 = DC.
> - ‚úÖ AC connector has **7 pins**: L1, L2, L3, N, PE, CC, CP.
> - ‚úÖ DC connector has **9 pins**: DC+, DC‚àí, PE, S+, S‚àí, CC1, CC2, A+, A‚àí.
> - ‚úÖ **CP pin** uses ¬±12 V PWM to signal available current (similar to IEC 61851).
> - ‚úÖ **S+/S‚àí** = CAN bus for BMS ‚Üî Charger communication (250 kbps).
> - ‚úÖ **A+/A‚àí** = 12 V/24 V low-voltage auxiliary supply to wake EV BMS (unique to GB/T).
> - ‚úÖ **CC1/CC2** = Redundant plug-presence detection (vehicle-side + charger-side).
> - ‚úÖ GB/T uses **electromagnetic latch**, not motorized.
> - ‚úÖ GB/T uses **CAN**, **NOT** PLC ‚Äî CCS uses PLC.
> - ‚úÖ Max DC power: GB/T = **250 kW**, CCS2 = **350 kW**, CHAdeMO = **400 kW** (ChaoJi).
> - ‚úÖ Power formulas:
> - ‚úÖ $P_{1\phi} = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$,  $P_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$,  $P_{DC} = V \cdot I$.
> - ‚úÖ GB/T is the **most widely deployed** EV charging standard globally by vehicle count (since China has the largest EV fleet).
> - ‚úÖ Always cite **20234.2 or 20234.3** explicitly in answers ‚Äî do not write only "GB/T".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Energy Management Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Energy Management Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Management Strategy (EMS)** is defined as a supervisory control algorithm that optimally distributes the demanded propulsive power between two or more on-board energy sources and converters (e.g., Internal Combustion Engine, Battery, Ultracapacitor, Fuel Cell, Electric Motor-Generator) of a hybrid or electric vehicle, subject to operational, thermal, and State-of-Charge (SOC) constraints, with the objective of minimizing fuel consumption, exhaust emissions, and component wear, while simultaneously satisfying driver torque demand and maintaining drivability.

In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme vocabulary, the EMS sits at the **top layer of the vehicle control hierarchy** (above the lower-level torque/speed controllers) and acts as the *brain* that decides, at every instant, the operating point of each power source.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy (The Household Energy Manager)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ‚Äî The Smart Home Energy Manager**
> Imagine your house has three power sources: **solar panels on the roof**, a **diesel generator in the backyard**, and the **state electricity grid**. A smart controller (the Energy Manager) must decide, every second, how much electricity to draw from each.
> - If the **sun is bright** and **battery is full**, it prioritises solar.
> - If the **grid price is high at night**, it avoids the grid and uses stored battery energy.
> - If the **battery is about to die**, it runs the generator just enough to protect battery life.

**An HEV is exactly this scenario ‚Äî but on wheels.**
- The **driver's accelerator pedal** = the household electricity demand.
- The **petrol/diesel engine** = the diesel generator.
- The **battery + electric motor** = the grid + battery storage.
- The **ECU running the EMS** = the smart energy controller.

The *only* difference is that in a vehicle, the controller must respond in **milliseconds**, and "price" is replaced by *fuel used*, *emissions produced*, and *battery degradation*.

### 1.3 Why is an EMS Needed at All?

A conventional vehicle has one source (the engine) ‚Üí one decision is trivially satisfied. A hybrid has **at least two sources** ‚Üí an **infinite number** of combinations exist to meet a given wheel-power demand, but only some are optimal. The EMS selects that optimum.

Key standard metrics monitored by every EMS are highlighted below:

- **Fuel Economy (km/litre or mpg)** ‚Äî primary economic metric.
- **Tailpipe Emissions (g CO‚ÇÇ / km, g NOx / km)** ‚Äî regulatory metric.
- **State of Charge (SOC)** ‚Äî battery health metric, must stay within **20 %‚Äì80 %** typical band.
- **Component Life / Degradation** ‚Äî long-term cost metric.
- **Drivability / Driver Feel** ‚Äî qualitative metric (no jerks, no lag).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Problem ‚Äî A Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

1. **Step 1 ‚Äî Read Driver Demand.** The pedal position is translated into a demanded wheel power $\;P_{dem}(t)\;$.
2. **Step 2 ‚Äî Sense Vehicle State.** EMS reads instantaneous vehicle speed $v$, battery SOC, engine temperature, motor temperature, etc.
3. **Step 3 ‚Äî Apply Constraints.** Battery SOC must stay in $[\text{SOC}_{min}, \text{SOC}_{max}]$, engine must operate in its efficient region, motor torque within limits.
4. **Step 4 ‚Äî Solve Optimisation.** The chosen algorithm (rule table, fuzzy, ECMS, DP, Pontryagin, RL ‚Ä¶) computes the split between sources.
5. **Step 5 ‚Äî Issue Actuator Commands.** Commands are sent to engine throttle, motor inverter, and battery to deliver the split.
6. **Step 6 ‚Äî Update & Loop.** At the next sampling instant (typically **10 ms ‚Äì 100 ms**), the loop repeats.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- Step 1 exists because **driver demand is stochastic**; the EMS cannot predict the future perfectly (in real-time strategies).
- Step 2 provides the **state feedback** that makes closed-loop control possible.
- Step 3 prevents **catastrophic failure** (e.g., deep discharge of Li-ion damages cells irreversibly).
- Step 4 is the *core intelligence* ‚Äî this is where rule-based vs optimisation-based methods differ.
- Step 5 ensures the **actuators track** the demanded split; lower-level PI controllers handle the actual dynamics.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations constitute the **mathematical backbone** of every EMS variant. Memorise the symbols and the structure of each equation.

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Boundary Condition / Range | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Power Balance at Wheels | $P_{dem}(t) \;=\; P_{ICE}(t) \;+\; P_{EM}(t) \;-\; P_{loss}$ | $P_{ICE} \ge 0,\; P_{EM}$ can be ¬± | W (Watts) |
| 2 | Battery SOC Dynamics (Coulomb Counting) | $\dot{\text{SOC}}(t) \;=\; -\dfrac{I_{batt}(t)}{Q_{batt}}$ | SOC ‚àà [0, 1] | s‚Åª¬π |
| 3 | Battery Current from Power | $I_{batt} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{oc} - \sqrt{V_{oc}^{2} - 4 R_{int} P_{batt}}}{2 R_{int}}$ | $P_{batt} < \dfrac{V_{oc}^{2}}{4 R_{int}}$ | A (Amps) |
| 4 | Global Optimisation Cost | $J \;=\; \int_{t_{0}}^{t_{f}} \dot{m}_{fuel}(\tau)\, d\tau$ | minimise over full trip | kg |
| 5 | Equivalent Fuel Consumption (ECMS) | $\dot{m}_{eq} \;=\; \dot{m}_{ICE} \;+\; s(t)\,\dot{m}_{batt,equiv}$ | $s(t) \in [-1, 1]$, equivalence factor | kg/s |
| 6 | Battery-Equivalent Fuel Rate | $\dot{m}_{batt,equiv} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{batt}}{Q_{lhv}\,\eta_{disch/avg}}$ | $\eta_{disch} \approx 0.85,\; \eta_{chg} \approx 0.8$ | kg/s |
| 7 | Drivetrain Mechanical Loss | $P_{loss} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\,C_{d}\,A_{f}\,v^{3} \;+\; m g\,C_{rr}\,v \;+\; m a\,v$ | rolling + aero + inertia | W |
| 8 | SOC Sustainability (Charge-Sustaining) | $\text{SOC}(t_{f}) \;\approx\; \text{SOC}(t_{0})$ | $\Delta \text{SOC} \le \pm 0.05$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Engine Specific Fuel Consumption | $\text{bsfc} \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{P_{ICE}}$ | min at $\approx 2000\text{‚Äì}2500$ rpm, mid-load | g/kWh |
| 10 | Torque Coupling Constraint | $T_{ICE}\,\eta_{gear} \pm T_{EM} \;=\; T_{wheel}$ | clutch state ‚àà {locked, slipping, open} | N¬∑m |

**Real-World Engineering Utility** ‚Äî These equations are implemented directly inside Simulink/MATLAB models (e.g., *Autonomie*, *ADVISOR*) used by every OEM (Toyota, Hyundai, Tata, Mahindra) when calibrating an HEV. ECMS (Eq. 5) is the *de-facto* real-time benchmark; Eq. 4 is the theoretical lower bound computed offline using Dynamic Programming.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚Äî Vehicle Longitudinal Power Demand

The total tractive force required at the tyre contact patch is the sum of rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, and inertia force (assuming flat road, no slope):

$$
F_{trac} \;=\; F_{roll} \;+\; F_{aero} \;+\; F_{inertia}
$$

Substituting each term:

$$
F_{trac} \;=\; m g C_{rr} \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_{d}\,A_{f}\,v^{2} \;+\; m a
$$

Multiplying both sides by vehicle velocity $v$ gives the demanded mechanical power at the wheels:

$$
P_{dem}(t) \;=\; F_{trac}\cdot v \;=\; \underbrace{m g C_{rr}\,v}_{\text{rolling loss}} \;+\; \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_{d} A_{f}\,v^{3}}_{\text{aero loss}} \;+\; \underbrace{m\,a\,v}_{\text{inertia (kinetic)}}
$$

**Interpretation of Each Term** ‚Äî The rolling term grows linearly with speed; the aerodynamic term grows *cubically* (this is why highway mileage drops at high speed); the inertia term depends entirely on acceleration (zero during cruise). This equation is the **input** to every EMS algorithm.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚Äî Battery SOC Dynamics

The battery SOC at time $t+\Delta t$ equals its previous SOC minus the charge drawn out, divided by total capacity:

$$
\text{SOC}(t+\Delta t) \;=\; \text{SOC}(t) \;-\; \dfrac{1}{Q_{batt}}\int_{t}^{t+\Delta t} I_{batt}(\tau)\,d\tau
$$

Differentiating and taking the limit $\Delta t \to 0$:

$$
\dfrac{d(\text{SOC})}{dt} \;=\; -\,\dfrac{I_{batt}(t)}{Q_{batt}}
$$

> **Conversion Logic** ‚Äî SOC is dimensionless (ratio of remaining charge to full charge). Current $I_{batt}$ has units of Coulombs/sec, and $Q_{batt}$ has units of Coulombs. Their ratio is in sec‚Åª¬π, matching $d(\text{SOC})/dt$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚Äî Closed-Form Battery Current (from Power Request)

Given a requested battery power $P_{batt}$ and an equivalent-circuit model with open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ and internal resistance $R_{int}$:

$$
P_{batt} \;=\; V_{oc}\,I_{batt} \;-\; I_{batt}^{2}\,R_{int}
$$

Rearranging into standard quadratic form $a x^{2}+b x+c = 0$ with $x = I_{batt}$:

$$
R_{int}\,I_{batt}^{2} \;-\; V_{oc}\,I_{batt} \;+\; P_{batt} \;=\; 0
$$

Applying the quadratic formula:

$$
I_{batt} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{oc} \pm \sqrt{V_{oc}^{2} - 4 R_{int} P_{batt}}}{2 R_{int}}
$$

We select the **negative root** (the physically stable operating point, since positive root leads to high current heating):

$$
I_{batt} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{oc} - \sqrt{V_{oc}^{2} - 4 R_{int} P_{batt}}}{2 R_{int}}
$$

> **Validity Condition** ‚Äî For real $I_{batt}$ to exist, the discriminant must be non-negative:

$$
P_{batt} \;\le\; \dfrac{V_{oc}^{2}}{4 R_{int}}
$$

> **Conversion Logic** ‚Äî This boundary corresponds to the *maximum discharge power* the battery can deliver at a given SOC. Beyond it, the requested power is physically infeasible regardless of EMS algorithm.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 ‚Äî Equivalent Consumption Minimisation Strategy (ECMS) Cost Function

The ECMS translates the *electrical* energy leaving the battery into an *equivalent* mass of fuel, so the entire problem becomes a single scalar minimisation at every instant.

Step 1: Define instantaneous ICE fuel rate as a function of torque $T$ and speed $\omega$:

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel}(t) \;=\; f\!\left(T_{ICE}(t),\, \omega_{ICE}(t)\right)
$$

Step 2: Express battery power as fuel equivalent using the lower-heating-value of fuel $Q_{lhv} \approx 43\text{ MJ/kg}$ and a charge/discharge efficiency average $\bar{\eta}$:

$$
\dot{m}_{batt,equiv} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{batt}}{Q_{lhv}\;\bar{\eta}}
$$

Step 3: Introduce the **equivalence factor** $s(t)$ ‚Äî a tunable scalar that penalises battery use to maintain SOC sustainability:

$$
\dot{m}_{eq}(t) \;=\; \dot{m}_{fuel}(t) \;+\; s(t)\cdot \dot{m}_{batt,equiv}(t)
$$

Step 4: The optimal control at time $t$ is the one that minimises $\dot{m}_{eq}(t)$ subject to constraints:

$$
u^{*}(t) \;=\; \arg\min_{u} \dot{m}_{eq}\!\left(u(t)\right)
$$

subject to

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SOC}_{min} \;&\le\; \text{SOC}(t) \;\le\; \text{SOC}_{max} \\
P_{ICE,min} \;&\le\; P_{ICE}(t) \;\le\; P_{ICE,max} \\
P_{EM,min} \;&\le\; P_{EM}(t) \;\le\; P_{EM,max}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic** ‚Äî $s(t)$ is the *single most important tunable parameter* of ECMS. A common adaptive rule is $s(t) = s_{0} + k_{p}\!\left(\text{SOC}(t) - \text{SOC}_{ref}\right)$, making the system self-correcting for SOC drift.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix ‚Äî EMS Classification

| Strategy Type | Sub-Type | Knowledge of Future Trip? | Computational Load | Optimality | Real-Time Capable? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rule-Based** | Deterministic (IF-THEN) | No | Very Low | Sub-optimal | ‚úÖ Yes |
| **Rule-Based** | Fuzzy Logic | No | Low | Sub-optimal | ‚úÖ Yes |
| **Optimisation-Based (Global)** | Dynamic Programming (DP) | **Yes (full trip)** | Very High | Global Optimum | ‚ùå No (offline only) |
| **Optimisation-Based (Global)** | Pontryagin's Minimum Principle (PMP) | Yes | Medium | Near-Global | ‚ùå No |
| **Optimisation-Based (Real-Time)** | ECMS | No (assumed) | Low | Near-optimal | ‚úÖ Yes |
| **Optimisation-Based (Real-Time)** | Model Predictive Control (MPC) | Short horizon (e.g. 5 s) | Medium-High | Near-optimal | ‚úÖ Yes |
| **Learning-Based** | Neural Networks | Implicit (learned) | Low (after training) | Approximate | ‚úÖ Yes |
| **Learning-Based** | Reinforcement Learning (Q-Learning, DDPG) | Implicit (learned) | High (training only) | Asymptotically optimal | ‚úÖ Yes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram ‚Äî Hierarchical Classification of EMS Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Energy Management Strategy] --> brA[Rule Based]
    rootA --> opA[Optimization Based]
    rootA --> lrA[Learning Based]

    brA --> brA1[Deterministic IF THEN]
    brA --> brA2[Fuzzy Logic]
    brA --> brA3[Lookup Table Thermostat]

    opA --> opA1[Global Offline]
    opA --> opA2[Real Time Online]

    opA1 --> opA1a[Dynamic Programming DP]
    opA1 --> opA1b[Pontryagin Minimum Principle]
    opA1 --> opA1c[Genetic Algorithm]

    opA2 --> opA2a[Equivalent Consumption Minimisation ECMS]
    opA2 --> opA2b[Model Predictive Control MPC]
    opA2 --> opA2c[Adaptive ECMS]

    lrA --> lrA1[Neural Network Supervised]
    lrA --> lrA2[Reinforcement Learning Q]
    lrA --> lrA3[Deep RL DDPG PPO]

    brA1 -.low compute.-> optgoal((Practical EMS))
    opA2a -.good balance.-> optgoal
    lrA3 -.future tech.-> optgoal
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram ‚Äî EMS Block-Level Functional Architecture in a Series-Parallel HEV

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    drv[Driver Accelerator Pedal] --> vcu[Vehicle Control Unit VCU]
    spd[Vehicle Speed Sensor] --> vcu
    socb[Battery SOC Sensor] --> vcu
    ten[Temperature Sensors] --> vcu

    vcu --> emsblock{EMS Algorithm Core}

    emsblock -- torque split command --> ice[ICE Controller Throttle]
    emsblock -- torque split command --> mcu[Motor Inverter MCU]
    emsblock -- mode select EV HEV Charge --> tranny[Planetary Gear Coupling]

    ice --> tranny
    mcu --> tranny
    batt[High Voltage Battery] --- mcu
    tranny --> wheels[Final Drive Wheels]

    wheels -. feedback .-> spd
    batt -. feedback .-> socb

    style emsblock fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
    style vcu fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the Block Diagram:**
- The **VCU (Vehicle Control Unit)** is the *host processor*.
- The **EMS Core** (highlighted in gold) is the *decision-making software* ‚Äî typically running on a 32-bit automotive-grade microcontroller (e.g., Infineon Aurix TC277).
- The *three feedback arrows* (SOC, speed, temperature) form the **closed loop**.
- The *three command arrows* (throttle, inverter, mode select) are the **actuator signals**.

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram ‚Äî Sampling-Time and Control Hierarchy View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Driver Layer Pedal Steering] -->|seconds| L1
    L1[EMS Layer Power Split] -->|10 to 100 ms| L2
    L2[Device Layer Torque Speed PI] -->|1 to 5 ms| L3
    L3[Power Electronics PWM Gate Drive] -->|10 to 100 micro s| L4
    L4[Power Devices IGBT MOSFET Switching]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]`

**Define Energy Management Strategy (EMS) for a hybrid electric vehicle. List any four objectives it must satisfy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Energy Management Strategy (EMS)** is a supervisory real-time control algorithm that optimally splits the demanded propulsive power between two or more on-board energy sources (e.g., ICE, battery, ultracapacitor) of an HEV, subject to constraints on component limits and battery SOC.

**Four objectives (¬Ω mark each):**

1. **Minimisation of fuel consumption** (improve km/litre).
2. **Minimisation of exhaust emissions** (CO‚ÇÇ, NOx, HC, PM).
3. **Maintenance of battery SOC** within safe limits to ensure battery longevity.
4. **Preservation of drivability** (smooth torque delivery, no jerks, meeting driver demand).

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2024]`

**Differentiate between Rule-Based and Optimisation-Based Energy Management Strategies. (Any 4 points)**

**Model Answer (3 marks ‚Äî 4 √ó ¬æ marks each):**

| # | Rule-Based EMS | Optimisation-Based EMS |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Uses pre-defined IF-THEN rules or fuzzy logic | Uses a mathematical cost function minimised analytically or numerically |
| 2 | No prior knowledge of trip required | Global methods (DP, PMP) require *full* future trip knowledge |
| 3 | Computationally very cheap ‚Üí easily real-time | DP is computationally expensive ‚Üí typically offline; ECMS/MPC are real-time variants |
| 4 | Sub-optimal (engineering heuristic) | Global optimum (DP) or near-optimal (ECMS) achievable |
| 5 | Easy to implement and calibrate | Tuning of cost-function weights / equivalence factor needed |

---

### 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the hierarchical control architecture of a hybrid electric vehicle. Identify the layer in which the EMS resides. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the closed-form expression for battery current as a function of requested battery power, open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$, and internal resistance $R_{int}$. State the physical validity condition. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution ‚Äî Part (a) ‚Äî 7 marks**

The HEV control hierarchy consists of four layers, ordered from slowest (top) to fastest (bottom):

1. **Driver Layer (s):** Translates accelerator/brake pedal into a *desired wheel torque* $T_{wheel}^{*}$. *(1 mark)*
2. **Energy Management Layer (10‚Äì100 ms):** This is where the **EMS** resides. It reads driver demand + vehicle state (SOC, speed, temperature) and decides the **operating mode** (EV-only, ICE-only, hybrid, regenerative) and the **torque split** between ICE and EM. *(3 marks for identification + explanation)*
3. **Device / Torque Control Layer (1‚Äì5 ms):** Lower-level PI controllers track the commanded torques by modulating throttle angle, motor current, and clutch state. *(1.5 marks)*
4. **Power Electronics Layer (10‚Äì100 ¬µs):** Generates PWM gate signals to IGBTs/MOSFETs that actually switch DC link voltage to the motor windings. *(1.5 marks)*

The block diagram should depict the four-layer cascade with the EMS highlighted in the second layer. *(Block diagram carries full visual credit.)*

##### **Model Solution ‚Äî Part (b) ‚Äî 7 marks**

Starting with the equivalent-circuit model power equation:

$$
P_{batt} \;=\; V_{oc}\,I_{batt} \;-\; R_{int}\,I_{batt}^{2}
$$

Rearranged into standard quadratic form in $I_{batt}$:

$$
R_{int}\,I_{batt}^{2} \;-\; V_{oc}\,I_{batt} \;+\; P_{batt} \;=\; 0
$$

Applying the quadratic formula:

$$
I_{batt} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{oc} \pm \sqrt{V_{oc}^{2} - 4 R_{int} P_{batt}}}{2 R_{int}}
$$

Physical Interpretation: The **negative root** is selected because it represents the *stable* operating point (low current, low I¬≤R heating):

$$
\boxed{\,I_{batt} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{oc} - \sqrt{V_{oc}^{2} - 4 R_{int} P_{batt}}}{2 R_{int}}\,}
$$

**Validity Condition** *(2 marks)*: For a real-valued current, the discriminant must be non-negative:

$$
V_{oc}^{2} - 4 R_{int} P_{batt} \;\ge\; 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{\,P_{batt} \;\le\; \dfrac{V_{oc}^{2}}{4 R_{int}}\,}
$$

Beyond this $P_{batt}$, the requested power is **physically infeasible** ‚Äî the EMS must cap its request.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2022]`

**(a)** List and briefly explain any three classification categories of Energy Management Strategies used in HEVs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With necessary equations, explain the **Equivalent Consumption Minimisation Strategy (ECMS)**. Mention the role of the equivalence factor $s(t)$. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution ‚Äî Part (a) ‚Äî 7 marks**

The three categories are:

1. **Rule-Based Strategies (~2.5 marks)** ‚Äî Use pre-determined rules (IF-THEN) or fuzzy logic to decide power split based on current SOC and demanded power. *Example:* "IF SOC > 50 % AND demanded power < 10 kW ‚Üí EV mode". Advantages: simple, real-time, robust. Disadvantage: sub-optimal.

2. **Optimisation-Based Strategies (~2.5 marks)** ‚Äî Formulate a mathematical cost function (typically fuel consumption) and minimise it subject to constraints. Sub-divided into:
   - *Global optimisation* (DP, PMP) ‚Üí needs full trip knowledge, run offline.
   - *Real-time optimisation* (ECMS, MPC) ‚Üí use instantaneous or short-horizon cost.

3. **Learning-Based Strategies (~2 marks)** ‚Äî Use Artificial Intelligence (Neural Networks, Reinforcement Learning) trained on drive-cycle data. RL agents learn the optimal split through trial-and-error with a reward signal penalising fuel use and SOC deviation. Future of EMS, but requires extensive training data.

##### **Model Solution ‚Äî Part (b) ‚Äî 7 marks**

**ECMS Core Idea** *(2 marks)*: Convert *all* power flows (mechanical and electrical) into an equivalent fuel rate, then minimise the total at every instant.

**Equivalent fuel rate expression** *(3 marks)*:

$$
\dot{m}_{eq}(t) \;=\; \dot{m}_{fuel}(T_{ICE},\omega_{ICE}) \;+\; s(t)\,\cdot\,\dfrac{P_{batt}(t)}{Q_{lhv}\,\bar{\eta}}
$$

**Optimisation problem** *(1 mark)*:

$$
u^{*}(t) \;=\; \arg\min_{u(t)} \dot{m}_{eq}(t)
$$

subject to $\text{SOC}_{min} \le \text{SOC}(t) \le \text{SOC}_{max}$ and component power limits.

**Role of Equivalence Factor $s(t)$** *(1 mark)*: It converts battery power into equivalent fuel mass. When $s>0$, battery use is *penalised*; when $s<0$, battery use is *rewarded* (e.g., during charging). An adaptive rule keeps SOC sustained:

$$
s(t) \;=\; s_{0} \;+\; k_{p}\bigl[\text{SOC}(t) - \text{SOC}_{ref}\bigr]
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ‚Äî Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing EMS with the lower-level torque controller.** EMS is the *supervisory* layer; motor controller is below it. Marks lost if you mix them up.
> 2. **Forgetting the validity condition** $P_{batt} \le V_{oc}^{2}/4 R_{int}$ ‚Äî this is a guaranteed 2-mark question in any derivation.
> 3. **Writing "ECMS gives the global optimum"** ‚Äî ECMS is *near-optimal and real-time*, NOT globally optimal. DP is the global benchmark. Examiners specifically penalise this confusion.
> 4. **Drawing rule-based EMS as just "logic gates"** ‚Äî must show the *inputs* (SOC, $P_{dem}$, $T_{engine}$) and the *outputs* (torque split, mode) clearly.
> 5. **Using SI units inconsistently** ‚Äî power in W (not HP), torque in N¬∑m, energy in J or kWh.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ‚Äî Introduction to Energy Management Strategies**

- **EMS** = supervisory controller deciding power split between multiple sources.
- **Primary objective:** minimise fuel + emissions while keeping SOC stable.
- **Power balance equation:** $P_{dem} = P_{ICE} + P_{EM}$ ‚Äî the *master constraint*.
- **SOC dynamics:** $\dot{\text{SOC}} = -I_{batt}/Q_{batt}$.
- **Battery current closed form:** $I_{batt} = \dfrac{V_{oc} - \sqrt{V_{oc}^{2} - 4 R_{int} P_{batt}}}{2 R_{int}}$.
- **Maximum battery power:** $P_{batt,max} = V_{oc}^{2}/4 R_{int}$.
- **EMS categories (3):** Rule-Based, Optimisation-Based, Learning-Based.
- **Within Optimisation:** Global (DP, PMP ‚Äî offline) vs Real-Time (ECMS, MPC ‚Äî online).
- **ECMS** uses equivalence factor $s(t)$ to charge-discharge battery as virtual fuel.
- **Charge-Sustaining:** final SOC ‚âà initial SOC (¬±5 %).
- **Sampling times:** EMS runs at **10‚Äì100 ms**; lower-level torque PI at **1‚Äì5 ms**; PWM at **¬µs**.
- **ECMS ‚âÝ global optimum** (common exam trap).
- **DP is computationally expensive** because state-space grows as $N \times$ SOC-discretisation.
- **Reinforcement Learning** is the *future* of EMS ‚Äî agent learns split from reward = ‚Äì(fuel + Œ±¬∑ŒîSOC¬≤).
- **Standard metrics:** km/litre, g CO‚ÇÇ/km, SOC band, bsfc in g/kWh.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Battery Management System
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification of Battery Management System

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Battery Management System (BMS)** is an embedded electronic control unit that monitors, regulates, and protects a rechargeable battery pack ‚Äî particularly lithium-ion chemistries used in Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs), Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (PHEVs), and Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs). The BMS performs real-time measurement of cell voltage, current, temperature, and State of Charge (SoC), while executing cell balancing, fault diagnostics, thermal management, and communication with the Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) over the **Controller Area Network (CAN)** bus.

The **Classification of a BMS** refers to the systematic categorization of battery management architectures based on **hardware topology, functional hierarchy, control strategy, balancing methodology, and communication protocol**. KTU 2024 syllabus treats this classification as a foundational prerequisite for understanding pack-level energy management in modern electric powertrains.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students must be able to differentiate between **Centralized, Distributed, and Modular BMS architectures**, and classify BMS based on **functionality (SoC/SoH/SoP estimation), balancing type (passive/active), and communication interface (wired CAN / wireless BMS)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Hospital ICU" of an EV

Think of a battery pack as a **team of 96 to 396 marathon runners** (individual lithium-ion cells) connected in series-parallel to power your EV. Just like a marathon team:
- A **head coach** must constantly monitor every runner's heart rate, hydration, and pace.
- If one runner is overheating or lagging, the coach must either slow them down, cool them, or redistribute effort.
- The coach must also report the team's overall stamina to the race director (the VCU).

That "head coach with multiple assistants" is the **Battery Management System**. The way these assistants are deployed (one big office, satellite clinics, or a hybrid structure) defines the **classification of the BMS**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world reference metric:** A typical Tesla Model 3 pack uses a **distributed BMS** with one slave board per module of approximately **21700 cells** (each ~4.2 V fully charged, ~3.6 V nominal), communicating via a **2 Mbit/s CAN FD** backbone.

## 1.3 Physical & Standard Metrics Used in BMS Classification

| Metric | Typical Range / Standard | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Cell voltage (Li-ion) | **2.5 V ‚Äì 4.2 V** | Operational window for monitoring |
| Pack voltage (BEV) | **350 V ‚Äì 800 V** | Modern 800 V architectures (Hyundai E-GMP, Porsche Taycan) |
| Operating temperature | **‚àí20 ¬∞C to +60 ¬∞C** | Thermal envelope for SoC/SoH accuracy |
| CAN bus speed | **500 kbps (Classical CAN) / 2 Mbps (CAN FD)** | Communication bandwidth |
| Cell balancing current | **0.1 A ‚Äì 5 A (passive)**, up to **10 A (active)** | Balancing throughput |
| Sampling rate | **1 Hz ‚Äì 100 Hz** | Per-cell voltage/thermal scan rate |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Primary Classification: Hardware Architecture

The hardware architecture of a BMS ‚Äî *how* the electronic circuitry is physically distributed across the battery pack ‚Äî is the principal classification axis.

### A. Centralized BMS Architecture
A **single, powerful master BMS controller** directly interfaces with **every cell** in the pack via a dense wiring harness.

- **How it works:** One central PCB (typically mounted on top of the pack) contains the MCU, AFE (Analog Front End), and a large multi-channel multiplexer scanning all cell voltages.
- **Why it exists:** Lowest BOM cost, simplest firmware, single point of diagnostics.
- **How it fails in practice:** Massive wiring harness, high parasitic weight, single point of failure, signal integrity issues above ~100 cells.

### B. Distributed (De-centralized) BMS Architecture
**Slave/daughter boards** are physically attached to each cell or small cell group, communicating digitally with a central master.

- **How it works:** Each slave ASIC measures local V, T, and routes data via an **isolated daisy-chain** (e.g., TI BQ76PL536) or **CAN bus** to the master.
- **Why it is the EV industry standard:** Drastically reduces wiring, supports long packs, offers redundancy.
- **How it advances:** Modern **Wireless BMS (wBMS)** by Analog Devices (used in General Motors Ultium platform) removes the daisy-chain entirely.

### C. Modular (Semi-Distributed) BMS Architecture
A **hybrid** between centralized and distributed: a master controller connects to several **sub-modules**, each sub-module managing a fixed number of cells (e.g., 12 or 16).

- **How it works:** Modules group 10‚Äì16 cells; each module has its own slave controller, communicating with the master via CAN, SPI, or daisy-chain.
- **Why it matters:** Sweet spot for **commercial EVs and energy storage**; balances cost, scalability, and serviceability.

## 2.2 Secondary Classification: Functional Hierarchy

KTU frequently asks students to classify BMS by the **functions** it performs. A complete BMS executes the following ten core functions, often grouped into tiers:

| Tier | Function | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Tier 1 ‚Äî Measurement** | Voltage, Current, Temperature sensing | Raw telemetry acquisition |
| **Tier 2 ‚Äî Estimation** | SoC, SoH, SoP, SoE | State estimation algorithms |
| **Tier 3 ‚Äî Protection** | Over/under-voltage, over-current, short-circuit, over-temperature | Safety interlocks |
| **Tier 4 ‚Äî Balancing** | Cell-to-cell, pack-to-cell, passive/active | Capacity equalization |
| **Tier 5 ‚Äî Thermal Mgmt** | Cooling/heating loop control | Temperature regulation |
| **Tier 6 ‚Äî Communication** | CAN, LIN, SPI, wireless | VCU / OBD-II / charger handshake |
| **Tier 7 ‚Äî Diagnostics** | Insulation resistance, isolation monitoring | Functional safety (ISO 26262) |
| **Tier 8 ‚Äî Data Logging** | Lifetime tracking, cycle counting | Warranty + analytics |

## 2.3 Classification by Balancing Method

### Passive BMS
- Dissipates excess energy from higher-SoC cells as heat through **resistor bypass switches** (e.g., MOSFETs switching 50‚Äì100 Œ© resistors).
- Energy is **wasted**; thermal load increases; balancing is slow.
- Cost: **Low**; Complexity: **Low**.

### Active BMS
- Redistributes charge from higher-SoC cells to lower-SoC cells using switched capacitor, inductor, or **DC-DC converter** topologies.
- Energy is **conserved**; faster balancing; higher efficiency.
- Cost: **High**; Complexity: **High**.

| Active Topology | Energy Transfer Element | Efficiency |
|---|---|---|
| Switched Capacitor | Capacitor bank | ~85‚Äì92 % |
| Inductor-based | Coupled/uncoupled inductors | ~90‚Äì95 % |
| Transformer-based | HF transformer | ~92‚Äì97 % |

## 2.4 Classification by Communication Protocol

- **Wired BMS:** Classical CAN, CAN FD, SPI daisy-chain, UART.
- **Wireless BMS (wBMS):** 2.4 GHz proprietary RF (e.g., Analog Devices), reduces harness weight by up to **90 %** and removes connector failure modes.
- **Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1):** Emerging in zonal E/E architectures.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the canonical formula set the KTU examiner expects for the **Classification of BMS** topic, particularly the SoC estimation and balancing loss equations.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{SoC}(t) = \text{SoC}(t_0) - \dfrac{1}{Q_{\text{rated}}} \int_{t_0}^{t} I(\tau)\, d\tau$ | Coulomb-counting SoC | %, Ah |
| 2 | $Q_{\text{cell}} = \int_{0}^{t_{\text{discharge}}} I(t)\, dt$ | Cell capacity from current integration | Ah |
| 3 | $P_{\text{loss, passive}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} I_{\text{b}}^{2} R_{\text{b}}$ | Power lost in passive balancing resistors | W |
| 4 | $E_{\text{wasted, passive}} = \sum_{i} (V_i - V_{\text{avg}})\, Q_i$ | Energy dissipated during passive equalization | Wh |
| 5 | $\eta_{\text{active}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{out, balanced}}}{E_{\text{in, unbalanced}}}$ | Active balancing efficiency | % |
| 6 | $\text{SoH} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{max,aged}}}{Q_{\text{max,new}}} \times 100\%$ | State of Health | % |
| 7 | $\text{C-rate} = \dfrac{I_{\text{continuous}}}{Q_{\text{rated}}}$ | Charge/discharge rate | h‚Åª¬π |
| 8 | $R_{\text{insulation}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{pack}}}{I_{\text{leakage}}}$ | Insulation resistance (diagnostic) | Œ©/V |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: SoC Update via Coulomb Counting (Foundation of BMS Classification by Function)

The **coulomb-counting method** is the baseline algorithm underlying *every* BMS in Tier 2 estimation. KTU expects you to derive it for any 14-mark question on BMS functionality.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Define State of Charge (SoC)**
The SoC of a cell is defined as the ratio of its **remaining capacity** to its **rated capacity**, expressed as a percentage.

$$\text{SoC}(t) = \frac{Q_{\text{remaining}}(t)}{Q_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Express remaining capacity as an integral**
The remaining capacity at time $t$ equals the rated capacity minus the cumulative charge drawn (or plus the cumulative charge added) since a known reference instant $t_0$:

$$Q_{\text{remaining}}(t) = Q_{\text{rated}} - \int_{t_0}^{t} I(\tau)\, d\tau$$

where the sign convention is **$I > 0$ for discharge, $I < 0$ for charge** (or vice versa ‚Äî examiners expect you to declare your sign convention explicitly).

**Step 3 ‚Äî Substitute into the SoC definition**

$$\text{SoC}(t) = \frac{Q_{\text{rated}} - \int_{t_0}^{t} I(\tau)\, d\tau}{Q_{\text{rated}}}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Introduce the initial SoC**
Let the SoC at reference instant $t_0$ be denoted $\text{SoC}(t_0) = \text{SoC}_0$. Then:

$$\text{SoC}(t) = \text{SoC}_0 - \frac{1}{Q_{\text{rated}}} \int_{t_0}^{t} I(\tau)\, d\tau$$

This is the **coulomb-counting equation**, used in every centralized, modular, and distributed BMS.

**Step 5 ‚Äî Discrete-time implementation**
In a digital BMS sampled at interval $\Delta t$:

$$\text{SoC}[k+1] = \text{SoC}[k] - \frac{I[k] \cdot \Delta t}{Q_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\%$$

This discrete form is what the MCU actually computes every **10 ms ‚Äì 100 ms**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Passive Balancing Power Loss (Justifies why BMS is classified as "Passive" vs. "Active")

**Step 1 ‚Äî Setup**
Consider $n$ cells in series. Cell $i$ has excess charge $\Delta Q_i$ relative to the average; the BMS activates a bypass resistor $R_{\text{b}}$ through cell $i$.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Bypass current**
The bypass current through $R_{\text{b}}$ is:

$$I_{\text{b},i} = \frac{V_i}{R_{\text{b}} + R_{\text{ds(on)}}}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Time to balance**
The time required to bleed $\Delta Q_i$ of charge is:

$$t_{\text{balance},i} = \frac{\Delta Q_i}{I_{\text{b},i}} = \frac{\Delta Q_i \cdot R_{\text{b}}}{V_i}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Power dissipated**
The instantaneous power loss in the bypass resistor is:

$$P_{\text{loss},i}(t) = I_{\text{b},i}^{\,2} R_{\text{b}} = \frac{V_i^{\,2}}{R_{\text{b}}}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Total energy wasted during full pack equalization**

$$E_{\text{wasted}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \int_{0}^{t_{\text{balance},i}} \frac{V_i^{\,2}}{R_{\text{b}}}\, dt = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (V_i - V_{\text{avg}})\, Q_i$$

This is exactly Equation 4 in the formula sheet. It quantifies **why passive BMS is energy-wasteful** and motivates the **active BMS classification**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Active Switched-Capacitor Balancing Efficiency

**Step 1 ‚Äî Topology**
A switched-capacitor balancer uses $n-1$ capacitors to shuttle charge between adjacent cells. Switches operate at frequency $f_s$.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Charge transfer per cycle**
Per switching cycle, charge $\Delta Q = C \cdot \Delta V$ is transferred, where $\Delta V$ is the voltage difference between adjacent cells.

**Step 3 ‚Äî Energy transferred per cycle**

$$E_{\text{cycle}} = \tfrac{1}{2} C (V_i - V_{i+1})^{2}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Efficiency expression**
For a perfectly lossless SC balancer:

$$\eta_{\text{active}} = \frac{E_{\text{out}}}{E_{\text{out}} + E_{\text{loss,sw}}} \times 100\%$$

where $E_{\text{loss,sw}}$ captures MOSFET switching + conduction + capacitor ESR losses. Typical measured values: **85‚Äì92 %**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Comparing the Three BMS Architectures

| Parameter | Centralized | Distributed | Modular |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wiring complexity | **Very High** | **Low** | **Medium** |
| Cost (BOM) | Lowest | Highest | Medium |
| Scalability | Poor (‚â§ 100 cells) | Excellent (1000+ cells) | Good (‚â§ 200 cells) |
| Fault tolerance | None (single point) | High (slave redundancy) | Medium |
| Signal integrity | Degrades with distance | Excellent (digital isolation) | Good |
| Serviceability | Difficult | Easy (replace slave boards) | Easy (swap modules) |
| Use case | e-bikes, low-voltage packs | Tesla, BYD Blade packs | Commercial EV buses, ESS |
| Communication | Direct analog routing | Daisy-chain / CAN / wireless | Sub-module CAN / SPI |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Top-Level Classification Tree of BMS

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Battery Management System] --> B[By Hardware Architecture]
    A --> C[By Function]
    A --> D[By Balancing Method]
    A --> E[By Communication]

    B --> B1[Centralized BMS]
    B --> B2[Distributed BMS]
    B --> B3[Modular BMS]

    C --> C1[Measurement Tier]
    C --> C2[Estimation Tier]
    C --> C3[Protection Tier]
    C --> C4[Balancing Tier]
    C --> C5[Thermal Tier]
    C --> C6[Communication Tier]

    D --> D1[Passive Resistor Bleed]
    D --> D2[Active Switched Capacitor]
    D --> D3[Active Inductor Based]
    D --> D4[Active Transformer Based]

    E --> E1[Wired CAN FD]
    E --> E2[Daisy Chain SPI]
    E --> E3[Wireless 2.4 GHz]
    E --> E4[Automotive Ethernet]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Centralized BMS Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    PACK[Battery Pack 96 to 400 cells] --> HVH[High Voltage Wiring Harness]
    HVH --> MUX[Analog Multiplexer Array]
    MUX --> AFE[Analog Front End AFE]
    AFE --> MCU[Central Master MCU]
    MCU --> CAN[CAN FD Transceiver]
    MCU --> DRV[Contactor Driver]
    MCU --> BAL[Passive Balancing Resistor Bank]
    CAN --> VCU[Vehicle Control Unit VCU]
    DRV --> CON[Main HV Contactor]
    BAL --> PACK
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Distributed BMS with Wireless Communication

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Slave Module 1 cell group 1] --> RF1[2.4 GHz RF Transceiver]
    S2[Slave Module 2 cell group 2] --> RF2[2.4 GHz RF Transceiver]
    S3[Slave Module 3 cell group 3] --> RF3[2.4 GHz RF Transceiver]
    SN[Slave Module N cell group N] --> RFN[2.4 GHz RF Transceiver]

    RF1 --> AP[Wireless Access Point on Master]
    RF2 --> AP
    RF3 --> AP
    RFN --> AP

    AP --> MBMS[Master BMS Controller]
    MBMS --> VCU2[VCU over CAN FD]
    MBMS --> ISO[Insulation Monitor]
    MBMS --> DTC[Diagnostic Trouble Code Store]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Functional Tier Flow Inside Any BMS

```mermaid
graph LR
    T1[Tier 1 Sensing] --> T2[Tier 2 SoC SoH SoP]
    T2 --> T3[Tier 3 Protection Logic]
    T3 --> T4[Tier 4 Active Passive Balancing]
    T4 --> T5[Tier 5 Thermal Control]
    T5 --> T6[Tier 6 CAN LIN Wireless]
    T6 --> T7[Tier 7 ISO 26262 Diagnostics]
    T7 --> T8[Tier 8 Lifetime Data Log]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for the examiner:** In a 14-mark KTU question, the **functional tier diagram** is the most common diagram students are asked to reproduce. Memorize the eight tiers in order.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]
**Differentiate between centralized and distributed BMS architectures. Mention two advantages of the distributed topology.**

**Model Answer:**
A *centralized BMS* uses a single master controller that directly scans every cell in the pack, leading to a large wiring harness and a single point of failure. A *distributed BMS* mounts small slave boards on each cell or cell group, communicating digitally (CAN, SPI, or wireless) with a central master. **Advantages of distributed BMS:** (i) drastically reduced wiring harness weight and complexity, and (ii) inherent redundancy ‚Äî failure of one slave does not collapse the entire pack monitoring. *(3 marks)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]
**What is the difference between passive and active cell balancing? State one limitation of passive balancing.**

**Model Answer:**
*Passive balancing* dissipates excess energy from higher-SoC cells as heat through resistor‚ÄìMOSFET bypass circuits. *Active balancing* shuttles energy from higher-SoC to lower-SoC cells using capacitors, inductors, or DC-DC converters, conserving the energy. **Limitation of passive balancing:** the energy is wasted as heat, lowering pack efficiency and increasing thermal load on the cells. *(3 marks)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ‚Äî Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) ‚Äî [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** Classify Battery Management Systems based on (i) hardware architecture and (ii) communication protocol. Explain the distributed BMS architecture with a neat block diagram. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of the coulomb-counting equation, derive the expression for State of Charge (SoC). A 50 Ah Li-ion cell is discharged at a constant current of 10 A for 1.5 hours. If the initial SoC was 90 %, find the final SoC. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution ‚Äî (a)

The **classification of BMS by hardware architecture** yields three categories:

1. **Centralized BMS** ‚Äî single master, dense harness.
2. **Distributed BMS** ‚Äî slave boards at every cell group, digital communication to master.
3. **Modular BMS** ‚Äî sub-modules each managing 10‚Äì16 cells, master aggregates.

**Classification by communication protocol:**
- Wired: Classical CAN, CAN FD, SPI daisy-chain, UART, LIN.
- Wireless: 2.4 GHz proprietary RF, automotive Ethernet 100BASE-T1.

**Distributed BMS block diagram (drawn in exam):**

```
Cell group 1 -> Slave 1 ->|
Cell group 2 -> Slave 2 ->|--> Isolated daisy-chain / CAN FD --> Master BMS --> VCU
Cell group N -> Slave N ->|
```

**Working:** Each slave measures local V and T, performs local balancing decision, and reports telemetry to the master. The master computes pack-level SoC, SoH, issues protection commands, and drives HV contactors. **Advantages:** scalability, fault tolerance, low harness weight. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution ‚Äî (b)

**Step 1 ‚Äî Coulomb-counting SoC derivation** (as shown in Section 3.1 of these notes):

$$\text{SoC}(t) = \text{SoC}_0 - \frac{1}{Q_{\text{rated}}} \int_{t_0}^{t} I(\tau)\, d\tau$$

For constant current $I = \text{const}$:

$$\text{SoC}(t) = \text{SoC}_0 - \frac{I \cdot (t - t_0)}{Q_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Numerical evaluation**

Given: $Q_{\text{rated}} = 50 \text{ Ah}$, $I = 10 \text{ A}$, $\Delta t = 1.5 \text{ h}$, $\text{SoC}_0 = 90\%$.

Charge drawn:

$$\Delta Q = I \cdot \Delta t = 10 \times 1.5 = 15 \text{ Ah}$$

SoC drop:

$$\Delta\text{SoC} = \frac{15}{50} \times 100\% = 30\%$$

Final SoC:

$$\text{SoC}_{\text{final}} = 90\% - 30\% = \boxed{60\%}$$

*(7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024 Model]

**(a)** Classify BMS by balancing method. Derive the energy wasted in passive balancing for an $n$-cell series pack. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare centralized, modular, and distributed BMS across at least five parameters. Justify why a wireless distributed BMS is preferred in modern 800 V BEV platforms. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution ‚Äî (a)

**Classification by balancing method:**

- **Passive:** resistor-bypass dissipation.
- **Active (lossless):** switched-capacitor, inductor-based, transformer (flyback/forward) based, DC-DC converter.

**Derivation of energy wasted in passive balancing** (as in Section 3.2):

For cell $i$ with excess charge $\Delta Q_i$ bled through resistor $R_{\text{b}}$:

$$I_{\text{b},i} = \frac{V_i}{R_{\text{b}}}, \quad t_{\text{balance},i} = \frac{\Delta Q_i R_{\text{b}}}{V_i}$$

Energy dissipated:

$$E_{\text{loss},i} = \frac{V_i^{\,2}}{R_{\text{b}}} \cdot \frac{\Delta Q_i R_{\text{b}}}{V_i} = V_i \cdot \Delta Q_i$$

Total energy wasted across the pack:

$$E_{\text{wasted}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (V_i - V_{\text{avg}})\, Q_i$$

This quantifies the inefficiency penalty of the **passive BMS** classification. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution ‚Äî (b)

**Comparison table** (as in Section 3.4):

| Parameter | Centralized | Modular | Distributed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wiring | Very high | Medium | Low |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | High |
| Scalability | Poor | Good | Excellent |
| Fault tolerance | None | Medium | High |
| Serviceability | Hard | Easy | Easy |

**Justification for wireless distributed BMS in 800 V BEVs:**

1. **Harness weight reduction (~90 %)** is critical at 800 V where thick copper HV cables already dominate pack mass.
2. **Removal of HV connectors** at every cell module eliminates creepage and arcing failure modes.
3. **Pack-level assembly simplification** ‚Äî modules can be added/removed for service without disturbing HV busbars.
4. **Native support for zonal E/E architectures** aligning with ISO 21434 cybersecurity.
5. **Redundancy** ‚Äî if one wireless node fails, telemetry from neighbors is sufficient for safe limp-home operation.

Examples: **GM Ultium** uses Analog Devices wBMS; **Hyundai E-GMP** uses distributed CAN-FD daisy-chain.

*(7 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse *hardware architecture classification* (Centralized / Modular / Distributed) with *balancing classification* (Passive / Active) ‚Äî they are **orthogonal axes**. A centralized BMS can still do active balancing.
> - **Do NOT** drop units in the coulomb-counting substitution ‚Äî write Ah consistently, then convert to %.
> - **Do NOT** use the reserved Mermaid keyword `end` as a node ID ‚Äî it will break the diagram and cost you easy marks.
> - When asked to "derive", at least **three logical steps** (definition ‚Üí integral ‚Üí substitution) are mandatory. A one-line formula copy attracts zero marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap ‚Äî Important Things to Remember

- **Three hardware architectures:** Centralized, Modular, Distributed ‚Äî distinguished by *where* the cell-monitoring electronics physically reside.
- **Distributed BMS** is the industry default for modern BEVs; its wireless variant (wBMS) eliminates the wiring harness entirely.
- **Centralized BMS** suits low-cell-count packs (e-bikes, 48 V mild hybrids); it is cheap but fragile.
- **Modular BMS** is the commercial-EV sweet spot (city buses, light commercial vehicles, BESS).
- **Classification by function** yields an 8-tier hierarchy: Sensing ‚Üí Estimation ‚Üí Protection ‚Üí Balancing ‚Üí Thermal ‚Üí Communication ‚Üí Diagnostics ‚Üí Logging.
- **Classification by balancing:** Passive (resistor bleed, ~30‚Äì60 % energy lost) vs. Active (SC, inductor, transformer; 85‚Äì97 % efficient).
- **Coulomb-counting equation** $\text{SoC}(t) = \text{SoC}_0 - \dfrac{1}{Q_{\text{rated}}} \int_{t_0}^{t} I\, d\tau$ is the **mandatory formula** for any KTU BMS numerical.
- **Communication protocols:** Classical CAN (500 kbps), CAN FD (2 Mbps), SPI daisy-chain, 2.4 GHz wBMS, automotive Ethernet.
- **State metrics to know:** SoC (instantaneous charge %), SoH (capacity fade %), SoP (power capability kW), SoE (energy Wh).
- **Safety standards governing BMS classification choices:** ISO 26262 (functional safety), ISO 21434 (cybersecurity), IEC 62660 (Li-ion performance), AIS-156 / AIS-138 (India EV safety).
- **For numerical problems:** always declare the **sign convention** of current (discharge positive) before plugging into the integral.
- **KTU diagram rule:** always label the **master, slaves, communication bus, and VCU interface** explicitly ‚Äî a diagram without these four labels is incomplete.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle Communication Protocols: Need & Requirements, Functions of Control Pilot (CP) and Proximity Pilot (PP) Pins
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Communication Protocols: Need & Requirements, Functions of Control Pilot (CP) and Proximity Pilot (PP) Pins

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ‚Äî Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Vehicle Communication Protocol (in the EV context)?

In Hybrid and Electric Vehicles (HEV/EV), a **Vehicle Communication Protocol** refers to the standardized set of electrical signalling rules and digital message structures that allow the **Electric Vehicle (EV)** to "talk" to the **Electric Vehicle Supply Equipment (EVSE ‚Äî the off-board charger)** before, during, and after a charging session.

The most important basic-level communication is defined by:
- **IEC 61851-1** (International standard for conductive AC/DC charging)
- **SAE J1772** (North American equivalent ‚Äî Type 1 connector)
- **IEC 62196** (Connector types ‚Äî Type 2, CCS, etc.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 ‚Äî OEEET722 Module 4):**
> Communication between the EV and the EVSE is *not optional* ‚Äî it is the **legal and safety backbone** of conductive charging. Without it, the grid cannot safely energize the vehicle.

## 1.2 Need & Requirements of Vehicle Communication

### Why is communication needed?

| # | Need | Real-World Consequence if Missing |
|---|------|-----------------------------------|
| 1 | **Personal Safety** | User electrocuted by un-coordinated contactor closing |
| 2 | **Equipment Protection** | Onboard charger (OBC) overloaded, EV cable overheats |
| 3 | **Grid Stability** | Simultaneous uncoordinated charging causes voltage sag |
| 4 | **Billing / Authentication** | No way to identify user, no smart metering possible |
| 5 | **Ventilation Control** | Lead-acid EVs in closed garages need forced air ‚Äî must be coordinated |
| 6 | **Cable Current Rating Discovery** | EVSE doesn't know if the cable is rated 16 A or 32 A |

### Functional Requirements (as per IEC 61851-1)

1. **Pilot signalling** ‚Äî A low-voltage signal that handshakes between EV and EVSE.
2. **Proximity detection** ‚Äî Confirms the connector is physically latched to the inlet *before* mains contactors close.
3. **Current advertisement** ‚Äî EVSE informs the EV of the maximum allowable charge current.
4. **Status reporting** ‚Äî EV informs EVSE whether it is "Connected", "Ready", "Charging", or in "Ventilation Required" mode.
5. **Fault detection** ‚Äî Loss of pilot tone, loss of PE (earth), or over-current ‚Üí both sides must open contactors within milliseconds.

## 1.3 The Five / Seven-Pin Connector Architecture

The Type 1 (SAE J1772) and Type 2 (IEC 62196-2) connectors carry the following key pins:

| Pin | Symbol | Function |
|-----|--------|----------|
| Power pins | L1, L2, N | AC line and neutral conductors |
| Earth | PE | Protective earth (ground) |
| **Control Pilot** | **CP** | Bidirectional low-voltage signalling line |
| **Proximity Pilot** | **PP** | Cable-presence + current-rating resistor line |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Knock-Knock" and "Who Are You?" handshake**
> Think of plugging in an EV the same way you plug in a USB-C phone charger:
> - **PP (Proximity Pilot)** = the *mechanical click*. It answers: *"Are you physically plugged in and latched?"* Just like you cannot push current through a half-inserted USB plug, the EVSE will not energize the AC pins until PP is happy.
> - **CP (Control Pilot)** = the *digital conversation*. It answers: *"Who are you, how much current can I draw, and is it safe to start?"* Just like USB-C's CC pins negotiate 5 V/3 A vs 20 V/5 A, CP negotiates 6 A vs 32 A using a PWM duty cycle.

---

# SECTION 2 ‚Äî Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Control Pilot (CP) ‚Äî Operating Principle

The CP pin is a single-wire bidirectional signal line. On the **EVSE side**, a circuit generates a **+12 V DC bias** modulated by a **1 kHz (¬±0.5 %) PWM square wave** and applies it through a resistor divider to the CP line. On the **EV side**, a resistor network pulls the voltage to specific levels that the EVSE detects.

### CP Voltage States (per SAE J1772 / IEC 61851-1)

The EVSE measures the CP line voltage with respect to PE. The **state of the EV** is encoded by the **DC level** of this voltage:

| CP Voltage (V) | EV State | Meaning |
|:---:|---|---|
| **+12 V** | State A | EV *not connected* |
| **+9 V** | State B | EV *connected, not ready* to charge |
| **+6 V** | State C | EV *ready*, charging allowed, no ventilation needed |
| **+3 V** | State D | EV *ready*, charging allowed, **ventilation required** |
| **0 V** | State E | EVSE fault / pilot short to ground / EV disconnected during charge |

### Duty-Cycle to Current Mapping (the "advertised current")

The 1 kHz PWM square wave's **duty cycle** tells the EV how much current it is allowed to draw:

$$\text{Available Current } I_{max} = D \times 0.6 \text{ A per } \% \text{ of duty cycle}$$

| Duty Cycle D | Advertised Current $I_{max}$ |
|:---:|:---:|
| 5 % | No charging available (digital communication only) |
| 10 % | 6 A |
| 25 % | 15 A |
| 50 % | 30 A |
| 90 % | 54 A |
| 96 % | 63 A (max ‚Äî pilot becomes digital comm channel) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a 1 kHz square wave?** Frequencies in the **kHz band** are easily filtered by simple RC networks yet are far above 50/60 Hz mains, allowing them to be coupled onto the same conductor that carries the AC mains. The 1 kHz also lies in the **audio band**, allowing cheap optocouplers and audio-range detectors to be used.

## 2.2 Proximity Pilot (PP) ‚Äî Operating Principle

PP is a **passive, un-powered** signal line ‚Äî it does not carry PWM. Instead, the **EVSE measures a DC resistance** between the PP pin and PE to identify the cable.

### How PP works

1. The connector's latch mechanism contains a **resistor** $R_{PP}$ wired between PP and PE.
2. The EVSE applies a small sensing current (a few mA) and measures the voltage.
3. The **resistance value encodes the cable's current rating** $I_{cable}$ and the **connector's latch state**.

### PP Resistor Coding (SAE J1772 / IEC 61851-1)

| $R_{PP}$ | Cable Rating $I_{cable}$ | Application |
|:---:|:---:|---|
| **Open (‚àû)** | Connector *unplugged* | EVSE keeps contactors open |
| 1.5 kŒ© | 13 A | Light portable EVSE |
| 680 Œ© | 20 A | Standard household |
| 220 Œ© | 32 A | High-power AC |
| 100 Œ© | 63 A | 3-phase fast AC |

### Latch-State Trick

The PP pin is mechanically switched: when the latch button is **pressed**, the resistor is **disconnected** (open circuit). When the latch is **released (engaged)**, the resistor is reconnected. The EVSE uses this to:
- **Open the contactor immediately** if the user presses the latch button mid-charge.
- Detect a **half-inserted** connector (resistor present but latch not engaged).

## 2.3 Why BOTH CP and PP? (The "Defense in Depth" Principle)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> CP handles the **electrical handshake and current advertisement**, while PP handles the **mechanical safety and cable identification**. Together they form a **redundant, fail-safe interlock** that satisfies both IEC 61851-1 and SAE J1772.

If only CP existed ‚Üí EV could start charging before the latch clicked ‚Üí arcing on the pins.
If only PP existed ‚Üí EV would not know the available current ‚Üí onboard charger could be overloaded.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Value | Unit |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|
| $f_{PWM}$ | CP pilot signal frequency | $\mathbf{1000}$ | Hz |
| $D$ | PWM duty cycle | $D = \frac{t_{high}}{T}$ | dimensionless (0‚Äì1) |
| $I_{max}$ | Advertised max charge current | $I_{max} = D \times 0.6 \text{ A per } 1\% \text{ duty}$ | A |
| $V_{CP}$ | CP line voltage (DC level) | 12 / 9 / 6 / 3 / 0 V | V |
| $R_{PP}$ | Proximity pilot resistance | 1500 / 680 / 220 / 100 Œ© | Œ© |
| $I_{cable}$ | Cable current rating | From $R_{PP}$ lookup | A |
| $V_{EVSE}$ | EVSE-side CP bias voltage | **+12 V DC** | V |
| $R_1$ | EVSE series resistor (high side) | **1000 Œ©** | Œ© |
| $R_2$ | EVSE pull-down resistor | **1300 Œ©** | Œ© |
| $R_3$ | EV-side pull-up | **2740 Œ©** | Œ© |
| $R_4$ | EV-side diode + $R_5$ | **2740 Œ©** | Œ© |

> **Pitfall to remember:** The advertised current is a *ceiling* ‚Äî the EV's OBC may still draw less based on its SoC algorithm.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart charging (V2G)** ‚Äî ISO 15118 uses the CP line as a **digital communication channel** at higher duty cycles (>5 %) to carry PLC-modulated broadband data for billing, scheduling, and bi-directional power flow.
- **Tethered vs untethered EVSEs** ‚Äî Detachable cables (untethered) need PP more, because the EVSE doesn't "know" the cable rating unless PP tells it.
- **Ventilation safety** ‚Äî In indoor charging (e.g., underground garages with classic lead-acid EVs converted), the +3 V state commands the EVSE to switch on a fan.

---

# SECTION 3 ‚Äî Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of CP Line Voltage States

The CP circuit is a classic **resistor divider** problem. Let us model the Thevenin equivalent as seen at the CP pin by the EVSE's measurement circuit.

### Circuit Model

- EVSE side: $V_{EVSE} = 12$ V, in series with $R_1 = 1000$ Œ© and $R_2 = 1300$ Œ©, with $R_2$ to PE.
- EV side: $R_3 = 2740$ Œ© from +12 V (in-vehicle) and $R_4 = 2740$ Œ© with a diode in series to PE.

When the EV is **not connected**, the EV only "sees" the divider $R_1 + R_2$:

$$V_{CP,A} = V_{EVSE} \times \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

Substituting:

$$V_{CP,A} = 12 \times \frac{1300}{1000 + 1300} = 12 \times \frac{1300}{2300}$$

$$V_{CP,A} = 12 \times 0.5652 = 6.78 \text{ V}$$

> **Wait** ‚Äî the EVSE also has a controller that drives the CP line *actively* to +12 V via a low-impedance source. So in the *no-EV* state, the EVSE actively drives CP = +12 V (State A).

When the EV is **connected but not ready**, the EV closes the diode path, which adds $R_4 = 2740$ Œ© in parallel with $R_2 = 1300$ Œ©:

$$R_{2\parallel 4} = \frac{R_2 \cdot R_4}{R_2 + R_4} = \frac{1300 \times 2740}{1300 + 2740}$$

$$R_{2\parallel 4} = \frac{3\,562\,000}{4040} = 881.7 \text{ Œ©}$$

Now the divider becomes:

$$V_{CP,B} = V_{EVSE} \times \frac{R_{2\parallel 4}}{R_1 + R_{2\parallel 4}}$$

$$V_{CP,B} = 12 \times \frac{881.7}{1000 + 881.7} = 12 \times \frac{881.7}{1881.7}$$

$$V_{CP,B} = 12 \times 0.4686 = 5.62 \text{ V}$$

> Hmm, the *nominal* table says **+9 V**. This is because in practice, $R_1$ is only **2740 Œ©** in some implementations, and the EVSE uses an *active* 12 V source through a switch. For State B, the EV closes a switch to put $R_3 = 2740$ Œ© in **parallel** with the EVSE's 12 V source, which the controller then reads. The **9 V, 6 V, 3 V** levels are produced by the EV sequentially switching in different resistor networks, and the EVSE detects these voltage thresholds with a comparator.

For exam purposes, the **key derived result** students must remember is:

$$\boxed{V_{CP,\text{detected}} = 12 \text{ V, 9 V, 6 V, 3 V, 0 V} \;\; \Longleftrightarrow \;\; \text{States A, B, C, D, E}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Available Charge Current from Duty Cycle

The standard mapping (per SAE J1772) is **linear**:

$$I_{max}(\text{A}) = 0.6 \times \frac{D(\%)}{1}$$

Or equivalently:

$$I_{max} = 0.6 \times D \times 100 \quad \text{[A, with D in 0‚Äì1]}$$

### Worked Numerical Example

> *An EVSE advertises a duty cycle of 50 % on the CP line. What is the maximum continuous AC charge current the EV's onboard charger may draw?*

**Step 1** ‚Äî Identify the mapping:
$$D = 0.50$$

**Step 2** ‚Äî Apply the formula:
$$I_{max} = 0.6 \times 0.50 \times 100 = 30 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3** ‚Äî Verify with the lookup table: 50 % duty ‚Üí **30 A** ‚úì

### Inverse Problem

> *The EV onboard charger needs 22 A. What duty cycle should the EVSE transmit on CP?*

$$D = \frac{I_{max}}{0.6 \times 100} = \frac{22}{60} = 0.367 = 36.7\%$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî CP vs PP Comparison

| Parameter | Control Pilot (CP) | Proximity Pilot (PP) |
|---|---|---|
| **Signal type** | 1 kHz PWM, DC-biased | DC resistance only |
| **Power source** | EVSE supplies +12 V | EVSE supplies sensing current |
| **State info carried** | EV status + advertised current | Cable rating + latch state |
| **Bidirectional?** | Yes (EV can modulate) | No (passive resistor) |
| **Failure mode** | Loss of pilot ‚Üí shutdown | Open circuit ‚Üí shutdown |
| **Standard** | IEC 61851-1, SAE J1772 | IEC 61851-1, SAE J1772 |
| **Pin position (Type 1)** | Pin 4 | Pin 5 |

## 3.4 Sequence of Events During a Typical AC Charge

1. User inserts the connector ‚Üí **PP detects latch engagement** ($R_{PP}$ = 220 Œ©, e.g., 32 A cable).
2. EVSE measures $R_{PP}$ ‚Üí concludes cable is 32 A rated.
3. EVSE energizes CP at **+12 V**.
4. EV closes diode path ‚Üí CP drops to **+9 V** (State B ‚Äî connected).
5. EV requests charge ‚Üí closes $R_3$ switch ‚Üí CP drops to **+6 V** (State C ‚Äî ready).
6. EVSE starts 1 kHz PWM, **D = 50 %** ‚Üí informs EV of 30 A max.
7. EVSE closes AC contactors; charging begins.
8. If user presses latch button mid-charge ‚Üí **PP opens** ‚Üí EVSE trips contactors within <100 ms.
9. When EV finishes ‚Üí opens switch ‚Üí CP returns to +9 V (State B).
10. User removes connector ‚Üí CP returns to +12 V (State A).

---

# SECTION 4 ‚Äî Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CP and PP Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[EVSE Controller] -->|12V + 1kHz PWM| B[CP Pin]
    C[Cable Resistor Rpp] -->|DC resistance| D[PP Pin]
    E[Latch Mechanism] -->|Mechanical switch| D
    B -->|Voltage level feedback| A
    D -->|Resistance measurement| A
    A -->|Drive signal| F[AC Contactor K1]
    F -->|Energizes L1, L2, N| G[Onboard Charger OBC]
    B -->|State sensing| H[EV Controller]
    H -->|R3, R4 switching| B
    A -->|Advertised current via duty| H
    H -->|Current command| G
    G -->|DC link| I[HV Battery Pack]
    style A fill:#FFD700
    style B fill:#87CEEB
    style D fill:#FFB6C1
    style F fill:#90EE90
    style G fill:#DDA0DD
    style I fill:#FFA07A
```

## 4.2 CP State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StateA: No EV
    StateA: State A ‚Äî +12V (Disconnected)
    StateB: State B ‚Äî +9V (Connected, Not Ready)
    StateC: State C ‚Äî +6V (Ready, Charge)
    StateD: State D ‚Äî +3V (Ready, Ventilation)
    StateE: State E ‚Äî 0V (Fault)
    StateA --> StateB: Connector inserted, PP closed
    StateB --> StateC: EV closes R3 switch
    StateC --> StateD: EV signals ventilation needed
    StateB --> StateD: EV requests vent at plug-in
    StateC --> StateB: Charging complete
    StateD --> StateB: Charging complete
    StateB --> StateA: Connector removed
    StateC --> StateE: Pilot short / overcurrent
    StateD --> StateE: Pilot short / overcurrent
    StateE --> StateA: Manual reset
```

## 4.3 Communication Protocol Stack (Module 4 Overview)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1: Analog Pilot CP and PP] --> L2[Layer 2: PWM Signalling 1 kHz]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: ISO 15118 PLC over CP]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: OCPP Open Charge Point Protocol backhaul]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5: Grid Services V2G V2H]
    style L1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style L2 fill:#FFD700
    style L3 fill:#87CEEB
    style L4 fill:#90EE90
    style L5 fill:#DDA0DD
```

> [!NOTE]
> CP and PP belong to **Layer 1 (physical / pilot)** of the EV‚ÄìEVSE communication stack. ISO 15118 sits on top using PLC, and OCPP is the cloud-level protocol used between EVSE and the central charging management system.

## 4.4 Connector Pin Mapping (Type 1 ‚Äî SAE J1772)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TYPE1[Type 1 Connector J1772]
        P1[L1 Power] --- P2[L2 Neutral] --- P3[PE Earth] --- P4[CP Control Pilot] --- P5[PP Proximity Pilot]
    end
    P1 --> M1[AC Mains]
    P2 --> M1
    P3 --> M1
    P4 --> M2[EVSE Controller]
    P5 --> M2
    style P4 fill:#FFD700
    style P5 fill:#FFB6C1
```

---

# SECTION 5 ‚Äî KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A ‚Äî 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]`
**List any three functions of the Control Pilot (CP) pin in an AC EV charging system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. It performs the **electrical handshake** between the EV and EVSE, indicating connection status (States A‚ÄìE). **[1 mark]**
2. It carries a **1 kHz PWM signal** whose **duty cycle encodes the maximum available charge current** (e.g., 50 % ‚Üí 30 A). **[1 mark]**
3. It acts as a **safety interlock** ‚Äî loss of the pilot signal causes both sides to open the contactors, preventing electrocution. **[1 mark]**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]`
**What is the purpose of the Proximity Pilot (PP) pin? How does the EVSE determine the cable's current rating from it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Proximity Pilot (PP) pin serves two purposes:
1. **Connection detection** ‚Äî the EVSE measures a DC resistance between PP and PE; an *open circuit* means the connector is not plugged in. **[1 mark]**
2. **Cable current rating identification** ‚Äî the connector contains a fixed resistor $R_{PP}$ whose value is standardized (1000 / 680 / 220 / 100 Œ© ‚âà 13 / 20 / 32 / 63 A). The EVSE applies a small sensing current, measures the resulting voltage, decodes the resistance, and thus determines the cable's current rating. **[2 marks]**

---

## Part B ‚Äî 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### `Question A ‚Äî 14 Marks` `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the operating principle of the **Control Pilot (CP) function** in an EV charging system, and list the four EV states A, B, C, D with their corresponding CP voltages. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A public AC charging station uses a Type 2 connector with the CP signal transmitting a **1 kHz PWM at 35 % duty cycle**. Determine the maximum continuous AC charging current the EV may draw. Also explain the role of the **EVSE-side resistor divider ($R_1 = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 1.3\text{ k}\Omega$)** in setting the no-load CP voltage. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

### (a) CP Operating Principle & State Voltages [7 marks]

**Diagram (1 mark):**
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EVSE[EVSE Controller 12V PWM 1kHz] -->|CP line| R1[R1 1kŒ©] --> CP[CP Pin]
    CP --> R2[R2 1.3kŒ©] --> PE[PE]
    EV[EV Controller] --> R3[R3 2.74kŒ©] --> CP
    EV --> D[Diode] --> R4[R4 2.74kŒ©] --> PE
    style EVSE fill:#FFD700
    style CP fill:#87CEEB
    style EV fill:#90EE90
```

**Operating Principle (4 marks):**
The Control Pilot (CP) is a **bidirectional pilot signal line** defined by IEC 61851-1 / SAE J1772. The EVSE applies a **+12 V DC bias** modulated by a **1 kHz square wave (PWM)**. The EV switches in different resistor networks ($R_3$, $R_4$) to pull the CP line to specific voltage levels. The EVSE measures these DC levels using a comparator to determine the EV's state.

**EV States (2 marks):**
| State | CP Voltage | Meaning |
|:---:|:---:|---|
| A | +12 V | EV not connected |
| B | +9 V | Connected, not ready to charge |
| C | +6 V | Ready to charge, no ventilation |
| D | +3 V | Ready to charge, **ventilation required** |
| E | 0 V | EVSE fault |

### (b) Maximum Charging Current [7 marks]

**Step 1 ‚Äî Given data (1 mark):**
$$f_{PWM} = 1 \text{ kHz}, \quad D = 35\% = 0.35$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Apply the duty-to-current mapping (3 marks):**
$$I_{max} = 0.6 \times D \times 100 = 0.6 \times 0.35 \times 100$$

$$I_{max} = 21 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Interpretation (1 mark):**
The EV's onboard charger must limit its AC input current to **21 A** even if its maximum rating is higher.

**Step 4 ‚Äî Role of $R_1$ and $R_2$ (2 marks):**
When no EV is connected, the CP line voltage is set by the divider formed by $R_1 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ (in series with the active 12 V source) and $R_2 = 1.3 \text{ k}\Omega$ (to PE). However, in practice the EVSE **actively drives** CP to +12 V with a low-impedance source so that even with the divider loading, the voltage is held at exactly **+12 V (State A)**. The divider provides a **detectable, predictable impedance** that the EV can pull against, allowing it to switch in $R_3$ and force CP down to 9 V / 6 V / 3 V.

$$\boxed{I_{max} = 21 \text{ A}}$$

---

### `Question B ‚Äî 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)` `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the function of the **Proximity Pilot (PP) pin** in detail. Include the standard resistor coding for cable current ratings, and explain the **latch-state interlock mechanism**. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Compare the **Control Pilot (CP)** and **Proximity Pilot (PP)** pins in a tabular form across any five parameters. Why are *both* pins necessary ‚Äî what unique safety function does each perform that the other cannot? **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

### (a) Proximity Pilot (PP) Detailed Function [7 marks]

**Definition (1 mark):** The PP pin is a passive signal line that uses a **fixed resistor** inside the connector to identify cable current rating and latch state.

**Resistor Coding (3 marks):**
| $R_{PP}$ | $I_{cable}$ | Use Case |
|:---:|:---:|---|
| Open (‚àû) | ‚Äî | Connector not plugged in |
| 1500 Œ© | 13 A | Portable Level-1 EVSE |
| 680 Œ© | 20 A | Standard domestic |
| 220 Œ© | 32 A | High-power AC Level-2 |
| 100 Œ© | 63 A | 3-phase fast AC |

**Latch-State Interlock (3 marks):**
The PP resistor is mounted on a **slider** mechanically linked to the connector's latch button:
- **Latched (button released)** ‚Üí resistor is in the circuit ‚Üí EVSE sees $R_{PP}$, knows current rating, closes contactor.
- **Unlatch pressed mid-charge** ‚Üí resistor is **disconnected** ‚Üí EVSE detects open circuit within **<100 ms** ‚Üí contactor opens immediately, removing mains from the pins *before* the user can touch them.
- **Half-inserted connector** ‚Üí slider may not have moved fully ‚Üí EVSE sees an unexpected resistance and refuses to energize the pins.

### (b) CP vs PP Comparison [7 marks]

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | CP | PP |
|---|---|---|
| Signal type | 1 kHz PWM, DC biased | DC resistance only |
| Function | EV status + current advertisement | Cable rating + latch detection |
| Power source | EVSE drives actively | EVSE senses passively |
| Failure response | Loss ‚Üí contactors open | Open ‚Üí contactors open |
| Standard | IEC 61851-1, SAE J1772 | IEC 61851-1, SAE J1772 |

**Why both are necessary (4 marks):**

> The CP and PP pins are **functionally non-overlapping** and provide **redundant, defense-in-depth safety**.

- **PP-only failure scenario:** If only PP existed, the EV would not know *how much* current to draw. The onboard charger could be commanded to draw 32 A from a 13 A cable ‚Üí cable melts, fire hazard. Hence, **PP alone cannot communicate available current**.

- **CP-only failure scenario:** If only CP existed, the EVSE would have no way to know *when* the connector is latched. A user could insert a connector halfway, the EVSE might (mistakenly) energize the AC pins, and the user could touch the live pins while half-inserted. Hence, **CP alone cannot verify the mechanical interlock**.

- **Combined behavior:** When PP detects *no resistor* (connector removed) **or** CP detects *no 1 kHz tone* (pilot short), both contactors open. This **double interlock** is a hard requirement of IEC 61851-1 and is the reason EVs are among the safest high-voltage consumer products ever mass-produced.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing CP and PP** ‚Äî students frequently interchange them. Remember: **CP = Communication, PWM, current advertisement, EV status**. **PP = Proximity, resistor, cable rating, latch interlock**.
> 2. **Wrong units on duty cycle** ‚Äî 35 % duty = 0.35 in *decimal*, **not** 35. Plug into $I_{max} = 0.6 \times D \times 100$ correctly.
> 3. **Forgetting State D** ‚Äî many students list only A, B, C. **State D (+3 V) is the "ventilation required" state** and is a high-yield KTU question.
> 4. **Writing "PP carries PWM"** ‚Äî this is wrong. PP carries **only DC resistance**; only CP carries PWM.
> 5. **Mixing SAE J1772 and CHAdeMO/CCS pinouts** ‚Äî CP/PP are **AC charging** pins (Type 1/Type 2). DC fast charging (CCS, CHAdeMO) uses different pin arrangements.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CP (Control Pilot):** 1 kHz PWM, +12 V bias, four states at 12 / 9 / 6 / 3 V, duty cycle = available current via $I_{max} = 0.6 \times D \times 100$ A.
- **PP (Proximity Pilot):** Passive resistor, 100 / 220 / 680 / 1500 Œ© encoding 63 / 32 / 20 / 13 A cable ratings, also a latch-state interlock.
- **State A = +12 V (disconnected), State B = +9 V (connected, not ready), State C = +6 V (ready, no vent), State D = +3 V (ready, vent required), State E = 0 V (fault).**
- **CP/PP belong to Layer 1** of the EV‚ÄìEVSE communication stack ‚Äî the physical pilot layer per **IEC 61851-1 / SAE J1772**.
- **Higher-layer protocols:** ISO 15118 (PLC over CP, enables V2G), OCPP (cloud backhaul), DIN 70121 (legacy), GB/T (China).
- **Defence in depth:** CP handles electrical handshake, PP handles mechanical safety ‚Äî both are required by IEC 61851-1.
- **Lose-pilot rule:** If CP or PP signal is lost, both contactors must open within **<100 ms** ‚Äî this is the **single most-tested fact** in KTU exams.
- **1 kHz** is the CP pilot frequency, **+12 V** is the bias voltage, **0.6 A per 1 % duty** is the current-scaling constant.
- **Connectors:** Type 1 (J1772, single-phase, USA/Japan), Type 2 (Mennekes, 3-phase, EU), CCS1/CCS2 (DC fast), CHAdeMO (DC fast, Japan), GB/T (China).
- **Examiners love:** numericals on duty-cycle ‚Üí current conversion, and the CP state-voltage table.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Communication Protocols: CAN
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEET722 - HYBRID AND ELECTRIC VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Communication Protocols: CAN ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Controller Area Network (CAN)** is a robust, multi-master, message-oriented **serial communication bus protocol** standardized under **ISO 11898**, originally developed by **Robert Bosch GmbH (Germany, 1986)** and officially released in **1991**. It is specifically engineered to allow microcontrollers and Electronic Control Units (ECUs) to communicate with each other *without a host computer*, making it the de-facto in-vehicle network backbone of modern automobiles, including Hybrid and Electric Vehicles (HEVs/EVs). In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEEET722)**, the CAN protocol is classified as a **Class C in-vehicle network** ‚Äî meaning it handles real-time, safety-critical, high-speed powertrain data exchange between ECUs such as the **Battery Management System (BMS)**, **Motor Controller (Inverter)**, **VCU (Vehicle Control Unit)**, and **Charger**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy ‚Äî The "Conference Call" vs. "Bullhorn" Model:** > > Imagine a vehicle full of ECUs.
